ahead of print]. Lyon (France): International Agency for Research on Cancer; 2022 [cited
YYYY Mmm D]. (WHO classification of tumours series, 5th ed.; vol. 9). Available from:
https://tumourclassification.iarc.who.int/chapters/52.

Foreword with changes from the book, including corrigenda
The WHO Classification of Tumours, published as a series of books (also known as the WHO Blue
Books) and now as a website (https://tumourclassification.iarc.who.int), is an essential tool for
standardizing diagnostic practice worldwide. It also serves as a vehicle for the translation of cancer
research into practice. The diagnostic criteria and standards that make up the classification are
underpinned by evidence evaluated and debated by experts in the field. About 200 authors and
editors participate in the production of each book, and they give their time freely to this task. I am
very grateful for their help; it is a remarkable team effort.
This ninth volume of the fifth edition of the WHO Blue Books has, like the preceding eight, been led
by the WHO Classification of Tumours Editorial Board, composed of standing and expert members.
The standing members, who have been nominated by pathology organizations, are the equivalent
of the series editors of previous editions. The expert members for each volume, equivalent to the
volume editors of previous editions, are selected on the basis of informed bibliometric analysis and
advice from the standing members. The diagnostic process is increasingly multidisciplinary, and
we are delighted that several radiology and clinical experts have joined us to address specific
needs.
The most conspicuous change to the format of the books in the fifth edition is that tumour types
common to multiple systems are dealt with together – so there are separate chapters on
mesenchymal tumours, melanocytic tumours, haematolymphoid tumours, and neuroendocrine
tumours. There is also a chapter on genetic tumour syndromes. Genetic disorders are of increasing
importance to diagnosis in individual patients, and the study of these disorders has undoubtedly
informed our understanding of tumour biology and behaviour over the past decade.
We have attempted to take a more systematic approach to the multifaceted nature of tumour
classification; each tumour type is described on the basis of its localization, clinical features,
epidemiology, etiology, pathogenesis, histopathology, diagnostic molecular pathology, staging, and
prognosis and prediction. We have also included information on macroscopic appearance and
cytology, as well as essential and desirable diagnostic criteria. This standardized, modular
approach makes it easier for the books to be accessible online, but it also enables us to call
attention to areas in which there is little information, and where serious gaps in our knowledge
remain to be addressed.

9

The organization of the WHO Blue Books content now follows the normal progression from benign
to malignant – a break with the fourth edition, but one we hope will be welcome.
The volumes are still organized by anatomical site (digestive system, breast, soft tissue and bone,
etc.), and each tumour type is listed within a hierarchical taxonomic classification that follows the
format below, which helps to structure the books in a systematic manner:
Site: e.g. Head and Neck
Category: e.g. Nasopharynx
Family (class): e.g. Carcinomas
Type: e.g. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma
Subtype: e.g. Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
The issue of whether a given tumour type represents a distinct entity rather than a subtype
continues to exercise pathologists, and it is the topic of many publications in the literature. We
continue to deal with this issue on a case-by-case basis, but we believe there are inherent rules
that can be applied. For example, tumours in which multiple histological patterns contain shared
truncal mutations are clearly of the same type, despite the differences in their appearance. Equally,
genetic heterogeneity within the same tumour type may have implications for treatment. A small
shift in terminology in the fifth edition is that the term “variant” in reference to a specific kind of
tumour has been wholly superseded by “subtype”, in an effort to more clearly differentiate this
meaning from that of “variant” in reference to a genetic alteration.
Another important change in this edition of the WHO Classification of Tumours series is the
conversion of mitotic count from the traditional denominator of HPF to a defined area expressed in
mm2. This serves to standardize the true area over which mitoses are enumerated, because
different microscopes have high-power fields of different sizes { 34079071 }. This change will also
be helpful for anyone reporting using digital systems.
We are continually working to improve the consistency and standards within the classification. In
addition to having moved to the International System of Units (SI) for all mitotic counts, we have
standardized genomic nomenclature by using Human Genome Variation Society (HGVS) notation.
For CNS and some other tumours, mutation of histone genes is of particular importance, and we
have incorporated the latest nomenclature to ensure that histone sequence variants have an
unambiguous description in the classification. We have also further standardized our use of units
of length, adopting the convention used by the International Collaboration on Cancer Reporting
(http://www.iccr-cancer.org) and the UK Royal College of Pathologists (https://www.rcpath.org/), so
that the size of tumours is now given exclusively in millimetres (mm) rather than centimetres (cm).
This is clearer, in our view, and avoids the use of decimal points – a common source of medical
errors.
The WHO Blue Books are much appreciated by pathologists and of increasing importance to
practitioners of other clinical disciplines involved in cancer management, as well as to researchers.
The editorial board and I certainly hope that the series will continue to meet the need for standards
in diagnosis and to facilitate the translation of diagnostic research into practice worldwide. It is
particularly important that cancers continue to be classified and diagnosed according to the same
standards internationally so that patients can benefit from multicentre clinical trials, as well as from
the results of local trials conducted on different continents.
Dr Ian A. Cree
Head, WHO Classification of Tumours Programme
International Agency for Research on Cancer
February 2022

Website beta version

of Tumours Online community. The content may change during further editing and the review of
proofs
of
the
book.
The
book
will
be
published
in
2022.
Comments can be added using the feedback tool within the individual sections. We hope you find

Differences between the website and the printed book

10

The content of the printed book and that of this website are derived from the same database, so
you will see the same headings online as you do in the book, and the text is identical (except for a
few minor corrections – please see the corrigenda below). The major differences are in the figures.
To keep these large enough to display in all formats, all images are shown as single panels online,
even if they are presented as composite figures with multiple panels in the book. If you click through
the images in the figure menu on the right side of each page online, this should be obvious. You
won’t find the whole slide images in the printed book, but clicking on the thumbnail images in the
online figure menu will open a new window that will allow you to move around the figures and zoom
in or out with a mouse or trackpad. It is the nearest thing to looking down a microscope and gives
context to the features described in the text. You will also find that clicking on the references, which
are cited in the online text by their unique PubMed IDs, takes you straight to the abstract of the
cited article in PubMed.
Classifications evolve, and so will the website, with more hyperlinks planned to make navigation
between the text and relevant figures easier. We also hope to add more whole slide images and
we encourage readers with excellent examples to send in whole slide images for consideration: if
the editors agree, we can add these.
Third-party materials
If you wish to reuse material from this work that is attributed to a third party, such as figures, tables,
or boxes, it is your responsibility to determine whether permission is needed for that reuse and to
obtain permission from the copyright holder (see the source information included for each figure,
table, and box). The risk of claims resulting from infringement of any third-party-owned component
in the work rests solely with the user.
General disclaimers
The designations employed and the presentation of the material in this publication do not imply the
expression of any opinion whatsoever on the part of WHO or contributing agencies concerning the
legal status of any country, territory, city, or area, or of its authorities, or concerning the delimitation
of its frontiers or boundaries. Dotted and dashed lines on maps represent approximate border lines
for which there may not yet be full agreement. The mention of specific companies or of certain
manufacturers’ products does not imply that they are endorsed or recommended by WHO or
contributing agencies in preference to others of a similar nature that are not mentioned. Errors and
omissions excepted, the names of proprietary products are distinguished by initial capital letters.
All reasonable precautions have been taken by WHO to verify the information contained in this
publication. However, the published material is being distributed without warranty of any kind, either
expressed or implied. The responsibility for the interpretation and use of the material lies with the
reader. In no event shall WHO or contributing agencies be liable for damages arising from its use.
Nothing in this website or any materials shall be construed, implicitly or explicitly, as containing any
medical advice. The materials contained in the website are intended neither as a substitute for
professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment, nor for use as the basis for medical decisionmaking.
Corrigenda
Errors categorized as minor will be corrected on the website; moderate errors will be considered
in-house and may be referred to the WHO Classification of Tumours Editorial Board for changes in
an interim update; serious errors will be considered by the next edition.

Introduction
The sinonasal tract (including nasal cavity, paranasal sinuses and the skull base) has always been
an anatomic region in which a remarkable diversity of neoplasms can develop. It is because of this
broad coverage that a taxonomic classification must be very focused to include entities that either
develop in these sites exclusively (i.e., olfactory neuroblastoma) or they develop anywhere in the
head and neck but account for a significant proportion of disease in this location. Further, some
entities are considered in this chapter because they are potential candidates in the differential
diagnosis with other lesions and should be mentioned. As such, squamous cell carcinoma is
included in this chapter, but only keratinizing and non-keratinizing types which predominate in this
region, while subtypes (acantholytic, adenoid, basaloid, spindle cell, papillary, verrucous,
adenosquamous, carcinoma cuniculatum), which rarely occur in the sinonasal tract are covered
more thoroughly in other chapters (larynx, oral cavity, nasopharynx, and oropharynx). One of the
most significant differences between the 4th and 5th editions is the exclusive coverage in their own
chapters of the soft tissue tumours, haematolymphoid proliferations and neoplasms, melanocytic
tumours, metastatic tumours to head and neck, and most significantly, neuroendocrine neoplasms
(including paraganglioma). Accordingly, aside from olfactory neuroblastoma, neuroendocrine
tumours and neuroendocrine carcinomas (small cell and large cell types) are not discussed in this
chapter other than in the differential diagnosis context. Bone tumours, including fibro-osseous
lesions, occur in the sinonasal tract but are covered in the odontogenic and maxillofacial bone
tumours chapter, where histologic overlap and tumour centering are more likely to be problematic.

12

There are always exceptions to rules, and so, for tumours which develop exclusively (sinonasal
glomangiopericytoma, biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma, sinonasal angiofibroma) or predominantly
in the sinonasal tract (chordoma, meningioma, craniopharyngioma), are covered in this chapter
rather than in general topic chapters.
New entities in this edition include HPV-related multiphenotypic sinonasal carcinoma (provisionally
included as HPV-related carcinoma with adenoid cystic-like features in the 4th edition) and SWI/SNF
complex-deficient sinonasal carcinoma (provisionally included as SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal
carcinoma in the 4th edition). The SWI/SNF complex-deficient sinonasal carcinomas is a group of
tumours defined by inactivation of one of the SWI/SNF complex genes, and include SMARCB1deficient sinonasal carcinoma, SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal adenocarcinoma, and SMARCA4deficient sinonasal carcinoma. A subset of teratocarcinosarcomas also exhibit SMARCA4 loss, but
are considered separately because of the tumour’s unique histologic features and because
SMARCA4 loss is not always present. Developing and emerging entities include the IDH-mutant
sinonasal malignancies, high grade sinonasal tract neoplasms presently classified predominantly
as sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma but also sometimes as neuroendocrine carcinoma,
sinonasal adenocarcinoma, or even olfactory neuroblastoma. In future editions, these tumours may
be better categorized separately as IDH-mutant sinonasal malignancies { 34265800 }. Along
similar lines, DEK::AFF2 carcinoma is an emerging category presently covered as a subtype of
nonkeratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, sometimes showing a deceptively bland histologic
appearance
{ 33002918 ; 34049316 ; 34108636 }.
With
additional
experience DEK::AFF2 carcinoma may be recognized as a distinct entity. A few specific fusions
and mutations have been identified in non-intestinal type sinonasal adenocarcinomas, but these
alterations are not yet sufficiently well defined as to warrant separate categorization. In the context
of worldwide global access to certain testing modalities (immunohistochemistry and genetic
studies), the tumours of the sinonasal tract for the most part are still defined by their histological
features, utilizing ancillary testing to narrow the diagnosis when required for differences in treatment
and prognostication.
Improvements in imaging studies continue to guide diagnosis and management. To wit, for the first
time, this edition of the classification included a radiologist as a member of the writing team to
incorporate pertinent imaging findings into the classification as a multidisciplinary approach to
meaningful diagnosis and patient management.
Hamartomas

Respiratory epithelial adenomatoid hamartoma
Definition
Respiratory epithelial adenomatoid hamartoma (REAH) is an overgrowth of surface epithelium
derived medium-sized, ciliated glands surrounded by thickened basement membrane.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Not recommended: glandular hamartoma
Subtype(s)
Chondro-osseous respiratory epithelial (CORE) hamartoma
Localization
Most occur in the nasal cavity, particularly the posterior septum and olfactory clefts
{ 7639474 ; 23883815 ; 31690100 },
sometimes
secondarily
involving
the
ethmoid
labyrinth { 24121171 }. Less common sites include lateral nasal wall, middle meatus and
turbinates, while rare in the nasopharynx, maxillary or frontal sinus. Bilateral lesions are not
uncommon { 7639474 ; 31690100 }.
Clinical features
Patients may present with nasal obstruction (most commonly), anosmia, headache and epistaxis,
often with longstanding chronic rhinosinusitis.

13

Imaging: Imaging studies may demonstrate olfactory cleft expansion without bone erosion
{ 7639474 ; 23179654 ; 23883815 ; 31690100 }.
Epidemiology
Predominantly affected are adults, with a male predominance. Patients of a wide age range are
affected, with a median presentation in the sixth decade { 7639474 ; 23883815 ; 32208747 }.
Etiology
REAH commonly arises in association with allergy and sinonasal inflammatory disorders,
suggesting a relationship to central compartment atopic disease { 32208747 }.
Pathogenesis
Increased fractional allelic loss (31%) has been reported, raising the possibility that REAH is a
neoplasm rather than a hamartoma { 17122514 }. Other studies have explored the clonal nature of
sinonasal hamartomas (REAH, seromucinous hamartoma or combined) { 31698004 }, but without
documenting a clonal origin for pure REAH. Tryptase-producing mast cells and
metalloproteinase expression has been proposed in REAH development { 24121171 }.
Macroscopic appearance
REAHs are polypoid or exophytic with a rubbery consistency, tan-white to reddish-brown, up to 60
mm in greatest dimension { 31690100 }.
Histopathology
REAH consists of a polypoid proliferation of medium-sized, elongated to rounded, branching glands
that expand from the surface epithelium downward into the stroma. They are separated by varying
amounts of edematous or fibrous stroma, usually with chronic inflammation. The epithelium is
pseudostratified, ciliated type, frequently with mucinous metaplasia which can be extensive
resulting in glands dilated by mucin. The epithelium may be atrophic, with a flattened appearance
to the limited cell layers. The glands are typically surrounded by thickened, brightly eosinophilic
basement membrane { 7639474 }. There is often a limited proliferation of small seromucinous
glands in between or budding from the end of typical REAH glands. In large numbers, these may
produce a hamartoma with combined features of REAH and seromucinous hamartoma
{ 21618016 }. Associated findings include hyperplasia or squamous metaplasia of the surface
epithelium or a background of chronic rhinosinusitis and polyps. REAH may be isolated or rarely
associated with inverted sinonasal papilloma or solitary fibrous tumour { 7639474 }.
Subtype: Chondro-osseous respiratory epithelial (CORE) hamartoma is a subtype of REAH.
However, in addition to the glandular component (less prominent than is present in REAHs), CORE
hamartomas have an admixture of cartilaginous and/or osseous trabeculae that are intimately
associated
with
the
glandular
proliferation
{ 15627453 ; 25992017 ; 26601559 ; 26669890 ; 31428496 }. A spectrum of chondro-osseous
differentiation can be found, with some cases manifesting immature-appearing mesenchyme in
which cartilaginous plates display a zonal phenomenon resembling endochondral ossification in
fetal skeletal development to cases with well- developed bony trabeculae in a myxoid to fibrous
stroma.
Histochemistry: PAS highlights the thickened basement membrane.
Immunohistochemistry: The epithelium of REAH stains with CK7, MUC4 and CK8/18, but not with
CK5/6, CK20, CDX2 or SATB2 { 31698004 }. The basal layer is intact and stains with p63, p40 or
S100 protein, lacking in the small gland component { 24121171 }. There is no EBV-association
{ 24667091 }.
Differential The differential diagnosis includes an inflammatory polyp, inverted papilloma,
biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma, and low-grade non-intestinal type sinonasal adenocarcinoma.
Cytology
Not performed
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: surface epithelial origin; ciliated glands; retained basal layer; thickened basement
membrane; displacing normal elements
Staging

14

Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence is rare following complete excision { 7639474 ; 23883815 ; 24121171 }.

#27116: Polypoid mass consisting of elongated or ovoid glands, descending from the surface.

#27117: Marked stromal hyalinization with flattening of the epithelium is common.

#27118: Glands are lined by ciliated epithelium with mucinous cells, enveloped by thick,
hyalinized stroma. There is intervening chronic inflammation.

15

#27119: In most cases, collections of small seromucinous glands are also noted between the
larger glands.

#28064: This lesion consists of a proliferation of small surface-type and seromucinous glands,
associated with spicules of mature lamellar bone.

16

#26907:There is an inverted surface epithelium with remarkably thickened basement membrane.
There are well developed cilia in the hyperplastic epithelium.

Seromucinous hamartoma
Definition
Seromucinous hamartoma (SH) is a benign proliferation of small eosinophilic glands arising in the
sinonasal tract.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Not recommended: microglandular adenosis of the nose; glandular hamartoma; serous hamartoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
SH arises in nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses, typically in the posterior nasal septum and
nasopharynx { 29594917 }.
Clinical features
The most common symptoms are nasal obstruction, purulent rhinorrhea, and epistaxis
{ 29380040 ; 30407294 }. Endoscopically, a papillomatous lesion is usually seen, sometimes with
concurrent inflammatory nasal polyps { 21618016 }.
Imaging: SH is well-defined and homogeneous on both CT and MRI { 30765377 }.
Epidemiology
SH is rare. It has an equal sex distribution, with a wide age range (11-86 years) at presentation
{ 19207945 ; 20922408 ; 21618016 ; 24817677 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
One case harboured an EGFR::ZNF267 gene fusion, and another case displayed monoclonality
by human androgen receptor X-chromosome inactivation assay, supporting the hypothesis of a
neoplastic process { 31698004 }. Mitochondrial DNA revealed a slightly increased mutation rate
mainly in heteroplasmy, consistent with a benign neoplasm { 20922408 }.
Macroscopic appearance

17

SH presents as a polypoid mass, ranging from 4 to 60 mm { 19207945 }.
Histopathology
SH is a polypoid lesion lined by respiratory ciliated epithelium, with many small eosinophilic glands
in
the
stroma,
resembling
microglandular
adenosis
of
the
breast
{ 19207945 ; 20922408 ; 21618016 ; 24817677 }. SH displays a lobular or horizontal distribution,
with no infiltrative growth. Tubules are small, uniform and may contain an amorphous eosinophilic
material; they are lined by epithelial cuboidal cells with small nuclei, without mitoses.
Myoepithelial/basal cells are absent, or only focally present { 22392408 }, but basal lamina around
tubules is discernible. The tubules may be seen between native bony elements, but no true
destructive infiltration is seen. No complex architectural patterns are identified, such as exophytic
growth, papillary architecture, or gland fusion.
The small glands of SH are sometimes combined with cystic structures lined by respiratory
epithelium and p63-positive basal cells, more characteristic of respiratory epithelial adenomatoid
hamartoma. Mixed forms exist as well.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: monotonous proliferation of small tubules without atypia; absent/rare myoepithelial/basal
cells; discernible basal lamina; no infiltrative pattern; no papillary architecture or gland fusion.
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrences are rare following complete excision { 19207945 }.

#23794: The lesion is polypoid and contains many small glands in the stroma with a
horizontal/lobular distribution.

18

#23795: Glands are small, eosinophilic, uniform and may contain an amorphous eosinophilic
material; basal lamina around tubules is discernable.

#23797
Seromucinous hamartoma
Glands are immunoreactive for S100.

19

#23800
Seromucinous hamartoma
p63-positive basal cells are absent around small glands but are evident around cystic
structures lined by respiratory epithelium.

#27210
Seromucinous hamartoma
Small eosinophilic glands may be admixed with cystic structures lined by respiratory
epithelium.

20

#23452
Seromucinous hamartoma
The lesion is polypoid and is composed of small round glands with an horizontal
distribution. Glands are lined by cuboidal epithelium without atypia and may contain
eosinophilic amorphous material. Tubules are encircled by thick basal lamina.

Nasal chondromesenchymal hamartoma
Definition
Nasal chondromesenchymal hamartoma (NCMH) is a benign mesenchymal sinonasal tract tumour
composed of cysts lined by respiratory epithelium associated with nodules of cartilage and a
variably myxoid spindle cell stroma.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E90.6 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx & XH2P15 Mesenchymal hamartoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: chondroid hamartoma; nasal hamartoma; congenital mesenchymoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Frequently bilateral (~25%), tumours involve the paranasal sinuses (mostly ethmoid) and nasal
cavity, sometimes with skull base extension { 9537469 ; 20822816 ; 26138824 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms
are
nonspecific,
with
a
mass
identified
{ 9537469 ; 16253025 ; 20822816 ; 25118636 ; 26138824 }. Patients may have a history of other
neoplasms
associated
with DICER1 pathogenic
variants { 20822816 ; 24761742 ; 25118636 ; 25356068 }.

21

Imaging: Imaging studies shows a complex, solid and cystic heterogeneous soft-tissue mass,
frequent
calcification,
and
associated bone
erosion
{ 9537469 ; 25117604 ; 31620849 ; 31871336 }.
Epidemiology
Less than 1% of patients with pleuropulmonary blastoma have NCMH { 20822816 }. There is a
wide age range, but most patients are <1 year of age (mean: 10 years). There is a male
predominance.
Etiology
NCMH is associated with DICER1 pathogenic variants { 20822816 ; 22356457 }.
Pathogenesis
Similar to other neoplasms in DICER1 syndrome, NCMH show biallelic, loss of function
and missense RNase IIIb mutations in DICER1. This combination of mutations is expected to alter
expression
of
miRNAs important
in
regulating
the
cell
cycle
and
limiting
differentiation { 20822816 ; 24909177 ; 29343557 ; 31620849 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Polypoid, fleshy soft-tissue masses
Histopathology
NCMH is composed of epithelial cysts lined by respiratory epithelium associated with nodules of
immature to mature cartilage with a rim of spindled cells set in a loose, myxoid to spindled cell
stroma. Focal osteoclast-like giant cells may be seen in the stroma near erythrocyte-filled spaces
resembling aneurysmal bone cyst { 9537469 ; 15129948 ; 16253025 ; 20822816 }. A fibroosseous
proliferation with ossicles or trabeculae of immature (woven) bone can be seen { 11231491 }.
Concurrent sinonasal papilloma and chronic rhinosinusitis may be seen.
Immunohistochemistry: The cartilage is highlighted with S100 protein or SOX9
immunohistochemistry, while SMA highlights stromal myofibroblasts { 16253025 ; 11231491 }.
Differential The differential diagnosis includes sinonasal tract hamartomas, biphenotypic
sinonasal sarcoma, and embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma (which can be seen in DICER1 syndrome).
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
When NCMH is diagnosed, germline DICER1 genetic testing is suggested { 29343557 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: cysts lined by respiratory epithelium associated with nodules of immature to mature
cartilage within a bland spindled cell stroma
Desirable: biallelic DICER1 pathogenic variants in selected cases
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Local
recurrences
may
occur
with
incomplete
excision
(about
25%)
{ 9537469 ; 26138824 ; 28385583 }. Rare malignant transformation has been reported
{ 23348387 }.

22

#22220

Sinonasal chondromesenchymal hamartoma
A whole slide image showing primitive mesenchyme with isolated islands of immature
cartilage.

Respiratory epithelial lesions
Sinonasal papillomas

Sinonasal papilloma, inverted type
Definition
Inverted sinonasal papilloma (ISP) is a benign epithelial neoplasm of ectodermally derived
sinonasal epithelium showing inverted non-destructive growth into the stroma.
ICD-O coding
8121/1 Sinonasal papilloma, inverted type

23

ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XH7YQ5 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory system &
Squamous cell papilloma, inverted
Related terminology
Not recommended: inverted Schneiderian papilloma; inverting papilloma; endophytic papilloma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
ISP usually involves the lateral nasal cavity wall and/or paranasal sinuses (most commonly ethmoid
or
maxillary);
may
rarely
occur
on
the
nasal
septum
{ 2184302 ; 4323842 ; 7436854 ; 7877417 ; 8189990 ; 31743131 }. ISP rarely involves the lacrimal
sac, middle ear, nasopharynx, and/or pharynx { 8189990 ; 8615588 ; 10214788 ; 34021464 }.
Clinical features
Patients with ISP typically present with unilateral nasal obstruction, and may also manifest with
rhinorrhea, epistaxis, facial pain/sinus pressure, headache, anosmia, and/or epiphora
{ 2184302 ; 4323842 ; 7877417 ; 8189990 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies document a mass centered around the middle meatus, often with bone
remodeling { 18416970 ; 18499786 ; 19216815 }. Imaging is not definitive for ISP, but there are a
number of features which are highly suggestive including: origin from the lateral wall of the nasal
cavity (with associated focal osteitis at this site on CT), lobulated shape, ‘cerebriform’ (columnar)
pattern on T2W and post-contrast T1W MRI, plateau-enhancement on time-intensity curves (early
peak and sustained high signal) and relatively high ADC values in keeping with benign disease. ISP
usually causes bone thinning and remodeling on CT but can occasionally be locally aggressive.
The combination of columnar enhancement and absent bone erosion allows for the confident
discrimination of IP from malignant sinonasal tumours { 15586802 ; 29987980 }.
Epidemiology
ISP comprises 47-73% of all sinonasal papillomas { 4323842 ; 6370825 ; 7668940 ; 8238761 }.
Although ISP is the most common type of SP, it is still relatively uncommon overall, with an
estimated incidence of 0.2-1.5 cases per 100,000 people-years; reported incidence rates are higher
in Caucasian compared to Asian populations { 7837917 ; 8876075 ; 19401051 ; 24519376 }. It
typically occurs in older adults (peak incidence in 6th decade) but may rarely be observed in
children; there is a marked male predilection (male-to-female ratio approximately 3-5:1)
{ 2184302 ; 4323842 ; 7877417 ; 8189990 }.
Etiology
ISP may be associated with various occupational and/or industrial exposures; however, its
relationship
to
tobacco
smoke
exposure
remains
uncertain
{ 2184302 ; 6715979 ; 8908257 ; 20109321 }. Whereas historical studies documented HPV DNA
(both low- and high-risk subtypes) in subsets of ISP and ISP-associated sinonasal carcinomas
{ 2823523 ; 7478645 ; 23161522 ; 23253489 }, modern RNA in situ hybridization (ISH) analyses of
ISP and ISP-associated sinonasal carcinomas have demonstrated a consistent lack of high-risk
HPV E6/E7 transcripts, despite the presence of low-risk HPV E6/E7 transcripts in a subset of cases
{ 28181187 ; 31743131 }.
Further,
molecular
profiling
studies
demonstrated
somatic EGFR mutations in up to 88% of ISP and 77% of ISP-associated sinonasal carcinomas
{ 25931286 ; 30916792 ; 31743131 }. The vast majority of EGFR mutations are frame-preserving
indels in exon 20, although exon 6 or 19 mutations occur in a small subset
{ 25931286 ; 33203919 }. Somatic EGFR mutations and low-risk HPV infection are mutually
exclusive in ISP and ISP-associated sinonasal carcinomas, suggesting that they are essential
alternative oncogenic mechanisms { 29145573 ; 31743131 }.
Pathogenesis
Oncogenic hotspot mutations in EGFR exons 19 or 20 stimulate tumour growth via constitutive
EGFR activity and downstream activation of the MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways { 21764376 }. In
HPV-associated ISPs, low-risk HPV infection is transcriptionally active, generating corresponding
E6 and E7 transcripts { 31743131 }. Malignant progression of ISP to sinonasal carcinoma is
associated with deleterious TP53 and/or CDKN2A alterations { 33203919 }.
Macroscopic appearance

24

ISP has a polypoid gross appearance with a convoluted cerebriform surface and typically
demonstrate broad attachment to the underlying mucosa { 8189990 ; 13938966 }.
Histopathology
ISP is a noninvasive epithelial neoplasm with predominantly endophytic growth
{ 4323842 ; 8189990 }. Tumours are comprised of nests and/or ribbons of hyperplastic immature
squamous epithelium (formerly transitional epithelium) within edematous hypocellular stroma. The
nests are rounded with an intact basement membrane. Prominent transmigrating intraepithelial
neutrophilic inflammation is a characteristic feature. Scattered admixed ciliated or mucous
columnar cells may be present superficially, and irritative changes may include increased
squamous maturation, overlying keratosis, and/or underlying fibrosis. Low-risk HPV-associated
ISPs may have a thickened epithelium with koilocyte-like cells, clear cell change, and binucleation;
and, verrucoid growth with limited involvement of the underlying subepithelial stroma
{ 31743131 }. Rare papillomas have overlapping features of inverted and oncocytic papilloma
{ 30916792 }.
Epithelial dysplasia is uncommon in ISP and may manifest in keratinizing and/or nonkeratinizing
forms { 4323842 ; 24519376 }. Keratinizinq epithelial dysplasia is similar to that observed in the
oral cavity or larynx and may include hyperkeratosis, squamous dysmaturation, pleomorphism,
and/or increased mitotic activity. Nonkeratinizing epithelial dysplasia may resemble NKSCC and
include loss of transmigrating intraepithelial neutrophilic inflammation, basaloid morphology,
conspicuous cytologic atypia, and/or increased mitotic activity. While there is no consensus grading
system for dysplasia in ISP, it is important to recognize and report severe dysplasia/carcinoma in
situ when present { 24519376 }.
Differential Given distinct differences in etiology and clinical behavior, the most important
differential diagnosis for ISP is NKSCC. Although both tumours may demonstrate an endophytic
growth pattern, NKSCC frequently has a basaloid appearance, conspicuously pleomorphism
and/or mitotic activity; atypical mitoses; predominantly exophytic growth; and/or lack of
transmigrating intraepithelial neutrophilic inflammation. Furthermore, while NKSCC is often positive
for high-risk HPV by RNA ISH for high-risk HPV E6/E7 transcripts, ISP is consistently
negative { 28181187 ; 31743131 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: predominantly endophytic non-destructive growth; ribbons/nests of hyperplastic,
immature squamous epithelium; transmigrating intraepithelial neutrophilic inflammation.
Staging
No Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification, but the Krouse clinical staging
system (T1-T4) classifies ISP by anatomic location, tumour extent, and presence of concurrent
malignancy { 10852514 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Reported recurrence rates vary widely for ISP – particularly when historical cohorts are considered
– and depend on a number of clinical factors, including: surgical approach (higher with nonendoscopic techniques); tumour anatomic location (higher for frontal and anterior maxillary sinus
tumours);
and Krouse
stage
(higher
for
T3
vs.
T2
tumours)
{ 16500448 ; 22253070 ; 28973390 ; 31777289 }. Based on a large multi-center study and metaanalysis, the overall estimated recurrence rate for ISP is 12-16% { 22253070 ; 28973390 }.
Malignant transformation – either synchronous or metachronous – develops at a rate of about 24% based on large longitudinal and multi-center studies { 22253070 ; 24519376 } rather than the
higher rates reported by referral centers. The vast majority of ISP-associated sinonasal carcinomas
are keratinizing or nonkeratinizing squamous cell carcinomas, however, other types of
malignancies have been reported, including undifferentiated carcinoma { 29070275 }. Although
ISP-associated mucoepidermoid carcinomas have been rarely reported, they likely represent
adenosquamous carcinomas { 24519376 }. Cigarette smoke exposure is a risk factor for ISP

25

malignant progression { 23619620 }, and recent evidence suggests that low-risk HPV infection in
ISP may be associated with an increased risk of malignant progression { 29145573 ; 31743131 }.

30

Sinonasal papilloma, oncocytic type
Definition
Oncocytic sinonasal papilloma (OSP) is a benign epithelial neoplasm of ectodermally derived
sinonasal epithelium showing multilayered oncocytic cuboidal to columnar epithelium.
ICD-O coding
8121/1 Sinonasal papilloma, oncocytic type
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XH17Q9 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory system &
Papilloma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: oncocytic Schneiderian papilloma; cylindrical cell papilloma; columnar cell
papilloma; Ringertz papilloma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
OSP involves the lateral wall of the nasal cavity and/or paranasal sinuses (most commonly the
maxillary sinus) and may also rarely involve other head and neck sites, including the middle ear
{ 4323842 ; 6370825 ; 8615588 ; 23595801 ; 31890872 ; 34021464 }.
Clinical features
Patients with OSP typically present with unilateral nasal obstruction and may also manifest with
rhinorrhea, epistaxis, and/or anosmia { 6370825 ; 23595801 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies document a mass centered around the middle meatus, often with bone
remodeling { 23595801 ; 28861190 ; 29987980 }. OSP appears similar to inverted sinonasal
papilloma on imaging but studies have shown three distinctive features which may be helpful: T1W
high signal; multiple mucinous cystic foci; and lack of focal osteitis { 29987980 }.
Epidemiology
OSP comprises 3-19% of all sinonasal papillomas { 4323842 ; 8238761 ; 23595801 }, estimated to
have an incidence of 0.05 cases per 100,000 people years { 7837917 }. They typically occur in
older adults (peak incidence in 6th to 7th decades), are rare in patients younger than 30 years old,
and have an equal sex distribution { 23595801 ; 31890872 }.
Etiology
There are no confirmed risk factors. Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection is not an etiologic factor
{ 7837916 ; 29145573 }.
Pathogenesis
Oncogenic hotspot mutations in KRAS exons 2 or 3 stimulate tumour growth via constitutive KRAS
activity and downstream activation of the MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways { 17428555 ; 32203683 }.
Malignant
progression
of
OSP
to
sinonasal
carcinoma
is associated
with
deleterious TP53 and CDKN2A mutations { 33203919 }.
Macroscopic appearance
OSP has a papillary and/or polypoid gross appearance; tumours can be very large (>100 mm) but
are typically less than 50 mm { 6370825 }.
Histopathology
OSP is a noninvasive papillary epithelial neoplasm with mixed exophytic and endophytic growth
{ 4323842 ; 6370825 }. Tumours are comprised of multiple layers of cuboidal to columnar cells with
abundant oncocytic cytoplasm, slightly enlarged and irregular but centrally-placed nuclei, and small
nucleoli; scattered admixed ciliated or mucous columnar cells may be present superficially.
Intraepithelial neutrophilic inflammation is common, while microcysts with mucin and/or neutrophilic
microabscesses are characteristic. Epithelial dysplasia is uncommon in OSP but may manifest as
increased nuclear atypia and/or mitotic activity { 8238761 ; 2321700 ; 34021464 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

31

Essential: mixed exophytic and endophytic growth pattern; oncocytic cells with cuboidal to
columnar morphology; intraepithelial microcysts with mucin and/or neutrophilic microabscesses.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Reported recurrence rates vary widely – from 6-40% of tumours in large studies with long-term
follow-up – and these observed differences are likely multifactorial in origin, including tumour
anatomic location (higher for paranasal sinus tumours) and surgical approach (higher with nonendoscopic techniques) { 23595801 ; 31890872 }.
Malignant transformation – either synchronous or metachronous – occurs in 3-17% of OSP
{ 8238761 ; 12172247 ; 23595801 }; the vast majority of these tumours are keratinizing or
nonkeratinizing squamous cell carcinomas, however, other types of malignancies have been rarely
reported, including adenocarcinoma, small cell carcinoma, and undifferentiated carcinoma
{ 11570918 ; 29879069 }. Although OSP-associated mucoepidermoid carcinomas have been
reported { 8238761 }, based on contemporary criteria, they most likely represent adenosquamous
carcinomas.

#28519

Sinonasal papilloma, oncocytic type
A whole slide of an sinonasal papilloma,oncocytic type showing a multilayared epithelium
comprised of oncocytic cells with columnar morphology and numerous intraepithelial

Sinonasal papilloma, exophytic type
Definition
Exophytic sinonasal papilloma (ESP) is a benign sinonasal tract epithelial neoplasm composed of
broad papillary fronds with delicate fibrovascular cores covered by a maturing multilayered
epithelium.
ICD-O coding
8121/0 Sinonasal papilloma, exophytic type
ICD-11 coding

35

2F00.Y & XH0TP8 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory system &
Sinonasal papilloma, exophytic
Related terminology
Not recommended: Schneiderian papilloma, exophytic type; fungiform papilloma; everted
papilloma; transitional cell papilloma; septal papilloma; Ringertz tumour.
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
ESP usually arise on the lower anterior nasal septum { 7837917 ; 29341451 }. As they enlarge,
they may secondarily involve the lateral nasal wall, but rarely originate there. Involvement of the
paranasal sinuses is extremely rare. Bilateral lesions are exceptional { 7837917 ; 29341451 }.
Clinical features
Typical presenting symptoms include epistaxis and unilateral nasal obstruction caused by a mass
lesion. Endoscopy aids in defining the site and extent of disease, but imaging is unnecessary.
Epidemiology
These tumours are 2–10 times as common in males as females, and typically occur in individuals
aged 20–50 years (range: 2–87 years) { 3957337 ; 23883810 }.
Etiology
The majority of these tumours harbour low-risk human papillomavirus (mostly types 6 and 11)
{ 7837916 ; 23161522 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
The tumours present as papillary or warty, grey, pink, or tan growths attached to the nasal septum
by a relatively broad base. They range in size up to about 20 mm.
Histopathology
ESP are composed of papillary fronds with fibrovascular cores covered by a
proliferative, multilayered epithelium that is 5–20 cells thick. The epithelium varies from squamous
to ciliated pseudostratified columnar (respiratory), or may be transitional between the two.
Scattered mucocytes and intraepithelial neutrophils are common. Surface keratinization is absent
or scant, unless irritated by trauma or dried by air. Mitoses are rare and not atypical. Unless infected
or irritated, the stroma contains few inflammatory cells. Malignant change in exophytic papilloma is
extremely rare { 3957337 ; 11236954 }.
Differential Exophytic papillomas must be distinguished from cutaneous keratinizing squamous
papilloma, which are much more common in the nasal vestibule, inverted SP, and from
papillary squamous cell carcinoma.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: proliferative, exophytic, and papillary growth; lined by a multilayered sinonasal-type
epithelium; no high-grade squamous dysplasia; no inverted or destructive infiltrative growth
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Local recurrence rates are 22-50%, associated with inadequate excision { 3957337 ; 7837917 }.
Malignant transformation is exceptionally rare { 24519376 ; 26261100 }.

36

#27213: Most sinonasal papilloma, exophytic type harbour low-risk HPV, here demonstrated by
RNA in situ hybridization for HPV types 6 and 11.

#27246: The lesion is exophytic and covered by a maturing multilayered squamous epithelium.

37

#27247: Maturing squamous epithelium with superficial koilocytes lines these finger-like
projections. Scattered transmigrating intraepithelial neutrophils are seen.

#26936: There are complex papillary projections into the lumen, lined by a transitional-type
epithelium.

38

Carcinomas

Keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
Sinonasal keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma (KSCC) is an epithelial malignancy originating
from surface epithelium with squamous differentiation and keratin production.
ICD-O coding
8071/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, keratinizing, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2C20.4 & XH4CR9 Squamous cell carcinoma of nasal cavity & Squamous cell carcinoma,
keratinizing, NOS 2C22.1 & XH4CR9 Squamous cell carcinoma of accessory sinuses &
Squamous cell carcinoma, keratinizing, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: squamous cell carcinoma NOS; conventional squamous cell carcinoma;
epidermoid carcinoma
Subtype(s)
Papillary squamous cell carcinoma; verrucous carcinoma; spindle cell squamous carcinoma;
acantholytic squamous cell carcinoma; adenosquamous carcinoma; carcinoma cuniculatum.
Localization
Maxillary antrum, lateral nasal wall, and sphenoid sinuses are the most commonly affected sites,
while nasal septum, nasal floor, and frontal and sphenoid sinuses are less frequently
involved. KSCC is the most common malignancy of the nasal vestibule { 19475548 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with non-specific nasal obstruction, facial pain, rhinorrhea, and epistaxis
{ 26830402 }. Tumours grow by local extension, infiltrating the neighbouring structures
{ 26830402 }. Lymph node metastases are rare.
Imaging: Imaging studies demonstrate an aggressive soft tissue-density mass with poorly defined,
irregular to spiculated margins, often with bone destruction, and documentation of metastatic
disease or multifocal primaries { 17893591 ; 23777920 ; 24393570 ; 24929443 ; 26054571 }.
Epidemiology
KSCC
represents
up
to
45–50%
of
sinonasal
tract
malignancies
{ 22127982 ; 26228792 ; 31143568 ; 32582473 }. KSCC predominates in males (male-to-female
ratio
2:1)
with
the
majority
arising
in
patients
older
than
50
years
{ 22127982 ; 26228792 ; 31143568 ; 32582473 }.
Etiology
Occupational hazards explain some KSCC risk as well as male predominance. Long exposures to
nickel, chrome, arsenic, formaldehyde, welding fumes, leather dust, glues, and various textilerelated compounds are attributed to tumourigenesis in up to 30% { 32582473 }. A clear association
between KSCC and cigarette smoking has been documented { 26830402 }. Chronic sinonasal
inflammation is considered a predisposing factor.
Pathogenesis
KSCC originates in the sinonasal mucosa from areas of pre-existing squamous metaplasia. About
60%
of
KSCC arising
from
inverted
papillomas
present
synchronously
{ 24519376 ; 26830402 }. Aside
from KSCC-ex
sinonasal
papilloma,
squamous
dysplasia/carcinoma-in-situ is uncommon in adjacent mucosa.
The genomic landscape of KSCC is poorly defined, with reports of TP53 mutations, p53 expression
alterations, microsatellite instability, chromosomal aberrations { 32490686 }, EGFR mutations
{ 30117182 }, and alterations in PTEN, CDKN2A, and KMT2D { 28572459 }. Approximately 20% of
sinonasal KSCC harbour EGFR mutation, and most of them are associated with inverted papilloma
{ 29145573 ; 30117182 ; 32868526 }. High microsatellite instability is very rare. Sinonasal KSCC
is much less likely to harbour high-risk HPV infection than NKSCC { 31743130 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Nonspecific, similar to KSCC at other sites
Histopathology
39

KSCC shows infiltrative proliferations of malignant epithelial cells with squamous differentiation in
the form of glassy eosinophilic cytoplasm, intercellular bridges, and frequent keratin production.
Differentiation degree, cellular pleomorphism, and mitotic activity account for three grades
(well/moderately/poorly differentiated). Well-differentiated KSCC is uncommon. Moderately or
poorly differentiated KSCC constitute the majority. Special types, such as papillary, verrucous,
spindle cell, acantholytic, adenosquamous carcinoma, and carcinoma cuniculatum each constitute
a few percent of tumours (and are more extensively discussed in the larynx sections).
Immunohistochemistry: Immunohistochemistry is seldom required, but pancytokeratin, p40, p63,
and CK5/6 are usually identified.
Differential
The primary differential diagnosis is with sinonasal papilloma, necrotizing
sialometaplasia, pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia, verrucous hyperplasia, while other spindled
cell neoplasms would be considered in the spindle cell squamous cell carcinoma category.
Cytology
Smears show plentiful dispersed spindle, polygonal and tadpole cells, showing varying degrees of
keratinization with dense light blue (Giemsa stain) or orangophilic (Pap stain) cytoplasm, as well
as, relatively cohesive tissue fragments of similar cells with well defined dense cytoplasm. Nuclei
vary from various stages of pyknosis in the keratinized cells to more open large often centrally
placed nuclei with coarse chromatin and prominent nucleoli. There is often a large amount of
keratinous debris in the background, with or without a granulomatous reaction { 12704690 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: malignant epithelial cells with squamous differentiation and keratinization
Desirable: positivity for p40 immunostain (in selected cases)
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
While incidence is decreasing, 5-year overall survival (OS) rates are around 50%
{ 23674262 ; 23775607 ; 32582473 }. 5-year relative survival (RS) for patients with nasal
cavity KSCC is 74.5% compared with 35% for maxillary and ethmoid sinus KSCC and <30% for
frontal and sphenoid KSCC { 32582473 }. While tumours with PD-L1 expression show improved
outcomes for oropharynx, oral cavity and larynx { 32490686 }, studies in sinonasal KSCC are still
ongoing { 29356178 ; 31190998 }.

Keratinizing Squamous Cell Carcinoma

Proliferation of malignant epithelial cells with squamous differentiation and intercellular
bridges.

Keratinizing Squamous Cell Carcinoma
Keratin production is frequent.

Keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
Malignant epithelial cells with squamous differentiation and intercellular bridges; keratin
production is frequent.

#25241
Carcinoma cunniculatum

Keratin-filled crypts resembling a rabbit burrow, minimal atypia and extensive bone loss
are diagnostic hallmarks of carcinoma cunniculatum, anecdotally reported in these
sites/maxilla.

43

Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma (NKSCC) is an epithelial malignancy of sinonasal
surface origin that shows histologic and immunohistochemical evidence of squamous
differentiation but has no or minimal keratinization.
ICD-O coding
8072/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, non-keratinizing, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2C20.4 & XH6705 Squamous cell carcinoma of nasal cavity & Squamous cell carcinoma, large cell,
nonkeratinizing, NOS
2C22.1 & XH6705 Squamous cell carcinoma of accessory sinuses & Squamous cell carcinoma,
large cell, nonkeratinizing, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: schneiderian carcinoma, transitional cell carcinoma, cylindrical cell carcinoma.
Subtype(s)
HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma; DEK::AFF2 carcinoma
Localization
Sinonasal NKSCC most commonly arises in the nasal cavity or maxillary sinuses
{ 5014917 ; 22127982 ; 23674262 ; 23775607 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with mass effect, including nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and pain. Paranasal sinus
tumours present at a higher stage than nasal cavity tumours { 22127982 ; 23674262 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies cannot accurately differentiate non-keratinizing for keratinizing SCC and
in both cases demonstrates an aggressive soft tissue lesion with bony destruction of adjacent sinus
walls, most frequently arising from the maxillary sinus, followed by ethmoid air cells and the nasal
cavity. As many present at an advanced stage invasion of the orbit, infratemporal fossa and skull
base is frequently observed. A combination of post-contrast CT and MRI is helpful to accurately
delineate tumour extension through bone and soft tissues. Intratumoural necrosis is commonly
seen in larger lesions with average solid tumour ADC values of 0.95 x 10 -3 mm2/s
{ 23762617 ; 29211048 }.
Epidemiology
NKSCC accounts for 20-48% of sinonasal SCC, recognizing historical overlap with papillary and
basaloid patterns { 16160480 ; 23095507 ; 24030745 ; 26830402 }. Tumours most frequently
arise in the sixth to seventh decade, and affect males more frequently than females
{ 393432 ; 5410316 ; 16160480 ; 23095507 }.
Etiology
Like other sinonasal SCCs, NKSCC has a weak association with tobacco use { 24935016 }. Highrisk human papillomavirus (HPV) is implicated in 20-62% of all sinonasal SCCs in North America
and Europe and 7-21% in Asia. Specifically, 36-58% of NKSCC are HPV-associated
{ 16160480 ; 19365846 ; 23095507 ; 24030745 ; 24338611 ; 26229021 ; 28271500 ; 31743130 ;
32149447 ; 32683856 ; 33364396 }. HPV16 is relatively less common in the sinonasal tract than
in
the
oropharynx,
representing
41-82%
of
HPV-associated
cases
{ 23095507 ; 26229021 ; 32683856 ; 33232848 }. Only rare EBV-positive NKSCC have been
reported { 7661287 }, and about 15% of carcinomas ex-sinonasal papilloma are NKSCC
{ 11904343 ; 24519376 }.
Pathogenesis
The molecular underpinnings of sinonasal NKSCC have not been comprehensively characterized,
although those sequenced show similar molecular profiles to other head and neck SCCs
{ 20848437 ; 24935016 ; 25712460 ; 31743130 ; 32868526 }. Transcriptional activity is identified
in HPV-associated NKSCC via p16 overexpression combined with PCR positivity or RNA in-situ
hybridization,
confirming
oncogenically relevant
virus
{ 23095507 ; 24030745 ; 26229021 ; 28181187 }. Nearly half of HPV-negative NKSCC tested
have been shown to harbour recurrent fusions between the DEK gene on chromosome 6p22.3 and
the AFF2 gene
on
chromosome
Xq28

44

{ 31011208 ; 32366754 ; 33002918 ; 34049316 ; 34108636 } that likely represent oncogenic
drivers.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours can be endophytic or exophytic and have a friable white-tan surface with hemorrhage and
necrosis. Tumours are typically large and bulky.
Histopathology
NKSCC is arranged in expansile nests, lobules, or ribbons that display a pushing pattern of invasion
with a smooth stromal interface and minimal associated desmoplastic response despite often deep
and destructive growth. A subset shows predominantly papillary architecture with extensive
(sometimes complete) surface growth and colonization of adjacent flat surface epithelium
{ 19745700 ; 26830402 }. Invasive growth, as classically defined for SCC in other anatomic sites,
is not necessary in mass-forming tumours. Tumour cells have a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio,
a columnar basal layer with peripheral palisading, a more flattened superficial layer, and lack
significant keratinization. Cytologic atypia is highly variable, ranging from minimal to pronounced,
but grading is not applied. Mitotic rates also vary widely and necrosis may occasionally be seen.
Subtypes: HPV-associated SCC is a subtype of sinonasal NKSCC defined by the presence of
transcriptionally active high-risk HPV. The majority of these tumours demonstrate otherwise classic
NKSCC morphology, although a minority of HPV-associated sinonasal carcinomas
represent keratinizing,
basaloid,
or
adenosquamous
subtypes
{ 16160480 ; 23095507 ; 24030745 ; 24338611 ; 26229021 ; 33364396 }.
DEK::AFF2 carcinoma is an emerging subtype of NKSCC defined by recurrent DEK::AFF2 fusions
{ 31011208 ; 32366754 ; 33002918 ; 34108636 }. They demonstrate complex exophytic and
endophytic growth, with broad papillary fronds and anastomosing lobules lined by transitional
epithelium with amphophilic to eosinophilic cytoplasm. The presence of strikingly monotonous
round to oval nuclei, surface or intraepithelial discohesion, and frequent tumour-infiltrating
neutrophils or lymphocytes may help differentiate them from other NKSCC. DEK::AFF2 carcinoma
shows substantial morphologic overlap with tumours reported as low-grade papillary sinonasal
(Schneiderian)
carcinoma
{ 25634744 ; 28639124 ; 30269519 ; 31473940 ; 33539575 ; 34041710 }, and recent data suggest
that many if not most of these tumours also harbour DEK::AFF2 fusions { 34108636 }.
Immunohistochemistry: NKSCC are diffusely positive for cytokeratins, particularly high-molecularweight cocktails such as CK5/6 and CK34βe12/CK903, and for p63 and p40
{ 19077909 ; 24114197 }. They are negative for synaptophysin, chromogranin, INSM1, S100
protein, NUT, and CD99 with intact SMARCB1 (INI1) and SMARCA4 (BRG1)
{ 10091761 ; 25007146 ; 26034869 ; 29438167 32520761}.
Differential Although some NKSCC were historically classified as basaloid SCC, this variant should
be restricted to tumours with nested architecture, prominent basement membrane deposition, and
comedonecrosis. While inverted growth in NKSCC may be reminiscent of inverted papilloma,
definitive synchronous or metachronous benign papilloma must be present for a designation of
carcinoma ex-papilloma. DEK::AFF2 carcinomas can overlap with benign papillomas due to bland
cytology but demonstrate more irregular stromal interfaces and complex architecture. HPV-related
multiphenotypic sinonasal carcinoma displays basaloid areas similar to NKSCC but also
demonstrates ductal and/or myoepithelial differentiation. Several other sinonasal tumours with
squamous differentiation are now regarded as distinct entities, including NUT carcinoma,
adamantinoma-like Ewing sarcoma, and SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal carcinoma. Other
sinonasal carcinomas lack squamous differentiation by immunohistochemistry, but are unique
tumours, such as sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma, neuroendocrine carcinoma, and
SMARCA4-deficient sinonasal carcinoma.
Cytology
Smears show predominantly large sheets and tissue fragments consisting of cells with well defined
dense cytoplasm, lacking keratinization, with large pleomorphic nuclei with coarse chromatin and
prominent one or more pleomorphic nucleoli. Necrosis and variable keratinous debris are seen in
the background.
Diagnostic molecular pathology

45

While routine HPV testing is not recommended in sinonasal NKSCC, it can occasionally be helpful
for diagnostic purposes. If utilized, HPV-specific testing such as in-situ hybridization or PCR must
be
performed
because
p16
has
poor
specificity
in
sinonasal
tumours
{ 21805120 ; 23095507 ; 27392929 ; 32671903 }. DEK::AFF2 fusions can be confirmed via RNA
sequencing or DEK breakapart fluorescent in-situ hybridization.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: morphological and/or immunohistochemical evidence of squamous differentiation; no to
minimal keratinization; active exclusion of mimics, such as NUT carcinoma, SMARCB1-deficient
sinonasal carcinoma, and adamantinoma-like Ewing sarcoma
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
Although specific outcomes of NKSCC are unknown, sinonasal SCC overall has a 5-year survival
of approximately 60% { 393432 ; 20001493 ; 22127982 ; 23674262 ; 23775607 }. While singleinstitution and large database studies have often shown better prognosis in HPV-associated
tumours,
the
benefit
has
not
translated
in
clinical
practice
{ 16160480 ; 19365846 ; 23095507 ; 24030745 ; 24338611 ; 26229021 ; 28271500 ; 28859244 ;
31743130 ; 31886908 ; 32149447 ; 32476435 ; 32683856 ; 33364396 }.
In
limited
experience, DEK::AFF2 carcinomas showed frequent local recurrence, and 25% of patients died
of disease { 34049316 }.

#26054

Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma

Nested and lobulated growth of cells with high-nuclear cytoplasmic ratio and no
keratinization with peripheral palisading and minimal stromal desmoplasia.

46

#26055

Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
Predominantly papillary growth can be seen in a subset of tumours, and cytologic atypia
can vary widely.

#26057
Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma

HPV-associated SCC is a subtype of NKSCC defined by the presence of transcriptionally
active high-risk HPV, highlighted here by RNA in-situ hybridization.

47

#26063

Non-keratinising squamous cell carcinoma, DEK::AFF2 subtype

Confluent exophytic and endophytic growth with surface epithelial discohesion and
strikingly monotonous oval nuclei.

#26064

Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, DEK::AFF2 subtype

Dense tumour-infiltrating neutrophils are frequently seen.

48

#27229
Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
The classic growth pattern of NKSCC is partially papillary and exophytic, with invasion as
broad, pushing, smooth-edged ribbons. This pattern is reminiscent of inverted papilloma,
but NKSCC is much more basophilic due to the high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios of the
neoplastic cells.

#28354
Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
Coronal CT (bone window) shows a large soft tissue lesion filling the left nasal cavity with
attenuation of the left lamina paparacea.

49

#28355
Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
Coronal STIR MRI shows clear differentiation between the soft tissue tumour
(intermediate signal), extending into the left maxillary sinus and retained sinus secretions
(high signal).

#28356
Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma
Axial post-contrast fat suppressed T1W MRI shows linear ‘cerebriform’ enhancement
suggesting this NKSCC may have arisen from inverted papilloma.

#25933

Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma

Expansile nests of cells with a high nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio and minimal keratinization
that display a smooth stromal interface and minimal associated desmoplastic response.

#25934: Complex exophytic and endophytic growth with anastomosing lobules lined by
transitional epithelium with strikingly monotonous oval nuclei and dense tumour-infiltrating
neutrophils.

51

NUT carcinoma
Definition
NUT carcinoma (NC) is an epithelial malignancy with a monotonous appearance genetically
defined by a rearrangement of the Nuclear Protein in Testis (NUTM1) gene.
ICD-O coding
8023/3 NUT carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20 & XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of nasal cavity & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS 2C22
& XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of acessory sinuses & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: Nuclear protein in testis carcinoma; NUT midline carcinoma; NUT-rearranged
carcinoma; t(15;19) carcinoma; carcinoma with t(15;19) translocation; aggressive t(15;19)-positive
carcinoma; midline lethal carcinoma; midline carcinoma of children and young adults
with NUT rearrangement.
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Head and neck primary sites constitute about 40% of NCs { 32328562 }. Most head and neck cases
affect the sinonasal tract (57%), nasopharynx (6%), or major salivary glands (4%)
{ 27509377 ; 29649019 ; 32077054 }. Rare cases involve the orbit, pharynx, or larynx
{ 20352379 ; 22896655 ; 24892275 ; 27509377 ; 27926786 ; 31900183 }.
Clinical features
Patients with NC present with symptoms caused by a rapidly growing mass. In the sinonasal tract,
this manifests as nasal obstruction, pain, headache, epistaxis, and nasal discharge, while ocular
symptoms (proptosis, diplopia) may be seen { 22534723 ; 30640313 }. NC commonly spreads by
local
invasion,
lymphatic,
and
hematogenous
metastasis
{ 22896655 ; 27509377 ; 31903701 ; 32328562 }. In 33%, NC presents with neck lymph node
metastases, and in 13% with distant metastases { 27509377 }. Bone metastases are observed
early, and multi-organ dissemination is seen later in the disease course
{ 11571733 ; 22033856 ; 29079177 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies reveal extensive local destructive invasion into neighboring structures
such as bone, sinus, orbit or brain { 22534723 ; 30640313 ; 31903701 }.
Epidemiology
NC is reported to account for up to 18% of poorly differentiated carcinomas of the upper
aerodigestive
tract,
and
about
1%
of
all
head
and
neck
carcinomas
{ 15483023 ; 18391746 ; 31903701 }. The sexes are equally affected over a very broad age range
but with a median of 24 years { 15483023 ; 18391746 ; 22017582 ; 32328562 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
NC is characterized by chromosomal translocation and fusion of the NUTM1 gene
to BRD4 (t(15;19)(q14;p13.1) (in 78%), genes coding for BRD4-interacting proteins, such
as BRD3 (9q34.2) (15%), NSD3 (8p11.23) (6%), and other (ZNF532, ZNF592) (2%), or
unidentified gene(s) (7%) { 12543779 ; 17934517 ; 24875858 ; 28484033 ; 30139738 }. (See
Figure #23791 #23791) NUT-fusion oncoproteins, most commonly BRD4-NUT, act as single
drivers of NC that function by blocking differentiation and maintaining proliferation
{ 12543779 ; 17934517 ; 24736545 ; 24875858 }.
Macroscopic appearance
NC presents as an exophytic, ulcerated, necrotic mass { 11571733 }.
Histopathology
NC typically shows sheets and nests of small to intermediate sized undifferentiated cells with a
monomorphic appearance. The cells have evenly-sized nuclei with irregular outlines, vesicular
chromatin, and prominent nucleoli. The cytoplasm varies from pale eosinophilic to basophilic. A
characteristic feature of the sheet-like growth pattern is even spacing of cells, often with separation

52

between cells, and lack of nuclear molding. There is brisk mitotic activity and necrosis is often
present. NCs demonstrate characteristic abrupt foci of keratinization in about a third of cases
{ 32328562 }. Definitive glandular differentiation is not seen, but mesenchymal differentiation can
occur, both in primary and metastatic sites { 19561446 ; 29079177 }. Histological features alone
are not specific for NC.
Immunohistochemistry: NC is positive for NUT protein (usually in a speckled nuclear pattern) in
87% of cases using a highly specific monoclonal NUT antibody { 19363441 }. With the exception
of weak staining in some germ cell tumours, staining with this antibody is highly specific for NC
{ 19363441 }. Cytokeratins are positive in the majority, although a subset are negative
{ 21210877 }. Most cases show nuclear staining for p63, however p40 staining is less consistent
and is not a reliable marker of NC or other more rare NUTM1-rearranged malignancies
{ 24114197 ; 32981612 }. Occasional NCs can stain for chromogranin, synaptophysin, or TTF-1,
but these have only been reported in thoracic, not head and neck primaries
{ 32149149 ; 33231320 }. NCs often stain for CD34, which may lead to a misdiagnosis of acute
leukemia { 15483023 }. Ki67 expression is high.
Differential NC must be considered in the differential diagnosis of poorly differentiated carcinomas
of the head and neck, including poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, sinonasal
undifferentiated carcinoma, and SWI/SNF complex-deficient sinonasal carcinoma
{ 18391746 ; 19561446 ; 25007146 ; 28291122 }, along with conventional/adamantinoma-like
Ewing
sarcoma,
neuroendocrine
carcinomas
and
olfactory
neuroblastoma
{ 15483023 ; 26034869 }.
Cytology
Smears usually appear hypercellular with large numbers of round to oval nuclei with intermediate
sized cells with high N:C ratio and a narrow rim of cytoplasm. The nuclei are monomorphic with
pale, fine evenly dispersed chromatin and single discrete small nucleoli. Occasional small syncytial
tissue fragments can be present with nuclear molding resembling neuroendocrine differentiation,
and mitoses are prominent. Considerable karyorrhectic debris and variable lymphocytes are seen
in
the
granular
background.
Squamous
differentiation
is usually
not
seen
{ 19795508 ; 21210877 ; 27338676 ; 27400194 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Demonstration of NUTM1 rearrangement is required, such as by positive immunostaining for
nuclear NUT expression in >50% of nuclei that achieves 100% specificity and 87% sensitivity
{ 19363441 }. In the remaining cases, cytogenetic analysis, in situ hybridization, reverse
transcription
PCR,
or
next
generation
sequencing
may
be
necessary
{ 29356724 ; 30139738 ; 30723300 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: demonstration of NUTM1 rearrangement (immunohistochemistry or molecular
technique) in a monotonous, undifferentiated epithelioid malignancy
Desirable (in selected cases): demonstration of the NC-related NUTM1-fusion partner gene to
distinguish NUT carcinoma from other rare NUTM1-rearranged neoplasms of either cutaneous or
bone/soft tissue origin
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification
Prognosis and prediction
NC is an extremely aggressive cancer, having a median survival of 6.5 months across all molecular
subsets and anatomic sites { 32328562 }. Head and neck patients with non-BRD4::NUTM1 fusions
(BRD3- or NSD3-NUTM1, median overall survival, 36.5 months) have significantly better survival
than those with BRD4::NUTM1 fusions (median overall survival, 10 months), independent of
metastatic disease extent at presentation. Overall survival is significantly lower in the presence of
metastasis but not significantly correlated with age or sex { 32328562 }.

53

#23628: Axial non-contrast CT shows a destructive soft tissue mass containing coarse foci of
calcification centred on the right nasal cavity/ ethmoid air cells

#23793: Papanicolaou stain of a fine-needle aspiration of a head and neck primary NUT
carcinoma reveals some spindling of medium-sized monomorphic cells with delicate cytoplasm
and distinct nucleoli.

54

#23849: Sheet-like growth pattern of round, medium-sized, monomorphic cells with distinct
nucleoli, vescicular open chromatin. The glycogenated, clear cytoplasm gives the cells a fried-egg
like appearance. Single cell necrosis and frequent mitoses are seen.

#23850: Abrupt, keratinized squamous differentiation is a characteristic feature, seen in 33% of
cases.

#23855: Schematic of predicted NUT fusions and respective wild type proteins. Arrows indicate
breakpoints. PWWP, Pro-Trp-Trp-Pro domain; PHD, plant homeodomain; SET, Su(var)3-9,

#23860: Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) demonstrates BRD4 rearrangement by splittingapart of red and green DNA probes flanking the BRD4 genomic locus in a tumour corresponding
with <<#23858>>. Demonstration by FISH of BRD4 and NUTM1 split-aparts indicates
a BRD4::NUT fusion.

#28275: Diffuse nuclear staining with the NUT monoclonal antibody (clone C52B1). The speckled
pattern of staining is characteristic.

#25589

NUT carcinoma

The monotonous nature of NUT carcinoma can be seen in this WSI, showing areas of
abrupt keratinisation (keratin pearl formation), along with areas of inflammation.

SWI/SNF complex-deficient sinonasal carcinoma (SMARCB1 & SMARCA4)
Definition
SWI/SNF-deficient sinonasal carcinomas are poorly to undifferentiated epithelial malignancies
defined by loss of one SWI/SNF complex subunit (either SMARCB1 or SMARCA4) without
histologic features allowing classification into another specific entity.
ICD-O coding
8020/3 SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20 & XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of nasal cavity & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS 2C22
& XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of accessory sinuses & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: SMARCB1-deficient undifferentiated sinonasal carcinoma; SMARCA4-deficient
undifferentiated sinonasal carcinoma.
Subtype(s)
SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal carcinoma; SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal adenocarcinoma;
SMARCA4-deficient sinonasal carcinoma.
Localization
The paranasal sinuses, especially ethmoid, are most often involved by SMARCB1-deficient
carcinomas, while SMARCB1-deficient adenocarcinoma and SMARCA4-deficient carcinoma
predominantly involve the nasal cavity and less frequently contiguous or multiple sites
{ 28291122 ; 31468350 ; 31934917 }.
Clinical features
Most patients present with locally advanced disease (T4), including involvement of orbit and skull
base. Symptoms are non-specific (nasal obstruction, sinusitis, epistaxis, and headache) and are
related to obstruction of the sinonasal cavities with frequent orbital complications
{ 28291122 ; 30120966 ; 31468350 ; 31934917 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies document extensive invasion, often into nearby structures { 28291122 }.
Epidemiology
SWI/SNF complex-deficient carcinomas comprise 1-3% of sinonasal carcinomas and 3-20% of
tumours
diagnosed as
sinonasal
undifferentiated
carcinoma
(SNUC)
{ 24832165 ; 25007146 }. SMARCB1-deficient
carcinoma
is
rare
{ 24832165 ; 25007146 ; 28291122 }, with even fewer cases of SMARCB1-deficient
adenocarcinoma and SMARCA4-deficient carcinoma { 29166820 ; 31468350 ; 31934917 }.
SMARCB1-deficient carcinomas affect patients ranging from 11-89 years with a peak in the 6th
decade. SMARCA4-deficient carcinoma occurs at a lower median age of 44 years (range, 2067). SWI/SNF
complex-deficient
carcinomas
affect
predominantly
males
{ 28291122 ; 31934917 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Inactivation of the respective SWI/SNF complex gene represents the central driver event and likely
the sole genetic alteration in these tumours { 28291122 ; 31468350 ; 31934917 ; 32818509 }. For
SMARCB1-deficient carcinomas, biallelic (homozygous) deletions of the SMARCB1 gene are
detected by FISH in two-thirds of cases { 28291122 }. Inactivating SMARCB1 gene mutations have
been detected by sequencing, but the sensitivity of NGS panels to detect the deletions is unclear.
For SMARCA4-deficient carcinomas, biallelic inactivation of SMARCA4 is the consequence of lossof-function (mainly truncating) mutations { 28084339 }. Loss of additional SWI/SNF subunits
(mainly SMARCA2) is seen in a few cases { 28291122 ; 31934917 }. These tumours
lack IDH mutations
characteristic
of
many
sinonasal
undifferentiated
carcinomas
{ 28493366 ; 28084339 ; 31186531 ; 31934917 }.
Macroscopic appearance
SWI/SNF complex-deficient carcinomas present as diffusely infiltrating, soft, fragile and necrotic
masses with variable polypoid component associated with extensive ulceration and necrosis.
Histopathology

58

SMARCB1-deficient carcinomas display monomorphic predominantly basaloid (60%) or
plasmacytoid/rhabdoid (33%) cell morphology. In basaloid cases, rhabdoid cells may be present
but are usually focal. Cell spindling is rare. Unequivocal squamous cells, prominent glandular
features, and surface epithelial dysplasia are absent. Secondary diffuse or pagetoid surface spread
may mimic dysplasia and carcinoma in-situ.
SMARCB1-deficient adenocarcinoma displays predominantly an oncocytoid/plasmacytoid cell
pattern with prominent gland formation (well-formed tubules, cribriforming, intracellular and/or
intraluminal mucin). Different patterns of yolk sac tumours are seen focally in 25% of cases and
may rarely be the predominant pattern. The tumour cells are uniformly high-grade with severe
nuclear pleomorphism, brisk mitotic activity, and foci of tumour necrosis { 31468350 }.
SMARCA4-deficient carcinomas are histologically undifferentiated, composed of large epithelioid
anaplastic cells in nests and trabeculae reminiscent of sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma.
Rhabdoid and basaloid cells are encountered less frequently. They lack squamous and glandular
differentiation histologically. Abortive rosettes may be seen.
Immunohistochemistry:
SMARCB1-deficient carcinomas: Tumour cells are positive with pancytokeratins (97%), and
variably with CK5 (64%), p63, or p40 (55%), and CK7 (48%). Focal and weak neuroendocrine
marker expression may occur (8-18%) { 28291122 ; 30120966 }. Complete SMARCB1 (INI1) loss
is required; rarely co-loss of SMARCA2 is seen, but not SMARCA4 { 25007146 ; 28291122 }.
SMARCB1-deficient adenocarcinoma: By immunohistochemistry, in addition to loss of SMARCB1,
CK7 expression (83%), and variable p40 (33%), CK20 (25%) and CDX2 (27%) are seen
{ 31468350 }. Variable reactivity with yolk sac tumour markers is observed irrespective of presence
or absence of overtly yolk sac tumour-like foci { 31468350 }.
SMARCA4-deficient carcinomas: These tumours lack squamous and glandular differentiation by
IHC. Tumour cells are reactive with pancytokeratin and rarely CK7, but negative with CK5, p40,
and p16. Focal reactivity with synaptophysin (90%), chromogranin-A (40%) and CD56 (60%) is
observed. Loss of SMARCA4 (BRG1) is definitional, rarely accompanied by co-loss of SMARCA2,
but SMARCB1 is retained { 31934917 ; 33663878 }.
No viral association (human papillomavirus and Epstein-Barr virus) has been reported. NUT
immunohistochemistry is consistently negative.
Differential While some sinonasal teratocarcinosarcomas are SMARCA4-deficient, they are
distinguished from SMARCA4-deficient carcinoma by the presence of sarcomatoid and teratoid
tumour elements.
Cytology
Cellular smears show loosely cohesive sheets of relatively monomorphic polygonal or plasmacytoid
epithelioid cells with large round to oval nuclei, small single nucleoli, occasional nuclear
pseudoinclusions and eccentric granular cytoplasm. Occasional larger cells with large nuclei are
present. Mitotic figures and background necrosis are seen, but there is no stromal matrix,
squamous or glandular differentiation { 27177850 ; 29797680 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: sinonasal tract location; undifferentiated carcinoma with SMARCB1(INI1) or SMARCA4
(BRG1) loss but no features of other entities; unequivocal gland-formation (and/ or yolk sac tumourlike features are) required for the adenocarcinoma subtype
Desirable: presence of rhabdoid cells; yolk sac-like features for the adenocarcinoma variant
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction

59

SWI/SNF complex-deficient sinonasal carcinomas are highly aggressive with more than half of
patients dying within 2 years. Mortality seems higher for SMARCA4-deficient cases compared to
others { 28291122 ; 31934917 }.

High-grade tumour cells with a rhabdoid morphology.

#25681

Anastomosing islands of tumour cells with vesicular nuclei, conspicuous nucleoli and
moderate amounts of cytoplasm.

stromal cells, endothelial cells and lymphocytes retain nuclear expression

carcinoma

#27416

Focal synaptophysin reactivity is common in SMARCA4-deficient sinonasal carcinoma.

#27420

#27422

#27580

#24603
SWI/SNF complex-deficient sinonasal carcinoma. SMARCA4-deficient sinonasal carcinoma
Undifferentiated tumour, composed of large epithelioid anaplastic cells in nests and
