#27244
SWI/SNF complex-deficient sinonasal carcinoma, SMARCB1-deficient
This example of SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal carcinoma is composed almost entirely of
monotonous cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm resulting in a plasmacytoid or
rhabdoid-like appearance

64

Sinonasal lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Definition
Lymphoepithelial carcinoma (LEC) is an undifferentiated-appearing carcinoma arising within the
sinonasal tract showing an associated prominent, non-neoplastic lymphoplasmacytic cell infiltrate,
strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus.
ICD-O coding
8082/3 Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20.4 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of nasal cavity & Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
2C22.1 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of acessory sinuses & Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: lymphoepithelioma-like carcinoma; primary sinonasal nasopharyngeal-type
undifferentiated carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
LEC affects the nasal cavity and less commonly the paranasal sinuses
{ 11780191 ; 21698444 ; 29995775 }. The sinonasal tract location defines LEC as a distinct entity
from nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) { 11859210 }.
Clinical features
Patients usually have nasal obstruction, bloody nasal discharge, facial swelling, and/or pain
{ 11859210 ; 19135324 ; 29995775 }. Advanced tumour with skull base invasion may cause cranial
nerve palsy { 11859210 ; 25804344 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies document tumour site and extent { 25525589 ; 29995775 ; 32998538 }.
Epidemiology
Tumours are more common in males (male to female ratio of 2-3:1) in their fifth to seventh decades
(median 58 years) { 11780191 ; 11859210 ; 25804344 }, with increased incidence in ethnic patients
from
regions
where
NPC
has
a
high
prevalence,
i.e.
Southeast
Asia
{ 7661287 ; 11780191 ; 11859210 }.
Etiology
Sinonasal LEC is strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection, especially in endemic
areas { 7661287 ; 11780191 ; 11859210 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Sinonasal LEC presents with an irregular or polypoid mass which may have erosion and
hemorrhagic surfaces { 21698444 ; 22078847 }.
Histopathology
Tumours are composed of syncytium of large cells arranged in lobules, nests, trabeculae or cords,
and associated with a rich lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate which can be florid, obscuring the tumour
cells. The malignant cells typically have large round nuclei, vesicular chromatin, prominent nucleoli,
and ill-defined cell borders (a syncytial pattern); occasional tumour cell spindling may be
encountered. Necrosis or keratinization is typically absent { 11859210 ; 21698444 ; 25804344 }. In
some cases, thick fibrous septa separating tumour islands may be observed. Extracellular amyloid
deposits may be present.
Immunohistochemistry: Tumour cells are positive for cytokeratins and usually positive for
squamous markers such as p40, p63, and CK5/6, while negative for neuroendocrine, lymphoid,
and melanocytic markers. p16 is usually negative or patchy and weak { 25804344 }. Sinonasal
LECs are typically reactive for EBV-encoded small RNA (EBER) by in situ hybridization
{ 11780191 ; 11859210 ; 25804344 }
but
rare
cases
may
be
EBER-negative
{ 19135324 ; 21698444 }.

65

Differential
Tumours must be separated from sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma,
nasopharyngeal carcinoma, oropharyngeal carcinoma, SMARCB1-deficient carcinoma, NUT
carcinoma, neuroendocrine carcinoma, melanoma, lymphoma, and undifferentiated pleomorphic
sarcoma, among others.
Cytology
Smears show irregular tissue fragments of epithelial cells with spindle or epidermoid cytoplasm that
can be poorly defined, and large highly pleomorphic nuclei, in an often prominent and obscuring
lymphoplasmacytic background. Lymphocytes can infiltrate the epithelium. The epithelial
component resembles non-keratinizing nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Most cases will demonstrate EBV by in situ hybridization.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: syncytial clusters of crowded, large, undifferentiated cells in an intimate relationship with
the
lymphoplasmacytic
infiltrate;
cytokeratin
immunoreactivity;
exclusion
of
nasopharyngeal/oropharyngeal primary
Desirable: EBER in situ hybridization (especially in endemic cases)
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
Sinonasal LEC appears to metastasize to cervical lymph nodes (15-25%) less frequently than NPC
{ 19135324 }, while distant metastasis are exceptional { 11859210 ; 29995775 }. Sinonasal LEC
prognosis is favorable even with cervical lymph node metastasis; but survival data is limited by
disease rarity { 21698444 ; 25804344 }.

#27230
Sinonasal lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Mass composed of solid nests and trabeculae of tumour cells accompanied by a marked
inflammatory infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells.

66

#27231
Sinonasal lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Thick fibrous bands separating tumour islands can be observed.

#27232
Sinonasal lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Tumour cells show large nuclei, vesicular chromatin, prominent nucleoli, indistinct cell
borders, and an associated inflammatory infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells.

67

#27233
Sinonasal lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Most cases are positive for EBV-encoded small RNA (EBER) by in situ hybridization.

#25554
Sinonasal lymphoepithelial carcinoma
There is a polypoid tumour composed of irregular nests of syncytial epithelial cells with
poorly to undifferentiated appearance and associated with a lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate.
The tumour cells have fine chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and no keratinization.

68

Sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma
Definition
Sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma (SNUC) is a malignant epithelial tumour without any
identifiable line of differentiation (including squamous, glandular, and neuroendocrine) and is a
diagnosis of exclusion.
ICD-O coding
8020/3 Sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20 & XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of nasal cavity & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS 2C22
& XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of acessory sinuses & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
SNUCs most often present as very large masses involving multiple sinonasal tract sites, with the
exact site of origin difficult to determine. { 23663264 }. Up to 60% show invasion into adjacent
structures, including the orbital apex, skull base, and brain { 22460731 }. Despite large primary
tumour size, nodal metastases are uncommon, occurring in 10-30% of cases
{ 19283847 ; 22460731 ; 24421248 ; 30733906 }.
Clinical features
Common presenting symptoms are nasal obstruction, sinusitis, epistaxis, and/or headache
{ 23663264 ; 30733906 }. With orbital involvement, patients may experience diplopia or other visual
symptoms, proptosis, and periorbital swelling { 23663264 }.
Imaging: Destructive tumours with poorly defined margins are seen radiographically { 9015077 }.
Epidemiology
SNUC is rare, accounting for about 3-5% of all sinonasal carcinomas { 24935016 }. The median
patient age is between 50-60 years, with a wide age range affected { 22460731 ; 25844294 }.
Tumours arise more frequently in males (60-70%) { 22460731 ; 25844294 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
IDH2 hotspot
mutations
are
identified
in
a
significant
subset
(33-85%)
{ 28084339 ; 28493366 ; 29683816 ; 31876581 }. IDH2 p.R172S is most common; others include
p.R172M, p.R172T, p.R172G, or p.R172K. Rarely, IDH1 mutations are reported
{ 29683816 }. IDH-mutant SNUC demonstrates a hypermethylator phenotype, and DNA
methylation-based profiling studies show that IDH-mutant sinonasal malignancies constitute a
distinct group from IDH-wildtype tumour types { 31186531 }. Given the varied sensitivity and
specificity for mutant IDH1/2 immunohistochemistry, sequencing-based methods are most reliable
for identifying IDH-mutant SNUC.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are usually large (>40 mm) at presentation, and show a fungating endoscopic
appearance.
Histopathology
SNUC is composed of sheets, lobules, nests, and trabeculae of malignant cells with enlarged round
nuclei, varying amounts of cytoplasm, and well-defined cell borders. Tumour cell nuclei are
hyperchromatic or vesicular, with open to vesicular chromatin and prominent nucleoli. Some
examples may appear basaloid with tumour cells having higher nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios.
Despite its high-grade morphology, SNUC has a characteristically uniform appearance with
relatively consistent nuclear size and shape. By definition, there is no squamous or glandular
differentiation, and surface squamous dysplasia is absent.
Immunohistochemistry: By immunohistochemistry, SNUC is positive for cytokeratins (pancytokeratin, AE1/AE3, CAM5.2, OSCAR and CK18) and ocassionally for CK7. Notably, CK5/6 and

69

p40 are negative, while patchy non-specific p63 reactivity may be seen { 25017972 }. While SNUC
can show very focal or patchy synaptophysin and chromogranin reactivity
{ 11176064 ; 19283847 }, but the histologic features of neuroendocrine carcinoma are absent.
Differentiation-specific markers, including CD45, S100 protein, SOX10, and desmin are
negative. SNUC may show positivity for p16, however this does not reflect transcriptionally active
high-risk HPV infection { 21805120 ; 24421248 }. Immunohistochemistry for mutation-specific
IDH1/2 (both multi-specific and monoclonal mutant IDH antibodies) identifies a proportion of SNUC
with IDH mutations including most with R172S, though it lacks sensitivity for the full range
of IDH mutations { 29683816 ; 30206411 ; 33017591 }. Presently, no morphologic or
immunohistochemical differences between SNUCs that are IDH-mutant or wild-type are recognized
{ 28493366 ; 29683816 ; 31876581 }.
Differential The differential diagnosis for SNUC is broad, and as a diagnosis of exclusion, other
entities must be excluded with a thorough histologic and immunohistochemical evaluation, directed
to exclude squamous, glandular, neuroendocrine, salivary, olfactory, neuroendocrine, melanocytic,
and hematolymphoid differentiation.
Cytology
Smears are hypercellular, showing predominantly single dispersed cells and scattered cohesive
tissue fragments with stripped, naked nuclei and necrotic debris in the background. Tumour cells
are intermediate in size with high N:C ratio, a small amount of ill defined cytoplasm and irregular
large pleomorphic nuclei which may appear molded and crushed and have easily identified nucleoli.
There are frequent intracytoplasmic vacuoles including signet-ring like cells but mucin is absent.
Occasional mitotic figures and abundant karyorrhectic debris are seen { 18285258 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Although IDH mutations define a subset of SNUC, identification is not required for diagnosis.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: high grade tumour with cytokeratin-positive tumour cells; no histologic and
immunohistochemical evidence of any specific line of differentiation; all histologic mimics (see
above) excluded
Desirable: verification of IDH mutational status (in selected cases)
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
SNUC is a highly aggressive malignancy with a poor prognosis { 22460731 }. SEER data analysis
revealed a median overall survival of 22.1 months and 3-, 5-, and 10-year survival rates of 44.3%,
34.9%, and 31.3%, respectively { 22460731 ; 25844294 }. IDH2 mutations have been associated
with better disease-specific survival { 31876581 }.

Lobules, trabeculae, and nests of
malignant epithelial cells.

Invasion into bone by tumour cells and abundant necrosis.

Undifferentiated tumour cell population showing high nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio, open or

#25496

Tumour showing more basaloid morphology.

There are nests and islands of neoplastic cells set within a vascularized and degenerated

was confirmatory

74

Teratocarcinosarcoma
Definition
Teratocarcinosarcoma (TCS) is a malignant sinonasal tract neoplasm with mixed epithelial,
mesenchymal, and primitive neuroepithelial elements.
ICD-O coding
9081/3 Teratocarcinosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20.Y & XH2RK1 Other specified malignant neoplasm of nasal cavity & Carcinosarcoma,
Embryonal
Related terminology
Not recommended: malignant teratoma; teratocarcinoma; teratoid carcinosarcoma; blastoma;
mixed olfactory neuroblastoma-craniopharyngioma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most cases of TCS are centered in the superior aspect of the nasal cavity, with frequent extension
into the ethmoid, maxillary, or sphenoid sinuses { 18559724 ; 6322958 }.
Clinical features
Presenting symptoms result from mass effect, including nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and
headache, and usually are of short duration { 18559724 }. Cervical lymph node metastases are
rarely seen { 6322958 }, but distant metastases may occur { 20015764 ; 23462185 }.
Epidemiology
Teratocarcinosarcoma tends to affect adults with a median age of 50 years; approximately 80% of
cases occur in males { 18559724 ; 32954838 }.
Etiology
No risk factors or tumour predisposition syndrome associations are known.
Pathogenesis
Recurrent molecular driver alterations have been documented, particularly biallelic inactivation
of SMARCA4 and activating CTNNB1 mutation { 28725522 ; 32520761 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours generally have a variable grey-tan fleshy to reddish-brown hemorrhagic appearance with
areas of necrosis.
Histopathology
TCS is composed of mixed epithelial, mesenchymal, and neuroepithelial elements. The epithelial
components can include both squamous epithelium, which may be keratinizing or non-keratinizing,
and glandular structures, which can be simple or stratified and can include mucinous or ciliated
cells. Frequently the squamous and glandular elements have a clear-cell appearance, reminiscent
of fetal tissues. The mesenchymal components are most often composed of non-descript
hypercellular fascicles of spindle cells, but areas of overt smooth muscle, rhabdomyoblastic,
adipocytic, cartilaginous, or osteoblastic differentiation can be seen. Both the epithelial and
mesenchymal components can show variable cytologic atypia, ranging from relatively bland to
overtly malignant appearances. The neuroepithelial component consists of sheets of primitive
epithelioid cells with large hyperchromatic nuclei arranged in nests and sheets with patchy
neurofibrillary background and rosette formation { 18559724 ; 23462185 ; 32520761 }. In some
instances, the primitive neuroepithelial component may undergo neuronal maturation postchemotherapy { 20596986 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The immunoprofile of TCS corresponds to its constituent elements, with
consistent strong positivity for cytokeratin in epithelial elements and weak positivity in
neuroepithelial elements, reactivity for desmin in rhabdomyosarcomatous elements (which may
only be identified immunohistochemically), staining for CK5/6, p63, and p40 in squamous
components, and expression of neuroendocrine markers synaptophysin, chromogranin-A, and
INSM1 in neuroepithelial elements { 23462185 ; 29438167 }. Markers of germ cell derivation,
including PLAP, SALL4, alpha-fetoprotein, and hCG are negative { 6322958 ; 9670829 }.
75

Approximately 80% of cases show some degree of SMARCA4 (BRG1) loss, with complete loss in
up to 70% of cases { 32520761 }. A subset of cases displays aberrant nuclear ß-catenin localization
{ 28725522 }.
Differential The admixture of various tissue elements in TCS is unique for the diagnosis, but can
show overlap with a wide range of other sinonasal tumours, including olfactory neuroblastoma,
squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, neuroendocrine carcinomas, and various sarcomas
when not all components are sampled. Immunohistochemistry for SMARCA4 or ß-catenin can
support the diagnosis in such cases. SMARCA4-deficient sinonasal carcinoma may overlap with
TCS in limited samples, but frequently shows a large cell epithelioid or rhabdoid cell pattern that is
absent in TCS { 31934917 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: sinonasal tract location; admixture of epithelial, mesenchymal, and primitive
neuroepithelial elements
Desirable: fetal-like clear cell appearance in epithelial components; SMARCA4 loss and/or nuclear
ß-catenin expression (in selected cases)
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification
Prognosis and prediction
TCS is an aggressive tumour that shows frequent local recurrences within the first 2 years
{ 20156370 }. However, more recent 5-year survival is >50% { 32954838 }.

#26136

Teratocarcinosarcoma

Intermixed squamous epithelium with a fetal-like clear cell appearance and spindle cells
embedded in chondroid and osseous matrix.

76

#26137

Primitive neuroepithelium with abundant rosette formation surrounded by hypercellular
fascicles of spindle cells.

#26143
Teratocarcinosarcoma

Primitive neuroepithelium with transition to gland-like structures.

77

#26146
Teratocarcinosarcoma
Loss of SMARCA4 in tumour elements including glands and neuroepithelium with
retained expression in blood vessels.

#26239
Teratocarcinosarcoma

Mixed epithelial, mesenchymal, and primitive neuroepithelial elements.

and retromaxillary fat.

#25924

The tumour is composed of mixed epithelial, mesenchymal, and neuroepithelial elements.

80

HPV-related multiphenotypic sinonasal carcinoma
Definition
Human papillomavirus (HPV)-related multiphenotypic sinonasal carcinoma (HMSC) is an epithelial
neoplasm exhibiting features of both surface- and minor salivary gland-derived elements,
harbouring transcriptionally active HPV.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2C20 & XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of nasal cavity & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS 2C22
& XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of acessory sinuses & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: HPV-associated multiphenotypic sinonasal carcinoma
Not recommended: HPV-related carcinoma with adenoid cystic-like features
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The vast majority of HMSC affect the nasal cavity (89%) with predilection for the turbinate, with or
without concurrent paranasal sinus involvement. Rare cases affect the sinuses exclusively
{ 23598962 ; 28035703 ; 28664668 ; 28877065 ; 30527234 }.
Clinical features
Most patients present with obstruction and epistaxis. Tumours are nearly always localized to the
sinonasal tract at presentation { 23598962 ; 28035703 ; 28664668 ; 28877065 ; 30527234 }.
Epidemiology
HMSC typically affects adults (mean age, 54 years), with a slight female predominance
{ 23598962 ; 28035703 ; 28664668 ; 28877065 ; 30527234 }.
Etiology
By definition, HMSC harbours high-risk HPV, most commonly type 33 (about 80%), and
occasionally types 35, 16, 52, 56, or 82 { 30259271 ; 30527234 ; 31571045 ; 32159863 }. As
HPV16 is uncommon, HPV assays must include other types, especially type
33. MYB rearrangements are not identified by FISH { 28877065 }. Origin from a terminal excretory
duct at its transition with surface epithelium may explain mixed lines of phenotypic differentiation
{ 30527234 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
HMSC grossly appears as a tan-white, often polypoid mass.
Histopathology
"Multiphenotypic” refers to multiple lines of cellular differentiation encountered in any single HMSC.
Tumours typically grow in solid sheets, lobules, and cribriform nests as basaloid cells, reminiscent
of solid adenoid cystic carcinoma. The salivary gland-type features are supported by the presence
of both ductal and myoepithelial components. The myoepithelial cells are typically basaloid with
varying degrees of spindling, plasmacytoid, or clear cell change within a myxohyaline stroma, while
the scattered ducts, variable in number and sometimes subtle, are more eosinophilic. Occasionally,
these dual components are arranged as an inner luminal ductal layer surrounded by an outer
peripheral layer of cleared myoepithelial cells, resembling epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma.
Squamous differentiation often takes the form of high-grade squamous dysplasia of the surface
epithelium. Less frequently, the invasive component can demonstrate areas of overt squamous
differentiation with keratin production. Bizarre pleomorphism, sarcomatoid transformation, and
heterologous cartilaginous differentiation is rare. HMSC typically exhibits high-grade cellular
features in addition to a high mitotic index and tumour necrosis. Bone invasion is common, but
perineural or lymphovascular invasion are not.
Immunohistochemistry: HMSC usually exhibits both ductal and myoepithelial differentiation which
can be demonstrated by immunostaining for p40, p63, SMA, and calponin (myoepithelial), CD117
(ducts), and S100 protein and SOX10 (both cell types). MYB immunohistochemistry and MYB RNA

81

in situ hybridization are also usually positive, despite the lack of MYB rearrangements
{ 30027386 ; 33165092 }. HMSC is positive for p16 and high-risk HPV by direct HPV assays such
as RNA in situ hybridization. A negative p16 helps exclude the tumour, but a positive p16
immunohistochemistry is a poor HPV surrogate in this tumour { 32671903 }.
Differential Unlike true adenoid cystic carcinomas, HMSC is restricted to the sinonasal tract in all
reported cases, is often associated with surface squamous dysplasia, and harbors HPV, requiring
direct HPV-specific testing. Furthermore, HMSC does not harbour the MYB, MYBL1,
and/or NFIB fusions that characterize most adenoid cystic carcinomas. In contrast to squamous
cell carcinoma (SCC), basaloid SCC, and adenosquamous carcinoma, HMSC shows myoepithelial
differentiation by histology and immunohistochemistry, recognizing SOX10 may be coexpressed in
basaloid SCC { 30498968 }.
Cytology
Not yet reported in cytopathology literature
Diagnostic molecular pathology
High-risk HPV must be demonstrated by in situ hybridization or PCR-based techniques, specifically
to include type 33.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: mixed ductal and myoepithelial carcinoma composed of basaloid-type cells; high-risk
HPV by HPV-specific assay (not p16 alone); myoepithelial differentiation by immunohistochemistry
Desirable: sinonasal tract location; ductal differentiation; overlying squamous dysplasia
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification
Prognosis and prediction
HMSC has paradoxically indolent behavior despite a typically high-grade histologic appearance.
Local recurrences are common (~ one-third), including some late recurrences, while distant
metastases are rare (5%) { 30027386 ; 30527234 ; 31737465 }. Regional spread and tumourrelated death are not yet reported.

#24871
HPV-related multiphenotypic sinonasal carcinoma

The tumour typically grows as solid and cribriform nests, with frequent myxohyaline
stroma and necrosis.

#24873

The predominant myoepithelial-type cells are basaloid with clear cell change, with
scattered eosinophilic ducts (center). The tumour is pleomorphic and mitotically active.

#24882: This example in the turbinate closely resembles adenoid cystic carcinoma in areas, with
its prominent cribriform growth pattern. In other areas there is more solid growth, and importantly,
there are foci of high-grade squamous dysplasia in the overlying surface epithelium. This tumour
was positive for high-risk HPV by RNA in situ hybridization (not shown).
Adenocarcinoma

Intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma
Definition
Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma (ITAC) is a gland-forming malignant epithelial neoplasm of the
sinonasal tract morphologically similar to primary intestinal adenocarcinoma.
ICD-O coding
8144/3 Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20.0 & XH0349 Adenocarcinoma of nasal cavity & Adenocarcinoma, intestinal type 2C22.0 &
XH0349 Adenocarcinoma of accessory sinuses & Adenocarcinoma, intestinal type
Related terminology
Not recommended: colloid-type adenocarcinoma; colonic-type adenocarcinoma; enteric-type
adenocarcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
ITAC is thought to originate from the olfactory cleft { 18085026 }, and most cases are localized in
the ethmoid sinus or nasal cavity. The maxillary sinus is affected in a minority of cases, usually in
patients without occupational exposure. ITAC invades the osseous structures and may spread to
other sinuses, orbit and skull base. Bilateral extension is seen in about 10% of
cases { 18085026 ; 18176354 ; 20665741 }.
Clinical features
Presentation includes unilateral nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and rhinorrhea. Patients with
advanced stage tumours present with pain, exophthalmos, diplopia, headache, and facial contour
changes { 18176354 ; 18560862 }. Anosmia is an initial symptom in a subset { 19541776 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies shows a poorly defined, enhancing sinonasal tract mass, and is used to
document destructive extension into orbit or skull base { 17893591 ; 2368169 }.
Epidemiology
Geographic regional incidence variation is noted { 24138871 }, with Europe reporting 0.24 in males
and 0.04 in females per 100,000 person-years { 29114957 }, whereas the United States of America
reports 4.4 per 100,000 person-years { 29205085 }. Males are affected much more frequently than
females. The peak age is in the fifth and sixth decades.
Etiology
An occupational etiology is identified with exposure to wood dusts, especially from hardwood
species such as oak and beech, and also leather dusts, with often prolonged exposure (>30 years)

86

and a long latency (>40 years) { 19885670 }. Other associated occupational exposures suggested
include textile manufacturing and exposure to cork, chrome, nickel, formaldehyde, solvents,
tannins,
and
pesticides
{ 19885670 ; 20870420 ; 23441309 ; 25885319 ; 26976946 ; 27040559 ; 27747921 ; 33072596 }.
Pathogenesis
ITAC is thought to develop through intestinal metaplasia of the sinonasal epithelium, observed
adjacent to invasive ITAC { 21559194 ; 25431194 }. Chronic inflammation, possibly induced by
occupational agents, is thought to play an important role in the pathogenesis { 18186150 }. The
molecular mechanisms of tumour development are beginning to be explained. The genetic
alterations in ITAC are partially similar to those observed in colorectal adenocarcinoma
{ 33051725 }. TP53 is
the
most
frequently
mutated
gene
(40-50%)
{ 12673679 ; 19950227 ; 22575263 }, while APC, KRAS, and BRAF mutations are present in a
minor subset { 16906516 ; 22459936 ; 23055340 ; 24338245 ; 33051725 }. High levels of EGFR
expression and gene amplification are reported in a subset { 19213595 ; 23055340 }, while
overexpression of MET { 24913906 } and nuclear ß-catenin expression are frequently present
{ 15452164 ; 22125792 }. NGS has identified recurrent somatic sequence variants
in PIK3CA, APC, ATM, KRAS, NF1, LRP1B, BRCA1, ERBB3, CTNNB1, NOTCH2 and CDKN2A {
33500480 }. These variants mainly affected PI3K, MAPK/ERK, WNT and DNA repair signaling
pathways { 33500480 }.
Macroscopic appearance
ITACs appear as large, polypoid, papillary or nodular lesions. They are friable, sometimes
hemorrhagic, or uncommonly gelatinous or mucoid.
Histopathology
ITACs show a morphologic spectrum similar to gastrointestinal primary adenocarcinomas. They
extensively infiltrate the submucosa and bone and may show perineural invasion. Stromal tissues
are loose and fibrovascular, often containing abundant chronic inflammation. They consist of
proliferations of dysplastic columnar cells with interspersed goblet cells, forming papillae and
glands. Paneth cells and endocrine cells are also present to varying proportions. Differentiation
varies from very well-differentiated to poorly-differentiated tumours classified as papillary, colonic,
and solid, or mixed. Well-differentiated ITACs predominately show a papillary or tubulopapillary
architecture and consist of elongated outgrowths lined by atypical intestinal-type cells. Sometimes
atypia may be difficult to appreciate, but nuclear changes that appear at least “adenomatous” are
the rule. Nuclei are cigar-shaped, hyperchromatic and enlarged. As ITACs become poorly
differentiated, tubular and papillary structures are replaced by nested, cribriform, and solid growths.
Mitotic figures are frequent, and central comedonecrosis is usually present. A minority of cases
(20-25%) show abundant mucus, resulting in two different growth patterns: alveolar spaces partially
lined by and containing glands or strips of attenuated epithelium rich in goblet cells, with the strips
suspended like ribbons within mucus lakes and sometimes form small cribriform structures; or, less
commonly, mucous lakes containing signet ring cells. Some tumours have a mixed growth pattern,
appearing both papillary/tubular and mucinous.
Histochemistry: Histochemical stains show intracytoplasmic, intraluminal and/or extracellular
mucicarmine and diastase-resistant, PAS-positive material.
Immunohistochemistry: Neoplastic cells express pancytokeratins, are variably CK7 and CEA
reactive and mostly CK20-positive. ITACs are also positive for CDX2, villin, MUC2 and SATB2
{ 15175880 ; 15333652 ; 27258560 }. In addition, there is variable expression of neuroendocrine
markers { 25294889 ; 20614280 }.
Differential
The differential diagnosis between ITAC and sinonasal non-intestinal type
adenocarcinomas is made on morphologic grounds, aided by immunohistochemistry. Metastatic
gastrointestinal tumours must be excluded by clinical and/or imaging findings. Mucoepidermoid and
adenosquamous carcinomas show squamous differentiation, lacking in ITAC. A mucocele lacks
pleomorphism, necrosis and brisk mitotic activity.
Cytology
Primary tumours are not usually aspirated, but smears of cervical lymph node or distant metastases
show large stripped nuclei, columnar and sometimes signet ring cells with prominent mucin
vacuoles seen singly or in small tissue fragments in a background of a variable amount of mucin

87

and granular necrotic debris { 21710644 }. The features are diagnostic of adenocarcinoma but
ancillary tests on cell blocks are required.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: atypical intestinal-type columnar cells forming papillae, tubules, or cribriform and solid
structures; exclude gastrointestinal primary metastasis
Desirable: abundant extracellular or intracellular mucus; immunolabelling for markers of intestinal
differentiation (in selected cases)
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification
Prognosis and prediction
Sinonasal ITAC is a high-grade malignancy, with frequent local recurrences (30-60%), associated
with a poor prognosis when there is skull base, orbit, and/or dural invasion
{ 18176354 , 27040559 ; 28067974 }. Additional negative prognostic factors are local recurrence,
distant metastases and positive surgical margins { 18176354 ; 20665741 ; 26040823 ; 28067974 }.
Lymph node and distant metastases are infrequently detected (1-5% and 5-10% of the patients,
respectively){ 18176354 ; 20665741 ; 25332183 ; 25753257 }. Stage is more significant than
tumour
grade
or
type
{ 19885670 ; 24596075 ; 25332183 ; 27040559 ; 27165676 ; 28963820 ; 29205085 }. Still, well
differentiated papillary ITAC tends to have an indolent behavior, while mucinous and poorly
differentiated tumours have poorer outcomes { 21668475 ; 26040823 }. Tumour budding seems to
be an adverse prognostic factor in ITAC { 31980958 }. By comparative genomic hybridization,
subgroups of ITAC with distinct genetic signatures and copy number alterations and clinical
outcomes independent from disease stage or histological subtype, have been identified
{ 28963820 }. The median time to recurrence is 25-30 months { 18176354 ; 20665741 }. The 5year survival rate is 50-84% and the 10-year survival rate is 30-50%
{ 18176354 ; 20665741 ; 25332183 ; 26040823 ; 27040559 ; 27165676 }.

#24498
Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma of the sinonasal tract
Well differentiated tumour showing a papillary architecture with fibrovascular cores lined
by dysplastic columnar epithelium.

88

#24499
Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma of the sinonasal tract
Moderatly differentiated tumour with tubular arrangement of neoplastic cells.

#24500
Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma of the sinonasal tract
This mucinous tumour consists of small groups of neoplastic cells interspersed within
abundant mucus.

89

#24501
Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma of the sinonasal tract
This mucinous tumour presents a signet-ring morphology.

#27248
Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma of the sinonasal tract
This tumour consists of a proliferation of dysplastic columnar cells, with interspersed
goblet cells. Numerous Paneth cells with granular eosinophilic cytoplasm are also visible
in this example.
90

Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma
Definition
Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma (SNAC) is a sinonasal tract, gland-forming
malignancy lacking intestinal-type features or characteristics of salivary gland-specific tumour
entities.
ICD-O coding
8140/3 Adenocarcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2C20.0 & XH74S1 Adenocarcinoma of nasal cavity & Adenocarcinoma, NOS
2C22.0 & XH74S1 Adenocarcinoma of paranasal sinuses & Adenocarcinoma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: terminal tubulus adenocarcinoma; tubulopapillary low-grade adenocarcinoma;
low-grade adenocarcinoma; seromucinous adenocarcinoma
Subtype(s)
Renal cell–like sinonasal adenocarcinoma
Localization
Most low grade (LG) SNACs (64%) arise in the nasal cavity (frequently the middle turbinate), and
20% arise in the ethmoid sinus { 7083137 ; 19011560 }. The remaining tumours affect the other
individual sinuses or expand to involve multiple subsites. Approximately half of all high grade
(HG) SNACs are locally advanced at presentation and involve multiple sinonasal tract sites
{ 7083137 ; 21677536 }.
Clinical features
Most patients with LG SNACs present with obstruction { 12808566 ; 12827515 }, and less
commonly epistaxis and pain. Patients with HG SNAC's present with obstruction, epistaxis, pain,
deformity, and proptosis { 7083137 }.
Imaging: On imaging, LG SNACs present as solid masses, filling the nasal cavity and/or paranasal
sinuses. HG SNAC's show a more destructive appearance, with osseous involvement and direct
extension into the orbit and skull base.
Epidemiology
LG SNACs are uncommon, without a sex predilection { 7083137 ; 12808566 ; 19011560 },
affecting a broad age range (9 to 89 years), with a 6th decade peak. HG SNACs are rare, affect
males more frequently, and occur over a wide age range with a similar peak
{ 7083137 ; 21677536 }.
Etiology
Unknown for LG SNACs. Rare HG SNACs have been associated with high-risk HPV or sinonasal
papillomas { 21677536 }. Exposure to carcinogens such as wood or other dusts and solvents may
be related to the development of some HG SNACs { 18176354 ; 27681565 }.
Pathogenesis
Some
LG
SNACs harbour fusions,
such
as ETV6NTRK3/RET { 28719468 ; 29683817 ; 30666415 ; 31698004 },
while
others
have CTNNB1 mutations { 30302299 }. Rare BRAF mutations have been reported { 24338245 },
whereas KRAS mutations are not seen. The molecular underpinnings of HG SNAC are still
unknown.
Macroscopic appearance
Non-intestinal SNACs may appear red and polypoid or raspberry-like and firm { 3977770 }.
Histopathology
Tumours are histologically separated by grade: low-grade (LG) and high-grade (HG).
LG SNACs have predominately papillary and/or tubular (glandular) features with complex growth,
including
back-to-back
(cribriform)
glands
with
little
intervening
stroma
{ 7083137 ; 19011560 ; 3977770 }. A single layer of uniform mucinous cuboidal to columnar
epithelial cells lines the structures. These cells have eosinophilic cytoplasm and uniform, basally
located nuclei. Mitotic figures are rare and necrosis is not seen. Invasive growth, including within
the submucosa as well as into bone, may be present. Calcospherites and squamous morules are

91

sometimes seen { 7083137 ; 30302299 }. Occasional tumours have more dilated glands
{ 3977770 ; 12808566 }.
HG SNACs show much more diversity in their histology { 7083137 ; 21677536 }. Many have a
predominately solid growth with occasional glandular structures and/or individual mucocytes. Some
have a nested growth and are destructively infiltrative into bone. Numerous mitotic figures are seen
with necrosis (individual-cell and confluent)
Subtype: Occasional SNACs are composed predominately of clear cells, reminiscent of renal cell
carcinoma { 20614326 ; 26299508 ; 28032289 ; 33152820 }. These tumours have been referred to
as sinonasal renal cell–like adenocarcinomas. The tumours are composed of monomorphous
cuboidal to columnar glycogen-rich clear cells that lack mucin production. The cellular cytoplasm
may be crystal clear or slightly eosinophilic. Intranuclear inclusions are common. Perineural
invasion, lymphovascular invasion, necrosis, and severe pleomorphism are absent, and the overall
histological impression is that of a low-grade neoplasm.
Histochemistry: In most SNACs, intraluminal mucin or material that gives a diastase-resistant
positive reaction with periodic acid–Schiff (PAS) can be identified. In HG SNACs, cells with
intracytoplasmic mucin or diastase-resistant PAS positivity may be present.
Immunohistochemistry: The tumours express cytokeratins (typically CK7 and infrequently focal
CK20) { 21677536 }. Squamous antigens such as p40 are typically not expressed or are expressed
only focally { 12827515 }. Markers of intestinal differentiation, such as CDX2, SATB2, and MUC2,
may be focally present, but are usually absent { 15252308 ; 21677536 ; 27258560 }. Tumour cells
may also express DOG1, SOX10, and S100 protein { 25690258 }. HG SNACs can focally express
neuroendocrine antigens { 21677536 }. Renal cell–like adenocarcinoma expresses CAIX and
CD10, but do not express PAX8 or renal cell carcinoma marker { 26299508 }. ß-catenin and
mismatch repair protein expression is wildtype in HG SNACs { 15492756 } although a subset of LG
SNACs (often tubular with squamous morules) will show nuclear localization of ßcatenin { 30302299 }. Overexpression of p53 may be seen { 12827515 }.
Cytology
Not yet reported in cytopathology literature. Features are expected to be similar to intestinal type
adenocarcinoma.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: gland-forming sinonasal tract malignancy with no intestinal phenotype or features of a
specific salivary gland neoplasm
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification
Prognosis and prediction
Approximately 25% of LG SNACs recur, while only 6% of patients die from their tumours, usually
due to lack of local control { 7083137 ; 19011560 ; 12808566 }. Patients with HG SNACs fare worse
{ 7083137 }, with most dead from the disease within 5 years. Occasional HG SNACs metastasize
locally and distally. The reported cases of renal cell–like adenocarcinoma have neither recurred
nor metastasized { 20614326 ; 26299508 ; 28032289 ; 33152820 }.

92

#25700
Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma, low grade
The tumour cells are uniform with regular rounded nuclei with finely granular chromatin
and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm.

#25910
Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma, high-grade
This tumour was positive for high-risk human papilloma virus.

93

#27250
Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma, high-grade
Many high-grade sinonasal adenocarcinomas have nests and ribbons of tumour cells
intermixed with smaller glands or rosettes.

#27251
Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma, low grade
The tumour consists of uniform small glands and tubular structures formed by a single
row of cuboidal or columnar cells and displays a papillary growth pattern. Most papillae
are simple and composed of a loose fibrovascular core and bland looking epithelium
arranged in one row or in a bilayered pattern. Some papillae are more complex with a
branching pattern.
94

#27252
Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma, low grade
The tumour is composed of closely packed tubules with occasional squamous morules.

#27254

Non-intestinal-type sinonasal adenocarcinoma, high grade
This tumour is composed of small cysts with papilla projecting into them.

95

#28074: The tumour consists of a tubulo-papillary proliferation of polygonal cells with optically
clear cytoplasm and small, uniform, round nuclei.

#28075: Occasional
cases exhibit abnormal nuclear localization with beta-catenin immunohistochemistry.

Mesenchymal tumours of the sinonasal tract

Sinonasal tract angiofibroma
Definition
Sinonasal tract angiofibroma is a locally aggressive, variably cellular fibrovascular neoplasm.

96

ICD-O coding
9160/0 Angiofibroma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XA43C9 & XA43C9 & XH1JJ2 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or
respiratory system & nasal cavity & acessory sinuses & Angiofibroma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: angiofibroma, juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Tumours arise from the posterolateral wall of the roof of the nasal cavity or lateral nasopharynx
{ 4355257 ; 22588045 ; 23483486 }.
Clinical features
The classic triad of nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and sinonasal / nasopharyngeal mass is seen in
many patients, often over a prolonged symptom duration { 23483486 ; 24381014 }. Biopsy is
contraindicated due to potentially profuse bleeding. With tumour progression, facial deformity,
deafness, diplopia, and proptosis may be seen { 4355257 ; 22588045 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies { 2538688 ; 6087710 ; 8630204 ; 16550949 ; 20566910 ; 24381014 },
reveal a highly vascular soft tissue mass, often with internal flow voids on T2W MRI, typically
involving and widening the pterygopalatine fossa with anterior bowing of the posterior wall of the
adjacent
maxillary
antrum
(Holman-Miller
sign,
considered
pathognomonic)
{ 4355257 ; 22588045 }. Bone destruction and intracranial extension may be seen
{ 4355257 ; 21572079 ; 23483486 ; 23553370 }. Pre-operative angiography and embolization are
often performed to reduce intraoperative haemorrhage { 21819885 }.
Epidemiology
Sinonasal angiofibroma is rare, with an incidence of 3.7 cases per million at risk population
{ 17364367 ; 22588045 ; 23483486 }. The tumour develops exclusively in adolescent and young
males { 4355257 ; 6312826 ; 23483486 ; 28508272 }, and if identified in females, they should be
evaluated for testicular feminization.
Etiology
Hormone dependency is correlated with tumour growth at puberty onset and strong androgen
receptor expression { 4355257 ; 6312826 ; 9831211 ; 28508272 }.
Pathogenesis
Somatic mutations in ß-catenin encoding gene (CTNNB1) are seen in 75% of tumours, even though
immunohistochemical nuclear localization of ß-catenin is seen in most tumours { 11238055 }.
These findings and the increased incidence in patients with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP)
support the importance of aberrant Wnt-signaling as a central event in these
tumour { 26572152 }. Gain of X and loss of Y chromosome have been documented
{ 12883689 ; 14647927 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Polypoid to lobulated tumours ranging up to 220 mm, with an average of 40 mm.
Histopathology
There are numerous blood vessels of various sizes, from slit-like capillaries to irregularly dilated
and branching vessels. The vascular walls may be thin to focally or continuously thickened and
muscular. No elastic tissue is identified in the vessels except in the feeding arteries. The stroma is
composed of bipolar or stellate fibroblastic cells with plump, vesicular, spindled nuclei and indistinct
nucleoli. Isolated multinucleated stellate stromal giant cells may be seen. Mitoses are
inconspicuous. The stromal cellularity varies from loose and edematous to densely collagenized.
Mast cells are common. Embolized tumours show areas of necrosis and intravascular foreign
material. Androgen receptor blocker-treated tumours are hypocellular with increased stromal
collagen { 1318484 }.
Immunohistochemistry: By immunohistochemistry, stromal cells show strong nuclear expression of
androgen receptor and ß-catenin, while vascular markers highlight the vessels and SMA stains the
smooth muscle vascular walls { 9831211 ; 11238055 ; 28508272 }. EBV and HHV8 are absent
{ 20614308 }.

97

Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: male patient; posterior nasal cavity/nasopharynx location; numerous vessel types and
sizes, some with muscular walls; stromal stellate fibroblasts with variably collagenized stroma
Desirable: in selected cases, immunoreactivity in stromal cell nuclei with ß-catenin and androgen
receptor
Staging
Radiographic and clinical staging systems are used to guide treatment and determine recurrence
risk { 6087710 ; 20566910 ; 2538688 ; 8630204 ; 16550949 ; 24381014 }.
Prognosis and prediction
The course is characterized by recurrence in 5-25% of cases, sometimes multiple
{ 23483486 ; 24381014 ; 23553370 }. Prognosis depends on the size and extent of the tumour and
the completeness of surgical resection { 20566910 ; 21572079 }. Rare examples of sarcomatous
transformation after radiation have been reported { 6312826 }. Spontaneous regression after
puberty may rarely occur { 6312826 }.

#22750

Sinonasal tract angiofibroma

Fluoroscopic angiography documents the vascular nature of the tumour, while also
allowing for presurgical embolization.

98

#22751
Sinonasal tract angiofibroma
A T1 weighted coronal MR shows a large, central destructive neoplasm. Even though a
benign tumour, it can reach a large size and be locally aggressive.

#22754
Sinonasal tract angiofibroma
The stroma may be quite cellular, with fibroblastic cells set within a collagenized stroma.
99

#22755
Sinonasal tract angiofibroma
The stromal cells show a strong and diffuse nuclear reaction with ß-catenin by
immunohistochemistry.

#22756
Sinonasal tract angiofibroma
The stromal spindled cells show a strong and diffuse nuclear reaction with androgen
receptor by immunohistochemistry.

100

#27214
Sinonasal tract angiofibroma
The surface epithelium is unremarkable, overlying a variably cellular vascular neoplasm.
Patulous vessels with and without smooth muscle walls are noted.

#27225
Sinonasal tract angiofibroma
Several vessel types are set within a fibrocollagenous stroma. There is embolic material,
a finding noted after embolisation.

#22387

Sinonasal angiofibroma
There is a mixed vascular proliferation set within a collagenized stroma. Note the embolic
foreign material from pre-surgical embolization.

Sinonasal glomangiopericytoma
Definition
Glomangiopericytoma is a sinonasal soft tissue tumour showing perivascular myoid differentiation,
usually associated with CTNNB1 mutations.
ICD-O coding
8824/0 Myopericytoma
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XA43C9 & XA3523 & XH2HE9 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or
respiratory system & nasal cavity & paranasal sinuses & myopericytoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: sinonasal-type hemangiopericytoma; sinonasal hemangiopericytoma;
intranasal myopericytoma.
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Unilateral nasal cavity involvement (turbinate and septum) is most common (<5% bilateral) with
frequent extension into paranasal sinuses (usually ethmoid and maxillary sinuses); rare isolated
paranasal sinus involvement { 970369 ; 1540341 ; 8774921 ; 12766577 ; 28497509 }.
Clinical features
Most patients present with nasal obstruction and/or epistaxis and other nonspecific findings
{ 970369 ; 1540341 ; 8774921 ; 12766577 }. There is rare association with severe oncogenic
osteomalacia { 8774921 ; 11195105 ; 22430770 ; 24917914 ; 28497509 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies show a destructive, polypoid mass often accompanied by bone erosion,
frequently with concurrent sinusitis { 12766577 ; 21552026 ; 21656202 ; 31806599 }.
Epidemiology
Glomangiopericytomas comprise about 1% of all sinonasal tract neoplasms { 12766577 }. Identified
from infancy to old age, there is a peak in the 6th-7th decades. There is a slight female predilection
{ 970369 ; 2389815 ; 8774921 ; 12766577 ; 28497509 }.

102

Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Key molecular drivers are recurrent missense mutations within CTNNB1 exon 3, consisting of
somatic single nucleotide substitutions clustering at positions 32 to 45 (most commonly codon 33)
of the glycogen serine kinase-3 ß-phosphorylation (GSK3ß) region. The affected region
corresponds to the recognition site of ß-catenin destruction complex, which prevents ß-catenin
phosphorylation and proteosomal degradation, leading to aberrant nuclear translocation and
accumulation of ß-catenin. Subsequent CCND1 upregulation and overexpression of cyclin D1
ultimately
leads
to
oncogenic
activation
{ 25431235 ; 25482924 ; 29736797 ; 30206803 ; 30739805 }. Tumours lack: NAB2::STAT6 fusion
of solitary fibrous tumour { 24030747 }, MIR143::NOTCH fusion of glomus tumour { 23999936 },
and GLI1 fusions
or
amplification
of
GLI1-altered
soft
tissue
tumours
{ 15111311 ; 15555571 ; 31934916 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Intact tumours are polypoid, non-translucent, soft, edematous and fleshy masses, beefy red to
gray-pink. Cut surfaces appear sponge-like to solid with edematous and/or hemorrhagic areas.
Tumours range up to 140 mm, but average 30 mm { 1540341 ; 12766577 ; 28497509 }.
Histopathology
Tumours are unencapsulated but well-delineated within the submucosa separated from a generally
intact surface epithelium by a tumour-free zone. The diffuse, patternless cellular proliferation
effaces the normal submucosal elements, although fascicular, storiform, whorled, reticular, and
meningothelial-like patterns may be identified. Tumour cells demonstrate a syncytial arrangement
of spindle-shaped to ovoid cells containing blunt-ended nuclei with coarse nuclear chromatin and
slightly eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm. There is a complex, ramifying vascular network of thinwalled capillary-sized to large patulous, stag-horn vessels, with characteristic peritheliomatous
hyalinization. Limited mitotic figures without atypical forms and mild nuclear pleomorphism, but no
tumour necrosis, is seen. Extravasated erythrocytes, mast cells, and eosinophils are seen in nearly
all tumours. Rarely, tumour giant cells, lipomatous change, extramedullary hematopoiesis, and
collision tumours (solitary fibrous tumour, respiratory epithelial adenomatoid hamartoma) are
reported { 12766577 ; 24807787 }. Concurrent inflammatory sinonasal polyps are frequently
present. Rare cases show profound nuclear pleomorphism, atypical and increased mitoses, and
tumour
necrosis,
which
are
features
associated
with
aggressive
behavior
{ 970369 ; 8774921 ; 11360312 ; 15529319 ; 28497509 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Immunohistochemical analyses demonstrate strong tumour cell
immunoreactivity with actins (SMA > MSA), nuclear β-catenin, LEF1, cyclin D1, and factor XIIIa.
No significant staining is observed for CD31, ERG, D2-40, factor VIIIRAg, pancytokeratin, STAT6,
desmin,
S100
protein,
SOX10,
CD117,
and
SATB2
{ 2389815 ; 8774921 ; 11360312 ; 12766577 ; 16362413 ; 24030747 ; 24807787 ; 24977739 ; 25
482924 ; 25873501 ; 28497509 ; 28614212 ; 29282671 ; 29736797 ; 30739805 ; 31399905 }.
CD34 is variably present, clone dependent (absent with clone My10) { 12766577 ; 31399905 }.
Differential Several spindle-cell tumours are considered in the differential diagnosis, but most
commonly distinguished from smooth muscle tumours, solitary fibrous tumour, meningioma,
schwannoma, biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma, desmoid-type fibromatosis, and synovial sarcoma
by histomorphology and a pertinent immunohistochemical panel.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Mutations in CTNNB1 gene may aid in diagnosis, although rarely necessary
{ 25431235 ; 25482924 ; 26645457 ; 27325236 }
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: ovoid to spindled syncytium of myoid-type cells within richly vascularized stroma
Desirable: peritheliomatous hyalinization; extravasated erythrocytes, mast cells and eosinophils; in
selected cases, actins, nuclear ß-catenin immunoreactivity, with absent STAT6, S100 protein,
SOX10, and cytokeratins
Staging

103

Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Considered a very indolent tumour especially in patients <60 years of age { 28497509 },
recurrences
are
seen
in
20%,
often
due
to
incomplete
excision
{ 970369 ; 1540341 ; 2389815 ; 8774921 ; 12766577 ; 22021153 ; 28497509 }.
Late
local
recurrence (>10 years) suggests prolonged clinical follow-up. Features suggestive of aggressive
behaviour include tumour size > 50 mm, destructive bone invasion, intracranial extension, profound
nuclear
pleomorphism,
>4
mitoses/2mm2,
and
tumour
necrosis
{ 970369 ; 8774921 ; 11360312 ; 12766577 ; 15529319 }.

The characteristic features of glomangiopericytoma with synctial cells arranged in focal

Biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma
Definition
Biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma (BSNS) is a bland spindle cell malignancy demonstrating a neural
and myogenic phenotype, exclusively involving the sinonasal tract and usually associated
with PAX3 rearrangement.
ICD-O coding
9045/3 Biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20.Y & XH4UM7 Malignant neoplasms of nasal cavity & sarcoma NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: low-grade sinonasal sarcoma with neural and myogenic features
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
BSNS primarily develops in the nasal cavity and ethmoid sinus but any sinonasal tract site may be
involved { 22301502 ; 30829729 }. BSNS often develops locoregional infiltration, including the
lamina papyracea, anterior skull base, cribriform plate, orbit, and brain { 30015731 ; 31966535 }.
Clinical features
BSNS may cause nasal obstruction, pain, epistaxis, rhinorrhea, or mucocele. Regional infiltration
may cause diplopia or cerebral haemorrhage { 30015731 ; 31966535 ; 32991500 }. Involved bones
show mixed lytic and sclerotic changes on imaging { 31637290 }.
Epidemiology
Patients are adults with a mean age of 53 years (range 24-85 years) { 22301502 ; 30829729 }.
There is a notable female predominance { 30109475 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
BSNS is associated with recurrent driver gene fusions, most frequently PAX3::MAML3 (60%)
{ 27454570 }.
Alternative
3’
partners
include NCOA1,
NCOA2, FOXO1, and WWTR1 { 26355893 ; 26371783 ; 27454570 ; 30829729 }.
The
fusion

111

deregulates
the
expression
of
factors
involved
in
neural
development,
in
particular NTRK3 { 24859338 }. The translocation may be unbalanced { 29863547 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Nonspecific tan-white, firm mass. Tumours are often large, with a median size of 40 mm
{ 22301502 ; 30829729 }.
Histopathology
BSNS is unencapsulated, with an infiltrative growth composed of spindled cells forming mediumto-long fascicles, often with a herringbone pattern. BSNS are mostly densely cellular with
overlapping nuclei, although they can show variations in cellular density. Tumour cells are uniform,
harbouring ovoid or wavy nuclei with fine chromatin dotted with small or inconspicuous nucleoli.
Cytoplasm tends to scant and palely eosinophilic. Rhabdomyoblastic differentiation is rare and
typically focal when present, and may be associated with specific fusion gene partners
{ 26371783 }. Foci of hyperplastic respiratory epithelium are commonly entrapped within the
tumour. Infiltration of bone is common. Mitotic activity is consistently low, mostly below 2/mm2.
Necrosis is not present. Hypocellular fibrous areas and thin-walled haemangiopericytoma-like
vessels are common { 29863547 }.
Immunohistochemistry: BSNS are consistently S100 protein positive, although staining extent may
be heterogeneous. SOX10 is always negative. SMA is consistently positive. Desmin is expressed
in half to two-thirds of cases { 27137987 }. MYOD1 and myogenin staining is inconsistent and
typically patchy or focal when positive. Most cases show nuclear PAX3 staining (using clone
274212) { 29863547 }. Pan-TRK staining is common, as well as focal nuclear expression of ßcatenin { 27137987 ; 29566950 }. Occasional reactivity with calponin, GFAP, cytokeratins, and
CD34 have been reported { 29863547 }.
Differential Entities in the differential diagnosis are summarized in (see #26185Table #26185), the
main mimics being synovial sarcoma, cellular schwannoma, and respiratory epithelial adenomatoid
hamartoma.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Fluorescence in situ hybridization with PAX3 break-apart probe is helpful to confirm the diagnosis
{ 27454570 }. Technical failures are mostly related to decalcification, with next-generation
sequencing having the advantage of detecting all possible fusion genes. A possibly related tumour
of the head and neck with a similar morphology shows a RREB1/MKL2 fusion
{ 29266774 ; 33715240 }. No fusion is detected in a minority of cases.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: monotonous spindle cell proliferation with fascicular architecture with variably high
cellular density; reactivity for S100 protein and myogenic markers (most commonly SMA)
Desirable: confirmation of PAX3 rearrangement in selected cases
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification, head
and neck soft tissue sarcomas
Prognosis and prediction
Local recurrences are seen in one-third to half of cases { 33813901 } and may occur years after
initial diagnosis { 30015731 }. No distant metastases have been reported.

112

119

Chordoma
Definition
Primary malignant bone tumour with neoplastic cells recapitulating notochordal differentiation.
ICD-O coding
9370/3 Conventional chordoma
9370/3 Poorly differentiated chordoma
9372/3 Dedifferentiated chordoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20.Y & XH9GH0 Malignant neoplasms of nasal cavity & Chordoma, NOS
2C20.Y & XH17D8 Malignant neoplasms of nasal cavity & Chondroid chordoma
2C20.Y & XH7303 Malignant neoplasms of nasal cavity & Dedifferentiated chordoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Conventional chordoma; poorly differentiated chordoma; dedifferentiated chordoma; chondroid
chordoma
Localization
Approximately 30% present in the skull base and upper mobile spine { 11227920 ; 21184634 }.
Clinical features
Pain, a mass lesion and neurological deficits are noted.
Imaging: Tumours expand bone as large, destructive lytic midline masses. On MRI, there is loss of
bone marrow fat (high T1W signal), replaced with intermediate signal material on T1W which is
T2W
hyperintense
and
demonstrates
variable
post-contrast
enhancement { 15480648 ; 22920610 ; 28184416 }.
Epidemiology
Incidence is 0.08 cases per 100,000 person-years; rare in black patients { 32342333 }. Males are
more commonly affected. All ages are affected, but children/young adults most often present with
craniocervical tumours. Poorly differentiated tumours generally present in children, rarely over 30
years { 29483606 }.
Etiology
Rarely, there is an autosomal dominant trait resulting from tandem duplication of TBXT (brachyury)
{ 24990759 } or in the setting of tuberous sclerosis with germline alterations
in TSC1 or TSC2 { 15236319 ; 28498973 }.
Pathogenesis
Aberrant expression of TBXT is implicated in the pathogenesis of chordomas
{ 16538613 ; 22847733 ; 27102572 ; 29026114 ; 29237806 ; 32855205 },
resulting
in
the
expression of brachyury. Alterations of PBRM1/SETD2, members of the SWI/SNF complex,
represent the most common events (in 16%) in skull base chordomas, along with
homozygous CDKN2A deletion { 33536423 ; 31916407 }. In poorly differentiated chordoma, there
is homozygous deletion of SMARCB1 with loss of protein expression { 27067307 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Bone-destructive, lobular, solid, gelatinous mass expanding into soft tissues.
Histopathology
Conventional chordomas exhibit lobules separated by fibrous septa, composed of large epithelioid
cells with bubbly cytoplasm embedded in a myxoid or chondroid matrix as cords or nests of cells.
Subtypes: Chondroid chordoma represents a subtype composed predominantly of hyaline
cartilage-like extracellular matrix.
The poorly-differentiated subtype is composed of solid epithelioid cells with areas of rhabdoid
morphology and loss of SMARCB1 immunoreactivity.
Dedifferentiated chordoma is a biphasic tumour in which the de-differentiated component
commonly exhibits features of high-grade sarcoma.

120

Immunohistochemistry: Neoplastic cells are strongly immunoreactive with cytokeratin, EMA, and
brachyury (TBXT),
with
variable
with
S100
protein,
CEA
and
GFAP
{ 7922977 ; 23588366 ; 24554551 }.
Differential Tumours are distinguished from chondrosarcoma, carcinoma, meningioma, and
myoepithelial tumours.
Cytology
Smears are usually cellular with with small polygonal to elongated cells with granular bluish
(Giemsa) cytoplasm and round bland nuclei embedded in abundant chondro-myxoid magenta
matrix (Romanowsky stain). Larger cells with copious finely vacuolated cytoplasm and similar round
nuclei are also present and resemble physaliferous cells { 12685190 ; 24554551 }. Bi- and
multinucleated cells and occasional mitoses may be seen.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not diagnostically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: bone tumour by imaging; lobules of large epithelioid cells embedded in myxoid/chondroid
matrix
Desirable: brachyury and cytokeratin expression; SMARCB1 loss in poorly-differentiated subtype;
brachyury not expressed in the dedifferentiated component
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification, with
specific reference to bone sarcoma protocols
Prognosis and prediction
The median overall survival is 7 years for conventional chordoma, while less favourable for
dedifferentiated
and
poorly
differentiated
subtypes
{ 27067307 ; 29361006 ; 29483606 ; 31528536 }.

nasopharynx and skull base.

Physallipherous cells are quite characteristic of chordoma, where there are numerous
vacuolations in the cytoplasm that result in a spider-like appearance.

Other sinonasal tumours

Sinonasal ameloblastoma
Definition
Sinonasal ameloblastoma (SA) is a benign but locally aggressive extragnathic sinonasal tract
epithelial odontogenic neoplasm composed of ameloblast-like cells and stellate reticulum.
ICD-O coding
9310/0 Ameloblastoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Ameloblastoma, NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
SA affects the nasal cavity (septum, lateral wall), paranasal sinuses, or
both { 9477098 ; 23243540 }.
Clinical features
Patients commonly present with nasal obstruction and/or epistaxis { 9477098 }. With tumour
progression, extension into the nasal vestibule { 3927244 ; 22507424 } or orbitofacial deformity
may develop { 3927244 ; 9477098 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies reveals a solid rather than multicystic appearance, with remodeled and/or
destroyed sinus bony walls { 22507424 }. Radiographic evidence of an intraosseous maxillary
alveolar bone component precludes a diagnosis of SA { 9477098 }.
Epidemiology
Very rare, with a strong male predilection affecting adults in the 6th-7th decades of life, notably older
than gnathic counterparts { 9477098 ; 29846904 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
While not studied in sinonasal tract tumours, in jaw tumours BRAF activating mutations are most
common,
followed
by
mutually
exclusive RAS mutations
{ 24374844 ; 24859340 ; 24993163 ; 30216733 }, with SMO a common co-occurring mutation
{ 24859340 ; 24993163 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Cut surface is solid with limited cystic change { 9477098 ; 29846904 }.
Histopathology
SA is a basaloid epithelial neoplasm commonly arranged in a plexiform or follicular pattern, with a
loosely arranged stellate reticulum-like component centrally. Peripheral cells palisade with reverse
polarity and subnuclear vacuolization. Direct surface epithelial continuity may be identified
{ 9477098 }.
Differential This includes salivary gland neoplasms, skin basal cell carcinoma, and basaloid
squamous cell carcinoma. Craniopharyngioma is separable with clinicoradiographic correlation.
Cytology
Reports are scant in the sinonasal region, but in the mandible, hypercellular smears show tissue
fragments of small basaloid cells with hyperchromatic round nuclei and peripheral palisading, and
distinct larger cells with more open chromatin. There may be occasional stromal fragments with
spindle cells with elongated nuclei and abundant clear cytoplasm { 9250285 }. Keratin and
cholesterol crystals are not seen { 32841532 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: sinonasal location; radiographic exclusion of jaw origin; ameloblastic-type epithelium
with central stellate reticulum-like material
126

Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Morbidity depends on size and sites of tumour extension. Local recurrences may occur, but no
distant metastases are reported { 9477098 }.

#25169

Polypoid nasal vestibular projection of sinonasal ameloblastoma.

#25170

Tumour expands the maxillary sinus with extension into the nasal cavity (computed
tomography, axial plane).

#25172
