#24726

Sinonasal Ameloblastoma arising at respiratory epithelium (slide marking)

The tumour exhibits a plexiform or follicular pattern, with a loosely arranged stellate
reticulum-like component centrally. Peripheral cells palisade with reverse polarity and
subnuclear vacuolization.

Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma
Definition
Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma is a mixed solid and cystic squamous epithelial tumour with
stellate reticulum and 'wet' keratin (ghost cells). It is rare in the sinonasal tract.
ICD-O coding
9351/1 Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH15X9 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Craniopharyngioma, adamantinomatous
Related terminology
None

129

Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma can arise anywhere along the craniopharyngeal canal, with
rare
presentation
in
the
nasopharynx
or
sinonasal
tract
{ 2200325 ; 22018920 ; 21255479 ; 24605317 ; 25758284 ; 26623237 ; 26636252 ; 27554305 ; 2
7908738 ; 31699993 }. This sections refers to sinonasal tumours; sellar tumours are covered within
other volumes [[PAED5]] [[CNS5]].
Clinical features
Infrasellar craniopharyngioma within the sinonasal tract present with non-specific clinical findings,
different from the headaches, visual changes, and endocrine deficits of intra- or suprasellar tumours
{ 2200325 ; 22018920 ; 21255479 ; 24605317 ; 25758284 ; 26623237 ; 26636252 ; 27554305 ; 2
7908738 ; 31699993 }.
Imaging: Imaging findings demonstrate cystic masses with prominent calcification and contrast
enhancing walls { 3183117 ; 3392286 ; 21255479 ; 26623237 ; 31699993 }.
Epidemiology
Sinonasal tract tumours are exceptionally rare, with tumours reported in all ages without a sex
predilection
{ 2200325 ; 22018920 ; 22735773 ; 21255479 ; 24605317 ; 25758284 ; 26623237 ; 26636252 ; 2
7554305 ; 27908738 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
While not studied in sinonasal tract sites, craniopharyngioma are proposed to arise from cellular
elements related to the Rathke pouch/hypophyseal duct { 31699993 }. Expression of oncogenic βcatenin in early embryonic precursors and in SOX2+ stem cell populations of the pituitary drive
formation of tumours resembling adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma { 21636786 ; 24094324 }.
Further, SOX2+ stem cells may contribute to formation of epithelial whorls with nuclear localized
β-catenin. The whorls are senescent and act as local signalling hubs through the synthesis and
secretion of numerous growth factors (e.g., SHH, EGF, FGF, TGFβ, BMPs) and inflammatory
cytokines
and
chemokines
(e.g.,
IL1,
IL6)
{ 17221204 ; 22349813 ; 27562488 ; 29180744 ; 29509940 ; 29541918 ; 32453714 }.
These
epithelial whorls are analogous to the enamel knot, a critical signalling centre that controls tooth
morphogenesis { 29541918 ; 32453714 } and implicate paracrine signaling in tumour formation
{ 27562488 }. Histologic and molecular parallels with odontogenic tumours suggest similar cells of
origin and similar mechanisms of pathogenesis { 6196702 ; 9067743 } and explain the occasional
presence of teeth in adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma { 24571758 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Craniopharyngioma are solid and cystic. Cyst fluid is dark greenish-brown, often described as
‘machinery or crank-case oil.’ They are frequently calcified.
Histopathology
The tumours consist of epithelium arranged in cords, lobules, ribbons, nodular whorls and irregular
trabeculae. Basal palisading is prominent, and nodules of anuclear ghost-like squamous cells
termed 'wet keratin' are common. Loose microcystic areas of stellate reticulum often intermingle
between the wet keratin and more densely arranged areas of tumour epithelium. The cysts are
often lined by flattened epithelium. Degenerative changes (fibrosis, calcification) are common, and
there may be a xanthogranulomatous reaction to ruptured cyst material. Adamantinomatous
craniopharyngioma is regarded as WHO CNS grade 1. Malignant progression is exceedingly rare
{ 17592268 ; 24978859 ; 28290422 ; 31785438 }, resembling squamous cell carcinoma, and
ameloblastic or odontogenic ghost cell carcinoma { 17592268 ; 31661068 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma show nuclear accumulation of βcatenin spatially restricted to small epithelial whorls and found in only a small percentage of cells
{ 15891929 ; 17221204 ; 28069929 ; 29509940 ; 32125720 }.
Differential The differential diagnosis includes papillary craniopharyngioma, xanthogranuloma,
Rathke cleft cyst, epidermoid and dermoid cysts, ameloblastoma, and teratocarcinosarcoma.

130

Cytology
Not clinically relevant, although the appearances have been reported intra-operatively
{ 15717747 ; 21630481 ; 33477138 }
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: benign squamous non-keratinizing epithelium; stellate reticulum; wet keratin
Desirable: nuclear immunoreactivity for β-catenin (in selected cases)
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Overall survival is excellent, but extent of disease and whether completely removed, combined with
patient
age
contribute
to
reported
outcome
{ 12134929 ; 15670196 ; 16216361 ; 18352781 ; 22645511 ; 25375987 }.
Malignant
transformation is not reported in sinonasal tract tumours.

#27476:
Even though mutations in exon 3 of the CTNNB1 gene that encodes β-catenin are clonal
and present across the neoplastic epithelium, β-catenin protein only accumulates in the
nucleus in subset of cells such as ones present in epithelial whorls or surrounding wet
keratin.

131

#26558: The tumour consists of epithelium that can be arranged in cords, lobules, ribbons,
nodular whorls and irregular trabeculae. Basal palisading is prominent in this example. Nodules of
anuclear ghost-like squamous cells termed 'wet keratin' are scattered throughout the tissue.
Stellate reticulum, loose microcystic areas of the tumour, is common through the tumour
epithelium interfacing with the wet keratin, whorls, basal palisading perimeter, and more densely
arranged areas of tumour epithelium with sheet-like growth. Thin and flattened epithelium from
cyst lining can be present in some examples as can be degenerative changes (fibrosis,
calcification) and xanthogranulomatous reaction when cysts rupture. In this example, there is
abundant infiltration and adherence of the tumour

Meningioma of the sinonasal tract, ear and temporal bone
Definition
Meningioma is a neoplasm of meningothelial/arachnoid cap cells.
ICD-O coding
9530/0 Meningioma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XH11P5 & XA43C9 & XH11P5 Other specified benign neoplasms of middle ear or
respiratory system & nasal cavity & acessory sinuses & Meningioma , NOS
Related terminology

132

None
Subtype(s)
Meningothelial meningioma (most common)
For other subtypes, please see [[WHO CNS5]]
Localization
Meningiomas of the head and neck most commonly arise by direct extension of an intracranial
lesion, but may occur ectopically within the sinonasal tract (nasal cavity more often than paranasal
sinuses) or ear/temporal bone without any discernible connection to the central nervous system
(CNS)
{ 10800982 ; 10919384 ; 12640104 ; 16134320 ; 19644540 ; 25560515 ; 26191274 ; 28739401 }.
Exclusion of an intracranial primary should be documented before diagnosing ectopic meningioma.
Multifocality is commonly present, and tumours are more frequently left-sided
{ 2104554 ; 6478422 ; 10800982 ; 19644540 }. Rarely, they reach these sites via metastasis from
an intracranial tumour.
Clinical features
Symptoms are non-specific and usually present for years (mean 5 years; up to 15 years). These
are secondary to mass effects and include nasal obstruction and epistaxis for nasal cavity lesions
{ 2104554 ; 6435426 ; 6478422 ; 10800982 } and hearing loss, tinnitus, otitis media, pain,
headaches,
dizziness,
and
vertigo
for
ear/temporal
bone
lesions
{ 19644540 ; 22494527 ; 24650749 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies document the extent, location, and intracranial involvement of the tumour.
This is important for meningiomas with an en plaque configuration. Computed tomography (CT)
scans demonstrate a hyperdense mass compared with brain tissue. Magnetic resonance (MR)
shows uniform enhancement of the tumour. Calcifications may be seen. Bone invasion is often
accompanied
by
hyperostosis
{ 1749851 ; 7862283 ; 7998492 ; 10800982 ; 19934984 ; 20079153 ; 21600866 ; 22406780 ; 256
81214 }. Nuclear medicine radiopharmaceutical labelled scans may identify incidental
meningiomas { 27399848 ; 31040748 }.
Epidemiology
About 20% of intracranial meningiomas extend beyond the cranial cavity (orbit, middle ear,
sinonasal tract, skin) { 4752462 ; 25560515 ; 26881652 }. Primary extracranial meningiomas are
rare, comprising 0.2% of primary sinonasal tract neoplasms { 10800982 } and 2% of all
meningiomas. Sinonasal meningiomas account for about a quarter of all primary extracranial
meningiomas { 19644540 }. Meningiomas within the head and neck show a slight female
predominance (1.2:1), with a peak incidence in the 5th decade of life. However, there is a broad age
range (9-90 years), with women presenting about a decade later than men (49 vs. 37 years)
{ 8285520 ; 10800982 ; 10919384 ; 12640104 ; 19644540 ; 22078714 ; 23326015 ; 24650749 ; 2
5560515 ; 25744347 }.
Etiology
Similar to intracranial meningiomas, ionizing radiation exposure is a potential etiologic factor
{ 2104554 ; 8988089 ; 10800982 }, with a long latency period, especially in patients who were very
young or had a high radiation dose { 23816298 ; 28339329 }. Hormones may play a role as there
is a higher incidence in reproductive-aged women and those who use hormone replacement
therapy. Hormone receptors are expressed in the tumours, especially progesterone receptor;
however, a causal role is difficult to prove { 7861224 ; 8587697 ; 11108890 ; 25335165 }. There
are several genetic predispositions, most commonly neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2).
Pathogenesis
Meningiomas are derived from arachnoid cap cells. Extracranial meningiomas are postulated to
develop from arachnoidal cells in the proximal sheaths of cranial/spinal nerves and blood vessels
where they emerge through skull foramina, from displaced arachnoid cap cells which become
detached or entrapped during embryologic development in an extracranial location, from a
traumatic event or cerebral hypertension that displaces cap cells, or from
undifferentiated/multipotential mesenchymal cells { 6478422 ; 10800982 ; 17690614 ; 19644540 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Meningiomas range up to 80 mm, with an average of 30 mm. Tumours are often polypoid and
covered by intact epithelium. Bone insinuation is usually present, whether ectopic or by direct

133

extension from an intracranial primary. The tumours are firm to rubbery with granular, gritty cut
surfaces related to either psammoma bodies or entrapped bone.
Histopathology
Neoplastic cells are usually intimately blended with an intact respiratory or metaplastic squamous
surface epithelium. Cells have an infiltrative appearance, meandering around native minor
mucoserous glands and within bone interstices. Remodeled bone may show numerous osteoclasts
at the junction with tumour. The cells are arranged in lobules, whorls, and fascicles of epithelioid to
spindled cells. They have low-to-intermediate nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, with slightly
eosinophilic to opaque cytoplasm surrounding round to oval, bland, regular nuclei. Nuclei have
delicate nuclear chromatin, small nucleoli, and frequent intranuclear clear spaces and cytoplasmic
pseudoinclusions { 10800982 }. Psammoma bodies are occasionally present and can be extensive.
There are numerous histologic subtypes of meningioma [[CNS5 MENINGIOMA]], but
meningothelial meningioma accounts for the vast majority, while psammomatous, transitional,
metaplastic, fibroblastic, and atypical subtypes are occasionally seen. Most tumours in the
sinonasal
tract
correspond
to
CNS
WHO
grade
1
lesions
{ 9414189 ; 10223247 ; 10800982 ; 29682381 }. The association of grade with prognosis is unclear
in head and neck tumours.
Immunohistochemistry: Meningiomas typically react immunohistochemically with epithelial
membrane antigen (EMA, often weak and focal), somatostatin receptor 2a (SSTR2A), and MUC4
{ 24393170 ; 26195322 ; 26722517 ; 30112770 ; 33014821 }. Some variants may express
cytokeratins,
CD34,
or
S100
protein
{ 2648875 ; 19644540 ; 23326015 ; 25724000 ; 25744347 ; 28340171 }.
Although
variable,
progesterone
receptors
are
usually
positive
in
grade
1
meningiomas
{ 7861224 ; 10800982 ; 11079777 }. A Ki-67 proliferation index may aid in grading
{ 9610708 ; 19644540 }.
Differential In small samples, the meningothelial and whorled quality of the proliferation may be
difficult to detect, raising a differential diagnosis including epithelioid as well as spindled tumours.
Psammoma body-containing lesions may also present a diagnostic challenge.
(#24737Table #24737) contains clinical, histologic, and immunohistochemical features of entities
which may be confused with meningioma.
Cytology
Smears show cohesive tissue fragments of large cells with abundant delicate cytoplasm that may
be pulled out and broadly attached to adjacent cells, with oval nuclei showing fine to coarse
chromatin, small nucleoli and scattered intranuclear pseudoinclusions. Whorls may be prominent
on intraoperative touch preparations, and psammoma calcifications can be seen. Degenerative
changes with larger nuclei with smudged chromatin and inclusions, and considerable cytoplasm
can be seen [Zarka MA, Moes GS. Chapter 11, in Practical Cytopathology: a diagnostic approach
to FNAB, edited by Field AS, Zarka MA. Elsevier. Philadelphia. 2017 ISBN: 978-1-4160-5769-7].
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: meningothelial or whorled epithelioid cells with low nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios;
delicate nuclear chromatin in round nuclei, often with intranuclear cytoplasmic inclusions
Desirable: immunoreactivity with EMA and/or SSTR2a; lack of melanocytic and hematolymphoid
markers
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Most meningiomas are slow growing, indolent tumours. Given difficulties in obtaining complete
excision in this region, recurrences are common (about 30%) { 6478422 ; 10223247 ; 10800982 }.
Women and patients older than 40 years at presentation are more likely to develop recurrence
and/or die with disease. Tumours that have necrosis have a shorter overall patient survival
compared with those without. Tumours with a higher proliferation index (>4 mitoses/2mm2) have a
lower 10-year survival than tumours with a lower proliferation index. The best predictor of diseasespecific survival is recurrence { 10800982 ; 19644540 }.

134

#23040: It is common to see meningioma within the interstices of bone. The tumour frequently

#23046: Fibrous meningioma are the most difficult to recognize as the neoplastic cells take on a

#23048: The psammomatous subtype of meningioma is seen more commonly in the ear than

#23049: The rhabdoid subtype of meningioma has eccentric eosinophilic cytoplasm in cells that
show a syncytial architecture. This type of meningioma raises a different differential diagnosis
than other subtypes, potentially requiring immunohistochemistry to resolve the diagnosis.

#23058: A whole slide image of meningioma affecting the sinonasal tract. Note the involvement of

Olfactory neuroblastoma
Definition
Olfactory neuroblastoma (ONB) is a malignant neuroectodermal neoplasm derived from the
specialized sensory olfactory neuroepithelium.
ICD-O coding

142

9522/3 Olfactory neuroblastoma
ICD-11 coding
2C20.3 Olfactory neuroblastoma
Related terminology
Not
recommended:
esthesioneuroblastoma;
esthesioneurocytoma; esthesioneuroepithelioma; olfactory placode tumour
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The anatomic distribution of ONB is confined to the cribriform plate, superior turbinate (concha),
and superior half of the nasal septum. Jacobson organ (vomeronasal organ), sphenopalatine
ganglion, olfactory placode, and the ganglion of Loci (nervus terminalis) are included in the areas
of proposed origin. Ectopic tumours within the paranasal sinuses excluding ethmoid, are
vanishingly rare (except in recurrent tumours) and a diagnosis of exclusion without cribriform plate
involvement { 11904342 ; 20596981 }.
Clinical features
Nasal obstruction is the most common symptom. Other manifestations of local disease include
epistaxis, nasal discharge, and/or pain. Symptoms can also be due to invasion of adjacent
structures: anosmia (due to extension into the cribriform plate); pain, proptosis, diplopia, excessive
lacrimation (with orbital extension); ear pain and otitis media (eustachian tube obstruction); frontal
headache (invasion into frontal sinus). Paraneoplastic syndromes (ectopic adrenocorticotrophic
hormone or syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion) are rare { 24170477 }.
Imaging: MRI is better than CT at delineating sinonasal, orbital and/or intracranial extension, the
latter a common finding. ONB shows avid homogeneous enhancement with contrast. Bony erosion
is better demonstrated by CT, with careful evaluation for erosion of the lamina papyracea, cribriform
plate, and fovea ethmoidalis. Peripheral tumour cysts and speckled calcifications are characteristic.
As most ONB express somatostatin receptors, radionucleotide scans (indium in-111 pentreteotide
(OctreoScan)
{ 16470879 ; 29807052 }
or
gallium
Ga-68-DOTATATE
PET-CT
{ 16470879 ; 32796449 } may document disease and/or recurrence/metastasis.
Epidemiology
ONB has an estimated incidence of 0.04 cases per 100,000 people while accounting for
approximately 3% of all sinonasal tumours { 9252701 }. Patients range in age from 2 to 90 years,
with a peak in the 5th and 6th decades { 9252701 ; 17372086 ; 21518449 }. Males are affected
slightly more often than females (1.2:1); there is no reported race or familial predilection
{ 30194694 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
ONB appears to be of neuronal or neural crest origin (neural filaments are present in tumour cells
{ 7201076 }), and molecular analysis suggests that ONB is derived from immature or progenitor
olfactory epithelium cells { 8583243 ; 15272537 ; 24065686 }). Comprehensive genetic profiling
studies suggest that ONB is genetically a heterogeneous entity. While high frequency recurrent
somatic
mutations
are
uncommon,
subsets
of
cases
harbour
mutations
involving TP53, PIK3CA, NF1, and CDKN2A { 28495808 }. FGFR3 and CCND1 copy number
alterations or amplifications are reported { 28775129 }. Deletions involving dystrophin (DMD)
suggest a functional role for genes causing hereditary muscular dystrophies in ONB
{ 30575736 }. Molecular-based subtype classifications have been proposed, but are not currently
in wide use { 29730775 ; 30332658 }.
Macroscopic appearance
ONB is usually a unilateral, polypoid, glistening, soft, red-grey mass with an intact mucosa; the cut
surface is grey-tan to pink-red and hypervascularized. Tumours range from < 10 mm up to large
masses involving the nasal cavity and intracranial region. Tumours frequently expand into adjacent
paranasal sinuses, orbits, and cranial vault { 20596981 }.
Histopathology
Low-grade ONB forms submucosal, sharply-demarcated nests, lobules, or sheets of cells, often
separated by a richly vascular stroma. The cells are uniform, with “salt-and-pepper” nuclear
chromatin. ONB is characterized by fibrillary cytoplasm and interdigitating neuronal processes

143

(neuropil), created by a syncytium of cells. Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes and Homer Wright
pseudorosettes can be seen. High-grade tumours show a more sheet-like growth pattern with loss
of sustentacular cells, greater nuclear pleomorphism, increased mitotic activity and tumour
necrosis. Rosettes by themselves are not diagnostic of ONB, although Homer Wright
pseudorosettes with neuropil are nearly pathognomonic in the nasal cavity. The mitotic rate can be
variable, but is usually low. In-situ tumour within the specialized mucosa is rarely seen in clinical
material, although identified in overlying mucosa in some cases. Calcifications (concretion-like or
psammomatous) may be seen, less frequently as the grade increases. Melanin pigment, ganglion
cells, rhabdomyoblasts, divergent differentiation as islands of true epithelium (squamous pearls,
gland formation), clear cell change, and even metastatic tumours may occasionally be encountered
in ONB { 2494800 ; 12353137 ; 19700942 ; 21915707 ; 32661671 }.
Grading: The most widely used grading system was developed by Hyams et al. [[Hyams VJ,
Batsakis JG, Michaels L. Tumours of the upper respiratory tract and ear. Washington, DC: Armed
Forces Institute of Pathology, 1988; p240-8]]. This scheme captures the spectrum of ONB
maturation and divides it into four grades ranging from well (I, II) to least differentiated (III, IV) based
on the tumour architecture, pleomorphism, presence of neurofibrillary matrix and rosettes, mitotic
activity, presence of necrosis, and presence of calcifications. Validation of this prognostic grading
scheme are reported { 23266076 ; 24668054 ; 25416317 ; 25356502 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The typical immunohistochemical profile includes diffuse staining for
conventional neuroendocrine markers and S100-protein reactivity in a typical sustentacular cell
pattern. Up to one-third of ONB may react focally for pancytokeratin { 11904342 ; 21792956 }.
Rarely, cases can be more diffusely positive with epithelial structures, blurring the distinction
between ONB and neuroendocrine carcinoma or non-intestinal adenocarcinoma { 28168398 }. Ki67 proliferation index is variable, and grade associated { 11904342 ; 20596981 ; 31306501 }. Rare
desmin or myogenin reactivity is seen in ONB with rhabdomyoblastic differentiation { 25757816 }.
Somatostatin receptor 2 (SSTR2) is consistently expressed in ONB suggesting a role for
somatostatin-analog based imaging and therapy in this disease { 33929681 }.
Differential The differential diagnosis includes many small round blue cell tumours in the sinonasal
tract, which must be actively excluded due to unique management differences.
Cytology
Smears are hypercellular with plentiful stripped nuclei, single cells with high N:C ratio and a variable
number of Homer-Wright rosettes with fibrillary cytoplasm and neuropil centrally and a ring of round
nuclei with usually smooth outlines, finely granular chromatin and inconspicuous nucleoli.
Paranuclear ‘blue bodies’ may be seen in the Giemsa stain, in which the neuropil is poorly seen.
Some cases show greater nuclear pleomorphism and mitoses. Necrosis is usually absent
{ 16456848 ; 18285258 ; 21485026 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: tumour originating from and centered around the ethmoid sinus cribriform plate; small
round cell tumour with lobulated pattern and variable amounts of neuropil; expression of
neuroendocrine markers and the presence of S100-protein positive sustentacular cells (in selected
cases).
Staging
Several staging systems have been proposed with no system universally accepted. The Kadish
system is most commonly used { 1260676 }, but classifies local disease only; it divides tumours
that involve the nasal cavity alone (Kadish A), extend into the paranasal sinuses (Kadish B), and
extend beyond the paranasal sinuses (Kadish C) { 1260676 } (see Table 1A). Morita modified the
Kadish system { 8492845 }, designating category D as those tumours with metastases (regional
nodal disease and distant metastasis) (see Table 1B). The Dulguerov classification system
separates those patients with and without sphenoid sinus disease, as well as differentiates between
those with intracranial and/or orbital extension from those with brain parenchymal invasion, while
considering lymph node and distant metastasis separately (Table 1C) { 1495347 }. The Biller
system is based on tumours with extension into the brain that are amenable to surgery and those
that are not, while segregating those with lymph node and distant metastases (Table 1D)

144

{ 2233084 }. The TNM staging system for paranasal sinus tumours can potentially be applied
{ 20180029 }, but the biologically unique behavior of ONB compared to other sinonasal tumours
makes the previously mentioned classification systems more prognostic.
Prognosis and prediction
Beyond stage, the histologic grade is an essential tool in prognostication and management
{ 23266076 ; 23312882 ; 24083125 ; 24806334 }, with Hyams grade an accurate predictor of
metastasis and overall survival { 31251848 }. Neck metastasis was observed in 18% of high grade
vs. 8% of low grade ONB. Distant metastasis was noted in 21% of high grade vs. 9% of low grade
tumours. Patient with low Hyams grade ONB had 5- and 10-year overall survival rates of 81% and
64%, respectively, compared with 61% and 41%, respectively, for high Hyams grade tumours.

#24610: Hyams grade I tumour showing a lobular architecture and a prominent fibrillary matrix.
Homer-Wright rossettes are noted.

#24611: Hyams grade II tumour showing a lobular architecture. Moderate nuclear pleomorphism
and ocassional mitotic figures are noted.

145

#24612: Hyams grade III tumour showing Flexner- Wintersteiner rosettes.

#24613: Hyams grade IV tumour showing no fibrillary matrix. Mitotic figures are seen. Necrosis is
seen.

146

#24617: The nuclei are round with delicate, ‘salt‐and‐pepper’ chromatin distribution with small
nucleoli.

#28350: Axial T2W MRI showing a large soft tissue lesion involving bilateral ethmoid aircells,
destroying the intervening bony septum.

147

#28351: Sagittal post contrast fat suppressed T1W MRI demonstrates intracranial extension and
small, typical, tumoral cyst.

#28352: 4 years following treatment the patient developed multiple metastatic cervical lymph

148

#28817: Gross appearance of polypoid red-grey mass.

#24620: Submucosal lobular architecture. The cells are small to medium sized, set in a richly
vascularized fibrovascular stroma.

149

#28353: A lobular architecture is noted in this tumour, that demonstrates small to medium cells
with scant cytoplasm arranged in a syncytial architecture. Neural matrix is focally noted in this
grade 2 tumour.

3. Nasopharynx
Introduction
Benign and borderline lesions
Hairy polyp
Salivary gland anlage tumour
Carcinomas
Low grade nasopharyngeal papillary adenocarcinoma
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma

Introduction
The nasopharynx is the narrow tubular passage behind the nasal cavity. Since the nasopharynx is
in close proximity to many different anatomic structures, tumours arising in the adjacent sites can
also present clinically as a nasopharyngeal mass, for example, chordoma arising in the clivus. In
some cases, it can be difficult to ascertain whether tumour arises in the nasopharynx or the nasal
cavity
The most common tumour of the nasopharynx is nasopharyngeal carcinoma, which is remarkable
for the striking geographic differences in its incidence as well as the near consistent association
with Epstein-Barr virus. In addition, a broad range of neoplasms can arise in the nasopharynx,
from epithelial to lymphoid, mesenchymal and neuroendocrine. Rarely, tumours derived from
embryonic remnants either entrapped in their normal pathway of ascent or descent (ectopic pituitary
tumour, craniopharyngioma) can occur.
In this 5th edition of WHO Classification of Head and Neck Tumours, tumours not unique to the
nasopharynx are covered in the respective generic chapters, including salivary gland tumours, soft
tissue tumours, haematolymphoid neoplasms, melanocytic tumours and neuroendocrine
neoplasms. As a result, only a few entities are covered in the chapter on tumours of the
nasopharynx:
Hairy polyp Salivary gland anlage tumour Low grade nasopharyngeal papillary adenocarcinoma
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma
Changes compared with the 4th edition series
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma, including the subtypes, remains unchanged compared with the
previous edition. The main advances are the increase in knowledge on the molecular genetics of
nasopharyngeal carcinoma and adoption of the updated staging system: 8th edition of the Union
for International Cancer Control (UICC)/ American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM staging
system { 26588425 } [[Lee AWM, Lydiatt WM, Colevas D, Glastonbury CM, Le QTX, O’Sullivan B,
Weber RS and Shah JP. Nasopharynx. In: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual. 8th Edition. Springer,
pp103-111, 2017.]]
Nasopharyngeal papillary adenocarcinoma has been renamed “low grade nasopharyngeal
papillary adenocarcinoma” to emphasize the bland morphology as well as lack of aggressive
behaviour. Salivary gland-type tumours occurring in the nasopharynx, such as adenoid cystic
carcinoma, are covered in the chapter on salivary gland tumours. Ectopic pituitary adenoma is
covered in the chapter on neuroendocrine neoplasms and paraganglioma, under the designation
“ectopic or invasive PitNET/adenoma”. Craniopharyngioma is described in the chapter on nasal
cavity, paranasal sinuses and skull base, under the designation “adamantinomatous
craniopharyngioma”. Chordoma, which typically occurs in the clivus (skull base), is also covered in
the same chapter. Nasopharyngeal angiofibroma is covered in the chapter on nasal cavity,
paranasal sinuses and skull base, as “sinonasal tract angiofibroma”.
Haematolymphoid tumours are covered in the chapter on haematolymphoid neoplasms.
Nasopharyngeal lymphomas account for 2.5% of all extranodal non-Hodgkin lymphomas
{ 5007387 } and about 15% of all head and neck lymphomas { 20538427 ; 22471466 ; 23041160 }.
Diffuse
large
B-cell
lymphoma
is
the
most
common
type
{ 11400243 ; 19919708 ; 22471466 ; 23041160 }. NK and T-cell lymphomas occur at a higher
frequency in Asia compared to western countries, accounting for almost half of all cases in some

151

series { 1607151 ; 9440725 }. Other lymphoma types, such as Burkitt lymphoma, Follicular
lymphoma, Mantle cell lymphoma, Extranodal marginal zone B-cell lymphoma of MALT, and Tlymphoblastic lymphoma may rarely affect the nasopharynx. Solitary extraosseous plasmacytoma
and extramedullary myeloid sarcoma can also occur in the nasopharynx.
Benign and borderline lesions

Hairy polyp
Definition
Hairy polyp is a benign polypoid lesion of the nasopharynx containing both ectodermal and
mesodermal elements.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E90.6 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx
Related terminology
Not recommended: Dermoid polyp; teratoid polyp; naso(oro)pharyngeal choristoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most hairy polyps (60%) arise in the lateral wall of the nasopharynx, but they can also occur in the
oropharynx, palate, tongue, lip, and middle ear { 8861486 ; 9738766 ; 24771213 ; 30702604 }.
Clinical features
The clinical presentation varies depending on the size, location, and mobility of the lesion. The
symptoms include respiratory obstruction, stridor, cough, dyspnea, feeding and swallowing
difficulty, hypersalivation, and vomiting. Rarely, hairy polyp is associated with other congenital
abnormalities such as branchial arch anomalies, cleft palate, or Dandy-Walker syndrome
{ 2074566 ; 2286505 ; 19720255 ; 26746607 ; 33762553 }.
Imaging: Preoperative imaging with CT scan often shows a smooth polypoid mass with fat
attenuation and the presence of a fibrovascular stalk { 25939422 }.
Epidemiology
Hairy polyp is rare with an incidence rate of 1/40,000 live births. It mostly occurs in neonates and
young children and is extremely rare in adults. It has a female predilection with a female to male
ratio of 6:1 { 8861486 ; 24771213 ; 30702604 ; 33762553 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
Hairy polyp is a manifestation of rare developmental abnormalities. It may be derived from
multifunctional tissue with differentiating potentiality forming a choristoma; or it may occur due to
malformation of the first and the second branchial arch { 15069572 ; 30702604 ; 32911888 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Grossly, the hairy polyp is a long, cylindrical structure and the external surface resembles skin. Cut
surfaces are solid without cystic change { 30702604 }.
Histopathology
Hairy polyps are covered by an ectodermal layer characterized by keratinizing squamous
epithelium with underlying associated adnexal structures, including hair follicles and sebaceous
units. The ectodermal layer overlies various combinations of mesodermal tissue. Fibroadipose
tissue is uniformly present, but cartilage, bone, and/or skeletal or smooth muscle is also commonly
found { 30702604 }. Rarely, meningothelial, minor salivary gland, or odontogenic elements may be
observed { 7614207 ; 33723758 }. Endodermal tissue has not been reported as a component of
hairy polyp.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant

152

Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: polypoid structure with ectodermal outer layer resembling skin, covering mesodermal
tissue; no endodermal tissue should be present
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Surgical excision is curative in the majority of cases { 26829696 }.

#27114: Squamous epithelium with pilosebaceous units overlying mesodermal (fibroadipose)
tissue.

#27115: Central portion of hairy polyp containing mesoderm derived tissue including fibroadipose
tissue and cartilage.

153

#27642: The external surface of the hairy polyp is lined by ectoderm, characterized by squamous
epithelium and associated pilosebaceous units. This ectodermal layer overlies a central core of
mesenchymal-derived tissue.

#28520: A plate of cartilage is noted in the center of this polyp, lined by squamous epithelium with
follicles. This had an ear-like appearance clinically.

154

Salivary gland anlage tumour
Definition
Salivary gland anlage tumour is a predominantly infantile nasopharyngeal lesion with biphasic
squamoid epithelial and myoepithelial components continuous with the surface
epithelium, resembling developing salivary gland.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E90.6 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx
Related terminology
Not recommended: congenital pleomorphic adenoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Salivary gland anlage tumours occur in the posterior nasal septum or posterior nasopharyngeal
wall.
Clinical features
Patients present with feeding difficulty and respiratory distress due to nasal airway obstruction
{ 15656947 }. Before birth, salivary gland anlage tumour may be associated with polyhydramnios
{ 25149159 }.
Epidemiology
Approximately 40 examples of salivary gland anlage tumour have been reported
{ 7402665 ; 8279626 ; 15656947 ; 19025657 ; 19112069 ; 19289716 ; 20704478 ; 21391752 ; 22
896937 ; 25149159 }. The affected patients are infants (diagnosed by 3 months of age) and there
is a male predilection. A case suspected to have developed in utero has been reported
{ 25149159 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Whole exome sequencing of 3 cases showed no plausible driver mutations { 30598408 },
suggestive of a possible hamartomatous origin for these tumours.
Macroscopic appearance
A polypoid to pedunculated smooth tan-brown mass with solid to microcystic cut surface
{ 15656947 }.
Histopathology
Salivary gland anlage tumour is characterized by a complex network of tubules and ducts.
Submucosal ducts and solid and cystic squamous nests with variable keratinization are continuous
with the surface squamous epithelium. The subepithelial stroma tends to be hypocellular, with more
cellular stromal nodules in the center of the polyp. Cellular atypia and mitoses are absent
{ 15656947 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The myoepithelial nature of the stromal cells is confirmed by
positive immunohistochemistry for cytokeratins and smooth muscle actin.
Cytology
Cytologic examination is rarely required, but if done, may reveal cohesive clusters, some with ductlike structures, of uniform cells with small oval nuclei, inconspicuous nucleoli, and sparse cytoplasm
with indistinct borders. A component of metaplastic squamous epithelium may be present
{ 9181318 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: polypoid lesion with complex network of ducts and solid-cystic squamous nests,
showing continuity with surface squamous epithelium; hypocellular to cellular spindle cell stroma
Desirable: spindly cells positive for cytokeratins and smooth-muscle actin
Staging

155

Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
No recurrences after excision were reported

#23958: Polypoid mass with complex squamous proliferation, squamous eddies and myxoid
stroma, ducts connecting to the surface.

#23959: Epithelial tubules and hypocellular myxoid stroma with bland myoepithelial cells.

156

#26084: In the core of the polypoid mass, bland-looking spindle cells merge with tubules and
squamous islands.

Carcinomas

Low grade nasopharyngeal papillary adenocarcinoma
Definition
Low-grade nasopharyngeal papillary adenocarcinoma is a surface-derived malignant glandular
neoplasm showing predominant papillary architecture and indolent behaviour.
ICD-O coding
8260/3 Low-grade nasopharyngeal papillary adenocarcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B6B.Y & XH6LV9 Other specified malignant neoplasms of nasopharynx & Papillary
adenocarcinoma, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: nasopharyngeal papillary adenocarcinoma
Not recommended: thyroid-like low-grade nasopharyngeal papillary adenocarcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The tumour may occur anywhere in the nasopharynx but most often involves the posterior
nasopharyngeal wall { 2462370 }, the roof of the nasopharynx and posterior margin of the nasal
septum { 33603472 }.
Clinical features
The most common symptoms are nasal obstruction { 2462370 } and blood stained rhinorrhea
{ 33603472 }.
Epidemiology

157

The tumour shows no gender predilection, and occurs over a wide age range from first
to eighth decades of life.{ 2462370 ; 33603472 }
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
ROS1::GOPC fusion reported in one case { 33546077 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Exophytic, papillary or cauliflower-like lesion with soft to gritty consistency, measuring from a few
millimeters to 40 mm.
Histopathology
This is an unencapsulated neoplasm with papillary and glandular growth patterns. The papillae are
complex with arborization and fibrovascular cores. Complex (back-to-back) glandular
and cribriform/microcystic architecture are present. The cells vary in appearance from
pseudostratified columnar to cuboidal. The nuclei are round to oval with vesicular to optically clear
chromatin, indistinct nucleoli, and eosinophilic cytoplasm. Mild to moderate nuclear pleomorphism
is seen. Scattered mitotic figures can be seen, but atypical mitoses are not present. Squamous
differentiation { 28601658 } and prominent spindle cell component have been reported
{ 21424532 ; 32438756 }. Psammoma bodies may be present. This tumour has an infiltrative
growth into the submucosa. Transition from surface epithelium to carcinoma may be present
supporting primary nasopharyngeal origin { 2462370 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Diffuse immunoreactivity is seen for cytokeratins and EMA. Thyroid
transcription factor 1 staining is positive (including spindle and squamous cells) but thyroglobulin
and PAX8 are negative { 16084450 ; 28601658 ; 33546077 }. EBERs, p16, p40/p63, S100 protein
and BRAF p.V600E are negative { 21424532 ; 28601658 ; 32438756 }. Ki67 staining consistently
shows a low proliferation index (<5%).
Differential The absence of thyroglobulin and PAX8, and transition from surface epithelium to
carcinoma allow for differentiation from papillary thyroid carcinoma. The complexity of papillary and
glandular growth, and extensive involvement of the submucosa would differentiate the tumour from
papilloma.
Cytology
There are no reports of this rare tumour in the cytopathology literature.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: complex papillary and glandular growth with infiltration of the submucosa; low-grade
cytology
Desirable: transition from surface epithelium to tumour; TTF-1 positive and absence of
thyroglobulin and PAX8
Staging
TNM system as published by the UICC/AJCC 8th edition
Prognosis and prediction
Surgical excision is the treatment of choice and is curative { 2462370 }. This slow growing tumour
has a potential to recur if incompletely excised. Metastatic disease has not been reported.

158

#25642: At low magnification, the tumour is diffusely infiltrative with papillary and glandular
growth.

#25643: Characteristic papillary architecture with fibrovascular cores.
159

#25644: The nuclei show features similar to those seen in association with papillary thyroid
carcinoma including nuclear enlargement and elongation with dispersed (very fine) nuclear
chromatin, and crowding and overlapping.

#25646: In conjunction with papillary architecture, the neoplasm may have focal or more
widespread areas of complex glandular growth characterized by microcystic/cribriform pattern
and/or back-to-back glands without intervening stroma.

160

#25649: Psammomatoid concretions may be identified that in association with papillary
architecture and nuclear features would suggest a possible diagnosis of papillary thyroid
carcinoma.

#25652: Transition from normal surface respiratory epithelium to the neoplasm supports primary
origin of this tumour from the nasopharyngeal mucosa and weighs against a possible diagnosis of
metastatic papillary thyroid carcinoma.

161

#25653: The neoplasm is diffusely positive for thyroid transcription factor 1 (TTF1), a
characteristic finding, but lacks immunoreactivity for thyroglobulin and PAX8.

#26056: An example showing a prominent component of spindly cells merging with the papillae.
The spindly cells exhibit bland-looking nuclei.

162

#26919: The tumour shows a characteristic papillary architecure with fibrovascular cores. The
nuclei show features similar to those seen in association with papillary thyroid carcinoma
including nuclear enlargement and elongation with dispersed (very fine) nuclear chromatin,
crowding and overlapping.

Nasopharyngeal carcinoma
Definition
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) is a malignant mucosal epithelial neoplasm usually showing
evidence of squamous differentiation.
ICD-O coding
8071/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, keratinizing, NOS
8072/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, non-keratinizing, NOS
8083/3 Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B6B.0 & XH6705 Squamous cell carcinoma of nasopharynx & Squamous cell carcinoma,
keratinizing, NOS
2B6B.0 & XH4CR9 Squamous cell carcinoma of nasopharynx & Squamous cell carcinoma,
nonkeratinizing, NOS 2B6B.0 & XH3GS1 Squamous cell carcinoma of nasopharynx & Basaloid
squamous cell carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: lymphoepithelioma-like carcinoma, lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Subtype(s)
Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma (NK-NPC); Keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma (KNPC); Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
Localization
The commonest site of origin is the lateral wall of the nasopharynx, especially the fossa of
Rosenmuller, followed by the superior posterior wall.
Clinical features
Majority of patients present with locoregionally advanced disease. The commonest presentation is
a painless enlarging upper neck mass (up to 70%); the first echelon includes the retropharyngeal
node and jugulodigastric node { 2211261 ; 6190547 ; 10213566 ; 11847385 }. Nearly half of
patients complain of symptoms related to the presence of a mass in the nasopharynx, such as
blood-stained postnasal drip, epistaxis and nasal obstruction. Aural symptoms related to
Eustachian tube blockage include tinnitus, hearing impairment and serous otitis media. More
163

advanced cases may present with headache due to skull base infiltration, and facial numbness and
diplopia due to fifth and sixth cranial nerve involvement. Approximately 5% of patients already have
distant metastasis at the time of diagnosis, the commonest sites being bone, lung, liver, and distant
lymph nodes { 8640688 }. In endemic areas, 1% of patients with NPC present with dermatomyositis
{ 2921149 ; 7576478 }.
Serology: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) serology is positive in most patients with NK-NPC { 3025351 }.
IgA antibody against EBV viral capsid antigen and IgG/IgA against EBV early antigens are
commonly used diagnostic tools, with detection rates of 69–93% { 11526145 ; 12740922 }.
Elevated levels of circulating EBV DNA or RNA, using techniques to detect BamHI-W region of the
EBV genome, EBV-encoded small RNAs (EBERs) or EBNA1 in plasma or serum, can be
demonstrated
in
85-96%
of
patients
{ 10096545 ; 10741733 ; 11352952 ; 12450734 ; 31527689 }. Not only is pre-treatment level
useful as an independent prognostic factor, post-treatment response level is valuable for adapting
subsequent treatment { 15190138 ; 31477699 }. Circulating EBV DNA assay is useful for
surveillance and early detection of disease relapse, particularly distant metastasis.
Imaging: MRI is widely preferred as the imaging modality of choice in the assessment and staging
of NPC due to its superior soft tissue resolution and ability to visualize perineural extension, bone
marrow involvement and intracranial spread. 18F-FDG-positron emission tomography (PET)/CT is
highly sensitive in detecting distant metastases, small lymph node metastases, and local
residual/recurrent disease { 19816641 ; 20190244 }.
Epidemiology
NPC is considered a rare malignancy in most parts of the world, comprising an estimated 133,500
new cases and 80,000 deaths in the year 2020, or about 0.7% and 0.8% of the respective global
cancer incidence and mortality burden { 33538338 ; 33818764 } [[Ferlay J, Ervik M, Lam F, et al.
Global Cancer Observatory: Cancer Today. 2021. https://gco.iarc.fr/today/home (accessed
28/04/21 2021)]]. Age-adjusted incidence rates tend to be less than 1-2 per 100 000, although in
several regions, rates are much higher, including several provinces in southern China (Guangdong,
Zhejiang) and among Inuit populations in Alaska and Canada (Yukon, Nunavut). NPC occurs with
moderately raised incidence in certain populations in northern Africa (Algeria, Tunisia), and in
south-east Asia (Malaysia, the Philippines and Thailand) { 29122009 } [[Bray F, Colombet M, Mery
L, et al. Cancer Incidence in Five Continents, Vol. XI (electronic version). Lyon: International
Agency for Research on Cancer. Available from: http://ci5.iarc.fr, accessed [date]. : IARC; 2017]].
The aetiology of NPC has been described as enigmatic, with viral, environmental, and genetic
components { 17035381 }. While EBV is considered a critical step in disease progression, only a
fraction of infected cases develop NPC { 9705682 }. Persons migrating from high- to low-risk
countries retain NPC incidence rates intermediate between those native to their host country and
their country of origin, implicating roles for both environmental and host (genetic) factors, possibly
interacting with EBV. Much of the current understanding of the underlying risk determinants of NPC,
including the role of a nitrosamine-rich diet, has arisen from studies based on individuals from
populations at high to intermediate risk { 1855886 ; 8174667 ; 17035381 }. The age-incidence
curves in such populations, the vast majority of which are of the nonkeratinizing subtype, exhibit a
single peak with decline observed at around 50 years { 18768504 }, compatible with either viral or
carcinogenic exposure early in life (such as preserved foods), or an exhaustion of the pool of
genetically susceptible individuals { 19329819 }. In contrast, in most low-risk populations, the ageincidence curves exhibit a small peak at ages 15-24, with rates steadily increasing to a second
peak at 65-74 years, and decreasing thereafter. K-NPCs are predominantly associated with alcohol
and cigarette consumption, whereas EBV is the primary risk factor for NK-NPCs { 18768504 }.
In general, NPC incidence rates have been falling in the recent years, an observation attributed to
favourable trends in lifestyle and environmental factors including tobacco control in reducing
keratinizing NPC, and changing diet and socioeconomic development in reducing NK-NPC
{ 26828135 ; 31178151 ; 33849968 }. Decline in mortality rates are due to advances in diagnostics
and chemoradiotherapy strategies { 26828135 ; 28224615 ; 31178151 }. However, several studies
report an increase in NK-NPC in low-risk populations { 23089459 ; 31524955 }.
Etiology
undefined

164

Pathogenesis
Apart from inherited factors, interplay between EBV infection and acquired genetic alterations
contributes to tumour initiation, promotion and progression { 28893937 ; 31521748 }. Deletions on
3p and 9p appear to be early events in NPC, being found in most dysplastic lesions, even prior to
EBV infection { 11034072 ; 12397655 }. Inactivation of key tumour suppressor genes in these
chromosomal loci (p16/CDKN2A on chromosome 9p21.3 and TGFBR2 on chromosome 3p24.1)
are shown to predispose nasopharyngeal epithelial cells to persistent EBV latent infection initiating
malignant transformation and clonal expansion { 23161911 ; 34234122 }. Expression of
EBV-encoded latent proteins (EBNA1, LMP1, LMP2) and noncoding RNAs (EBERs, miR-BARTs)
targets multiple cancer hallmarks including inflammation, immune evasion, genomic instability, cell
proliferation and cell survival, driving progression of NK-NPC { 31521748 }. EBV infection also
facilitates epigenetic changes such as global hypermethylation of the NPC genome
{ 22245473 ; 25924914 ; 31235597 ; 31521748 }. Genomic sequencing reveals distinctive
mutational signatures associated with high APOBEC3 activity, DNA mismatch repair and
homologous recombination repair deficiencies, evidencing viral driven genomic instability
during pathogenesis { 24952746 ; 27647909 ; 28098136 }. In addition to the prevalent deletions
of p16/CDKN2A (43%) which disrupt cell cycle regulation, acquired genetic alterations activating
NF-kB pathways (TRAF3, CYLD, NFKBIA, NLRC5, LTBR, BIRC2/3) are commonly found in NKNPC (63%). Along with expression of viral oncoprotein LMP1 (26-33%), genomic aberrations
underpin constitutive activation of NF-kB signaling in almost all NK-NPCs, resulting in induction of
various chemokines/cytokines for its distinct pro-inflammatory phenotype { 28098136 ; 34234122 }.
NPC cells successfully escape from the host innate and adaptive immune responses in a highly
inflammatory tumour microenvironment through either viral gene products or acquired genetic
alterations. Multiple somatic changes reported including inactivating alterations of TRAF3,
CYLD and type I interferon gene cluster, along with expression of EBV-encoded LMP1,
dysregulating TLR3/RIG1-IRF3 pathway, and suppressing viral-induced innate immunity. Somatic
gene alterations disrupting antigen presentation mechanisms (MHC class I: NLRC5, HLA-A, HLAB; MHC class II: CIITA) contribute to impair T-cell based adaptive immunity in up to 30% of NKNPCs { 34234122 }. In addition, acquired genomic events commonly alter the chromatin
modification machinery (41%; e.g. KMT2C, KMT2D, EP300, BAP1) as well as PI3K/MAPK (49%;
e.g. PTEN, PIK3CA, NRAS), NOTCH (20%; e.g. NOTCH1, MAML2) and TGFB pathways (24%;
e.g. TGFBR2). Unlike other head and neck cancers, TP53 mutations are uncommon in primary
tumours (10-20%) but are enriched in advanced disease and recurrences.
Macroscopic appearance
The tumour can produce a smooth bulge, a discrete raised nodule with or without surface
ulceration, or a frankly infiltrative exophytic mass. In up to 8% of cases, no grossly visible tumour
is identified { 21636837 }.
Histopathology
Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma (NK-NPC)
NK-NPC is the commonest subtype of NPC. Nasopharyngeal biopsies vary in appearance from the
presence of a frank tumour with surface ulceration to subtle involvement of the submucosa beneath
an intact surface epithelium. The tumour exhibits a variety of architectural patterns, frequently
within the same tumour, ranging from solid sheets to irregular islands, trabeculae, and isolated
discohesive cells intimately intermingled with variable numbers of lymphocytes and plasma cells.
Classification into undifferentiated and differentiated subtypes has no clinical or prognostic
value.The undifferentiated subtype are composed of large neoplastic cells with a syncytial
appearance, round to oval vesicular nuclei, and large central nucleoli. The neoplastic cells often
appear crowded or even overlapping. They generally have scanty amphophilic to eosinophilic
cytoplasm. Small foci of primitive squamous differentiation, where groups of tumour cells exhibit
more distinct cell borders and a greater amount of lightly eosinophilic cytoplasm are noted. The
malignant cells may be spindly, showing fascicular, whorling or reticulated growth patterns. The
less common differentiated subtype exhibits cellular stratification and pavementing, often with a
plexiform growth, reminiscent of urothelial carcinoma. Compared to neoplastic cells of the
undifferentiated subtype, the cells are often slightly smaller, cell borders are better defined, nuclearcytoplasmic ratio is lower, nuclei are more chromatin-rich, and nucleoli are usually smaller. Vague
intercellular bridges may be focally identified, and keratinized cells may rarely be present.
The density of lymphocytes and plasma cells within the tumour is highly variable. When abundant,
the inflammatory cells break up the nests and sheets of tumour cells into tiny clusters and/or single

165

cells, making it difficult to recognise the epithelial nature of the neoplasm. The term
“lymphoepithelial carcinoma” may be applied for such cases. Some cases may demonstrate
abundant
eosinophils,
neutrophils,
or
epithelioid
granulomas
{ 1717637 ; 3791157 ; 8630949 ; 11893453 }. Desmoplastic stromal reaction is uncommon. In
approximately, 10% of cases, interspersed intra- or extracellular small spherical amyloid globules
are present { 6706312 }. Other uncommon features include formation of papillary fronds, clear cell
change, intracytoplasmic hyaline globules, accumulation of extracellular oedema fluid
or mucosubstances, focal glandular differentiation, and focal presence of intracytoplasmic mucin
{ 10975941 ; 11145255 ; 24323539 ; 30523510 ; 30822626 }.
Cervical lymph node metastases show neoplastic cells forming sheets, irregular islands, strands or
dispersed cells. Isolated neoplastic cells may display Reed–Sternberg cell–like features in a mixed
inflammatory background, mimicking Hodgkin lymphoma { 83286 ; 7104977 }. Epithelioid
granulomas (sometimes necrotizing) are present in up to 20% of cases { 83286 }. Rarely, the
metastatic tumour is completely or partially cystic, simulating metastasis from oropharyngeal
carcinoma.
Keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma (K-NPC)
K-NPC is an invasive carcinoma showing obvious squamous differentiation, with
prominent intercellular bridges and/or various degrees of keratinization, often accompanied by a
desmoplastic stroma, similar to conventional keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma at other head
and neck sites. K-NPC can arise de novo or as a radiation-associated carcinoma occurring many
years after radiation therapy for NK-NPC { 10685638 ; 19142643 }.
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma, morphologically identical to analogous tumours more
commonly occurring in other head and neck sites, has infrequently been reported in the
nasopharynx { 83286 ; 1384369 ; 1542993 ; 8625035 ; 10983946 ; 11190760 }. EBV may be
positive, especially in high-incidence ethnic groups { 8625035 ; 11190760 }.
Immunohistochemistry and in-situ hybridization: NPC shows strong immunostaining for pancytokeratin, and usually for high-molecular-weight cytokeratins and p40/p63. They often show
patchy expression of low-molecular-weight cytokeratins, EMA and desmoglein-3. CK7 and CK20
are negative { 12459626 }. In-situ hybridization for EBV (EBERs) is positive in practically all cases
of NK-NPC, but shows variable results in K-NPC and basaloid-squamous cell carcinoma. Postradiation K-NPC is EBV negative.
Cytology
Smears of involved lymph nodes and other metastatic sites are cellular and show a variable number
of irregular crowded tissue fragments and plentiful single cells, in a background of plentiful stripped
nuclei, lymphocytes and plasma cells with apoptotic debris. The tumour cells have high N:C ratio
and the cytoplasm is pale and poorly defined in the tissue fragments
{ 2543172 ; 12451563 ; 12508177 }. The nuclei are moderately pleomorphic and hyperchromatic
(Giemsa stain) or vesicular (Pap stain) with prominent single nucleoli. Scattered large stripped
nuclei with macronucleoli can be seen. Occasional cases show features of nonkeratinizing
squamous cell carcinoma. The diagnosis can be confirmed by immunostaining for cytokeratins and
in situ hybridization for EBER using cell block sections.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Positive in-situ hybridization for EBERs may aid in confirming the diagnosis of NPC, in particular
for specimens obtained from metastatic sites.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Non-keratinizing
squamous
cell
carcinoma
Essential: infiltrative poorly differentiated carcinoma; usually limited evidence of squamous
differentiation
Desirable: immunoreactivity for pan-cytokeratin and positive labeling for EBERs (in problematic
biopsies).
Keratinizing
squamous
cell
Essential: infiltrative squamous cell carcinoma with overt keratinization

166

carcinoma

Basaloid-squamous
cell
carcinoma
Essential: invasive high-grade basaloid carcinoma with nested growth pattern and frequent
comedonecrosis
Squamous cell carcinoma component in the form of abrupt keratinization in the basaloid nests,
conventional invasive squamous cell carcinoma or dysplasia/carcinoma in-situ in surface
epithelium
Desirable: variable myxoid to hyaline stroma
Staging
The 8th edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC)/ American Joint Committee on
Cancer (AJCC) TNM staging system is used { 26588425 } [[AJCC/UICC TNM8]]. NPC patients in
Asian centers show the following tumour stage distribution: stage I 4%, stage II 20%, stage III 35%,
stage IV; 41% { 26588425 }.
Prognosis and prediction
The stage of presentation is the most important prognostic factor. The validation study for the
current 8th Edition AJCC/UICC Staging System showed that the 5-year overall survival for Stage I
was 98%, Stage II 92%, Stage III 83% and Stage IVA 71% { 26588425 }.
Prognosis depends on multiple factors including tumour, host and treatment-related factors [[Lee
AWM, Ng WT, Le QT, Chan ATC, Chan JYW, Sze HCK. Nasopharynx. In: The UICC Manual of
Clinical Oncology. 9th edition. O’Sullivan B (ed). Wiley Blackwell, pp 512-523, 2015]]. Significant
tumour factors include histological type, gross tumour volume and EBV DNA. Incorporation of pretreatment plasma EBV DNA can refine TNM staging for NK-NPC, especially for segregating poorrisk subgroups among patients with early stages { 30192385 }; this may spare low-risk Stage II
patients of unnecessary chemotherapy. Significant host factors include age, sex, performance
status, co-morbidities (particularly for elderly patients), anemia and lactate dehydrogenase level
{ 21925925 }. Treatment-related factors affect the ultimate survival. Radiotherapy using the best
available conformal technique to deliver the cancericidal dose with minimal late damage of adjacent
normal structures is fundamental. Addition of optimal sequence and dose of chemotherapy is
needed for all Stage III-IVA and high-risk Stage II patients for eradication of micro-metastasis as
well as potentiation of locoregional control { 29153464 ; 31276776 ; 33405943 ; 33571626 }.

4. Hypopharynx, larynx, trachea and parapharyngeal space
Introduction
Precursor lesions
Squamous papilloma and papillomatosis
Laryngeal and hypopharyngeal epithelial dysplasia
Squamous cell carcinomas
Conventional squamous cell carcinoma
Verrucous carcinoma
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
Papillary squamous cell carcinoma
Spindle cell squamous carcinoma
Adenosquamous carcinoma
Lymphoepithelial carcinoma of the larynx
Mesenchymal tumours unique to the hypopharynx, larynx, trachea and parapharyngeal
space
Laryngeal cartilaginous tumours

Introduction
Tumours of the hypopharynx, larynx and trachea most often originate in the covering epithelium,
i.e., squamous cell or respiratory epithelium. The primary focus of the content are therefore these
entities. Compared to the previous edition, tumours not specifically unique to this site are
transferred to corresponding chapters (salivary gland tumours to Chapter 4, soft tissue tumours to
Chapter 8, haematolymphoid neoplasms to Chapter 9, melanoma to Chapter 10, and
neuroendocrine tumours to Chapter 12), with the exceptions of cartilage tumours. For all listed
tumours, particular attention is paid to practical application of histomorphology, with important
differential diagnoses and prognostic factors. Aetiology and updated genetic changes are
comprehensively reviewed.
Benign tumours are represented only by the most frequent: squamous papilloma. Precursor
lesions, usually referred to as dysplasia/squamous intraepithelial lesions, are discussed as a
spectrum of histomorphologic changes carrying a risk of eventual cancer development.
Classification of these lesions remains a challenging and controversial topic. In this chapter, as in
the previous edition, 2-3 tiered system of low-grade and high-grade dysplasia and carcinoma in
situ is proposed. NANOG, a stem cell precursor marker marker, has recently emerged as a new
potential diagnostic and prognostic marker for these lesions { 31484317 ; 32041418 }. Further, the
risk of malignant transformation of laryngeal leukoplakic lesions was reported to be statistically
significantly associated with melanoma-associated antigens (MAGE-A) expression by
immunohistochemical staining and real-time RT-PCR { 30936424 }. The central part of the chapter
is occupied by the conventional squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), together with several subtypes
(verrucous, papillary, spindle cell, basaloid, adenosquamous and lymphoepithelial). All entities
have specific morphological, genetic, and prognostic characteristics in comparison to the
conventional SCC.
Finally, metastatic tumours were included in earlier editions of the classification, but this edition
includes all metastatic disease to the head and neck in a new chapter (Chapter 15) with a
comprehensive table outlining the most common metastatic tumours in specific anatomic locations
in the head and neck.
Precursor lesions:

Squamous papilloma and papillomatosis
Definition
Squamous papilloma and papillomatosis are benign exophytic squamous epithelial tumours with
fibrovascular cores, usually associated with low risk HPV6 or HPV11 infection.
ICD-O coding
8052/0 Squamous papilloma

184

8060/0 Squamous cell papillomatosis
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XA2RH5 & XH50T2 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory
system & larynx & Squamous papilloma
2F00.Y & XA2RH5 & XH50N3 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory
system & larynx & Squamous papillomatosis 2F00.1 & XA2RH5 & XH50N3 Recurrent respiratory
papillomatosis & larynx & Squamous papillomatosis
Related terminology
Acceptable: squamous cell papilloma; recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (RRP); laryngeal
papillomatosis
Not recommended: juvenile papillomatosis; adult papillomatosis
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The distribution of squamous papillomatosis follows a predictable pattern, with the tumours
occurring at sites where ciliated and squamous epithelium is juxtaposed. The papillomatosis usually
involves the vocal cords and ventricles, followed by transmission to the false cords, epiglottis,
subglottic area, hypopharynx, and nasopharynx. Rarely (in 1–3% of cases), the papillomatosis may
extend into the lower respiratory tract (trachea, bronchi and pulmonary parenchyma) which is
associated with high mortality { 18281102 ; 20553530 ; 24367938 }.
Clinical features
The presentation includes progressive hoarseness, stridor and obstructive airway symptoms
associated with growths of exophytic lesions within the larynx.
Epidemiology
Squamous papilloma is the most common benign epithelial tumour of the larynx. Squamous
papillomatosis is characterized by multiple contiguous, locally recurrent florid squamous
papillomas, although solitary lesions present infrequently. Squamous papillomatosis is a rare
disease involving the respiratory tract that occurs in both children and adults. The true incidence
and prevalence of squamous papillomatosis are uncertain. The best projected estimates of annual
incidence are 4.3 cases per 100 000 children and approximately 1.8 cases per 100 000 adults
{ 18496162 ; 25999724 }. The bimodal age distribution demonstrates the first peak in children aged
< 5 years (juvenile cases) and the second peak in patients aged 20–40 years (adult cases)
{ 25999724 ; 23204170 }. The age of onset of squamous papillomatosis has a trimodal distribution
with peaks around the ages of 7, 35 and 64 years { 26460806 }. Squamous papillomatosis is more
common in children and is the most aggressive form of the disease, with 25% of cases presenting
during infancy { 12975271 ; 14631175 }. There is no sex predominance in children, but in adult
patients there is a male-to-female ratio of 3:2 { 8189980 ; 18496162 ; 25419846 }. Although the
disease is rare, morbidity is notoriously high, compromising functions such as vocalization,
swallowing, and breathing { 15514560 ; 18281102 }.
Etiology
HPV 6 and 11 are the most frequent genotypes (seen in 90% of cases) associated with squamous
papillomatosis as well as solitary papillomas { 7812515 ; 15514560 }. Integration of low-risk HPV
types into the cell genome is an early and common event in the etiology of juvenile and adult
recurrent laryngeal papillomas { 22052184 }. A minority of cases (4–5%) have coinfection with
genotypes HPV 6 and 11, and fewer cases (3-4%) with other HPV genotypes (e.g. 16, 31, 33, 35,
and 39) { 15514560 }. The modes of HPV transmission include sexual contact, non-sexual contact,
and maternal contact (direct or indirect) { 21600804 }. Most neonatal HPV infection occurs by
vertical transmission at birth { 20553530 }. A triad of factors (first-born child, vaginal delivery, and
maternal age < 20 years) has been noted to correlate with squamous papillomatosis in children
{ 1309932 }. An active maternal genital HPV infection at the time of delivery increases exposure to
a significant viral load, with a high risk for transmitting infection { 20553530 ; 21600804 }. Although
caesarean section provides a lower risk of transmission it is not completely protective against
infection. In adults, the mode of viral transmission remains unclear; transmission during sexual
contact and reactivation of a slow-progressing latent infection from childhood have been suggested
{ 21526134 ; 24764146 ; 24918765 }. The unpredictable clinical course of squamous
papillomatosis suggests possible host-specific genetic and immunological factors involving
dysregulation of T-lymphocyte activity { 32210959 }. Differences in HPV-specific immune

185

response have been demonstrated between patients with squamous papillomatosis and controls
{ 20553528 ; 24367938 ; 24449512 ; 25999724 }.
Pathogenesis
HPV are double-stranded DNA viruses, differentiated by the genetic sequence of the outer capsid
protein L1. The virons replicate within the nuclei of infected host cells { 15187189 }. More than 40
types affect mucosal epithelium, separated into nononcogenic (i.e., HPV 6 and 11) and oncogenic
types (i.e., HPV 16 and 18). Viral infection occurs at the basal epithelium, frequently in zones of
the body lined by squamous epithelium with a squamo-columnar transition. The transcripts initiated
at major viral promoter sites in oncogenic types are not seen in nononcogenic types.
Macroscopic appearance
These tumours are intraluminal exophytic, sessile, or pedunculated masses with bosselated
surfaces. The papillomas often grow along the airway as a friable cluster and bleed easily with
minor trauma.
Histopathology
Squamous papillomas have a complex, thin fibrovascular core covered by hyperplastic squamous
epithelium. Parabasal cell hyperplasia is often seen involving the lower half of the epithelium.
Pronounced to subtle koilocytic features are seen in the upper layers of the epithelium. Mitotic
features present along the basal to medial aspect of the epithelium. Premature keratinization of
individual epithelial cells contributes to a disorganized appearance. Surface keratinization is
minimal. HPV-induced cytologic atypia is well-recognized. High-grade dysplasia is uncommon and
if it occurs it is typically seen in recurrent disease years after the initial presentation. The histological
monitoring of squamous papillomatosis is necessary at every surgical procedure for the early
detection of potentially risky epithelial changes.
Immunohistochemsitry: Immunohistochemical staining is unnecessary in the diagnosis but tumour
cells are positive for cytokeratins and p40/p63, and negative for p16. In situ hybridization (ISH) for
high-risk HPV is negative. Ki67 and p53 are of limited utility.
Differential The differential diagnosis may includes high-grade dysplasia with papillary or verrucoid
features, and papillary squamous cell carcinoma. Differentiation can be made on the architectural
features of papillomas and their bland cytology.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Specific HPV genotyping is not diagnostically required { 25419846 }, but in situ hybridization (ISH)
can distinguish between episomal or integrated patterns { 25419846 }. Papilloma recurrence in
paediatric patients may be more attributable to HPV integration { 22052184 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: exophytic multilayered benign squamous cell proliferation; central fibrovascular cores
supporting epithelial proliferation
Desirable: isolated koilocytes; low-risk HPV type (in selected cases)
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
The clinical course of squamous papillomatosis is unpredictable and ranges from complete
remission, to relatively stable lesions, to an aggressive clinical course of rapidly progressive
recurrences requiring surgical intervention, and potentially life-threatening respiratory obstruction
{ 18496162 ; 24291228 ; 20553530 }. A variety of antivirals and antiangiogenics as adjuvant
therapy have not been curative. Early data suggests promising results for adjuvant immunotherapy
and therapeutic HPV vaccination { 33869767 }. Malignant transformation of squamous
papillomatosis into squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is reported in 1–4% of cases and occurs
primarily
in
the
setting
of
irradiation,
smoking,
or
another
promoter
{ 18496162 ; 21526134 ; 23641321 ; 24918765 }. Similiar findings were detected in a large cohort
study of RRP patients (163) identified progression to high-grade dysplasia and SCC in 21.5% and
4.3%, respectively { 33289174 }.
Some studies have found the HPV11 genotype to be the most important risk factor for an
aggressive clinical course, but this finding has not been consistently replicated

186

{ 15514560 ; 25419846 }. HPV11 is more closely associated with a younger age at diagnosis
{ 15514560 } and patient age at onset may be prognostic in the pediatric population { 18509465 }.
Children diagnosed at < 3 years of age are 3.6 times as likely to have more than four surgeries per
year as are children diagnosed at an older age { 12975271 ; 25419846 }. In adults, both HPV11
and an observation time >10 years have been found to be associated with an aggressive clinical
course { 25419846 }. A retrospective sequence analysis of HPV in squamous papillomatosis
showed no evidence of strain replacement in 95% of cases during a median follow-up of 4 years,
with one case having 22 years of follow-up { 23204170 }. These findings indicate that the frequent
recurrence of squamous papillomatosis is a consequence of the long-term persistence of the initial
HPV genome variant. Whether disease severity correlates with specific HPV variants has yet to be
determined, but some initial reports suggest that there may be significance
{ 23473839 ; 24291228 ; 25391185 ; 25634317 }.
The quadrivalent and nonvalent vaccines for HPV protect against the most common HPV
genotypes (6/11) associated with squamous papillomatosis. The effect of this vaccine on HPV
infection has resulted in a reported decreased incidence of squamous papillomatosis especially
where vaccination is a national mandate { 25999724 ; 29136168 }. Additional study revealed that
the quadrivalent HPV vaccine HPV 6, 11, 16, and 18 favorably influence the course of squamous
papillomatosis in patients with the rapid growth of the papillomas. It significantly prolongs the
intervals between the surgical procedures and reduces the number of procedures needed in the
majority of patients { 24964770 }.
