#26944: There are numerous papillary projections into the lumen. The epithelium is thickened,
showing focal koilocytic atypia.

189

Laryngeal and hypopharyngeal epithelial dysplasia
Definition
A spectrum of architectural and cytological changes of the squamous epithelium of laryngeal,
hypopharyngeal and tracheal mucosa, caused by an accumulation of genetic
alterations, associated with an increased risk of progression to invasive squamous cell carcinoma
(SCC).
ICD-O coding
8077/0 Squamous intraepithelial neoplasia, low grade
8077/2 Squamous intraepithelial neoplasia, high grade
8070/2 Squamous cell carcinoma in situ, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XA2RH5 & XH4611 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory
system & larynx & Squamous intraepithelial neoplasia, grade I
Related terminology
Squamous intraepithelial lesion (SIL)
Subtype(s)
Low grade dysplasia
High grade dysplasia - basaloid type and large, spinous cell type
Carcinoma in situ (CIS)
Localization
Dysplasia can occur anywhere in the larynx, although most frequently along one or, less frequently,
both vocal cords. The commissures, hypopharynx and trachea are rarely involved
{ 24595419 ; 22348834 ; 24295655 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms and signs are non-specific and vary according to the site and size of the lesion. In the
glottis, symptoms of dysplasia include voice change and hoarseness, while in the supraglottis and
subglottis, dysplasia is usually asymptomatic { 33051798 }.
Epidemiology
Dysplasia is mostly seen in adults and affects men more often than women, with a ratio as high as
4.6:1 { 18752537 }. This disparity is especially evident after the 6th decade. The annual incidence
of laryngeal keratosis in the United States has been reported as 10.2 and 2.1 lesions per 100,000
males and females, respectively; between 1988 and 2012, 3169 cases of glottic CIS were reported
in the 18-registry SEER database { 1791144 ; 26481178 }. The annual incidence elsewhere in the
world is unknown.
Etiology
The principal risk factor for laryngeal dysplasia is cigarette smoking, especially in combination with
alcohol abuse { 16372991 ; 16945612 }. Gastroesophageal reflux disease has been suggested as
possible risk factor { 12569600 ; 23794281 ; 25810702 }. High-risk human papillomavirus plays a
minor role in laryngeal dysplasia development; rare reported cases showed positivity for HPV 16/18
RNA
in-situ
hybridization
and
occurred
concurrently
with
invasive
SCC
{ 21573490 ; 24127203 ; 24609302 ; 27492445 }.
Pathogenesis
Pre-neoplastic cells are frequently aneuploid { 12973674 ; 15201498 } with chromosomal
changes/LOH accumulating at 9p21 (likely targeting CDKN2A), 17p13 (TP53), 3p26 and 3p14
{ 7738713 }. Other molecular alterations consistently detected include Cyclin D1 overexpression
{ 19139013 }, and telomerase activity reactivation { 13679446 ; 15375434 ; 22463766 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Laryngeal dysplasia does not have a characteristic macroscopic appearance. Patients may present
with leukoplakia (white patch), erythroplakia (red patch), leukoerythroplakia or chronic laryngitis. It
can be a localized or diffuse, flat or an exophytic lesion. Macroscopic appearance does not carry
any microscopic connotation { 24595419 }.
Narrow band imaging, applying narrow-band spectrum optical filters to enhance the visualization
of mucosal and submucosal blood vessels, may provide useful information: the lack of
perpendicular vascular patterns suggests a benign lesion, while irregularities of capillary loops and

190

narrow-angled perpendicular vascular patterns points to a high grade lesion or SCC
{ 20459558 ; 32112142 }.
Histopathology
Several classifications have been devised to represent the spectrum of histological changes and
their relation to biological behaviour { 16731030 ; 19432960 ; 21108746 ; 24689850 ; 29729029 }.
In recent reviews of classification systems of laryngeal dysplasia { 32141027 ; 32329013 }, it has
been suggested to use a unified terminology including dysplasia/SIL, with either a 2- or 3-tiered
grading system (Table 1) (see#26045 #26045). A disadvantage of the 2-tiered system of grading
is a wide category of high grade dysplasia/SIL (moderate, severe and CIS), which can be potentially
resolved by introducing CIS as the third category.
Lesions previously classified as moderate dysplasia have shown a higher risk of malignant
transformation, which supports their inclusion in the high grade dysplasia/SIL category
{ 26268427 }. Although grading laryngeal dysplasia is to a certain degree subjective, it remains the
most important prognostic factor for the biological behaviour of disease, helping the clinicians to
offer appropriate treatment { 11119129 ; 20857246 ; 21499237 }. The diagnosis of laryngeal
dysplasia should follow a multidisciplinary approach and pathologists should discuss with their
clinicians which system optimizes the treatment decisions of their specific patient populations
{ 33058010 }.
Morphological features of each grade of dysplasia are described in Table 2 (see#26046 #26046).
Epithelium in laryngeal dysplasia/SIL is usually thickened, but may be normal, or rarely atrophic. It
may be keratinizing or non-keratinizing; the presence of a surface keratinization is not considered
to be an important prognostic factor { 28060368 }. The basement membrane is preserved. The
subepithelial stroma may show inflammation, but there is no desmoplastic stromal reaction
{ 12138247 ; 15712177 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Reliable markers with diagnostic and prognostic value are lacking. Some
studies suggested the potential usage of p53, p16, Ki-67, EGFR, cytokeratin 19 and MAGE-A, but
they are currently not recommended for dysplasia classification { 30936424 ; 32329013 }.
A stem cell marker NANOG has recently emerged as a potential diagnostic and prognostic marker
in dysplasia of the head and neck { 28894270 ; 31484317 ; 32041418 ; 33643897 }. Studies have
shown that there is no immunohistochemical expression of NANOG in the normal mucosa, weak
expression in low grade dysplasia, and strong expression in high grade dysplasia, CIS and SCC.
The marked difference in intensity between low- and high grade dysplasia indicates a potential
diagnostic use of NANOG, enabling a distinction between reactive lesions and low grade dysplasia,
and between low and high grade dysplasia on the other. However, it does not distinguish between
high grade dysplasia and CIS.
Cytology
Cytology is not an appropriate method for diagnosing and grading dysplasia.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None of the molecular changes is currently of diagnostic or prognostic utility. Expression of
members of the melanoma-associated antigens A family is reported to predict malignant
transformation in precursor lesions { 30936424 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: full-thickness, properly oriented biopsy; appropriate cytological and architectural
abnormalities, preserved basement membrane
Staging
Dysplasia has no staging system; CIS is classified as Tis according to the 8th edition of the TNM
classification.
Prognosis and prediction
The risk of malignant transformation increases with the degree of laryngeal dysplasia. However,
some follow-up studies showed overlapping confidence intervals { 25654369 ; 26268427 }. A
retrospective follow-up study demonstrated a highly significant difference in the risk of malignant
progression between low- and high grade dysplasia, ranging from 1.6 to 12.5%, respectively
{ 24689850 }. Certain high grade dysplasias (i.e., CIS) have been associated with an increased
progression to invasive growth (40%) and may require more extensive surgery or radiation therapy,

191

depending on specific site (anterior commissure) and risk factors (alcohol, tobacco)
{ 22229875 ; 24595419 }.

There is a near full-thickness atypical epithelial proliferation showing architectural

Squamous cell carcinomas:

Conventional squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
Conventional squamous cell carcinoma is a malignant epithelial tumour with squamous
differentiation originating from the surface lining epithelium.
ICD-O coding
8070/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B6C.0 Squamous cell carcinoma of pyriform sinus
2B6D.0 Squamous cell carcinoma of hypopharynx and variants
2C23.10 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, glottis
2C23.20 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, supraglottis
2C23.30 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, subglottis
2C24.1 Squamous cell carcinoma of trachea
Related terminology
Not recommended: epidermoid carcinoma
Subtype(s)
Subtypes are covered in separate sections - please see sections 3.0.2.2 to 3.0.2.7
Localization
Most affected subsite varies in different geographic areas. Two thirds of cases in the US are glottic,
whereas 3/4 of the cases in Poland are supraglottic. Subglottis is less commonly affected
{ 4683046 ; 11489367 }. Most hypopharyngeal tumours develop in the pyriform sinus. Most
tracheal SCC originate in the intrathoracic segment { 8153914 ; 16427548 ; 18331654 }.
Clinical features
Hoarseness is the rule for glottic tumours and is also common for supraglottic lesions, usually along
with a sore throat { 10687946 }. Dyspnea is the most common presenting symptom for subglottic
and tracheal tumours { 10839493 ; 16427548 ; 18331654 }. Hypopharyngeal tumours cause
dysphagia and odynophagia. One quarter of the cases present with a neck mass { 11320826 }.
197

Distant metastases are twice as common in hypopharyngeal than in laryngeal tumours (20% vs
9%) { 20399703 ; 28650113 }.
Imaging: Post-contrast CT is the most commonly used imaging modality to evaluate laryngeal
cancer as it avoids breathing and swallowing artefact often seen on MRI. It also permits the thorax
to be staged at the same time. MRI is increasingly used however as a problem-solving tool to
assess the presence of laryngeal cartilage invasion.
Hypopharyngeal cancer may also be imaged with CT, but MRI is considered by many to be the
superior modality due to better soft tissue resolution, especially in the evaluation of retropharyngeal
and prevertebral tumour extension.
Epidemiology
Laryngeal cancer affects more than 1,000,000 individuals worldwide with more than 200,000 new
cases every year { 30496104 }. It is the second most common respiratory tract cancer { 31912902 }
and accounts for 25% of head and neck cancers { 32209823 }. From 1990 to 2017 the yearly agestandardized incidence of laryngeal cancer decreased with great variations among regions (more
than 4/100,000 in central Europe and the Caribbean vs. less than 1/100,000 in Andean Latin
America) { 31511035 }. It accounts for 0.6% of cancers (1.1% in men and 0.3% in women)
{ 31912902 } and peaks at 65 years of age { 32194301 }.
The incidence of hypopharyngeal carcinoma is less than 1/100,000 a year. The overall median age
at diagnosis is 62.1 years, for men 61.8 years, and for women 64 years.{ 31505992 }. Carcinoma
of the pyriform sinus and posterior pharyngeal wall occur in men whereas carcinoma of post-cricoid
area is more common in women in association with sideropenic dysphagia { 12560383 }.
Tracheal tumours are the rarest. Tracheal SCC shows no gender predilection and occur at a mean
age of 64.8 years { 8153914 }.
Etiology
Laryngeal SCC (LSCC) is strongly associated with cigarette smoking in a dose-dependent manner
regarding frequency, duration, and number of pack-years as well as with high-frequency alcohol
intake. Smoking and alcohol drinking have synergistic effects. A mean risk reduction of 2/3 is seen
after 10 years of quitting smoking { 17505073 ; 31178212 }. Glutathione S-transferases
are involved in phase II xenobiotic metabolism and provide protection against oxidative stress. The
null phenotype of GSTM1 results in inability to detoxify tobacco-related carcinogens increasing the
risk of developing LSCC { 22296360 ; 28331336 }. GSTT1 null genotype is associated with
laryngeal cancer risk in Asians { 24879623 }. In laryngeal SCC COSMIC mutational signature 4
(characterized by a high frequency of C>A-G>T- transversions) is prominent. This signature is
similar to that produced by benzo[α]pyrene exposure in cells in vitro and suggests that it arises
from misreplication of DNA damage (adducts) formed by carcinogens of tobacco smoke
{ 27811275 ; 30236628 }.
The
contribution
of
high-risk
HPV
is
marginal
in
conventional LSCC { 22360821 ; 25652585 ; 32683856 }. Hypopharyngeal cancer is also
associated with alcohol and tobacco smoking { 12560383 }. Gastroesophageal/laryngopharyngeal
reflux is an independent risk factor for development of LSCC { 30366155 ; 32841763 }. Pepsin is
considered the etiological agent { 32734742 }. Its relationship with hypopharyngeal carcinoma has
not been confirmed in large series { 23703970 ; 26451875 ; 32841763 }. Tracheal SCC is also
associated with smoking { 8153914 }.
Pathogenesis
LSCC develops as the result of multiple genetic alterations leading to chromosomal instability and
aneuploidy. Microsatellite instability is rare in LSCC { 14499693 ; 21980613 }. Laryngeal and
hypopharyngeal carcinomas have more copy number alterations than oral cancers { 22740888 }.
More than 50% of tumours harbor p53 mutations { 18094376 }. CDKN2A is frequently inactivated
in LSCC. Although EGFR expression is the rule in head and neck SCC, including laryngeal and
hypopharyngeal tumours, up to 40% express EGFRvIII, leading to a relative inability of blocking
mAbs to down-regulate this receptor { 21352589 }. Addition of EGFR inhibitors in laryngeal and
hypopharyngeal
cancer
treatments
does
not
modifies
overall
survival
{ 33587969 }. PIK3CA mutations occur with low frequency { 22994622 ; 25631445 } but could
predict response to PI3K/Akt/mTOR inhibitor therapy { 21216929 }. miRNAs (e.g. Let-7 family,
miRNA-7, and miRNA-206) and long non-coding RNAs may have a role in the pathogenesis of
LSCC { 22645613 ; 32456271 ; 26079642 ; 31336999 }.
Macroscopic appearance

198

Tumours can grow in exophytic, endophytic or in combinations of these patterns. They are white
and firm and ulceration is common. Supraglottic tumours tend to grow up and forward, invading the
prelaryngeal space. Invasion of the different laryngeal subsites sometimes makes it difficult to
determine the origin. Some glottic carcinomas grow upwards and may cross the lateral ventricles
(known as “transglottic” carcinomas, although “transglottic” is not a site itself). Pyriform sinus
carcinomas frequently invade ipsilateral aryepiglottic fold or vocal cords.
Histopathology
Tumour cells grow in irregular nests with evidence of stromal invasion. Squamous differentiation,
is seen as keratinization with keratin pearls and/or presence of intercellular bridges. Tumour nests
can show maturation: cells are larger at the center whereas proliferative activity accumulates at the
edges. Cell pleomorphism is variable and used for grading. The stroma shows desmoplastic
reaction with newly formed extracellular matrix deposition and a variable inflammatory
lymphoplasmacytic response. Tumour growth at the invasive front can be expansive, with large
cohesive tumour nests, or infiltrative, with small irregular nests or single cells. Presence of nests
composed of up to four cells is called “budding”, but is not included as a criterion for grading of
differentiation { 28548122 }.
Tumours are traditionally graded according to their similarity to normal squamous epithelium in
three (well, moderately, and poorly) or four (well, moderately, poorly and undifferentiated)
categories, loosely following Broders’ criteria. Roughly, grading is made according to nucleus to
cytoplasm ratio, nuclear morphology, mitotic count and severity of atypia, stromal reaction, and
keratinization. Well differentiated tumours resemble normal epithelium, making the diagnosis of
malignancy sometimes more difficult. Poorly/undifferentiated tumours may be difficult to identify as
squamous { 27342593 }. Jakobson proposed an improved grading system including mode and
stage of invasion, vascular invasion, and lymphoplasmacytic response { 4725642 }. Although there
is interobserver variation, grading retains prognostic significance { 19034903 ; 30500292 }.
Perineural invasion affects survival after salvage surgery for laryngeal cancer { 28681422 } as the
perineural plane is a preferential pathway for spread. It is defined as tumour in close proximity to
nerve and involving at least one third of its circumference, or tumour cells within any of the three
layers of the nerve sheath { 19484787 }, but there is large variability on how to detect and describe
it { 27140176 }.
Lymphovascular invasion (and surgical resection margin status) is associated with poor overall
survival, disease specific survival and disease-free survival among patients undergoing salvage
total laryngectomy after radiotherapy failure { 33418958 }.
Tumour infiltrating lymphocytes are strong prognostic factors for both overall survival and diseasefree survival, showing better clinical outcome regardless of method of assessment { 33925205 }.
Metastatic SCC: Histopathologic analysis of neck dissections of clinically node-negative patients
reveals occult metastases in 20% of cases. Occult metastases are more frequent in supraglottic
than in glottic tumours, and in advanced (T3-T4) tumours compared to localized tumours (T1-T2)
{ 32608245 }.
Extranodal extension is defined as extension of metastatic tumour (tumour present within the
confines of a lymph node and extending through the lymph node capsule) into the surrounding
connective tissue, with or without stromal reaction [[DOl 10.1007/978-3-319-40618-3, ISBN 978-3319-40617-6 ISBN 978-3-319-40618-3 (eBook), American Joint Committee on Cancer, Cancer
staging manual 8th edition]].
Immunohistochemistry: Immunohistochemically, SCCs are usually positive for cytokeratins (e.g.
AE1/AE3, CK5, CK5/6, CK14, and CK19) and show nuclear positivity for p63 and p40. CK7, CK20,
NUT-protein, CEA, neuroendocrine markers, CD-117, and TTF-1 should be negative { 24418859 }.

199

Differential This includes other malignant neoplasms with different prognostic significance
{ 28364287 }, as well as benign conditions such as pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia and
necrotizing sialometaplasia.
Pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia consists of deep, irregular tongues of squamous epithelium
mimicking infiltration but lacking cytologic atypia and abnormal mitoses. It can be associated with
specific diseases such as tuberculosis or other atypical infections { 30251032 ; 31522522 } or an
underlying granular cell tumour { 28783989 ; 32099640 }. In necrotizing sialometaplasia deep
nests of glandular origin undergo squamous metaplasia; this may also elicit reactive
pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia { 19482450 ; 30966809 }.
Well-differentiated keratinizing and non-keratinizing SCC must be distinguished from verrucous
carcinoma (VC) and papillary SCC. The former lacks atypias, which are always present in
conventional SCC, while the latter is characterized by papillae formation which is not the
predominant feature in conventional SCC.
Poorly differentiated SCC has a great spectrum of differential diagnostic possibilities. p16
overexpression should not be considered a surrogate for HPV infection in contrast to oropharyngeal
and sinonasal carcinomas { 25652585 ; 25688737 }. NUT carcinoma shows an abrupt transition
between undifferentiated areas and squamous differentiation and is comprised of rather uniform
appearing cells. NUT translocation can be proven by immunohistochemistry, usually in a speckled
nuclear pattern, by FISH or RT-PCR { 29623469 }. The differential diagnosis may also include
neuroendocrine neoplasms. These can be identified immunohistochemically with neuroendocrine
markers (chromogranin, synaptophysin, INSM1); CD56 should not be considered as evidence of
neuroendocrine differentiation if it is the only positive “neuroendocrine” marker. Additional
differential diagnostic possibilities are melanoma, which can be distinguished from poorly
differentiated SCC by the expression of S-100, HMB-45 and melan-A, lymphomas by the presence
of the common leucocyte antigen and markers of B and T- cells differentiation, and
adenosquamous carcinoma by the presence of glands and mucin secretion within the tumour cells.
Cytology
Ultrasound-guided FNAB can provide accurate diagnoses of the primary squamous cell carcinoma
avoiding general anesthesia and direct laryngoscopic biopsy complications or to minimize the risk
of unplanned tracheostomy { 30686768 ; 33289361 }. The usually keratinizing squamous cell
carcinoma can also be diagnosed in cervical lymph node metastases, although some cases may
yield extensive keratinous debris.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not required
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: tumour with evidence of stromal invasion and squamous differentiation
Staging
Staging follows the Union for International Cancer control TNM classification of malignant tumours
8th edition.
Prognosis and prediction
The five-year survival of laryngeal cancer is 60.3% in US. Site of origin carries prognostic value:
glottic tumours show 77% survival at five years, supraglottic 46%, and subglottic 53%. These
figures are related to tumour stage, as most glottic tumours are localized at diagnosis, whereas
supraglottic tumours show more frequent regional dissemination due to their richer lymphatic
drainage. Nodal status is the single most significant prognostic indicator { 32128433 }. Nodal
metastases with extranodal extension are associated with worse outcomes compared with node
negative or node positive without extranodal extension { 33193882 }. Lymph node ratio, calculated
as the number of positive lymph nodes divided by the lymph node yield removed through neck
dissection is a valuable prognostic factor for hypopharyngeal and laryngeal tumours { 32471483 }.
Stage by stage, prognosis in different locations is quite similar { 29947111 }. Anterior commissure
involvement in T1 glottic tumours is a negative prognostic factor for local recurrence { 31721216 }.
Tumour grading shows controversial results regarding prognosis: whereas a significant prognostic
value is reported { 19034903 }, prognosis in early-stage laryngeal cancers depends on non-glottic

200

location and T2 (vs T1) stage, but not on histological grade { 32487468 }. Tumour budding predicts
distant metastasis and survival { 30475254 ; 31328847 }. Interobserver variation is the major
problem in histological grading. A histologic risk model composed of worst pattern of invasion and
lymphocytic host response (both at advancing tumour edge), and perineural invasion correlates
with local recurrence and overall survival { 20414102 }. To date there is insufficient evidence to
include these parameters in the core dataset for pathology reporting { 30500292 }.
Patient gender carries different mortality. The prevalence of laryngeal cancer is 25% of head and
neck cancers for both men and women, but it causes 28% of head and neck cancer mortality among
men and only 12% among women { 32209823 }. Mortality is also lower for younger (<40 y) patients
{ 31380154 }. Patients with comorbidities have a worse prognosis { 29756307 ; 31747406 }.
PD-L1 positive tumours show better overall survival. Immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy improves
survival in recurrent or metastatic SCC in PD-L1 positive tumours, both in Combined Positive Score
(CPS)≥ 1 and CPS≥ 20 { 31674884 ; 31679945 }.
Adequate surgical margins for glottic cancers are usually limited to 1-2 mm because in early stage
disease, the impact of inadequate margins does not result in worse local control or overall survival
{ 30027440 ; 32601821 }. In advanced laryngeal cancers there are major differences in local
control between positive (cut through) and negative (either close – less than 5 mm- or clear – five
or more mm-) surgical margins { 32601821 }.
The most important prognostic markers in hypopharyngeal carcinomas are the size and extent of
local spread, and regional lymph node involvement { 12560383 }. Although overall prognosis for
hypopharyngeal carcinomas is worse than for laryngeal tumours, an improvement in survival has
been noted over the past 2 decades { 25220657 }. Submucosal spread is asymmetric: larger
caudally than laterally, with the shortest spread in the upper direction. This asymmetry could
predispose to inadequate margins. Radial clearance less than 1 mm is a prognostic factor for
overall survival, disease-free survival, and local recurrence-free survival { 32601821 }.
Tracheal SCC has a cumulative survival of 48% after one year but only 25% after five years
{ 8153914 }.

201

#27861

Worldwide age-standardized rate of mortality per 100,00 population per year in both
sexes of laryngeal and hypopharyngeal cancer.

Verrucous carcinoma
Definition
Verrucous carcinoma (VC) is a well-differentiated non-metastasizing squamous cell carcinoma
(SCC), that has a spiky keratinised surface and specific architecture, lacks cytologic features of
malignancy and is characterized by slow lateral spread and pushing invasion below the level of the
adjacent epithelium.
ICD-O coding
8051/3 Verrucous carcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B6C.0 & XH5PM0 Squamous cell carcinoma of piriform sinus & Verrucous carcinoma, NOS
2B6D.0 & XH5PM0 Squamous cell carcinoma of hypopharynx and variants & Verrucous
carcinoma, NOS
2C23.10 & XH5PM0 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, glottis & Verrucous carcinoma, NOS
2C23.20 & XH5PM0 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, supraglottis & Verrucous carcinoma,
NOS 2C23.30 & XH5PM0 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, subglottis & Verrucous
carcinoma, NOS
2C24.1 & XH5PM0 Squamous cell carcinoma of trachea & Verrucous carcinoma, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: Verrucous carcinoma; Hybrid verrucous carcinoma (for invasive squamous cell
carcinoma arising in verrucous carcinoma)
Not recommended: Ackerman's tumour
Subtype(s)
Hybrid verrucous carcinoma
Localization
The larynx is the second most common site of VC in the head and neck after the oral cavity. Most
(~85%) involve the true cords { 7417957 ; 27484231 }, but they may arise exclusively in the
supraglottis, subglottis, hypopharynx { 11443616 }, or trachea { 24558176 }.
Clinical features

210

The most common symptoms are hoarseness (90%) and dyspnea (10%)
{ 27484231 ; 9596216 ; 9570624 }. Most patients present with Stage I disease { 11443616 }, and
radiographic features are similar, stage for stage, to conventional SCC.
Epidemiology
VC is more common in men (M:F ratio 12-14:1) and presents at ~60 years of age
{ 27484231 }. Laryngeal VC accounts for <1% of all head and neck carcinomas. Up to 90% of
patients are Caucasian { 11443616 }. Primarily studied in the United States, it is difficult to identify
global distribution and trends. Data suggests rates are decreasing over time { 26124268 }.
Etiology
Studies show that VC is associated with smoking similar to conventional
SCC { 9596216 ; 11443616 }. Although early studies suggested an association with high risk HPV,
more recent studies using p16 plus HPV specific testing and using HPV E6/E7 mRNA detection
have shown conclusively that VC is not associated with transcriptionally-active HPV
{ 22684225 ; 24071016 ; 24350715 }.
Pathogenesis
VC has similar losses in microsatellite markers compared with well-differentiated, conventional
SCC { 15371945 }.
Macroscopic appearance
VC presents as a fungating, exophytic, tan to white, broad-based tumour with a granular, shaggy
surface. Cut surfaces are firm and tan to white with clefts and very well defined margins { 9570624 }.
Histopathology
Classic VC consists of thickened, undulating, crowded projections and invaginations of welldifferentiated squamous epithelium. There are from one to several layers of basal cells and an
extremely expanded spinous layer with cells with extremely prominent, densely eosinophilic
cytoplasm often described as "glassy" { 7417957 }. There is marked surface keratinization and
intraepithelial microabscesses and Candida superinfection may be present. Mitoses are rare and
confined to the basal and parabasal cell layers { 27484231 }. The "invasion' in VC is through
massive expansion of the number of cells and their volume in the existing squamous epithelium
with preservation of the basement membrane. Thus, the "invasive" areas of tumour have welldefined, pushing borders with no stromal desmoplasia, although a dense lymphoplasmacytic
inflammatory infiltrate is usually present. By definition, VC extends below the level of the
surrounding epithelium, and this may be difficult to assess on biopsy specimens. VC should have
no more than mild/low grade cytologic atypia. While severe/high grade dysplasia and limited
carcinoma arising within VC may not necessarily result in worse outcomes { 24071016 }, tumours
having these latter features should still be termed invasive ("hybrid VC" or "invasive well
differentiated SCC with verrucous features"). The morphology of overtly invasive tumour arising in
VC usually has identical features to conventional SCC.
Differential The differential diagnosis includes verrucous hyperplasia, which is identical but lacks
invasion below the level of the adjacent normal epithelium. Invasive well-differentiated SCC has
more atypia, with frank nuclear hyperchromasia and pleomorphism, pencilliform, hyperchromatic
basal
nuclei,
and
irregular
and
infiltrative
growth
with
stromal
reaction.
Florid pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia can be in the differential diagnosis, but this will lack the
dense, glassy eosinophilic cytoplasm and bulbous rete, and will have more inflammation, either
acute or giant cell/granulomatous, depending on the etiology.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
No proven molecular markers exist for use in VC patients. HPV is not of etiologic or prognostic
significance { 22684225 ; 24071016 ; 24350715 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: acanthotic, hyperkeratotic and undulating bland squamous epithelium; smooth interface
with the stroma; keratotic crypts; mitoses limited to basal/parabasal layers; pushing invasion below
the level of the surrounding normal epithelium
Desirable: dense lymphoplasmacytic submucosal chronic inflammation; "glassy", eosinophilic
cytoplasm
Staging

211

Staging uses the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) system and is the same as for
conventional laryngeal SCC.
Prognosis and prediction
VC is locally destructive through pushing invasion and expansive growth. It does not metastasize
and has better prognosis than does conventional SCC, with reported 5-year survival rates of 85–
95% { 27484231 ; 31914052 }. Patients are best treated with primary surgery { 9578870 } although
primary radiotherapy may also be effective { 28364287 }. VC with dysplasia appears to have a
similar clinical course as classical VC, but conventional SCC arising in VC has the potential
for metastasis and should be treated as conventional SCC.

A large verrucous carcinoma of the true cords from a laryngectomy specimen showing a
granular, tan, exophytic mass.

#24721

The "invasion" takes the form of expanded epithelium which pushes into the stroma in
rounded nests which have abundant "glassy" cytoplasm, round nuclei with prominent
nucleoli, and little mitotic activity. There is no stromal reaction.

#26371

The tumour shows large, maturing verrucous component above and small, infiltrative,
invasive nests below.

Thickened, undulating projections and invaginations of well-differentiated squamous
epithelium with marked surface keratinization. The invasive area has well-defined pushing
borders extending below the level of the surrounding epithelium.

#28476

Invaginations and pushing projections of well-differentiated squamous epithelium with one
layer of basal cells. The surrounding stroma is densely infiltrated by lymphocytes.

basal aspects with no stromal reaction.

beyond verrucous hyperplasia.

Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma (BSCC) is a distinctive form of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC)
characterized by prominent basaloid morphology, squamous differentiation, and aggressive
behavior.
ICD-O coding
8083/3 Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B6C.0 & XH3GS1 Squamous cell carcinoma of piriform sinus & Basaloid squamous cell
carcinoma 2B6D.0 & XH3GS1 Squamous cell carcinoma of hypopharynx and variants & Basaloid
squamous cell carcinoma
2C23.10 & XH3GS1 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, glottis & Basaloid squamous cell
carcinoma
2C23.20 & XH3GS1 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, supraglottis & Basaloid squamous cell
carcinoma
2C23.30 & XH3GS1 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, subglottis & Basaloid squamous cell
carcinoma
2C24.1 & XH3GS1 Squamous cell carcinoma of trachea & Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization

215

Most cases arise in the larynx, particularly the supraglottis, while the hypopharynx (pyriform sinus)
and trachea are less commonly involved { 3770734 ; 20614328 ; 23765951 ; 24158536 }.
Clinical features
Signs and symptoms vary by site, and include hemoptysis, dysphagia, neck mass, cough,
hoarseness, and otalgia. Patients often present at high-stage, with frequent lymph node and
occasional distant metastases { 20614328 ; 23765951 ; 24158536 }.
Epidemiology
BSCC is rare. It tends to affect older patients (27-88 years, median 63), with a striking predilection
for white men (80%) { 20614328 ; 23765951 ; 24158536 }.
Etiology
Laryngeal and hypopharyngeal BSCC is strongly linked to smoking and alcohol
consumption.{ 3770734 ; 20614328 ; 23765951 ; 24158536 }.Typically, high risk HPV is not
found in in laryngeal and hypopharyngeal BSCC cases.{ 18496144 ; 20236687 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
BSCC has a nonspecific gross appearance of a tan-white, firm, poorly-defined endophytic,
frequently ulcerated and/or necrotic mass { 20614328 }.
Histopathology
BSCC is characterized by a prominent basaloid component consisting of primitive-appearing cells
with high nuclear:cytoplasmic ratios growing as solid nests and lobules with frequent peripheral
palisading. Myxoid to hyaline stromal matrix is often deposited in and between tumour nests
resulting in duct-like formations and a jigsaw puzzle-like or pseudo-cribriform appearance. Foci of
clear cell change are common. The mitotic rate is high, with frequent apoptotic cells and comedo
necroses. Some cases show marked pleomorphism, while others appear more monotonous. Most
BSCCs also exhibit overt squamous differentiation, in the form of either a component of
conventional SCC (often intimately and abruptly associated with the basaloid areas) or
dysplasia/carcinoma-in-situ of the overlying epithelium.
Immunohistochemistry: BSCC is diffusely positive for squamous markers like p40, p63, and CK5/6,
and negative for neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin, INSM1), SMA,
S100 and TTF-1 { 18284937 ; 28528398 }. It is also frequently positive for SOX10, CD117, and
MYB,
which
are
often
unexpected
and
can
result
in
diagnostic
pitfalls
{ 14681323 ; 21572406 ; 30498968 }.
Differential BSCC is distinguished from high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma by its diffuse
squamous marker expression (e.g., p40, CK5/6) and lack of neuroendocrine marker
(synaptophysin, chromogranin, INSM1) and TTF-1 staining. Adenoid cystic carcinoma lacks overt
squamous differentiation, exhibits some degree of true ducts and myoepithelial differentiation by
immunohistochemistry (e.g., SMA, calponin, S100), is less diffusely positive for p40, and usually
harbors a fusion in MYB, MYBL1 and/or NFIB. SOX10, CD117 and MYB immunostains are not
helpful in this differential diagnosis. NUT carcinoma may look similar, but is positive for NUT protein
immunohistochemistry. Some HPV-related oropharyngeal SCC demonstrate prominent basaloid
features, but behave much more indolently and should be separated from BSCC { 18496144 }.
Accordingly, HPV testing must be performed on a laryngeal/hypopharyngeal BSCC that also
involves the oropharynx.
Cytology
Smears show variably-sized, tightly cohesive tissue fragments made up of basaloid cells with high
N:C ratio, round to oval small to medium sized hyperchromatic nuclei, granular chromatin, single
small nucleoli and scant cytoplasm with a tendency to molding and apoptotic debris. Single cells in
variable number can be seen. Chromatinic smearing is not seen. Numerous mitotic figures are
present and the background is frequently necrotic. Single keratinized cells are a helpful clue, but
keratinization is often scarce or absent { 1542993 ; 19582815 ; 22102491 }. Fragments of irregular
magenta (Giemsa stain) basement membrane material can be present, and the differential
diagnosis includes adenoid cystic carcinoma with its lesser degree of nuclear atypia and more
rounded sharply demarcated basement membrane material { 19582815 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology

216

Not well defined. High-risk HPV is not typically found in laryngeal/hypopharyngeal BSCC
{ 3770734 ; 20236687 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: tumour with prominent basaloid morphology, presence of squamous differentiation and
absence of neuroendocrine or myoepithelial differentiation by histology and/or
immunohistochemistry
Desirable: high-grade histologic features, myxoid to hyaline stromal alterations
Staging
Staging uses the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) system
Prognosis and prediction
BSCC is traditionally regarded as an aggressive type of hypopharyngeal and laryngeal SCC, with
most studies finding higher rates of nodal metastasis and distant metastasis, and worse overall
and disease-free survival when compared to conventional SCC. Some recent studies, however,
have disputed the notion that BSCC behaves differently when matched stage-for-stage
{ 20614328 ; 23765951 ; 24158536 }. Studies that include more indolent HPV-positive
oropharyngeal cases in their cohorts are difficult to interpret.

The tumour is characterized by compact solid nests and trabeculae of basaloid cells
arranged in a jigsaw puzzle-like arrangement, separated by myxoid stroma. Abrupt
keratinization (center) and comedo necrosis (top right) are observed.

#24880
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
Within one of the basaloid nests are numerous deposits of hyaline basement membranelike material, resulting in a pseudocribriform appearance. The tumour cells are
pleomorphic, with vesicular chromatin and prominent nucleoli.

#24881
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
The tumour is characteristically positive for p40 in a strong, diffuse pattern which helps to
distinguish it from mimickers like adenoid cystic carcinoma and small cell carcinoma.

#27938
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
This classic example consists primarily of infiltrative basaloid nests with comedo necrosis.
There is a frequent jigsaw puzzle like arrangement with compact basaloid nests
separated by thin strands of hyaline material. Squamous differentiation is evident in the
form of patchy foci of abrupt keratinization.

Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma
This classic tumour consists primarily of infiltrative basaloid nests with comedo necrosis.
There is a frequent jigsaw puzzle like arrangement with compact basaloid nests
separated by thin strands of hyaline material. Squamous differentiation is evident in the
form of patchy foci of abrupt keratinization.

219

Papillary squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
Papillary squamous cell carcinoma (PSCC) grows exophytically with papillary fronds covered by a
malignant stratified squamous epithelium or with immature basaloid cells with minimal or no
maturation.
ICD-O coding
8052/3 Papillary squamous cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B6C.0 & XH6S97 Squamous cell carcinoma of pyriform sinus & Papillary squamous cell
carcinoma
2B6D.0 & XH6S97 Squamous cell carcinoma of hypopharynx and variants & Papillary squamous
cell carcinoma 2C23.10 & XH0UU4 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, glottis & Papillary
squamous cell carcinoma
2C23.20 & XH0UU4 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, supraglottis & Papillary squamous cell
carcinoma
2C23.30 & XH0UU4 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, subglottis Papillary squamous cell
carcinoma 2C24.1 & XH6S97 Squamous cell carcinoma of trachea & Papillary squamous cell
carcinoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Tumours can occur throughout the larynx and hypopharyx. The majority are glottic, supraglottic,
and transglottic { 10229599 }.
Clinical features
Patients with laryngeal tumours most often present with hoarseness { 10229599 }. Other symptoms
can include dysphagia, sore throat, cough, and hemoptysis.
Imaging: This should be performed as per conventional laryngeal SCC and may demonstrate an
exophytic soft tissue lesion.
Epidemiology
Tumours are much more common in men and usually are identified in or after the sixth decade of
life (10229599).
Etiology
Tumours are related to tobacco and alcohol consumption and high-risk human papillomavirus
infection at ratios similar to other non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinomas from the head and
neck at the various subsites { 10229599 ; 18387989 ; 19745700 }.
Pathogenesis
The tumours are believed to arise through multi-step squamous metaplasia and dysplasia
{ 15371945 ; 24878013 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours have a grossly papillary appearance and lack grossly evident keratinization { 10862019 }.
They are typically friable and soft and vary in size.
Histopathology
Tumours are composed of numerous complex papillary and filiform structures extending in all
planes which often renders the assessment of true tissue invasion difficult { 16468420 }. The
papillary fronds are covered with a stratified squamous epithelium, which has overt features of
malignancy, replete with lack of maturation, increased nuclear to cytoplasmic ratios, nuclear
irregularities and numerous mitotic figures located throughout the entire thickness of the epithelium.
These morphological features are related to non-keratinizing type of PSCC. The keratinizing type
shows papillae covered with high-grade keratinizing dysplasia { 28060368 }. Koilocytic change is
sometimes noted { 10229599 }. Intracellular keratinization or dyskeratosis may be present,

220

however, surface keratosis is most often not seen. The papillary fronds have fibrovascular cores
that usually contain lymphocytes and plasma cells. The invasive component, when identified will
have infiltrating, irregular nests of squamous epithelium morphologically identical to most nonkeratinizing SCC.
Immunohistochemistry: Typical block-like p16 expression is seen with tumours secondary to highrisk human papillomavirus, especially in the non-keratinizing type, although testing by
immunostaining is not currently recommended { 19745700 }.
Differential Tumours must be distinguished from verrucous carcinoma, a tumour that typically has
surface keratinization and lack the atypia seen with papillary squamous cell carcinoma. Laryngeal
papillomas usually lack the cytologic atypia of PSCC.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: tumours are exophytic and composed predominantly of papillary fronds covered by a
non-keratinizing malignant stratified squamous epithelium or in the keratinizing type by high-grade
atypia
Desirable: stromal infiltration
Staging
Staging is performed as is typical for conventional SCC at the site.
Prognosis and prediction
Tumours behave, stage for stage, similar to or slightly better than conventional SCC at the site
{ 18387989 }. Because of their exophytic growth, they may be more likely to present at an early
stage. Given the infrequency of high-risk human papillomavirus at many sites within the upper
aerodigestive tract, it is unclear whether infection affects prognosis at sites outside of the
oropharynx { 19745700 }.

This low power image highlights the numerous, delicate papillae seen with papillary
squamous cell carcinoma.

#26198
Papillary squamous cell carcinoma
At higher power, the papillae are covered by a malignant stratified squamous epithelium.

Papillary squamous cell carcinoma

cell carcinoma that was secondary to high-risk human papilloma virus infection.

#26934

Papillary squamous cell carcinoma
There is a bulky, papillary proliferation of highly atypical squamous epithelium in this
papillary type squamous cell carcinoma.

Spindle cell squamous carcinoma
Definition
Spindle cell squamous carcinoma (SCSC) is composed of spindle and/or epithelioid pleomorphic
cells, usually associated with intraepithelial dysplasia and/or invasive conventional squamous cell
carcinoma.
ICD-O coding
8074/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, spindle cell
ICD-11 coding
2B6D.0 & XH6D80 Squamous cell carcinoma of piriform sinus & Squamous cell carcinoma,
spindle cell 2B6D.0 & XH6D80 Squamous cell carcinoma of hypopharynx and variants &
Squamous cell carcinoma, spindle cell
2C23.10 & XH6D80 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, glottis & Squamous cell carcinoma,
spindle cell
2C23.20 & XH6D80 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, supraglottis & Squamous cell carcinoma,
spindle cell
2C23.30 & XH6D80 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, subglottis & Squamous cell carcinoma,
spindle cell
2C24.1 & XH6D80 Squamous cell carcinoma of trachea & Squamous cell carcinoma, spindle cell
Related terminology
Acceptable: spindle cell carcinoma; sarcomatoid carcinoma
Not recommended: carcinosarcoma; pseudosarcoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The larynx is the most common site for SCSC, followed by the oral cavity, sinonasal tract,
oropharynx, nasopharynx and lastly the hypopharynx { 28591732 ; 33135805 }. In the larynx, three
quarters of cases involve the glottis { 9018257 ; 11812937 }.
Clinical features
Hoarseness is a common symptom. An exophytic firm mass limited to the vocal cords is usually
seen on a laryngoscopy exam { 9018257 ; 11812937 }.
223

Imaging: Imaging is as per conventional SCC, most often with post-contrast CT.
Epidemiology
SCSCC represents less than 1% of all laryngeal malignancies, occur across a wide age range, has
a peak incidence in the 5th and 6th decade, and shows a strikingly male predisposition
{ 11812937 ; 25994520 ; 33526270 }.
Etiology
Tobacco and alcohol abuse are strongly associated with SCSC. Radiation history has been
reported in subset of patients { 11812937 }. Regardless of location, identifying high-risk HPV in
SCSC is rare { 23536041 ; 24418859 }.
Pathogenesis
SCSC is an epithelial malignancy that has undergone epithelial–mesenchymal transition
{ 2482058 ; 18712413 ; 19381684 ; 21237487 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Usually a grossly polypoid tumour { 11812937 }.
Histopathology
The histological hallmark of SCSC is the presence of a spindle cell proliferation at a mucosa-based
site. The spindle cells usually show overt pleomorphism, hyperchromasia, and increased mitotic
activity. In rare occasions the SCSC can exhibit bland cytomorphology, and tumour cells can have
an epithelioid pleomorphic morphology. Most tumours are hypercellular and arranged in a diversity
of patterns (fascicular, storiform, cartwheel). Hypocellular variants with increased stromal
collagenization exist. Surface ulceration is common, and most tumours contained a certain degree
of acute/chronic inflammation. The spindle cell component generally predominates; however, some
tumours exhibit a biphasic morphology secondary to the presence of a differentiated SCC
component. The latter can be in form of intraepithelial dysplasia and/or invasive conventional SCC.
Heterologous differentiation occurs in up to 7% of cases and includes osseocartilaginous and
rhabdomyoblastic elements { 8494107 ; 11812937 ; 23054954 ; 33982148 }.
Immunohistochemistry: SCSC shows variable reactivity for cytokeratin in 48-83% of cases
{ 2430474 ; 2437812 ; 2482058 ; 9191000 ; 11812937 ; 12170089 ; 24418859 }. Immunoreactivity
for p63 and p40 often mirrors the reactivity of cytokeratin; however, in some occasions they are
only positive markers { 15976812 ; 24418859 }. SCSC is consistently positive for vimentin and may
express
other
mesenchymal
markers,
particularly
smooth
muscle
actin
{ 11812937 ; 20730609 ; 33982148 }. SCSC exhibits a wide range of Ki-67 labeling and a low
proliferation index can be observed { 33982148 }.
Differential Granulation tissue with reactive stromal changes, particularly after radiotherapy, can
be distinguished from SCSC by the lack of p40 reactivity { 24418859 }. Laryngeal sarcomas are
extremely rare and the most common one is chondrosarcoma { 33686585 }. Therefore, in absence
of cytokeratin and p63/p40 reactivity, a mucosa-based malignant spindle cell neoplasm is a SCSC
until proven otherwise. In such cases careful evaluation of the overlying and adjacent mucosa is
necessary and may identify intraepithelial dysplasia. Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour (IMT)
can occur in the larynx and most commonly affect the glottis { 8635024 }. IMT is characterized by
various gene rearrangements involving ALK, ROS1 and NTRK3, which have not been described in
SCSC { 26647767 }.
Cytology
Cellularity may be low and shows spindle cells with variable pleomorphism sometimes admixed
with epithelioid squamous cells showing varying degrees of keratinization with dense well defined
cytoplasm and large hyperchromatic pleomorphic nuclei { 19582815 }. The spindle cells can be
seen singly with poorly defined cytoplasm and hyperchromatic oval to in some cases large nuclei
with large nucleoli. Multinucleation may be seen and the differential diagnosis in the absence of the
more characteristic keratinized squamous cells can include a sarcoma or melanoma.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: mucosa-based tumour with a malignant spindle cell component
Desirable: polypoid tumour; intraepithelial dysplasia; invasive conventional SCC; immunoreactivity
for cytokeratin and/or p63/p40

224

Staging
TNM classification of the larynx or pharynx by the UICC
Prognosis and prediction
Majority of laryngeal SCSC present as T1 tumours (62.1%), when compared to 10% in the
hypopharynx { 25994520 ; 31608238 }. In the larynx the disease‐specific survival at 10 years is
57.9% and is comparable to conventional SCC. Glottic location confers a survival benefit due to
early detection { 11812937 ; 24634155 ; 25994520 }. The disease‐specific overall survival at 10
years of hypopharyngeal SCSC is 8% and is significantly lower than conventional SCC
{ 31608238 }. Tumours with a conventional SCC component appear to have a better prognosis
{ 33982148 }.
Radiotherapy-induced tumours have a worse prognosis than those arising de novo { 11812937 }.

Associated with a well-differentiated squamous carcinoma.

#26189

231

Adenosquamous carcinoma
Definition
Adenosquamous carcinoma (ASC) is a biphasic malignant tumour that arises from the surface
epithelium and shows squamous and glandular differentiation.
ICD-O coding
8560/3 Adenosquamous carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B6D.Y & XH7873 Other specified malignant neoplasms of piriform sinus & Adenosquamous
carcinoma 2B6D.Y & XH7873 Other specified malignant neoplasms of hypopharynx &
Adenosquamous carcinoma 2C23.1Y & XH7873 Other specified malignant neoplasms of larynx,
glottis & Adenosquamous carcinoma 2C23.2Y & XH7873 Other specified malignant neoplasms of
larynx, supraglottis & Adenosquamous carcinoma 2C23.3Y & XH7873 Other specified malignant
neoplasms of larynx, subglottis & Adenosquamous carcinoma
2C24.Y & XH7873 Other specified malignant neoplasms of trachea & Adenosquamous
carcinoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The larynx is the most frequently affected site in the head and neck { 26255977 } followed by oral,
pharyngeal and sinonasal mucosa { 21305368 ; 28412233 }.
Clinical features
Clinical symptoms are similar to SCC and include hoarseness, sore throat, dysphagia, haemoptysis
and/or neck mass { 28364287 }.
Epidemiology
ASC is rare and affects men two to four times more frequently than women without any race
predilection. The peak incidence is in the seventh and eighth decade of life (median 66 years, range
43 to 88 years) { 26255977 ; 26452061 ; 28412233 }.
Etiology
As with SCC, smoking and alcohol consumption are likely predisposing factors { 12019485 }. An
association with HPV has been reported in ASC of the oropharynx and nasal cavity but not larynx
and hypopharynx { 21305368 }.
Pathogenesis
Little is known about etiopathogenesis of the ASC, mainly due to it's rarity and lack of larger,
controlled studies { 21305368 }. However, cigarette smoking and alcohol abuse probably play an
important role in the pathogenesis of ASC, similarly to other subtypes of the conventional SCC
{ 15186272 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Most ASC present as glottic or supraglottic exophytic or polypoid masses (median size: 40 mm) or
as mucosal induration or ulceration, similar to SCC { 26452061 }.
Histopathology
ASC has a biphasic morphology showing squamous and glandular differentiation. The squamous
carcinoma is generally superficial, associated with dysplasia of the surface epithelium and may
show keratinization including keratin pearls while the adenocarcinoma tends to be deeper and
consists of cribriform and tubuloglandular structures. Mucus cells may be present. The two
components are distinct, separate or intermixed. Areas of transition and an undifferentiated
basaloid or clear cell component may be seen { 28153131 }. Necrosis, mitoses, and vascular and
perineural invasion are frequent. Metastatic ASC may display one or both components.
Special stains: periodic acid–Schiff (PAS), Alcian blue, and mucicarmine may demonstrate
intraluminal (or rarely, intracytoplasmic) mucin.
Immunohistochemistry: The tumour shows shows p63 expression in the squamous component;
carcinoembryonic antigen and low-molecular-weight cytokeratin (CAM5.2) in the

232

adenocarcinomatous component. CK7 and high-molecular-weight cytokeratin may be seen in both
components { 24698420 }. CK20 is usually negative { 24338588 }. Since CK7 may be focally
expressed in head and neck SCC, the diagnosis requires the presence of distinct squamous and
adenocarcinomatous components adjacent to each other on H&E.
Differential This includes mucoepidermoid carcinoma (MEC), and variants of SCC, e.g. adenoid,
conventional and basaloid SCC invading submucous seromucinous glands. Distinction from MEC
is important because ASC has a worse prognosis (see #25099Table #25099). Demonstration of
intracytoplasmic mucin and carcinoembryonic antigen helps to distinguish ASC from adenoid SCC.
SCC invading or entrapping seromucinous glands is differentiated by lobular architecture and
benign cytomorphology of the glandular cells. Basaloid SCC shows peripheral palisading and lacks
glandular differentiation { 28153131 }. Necrotizing sialometaplasia, rarely seen in the larynx, retains
the lobular architecture of the seromucous glands (despite being replaced by squamous
metaplasia) and may be associated with ischaemic necrosis of the acini, chronic inflammation, and
pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia of the overlying squamous epithelium. Superficial biopsies may
be misdiagnosed as SCC as the glandular component tends to be deeper.
Cytology
Reports of lesions in the H&N region are scant, but metastases may show features of
predominantly keratinizing or non-keratinizing SCC with a lesser glandular component
{ 19582815 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Importance of CRTC1::MAML2 translocation was found to be important in distinguishing ASC from
MEC. MAML2 translocation excludes the diagnosis of ASC while negativity does not necessarily
exclude MEC, especially the high-grade MEC { 26255977 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: biphasic tumour with squamous and glandular components that are distinctly recognized
adjacent to each other on H&E examination
Desirable: evidence of origin from surface epithelium e.g. squamous dysplasia. Mucin production
Staging
Similar to SCC
Prognosis and prediction
ASC is more aggressive than conventional SCC, presenting at an advanced stage with involvement
of lymph nodes in 40% and distant metastases in 10% of cases { 26452061 }. Despite surgery and
adjuvant chemo- and radiotherapy, tumours frequently recur and metastasize
{ 1007912 ; 15186272 }. The median survival is less than 3 years and 5-year survival rate ranges
from 30% to 50% { 26255977 ; 26452061 }. Advanced age and stage and large tumour size are
associated with decreased survival { 28412233 }. HPV-associated ASC in the oropharynx appear
to have a better prognosis similar to HPV-associated SCC { 21305368 }.

Lymphoepithelial carcinoma of the larynx
Definition
Lymphoepithelial carcinoma (LEC) is a poorly differentiated form of squamous cell carcinoma
characterized by its morphologic similarity to the non-keratinizing, undifferentiated form of
nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
ICD-O coding
8082/3 Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B6C.0 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of piriform sinus & Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
2B6D.0 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of hypopharynx and variants & Lymphoepithelial
carcinoma 2C23.10 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, glottis & Lymphoepithelial
carcinoma
2C23.20 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, supraglottis & Lymphoepithelial
carcinoma 2C23.30 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of larynx, subglottis & Lymphoepithelial
carcinoma 2C24.1 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of trachea & Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: lymphoepithelial-like carcinoma; lymphoepithelioma; lymphoepithelioma-like
carcinoma; undifferentiated carcinoma of the nasopharyngeal type; undifferentiated carcinoma with
lymphoid stroma.
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
LEC occurs more frequently in the larynx (usually supraglottis) than hypopharynx (piriform sinus),
with only rare tracheal involvement { 2361396 ; 17592428 ; 17593478 ; 32867094 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with hoarseness, neck mass, dysphonia, dysphagia, neck pain, and/or hemoptysis
{ 8912827 ; 9692636 ; 25804344 ; 32867094 }.

236

Epidemiology
LEC of the larynx, hypopharynx, and trachea is rare, with fewer than 50 reported cases. There is a
marked male predominance, and it affects older patients (mean, 62 years). Unlike nasopharyngeal
carcinoma which most frequently affects Asians, laryngeal LEC usually occurs in Caucasians
{ 421357 ; 2361396 ; 4033331 ; 8912827 ; 9692636 ; 9419099 ; 17593478 ; 17592428 ; 2580434
4 ; 26043818 ; 26316257 }.
Etiology
Laryngeal/hypopharyngeal LEC is associated with smoking and alcohol consumption
{ 8912827 ; 9692636 ; 25804344 }. Only rare cases are EBV-positive, but a subset harbour highrisk HPV { 9419099 ; 25804344 ; 26043818 ; 30552415 }.
Pathogenesis
LEC frequently harbours p53 mutations.
Macroscopic appearance
LEC appears as a nonspecific white, tan, or pink mass that may be endophytic or polypoid
{ 8912827 }.
Histopathology
LEC is defined by its resemblance to undifferentiated nasopharyngeal carcinoma: sheet-like or
nested architecture with syncytial growth, a prominent lymphoplasmacytic cell infiltrate, and tumour
cells with vesicular chromatin and prominent nucleoli. Mitotic figures and apoptotic cells are
common. LEC can be pure or mixed with conventional squamous cell carcinoma either in the
invasive tumour or the surface epithelium (i.e., carcinoma-in-situ).
Immunohistochemistry: LEC is diffusely positive for pan-cytokeratin, and usually positive for
squamous markers like CK5/6 and p40/p63.
Differential LEC is distinguished from large cell lymphoma and melanoma by its positivity for pancytokeratin and lack of staining with lymphoid markers (e.g., CD45, CD30, CD20) and melanoma
markers (e.g., SOX10, S100, HMB45), respectively. Absence of EBV helps exclude spread from
the nasopharynx.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
LEC are almost always negative for EBV, but a subset harbours high-risk HPV
{ 8912827 ; 9419099 ; 25804344 ; 26043818 ; 30552415 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: tumour with syncytial growth; inflammatory infiltrate; vesicular chromatin; prominent
nucleoli
Desirable: positive for pan-keratin and squamous markers (e.g., p40, CK5/6); exclude direct
extension or metastasis
Staging
Staging uses the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) system.
Prognosis and prediction
Laryngeal LEC has a five-year disease-specific survival of approximately 65%
{ 26316257 ; 32867094 }. Regional lymph node metastasis occurs in 50-75% with distant
metastasis in 20-25% { 8912827 ; 32867094 }.

237

#25479

The tumour is composed of large cells with indistinct cell borders, round nuclei with
vesicular chromatin and prominent nucleoli, and an intense infiltrate of chronic
inflammatory cells.

#28609

The carcinoma shows strong nuclear expression of p40.

#25478

The tumour consists of large cells with indistinct cell borders, round nuclei with vesicular
chromatin and prominent nucleoli, and an intense infiltrate of chronic inflammatory cells.
Mitotic figures are numerous.

239

Mesenchymal tumours unique to the hypopharynx, larynx, trachea and parapharyngeal
spaceLaryngeal cartilaginous tumours
Definition
Chondroma is a benign mesenchymal tumour of laryngeal hyaline cartilage. Chondrosarcoma is a
malignant mesenchymal tumour of laryngeal hyaline cartilage.
ICD-O coding
9220/0 Chondroma
9220/3 Chondrosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2E90.6 & XH0NS4 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx and Chondroma, NOS
2B6B.Y & XH8J23 Other specified malignant neoplasms of nasopharynx & Chondrosarcoma,
NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Clear cell chondrosarcoma; Dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma
Localization
Cricoid cartilage is the most common site (about 70%) for laryngeal cartilaginous tumours, followed
by
the
thyroid,
arytenoid,
and
tracheal
cartilages
{ 9041812 ; 12131151 ; 15167045 ; 25183187 ; 25960446 }; epiglottis is rarely affected
{ 12131151 ; 28822621 }.
Clinical features
Both tumours grow slowly, producing an endolaryngeal mass, with symptoms being tumour site
and size dependent. Slowly progressive hoarseness, dyspnoea, dysphagia, and stridor are usually
present. Thyroid cartilage tumours may be a palpable mass { 12131151 ; 15167045 ; 25183187 }.
Imaging: High resolution contrast enhanced CT on laryngeal chondrosarcoma shows ring-like, arc,
or “popcorn” calcifications within an expansile, bulky, destructive intracartilaginous mass below an
intact mucosal surface. The tumour frequently expands outward into the perilaryngeal soft tissues.
MR
shows
a
hyperintense
mass
on
T2
fat
suppressed
STIR
protocol
{ 8456728 ; 10609482 ; 12131151 ; 15167045 ; 18495402 }. FDG-PET may help with tumour
grading, metastasis detection, and local recurrence assessment { 25183187 }.
Epidemiology
Cartilaginous tumours account for 0.2-0.5% of all laryngeal tumours, with chondrosarcomas, the
most common laryngeal sarcoma, being significantly more common than chondromas
{ 9041812 ; 15290669 ; 18607979 ; 24213203 ; 24474667 ; 25183187 ; 31256243 ; 33686585 }.
Chondromas occur across a wide patient age range (24–79 years; median 56 years), with a maleto-female ratio of 2:1 { 9041812 }. Chondrosarcomas develop in slightly older patients, with a wide
patient age range (25–91 years; median 62 years), with a male-to-female ratio of 3.2:1
{ 9041812 ; 12131151 ; 24474667 ; 25183187 ; 27291822 }. Chondrosarcomas are significantly
more common in whites than in blacks (7:1) { 15167045 }.
Etiology
The etiology is unknown, with several hypotheses: disordered ossification of hyaline cartilage in
areas of muscle insertion { 15167045 }; ischaemic changes in chondroma leading to malignant
transformation { 12131151 }. Other possible predisposing factors are radiotherapy,
polytetrafluoroethylene
(Teflon)
injection,
and
repeated
laryngeal
trauma
{ 1913528 ; 2721410 ; 25183187 }.
Pathogenesis
Isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 and 2 (IDH1 and IDH2) gene mutations are commonly seen in centraltype chondrosarcomas, but detected in only 12% of laryngotracheal cases tested, suggesting an
alternate tumourigenesis in laryngeal tumours { 30296521 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Both tumours present as smooth, lobulated, submucosal masses with an intact mucosa. The cut
surfaces are glassy, firm, white, or grey. Chondromas are <20 mm in greatest dimension, while

240

chondrosarcomas are up to 120 mm (median: 35 mm). Dedifferentiated chondrosarcomas have
foci with a fleshy appearance { 9041812 ; 12131151 ; 12160266 ; 15290669 }.
Histopathology
Chondromas show mature hyaline cartilage histologically resembling normal cartilage and are
hypocellular, with evenly distributed, bland-looking tumour cells in an abundant basophilic matrix.
Chondrocytes have small, uniform, single nuclei surrounded by eosinophilic cytoplasm, with only
one cell per lacuna. Cellular pleomorphism, mitoses, and binucleated chondrocytes are absent.
Scattered foci of calcification and ossification may be seen.
Chondrosarcomas show a gradient of increased cellularity, pleomorphism, multinucleation, and
mitoses, features used in tumour grading. Most laryngeal chondrosarcomas are low-grade (grade
1), showing a pattern of lobular disarray and destructive invasion of native cartilage and bone, with
tumour lobules permeating and entrapping pre-existing bone trabeculae. The cellularity is higher
than chondromas, with binucleation, slight nuclear pleomorphism, and nuclear hyperchromasia.
Moderately differentiated (grade 2) tumours show a higher degree of cellularity and nuclear
pleomorphism than do grade 1 tumours, and may have scattered mitoses. The myxoid pattern
{ 12131151 ; 23032857 } shows a "string of pearls"-like pattern of neoplastic cells in a myxoid
background. High-grade (grade 3) tumours have high cellularity; significant multinucleation, nuclear
pleomorphism, and hyperchromasia; tumour necrosis; and increased mitoses. High-grade
chondrosarcomas are rare, accounting for only about 5% of all laryngeal chondrosarcomas
{ 12131151 }. Ossification and calcification can be seen in all tumour grades
{ 9041812 ; 12131151 ; 15167045 }.
Subtypes: These are rare in the larynx. Clear cell chondrosarcoma shows a sharp transition of
conventional chondrosarcoma to a proliferation of tightly-packed, large polygonal clear tumour cells
with distinct cellular membranes but lacking typical, dense chondroid matrix
{ 11458227 ; 11859213 ; 24499430 ; 24715432 ; 30181407 }. Dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma
shows a biphasic appearance with well-differentiated chondrosarcoma juxtaposed to a high-grade
non-cartilaginous
sarcoma
{ 12131151 ; 12160266 ; 15024362 ; 24222068 ; 26718693 ; 28965627 ; 29160138 }. Mesenchy
mal chondrosarcoma (separately discussed in 7.5.2.2: mesenchymal chondrosarcoma), is an
extremely rare malignancy of the larynx with a biphasic small round blue cell proliferation
associated with islands of differentiated hyaline cartilage { 8958297 ; 28060373 }.
Immunohistochemistry: IHC is rarely necessary, but the chondroid cells are immunoreactive with
S100
protein,
SOX9,
and
D2-40,
while
negative
for
keratin
and
EMA
{ 10555005 ; 17021752 ; 19194275 ; 22944296 }.
Differential The major differential is between chondroma and using a size cutoff of >20 mm
suggested to favor chondrosarcoma. However, in incisional biopsy or needle samples, distinction
may be difficult. Some low-grade chondrosarcomas of the larynx may better be diagnosed as an
atypical cartilaginous neoplasm as utilized in other sites { 18852676 }, but without supporting
evidence for laryngeal tumours specifically, this terminology is not advocated. Chondrometaplasia
usually shows multiple elastic cartilage nodules affecting the vocal cord that blend into the adjacent
stroma { 29202349 }. A spindle cell squamous carcinoma may have heterologous benign or
malignant cartilage elements, but the cartilage is set within a malignant spindled epithelial cell
population { 11812937 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Chondroma
Essential: lobular cartilaginous tumour without pleomorphism, destructive growth, and/or
binucleation
Desirable: Tumour size < 20 mm
Chondrosarcoma

241

Essential: malignant cartilaginous neoplasm with lobular cluster disarray with destructive invasion
of native cartilage and/or bone; increased cellularity with binucleation, nuclear pleomorphism, and
nuclear hyperchromasia
Desirable: imaging findings of an expansile, destructive intracartilaginous mass, usually with ringlike, arc or “popcorn” calcifications
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification for
head and neck sarcomas (chondrosarcoma).
Prognosis and prediction
The 1-year, 5-year, and 10-year disease-specific survival rates for laryngeal chondrosarcoma are
97%, 90%, and 85%, respectively, although the local recurrence rate is relatively high (18–50%),
usually due to incomplete resection { 12131151 ; 24474667 ; 27291822 }. Tumour location, grade,
subtype, and therapy do not seem to influence outcome (other than possibly for dedifferentiated
tumours) { 15024362 ; 27291822 }, which encourages conservative, function-preserving surgery
as primary treatment { 12131151 ; 15290669 ; 24700301 }. Distant metastases are exceedingly
rare { 24213203 }.

Sialolipoma

Introduction
The major and minor salivary glands are affected by a remarkable diversity of neoplasms. The
reclassification of salivary gland tumours has seen unparalleled changes in recent years.
Compared with tumours of other organs/systems, salivary gland neoplasms display one of the
highest morphologic, phenotypic, and genotypic diversity encountered in any single-organ system.
Given the number of already existing entities which show considerable overlap of histologic and
immunohistochemical features between different salivary gland neoplasms, only very well
documented new entities have been accepted in this edition. Reported tumours and different
morphologies lacking consensus support and validation by independent investigators have not
been included. This approach resulted in inclusion of microsecretory adenocarcinoma and

250

sclerosing microcystic adenocarcinoma as the new malignant tumour entities; and keratocystoma,
intercalated duct adenoma, and striated duct adenoma as new benign tumour entities. Further, the
neoplastic nature of sclerosing polycystic adenoma moved the lesion from a non-neoplastic
epithelial lesion (WHO 2017) into the benign neoplasm category.
Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) is an emerging entity comprising duct-centric
tumours with low-grade mucinous morphology; they share with mucinous adenocarcinoma (MA)
frequent AKT1 mutations. It is still not established if IPMN should be classified separately or within
the MA spectrum as a potential precursor { 29738361 ; 31219819 ; 31505033 }.
Molecular testing of salivary gland tumours for differential diagnostic accuracy and appropriate
clinical management has become routine in many developed countries { 29076877 ; 27523965 }.
Since the lWHO 2017 edition, molecular data has become widely reported, with many salivary
gland neoplasms shown to harbour tumour-specific rearrangements (Table #28682). The most
common molecular alterations were included in the definition of the following entities:
mucoepidermoid carcinoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma, secretory carcinoma, polymorphous
adenocarcinoma, hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma, mucinous adenocarcinoma, and microsecretory
adenocarcinoma.
Cytological findings have been included in most sections, in recognition of the importance of fine
needle aspiration (FNA) as an initial diagnostic approach, with the Milan system
recommended [[Faquin WC and Rossi ED, eds. Milan System for Reporting Salivary Gland
Cytopathology ]]. While FNA has emerged as the mainstay of the diagnostic workup of salivary
gland-type tumours, needle core biopsies are still performed occasionally, especially after
nondiagnostic aspirates. While offering more architectural information than FNAs, most core
biopsies do not allow for assessment of the interface between the tumour and the surrounding
tissues and thus, are insufficient to distinguish between neoplasms (i.e., myoepithelioma vs
myoepithelial carcinoma). Only full resected specimens allow for diagnostic clarity in such cases.
The histologic grading of salivary gland carcinomas has been shown to be an independent predictor
of behaviour and plays a role in optimizing therapy. Still, most salivary gland carcinomas have an
intrinsic biologic behaviour, and attempted application of a universal grading scheme is not
recommended { 21169736 }. Carcinoma types for which validated grading systems exist include
adenoid cystic carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma, and adenocarcinoma, not otherwise
specified { 30500293 }. High-grade transformation (HGT) has been shown to be an important
concept of tumour progression in salivary gland carcinomas { 33825717 }. The importance of this
phenomenon is that tumours demonstrating HGT show an aggressive clinical course that deviates
significantly from the usual behaviour for a given tumour type. HGT is included in the description of
appropriate entities.
The following controversial issues remain:
Are intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) and mucinous adenocarcinoma related; are
they part of a single spectrum; is IPMN a precursor or preneoplastic condition? Is IPMN related to
ductal papilloma? Further work and clarification are needed. Clarification around intraductal
carcinoma, given recent data showing the myoepithelial layer is part of the tumour and so these
may be biphasic neoplasms rather than truly in-situ neoplasms { 33086236 }, and how to classify
the tumour if invasion is noted. There is no consensus about the existence of oncocytic
carcinoma. Oncocytic appearance is a common change encountered in many different salivary
gland tumours. In the past, carcinomas consisting entirely of oncocytes were frequently
diagnosed as oncocytic carcinoma. Molecular studies have now shown many such tumours to be
oncocytic variants of other salivary carcinomas. As such, it is unclear if oncocytic carcinoma exist
as an independent entity. For this reason, it has been included in the emerging entity chapter. It is
not clear whether the sarcomatous component of salivary gland carcinosarcoma represents a
true sarcoma or an epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition in the carcinomatous component. For
this edition, it has remained as a separate entity.
