257

Non-neoplastic epithelial lesions

Nodular oncocytic hyperplasia
Definition
Nonneoplastic salivary gland lesion characterised by unencapsulated nodular proliferations of
epithelial oncocytes and/or clear cells.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA04.1 Hypertrophy of salivary gland
Related terminology
Acceptable: multifocal nodular oncocytic hyperplasia, nodular oncocytosis, clear cell oncocytosis
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Nodular oncocytic hyperplasia (NOH) is almost exclusively found in the parotid gland, rarely seen
in the submandibular gland { 2361662 ; 28411376 ; 28247227 }.
Clinical features
Patients with NOH present as a palpable painless mass, often present for years
{ 2361662 ; 28411376 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies show loss of normal architecture, usually with multiple nodules of various
sizes, but is not diagnostic { 18272563 }.
Epidemiology
NOH comprises < 1% of salivary tumours but accounts for approximately 7% of bilateral salivary
lesions { 17718138 ; 21689893 }. Peak incidence is in the 5th and 6th decades with a slight female
predilection { 28247227 }.
Etiology
Although the inciting factor is unknown the oncocytic phenotype is associated with accumulation of
aberrant mitochondria and mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) mutations { 20732299 ; 24614700 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
NOH displays multiple brown well circumscribed but uncapsulated nodules. A partly encapsulated
larger mass may indicate an oncocytoma arising in NOH { 18357950 }.
Histopathology
NOH consists of irregularly shaped unencapsulated nodules of oncocytes in solid to
tubulotrabecular
patterns
distributed
throughout salivary
parenchyma
{ 2361662 ; 17718138 ; 21689893 }. Lobular architecture is preserved. Clear cell change is
frequent and can form small satellite foci that mimic invasive clear cell tumours. Unifocality and
encapsulation can separate NOH from oncocytoma. Entrapped normal acini, ducts, and adipocytes
within nodules and non-perivascular p63 positive basal cells distinguish NOH from acinic cell
carcinoma or metastatic renal cell carcinoma { 19646823 ; 20614263 }.
Differential Absence of MAML2 rearrangement can rule out oncocytic mucoepidermoid carcinoma
{ 33002921 }. Negativity for SOX10, S100 and PLAG1 rearrangement helps distinguish from
pleomorphic adenoma and myoepithelioma with oncocytic metaplasia { 32673681 }.
Cytology
Smears are hypocellular with small to medium tissue fragments of oncocytic cells, with well defined
cytoplasmic margins and coarsely granular dense cytoplasm, in a clean background, although
these features overlap with oncocytoma, which cannot be distinguished from NOH, and the
oncocytic cells seen in Warthin’s tumour, although that has lymphoid cells in a dense proteinaceous
granular background { 28411376 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

258

Essential: circumscribed but uncapsulated nodules of oncocytic and/or clear cells
Desirable: multifocal occurrence
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
NOH is a nonneoplastic lesion and recurrence is rare, although it is frequently multifocal and
bilateral. Clear cell change may indicate increased risk for recurrence { 2031528 }. Malignant
transformation has not been reported.

#25717
Nodular oncocytic hyperplasia
Nodules of oncocytes in the salivary gland parenchyma.

#28059
Nodular oncocytic hyperplasia
Extensive nodules of oncocytes in the parotid gland parenchyma.
259

#28060

Nodular oncocytic hyperplasia
Parotid gland showing multiple separate clear-cell nodules.

#28061
Nodular oncocytic hyperplasia
High power view of nodules in parotid gland parenchyma demonstrating oncocytic cells
with granular cytoplasm.

260

#28048
Nodular oncocytic hyperplasia
There are multiple nodules of oncocytically altered cells within this salivary gland. They
are not encapsulated.

Lymphoepithelial sialadenitis
Definition
Lymphoepithelial sialadenitis is a chronic, systemic, lymphocytic autoimmune inflammation of
salivary glands causing acinar parenchymal atrophy and formation of lymphoepithelial lesions.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E91.1 Benign lymphoepithelial lesion of salivary gland
Related terminology
Not recommended: benign lymphoepithelial lesion; myoepithelial sialadenitis; Mikulicz disease
Subtype(s)
None
261

Localization
Typically, all salivary (and other mucosal) glands are affected, with minor glands mostly
microscopically without enlargement, while parotid glands often show bilateral enlargement.
Clinical features
Clinical symptoms of sicca-syndrome (dry mouth, dry eye), frequently associated with bilateral
parotid swelling.
Epidemiology
Patients are predominantly females (female-to-male ratio 4:1) in their 6th-7th decade.
Etiology
Multiglandular lymphoepithelial sialadenitis is usually a histological correlate of the autoimmune
Sjögren disease, characterized by progressive mucosal sicca-syndrome, either as isolated
disease or in combination with other autoimmune diseases, most frequently rheumatoid arthritis.
The etiology is unclear { 10335942 ; 17978770 }.
Pathogenesis
Not clinically relevant
Macroscopic appearance
Not clinically relevant
Histopathology
Early lesions show minor to moderate degree of T-cell-dominated periductal lymphocytic infiltration,
rarely with reactive follicles, usually without lymphoepithelial lesions, typically encountered in minor
labial glands, taken for diagnostic purpose of suspected Sjögren disease { 16891656 }. A positive
“focus score“ is defined as at least one dense, periductal aggregate of more than 50 lymphocytes,
representing a histological criterion within a diagnostic score { 12006334 ; 27789466 }. Focus score
is calculated as the number of lymphocytic foci per 4 mm2. Cases with ductal dilatation,
predominantly diffuse lymphocytic infiltration, and fibrosis, however, rather represent non-specific,
for example traumatic or obstructive sialadenitis of minor/labial glands.
Advanced lesions typically manifest as tumourous enlargement of parotid (rarely submandibular or
lacrimal) glands. They show intense, B-cell dominated, lymphoplasmacytic infiltrates with
preserved lobular architecture, variable, often massive degree of parenchymal atrophy, multiple
reactive follicles, and multiple, densely aggregated, partly cystic lymphoepithelial lesions. The
pathognomonic lymphoepithelial lesions (“islands“) consist of a netlike proliferation of basaloid
ductal
cells
and
permeating
(B-cell)
lymphocytes
{ 9894474 ; 10335942 ; 16891656 ; 17978770 }. Monocytoid B-cells typically constitute narrow,
bright halos around lymphoepithelial lesions, while intensely expanded and confluent halos might
indicate progression to extranodal marginal zone lymphoma (MALT lymphoma; see chapter
9.0.4.1.). Plasma cells may be frequent.
Immunohistochemistry: A keratin stain can be helpful for pattern analysis of preserved glandular
architecture
including
frequency
and
distribution
of
lymphoepithelial
lesions
{ 8917715 ; 10335942 }. Immunohistological staining for kappa/lambda light chains or,
alternatively, in situ hybridization detecting corresponding mRNA, may demonstrate their polyclonal
nature in lymphoepithelial sialadenitis, while molecular evidence of B-cell monoclonality may occur,
but in the absence of further histological evidence does not necessarily represent a malignant
transformation. There is no association with EBV { 9894474 ; 17978770 }.
Differential Sclerosis, obliterative phlebitis, and numerous IgG4-positive plasma cells, as seen in
IgG4-associated sialadenitis of submandibular glands, are absent { 21438912 }. HIV-associated
cystic lymphoepithelial lesion is histologically similar but is characterized by bizarre, enlarged
follicles and massive cystically dilated lymphoepithelial lesions { 2122582 ; 8917715 }. Salivary
lymphoepithelial carcinoma may show irregular lymphoepithelial lesions in lobular arrangement,
but represents a bluntly infiltrating neoplasia with increased proliferation, cellular atypia, and
presence of EBV in a significant subset of cases { 32462279 }. Clinical information is usually
indispensable for correct differential diagnosis.
Cytology
Smears are cellular with a mixed lymphoid population including lymphocytes, larger lymphoid cells,
dendritic cells and tingible body macrophages from germinal centers, and plasma cells, along with
a small number of cohesive ductal tissue fragments infiltrated by lymphocytes { 28411376 }. The

262

epithelial cells may show squamous metaplasia. Lymphoma with its often more homogeneous
lymphoid population, and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma with its necrosis and more
prominent nuclear pleomorphism are the differential diagnosis.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: dense benign lymphocytic infiltrate, in salivary glands, with lymphoepithelial lesions
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
The degree of lymphocytic infiltration, parenchymal atrophy, and corresponding clinical siccasyndrome is usually slowly progressive, lacking theurapeutic options. Approximately 4% progress
to marginal zone (MALT) lymphoma, mostly unilateral in parotid glands.

#24502

Lymphoepithelial sialadenitis
Minor labial gland with four foci of dense periductal lymphocytic infiltration, representing a
positive focus-score in a female with clinical Sjögren Syndrome.

263

#24503
Lympoepithelial sialadenitis
Fully-developed parotid lymphoepithelial sialadenitis with massive lymphocytic infiltration
(Haemalum: blue), total parenchymal atrophy, and preserved lobular architecture, with
multiple lymphoepithelial lesions (CK14: brown), and reactive follicles (Ki67: red),
alltogether representing an entirely reactive histological pattern (clinically Sjögren
Syndrome).

#25680
Lymphoepithelial sialadenitis of the parotid gland
Dense small lymphocytic infiltration accompanied by lymphoepithelial lesions (“islands”).

264

Benign epithelial tumours

Pleomorphic adenoma
Definition
Pleomorphic adenoma (PA) is a benign epithelial tumour characterized by cytomorphological and
architectural diversity with an admixture of ductal and myoepithelial cells usually embedded in a
chondromyxoid or fibrous stromal component.
ICD-O coding
8940/0 Pleomorphic adenoma
8940/3 Metastasizing pleomorphic adenoma
ICD-11 coding
2E91 & XA5T23 & XH2KC1 Benign neoplasms of major salivary glands & salivary gland
apparatus & pleomorphic adenoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: pleomorphic salivary adenoma; benign mixed tumour
Subtype(s)
Metastasizing pleomorphic adenoma; oncocytic pleomorphic adenoma
Localization
Most PAs arise in the parotid gland (~70-80%), followed by oral cavity and submandibular gland
{ 3744850 ; 4009321 ; 19926180 ; 26382619 ; 28249655 ; 33044721 }. In the oral cavity, the
palate is the most common location { 33044721 }. Rare locations include: sinonasal tract, skull
base, external auditory canal, larynx { 9282462 ; 9339807 ; 15104293 ; 25712714 ; 30122650 }.
Clinical features
Generally PA presents as a slowly growing painless mass. Deep parotid lobe tumours present as
parapharyngeal space masses; parotid tail tumours as level II neck lesions; minor salivary glands
PAs may present with dysphagia, airway obstruction, or obstructive sleep apnea { 19926180 }.
Recurrent PAs present as multinodular disease in the surgical bed or surrounding soft
tissues { 25289881 }. Malignant transformation can be suggested by rapid growth and facial nerve
symptoms.
Metastasizing PA is a rare subtype that occurs after multiple recurrences and must be distinguished
from malignancy. Metastases are most commonly seen in bone, lung, and neck lymph nodes
{ 1384375 ; 25958295 ; 31094927 }.
Imaging: MR imaging of PA demonstrates a number of typical features: lobulated tumour with welldefined margins, extremely T2W hyperintense, intermediate signal on T1W and avidly enhance
following intravenous contrast. PAs have high ADC valves on Diffusion Weighted Imaging (DWI) of
around 1.6. On ultrasound, PAs are hypoechoic with through-transmission, have lobulated welldefined margins and demonstrate internal vascularity. Use of doppler imaging is mandatory to avoid
mislabeling them as cysts. Although these features are characteristic of PA they are also seen in
other pathology and cytology/ histology is required to confirm the diagnosis { 32943368 }.
Epidemiology
PA is the most common salivary gland neoplasm worldwide, accounting for 50-70% of all salivary
gland tumours { 18620785 ; 19951834 ; 26382619 ; 27217890 ; 28249655 ; 33044721 }. With an
estimated incidence of 4.2-4.9 per 100,000 person-years, PAs occur across a wide age range with
a peak incidence in the 5th and 6th decades and slight female predilection { 26382619 ; 28249655 }.
Metastasizing PA is a rare subtype indistinguishable from the primary tumour, but with biopsyproven metastasis { 1384375 ; 31094927 }. The mean age of presentation is 49.5 years and the
mean interval between primary tumour diagnosis and metastatic disease is 14.9 years (range 0–
51) { 1384375 ; 25958295 }. It often occurs after multiple recurrences { 25958295 }.
Etiology
The etiology is unknown, but radiation exposure and estrogen / progesterone have been suggested
as risk factors { 9611101 ; 28249655 ; 33247629 }.
Pathogenesis
PAs harbour recurrent translocations or intrachromosomal rearrangements resulting in gene
fusions involving PLAG1 on 8q12 (>50%) or HMGA2 on 12q14.3 (10-15%) { 23821214 }. Six
recurrent fusion partner genes for PLAG1 have been reported: CTNNB1, FGFR1, LIFR, CHCHD7,

265

TCEA1, NFIB, BOC, while HMGA2 fuses with one of four recurrent fusion partners: NFIB, WIF1,
FHIT, TMTC2 { 9020842 ; 9525740 ; 10029085 ; 18828159 ; 31094927 ; 32654217 ; 33710800 }.
Oncocytic PAs have been shown to contain PLAG1 gene fusions with GEM, CHCHD7, NFT3,
FBXO32, and C1orf116 { 32673681 }. Concurrent or isolated HMGA2 amplification have also been
reported { 18828159 ; 34324456 }. Metastasizing PA is said to arise from embolism of benign
tumour cells.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are well-circumscribed with smooth, lobular or bosselated borders. The cut surface may
be tan, chondroid white, or gelatinous. Cystic changes, hemorrhage and infarction can occur.
Histopathology
The histological hallmark of PA is its morphological diversity derived from an admixture of bilayered
ducts, myoepithelial cells, and stroma. PAs are well delineated and/or encapsulated. The capsule
may be absent in minor salivary gland and chondromyxoid-predominant PAs.
The ductal and myoepithelial cells are commonly arranged in bilayered tubular structures with the
myoepithelial cells melting with the stroma. The cellular phenotype, particularly of the myoepithelial
cells, is broad and includes epithelioid, basaloid, stellate, spindled, clear cell, oncocytic and
plasmacytoid morphology. The stroma can be mucoid, myxoid, hyalinized, chondroid, osseous or
lipomatous. The proportion of the three components vary in each tumour; and one element may
predominate. Chondromyxoid-predominant stroma-rich lesions with few cellular structures, can
mimic true cartilaginous neoplasms. In cellular PAs the ductal and/or the myoepithelial cells are
dominant over the stromal elements. Despite the increased cellularity, invasion is absent and
immunohistochemical markers can highlight the biphasic nature of the tumour { 33368685 }. Nonneoplastic acini merging at the periphery, capsular bosselation, pseudopodia, satellite nodules,
multinodular
growth
are
not
indicators
of
malignancy
{ 11802025 ; 17252593 ; 19898859 ; 33368685 }. Intravascular tumour cells, possibly artifactually
displaced during biopsy, should not be interpreted as angioinvasion { 9253625 ; 23073326 }.
Bizarre tumour cells (ancient atypia) can be observed; and infarct-type necrosis can occur after
biopsy { 7800377 ; 10594846 ; 10629137 }.
Most recurrent PAs exhibit multiple discrete round nodules of variable sizes and chondromyxoid
morphology contained within residual salivary gland or soft tissues { 15720991 ; 25289881 }. The
term 'atypical PA' has been used to describe tumours with a features suggestive of malignancy but
insufficient for a diagnosis of carcinoma ex-PA (CXPA). Careful microscopic evaluation and
extensive sampling is necessary in such cases { 8782203 }.
Metastasizing PA is histologically and molecularly indistinguishable from a benign tumour at a
primary location { 1384375 ; 31094927 }. No histologic or molecular features can reliably predict
metastasis { 25958295 }.
The oncocytic subtype is mainly composed of cells with abundant eosinophilic granular cytoplasm
{ 10394890 }.
Immunohistochemistry: PLAG1 { 28796899 }, and HMGA2 { 28463429 }, respectively, are
emerging as sensitive, and specific IHC markers for pleomorphic adenoma.
Immunoexpression of S100 and SOX10 helps to differentiate oncocytic PA from other oncocytic
tumours { 32673681 }.
Differential PAs can show squamous metaplasia and mucocytes, and mimic squamous cell
carcinoma (SCC) or mucoepidermoid carcinoma (MEC) { 9736432 }. PA can be distinguished from
SCC by the absence of infiltrative growth, marked pleomorphism, and increased mitoses; and
separated from MEC by the identification of well-formed keratin pearls, presence of myoepithelial
differentiation, and absence of MAML2 rearrangements { 24121173 }. PA can exhibit cribriform
architecture mimicking adenoid cystic carcinoma, and areas of compact biphasic tubular structures
reminiscent of epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma { 33368685 }. Myoepithelial carcinoma is often
cytologically bland and may therefore be under-recognized as malignant and misclassified as a
myoepithelial-rich PA { 30789358 }.
Cytology
Usually highly cellular smears show variable amounts of the characteristic fibrillary matrix, admixed
with myoepithelial cells, that can be polygonal, plasmacytoid, spindled, clear or round with bland

266

round to oval nuclei, and ductal cells { 8732648 }. The myoepithelial cells can be seen in the stroma,
and merge into the epithelial component. When the diagnostic fibrillary stroma is scant, the
differential diagnosis from adenoid cystic carcinoma can be difficult, and squamous metaplasia can
raise the possibility of high grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma with its high grade nuclei, but is rare
in adenoid cystic carcinomas { 8960024 ; 32767837 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not applicable
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: admixture of bilayered ducts, myoepithelial cells, and chondromyxoid/fibrous stroma in
the absence of invasion and malignant cytomorphological features
Desirable: PLAG1 or HMGA2 alterations (in variants or diagnostically challenging cases)
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Complete surgical resection is curative and the treatment of choice. Recurrences occur in 2.9%6.7%
of
cases,
mostly
secondary
to
rupture
and
incomplete
tumour
excision { 15720991 ; 26382619 ; 28249655 }. The overall rate of malignant transformation is
difficult to assess since many CXPA are diagnosed without a prior clinical history of a PA
{ 24804831 }. In recurrent PAs, the rate of malignant transformation is around 3%
{ 26382619 ; 28249655 }. Out of 51 cases of metastasizing PA with reported survival data, 9
(17.6%) died secondary to the disease and 41 (80.4%) were alive at 1-year. Multiple metastases
appear to confer a poor prognosis { 25958295 }.

Pleomorphic adenoma
Capsular invasion by pseudopodia.

#27736

Classical marked hyperintensity (similar to CSF) on a T2 weighted fat suppressed

#27737
Pleomorphic adenoma
The classical histologic features are seen, with a chondromyxoid matrix blending
imperceptibly with the myoepithelial cells and showing small ducts in the background.

270

#27738
Pleomorphic adenoma
The imperceptible blending of the components of pleomorphic adenoma are an aid to
diagnosis. Small ducts are seen, surrounding by myoepithelial cells that merge into the
adjacent chondromyxoid matrix.

#27740
Pleomorphic adenoma
Clinically recurrent tumours demonstrate numerous nodules of pleomorphic adenoma set
within fibrous connective tissue and fat. The cellularity is variable. There is no destructive
infiltration and there is a lack of pleomorphism.

271

#27741
Pleomorphic adenoma
Many tumours are sampled by core needle biopsy. When all of the elements are present,
a diagnosis can be made, recognizing sampling limitations.

#27742
Pleomorphic adenoma
High power of the core needle sample demonstrates the characteristic features of
pleomorphic adenoma.

#27743
Pleomorphic adenoma
Use as a composite guide for the 2 core needle biopsy samples (Core1 and Core2).

#27744
Pleomorphic adenoma
Tumours show a remarkable variation in appearance, with a prominent spindled cell
myoepithelial component illustrated. Focal squamous metaplasia is present.

273

#27745
Pleomorphic adenoma
Calcification and even bone formation may be seen in pleomorphic adenoma, often in
those present for a long clinical duration.

Palate tumours very frequently show a predominant plasmacytoid population, although
small ducts can be seen in this tumour.

Radially arranged collagen fibers, sometimes called collagenous crystalloids, are
frequently seen in pleomorphic adenoma.

Axial ultrasound images show a lesion in the right parotid tail. This is predominantly

margins. Internal vascularity is demonstrated on Doppler imaging.

Axial MR images of the same patient confirm a lesion in the posterior aspect of the right

#26939

Pleomorphic adenoma

Basal cell adenoma
Definition
Basal cell adenoma (BCA) is a benign biphasic salivary gland neoplasm composed of basaloid and
luminal cells, and often containing basement membrane material.
ICD-O coding
8147/0 Basal cell adenoma
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z & XH60D1 Benign neoplasm of major salivary glands, unspecified & basal cell adenoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: monomorphic adenoma; dermal analogue tumour
Subtype(s)
Membranous basal cell adenoma
Localization
BCAs are largely restricted to major salivary glands, especially to the parotid gland (>80%)
{ 4009321 ; 11890618 ; 19951834 ; 27769738 ; 30528989 ; 33526221 }.
Clinical features
BCA usually presents as a well defined, mobile solitary mass { 33526221 }, though syndromic
cases
may
be
multiple
and
associated
with
dermal
cylindromas or
trichoepitheliomas { 19455704 }.
Epidemiology
BCA account
for
~1-4%
of
all
salivary
gland
tumours
{ 4009321 ; 11890618 ; 19951834 ; 27769738 ; 30528989 ; 33526221 },
presenting
more
frequently in the 6th-7th decades with a slight female predilection.
Etiology
Some
BCAs occur
in
the
setting
of
familial/multiple
cylindromatosis
syndromes { 12023583 ; 33526221 }.
Pathogenesis
CTNNB1 alterations
are
often
present,
usually
in
tubulotrabecular BCA
{ 27259009 ; 29224720 ; 29496310 ; 33526221 }. CYLD1 alterations are also common, more so
with membranous BCA { 29463883 ; 33526221 }.
Macroscopic appearance
BCAs are solitary and well-circumscribed, (range: 2–55 mm) { 25141971 }, the membranous type
may be multifocal. They show a homogenous solid grey texture, although cystic change is not
uncommon { 22978388 }.
Histopathology
BCAs are encapsulated or well circumscribed and show tubulotrabecular, cribriform, membranous
or solid growth. A subset shows a distinct spindled S100-positive “myoepithelial cell-derived stromal
component” { 3528058 ; 26337214 }. The tumour shows peripheral palisading of dark cells with
luminal
paler
cells
and
ducts.
Nuclei
are
vesicular
{ 19454360 ; 22978388 ; 24206768 ; 25141971 ; 26045798 }. A lipomatous subtype is described
{ 31869766 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Epithelial and myoepithelial markers highlight the dual cell composition
{ 23209336 ; 24299520 }. Coexpression of nuclear β-catenin and LEF-1 is detected in
BCAs { 21323741 ; 25497834 ; 27259009 ; 29224720 }.
Differential BCAs are distinguished from cellular pleomorphic adenoma by their basaloid
appearance, peripheral palisading and lack of blending with associated myoepithelial type stroma
{ 28060371 }. BCA are differentiated from basal cell adenocarcinoma (BCAC) by the absence of
invasion.
Cytology
Smears are usually markedly cellular with small sheets and round, trabecular or solid tissue
fragments consisting of monomorphic small basaloid cells, with scant cytoplasm and frequent
naked nuclei in the clean background with stripped round nuclei showing uniform fine
hyperchromasia { 11575660 }. A dense thin layer of hyaline stroma most typically surrounds the

281

basaloid tissue fragments and interdigitates the cells peripherally, and small rounded droplets of
stroma may be seen, but no myxofibrillary stroma is seen. Squamoid and sebaceous features may
be seen focally { 8701930 }. Separating BCA from BCAC is difficult on FNAB, and the differential
diagnosis from adenoid cystic carcinoma is not always possible, and the term 'basaloid neoplasm'
is often appropriate.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: noninvasive; biphasic basaloid morphology; peripheral palisading
Desirable: dual population immunophenotype; nuclear beta-catenin reactivity
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence rates are low (<2%); except for the membranous subtype (~25%). Malignant
transformation can occur { 9198102 }, more frequently with the membranous subtype { 1872522 }.

Basal cell adenoma
Most tumours are encapsulated.

Warthin tumour
Definition
Warthin tumour is a benign salivary gland tumour composed of oncocytic epithelial cells lining
ductal, papillary and cystic structures in a lymphoid stroma.
ICD-O coding
8561/0 Warthin tumour
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z & XA5T23 & XH9ZB2 Benign neoplasm of major salivary glands, unspecified & salivary
gland apparatus & Adenolymphoma
Related terminology
Not
recommended:
adenolymphoma;
papillary
cystadenoma
lymphomatosum;
cystadenolymphoma
Subtype(s)
Infarcted/metaplastic Warthin tumour
Localization
Tumours are almost exclusively restricted to parotid glands, especially the inferior pole, and periparotid lymph nodes { 9578258 ; 8431414 }; fewer than 1% are found in the deep lobe
{ 28013343 }. Tumours are multifocal in 12-20% of patients, either synchronously or
metachronously, and are bilateral in 5-17% { 11755819 ; 17050316 }. They may be associated with
other salivary tumour types { 2917323 ; 32331963 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with painless, slow-growing and fluctuant swellings. Pain or facial nerve palsy may
occur in metaplastic / infarcted variants { 2743609 ; 3458128 }.
Imaging: Characteristic imaging findings include multifocality, parotid tail location, cystic change,
and avidity on PET CT { 25523505 }.
Epidemiology
Warthin tumour accounts for 5-20% of all salivary tumours, except in black Africans where it is rare
{ 24455085 }. The mean age at diagnosis is 62 (range 12-92) { 6304434 }. The male to female ratio
has shifted in Europe and North America from 10:1 in 1953 { 6304434 } to almost equal nowadays
{ 3458128 }; however, a 2018 study found the 10:1 ratio remains true in China { 29627151 }.
Etiology
Warthin tumours, especially when bilateral, have been linked strongly to cigarette smoking
{ 8678050 ; 17050316 ; 18361448 }. Other suggested risk factors include radiation exposure in
atomic bomb survivors { 9118025 }, and autoimmune diseases { 9288224 ; 25814196 }, especially
thyroiditis. Some studies have suggested a role for IgG4 { 24565204 } and various viruses, but this
is still unproven { 29224806 }.
Pathogenesis
Warthin tumour probably arises from salivary ductal inclusions in parotid lymph nodes
{ 23868565 ; 32865726 }. Clonality studies have suggested a non-neoplastic nature
{ 11112212 ; 15861216 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Most are well-circumscribed spherical-oval masses, 20 - 50 mm in diameter, although one example
measured 200 mm { 31742077 }. Solid areas and multiple cysts with papillary projections are
apparent on cut surface.
Histopathology
Warthin tumour (WT) is composed of varying proportions of papillary-cystic structures lined by
bilayered oncocytic epithelial cells, surrounded by a lymphoid stroma including germinal centres.
The epithelial component comprises inner columnar and outer cuboidal cells. Limited foci of
squamous, mucous, ciliated and sebaceous cells can be present.
Subtype: In infarcted/metaplastic subtype of WT the bilayered epithelium is replaced by squamous
metaplastic epithelium with no atypia. Mucinous metaplasia may also be present
{ 24121173 ; 10583558 }. Infarcted/metaplastic subtype which may follow FNA or trauma, show
291

areas of necrosis, in which a ghost papillary architecture may be discerned (highlighted with
reticulin staining). There is usually marked squamous (less often, mucinous) metaplasia and a
stromal reaction, including granuloma formation { 2743609 ; 10583558 ; 10631719 }.
Differential The main differential diagnosis of the metaplastic WT subtype is Warthin-like
mucoepidermoid carcinoma; however, Warthin tumours lack MAML2 gene rearrangement
{ 24121173 ; 32222825 }.
Cytology
Smears show a characteristic triad of small cohesive sheets of oncocytes, with abundant granular
cytoplasm, well defined cytoplasmic margins and central rounded nuclei with a prominent single
nucleolus, as well as, numerous lymphocytes and a lesser number of larger lymphoid cells, and
granular background debris { 9099541 }. The proportion of the 3 components can vary. Diagnostic
challenges occur when there are metaplastic changes including squamous and mucinous
metaplasia raising a differential diagnosis with mucoepidermoid carcinoma, which lacks tissue
fragments of bland oncocytic cells, but may have a cystic background and a lymphoid component
{ 9285198 ; 32767837 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not directly relevant, but absence of MAML2 alteration can exclude mucoepidermoid
carcinoma { 24121173 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: circumscribed mass with spaces lined by papillary bilayered oncocytic cells; lymphoid
stroma.
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Complete surgical excision with an adequate margin is usually curative { 25543869 }. Uncommon
local recurrences are probably due to multifocal tumours or inadequate excision { 18550337 }.
Malignant transformation in Warthin tumour is rare; a few examples have been reported of both
epithelial { 7965888 ; 11026102 ; 11683932 ; 15455232 ; 21877991 ; 29627151 } and lymphoid
neoplasms { 16045788 ; 26119391 ; 26169920 }.

Smear showing group of epithelial cells in a lymphoid background.

Oncocytoma
Definition
Oncocytoma is a benign encapsulated neoplasm composed of large epithelial cells with abundant
eosinophilic granular cytoplasm due to accumulation of mitochondria.
ICD-O coding
8290/0 Oncocytoma
ICD-11 coding
2E91.1 & XH9Z86 Benign neoplasm of other specified major salivary glands & Oxyphilic
adenoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: oncocytic adenoma, oxyphilic adenoma
Subtype(s)
Clear cell oncocytoma
Localization
Over 80% of cases occur in the parotid, approximately 10% in the submandibular gland, and the
rest in minor salivary glands and sublingual gland { 2031528 ; 2361662 ; 8940996 ; 12087334 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms depend on anatomical location. Usually presents as a unilateral painless swelling.
Epidemiology
Oncocytomas represent fewer than 1.5% of salivary gland tumours. Incidence peak is in the
7th decade of life, with no gender predilection { 2031528 ; 2361662 }. Tumours may be multifocal
and bilateral { 2917323 ; 18357950 ; 22627459 }
Etiology
A history of radiotherapy or long-term radiation exposure has been reported { 2031528 }. The
oncocytic phenotype is associated with mitochondrial (mtDNA) mutations { 12087334 ; 20732299 }.
Pathogenesis
Oncocytoma may arise in background of multinodular oncocytic hyperplasia.
Macroscopic appearance
Usually small, single and lobulated well-circumscribed brownish tumours with or without cystic
component and/or central fibrosis.
Histopathology
Oncocytomas are well-circumscribed tumours consisting of oncocytes with abundant eosinophilic
granular cytoplasm, arranged in nests and sheets, separated by a thin fibrovascular stroma. The
nuclei are uniform, vesicular and centrally placed, with prominent single nucleoli. Dark cells with
pyknotic nuclei, likely representing degenerated oncocytes are present. Tumours lack entrapped
normal parenchyma.
Subtype: Clear cell oncocytoma-Presence of glycogen and fixation artefacts cause clear change
{ 3402976 }.
Immunohistochemstry and Differential The presence of p63 positive basal cells excludes
metastatic renal cell carcinoma and acinic cell carcinoma { 20614263 ; 19646823 }. Absence of
PAX2 and/or PAX8 expression favours oncocytoma over metastatic renal carcinoma
{ 24771139 }. MAML2 rearrangement helps distinguishing oncocytic mucoepidermoid carcinoma
{ 33002921 }. SOX10 and S100 protein immunopositivity, and PLAG1 gene rearrangement
distinguishes pleomorphic adenoma and myoepithelioma with extensive oncocytic metaplasia
{ 32673681 }.
Cytology
The smears of oncocytoma are indistinguishable from nodular oncocytic hyperplasia { 31051720 }.
The nuclei tend to be round and with prominent nucleoli. Acinic cell carcinoma can be distinguished
by the finely vacuolated cytoplasm that is often lost in smears. DOG1, p63 and S100 panel on FNA
cell blocks is useful in differential diagnosis { 31051720 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

297

Essential: circumscribed mass composed of large oncocytic epithelial cells; lack of entrapped
normal parenchyma
Desirable: basal cells positive for p63, absence of SOX10, S100
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Oncocytomas do not recur if completely resected. Malignant transformation is exceedingly rare
{ 31209701 }.

L#25714
Oncocytoma
Several p63 positive basal cells.
R#28062
Oncocytoma of parotid gland
The tumour is composed predominantly of sheets of oncocytes, bland large cells with
eosinophilic granular cytoplasm

#28063
Oncocytoma
Sheets of monomorphic cells with oncocytic cytoplasm.

298

Salivary gland myoepithelioma
Definition
Myoepithelioma (ME) is a benign salivary gland neoplasm that is composed almost exclusively of
myoepithelial cells and the stroma they produce.
ICD-O coding
8982/0 Myoepithelioma
ICD-11 coding
2E91.1 & XH3CQ8 Benign neoplasm of other specified major salivary glands & myoepithelioma
Related terminology
Not recommended: myoepithelial cell tumour; myoepithelial adenoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most MEs occur in the parotid gland followed by the palate, and less commonly in submandibular
gland { 33231965 }.
Clinical features
The tumour usually presents as a painless slow-growing mass.
Epidemiology
ME comprises 1.5% of all salivary gland tumours. It constitutes 2.2% and 5.7% of major and minor
salivary gland tumours, respectively. The patient age ranges from 9 to 85 years (mean age is 44
years).
Males
and
females
are
equally
affected
{ 1325089 ; 2423468 ; 2982059 ; 6277451 ; 7557900 ; 8734420 ; 11269218 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
A few cases show EWSR1-rearrangements without correlation with histological patterns
{ 28648935 }. NTF3::PLAG1, FBXO32::PLAG1, and GEM::PLAG1 fusions have been detected in
oncocytic ME { 32673681 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumour is solid, well-circumscribed, and exhibits tan to yellow glistening cut surfaces.
Histopathology
ME shows well-circumscribed and often encapsulated borders. The myoepithelial cells can be
spindled, epithelioid, plasmacytoid, clear or in variable combinations thereof
{ 25970687 ; 30665928 }, and may be arranged in nested, cord-like, trabeculated, or reticular
patterns. The stroma can be myxoid, collagenous, or vascular. Abundant intracellular mucin is a
rare reported finding { 23821216 ; 24595421 ; 28614209 } and oncocytic changes may be found.
Encapsulation is uncommon in the minor salivary gland tumours.
Immunohistochemistry: ME is positive for keratins, S100, SOX10, and myoepithelial markers such
as p63, calponin, and SMA.
Differential The tumour’s well-defined borders and lack of an invasive growth distinguish ME from
myoepithelial carcinoma (MECA) { 25970687 ; 30789358 }. The characteristic basaloid biphasic
pattern, peripheral palisading, and minimal to absent myxoid stroma of basal cell adenoma help to
separate it from ME. Monomorphic histology and rare or absent ductal structures in ME differentiate
it from pleomorphic adenoma { 19926180 ; 33231965 }. Differentiation from soft tissue ME may be
difficult given overlapping histological features and the presence of PLAG1 gene rearrangements
in both { 23630011 }.
Cytology
Smears show variable cellularity with spindled, epithelioid, clear or plasmacytoid bland
myoepithelial cells as a single cell type or mixed types in loosely cohesive tissue fragments with
strands of hyaline stroma or as single cells. There is no myxofibrillary stroma. Necrosis and nuclear
atypia suggest MECA, but due to the inability to assess tumour invasion, it is impossible to
differentiate ME from MECA on cytopathology { 25970687 }.

299

Diagnostic molecular pathology
Detection of PLAG1 rearrangement by cytogenetics or other methods can be helpful
{ 28648935 ; 32673681 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: almost exclusive myoepithelial differentiation and absence of invasion
Desirable: tumour encapsulation (except in minor salivary glands)
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
ME is a benign tumour that shows a low recurrence rate. The tumour is treated by complete surgical
resection. Malignant transformation to MECA has been reported but seems to be extremely rare
{ 8882360 ; 10843278 }.

#24885

Salivary gland myoepithelioma
Epitheloid and plasmacytoid myoepithelial cells.
Roderick Howard Wallace Simpson

302

Canalicular adenoma
Definition
Canalicular adenoma is a benign salivary gland neoplasm of monomorphous epithelial cells
arranged in anastomosing cords within a loose, vascularized stroma.
ICD-O coding
8149/0 Canalicular adenoma
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z & XH1TD7 Benign neoplasm of major salivary glands, unspecified & Canalicular
adenoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: canalicular tumour; canalicular mixed tumour; monomorphic adenoma,
canalicular type; cystic adenoma; adenomatosis of accessory salivary glands
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Canalicular adenomas occur in the upper lip (80%), followed by buccal mucosa, lower lip, hard
and soft palate { 25141970 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a slowly growing, mobile, painless, non-ulcerated mass { 25141970 }.
Multifocal tumours are seen in 9% of cases { 6281711 ; 8555146 ; 10102598 ; 15944978 }.
Epidemiology
Canalicular adenoma accounts for < 1% of all salivary gland tumours { 16053874 ; 17825603 } and
4-6%
of
all
benign
minor
salivary
gland
neoplasms
{ 2845326 ; 7549761 ; 6281711 ; 9038559 ; 9563662 ; 10426199 ; 10769568 ; 16979373 ; 25141
970 ; 22769409 }. There is a 1.7:1 female to male ratio { 6366687 ; 25141970 }, with a wide age
range (33–91 years), but a peak in the 7th decade.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours range from 2 to 30 mm. They are well circumscribed, often cystic, showing light yellow to
tan to brown translucent nodules { 25141970 }.
Histopathology
Tumours have a bosselated or lobulated periphery, occasionally multifocal. The tumour cells are
relatively monotonous, high cuboidal to columnar, and arranged in 1-2 cell layers. These layers
anastomose to create a lattice of cords, canaliculi, tubules, and/or strands. Papillary projections
into cystic spaces are common. The cell ribbons frequently abut one another (“beading”) with knots
of cells joining parallel rows { 6366687 ; 25141970 }. The nuclei appear pseudostratified.
Intraluminal squamoid morules may be seen in the canals. The nuclei are round to oval with coarse
chromatin. There is a syncytial appearance to the cells. There is no basal or myoepithelial layer.
The stroma is sparse, loose, fibrillary, and oedematous, sometimes containing histiocytes.
Laminated microliths are frequently present { 10583574 ; 25141970 }. Mitoses are limited. Rare
hybrid or collision tumours have been reported { 8776422 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The cells are immunoreactive for pancytokeratin, CK7, S100 protein,
SOX10,
and
CD117,
but
nonreactive
with
DOG1,
desmin,
and
actins { 7534898 ; 10458827 ; 11337269 ; 12447671 ; 12738950 ; 15153875 ; 25141970 }. There
is a characteristic linear peripheral GFAP immunoreactivity { 20614277 ; 25141970 }.
Differential The entities include include basal cell adenoma, polymorphous adenocarcinoma,
adenoid cystic carcinoma, reticulated myoepithelioma, selected odontogenic tumours, and skin
basal cell carcinoma.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant

303

Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: minor salivary gland location; monotonous, isomorphic syncytium of cuboidal to
columnar cells anastomosing in a lattice of cords and canaliculi; no basal/myoepithelial layer
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Complete conservative removal is best, although multifocality may suggest recurrence
{ 25141970 }.

#22768

Salivary canalicular adenoma
Multifocality identified by identical tumour foci separate from the main tumour mass.

#22769

Salivary canalicular adenoma
Low power demonstrates the interlacing canals and ribbons of neoplastic cuboidalcolumnar cells separated by a loose, oedematous stroma.

304

#22770
Salivary canalicular adenoma
This cellular tumour demonstrates a tight, glandular and ribbon-like architecture
composed of a syncytium of short columnar epithelial cells.

L#22771
Salivary canalicular adenoma
Adjacent ribbons of cells come together and form a knot (called "beading"), a feature
quite characteristic of canalicular adenoma.
R#22772
Salivary canalicular adenoma
The canals contain squamoid morules, histiocytes, and calcifications.

#22773

Salivary canalicular adenoma
All of the neoplastic cells show a strong and diffuse nuclear and cytoplasmic reaction with
S100 protein.

#22774

Salivary canalicular adenoma
There is a curious peripheral GFAP immunoreactivity, while the central tumour cells are
non-reactive.

A clinical image showing an upper lip subepithelial nodule, characteristic for canalicular

R#22778

Cystadenoma of salivary gland
Definition
Cystadenoma is a rare predominantly multicystic benign epithelial salivary gland neoplasm that is
lined by proliferative epithelium with variable papillary architecture and frequent oncocytic cytology.
ICD-O coding
8440/0 Cystadenoma
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z & XH5RJ2 Benign neoplasm of major salivary glands, unspecified & Cystadenoma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: cystic duct adenoma; intraductal papillary hyperplasia
Subtype(s)
Papillary cystadenoma; oncocytic cystadenoma; mucinous cystadenoma
Localization
Cystadenomas seem equally distributed between major and minor salivary glands. Almost half of
cases originate in the parotid, followed by minor glands of the lip and buccal mucosa { 25547059 }.
Clinical features
Cystadenomas present as slowly growing painless nodules or masses. Minor salivary gland
tumours are covered by smooth mucosa, frequently mimicking mucocele. Major salivary gland
tumours are clinically not distinguishable from other indolent cystic salivary gland lesions
{ 25547059 }.
Epidemiology

307

Cystadenomas represent 1-4% of all salivary gland neoplasms. Women are more frequently
affected than men with a mean age in the 5th to 7th decade of life { 25547059 }.
Etiology
None
Pathogenesis
Not well understood. Three tumours (two oncocytic) with genetic data lacked mutations
in BRAF, AKT1, PIK3CA, HRAS, KRAS and GNAS { 31505033 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours vary from unilocular to multilocular with variably sized cystic spaces. Their lining varies
from flat and smooth to granular-papillary lining. The cysts contents vary based on the predominant
epithelial type, ranging from watery to thick mucoid { 25547059 ; 25861198 }.
Histopathology
Cystadenomas are well circumscribed but non-encapsulated with entrapment of salivary tissue at
the periphery. Up to 20% of cases are unicystic. The cysts are variably sized and lined by epithelial
cells of different types with variable papillary configuration. The lining epithelium shows an
admixture of columnar, cuboidal and oncocytic cells in variable combinations. Mucinous,
squamous, apocrine and ciliated epithelia are uncommon. Cystadenomas lack cytological atypia,
mitotic activity and invasive growth. They also lack complex arborizing and hierarchical papillary
tufting of intercalated-type intraductal carcinoma and other entities { 31162284 }.
Subtypes: Papillary oncocytic subtype closely mimics lymphocyte-poor Warthin tumour. Purely or
predominantly mucinous and squamous subtypes are rare.
Immunohistochemistry: The lining cells show a simple luminal phenotype (CK8/18+), supported by
a p63+ basal cell layer { 23217538 }. S100 and SOX10 are usually absent or only focally expressed.
Differential Cystadenoma should be distinguished from other papillary ductal proliferations by
established criteria { 33526223 }. Immunohistochemistry may help to exclude macrocystic
secretory carcinoma (S100+, SOX10+, mammaglobin+, MUC4+) and intercalated-type intraductal
carcinoma (S100+, SOX10+, mammaglobin+) { 31464708 ; 32931030 }. Mucinous subtype has
organoid epithelium lacking irregularly distributed intermediate and epidermoid cells and distorted
mucous
cell
component
seen
in
purely
cystic
mucoepidermoid
carcinoma
{ 25861198 ; 27278378 }.
Cytology
Not described
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not directly relevant. However, testing can exclude other cystic tumour entities (i.e. ETV6 for
secretory carcinoma, NR4A3 for acinic cell carcinoma, and MAML2 for mucoepidermoid
carcinoma).
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: uni- or multicystic non-invasive bland neoplasm with oncocytic or mixed epithelial lining.
No lymphoid stroma. Exclusion of other cystic salivary gland tumours
Desirable: oncocytic cells and variable other-type cells, variable papillary component,
demonstrable basal cell layer
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Cystadenoma is cured by simple excision. Recurrence is rare. Rare cases of invasive carcinoma
originating from cystadenoma has been reported { 15847390 ; 11097376 }.

308

#27401

Cystadenoma of salivary gland
The cysts in this tumour are lined predominantly by apocrine cells.

#27405
Cystadenoma of salivary gland, oncocytic - papillary subtype
Unicystic lesion lined by oncocytic epithelium with variable papillary configuration.

309

L#27407
Cystadenoma of salivary gland, mucinous subtype
Multicystic tumour composed of large cysts lined by well oriented mucinous cells with
basal nculei and focal papilae supported by a basal cell layer. No MAML2 fusion was
detected.
R#26941
Papillary cystadenoma
There is a papillary architecture to the neoplastic proliferation within the parotid salivary
gland. There is no pleomorphism, no destructive growth and no tumour necrosis.

Ductal papillomas
Definition
Ductal papillomas (DPs) are benign epithelial neoplasms arising from the main excretory salivary
ducts.
ICD-O coding
8503/0 Intraductal papilloma
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z & XA5T23 & XH4LZ4 Benign neoplasm of major salivary glands, unspecified & salivary
gland apparatus & Intraductal papilloma
Related terminology
Acceptable: intraductal papilloma (IP); inverted ductal papilloma (IDP)
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Preferential sites are intraoral minor salivary glands, with lower lip, buccal mucosa and palate being
the most frequent { 11458248 ; 33526223 ; 31209701 }. Rare DPs affect the parotid or unusual
sites { 32744904 ; 29768362 }.
Clinical features
DPs present as slowly growing, painless submucosal or as polypoid intra-oral lesion. Duration of
symptoms range from a few weeks to several years { 11458248 ; 11458248 ; 26583524 }.
Epidemiology
DPs constitute about 0.3% of oral salivary gland tumours { 33526223 }. They usually affect adults,
with no gender predilection { 11555769 ; 12121243 ; 17598121 }. Rare paediatric cases have been
reported { 11458248 ; 33526223 ; 31209701 }.

310

Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
HPV DNA was detected in only one case { 18774261 ; 28329525 }. No genetic changes were
identified { 31505033 }. Relation with trauma has been reported { 24455058 }.
Macroscopic appearance
DPs appear as cystic nodules, not exceeding 15 mm { 11458248 }.
Histopathology
DP may be described as intraductal papilloma (IP) or inverted ductal papilloma (IDP) according to
the growth pattern { 11458248 ; 31209701 ; 33526223 }. IPs are usually located at the ostia of the
salivary excretory ducts, presenting as a unicystic lesion with well defined borders, and filled with
papillary projections. Papillae are lined by columnar cells, intermingled with rare mucoid cells. IDP
is an unencapsulated, endophytic epithelial tumour with well defined borders and a central opening
that is frequently in contact with the surface mucosa. It is composed of mucinous and
nonkeratinizing squamous cells. No atypia is seen, and mitoses are rare
{ 11555769 ; 12121243 ; 17598121 }.
Immunohistochemistry: This is of little help in the diagnosis.
Differential The entities include other salivary gland papillary lesions { 33526223 } such as
mucinous adenocarcinoma { 29738361 } and mucoepidermoid carcinoma. Presence of branching
papillae, lined by numerous pale gastric-like cells, favours mucinous adenocarcinoma. Atypia,
invasive borders and the presence of MAML2 fusion favour the diagnosis of mucoepidermoid
carcinoma.
Cytology
Rarely reported cases show smears with papillary tissue fragments and sheets of ductal or
oncocytic columnar cells with bland nuclei, and histiocytes in the background
{ 10349381 ; 10578993 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: IP is a unicystic lesion with internal papillae, lined by columnar cells lacking atypia
Desirable: IDP is a lesion with well delineated borders and endophytic growth, composed of
squamous and mucoid cells, devoid of atypia
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
DPs are benign, cured by complete surgical excision { 11458248 }.

#25174
Ductal Papilloma, Inverted
The tumour has well defined borders and is characterized by endophytic growth.

311

#25175

Ductal Papilloma, Inverted
At higher power is composed of squamoid cells and some mucous cells, with no atypia.

#25188

Intraductal papilloma
A unilocular lesion with intracystic papillary proliferation.

312

#25189
Intraductal papilloma
Bland cuboidal to columnar tumour cells are admixed with some mucoid cells.

L#25067
Intraductal papilloma
A unilocular lesion with intracystic papillary proliferation.
R#25176
Inverted Ductal Papilloma of oral cavity
The tumour has well defined borders and is characterized by endophytic growth.

313

Sialadenoma papilliferum
Definition
Sialadenoma papilliferum (SP) is a polypoid neoplasm with both exophytic and inward papillary
proliferation of mucosal and salivary ductal epithelium.
ICD-O coding
8406/0 Sialadenoma papilliferum
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z & XH65F9 Benign neoplasm of unspecified major salivary glands & Sialadenoma
papilliferum
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most SPs occur in the hard palate followed by buccal mucosa. Other sites include soft palate,
tongue, mouth floor, parotid gland, bronchus, and esophagus { 33443864 ; 33712056 }.
Clinical features
SP usually presents as a raised sessile or pedunculated mucosal lesion { 33712056 }.
Epidemiology
SP is rare and tends to occur in older adults. The age ranges between 2 to 91 years with a median
age of 62 years { 5353939 ; 28887760 ; 31473937 }. Males are more commonly affected
{ 33712056 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
BRAF p.V600E mutation has been reported in SP { 31473937 ; 33443864 }.
Macroscopic appearance
SP is typically polypoid with a papillary surface and well-circumscribed margins. The size is usually
less than 10 mm, and grossly visible cystic spaces might be visible on cut section
{ 5353939 ; 28887760 ; 31473937 ; 33712056 }.
Histopathology
SP is characterized by the presence of both exophytic surface squamous and endophytic ductal
papillary proliferation. The exophytic squamous proliferation is directly contiguous with submucosal
salivary gland ductal epithelium which shows endophytic papillary infoldings and cystic-like spaces
{ 5353939 ; 28887760 ; 31505033 }. The ductal epithelium has biphasic pattern with luminal
cuboidal or columnar cells and basally located basal cells { 28887760 }. The ductal luminal cells
may show oncocytic metaplasia { 31473937 ; 33712056 }. Cases with endophytic ductal
proliferation with BRAF mutation but no exophytic component are called SP-like intraductal
papillary tumours { 31505033 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The ductal cells are positive for CK7 and SOX10; the basal cells are
positive for p63. The exophytic squamous cells are positive for p63, CK13, and may focally be
positive for CK7 { 8887080 ; 31473937 ; 33712056 }. Positive immunohistochemistry
for BRAF p.V600E staining can be seen in both squamous and ductal components of SP
{ 31505033 ; 33712056 }.
Differential The presence of a typical exophytic and endophytic growth pattern can distinguish SP
from other salivary duct papillary tumours including squamous cell papilloma, ductal papilloma, or
cystadenoma. Lack of invasion and overt atypia distinguishes SP from adenosquamous carcinoma.
Cytology
Not reported in cytopathological literature
Diagnostic molecular pathology
BRAF p.V600E mutation by PCR or other methods may be helpful
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

314

Essential: surface exophytic mucosal squamous proliferation and submucosal ductal cystic-like
spaces
Desirable: evidence of BRAF p.V600E mutation
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Surgical removal is curative for SP. Recurrence and malignant transformation are very rare
{ 33712056 }.

#24153

Sialadenoma papilliferum
On low power, sialadenoma papilliferum (SP) is characterized by the presence of
exophytic and endophytic components.

#24154

The upper exophytic part of SP shows papillary proliferation of squamous cells mixed with
ductal cells. The lower part shows ductal component of SP with a cystic-papillary pattern.

#24155

The ductal component has p63-positive basal cells.

immunohistochemical stain.

316

Lymphadenoma
Definition
A benign salivary gland tumour consisting of a proliferation of ductal or sebaceous epithelial cells
forming nests within a reactive lymphoid background.
ICD-O coding
8563/0 Lymphadenoma
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z & XH7J33 Benign neoplasm of major salivary glands, unspecified & Lymphadenoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Sebaceous lymphadenoma; non-sebaceous lymphadenoma
Localization
Most sebaceous lymphadenomas occur in the parotid/periparotid area or submandibular gland
{ 20032620 ; 24000514 ; 33257384 } ; lacrimal and minor salivary gland tumours have been
reported { 12076329 ; 29460186 }. Non-sebaceous lymphadenomas frequently occur in parotid
gland or peri-parotid tissue { 24944675 ; 29460186 ; 32926569 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a slowly growing painless mass of a few months to several years duration
{ 24944675 }.
Epidemiology
Lymphadenomas represent 0.1% of salivary gland neoplasms and less than 0.5% of salivary gland
adenomas { 21892186 }. Sebaceous type is more common. Patient age range is 10 to 78 years
(median: 59 years) { 21892186 }. Non-sebaceous lymphadenomas tend to affect younger patients
including children { 12383217 ; 18329584 ; 19138644 ; 20512641 ; 23426964 }. Sebaceous
lymphadenomas have a male-to-female ratio of 4:3 and there may be a slight female preference
for non-sebaceous lymphadenomas { 21892186 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Sebaceous lymphadenomas are slightly larger than non-sebaceous lymphadenomas, 29 mm
(range, 10 – 60 mm) and 15 mm (range, 6–50 mm), respectively. They are typically wellcircumscribed to encapsulated with grey-white / tan-yellow / solid or multicystic cut surface
{ 29460186 }.
Histopathology
Sebaceous lymphadenomas are well-circumscribed, variably macro-or-microcystic. They are
composed of variably sized ducts, with foci of sebaceous differentiation in a benign lymphoid
background
containing
reactive
lymphoid
follicles
with
germinal
centers
{ 12383217 ; 21892186 ; 29850339 ; 32960941 }. Pleomorphism and cytological atypia are
minimal; necrosis and mitoses are uncommon. Squamous differentiation and oncocytic metaplasia
may be seen { 16878064 ; 20032620 }. Histiocytes and/or foreign-body giant cells, from sebum
leakage, are common { 12383217 ; 21892186 ; 32960941 }. Non-sebaceous lymphadenomas are
similar but without sebaceous cells { 12383217 ; 21892186 ; 32960941 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Sebocytes stain for p63, EMA, adipophilin, and perilipin. Basal cells are
positive for CK 5/6 and p63; luminal epithelial cells are positive for CK7, CK18, and CK19
{ 17431672 ; 21892186 }. Lymphocytes are polytypic. Ki67 index is <10%. P16 is positive in
sebaceous lymphadenoma; HPV, EBV, and HHV-8 are negative { 21892186 }.
Differential Absence of invasion, extensive epidermoid islands, and MAML2 gene rearrangement
help to distinguish lymphadenomas from Warthin-like variant of mucoepidermoid carcinoma
{ 32960941 }.
Cytology

317

Smears are cellular showing 3-D tissue fragments which may be glandular or solid composed of
bland basaloid or columnar cells, with lymphocytes and histiocytes in a proteinaceous background
{ 19670220 ; 31967730 ; 32926569 }. Sebaceous differentiation with abundant finely vacuolated
microvesicular cytoplasm and central nuclei is not prominent.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not applicable
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: lymphadenoma, sebaceous subtype: epithelial nests/ducts, sebaceous cells/nests,
benign lymphoid background
lymphadenoma, non-sebaceous subtype: epithelial nests/ducts in a benign lymphoid background
Desirable: well-defined capsule
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
These are benign tumours, which do not recur if completely excised. Malignant transformation is
exceptionally rare and has been reported in the sebaceous, basaloid and lymphoid components
{ 12913846 ; 6704903 ; 21892186 ; 31967730 }.

#26853

The cut surface of this parotid gland tumour shows numerous cysts filled with yellow,
greasy sebaceous material.

#26854

Numerous cystic spaces and small ducts are noted, many showing microvesicular
cytoplasm as would be expected in sebaceous differentiation.

318

#26855
Lymphadenoma, sebacous type
Small glands are lined by cuboidal cells, while numerous sebocytes are noted throughout,
a finding seen in sebaceous lymphadenoma. Note the lymphoid stroma in the
background.

#26832
Sebaceous lymphadenoma
The tumour is well circumscribed and is composed of variably sized ducts, with foci of
sebaceous differentiation in a benign lymphoid background containing reactive lymphoid
follicles with germinal centers.

Sebaceous adenoma
Definition
Sebaceous adenoma is a benign, well-circumscribed tumour composed of irregularly sized and
shaped nests of sebaceous cells without cytologic atypia, frequently with areas of cystic change
and squamous differentiation.
ICD-O coding
8410/0 Sebaceous adenoma

319

ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z & XH1NC5 Benign neoplasm of major salivary glands, unspecified & sebaceous
adenoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Approximately 60% arise in the major salivary glands, typically the parotid gland and 40% in the
oral cavity, frequently the buccal mucosa { 22430772 }.
Clinical features
Patients usually present with a painless mass.
Epidemiology
Sebaceous adenoma is rare, accounting for 0.1% of all salivary gland neoplasms and slightly less
than 0.5% of all adenomas. More than 30 cases have been reported { 31209701 }. Mean age at
initial clinical presentation is in the 6th decade (range, 22–90 years) with a male-to-female ratio of
4:3 { 22430772 ; 31209701 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
These tumours range in size from 4 to 60 mm in greatest dimension. They are commonly
encapsulated or sharply circumscribed, varying in color from grayish-white to pinkish-white to
yellow or yellowish-gray { 6704903 }.
Histopathology
Tumours are composed of closely associated dilated or microcystic salivary ducts with foci of
prominent sebaceous differentiation, often with areas of squamous change. Atypia and
pleomorphism are minimal; invasion of surrounding tissues is not found. Sebaceous glands vary
markedly in tortuosity and size; they are frequently embedded in fibrous stroma. Marked oncocytic
metaplasia may be found and histiocytes and/or foreign body giant cells, due to extravasated
sebum can be seen focally. Lymphoid infiltrates and follicles, cytologic atypia, necrosis, and
mitoses are not compatible with a diagnosis of sebaceous adenoma { 6704903 ; 22430772 }.
Cytology
Smears are moderately cellular with tissue fragments consisting of large epithelial cells with
abundant foamy, finely granular or finely vacuolated ‘sebaceous’ cytoplasm and centrally located
bland round nuclei with slightly irregular nuclear contours and inconspicuous nucleoli
{ 19670220 ; 31967730 ; 32926569 }. Basaloid cells, squamous cells and lymphocytes can be
present, as well as histiocytes. Oncocytes are not seen. Necrosis, nuclear atypia and mitotic figures
are absent { 7880973 ; 19697727 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: numerous closely packed sebaceous glands with minimal or no atypia
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
The tumours do not recur after complete excision.

320

#26709

Sebaceous Adenoma
The tumour is encapsulated and is composed of numerous closely associated nests with
prominent sebaceous differentiation; the cystic spaces previously contained sebum.

#26710

Sebaceous Adenoma
Nests of cells exhibiting prominent sebaceous differentiation.

321

#26719
Sebaceous adenoma, FNAC, Diff Quik
Smear showing a cluster of large vacuolated epithelial cells with centrally located
scalloped nuclei.

#26806

Sebaceous adenoma
This salivary gland tumour demonstrates sebaceous differentiation and is confined to the
parotid gland. There is no lymphoid stroma.

322

Intercalated duct adenoma and hyperplasia
Definition
Intercalated duct adenoma and hyperplasia are benign salivary ductal proliferations resembling bilayered (epithelial and myoepithelial) intercalated ducts.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z Benign neoplasm of unspecified major salivary glands
Related terminology
Not recommended: adenomatous ductal proliferation
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The majority (85%) of cases arise in the parotid gland followed by the submandibular gland (11%),
and
the
oral
cavity
(4%)
{ 7868091 ; 10971702 ; 11174600 ; 12072816 ; 19542868 ; 22271513 ; 22460810 ; 25497834 }.
Clinical features
Most cases are incidental, concurrently identified with other salivary gland lesions (e.g., basal cell
adenoma, epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma) { 10971702 ; 19542868 ; 24299520 ; 25497834 }.
Epidemiology
Rarely
reported
{ 7868091 ; 10971702 ; 11174600 ; 12072816 ; 19542868 ; 22271513 ; 22460810 ; 24299520 ; 2
5497834 }, there is a female-to-male ratio of 3:2, occurring in middle age (mean 52 years).
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Rarely detected macroscopically, most lesions are < 5 mm { 19542868 }.
Histopathology
Intercalated duct lesions manifest as a nodular, occasionally multinodular, proliferation of cuboidal
ductal cells with attenuated myoepithelial cells, sometimes containing acinic cells. Intercalated duct
lesions are subdivided into adenomas, when well circumscribed or encapsulated, and hyperplasias
when blending with adjacent acini { 19542868 }.
Differential
If well circumscribed, it should be distinguished from striated duct
adenoma (which lacks myoepithelial cells) { 19542868 ; 21054495 }.
Cytology
Smears show cohesive large tissue fragments of epithelial cells with high N:C ratio and bland oval
nuclei, with admixed thin stromal strands { 26477034 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: monotonous proliferation of cuboidal ductal cells surrounded by myoepithelial cells,
sometimes with acinic cells
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Most are incidental lesions, with an excellent prognosis

#23311
Salivary intercalated duct adenoma
Low power demonstrates an irregular periphery, with multinodularity. The proliferation is a
monotonous single cell population, with acinar cells at the periphery.

Salivary intercalated duct adenoma
A monotonous proliferation of cuboidal cells are arranged in acinar configuration. Note the
delicate, eosinophilic cytoplasmic granules.

#23314

Salivary intercalated duct lesion
The separation of adenoma from hyperplasia is challenging, as sometimes there is a
blending at the periphery with acinar cells (dark blue cytoplasmic granules), while the
proliferation contains eosinophilic cytoplasmic granules.

Intercalated duct hyperplasia
Proliferation of small eosinophilic tubules with transition from acini.

#23976
Intercalated duct hyperplasia
A well defined small lobular proliferation of bland eosinophilic ducts with inconspicuous
basal cells and admixed scattered acinic cells. Surrounding parotid gland tissue is
unremarkable.

Striated duct adenoma
Definition
Striated duct adenoma (SDA) is a benign salivary gland tumour composed of ducts resembling
normal striated ducts lined by a single layer of luminal cells with minimal intervening stroma.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z Benign neoplasm of unspecified major salivary glands
Related terminology
Not recommended: ductal adenoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
SDA can involve both the major and minor salivary glands, with a predominance in the parotid
{ 21054495 ; 28440555 }.
Clinical features
SDA presents as a painless mass { 21054495 ; 28440555 }.
Epidemiology
SDA are rare tumours. Those described have been in adults (range 47-78 years) with a slight
female predominance { 21054495 ; 28440555 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
SDA present as solid masses with a yellow cut surface.
Histopathology
SDA are well-circumscribed and encapsulated. They are composed of small, closely spaced ducts
with minimal intervening stroma. Small cysts can also be seen. The tumour may be highly vascular,
and some contain “staghorn” blood vessels. The ducts are lined by a single layer of columnar cells
with eosinophilic cytoplasm, unlike intercalated duct lesions { 19542868 }. Occasionally, the luminal
spaces may contain eosinophilic material reminiscent of colloid, as well as red blood cells. Some
cases show hyalinization and may mimic oncocytoma with oncocytosis. Occasional cases will show

326

striated duct hyperplasia of the background parotid. The duct caliber is comparable to normal
striated ducts. Fat cells may be focally or diffusely present { 9071732 ; 21054495 }.
Immunohistochemistry: SDA are strongly positive for keratins including CK7 and CK5/6, the latter
showing basal layer accentuation. Most tumours show strong nuclear and cytoplasmic staining for
S100 (unlike oncocytoma). In contrast, calponin, SMA, smooth muscle myosin heavy chain, and
p63 highlight isolated abluminal cells only. Markers of thyroid differentiation such as TTF-1 and
thyroglobulin are negative { 21054495 }.
Differential SDA do not show chondromyxoid stroma or any other matrix. They also do not show
morphologically visible bilayering { 21054495 }. They do not have the deeply eosinophilic
cytoplasm characteristic of oncocytoma.
Cytology
Not reported in cytopathological literature
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: closely spaced ducts with minimal intervening stroma lined by a single layer of
eosinophilic luminal cells
Desirable: absence of myoepithelial cells by immunohistochemistry
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
SDAs do not recur based on the limited cases reported to date and also do not metastasize
{ 21054495 ; 28440555 }.

#24480

Striated duct adenoma
Low power image of a striated duct adenoma with variable small calibre ducts and
microcysts.

327

#24481

Striated duct adenoma
The tumours are made of a pure population of eosinophilic ducts with minimal intervening
stroma. Some lumina contain numerous red blood cells.

#24482

Striated duct adenoma
The majority of the ductal spaces are similarly sized to normal striated ducts, and have
bland nuclei. Luminal secretions may resemble colloid.

328

#24483: These tumours are diffusely positive for S100 unlike their closest mimic, namely
oncocytomas.

#24486: Striated duct adenomas are unilayered with minimal contribution from basal or
myoepithelial cells. As such, the p63 stains only focal abluminal cells, if any.

#24488: Similarly, the calponin (shown here) or actins have only focal abluminal staining, if any,
in striated duct adenoma.
329

#24476: Multinodular tumour with general circumscription and hyalinization. The tumour is
composed exclusively of small to medium calibre ducts showing striated duct type morphology
and variable small cystic ducts. The cells are eosinophilic, bland and lack an outer myoepithelial
layer.

Sclerosing polycystic adenoma
Definition
Sclerosing polycystic adenoma (SPA) is a circumscribed salivary gland neoplasm composed of
large acinar cells with brightly eosinophilic intracytoplasmic granules, and variable ductal
components, including foamy, vacuolated, and apocrine cells.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z Benign neoplasm of unspecified major salivary glands
Related terminology
Not recommended: sclerosing polycystic adenosis
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The majority (80%) arise in the parotid gland, with occasional sinonasal, submandibular,
and lacrimal
gland involvement
{ 16434888 ; 17150380 ; 20549092 ; 20614339 ; 23465282 ; 23552085 ; 34510113 }.
Clinical features
Slow-growing, painless mass. Rarely multifocal { 16434888 ; 21286874 ; 24636837 }.
Epidemiology
SPA is rare { 16434888 }, with a mean age at presentation of 40 years (range 7-84) and a slight
female predominance { 28614209 }.
Etiology
Rare cases associated with polycystic/dysgenetic disease { 32031136 } and Cowden syndrome
{ 34410594 }.
Pathogenesis
Originally regarded as pseudoneoplastic { 8554105 ; 11942573 }, monoclonality was
demonstrated by polymorphism of human androgen receptor assay { 16861963 }. Consistent
mutations in the PI3 kinase pathway, most frequently PTEN, confirm its neoplastic nature and
suggest links with apocrine intraductal carcinoma and salivary duct carcinoma
{ 31605313 ; 31989433 ; 33526220 ; 34410594 ; 34510113 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Well-circumscribed, often encapsulated nodule, 10-120 mm (mean 30 mm). The cut section is
partially solid and cystic.
Histopathology

330

SPA is characterized by a cystic and multilobular pattern, composed of variably sized and shaped
ducts lined by flattened, cylindrical, or apocrine, foamy, or vacuolated epithelium, embedded in a
sclerotic collagenous stroma with focal lymphoid aggregates { 28060365 ; 28614209 ; 33526220 }.
The hallmark of SPA are acini containing large brightly eosinophilic cytoplasmic granules/globules.
SPA often display solid, cribriform, and micropapillary proliferations with variable degrees of
cytological atypia, surrounded by myoepithelial cells, resembling intraductal carcinoma
{ 23821217 }.
SPA
is
rarely
associated
with
invasive
carcinoma
{ 24584973 ; 34410594 ; 34510113 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Each cellular component demonstrates its expected immunophenotype:
SOX10 in ductules, myoepithelial cells and acini; SMA, p40, calponin are seen in myoepithelial
cells; apocrine cells react with AR and GCDFP-15. There is frequent loss of PTEN in ductal and
acinar, but not in myoepithelial cells { 31605313 ; 34410594 }.
Cytology
Smears show a mix of flat cohesive sheets of epithelial cells with variably granular oncocytic
cytoplasm and round nuclei, sheets of apocrine-type epithelium and histiocytic cells in a
proteinaceus background. The coexistence of different cell types in a single sheet is helpful
{ 17580340 ; 19937766 ; 28060365 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: mixtures of ductal and myoepithelial cells, with acinar cells containing large
hypereosinophilic cytoplasmic granules
Desirable: apocrine metaplasia and intraductal proliferation creating resemblance to low-grade
ductal carcinoma in situ
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
There is local recurrence in 10% of incompletely excised or multifocal cases
{ 8554105 ; 11942573 ; 23821217 ; 24595421 ; 33526220 }. Rare invasive carcinoma ex-SPA
have been reported { 24584973 ; 34510113 }.

#23318
Salivary sclerosing polycystic adenoma
An intermediate power image shows heavy stromal sclerosis with cystic dilatation of
hyperplastic appearing epithelium showing ducts and acinar cells. Apocrine change is
present, while foamy cytoplasm is seen in degenerating cells.

331

#23320

Salivary sclerosing polycystic adenoma
Eosinophilic, spherical laminated hyaline globules are noted in association with epithelial
nests which also have cells with prominent eosinophilic cytoplasmic granules.

#24360
Salivary sclerosing polycystic adenoma
This case shows a mixed population of acinar cells and ducts in a fibrous stroma. The
cystic duct in center shows apocrine hyperplasia that, by itself, is indistinguishable from
apocrine intraductal carcinoma.
332

#24361

PTEN immunohistochemistry shows an abnormal loss of expression in the ductal and
apocrine elements, but retained staining in the stromal and myoepithelial cells.

#27683

Foci of homogenous solid, micropapillary, and cribriform intraductal epithelial
proliferations reminiscent of an intercalated duct cell variant of intraductal carcinoma (IC).
The intraductal proliferations are composed of intercalated duct cell-like epithelium
and show solid and cribriform patterns.
333

#27687

Sclerosing polycystic adenoma
The intraductal proliferations show rigid bridging and cribriform patterns, and are
composed of intercalated duct cell-like epithelium positive for SOX10.

#27694

Salivary sclerosing polycystic adenoma
Closely attached solid, tubular and cribriform epithelial proliferations are composed of
apocrine cells with nuclear atypia and finely granular cytoplasm. These cells are positive
for androgen receptors and negative for SOX10.
