#23193
Salivary sclerosing polycystic adenoma
A well defined haphazard proliferation of acinar elements set within a sclerotic stroma.
Apocrine change and prominent hypereosinophilic cytoplasmic granules are noted.

Keratocystoma
Definition
Keratocystoma is a benign salivary gland tumour characterized by multicystic spaces, lined by
stratified squamous epithelium, containing keratotic lamellae and focal solid epithelial nests.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E91.Z Benign neoplasm of unspecified major salivary glands
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
All reported tumours arose in the parotid gland.
Clinical features
The tumour manifests as a painless, slowly growing mass in the parotid gland.
Epidemiology
The
patients
range
from
8to
49-yearsold { 10335947 ; 12218219 ; 20702483 ; 22218263 ; 25960022 ; 31230813 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
A maximum dimension of the tumour ranges from 13 to 40 mm. The cut surface shows a
multilocular cystic lesion filled with a keratin-like substance.
Histopathology
335

The tumour consists mainly of multiple cysts containing keratotic lamellae. Stratified squamous
epithelium lines the cystic spaces and shows a parakeratotic or orthokeratotic surface, usually
without a granular cell layer. The stratification of the epithelium is oriented regularly from the outer
basal to the inner keratotic cell layers. Focally, the outer layer demonstrates bud-like protrusions,
but no skin appendages are seen. In some areas, solid squamous cell islands, with sharply defined
margins, are surrounded by collagenous stroma. The solid areas of squamous cell nests are
localized partly within the parenchyma of the parotid gland adjacent to an excretory duct, and
should not be interpreted as invasive growth. Transformation from parotid ductal epithelium to
neoplastic cells, through a squamous-metaplasia-like process, may be present.
Cells of the neoplastic squamous epithelium have uniform, bland nuclei and abundant eosinophilic
cytoplasm. There is no oncocytic or mucous cell differentiation or a myxochondroid component.
Foci of foreign body reaction against keratin and a dense lymphoid infiltration may be evident.
Immunohistochemistry: Immunostaining for S100 protein, SMA, and calponin is negative.
Differential The entities include primary and metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, mucoepidermoid
carcinoma, metaplastic Warthin tumor, and necrotizing sialometaplasia. The absence of necrosis,
invasion, and cytologic atypia weighs against a malignancy.
Cytology
Not reported in cytopathological literature.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: multicystic structures containing keratinized lamellae. Bland stratified squamous
epithelial lining without a granular layer
Desirable: presence of sharply defined solid squamous cell islands
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
The tumours behave in a benign manner, with no recurrence after excision or subtotal
parotidectomy.

#24927

Keratocystoma
Cut surface of the parotid tumour, showing multiple cystic formations filled with keratinlike material.

336

#24928

Keratocystoma
Multiple cysts with focal bud-like epithelial outer protrusions and a few solid epithelial cell
nests. The cystic spaces contain lamellar keratin material.

#24929
Keratocystoma
The cyst wall lined by bland stratified squamous epithelium with keratinization through
parakeratotic cells. Note the lack of a granular cell layer.

337

#24832

Keratocystoma in the parotid gland of a 17-year-old man
The tumour consists of haphazardly distributed multiple cysts and scattered solid
squamous cell islands. The cystic spaces are filled with keratotic lamellae. The cyst walls
are lined by stratified squamous epithelium showing a keratotic surface without a granular
cell layer. Each cyst and some solid squamous cell nests are surrounded by collagenous
stroma. The squamous epithelial cells have uniform, bland nuclei and abundant
eosinophilic cytoplasm. The solid squamous cell nests are focally located within the
parotid gland parenchyma in conjunction with the ducts, but these findings should not be
interpreted as invasive growth.

Malignant epithelial tumours

Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
Definition
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma (MEC) is a malignant salivary gland neoplasm characterized by
mucous, intermediate and epidermoid (squamoid) tumour cells forming cystic and solid growth
patterns, usually associated with MAML2 rearrangement.
ICD-O coding
8430/3 Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Y & XH1J36 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Mucoepidermoid
carcinoma 2B68.2 & XH1J36 Other specified malignant neoplasms of submandibular or
sublingual gland & Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
2B65.Z & XH1J36 Malignant neoplasms of palate, unspecified & Mucoepidermoid carcinoma
2B66& X H1J36 Malignant neoplasms of other or unspecified parts of mouth & Mucoepidermoid
carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: mucoepidermoid tumour
Subtype(s)
Central intraosseous mucoepidermoid tumour
Localization

338

Approximately half of MECs occur in major salivary glands, predominantly in the parotid gland,
followed by the submandibular and the sublingual gland. Intraorally, the most frequent site is the
palate followed by the buccal mucosa and other sites { 33248902 }. Rare central MECs occur in
the mandible or the maxilla { 29198377 }.
Clinical features
MEC usually presents as a firm and fixed tumour but occasional tumours may be largely cystic with
only minor solid areas. Tumours situated below an intact oral surface epithelium may have a bluish
appearance and mimic vascular lesions or mucocele. Largely cystic tumours may present
diagnostic problems on imaging and fine needle aspiration.
Epidemiology
MEC is reported as the most frequent salivary malignancy in North America, many European
countries, and Brazil. Reported annual incidence of MEC in Britain and Japan is 0.2-0.4
cases/100.000 population { 19861510 ; 23103239 ; 31027324 ; 31174233 }. MEC patients have a
wide age range from childhood to elderly age, with a mean age of 45 years and a female
predilection of 1.1-1.5:1 { 30772619 }.
Etiology
Increased occurrence of childhood MEC has been reported after nuclear disasters and
chemotherapy { 30772619 }.
Pathogenesis
Most MECs harbour a tumour type-specific translocation at t(11;19)(q21;p13)
expressing CRTC1::MAML2 fusion gene { 20588178 ; 23018873 ; 32860299 }. Rare cases display
a t(11;15)(q21;q26) translocation with CRTC3::MAML2 fusion { 19749740 } or a highly unusual
t(6;22)(p21;q12)
translocation
with EWSR1::POU5F1 fusion
{ 18338330 }.
The CRTC1::MAML2 fusion is seen in most low and intermediate grade and some high grade
tumours. Low grade MECs show negligible copy number variation { 23018873 } and few somatic
mutations, such as TP53 and POU6F2 { 27340278 }.
Macroscopic appearance
MECs are usually firm or soft, partly cystic to solid masses with circumscribed or infiltrative borders,
occasionally a large cyst predominates.
Histopathology
MEC is characterized by mucous (mucin-producing), intermediate and squamoid cells.
Architectural configurations include cystic and solid areas where proportions of tumour cell types
vary widely. Cystic spaces are partly lined by mucous cells with abundant mucinous cytoplasm and
peripherally situated nuclei, and they display intracytoplasmic mucicarmine or periodic acid-Schiff
(PAS) staining with diastase resistance. Intermediate cells are often the most frequent cell type in
tumours. Extracellular mucin may be present. Significant keratinization is exceptional. Cell
populations with clear, columnar or oncocytic cells may be present, and occasionally they
predominate. Sclerosing { 29169286 }, clear cell { 26297211 }, oncocytic { 33002921 }, Warthin-like
{ 26457352 ; 28877061 }, ciliated { 28877061 }, spindle cell { 28637362 } and mucoacinar
{ 34091485 } patterns of MEC have been described.
Subtype: Central mucoepidermoid carcinoma is a location-specific intraosseous subtype
{ 29198377 }.
Grading: MECs are graded into three grades using a variety of grading systems
{ 1544111 ; 9529011 ; 11420454 ; 25040772 }. Low grade MECs are usually circumscribed, partly
cystic and contain groups of mucous cells. Intermediate grade MECs generally have more solid
areas, while high grade neoplasms are solid with fewer mucous cells, and may display nuclear
pleomorphism, mitotic figures, necrosis, and perineurial, lymphovascular or bony invasion.
Clinicopathological studies report that patients with low and intermediate grade MECs have similar
clinical outcomes { 25040772 ; 31021855 ; 32035991 ; 33437665 }, favouring the use of two
histological grades instead of three. AFIP grading evaluates five histological features which may
enhance grading reproducibility but some tumours graded as low-grade may behave more
aggressively { 1544111 ; 9529011 }. Brandwein grading evaluates many histological features and
tends to “upgrade” MECs compared with AFIP { 11420454 }. Memorial Sloan Kettering (MSK)
grading evaluates tumours qualitatively based on predominance of their features but many criteria
are not well-defined { 25040772 }.

339

Immunohistochemistry: Immunohistochemical p63 or p40 expression in absence of S100
protein/SOX10 staining may help to differentiate MEC from other salivary tumours { 23054955 }.
Differential The entities in the differential diagnosis of low grade MEC include mucocele,
necrotizing sialometaplasia, sclerosing sialadenitis with mucous metaplasia, pleomorphic adenoma
and Warthin tumour with oncocytic or squamous metaplasia, sclerosing polycystic adenoma, and
secretory carcinoma. High grade MEC must be distinguished from carcinoma ex pleomorphic
adenoma, poorly differentiated SCC, adenosquamous carcinoma, salivary duct carcinoma and
metastatic carcinomas.
Cytology
MEC produce variably cellular smears showing varying numbers of mucinous cells, admixed in
sheets and tissue fragments with intermediate cells with relatively dense cytoplasm, and polygonal,
epidermoid (squamoid) cells which are large with moderate to copious dense cytoplasm
{ 9258615 }. The mucinous cells can resemble goblet cells with bland round nuclei and abundant
granular to finely vacuolated eccentric cytoplasm, or glandular cells with single vacuoles in which
mucin droplets can be seen. Well differentiated MEC with mucinous cells in intermediate cell sheets
can be diagnosed confidently, but these tumours often show cystic degeneration and smears may
show only a mucinous or granular proteinaceous background with a variable number of histiocytelike mucinous cells, raising a differential diagnosis with mucocele-like lesions: any salivary gland
FNAB yielding mucin must be considered atypical. In intermediate and high-grade MEC, the
number of mucinous cells decreases, the number of intermediate and squamoid cells increase, and
nuclear enlargement, hyperchromasia and pleomorphism and larger nucleoli become more
prominent. Necrosis can be associated with high grade lesions. FNAB cytopathology attempts to
grade as only low and high grade. Lymphocytes may be prominent. Very occasional focal partial
keratinization may be present in some cases but extensive keratinization is not seen and if present
in a high grade lesion, is in keeping with squamous cell carcinoma.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Among salivary tumours, CRTC1::MAML2 fusion gene is specific for MEC and useful in diagnostic
workups.
FISH
result
positive
for MAML2 rearrangement
should
be
validated
by MAML2 polymerase chain reaction or next generation sequencing.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: mucous cells; intermediate cells; squamoid cells
Desirable: MAML2 rearrangement in selected cases
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
Decreased overall and disease-free survival in MEC is associated with advanced stage, advanced
age, male sex, high histologic grade and positive resection margin { 33248902 ; 33437665 }. Lack
of CRTC1::MAML2 fusion is associated with worse survival, however, fusion negative tumours may
actually
represent
other
high
grade
non-mucoepidermoid
carcinomas
{ 23018873 ; 31021855 ; 32860299 ; 34231055 }. The 5-year overall survival rates of low,
intermediate, and high grade MEC are reported as 90%, 86% and 55%, respectively
{ 33248902 ; 33437665 }.

Adenoid cystic carcinoma
Definition
Adenoid cystic carcinoma (AdCC) is an invasive carcinoma composed of epithelial and
myoepithelial neoplastic cells arranged in tubular, cribriform, and solid patterns associated with
basophilic matrix and reduplicated basement membrane material, often associated with MYB,
MYBL1, or NFIB rearrangement.
ICD-O coding
8200/3 Adenoid cystic carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B68.0 & XH4302 Malignant neoplasms of major salivary glands, NOS & Adenoid cystic carcinoma
2B65.Y & XH4302 Other specified malignant neoplasm of palate & Adenoid cystic carcinoma
2B68.2 & XH4302 Other specified malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands &
Adenoid cystic carcinoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Approximately 60% of AdCCs occur in the major and 30% in the minor salivary glands
{ 26476712 }. The parotid, submandibular, and minor glands of the palate are the predominant sites
{ 23706387 ; 26476712 }.
Clinical features
Patients usually present with a swelling or a slow-growing mass. Neural symptoms like pain,
numbness, and paresthesia are commonly reported { 25943783 }. Lymph node involvement is
uncommon, but in AdCC with high-grade transformation (AdCC-HGT), the clinical course tends to
be accelerated, with a high propensity for lymph node metastasis { 26895332 }. Distant metastases
are frequent, most commonly to the lungs, followed by bone, liver, and brain
{ 32613404 ; 32629365 }.

352

Imaging: AdCC usually presents as an ill-defined mass { 30546932 }.
Epidemiology
The annual incidence of AdCC is 1-2/100,000 { 22294420 ; 32691231 }. It is one of the most
common salivary malignancies, accounting for 25% of all primary salivary carcinomas
{ 32691231 }. The median patient age at diagnosis is approximately 60 years. AdCC is slightly more
common in females { 22294420 ; 19861510 }.
Etiology
Germline mutations in BRCA1/2 { 25257187 } and DNA double-strand repair genes { 31483290 }
may increase the risk of salivary gland AdCC.
Pathogenesis
The genomic hallmark of AdCC is t(6;9) or t(8;9) translocations, resulting
in MYB::NFIB and MYBL1::NFIB fusions,
respectively
{ 3015376 ; 19841262 ; 26631070 ; 26631609 }. The former alteration is found in >50% of cases
and the latter in approximately 5% of cases { 28594149 }. MYB::MYBL1 activation due to gene
fusion
or
other
mechanisms
is
a
key
event
in
AdCC
pathogenesis
{ 26829750 ; 28954282 ; 31877778 ; 32001675 }. Losses of 1p, 6q, and 15q are associated with
high-grade tumours, and loss of 14q is exclusively seen in low-grade tumours
{ 22505352 ; 29619555 }. Next-generation sequencing identified mutations in genes in the FGFIGF-PI3K,
chromatin-remodeling,
and NOTCH signaling
pathways
{ 23685749 ; 23778141 ; 31483290 }.
Macroscopic appearance
AdCC typically presents as a poorly circumscribed, tan, gray or white, firm solid mass. Necrosis
and/or haemorrhage are rare, their presence may indicate a high-grade tumour.
Histopathology
AdCC consists of two main cell types, ductal and myoepithelial cells. The former cell type
has eosinophilic cytoplasm and uniform round nuclei, and the latter has clear cytoplasm and
hyperchromatic angular nuclei. Mitotic figures are infrequent. Perineural invasion is a hallmark of
AdCC.
AdCC shows three growth patterns, tubular, cribriform, and solid { 20596994 }. The tubular pattern
consists of well-formed ducts and tubules lined with luminal ductal and abluminal myoepithelial
cells. The cribriform pattern, which is most frequent, is characterized by nests of tumour cells with
microcystic-like spaces, filled with hyaline or basophilic mucoid material. The spaces are
pseudocysts contiguous with the tumour stroma. The solid pattern is characterized by tumour
sheets composed of basaloid cells lacking tubular or cribriform formations. Combinations of these
growth patterns are common. Identification of both pseudocysts and true glandular lumina is usually
required to make the diagnosis.
AdCC with high-grade transformation shows a pleomorphic, mitotically active high-grade
carcinoma component, typically a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma or anaplastic carcinoma
{ 26895332 ; 27605055 ; 33825717 }. The high-grade component is negative for myoepithelial
markers { 18059225 ; 33825717 }.
Generally, AdCCs with >30% solid component pursue a more aggressive clinical course
{ 20596994 ; 25456010 ; 33377247 }. In recent studies, the presence of any solid tumour
component has been emphasized as an objective high-grade tumour marker
{ 25456010 ; 33377247 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Pan-cytokeratin is strongly positive in ductal cells and weakly positive in
myoepithelial cells. CK7 and KIT (CD117) are typically positive in ductal cells { 16140564 },
whereas p63, p40, calponin, and alpha-smooth muscle actin are positive in myoepithelial cells.
MYB protein overexpression is common AdCC, but its diagnostic value is limited by its suboptimal
specificity { 21572406 ; 27491495 ; 28727172 ; 30027386 ; 32661670 }.
Differential The main salivary gland tumours to be distinguished from AdCC include pleomorphic
adenoma, polymorphous adenocarcinoma, epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma, and basal cell
adenoma/adenocarcinoma. Since AdCC often shows mild cytological atypia and a cribriform
pattern, the distinction from benign tumours with a cribriform pattern, especially the cribriform
variant of basal cell adenoma, is critical { 31970840 }.
Cytology

353

AdCC show usually highly cellular smears with cohesive tissue fragments that form sheets or show
tubular, complex or cribriform architecture containing hard edged balls of stroma. These tissue
fragments consist of uniform, high N/C ratio basaloid cells with oval to angulated and
hyperchromatic nuclei. Atypia is generally mild and the distinction from other basaloid salivary gland
neoplasms can be difficult in the absence of the typical cribriform pattern and stromal matrix
particularly the stromal balls, and the predominance of sheets of basaloid cells in the solid subtype.
These cases raise differential diagnoses with pleomorphic adenomas and basal cell adenomas and
should be regarded as atypical or suspicious for malignancy. High nuclear grade can occur and be
associated with mitoses and necrosis. Romanowsky-Giemsa type stains highlight the characteristic
discrete spheres and branching tubules of the acellular magenta matrix { 9218901 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Demonstration of MYB/MYBL1 rearrangements or gene fusions by FISH or other methods can help
establish a diagnosis of AdCC
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: hyperchromatic, angulated nuclei, mixture of tubular, cribriform, and solid patterns
associated with basophilic matrix and reduplicated basement membrane material.
Desirable: Perineural invasion; demonstration of MYB or MYBL1 rearrangements in selected
cases
Staging
Staging follows the 8th edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM
classification
Prognosis and prediction
AdCC is characterized by a prolonged clinical course, with frequent local recurrences, late onset of
metastases and fatal outcome. Overall survival and recurrence-free survival rates at 10 years are
approximately 50% and 60%, respectively { 26476712 ; 32691231 }. Factors influencing survival
include patient age, tumour site, tumour grade, TNM stage, surgical margins, and NOTCH1
mutational status { 23784851 ; 29858025 ; 32613404 }. The median overall survival after diagnosis
of distant metastasis is 36 months { 23706387 }.

The solid pattern is characterized by dense cellular tumour nests lacking the pseudocystic
spaces observed in the cribriform and tubular patterns.

#25697

characterized as poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma.

#25699

Adenoid cystic carcinoma (MYB::NFIB-positive case)
The tumour shows a prominent peri- and intraneural invasion.

The fine-needle aspiration smear demonstrates the characteristic discrete spheres and
branching tubules of the acellular magenta matrix (Romanowsky-Giemsa stain).

Axial CT demonstrates a destructive mass involving the left palate, with bone remodeling.
While not diagnostic of adenoid cystic carcinoma, it is supportive of a malignant salivary
gland neoplasm.

#26937

Adenoid cystic carcinoma
The surface epithelium is uninvolved. There is a mixed cribriform, tubular and focally solid
architecture to this adenoid cystic carcinoma. There is easily identified perineural
invasion. Stromal to epithelial clefting is noted in many areas.

Acinic cell carcinoma
Definition
Acinic cell carcinoma is a salivary gland carcinoma exhibiting serous acinar and lacking mucinous
differentiation
ICD-O coding
8550/3 Acinic cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B68.2 & XH3PG9 Other specified malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands &
Acinic cell adenocarcinoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: acinic cell adenocarcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
90-95% of cases arise in the parotid gland. The remainder involve intraoral sites, submandibular
gland, and exceptionally the sinonasal tract { 23681074 ; 26685679 }.
Clinical features
Patients typically present with slow-growing, painless masses. High-grade tumours may be rapidly
growing and fixed to adjacent structures with facial nerve paralysis.
Epidemiology
Acinic cell carcinoma represents 10% of all salivary gland malignancies and up to 18.7% of parotid
carcinomas. It is the second most common salivary gland malignancy in children
{ 26685679 ; 33734517 }. It occurs over a wide age range (average age: 47.7 – 52)
{ 22301503 ; 26685679 }. Acinic cell carcinoma with high-grade transformation (AciCC-HGT)
occurs on average two decades later than conventional examples. Female to male ratio is 1.5:1
{ 19461506 ; 23754708 ; 26245749 ; 26685679 }.
Etiology

360

Unknown
Pathogenesis
The majority of cases harbour a t(4;9)(q13;q31) rearrangement that places the active enhancer
regions of the secretory Ca-binding phosphoprotein (SCPP) gene cluster upstream of
the NR4A3/NOR-1 gene resulting in upregulation of NR4A3 via enhancer hijacking
{ 30664630 ; 31094928 ; 32341238 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Well-circumscribed to variably lobulated, unencapsulated or variably pseudo-encapsulated, solid
to variably cystic. Infiltrative growth more common in high-grade tumours.
Histopathology
The tumour exhibits solid, microcystic, follicular, to less commonly papillary-cystic architectures,
often with a prominent lymphoid stroma. Neoplastic cells are heterogeneous with the most common
cell type being the serous acinar cell which features PAS-positive, diastase-resistant basophilic
cytoplasmic zymogen granules, with variable intercalated duct-type, nonspecific glandular,
vacuolated, oncocytic, and rarely clear cells. Cytoplasmic borders are ill-defined and nuclei are
small and peripherally placed { 6861091 ; 26685679 ; 31812438 }. High-grade (HG) tumours
exhibit, in addition to conventional areas, a component of high-grade adenocarcinoma (with
variable cribriform, solid, trabecular growth patterns) or poorly-differentiated/undifferentiated
carcinoma. AciCC HGT feature nuclear enlargement and pleomorphism, coarse chromatin,
necrosis, increased mitotic activity and Ki-67 index, and more frequent perineural and
lymphovascular invasion. Increased expression of cyclin-D1 and membranous beta-catenin have
been described { 19461506 ; 26245749 ; 31812438 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Acinic cell carcinoma cells express SOX10 and DOG1, and are negative
for p40/p63, mammaglobin and S100 protein, however S100 may label intercalated cells
{ 22460810 ; 23558573 }. Nuclear staining for NR4A3/NOR-1 or NR4A2/Nurr1 have been identified
in 98% and 2% of cases, respectively { 31094928 ; 32341238 }. The sensitivity and specificity of
immunohistochemistry
for
NR4A3
are
94.4%
and
99%,
respectively
{ 31094928 ; 31414988 ; 32341238 ; 32416209 ; 32809265 ; 32910350 ; 33152819 ; 33959239 }.
Cytology
The usually highly cellular smears show irregular loosely cohesive sheets and tissue fragments
with a variable microacinar architecture and occasional association with thin branching capillaries,
in a granular background with plentiful stripped round nuclei. The cells are polygonal with eccentric
round to oval nuclei, usually small but occasionally larger nucleoli and abundant eccentric, delicate,
finely vacuolated cytoplasm, best seen in Romanowsky-Giemsa type stains. The Papanicolaou
stain may show coarse basophilic cytoplasmic zymogen granules { 9360047 }. Cystic degeneration
can occur, but mitoses and necrosis are usually absent. There is no association with normal ‘grapelike’ acinar architecture or ducts. Lymphoid cells can be prominent raising a differential diagnosis
with Warthin tumour, and also with metastatic carcinoma in a lymph node.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Typically not needed. However, demonstrating NR4A subfamily rearrangements or positive
immunohistochemistry for NR4A3 or NR4A2 may be confirmatory in challenging cases.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: salivary gland carcinoma with serous acinar differentiation.
Desirable: nuclear staining for NR4A3/NOR-1 or NR4A2/Nurr1 or molecular demonstration
of NR4A3 rearrangement (in selected cases)
Staging
UICC 8th ed
Prognosis and prediction
AciCCs with a complete fibrous pseudocapsule, diffuse, dense lymphoid stroma and microcystic
growth pattern have superior outcomes { 9158708 }. The mean 5- and 10-year survival rates are
approximately
95
97.15%
and
83
93.81%,
respectively
{ 1985714 ; 23754708 ; 26685679 }. Distant metastasis drops the survival to about 22% and
occurs in about 20% of cases { 26685679 }. 72.7% of 22 patients with AciCC-HGT were dead at a
median time of 2.9 years { 26245749 }. Local recurrence rates are approximately 35% and 80%
and lymph node metastases occur in about 10% and 50% of conventional and high-grade tumours,
respectively { 19461506 ; 22301503 ; 23681074 ; 26245749 ; 27623207 }.

368

Secretory carcinoma
Definition
Secretory carcinoma (SC) is a monophasic salivary carcinoma composed of cells with abundant
eosinophilic or bubbly secretions, arranged in microcystic, tubular, and solid structures. It is usually
characterized by a specific rearrangement of the ETV6 or RET gene, and
harbours ETV6::NTRK3 or ETV6::RET fusion in most cases.
ICD-O coding
8502/3 Secretory carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Y & XH44J4 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Secretory carcinoma
2B68.2 & XH44J4 Other specified malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands &
Secretory carcinoma 2B65.Y & XH44J4 Other specified malignant neoplasm of palate &
Secretory carcinoma 2B60 & XH44J4 Malignant neoplasms of the lip & Secretory carcinoma
2B66.Y & XH44J4 Other specified malignant neoplasms of other or unspecified parts of
mouth & Secretory carcinoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: mammary analogue secretory carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The most common site of occurrence is the parotid gland followed by the oral cavity and
submandibular gland { 20410810 ; 21989350 ; 23821207 ; 25078757 }. In the oral cavity, the lip,
soft palate, and buccal mucosa are the most commonly affected subsites { 23459839 ; 23681074 }.
Rare cases originating from minor salivary glands of sinonasal mucosa have been reported
{ 25828788 ; 28980201 ; 29543674 }.
Clinical features
SC most
commonly
presents
as
a
painless
slowly
growing
mass
{ 20410810 ; 28781197 ; 30592355 }. SC with high grade transformation is associated with an
aggressive clinical behavior, a high propensity for cervical lymph node metastasis, risk of distant
metastasis, and a poor prognosis { 24145651 ; 25503077 ; 26089091 ; 28781197 }.
Imaging: SC typically shows a well-defined, predominantly cystic lesion with solid papillary
projections, and low incidence of associated pathological lymph nodes. Cystic areas are usually
T1W hyperintense or have a fluid-fluid level including a T1W hyperintense area. It has also been
suggested that haemosiderin deposition on MRI may help differentiate SC from acinic cell
carcinoma { 29493279 ; 30186957 ; 32642018 }.
Epidemiology
SC usually presents in adults with a mean patient age of 46.5 (range 10-86 years) and an equal
sex distribution { 20410810 ; 21989350 ; 23459839 ; 23821207 ; 25078757 }.
Etiology
No known causative factors have been identified.
Pathogenesis
Most cases of SC harbour a characteristic chromosomal translocation, t(12;15) (p13;q25) resulting
in an ETV6::NTRK3 fusion { 20410810 }. Small subset of cases demonstrates divergent molecular
findings
with
alternate ETV6::RET { 25651470 ; 26492182 ; 29076873 }, ETV6::MET { 29683815 }, ETV6::MA
ML3 { 30130630 }, and VIM::RET { 32675658 } fusions.
Macroscopic appearance
Grossly, SC has a rubbery, tan cut surface. The tumours are generally well-circumscribed and may
have a prominent cystic component { 23459839 ; 25503077 ; 31464708 }.
Histopathology
SC is usually well circumscribed but not encapsulated. The tumours have a lobulated growth
pattern separated by fibrous septa, and are composed of microcystic/solid, tubular, follicular, and
papillary-cystic structures with distinctive luminal secretions. It may have an infiltrative pattern with
occasional perineural invasion and may show abundant fibrosclerotic stroma with prominent, thick

369

hyalinized septae. Some SC show macrocystic morphology. The tumour cells have low grade
vesicular round-to-oval nuclei with finely granular chromatin and distinctive centrally located
nucleoli. The pale pink cytoplasm is granular to vacuolated. Cellular atypia is usually mild and
mitoses are rare.
Histochemistry: Abundant eosinophilic to bubbly secretions are positive for periodic acid-Schiff
(PAS, before and after diastase digestion) and Alcian-blue. Unlike acinic cell carcinoma, SC does
not show true PAS-positive secretory zymogen cytoplasmic granules.
Immunohistochemistry: SCs are positive for cytokeratin CK7, S100 protein, SOX10, vimentin, and
mammaglobin,
and
negative
for
p63,
p40,
NR4A3,
and
DOG1
{ 20410810 ; 25078757 ; 25456394 }.
Differential In contrast to SC, acinic cell carcinomas demonstrate intense apical membranous
staining for DOG1 around lumina and variable cytoplasmic positivity in most cases { 22460810 }.
Cytology
The moderately cellular smears show loosely cohesive sheets and papillary or cribriform tissue
fragments and dispersed cells in a usually mucinous background, although in some cases
hemosiderophages and blood may be seen. The cells show low to moderate N:C ratio, abundant
finely granular or vacuolated cytoplasm, with some cells resembling signet ring cells with large
vacuoles indenting the nuclei, and uniform round to oval nuclei with fine chromatin, distinct single
nucleoli and mild atypia { 23042752 ; 23225548 ; 24585770 ; 28738326 ; 30599505 ; 30942913 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Approximately 90% of SC harbour a characteristic chromosomal rearrangement, t(12;15) (p13;q25)
resulting in an ETV6::NTRK3 fusion { 20410810 }. Small subset of cases demonstrate divergent
molecular
findings
with
alternate ETV6::RET { 25651470 ; 26492182 ; 29076873 }, ETV6::MET { 29683815 }, ETV6::MA
ML3 { 30130630 }, and VIM::RET { 32675658 } gene fusions.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: single cell type with vacuolated colloid-like secretory material; no zymogen cytoplasmic
granules; IHC positivity for S100 protein, SOX10, and mammaglobin; lack of IHC staining for p40
and/or p63
Desirable: ETV6 or RET rearrangement demonstrated by FISH, RNA sequencing, or PCR
Staging
According to TNM classification in AJCC 8th ed
Prognosis and prediction
SC is usually an indolent salivary gland malignancy. Lymph node metastases are reported in as
many as 25% of cases { 23775296 ; 25078757 ; 25456394 ; 30592355 } but distant metastases
are rare { 22372712 ; 25456394 ; 25078757 ; 25503077 ; 29909898 }. Clinical stage and high
grade
transformation
are
the
main
adverse
prognostic
factors
{ 24145651 ; 25503077 ; 26089091 ; 28781197 }. There are no current predictive biomarkers in
SC, but translocation status itself may be a predictive marker with the ongoing development of
selective Trk inhibitors { 26884591 ; 32223935 }.

376

Microsecretory adenocarcinoma
Definition
Microsecretory adenocarcinoma (MSA) is a low-grade malignancy with an intercalated duct-like
phenotype and MEF2C::SS18 fusion.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2B66.Y Other specified malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified parts of mouth
2B65.Y Other specified malignant neoplasm of palate
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Almost all MSA have arisen in the oral cavity, with palate and buccal mucosa as the most frequently
affected subsites.
A
single
case
has
involved
the
parotid
gland
{ 31094920 ; 32687666 ; 33982215 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a painless, slow-growing mass.
Epidemiology
Fewer than 30 cases have been reported. Mean age is 49.5 years (range 17-83) with a slight female
predominance { 31094920 ; 32687666 ; 33982215 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
MSA is characterized by chromosomal rearrangement and fusion of the MEF2C gene (5q14.3)
with SS18 (18q11.2)
Macroscopic appearance
MSA grossly appears to be a non-ulcerated, well-circumscribed mass. They average 11 mm
(range, 6 to 30 mm).
Histopathology
MSA has very consistent morphologic features: (1) a microcystic-predominant growth pattern with
occasional cribriform structures or cords; (2) uniform intercalated duct-like tumour cells with
attenuated eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm; (3) monotonous oval hyperchromatic nuclei with
indistinct nucleoli; (4) abundant basophilic luminal secretions; (5) a variably cellular fibromyxoid
stroma; and (6) rounded borders with subtle infiltrative growth into nearby skeletal muscle, fat, or
minor salivary glands. Occasionally MSA is accompanied by pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia,
tumour-associated lymphoid proliferation, or metaplastic bone formation. Perineural invasion is
rare, necrosis is absent, and mitotic rates are low.
Immunohistochemistry: MSA has a consistent immunophenotype. It is positive for S100, SOX10,
and p63, and negative for p40, mammaglobin, and calponin. Occasional cases are focally SMApositive.
Differential Secretory carcinoma has more abundant cytoplasm, pink secretions, mammaglobin
positivity, and harbors different fusions (usually ETV6::NTRK3). Polymorphous adenocarcinoma
shows more architectural diversity and different genetic alterations (mutations or rearrangements
of PRKD1, 2 or 3). Adenoid cystic carcinoma has two cell populations, is far more infiltrative, and
harbors rearrangements of MYB, MYBL1 and/or NFIB. Sclerosing microcystic adenocarcinoma is
less cellular with more stroma, more cord-like growth, and a subtly biphasic tumour population.
Cytology
Not reported in cytopathological literature
Diagnostic molecular pathology
MEF2C::SS18 fusion can be demonstrated by RNA sequencing, RT-PCR, or SS18 break apart
FISH { 31094920 ; 32687666 ; 33394377 ; 33982215 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

377

Essential: microcystic-predominant growth pattern, bluish secretions, fibromyxoid stroma,
monotonous tumour cells, positivity for S100 and p63 but not p40 or mammaglobin
Desirable: SS18 rearrangement demonstrated by FISH, RNA sequencing, or PCR
Staging
TNM system as published by UICC
Prognosis and prediction
No reported cases of recurrence or metastasis

#24364
Microsecretory adenocarcinoma
The tumour often initially appears circumscribed, but close inspection reveals subtle
infiltration into nearby tissues such as skeletal muscle.

#24365

Microsecretory adenocarcinoma
The prototypical appearance of microsecretory adenocarcinoma is microcysts filled with
pale, bluish secretions, set in a cellular fibromyxoid stroma.

378

#24366

Microsecretory adenocarcinoma
The tumour cells are flattened with modest amounts of eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm
and monotonous, hyperchromatic oval nuclei.

The tumour has a consistent immunoprofile, with strong and diffuse immunoexpression
for S100 protein.

Polymorphous adenocarcinoma
Definition
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, conventional (PAC) is a malignant epithelial tumour characterized
by cytological uniformity, morphological diversity, an infiltrative growth pattern, and is usually
associated with alterations in PRDK gene family.
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, cribriform (cribriform adenocarcinoma of the salivary
glands; CASG) is characterized by predominant papillary and glomeruloid growth pattern, clear
nuclei, and common PRDK1, PRKD2, or PRKD3 fusions.
ICD-O coding
8525/3 Polymorphous adenocarcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B61 & XH5SD5 Malignant neoplasm of base of tongue & Polymorphous adenocarcinoma
2B65.Y & XH5SD5 Other specified malignant neoplasm of palate & Polymorphous
adenocarcinoma
2B67.Y & XH5SD5 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Polymorphous
adenocarcinoma
2B6A.Y & XH5SD5 Other specified malignant neoplasms of oropharynx & Polymorphous
adenocarcinoma
2B6B.Y & XH5SD5 Other specified malignant neoplasms of nasopharynx & Polymorphous
adenocarcinoma
2B6D.Y & XH5SD5 Other specified malignant neoplasms of hypopharynx & Polymorphous
adenocarcinoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: polymorphous low-grade adenocarcinoma; cribriform adenocarcinoma of
tongue/salivary gland
Subtype(s)
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, conventional subtype
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, cribriform subtype (cribriform adenocarcinoma of the salivary
glands; CASG)
Localization
Over 95% of PAC involves minor salivary glands or seromucous glands of the upper aerodigestive
tract.
The
palate
is
the
most
common site
{ 10421256 ; 11023093 ; 20403856 ; 25877006 ; 27016011 ; 27454943 }. Other sites include
buccal mucosa, retromolar trigone, floor of mouth, and sinonasal tract/nasopharynx
{ 10421256 ; 11023093 ; 20403856 ; 25877006 ; 27016011 ; 27454943 }. CASG are localized
mostly in the tongue, other site include the soft palate, retromolar buccal mucosa, tonsils, and upper
lip
{ 28614209 }. Less
than
5%
of
cases
affect
major
salivary
glands
{ 24942367 ; 27454943 ; 31492931 }.
Clinical features
PACs typically present as a slowly-growing painless mass of variable duration { 10421256 }. Very
few PACs, conventional subtype present with a lymph node metastasis { 31917707 }, but cervical
lymph node metastases are common in CASG { 21716087 ; 23821209 ; 28614209 ; 31917707 }.
Imaging: PAC has nonspecific imaging features, including occasional invasion / erosion of adjacent
bone, but otherwise smooth borders with a T2W hypointense fibrous capsule and progressive
enhancement pattern. Imaging is used to assess local tumour extent and identify potential
metastatic lymph nodes{ 31312967 }.
Epidemiology
PAC is the second most common intraoral salivary gland carcinoma { 2845326 } with an annual
incidence rate of 0.051 per 100,000 { 18327037 }. The female-to-male ratio is about 2:1
{ 10421256 ; 11023093 ; 25877006 ; 27454943 }. The patient age ranges from 16-94 years, with a
mean in the 60s { 10421256 ; 11023093 ; 25877006 ; 27454943 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
382

Pathogenic alterations of the PRKD gene family are highly prevalent. Approximately 70-89% of
PACs and 0-20% of CASGs harbour PRKD1 p.Glu710Asp activating hotspot mutations, whereas
70-94% of CASGs and 6-11% of PACs contain rearrangement of one of
the PRKD1, PRKD2 or PRKD3 genes { 24942367 ; 25240283 ; 31492931 ; 31917707 }.
Macroscopic appearance
PACs often present as firm, solid, unencapsulated, yellow/tan, ovoid submucosal masses
{ 10421256 }. Papillary-cystic tumours, particularly in CASG, may have gross cystic change and
haemorrhage.
Histopathology
PAC, the conventional subtype is typically an unencapsulated submucosal mass. The tumours are
characterized by cytological uniformity, histological diversity, and an infiltrative growth pattern.
Neoplastic cells are uniform in shape, with scant cytoplasm, bland oval nuclei, open chromatin,
occasional small nucleoli and nuclear grooves. A prominent feature is the wide variation of
architectural patterns including single file, fascicular, trabecular, tubular, microcystic, solid and
rarely papillary-cystic. A streaming or eddy-like formations can be present at the peripheral
boundary of the tumour. Foci of oncocytic, clear, squamous, apocrine, or mucous cells and
microcalcifications can be observed. Tumour stroma can be myxoid, mucinous or hyalinized.
Perineural involvement is frequent, being seen in approximately two thirds of cases, often with a
targetoid pattern. This pattern is also observed around vessels. High-grade transformation
characterized by marked nuclear atypia, mitotic activity and prominent necrosis, has been reported
in PAC { 12207787 ; 29594833 }.
PAC, cribriform subtype/cribriform adenocarcinoma of salivary gland (CASG) was initially reported
at the base of tongue { 10583573 } and later in other minor salivary gland sites { 21716087 } and
the parotid gland { 31917707 }. CASG is considered as a separate entity by some authors
{ 10583573 ; 21712087 ; 23821209 }; or a subtype within the PAC morphological spectrum by
others { 31917707 }. CASG is characterized by a multinodular growth pattern separated by fibrous
septa, relatively uniform solid, cribriform and microcystic architecture, and optically clear nuclei.
Glomeruloid and papillary structures, peripheral palisading and clefting is typically observed. CASG
is associated with a propensity to base of tongue location, higher risk of lymph node metastasis,
and higher frequency of PRKD gene rearrangement { 21716087 ; 24942367 ; 31492931 }.
Immunohistochemistry: PAC is immunoreactive for cytokeratins (e.g. CK7, in 100% of cases)
{ 17593078 } and S100 protein (97% to 100%) { 9675591 ; 27454943 }. Staining for p63 is reported
in 78% to 100% of cases, whereas p40 is typically negative; this pattern is helpful in the differential
diagnosis { 27454943 }. Other positive immunomarkers include mammaglobin (67% to 100%),
CD117 (60%), CEA (54%), GFAP (15%), MSA (13%), and EMA (12%)
{ 9675591 ; 12118104 ; 18084258 ; 23773480 ; 25040635 }.
Cytology
Due to their intra-oral location, PAC are rarely sampled by FNAB, and are rarely seen in metastases
to neck lymph nodes. If accessible, smears shows uniform polygonal, moderately sized tumour
cells with relatively dense cytoplasm and round to oval nuclei, forming pseudopapillary, tubular and
irregular solid tissue fragments, and may show small acini and some dense hyalinised stroma
{ 32421929 }. Occasional nuclear grooves and small metachromatic globules may be seen, and
misinterpretation as papillary thyroid carcinoma is a rare pitfall. The differential diagnosis is with
pleomorphic adenomas, which have fibrillary stroma, and adenoid cystic carcinoma with its usual
cribriform architecture.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Detection of PRKD1 p.Glu710Asp hotspot mutation or translocation of one of the PRKD1, PRKD2
or PRKD3 genes is highly specific for the diagnosis of PAC { 29266837 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: cytologic uniformity, architectural diversity, and infiltrative border
Desirable: Immunopositivity for S100. PRKD1 hotspot (PAC conventional subtype) mutation or
translocation of PRKD1, PRKD2 or PRKD3 genes CASG) in selected cases
Staging
TNM system as published by UICC
Prognosis and prediction

383

The overall prognosis of PAC is excellent with a 10-year disease specific survival of 94 to 99%
{ 10421256 ; 20403856 ; 25229805 ; 25877006 ; 27016011 }. The rate of local recurrence and
regional metastasis ranges from 5 to 33%, and from 9 to 15% respectively
{ 10421256 ; 25229805 ; 25877006 ; 27454943 }. Rare cases of distant metastases have been
reported { 20403856 ; 25877006 ; 27454943 }.
Factors that are associated with high risk of regional metastasis and/or an unfavorable prognosis
include high-grade transformation { 20403856 }, cribriform variant/CASG histology
{ 21716087 }, PRKD gene fusion { 31492931 }, 30% cribriform and 10% papillary architecture
{ 27454943 }, necrosis, extrapalatal and hard palate site, angiolymphatic and bone invasion, and/or
perineural infiltration around large nerves { 29761209 }.

#23924

Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, classic subtype
The tumour cells have uniform small oval nuclei, scanty cytoplasm, open chromatin and
inconspicuous nucleoli. Neurotrophism is seen.

384

#23927
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma
S100 is diffusely positive in PAC, regardless of morphologic subtype. (Please make a
composite of 1a-d)

#27164

Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, cribriform subtype
Glomeruloid structures.

385

#27165
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, cribriform type
Glomeruloid structures.

#27173
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, cribriform subtype
Optical nuclear chromatin clearing is a characteristic finding in this tumour. Note the
papillary and glomeruloid architecture.

386

#27748

Polymorhous adenocarcinoma, cribriform subtype
The low power photomicrograph demonstrates numerous patterns, with easily identified
papillary structures and glomeruloid bodies. Fibrous connective tissue stroma is present.
The nuclei show open chromatin, even at this magnification.

#27749
Polymorhous adenocarcinoma, cribriform subtype
Islands of neoplastic cells are separated by a fibrous connective tissue stroma. The cells
show glomeruloid projections into the lumen with clefting artefacts. The nuclei are
monotonous.

387

#27750

Polymorhous adenocarcinoma, cribriform subtype
Numerous patterns, with easily identified papillary structures and glomeruloid bodies. The
nuclei show open chromatin. Fibrous connective tissue stroma is present.

#27751

Polymorhous adenocarcinoma, cribriform subtype
Solid growth pattern and optically clear nuclei reminiscent of papillary thyroid cancer
nuclei.
388

#28729
Polymorphous adenocarcinoma, classic subtype
Various patterns, including tubules, trabeculae, microcysts, and cribriform architecture,
are seen with a mucoid/myxoid background. The tumour is often arranged in a targetoid
or streaming fashion in the periphery or around nerves.

#23467

Polymorphous adenocarcinoma originating from the palate
Various patterns, including tubules, trabeculae, microcysts, and cribriform architecture,
are seen. The tumour cells have uniform small oval nuclei, scanty cytoplasm, open
chromatin and inconspicuous nucleoli.

#23468

Cribriform adenocarcinoma of salivary gland
The tumour originated from the base of tongue. Fluorescence in situ hybridization detects
PRKD3 translocation in this tumour.

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
Definition
Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma (HCCC) is a carcinoma composed of clear and eosinophilic cells
in a variably hyalinized stroma, usually associated with EWSR1 rearrangement.
ICD-O coding
8310/3 Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B61 & XH6L02 Malignant neoplasm of base of tongue & Clear cell carcinoma, NOS
2B65.Y & XH6L02 Other specified malignant neoplasm of palate & Clear cell carcinoma, NOS
2B67.Y & XH6L02 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Clear cell carcinoma,
NOS
2B6A.Y & XH6L02 Other specified malignant neoplasms of oropharynx & Clear cell carcinoma,
NOS
2B6B.Y & XH6L02 Other specified malignant neoplasms of nasopharynx & Clear cell carcinoma,
NOS
2B6D.Y & XH6L02 Other specified malignant neoplasms of hypopharynx & Clear cell carcinoma,
NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: clear cell carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most HCCCs arise from oral minor salivary glands in locations, including soft palate, tongue, floor
of
mouth,
lips,
buccal
mucosa,
retromolar
trigone,
and
gingival
sites
{ 7506496 ; 21484932 ; 27898192 }. A minority develop in other sites including oropharynx,
nasopharynx,
parotid,
sinonasal
tract,
larynx,
and
hypopharynx
{ 7506496 ; 21484932 ; 27898192 }. Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma is morphologically and
genetically similar and arises within the gnathic bones { 23715163 }.
Clinical features

390

Most HCCCs present with localized swelling. The overlying mucosa may be ulcerated. Pain and
symptoms associated with cranial nerve involvement are rare { 23821218 }.
Imaging: Typical findings are a well-defined lesion, usually in the oral cavity or tongue base, with
smooth margins, hypo-to iso-echoic on ultrasound. It is homogeneous, T2W hyperintense and
enhances uniformly on CT and MRI. Cervical lymph node and distant metastases are uncommon
{ 17213438 ; 26600962 }.
Epidemiology
HCCC is quoted as having equal sex distribution, although a slight female predominance was seen
{ 21484932 }. The tumour develops in adults with a median age at diagnosis of 56 years (range 2387 years) { 7506496 ; 21484932 ; 27898192 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Most HCCCs harbor a translocation involving EWSR1 and ATF1 which typically results in a fusion
between EWSR1 exon 11 and ATF1 exon 3 { 21484932 }. Other fusions (EWSR1 exon 15
and ATF1 exon 5; EWSR1::CREM) have been described { 29975250 ; 32816231 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours range from 4 - 50 mm. The cut surface is white-tan and solid. The tumour is well
circumscribed but unencapsulated. Gross invasion into surrounding structures may be seen
{ 21484932 ; 23821218 ; 27898192 }.
Histopathology
HCCC is composed of clear or eosinophilic cells. Despite its name, tumours composed entirely of
clear cells are rare and some completely lack clear cells. The tumour is arranged in nests, cords,
and trabeculae. Ducts and small cysts may be seen. Squamous differentiation and mucocytes are
common
{ 1706299 ; 7506496 ; 18980290 ; 20596970 ; 21484932 ; 23821218 }.
Increased
mitoses, marked atypia, and necrosis are rare and when seen, represent high-grade transformation
(HGT) { 22350795 ; 33825717 }.
HCCCs are unencapsulated with cells infiltrating the surrounding tissues, particularly skeletal
muscle. Tumours arising in the oral cavity may connect with the overlying epithelium and show a
pagetoid pattern of spread. Perineural invasion is seen in about half of cases. The stroma ranges
from densely hyalinized basement membrane-like to desmoplastic or fibrocellular, and the
juxtaposition of these two stroma types is largely pathognomonic for HCCC.
Histochemistry: The clear cells are PAS positive and diastase sensitive. Mucicarmine highlights
intracellular mucin { 23821218 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The tumour cells express epithelial markers including CK7, CK19, CK14,
CAM5.2, and EMA. They also usually express p63, p40 and CK5/6, supporting squamous
differentiation. Myoepithelial markers including S100 protein, MSA, SMA, and calponin are negative
{ 1706299 ; 7506496 ; 18980290 ; 20596970 ; 21484932 ; 23821218 }. Some tumours express
p16 which can be a diagnostic dilemma when the tumour arises in the oropharynx; however they
lack transcriptionally active HPV { 29076874 }.
Differential The entities include clear cell mucoepidermoid carcinoma, epithelial-myoepithelial
carcinoma, myoepithelial carcinoma, oncocytoma, myoepithelioma, and metastatic clear cell
carcinoma, with distinction sometimes requiring molecular studies.
Cytology
This lesion is rarely sampled by FNAB, and shows large cells with clear to eosinophilic cytoplasm,
distinct cell borders and round nuclei lacking prominent nucleoli, and in some cases metachromatic
stroma { 26715185 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Demonstration of an EWSR1 fusion gene is helpful in selected cases
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: nests, cords, and trabeculae of clear and/or eosinophilic cells in a hyalinized stroma
Desirable: EWSR1 fusion in selected cases
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification

391

Prognosis and prediction
HCCC are slow growing with a good prognosis. The 5-year rate of local recurrence, lymph node
metastasis, and distant metastasis is 19%, 3%, and 2%, respectively { 27898192 }. Locoregional
recurrence may be associated with incomplete resection and/or perineural invasion. Cases with
HGT have been described, with distant metastases identified { 22350795 ; 33825717 }. Death from
disease is rarely reported { 14747068 }.

#24328

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
The tumour shows a combination of clear and eosinophilic cords of cells with perineural
invasion.

#24329

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
The tumour shows bland clear cells in cords and small nests in a sclerotic stroma.
392

#24330

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
The tumour may connect to the surface in the oral cavity, and may show pagetoid spread,
or pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia of the squamous mucosa (shown here).

#24331

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
Some hyalinizing clear cell carcinomas have minimal clear cell change. This tumour is
mostly eosinophilic with only a small focus of clear cells.

393

#24332

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
About half of cases show focal mucin and occasional cases have conspicuous goblet type
mucinous cells (Mucicarmine).

#24333

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
The majority of cases stain diffusely with p63.

394

#24334
Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma
Similarly, most hyalinizing clear cell carcinomas stain diffusely with high molecular weight
keratins. This staining pattern reflects a squamous phenotype and is a pitfall in the
differential diagnosis with mucoepidermoid carcinoma.

Basal cell adenocarcinoma
Definition
Basal cell adenocarcinoma (BCAC) is a malignant infiltrative basaloid salivary gland tumour
composed of a mixture of basal and ductal cells.
ICD-O coding
8147/3 Basal cell adenocarcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Z & XH9SA7 Malignant neoplasms of parotid gland, unspecified & Basal cell
adenocarcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: carcinoma ex-monomorphic adenoma, malignant dermal analogue tumour
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most BCACs occur in the parotid gland (>90%) { 2326038 ; 8734419 ; 17178498 ; 25555241 }.
Clinical features
BCAC usually presents as a slow-growing nodule in the parotid gland. Syndromic cases may also
have multiple skin adnexal tumours { 2326038 ; 2827601 ; 9726688 ; 19455704 }.
Epidemiology
BCAC accounts
for
less
than
3%
of
malignant
salivary
tumours
{ 8734419 ; 17178498 ; 25555241 }. Most affected patients are in their 6th-7th decades
{ 8734419 ; 33526221 ; 17178498 ; 25555241 }. There is no sex predilection.
Etiology
Some BCACs (~15%) occur in the setting of familial/multiple cylindromatosis
syndromes { 12023583 }. Most BCACs arise de novo, but a few develop from preexisting basal cell
adenoma (BCA) { 2769045 ; 3977010 ; 9198102 ; 27327192 }.
395

Pathogenesis
BCAC may show a complex genetic profile { 27259009 ; 27224988 }. A subset
shows CYLD or CTNNB1 alterations { 12023583 ; 29496310 ; 29463883 ; 30053869 }
Macroscopic appearance
BCACs typically range from 20-40 mm { 26451535 ; 33526221 } and present as a firm,
unencapsulated, tan masses.
Histopathology
BCACs show tubulo-trabecular, membranous or solid growth with the latter two patterns
predominating. Tumour nests show peripheral palisading of dark cells with paler cells and centrally
located ducts. Squamous and sebaceous elements may be seen. Nuclei are vesicular. ~25%
show perineural and vascular invasion; anaplasia is rare.
Immunohistochemistry: Epithelial and myoepithelial markers highlights the dual cell composition of
BCAC. Nuclear β-catenin immunoexpression is present in a subset of BCACs
{ 24143938 ; 27259009 ; 29224720 ; 29496310 ; 30053869 }.
Differential Presence of peripheral palisading and focal squamous metaplasia can help to
separate BCAC from adenoid cystic carcinoma. BCAC is distinguished from BCA by infiltration,
necrosis and mitoses (especially when >4 per 2mm2) { 9452259 }.
Cytology
BCAC shows very similar cytopathological features to BCA (see BCA). Squamoid and sebaceous
features may be seen focally helping to distinguish BCAC from adenoid cystic carcinoma
{ 8701930 }, but differentiating BCA from BCAC and from adenoid cystic carcinoma is not always
possible, and the term 'basaloid neoplasm' is often appropriate.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: invasive growth; basaloid morphology; peripheral palisading with paler inner cells and
ducts; dual population immunophenotype
Staging
UICC
Prognosis and prediction
Local recurrence rates are about 37% { 8952553 } but complete surgical excision with clear margins
is curative in >90% of the cases. Regional lymph node and distant metastasis and disease related
death are rare (<10%). Age and T categorization are key prognosticators { 26451535 }.

The carcinoma (right) is arising in a background of multiple precursor lesions (left). In
contrast to the precursor lesions, tumour nests are larger and bounded by more profound
sclerosis.

The tumour shows infiltration into soft tissue.

Aggressive features such as high cytonuclear grade with mitoses are rare.

#25390
Basal cell adenocarcinoma
Aggressive features such as high cytonuclear grade with necrosis are rare.

Basal cell adenocarcinoma
This tumour shows a tan-white homogenous cut surface and a multinodular pushing
border which may be deceptively innocuous.

399

#24811
Basal cell adenocarcinoma
The tumour shows a multinodular growth pattern with fibrous septation and infiltration of
adjacent adipose tissue. Perineural invasion is focally noted. Tumour nests range from
membranous with hyaline droplets to solid and show prominent peripheral palisading of a
"dark" cell layer. Centrally, squamous differentiation and scattered ducts are noted.

Intraductal carcinoma
Definition
Intraductal carcinoma (IDC) is a salivary gland malignancy characterized by papillary, cribriform,
and solid proliferations that are entirely or predominantly intraductal
ICD-O coding
8500/2 Intraductal carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
B67.Z & XH4V32 Malignant neoplasms of parotid gland, unspecified & Ductal carcinoma in situ,
NOS
2E60.0 Carcinoma in situ of lip, oral cavity or pharynx
Related terminology
Acceptable: intercalated duct carcinoma
Not recommended: low-grade cribriform cystadenocarcinoma; low-grade salivary duct
carcinoma; ductal carcinoma
Subtype(s)
Intercalated duct IDC; apocrine IDC; oncocytic IDC; mixed IDC
Localization
IDC
arises
predominantly
in
the
parotid
gland
{ 6298331 ; 15252310 ; 29443014 ; 29574881 ; 30045065 ; 31162284 },
with
uncommon
involvement of intraparotid lymph nodes { 15252310 ; 21442195 ; 32661669 }, submandibular
gland { 15252310 }, and oral cavity { 6298331 ; 15043319 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with longstanding painless swellings. IDC are indolent neoplasms, but rare cases
of invasive carcinoma ex IDC were reported { 29443014 ; 31162284 }.
Epidemiology
IDC is rare, and involves adults of wide age range (25-93 years) with an even sex distribution
{ 6298331 ; 15252310 ; 21442195 ; 29443014 ; 29574881 ; 31162284 ; 32661669 ; 33526215 }.
400

Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Intercalated
duct
IDC
usually
has NCOA4::RET,
rarely STRN::ALK, TUT1::ETV5,
or KIAA217::RET; some are fusion-negative { 29443014 ; 31162284 ; 32661669 }. The oncocytic
type may harbour TRIM33::RET, NCOA4::RET, or BRAF p.V600E mutations; some have no
alterations
{ 29574881 ; 33135196 }.
Mixed
intercalated
duct-apocrine
IDC usually
harbours TRIM27::RET, occasionally NCOA4::RET { 30045065 ; 31162284 }. Purely apocrine
IDC harbours multiple mutations (e.g., HRAS, PIK3CA, TP53), some are fusion-negative
{ 29443014 ; 33086236 } or harbour TRIM27::RET fusion { 30045065 }. One study reported that
myoepithelial and ductal cells of IDC harbour the same fusion { 33086236 }.
Macroscopic appearance
IDC is well-circumscribed and variably solid and cystic, measuring up to 46 mm (mean 14 mm).
Histopathology
All subtypes of IDC display rounded cysts and lobules, with variable solid, cribriform, micropapillary,
and Roman bridge-type ductal proliferations, surrounded by a continuous, flattened layer of
myoepithelial cells. The ductal cells vary with each subtype.
Subtypes: Intercalated duct IDC has small amphophilic-to-eosinophilic cells with oval nuclei.
Apocrine IDC has large cells with bubbly eosinophilic cytoplasm with snouting and decapitation
secretions. Oncocytic IDC has cells with abundant, granular eosinophilic cytoplasm and round
nuclei with prominent nucleoli, while mixed IDC shows hybrid features.
Intercalated duct and oncocytic IDC exhibit low-grade features and low mitotic rates, while
IDCs with apocrine components can be low- or high-grade. Background often shows degenerative
changes (fibrosis, inflammation, cholesterol clefts, haemorrhage). Frank invasive growth with loss
of myoepithelial cells can occasionally be seen in all types of IDC { 29443014 ; 31162284 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Intercalated duct and oncocytic IDCs are positive for S100, SOX10, and
mammaglobin, but negative for AR and GCDFP-15, while apocrine IDC has the opposite staining
pattern. Mixed IDC shows mixed immunoprofiles of the components present. Myoepithelial cells of
IDC are consistently positive for p40/p63, CK14, SMA, and calponin.
Differential Intraductal proliferations seen in carcinoma-ex-pleomorphic adenoma and sclerosing
polycystic adenoma are distinguished by their background elements (chondromyxoid stroma and
acinar cells, respectively). Cystadenoma lacks a fenestrated or cribriform appearance and the
complete layer of myoepithelial cells. Mucoepidermoid carcinoma, secretory carcinoma, and
salivary duct carcinoma do not have a complete circumferential layer of myoepithelial cells, and in
epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma the myoepithelial cells are larger and much more prominent.
Cytology
Low-grade IDCs exhibit populations of both mildly atypical ductal cells and smaller myoepithelial
cells with a clean background, while high-grade apocrine IDC has large, pleomorphic ductal cells
and necrosis { 31749241 ; 34597458 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Demonstration of molecular alteration can assist in diagnosis. Routine break apart FISH is often
falsely negative in IDC with NCOA4/RET, which is a tight inversion due to an intrachromosomal
rearrangement { 29443014 ; 30045065 ; 33086236 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: rounded lobules of ductal neoplastic cells; surrounded by a rim of myoepithelial cells;
ductal cells positive for S100, SOX10, and mammaglobin and/or AR
Desirable: cystic component; identification of RET fusion (intercalated duct, oncocytic, mixed
IDC) BRAF p.V600E mutation (oncocytic IDC), or PI3 kinase pathway mutations (apocrine IDC) in
selected cases
Staging
IDC is currently regarded as in situ disease by UICC staging. Staging of invasive carcinoma ex
IDC according to the UICC TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
Pure IDC of all types behave indolently following complete excision. Frankly invasive carcinomas
ex-IDC can behave aggressively { 29443014 ; 31162284 }.

be seen in this mixed IDC.

eosinophilic cytoplasm, apical
snouting, and large nucleoli.

have less, more amphophilic

indistinct nucleoli. The nests of
both components are rimmed by
a subtle layer of flattened
myoepithelial cells. This case
harboured TRIM27::RET.

#25626

This case consists of tightly packed, solid nests of oncocytic cells with abundant granular
eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei with small, distinct nucleoli. This tumour was
positive for S100 and negative for AR (not shown), and harboured BRAF p.V600E.

#28685

#28736
Intraductal carcinoma, intercalated duct cell subtype
This IDC has a typical low-power appearance of large cysts and rounded lobules with
solid, cribriform, and micropapillary growth. Although cell morphology cannot be seen at
this magnification, the blue appearance is in keeping with intercalated duct type IDC.
Hemorrhage and cholesterol clefts are evident. This case harboured NCOA4::RET.

406

#25640
Intraductal carcinoma, mixed intercalated duct type
This IDC is partially cystic, with solid and cribriform lobules in the cyst wall. There are
degenerative changes in the form of fibrosis and hemorrhage. The ductal cells resemble
intercalated ducts, with modest amounts of amphophilic cytoplasm and small, round oval
nuclei with indistinct nucleoli. There is minimal atypia or mitotic activity. Each nest and
cyst is surrounded by an intact layer of flattened myoepithelial cells with clear cytoplasm.
These cells are subtle on H&E, and easier to visualize with immunohistochemistry (e.g.,
p40 or SMA). This case harbored NCOA4-RET, which is the most common molecular
alteration in this IDC type.

Salivary duct carcinoma
Definition
Salivary duct carcinoma (SDC) is an aggressive carcinoma resembling mammary ductal
carcinoma, most typically with apocrine (immuno)phenotype.
ICD-O coding
8500/3 Salivary duct carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2E60.0 Carcinoma in situ of lip, oral cavity or pharynx
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Sarcomatoid
Mucin-rich
Micropapillary
Basal-like
Oncocytic
Localization
Most tumours involve major salivary glands { 12524620 ; 15900577 ; 23606370 }.
Clinical features
SDC presents as a rapidly growing tumour, with facial nerve palsy, pain, and cervical
lymphadenopathy. If arising from PA, a rapid increase in size of a longstanding pre-existing mass
is common.
Imaging: On cross-sectional imaging SDC most commonly presents as an ill-defined, infiltrative
salivary gland mass with frequent calcification and necrosis. Foci of marked hypointensity on T2weighted MR images may be a useful radiological feature to suggest the diagnosis { 22307272 }.

407

Epidemiology
SDC has a distinct male predilection with peak incidence in the fifth to seventh decades of life
{ 25871467 ; 31764219 }.
Etiology
None
Pathogenesis
Androgen receptor (AR) copy-number gain and splice variants have been reported { 25316813 }.
The most frequent genetic alterations are mutations in TP53 (55%), HRAS (23%), PIK3CA (23%),
amplification
of
ERBB2
(35%)
{ 27103403 }, PTEN deletion, and BRAF mutations
{ 25723113 ; 29416736 ; 32421944 }. PLAG1 or HMGA2 rearrangements, markers of pre-existing
PA, are identified in about half of SDCs { 23738717 ; 25439740 ; 27379604 }. A small subset of
SDC harbors ALK { 30946933 ; 33871952 } and ETV6::NTRK3 fusions { 32124419 }.
Macroscopic appearance
SDCs are infiltrative, firm, tan-white masses, frequently involving adjacent structures and may
contain well-defined nodules of pre-existing PA.
Histopathology
SDCs display complex solid, cribriform, and papillary-cystic architecture with frequent
comedonecrosis { 4301301 }. The cells have large pleomorphic nuclei with coarse chromatin and
prominent
nucleoli,
and
abundant
eosinophilic,
typically
apocrine
cytoplasm
{ 25723113 ; 25871467 }. Lymphovascular and perineural invasion are common { 31764219 }. A
hyalinized nodule of a pre-existing PA may be present. Rarely, SDC may be purely in situ
{ 18983607 }.
Subtypes: The sarcomatoid subtype of SDC contains areas of highly atypical spindle cells,
occasionally with foci of heterologous differentiation (e.g., osteoid production)
{ 10685635 ; 15806515 }. In the mucin–rich SDC there are significant pools of extracellular mucin
{ 12883239 ; 22431183 }. The micropapillary SDC includes small tumour nests, without
fibrovascular cores, with prominent retraction from adjacent stroma, and “inside-out” pattern of EMA
immunostaining { 23821208 }. Basal-like SDC is characterized by a less abundant and more
amphophilic rather than apocrine cytoplasm { 22882517 ; 23821208 }. Oncocytic SDC is defined
by abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm { 23821208 }. SDC with rhabdoid like features
contains usually dyscohesive ovoid cells with eccentric nuclei; immunohistochemically, the
rhabdoid cells are cytokeratin positive and negative for myoepithelial markers { 33538422 }. The
current definition of SDC subtypes does not include an universally agreed on minimal threshold for
variant morphology. All subtypes are commonly accompanied by areas of conventional SDC.
Immunohistochemistry: AR is expressed in about 90% of SDCs, indicative of apocrine
immunophenotype { 9831198 ; 22882517 ; 24553861 ; 25871467 ; 28938615 ; 29492965 }. Diffus
e and strong immunoreactivity for ERBB2/HER2 is identified in about one-third of SDCs
{ 28938615 ; 29492965 ; 32421944 ; 33526216 }. Cytokeratin 7 is consistently positive, while S100
and SOX-10 is negative. Staining for p63 may help to identify the intraductal component by
highlighting basal/myoepithelial cells surrounding neoplastic cells.
Differential
This includes other salivary carcinomas with high-grade transformation
{ 23821210 ; 25871467 ; 33825717 }.
Cytology
Often markedly cellular smears show irregular crowded tissue fragments that may show a cribriform
architecture and consist of large polygonal cells showing well defined cytoplasm, pleomorphic
nuclei, prominent nuceloli and plentiful mitoses, in a necrotic background, resembling Grade 3
carcinoma, no special type of breast { 9812225 ; 18528886 ; 32421944 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Because systemic therapies targeting androgen receptor, ERRB2 amplification,
phosphatidylinositol 3 kinase (PI3K) pathway, including mutations of PIK3CA and phosphatase and
tensin homolog (PTEN) loss, and BRAF p.V600E are being investigated, molecular investigation
may be warranted { 31437521 ; 32717621 ; 33526216 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: high-grade carcinoma, most typically with apocrine features
Desirable: AR expression

408

Staging
TNM system as published by UICC / AJCC
Prognosis and prediction
SDC is the most aggressive salivary tumour, with most patients presenting with regional lymph
node or distant metastases { 23606370 ; 25871467 ; 29492965 } and disease progression within
3-5 years { 15900577 ; 23606370 ; 25916947 ; 31764219 }.

#23930

Gross presentation of salivary duct carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma. Irregular tanwhite mass involves parotid gland (right) and extends to the skin. In the center of the
mass, there is a rounded focus of pre-existing hyalinized pleomorphic adenoma.

necrotic areas, and hypocellular hyalinized stroma of likely pre-existing pleomorphic
adenoma (lower part of the image).

#23929
Salivary duct carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
Mass involving parotid gland, infiltrative, surrounding blood vessels and excretory ducts.
In the left upper corner there is an encapsulated cystic hyalinized hypocellular nodule

architecture. Otherwise, the predominant growth pattern is solid. Foci of necrosis are
common. Cytologically, neoplastic cells show amphophilic cytoplasm and pleomorphic
nuclei with coarse chromatin and prominent nucleoli.

Myoepithelial carcinoma
Definition
Myoepithelial carcinoma (MECA) is a malignant salivary neoplasm that is almost exclusively
composed of myoepithelial cells and has an invasive growth.
ICD-O coding
8982/3 Myoepithelial carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Y & XH43E6 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Myoepithelial
carcinoma
2B65.Y & XH43E6 Other specified malignant neoplasms of palate & Myoepithelial carcinoma
2B68.2 & XH43E6 Other specified malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands &
Myoepithelial carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: malignant myoepithelioma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most tumours occur in the parotid gland followed by the palate, and the submandibular gland
{ 25970687 ; 33027073 }.
Clinical features
Patients often present with a painless mass, sometimes with recent rapid growth
{ 25970687 ; 28739497 }.
Imaging: Cross-sectional imaging shows an irregular, lobulated, ill-defined, heterogeneously
enhancing salivary gland mass, with avid FDG uptake on PET-CT. Lesions are often T1W and T2W
hypointense on MRI and some demonstrate punctate calcification on CT. Imaging is used to assess
the primary tumour and presence of lymph node and distant metastases (including to lungs, skin,
liver, bone and brain) { 28153562 ; 32804766 }.

415

Epidemiology
MECA is under-recognized. It may present at any age and has no sex predilection
{ 25970687 ; 28739497 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
PLAG1 fusions are identified in over 50% of both de novo and MECA ex-PA with different fusion
partner
genes,
including FGFR1, TGFBR3,
and
others
{ 29084941 ; 33027073 }. HMGA2 and EWSR1 fusions are detected in small subsets of tumours
{ 29084941 ; 33526224 }. EWSR1 rearrangement without fusion may be present in clear
cell MECA { 25581728 ; 33027073 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The tumour is typically unencapsulated, and has a gray to tan-white cut surfaces. Hemorrhage,
necrosis, and cystic change may occur.
Histopathology
MECA may arise de novo or within a preexisting pleomorphic adenoma (PA) ) and is the second
most common histologic subtype of carcinoma ex-PA { 20338616 ; 25970687 }. It exhibits diverse
morphologies with different cell types (spindle, epithelioid, plasmacytoid, vacuolated, and clear)
and architectures (solid, trabecular, and reticular) { 8732339 ; 9762928 ; 24446021 }. The stroma
can be myxoid, myxochondroid, or hyalinized. The uniform cellular myoepithelial proliferation with
the multinodular pattern and the transition from peripheral hypercellular zones to central
hypocellular areas are characteristic and help to differentiate MECA from PA { 30789358 }. Focal
duct-like or squamous differentiation may occur { 25581728 }. The presence of PA and tumour
necrosis correlate with worse outcome { 25970687 }.
Immunohistochemistry: MECA expresses cytokeratins, SOX10, S100, and myoepithelial markers
such as SMA, calponin, and p63/p40 { 9762928 ; 20338616 ; 25970687 ; 30789358 }.
Differential MECA is often cytologically bland and may therefore be under-recognized as malignant
and misclassified as a myoepithelial-rich PA { 30789358 }.
Cytology
In cytological specimens MECA shows a mixture of spindle, plasmacytoid, and epithelioid
cells. MECA cannot be distinguished from cellular PA on cytology { 11836700 ; 30789358 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: myoepithelial differentiation and invasive growth
Desirable: zonal pattern with hypercellular periphery and hypocellular center
Staging
TNM system
Prognosis and prediction
MECA has a diverse clinical behavior but is relatively aggressive even when it is intracapsular or
minimally invasive MECA ex-PA { 20338616 ; 25970687 ; 30789358 }. The risks of local
recurrence and distant metastasis are 35% and 22%, respectively { 30789358 }. MECA seems to
have
a
propensity
to
distant
rather
than
lymph
node
metastasis
{ 25636455 ; 25896567 ; 25970687 ; 30789358 }.

416

#25324

Myoepithelial carcinoma
Lobulated multinodular pattern with hypercellular periphery and hypocellular center.

#25325

Myoepithelial carcinoma
Spindle and epithelioid /plasmacytoid myoepithelial cells with myxoid stroma.

417

#26942
Myoepithelial carcinoma of parotid gland
Clear cells represent a prominent component of a subset of myoepithelial carcinomas.
Squamous pearls or overt squamous differentiation are present.

#26943
Myoepithelial carcinoma of parotid gland
The tumour has a multinodular architecture divided by fibrous bands. Characteristic
feature is a zonal arrangement with a hypercellular peripheral rim surrounding a
hypocellular and often myxoid center.

418

#25322

Myoepithelial carcinoma
Cellular proliferation of myoepithelial cells with multinodular invasive pattern.

Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma
Definition
Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma (EMC) is a salivary gland malignancy characterized by biphasic
tubular structures, usually composed of tightly coupled inner ductal and prominent outer
myoepithelial cells.
ICD-O coding
8562/3 Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Y & XH9JP2 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Epithelialmyoepithelial carcinoma
2B68.2 & XH9JP2 Other specified malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands &
Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma

419

Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Majority of EMCs develop in the parotid gland; the remainder affect the other major or minor salivary
glands, sinonasal tract, and bronchus { 8322454 ; 17197918 }.
Clinical features
EMC usually presents as a slow-growing painless mass. Lymphadenopathy is rare.
Imaging: Features are non-specific but lesions are frequently slow-growing and fairly well-defined
on CT and MRI, intermediate signal on T1W and T2W and enhance heterogeneously. Biopsy is
required to differentiate them from other low-grade salivary gland masses { 17939852 }.
Epidemiology
EMC is an uncommon neoplasm, accounting for 1% of all salivary gland tumours { 17197918 }. It
mostly occurs in the sixth and seventh decades, with a slight female predilection.
Etiology
A small subset arises in association with intercalated duct hyperplasia { 19542868 }. EMC may also
arise in a preexisting pleomorphic adenoma { 17197918 ; 29135520 ; 30994537 }.
Pathogenesis
A significant number of EMCs harbor HRAS mutations, most commonly at the codon 61
{ 24277618 ; 26053092 ; 29135520 ; 30994537 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The mean tumour size is 30 mm { 33526226 }. The cut surface shows a white-tan, nodular, firm
mass. Cystic changes may be noted { 17197918 ; 33526226 }.
Histopathology
EMC generally exhibits multinodular invasive growth. The histological hallmark is a biphasic
arrangement of inner (luminal) eosinophilic ductal epithelial cells and outer (abluminal), often clear
myoepithelial cells { 4340536 ; 6950345 }. The myoepithelial component varies from single-layered
to multilayered or even solid. The nuclear atypia is mild to moderate. There are several histologic
variations, including cribriform, basaloid, sebaceous, apocrine/oncocytic, and double-clear
appearances,
and
squamous
differentiation
{ 17197918 ; 18425042 ; 19492889 ; 22427262 ; 23821213 ; 29135517 }.
High-grade
transformation rarely occurs { 10335939 ; 20679885 ; 23821210 ; 33825717 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The luminal cells are positive for CK7, whereas abluminal cells are usually
positive for SMA, calponin, and p63/p40 { 17197918 ; 33526226 }. Diffuse and
membranous/cytoplasmic RAS Q61R expression is observed in 65% of EMCs { 33481388 }.
Differential The entities include other salivary gland tumours with biphasic or/and clear cell
morphology, such as adenoid cystic carcinoma, basal cell adenocarcinoma, pleomorphic adenoma,
myoepithelial carcinoma, and clear cell carcinoma { 33526226 }.
Cytology
Aspirates classically show 3D clusters with a dual epithelial and myoepithelial cell population
{ 9839128 ; 12619100 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The assessment of HRAS mutations is useful for diagnosing EMC in discrimination from mimics
{ 30994537 ; 33526226 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: usually multinodular invasive growth; at least partly with a dual arrangement of inner
ductal cells and outer prominent, and usually clear, myoepithelial cells
Staging
TNM
Prognosis and prediction
EMC has a favorable prognosis { 26195572 ; 30127663 ; 33526226 } but occasionally recurs
locally; lymph node and distant metastases are rare. High-grade transformation is clearly a poor
prognosticator { 10335939 ; 20679885 ; 23821210 ; 33825717 }. Other adverse features include
size, necrosis, angiolymphatic invasion, and margin status { 8322454 ; 17197918 ; 20679885 }.

420

A low-power view shows multinodular growth pattern.
