Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma
Bilayered tubular structures consisting of inner eosinophilic ductal cells and outer
polygonal clear myoepithelial cells.

cytoplasm.

#25437

Histologic variations. Apocrine differentiation of ductal cells.

#25438

Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma
Histologic variations. A double-clear tubular pattern. Both inner ductal cells and
outer myoepithelial cells exhibit clear cytoplasm.

#25439

Immunostaining for CK7 highlights the luminal ductal cells.

#25440

The abluminal myoepithelial cells are positive for SMA.

#25441

#25509

Epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma in the left parotid gland of a 51-year-old man
A low-power view shows multinodular invasive growth accompanied by intervening
sclerotic stroma. The tumour displays bilayered tubular structures consisting of inner
eosinophilic ductal cells and outer polygonal clear myoepithelial cells. The nuclear atypia
of the tumour cells is moderate. Scattered mitotic figures are seen within the tumour
nests.

425

Mucinous adenocarcinoma
Definition
Mucinous adenocarcinoma (MA) is a primary salivary carcinoma that displays prominent
intracellular and/or extracellular mucin, lacks diagnostic features of other tumour types, and is
usually associated with AKT1 alterations
ICD-O coding
8480/3 Mucinous adenocarcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B66.Y & XH1S75 Other specified malignant neoplasms of other or unspecified parts of
mouth & Mucinous adenocarcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: papillary cystadenocarcinoma; mucinous cystadenocarcinoma; mucinproducing adenopapillary carcinoma; colloid carcinoma; signet-ring carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
MA is most common in intraoral minor salivary glands { 8944036 ; 20738418 ; 33739781 }.
Clinical features
Most patients have painless masses or swellings. Nodal metastases are occasionally seen at
presentation { 19037659 ; 26908552 }.
Epidemiology
MA has peak incidence in the eighth decade with relatively equal sex distribution
{ 20738418 ; 33739781 ; 8944036 }.
Etiology
No risk factors are established.
Pathogenesis
MA has recurrent AKT1 p.E17K mutation, usually accompanied by TP53 mutations { 33739781 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are solid or cystic with a gelatinous cut surface.
Histopathology
MA is defined by abundant intracellular and/or extracellular mucin production in multiple forms,
including goblet cell-like vacuoles, apical caps, foveolar-type cytoplasmic droplets, or stromal
pools. Tumours show variable papillary, colloid, or signet ring architecture, with 40% displaying
mixed patterns { 3178562 ; 12425848 ; 20738418 ; 33739781 }. Most tumours display complex or
simple papillary fronds projecting into cystic spaces. Colloid pattern is second most common, with
tumour nests suspended in mucin pools. Discohesive signet ring cells are rarely seen. Cytologic
atypia varies widely.
Immunohistochemistry: MA is positive for CK7 and negative for CK20, CDX2, p63, p40, TTF1,
S100, calponin, SMA, and AR { 12425848 ; 33739781 }.
Differential To diagnose MA, other mucin-producing salivary carcinomas must be excluded,
including mucin-rich mucoepidermoid carcinoma (epidermoid/intermediate cells; p63/p40+),
mucinous and rhabdoid variants of salivary duct carcinoma (apocrine cytology; AR+), and
mucinous/secretory pattern of myoepithelial carcinoma (nested/corded pattern; S100, calponin, or
SMA+). Metastasis from gastrointestinal, pancreaticobiliary, or lung adenocarcinomas should be
ruled out clinically.
Cytology
Not reported in cytopathology literature
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Identification of AKT1 p.E17K mutation can support the diagnosis
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: abundant intracellular or extracellular mucin, CK7+, CK20-, no features of other mucinproducing salivary neoplasms
Desirable: A finding of AKT1 p.E17K mutation can support the diagnosis

426

Staging
AJCC/UICC TNM staging for the appropriate anatomic site can be applied
Prognosis and prediction
While papillary-predominant tumours are generally indolent, dominant colloid or signet-ring
patterns are associated with recurrence and metastases { 8944036 ; 19037659 ; 26908552 }.

#25876

Mucinous adenocarcinoma
Complex mucin-producing papillary fronds projecting into cystic spaces.

#25877

Mucinous adenocarcinoma
Nests and cords of tumour cells suspended in stromal mucin pools.
427

#25878

Mucinous adenocarcinoma
Dyscohesive signet-ring cells with prominent intracytoplasmic mucin vacuoles.

#25879

Mucinous adenocarcinoma
Mixed papillary fronds and colloid pools.

#26235

Mucinous adenocarcinoma
Columnar cells with apical mucin caps forming complex papillary fronds.

#26236

Mucinous adenocarcinoma

mucin.

#25892
Mucinous adenocarcinoma
The tumour demonstrates a mix of architecture including papillary and colloid patterns.

Sclerosing microcystic adenocarcinoma
Definition
Sclerosing microcystic adenocarcinoma (SMA) is an indolent infiltrative salivary
carcinoma composed of a biphasic population of ductal and myoepithelial cells embedded in a
dense collagenous stroma, similar to microcystic adnexal carcinoma.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2B66.Z & XH17P2 Malignant neoplasms of other or unspecified parts of mouth, unspecified
& Microcystic adnexal carcinoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Microcystic adnexal carcinoma of the tongue; sclerosing sweat duct-like carcinoma;
syringomatous adenocarcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
SMA arises from intraoral minor salivary glands in sites including tongue, lip mucosa, floor of mouth
and buccal mucosa { 27233253 ; 33526218 }, a single case was reported in the nasopharynx
{ 27233253 }.
Clinical features
Patients most commonly present with a painless mass or swelling; less common symptoms include
dysphagia { 8551161 }, numbness { 29987694 }, and diplopia { 27233253 }.
Epidemiology
SMA is more common in women than men occurring from 5th to 8th decades (mean, 56 years)
{ 2368175 ; 8551161 ; 29987694 ; 33109575 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Whole exome sequencing revealed moderate mutational burden and putative loss of function
mutations in CDK11B, but absence of mutations including cyclin dependent kinase inhibitor 2A
(CDKN2A)
and
cyclin
dependent
kinase
inhibitor
2B
(CDKN2B) { 33109575 }, TP53 and JAK1 genes { 31857679 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Gross examination of the surgical resection specimen revealed an ill-defined white-tan submucosal
mass in a single case report { 29987694 }.
430

Histopathology
SMA is characterized by deeply infiltrative ducts/tubules, strands/cords, nests, and rarely single
cells embedded in densely collagenous stroma. The tumour is composed of a biphasic cell
population including luminal lining bland appearing ductal cells with round to oval, monotonous
nuclei, evenly dispersed chromatin, occasional nucleoli, and eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm. Ductal
lining cells are peripherally surrounded by flattened myoepithelial cells. Mitotic figures are not
conspicuous. Perineural invasion is commonly seen and apposition to and/or invasion of minor
salivary glands can be seen.
Histochemistry: Mucicarmine positive luminal eosinophilic globular secretory material is seen.
Immunohistochemistry: Duct lining cells are positive for pancytokeratin and CK7 and the peripheral
myoepithelial cell population expresses p40/p63, S100, smooth muscle actin and calponin
{ 29398516 ; 29987694 ; 33526218 } CD117 is negative (27233253; 29987694 }. Low proliferation
indices are seen by Ki67 (MIB1) staining.
Differential The biphasic cell population, bland cytology, immunoreactive staining pattern and lack
of keratinization are helpful features in differentiation from squamous cell carcinoma, polymorphous
adenocarcinoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma and clear cell carcinoma.
Cytology
Not reported in cytopathology literature
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: infiltrative low-grade adenocarcinoma embedded in dense sclerotic stroma; biphasic cell
population with an attenuated myoepithelial cell layer
Desirable: perineural invasion
Staging
TNM system as published by UICC
Prognosis and prediction
Surgical excision is the treatment of choice. Adjunctive radiotherapy may be used in margin positive
excision { 29987694 }. SMA has a uniformly good outcome without locoregional recurrence or
distant metastases { 29987694 ; 33526218 }.

A finding that assists in confirming a diagnosis of malignancy (i.e., adenocarcinoma) is
the presence of perineural invasion which is commonly present.

Justin Bishop

435

Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
Definition
Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma (CXPA) is an epithelial and/or myoepithelial malignancy that
arises in association with a primary or recurrent pleomorphic adenoma (PA).
ICD-O coding
8941/3 Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67 & XH42V2 Malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: carcinoma ex benign mixed tumour
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most CXPA arise in the parotid gland, but they may originate from the submandibular gland or
minor salivary glands { 6322732 ; 20338616 ; 21744105 ; 22022178 ; 22014054 ; 33728082 }.
Clinical features
The typical clinical presentation is that of a long-standing painless mass with recent rapid
progression, or of previous diagnosis of a PA. Occasionally, the tumour may be asymptomatic
{ 21744105 }. Facial nerve palsy, pain, or skin ulceration may occur { 20338616 }.
Imaging: The majority present as an irregular, lobular and heterogeneously enhancing mass with
uneven margins on CT and MRI. Internal calcification is regarded as a specific sign for this tumour.
Cervical lymph node and distant metastases are commonly found { 34161740 }.
Epidemiology
CXPA comprises 3.6% of all salivary gland tumours and 12% of all salivary gland malignancies
{ 24804831 }. It occurs in the setting of a recurrent PA in about 12% of the cases. Patients present
in their 6th - 7th decades. The tumour is slightly more common in women
{ 22014054 ; 24804831 ; 25439740 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
About two thirds of CXPA displays PLAG1 or HMGA2 rearrangements and/or amplification,
markers of pre-existing PA. Additionally, tumours may exhibit the following markers of progression
to
carcinoma:
mutations
of TP53,
HRAS,
PIK3CA,
amplification
of MYC,
and/or EGFR { 11839563 ; 15920557 ; 18828159 ; 24468654 ; 27379604 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumour sizes range from 10 to 250 mm { 6322732 ; 11431714 }. Grossly, the tumour is often poorly
demarcated and may show a gross residual PA component which occasionally exhibits a wellcircumscribed encapsulated sclerotic nodule { 11431714 ; 20338616 }.
Histopathology
In most cases, the carcinoma component comprises > 50% of the tumour { 11431714 }. The PA
component, which may have variable cellularity, can be intermixed with the carcinoma or appears
as a discrete nodule (often hyalinized or rarely entirely sclerotic). CXPA can be sub-classified based
on the extent of invasion beyond the PA borders into: 1) intracapsular: when the carcinoma is
confined within the PA capsule; in situ intracapsular when the malignant tumour cells replace the
ductal cells while retaining an intact myoepithelial layer; 2) minimally invasive: when the carcinoma
invades < 4 - 6 mm beyond the PA borders; and 3) invasive: when the invasion beyond the PA
capsule measures ≥6mm.
Additionally, the histologic type of the malignant component should be reported. The carcinoma
component can be of any type but the most common is salivary duct carcinoma, followed by
myoepithelial carcinoma, and adenocarcinoma, NOS { 26934566 ; 29135520 ; 32749544 }.
Carcinosarcoma is a rare aggressive tumour that is composed of malignant epithelial and
sarcomatous components and can arise in association with a PA { 31291005 ; 32814949 }.

436

Differential Myoepithelial carcinoma and epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma ex PA can mimic
cellular PA especially when intracapsular or minimally invasive. The invasive multinodular pattern
helps to differentiate these tumours from PA.
Immunohistochemistry: Expression of PLAG1 and HMGA2 may help to identify the PA component
{ 23958548 ; 27473265 ; 28796899 }. Staining for AR may be indicative of salivary duct carcinoma
ex PA { 27334835 ; 33405400 }.
Cytology
FNAB smears can show poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma or salivary duct carcinoma, or other
salivary gland carcinomas including mucoepidermoid, adenoid cystic carcinoma and
carcinosarcoma, or the FNAB may show pleomorphic adenoma, depending on sampling. It is
uncommon that both components are identified, and necrosis in an otherwise pleomorphic
adenoma may be the only clue apart from previous history of a longstanding mass or previous
biopsy { 9723598 ; 10450099 ; 31675185 ; 33528074 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
PLAG1 or HMGA2 rearrangements detected by FISH serve as a marker of pre-existing PA,
and can assist in diagnosis of CXPA.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: evidence of a pre-existing PA clinically or histologically with a carcinoma (epithelial
and/or myoepithelial)
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification
Prognosis and prediction
Most CXPA are aggressive showing up to 70% local and distant recurrences. The 5-year survival
ranges from 25% to 75% { 11431714 ; 20338616 ; 22014054 ; 24804831 ; 31471126 }. Invasive
CXPA has worse outcomes compared to intracapsular and minimally invasive CXPA
{ 11431714 ; 20338616 ; 22014054 ; 24804831 ; 28353089 }. Large size ( > 40 mm), multiple
positive lymph nodes, and distant metastasis have been reported to increase the mortality risk
{ 31471126 }.

#25522

Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
High power view of the invasive carcinoma showing histology of salivary duct carcinoma.

437

#27940
Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
Macroscopic view showing the invasive carcinoma on the top and a residual wellcircumscribed sclerotic pleomorphic adenoma component on the bottom.

This coronal CT demonstrates a large destructive soft-tissue density mass in the right
parotid gland.

438

#28182

Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
This axial CT shows small calcifications within a large destructive right parotid gland
tumour.

Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
Nodules of pleomorphic adenoma with calcifications are noted in the center of this
tumour, while the malignant transformation to carcinoma is seen surrounding the tumour.

439

#28185

The lower half demonstrates the heavy stromal hyalinization within the pleomorphic
adenoma with associated calcifications, while the area of myoepithelial carcinoma
expands into the adjacent tissues.

#28187

Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
A heavily hyalinized residuum of pleomorphic adenoma is noted, while the carcinoma is
seen immediately adjacent.

440

#28189
Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
In many instances, the initial alterations are identified as profoundly pleomorphic epithelial
cells within the lumen of ducts in the pleomorphic adenoma. This degeree of atypia is
beyond a benign tumour.

#25518

Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma
The carcinoma invades beyond the capsule of the hyalinized sclerotic pleomorphic
adenoma.

441

Carcinosarcoma of the salivary glands
Definition
Salivary carcinosarcoma is characterized by variable combination of malignant epithelial and
sarcomatous tumour components, which may develop from a preexistent pleomorphic adenoma
(PA) or de novo.
ICD-O coding
8980/3 Carcinosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Y & XH2W45 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Carcinosarcoma, NOS
2B68.2 Other specified malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands &
Carcinosarcoma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: (true) malignant mixed tumour
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most cases of carcinosarcoma manifest in major salivary glands (parotid 70%, submandibular
19%), while minor gland occurrences are rare { 8380049 }.
Clinical features
Carcinosarcoma are typically rapidly growing, large, and infiltrative tumours.
Epidemiology
The mean age at presentation is 58 years (range, 14–87 years). No sex predominance was shown
{ 8380049 }.
Etiology
Approximately half of the cases develop from a pre-existent PA { 3014420 ; 11197153 }. On the
other hand, it has been shown that some carcinosarcomas arise de novo { 2422810 ; 8380049 }.
Few cases occurred after radiotherapy { 8035132 }.
Pathogenesis
Molecular alterations are heterogeneous and not diagnostic.
Macroscopic appearance
They are predominantly large, infiltrating tumours (up to 50-100 mm, average size 39 mm),
frequently with necrosis and haemorrhage. A smaller, often sclerotic nodule may represent a
preexistent PA.
Histopathology
The carcinomatous component is most commonly a squamous cell carcinoma or adenocarcinoma,
while the most common sarcomatous component is a chondrosarcoma { 8380049 }. The
sarcomatous components frequently dominate. Approximately half of the tumours show a preexisting, often sclerotic PA, usually with a carcinomatous, rarely sarcomatous intracapsular
component. Pure intracapsular carcinosarcoma ex PA is very rare { 2422810 }.
Immunohistochemistry: IHC is recommended to characterize the usually heterologous
sarcomatous component, and especially to distinguish carcinosarcoma from other malignancies
with high-grade transformation { 2422810 ; 8380049 ; 22847723 }.
Cytology
Scattered irregular tissue fragments of large epithelial cells with abundant cytoplasm, large
pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei and prominent nucleoli are present along with occasional giant
cells and spindle cells showing atypical nuclei in a background of necrosis. The epithelial
component can show squamoid features { 29281871 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: combined salivary tumour with malignant epithelial and mesenchymal component;
exclude sarcomatoid subtypes of other salivary gland malignancies
Staging
TNM system as published by UICC
Prognosis and prediction

442

Prognosis is poor with high frequency of local recurrence and lymphatic and hematogenous spread.
The largest series showed mortality of 50% and mean survival of 3.6 years { 3014420 ; 8380049 }.
Most cases have been reported as case reports with insufficient clinical follow-up
{ 11197153 ; 17630097 ; 22847723 }.

The tumour is composed of spindle-shaped sarcomatous and high-grade

#25707

Sclerotic pleomorphic adenoma with high-grade cystic intracapsular adenocarcinoma
(left) and extracapsular invasive high-grade myogenic sarcoma (right).

Sebaceous adenocarcinoma
Definition
Sebaceous adenocarcinoma is a malignant tumour composed mainly of neoplastic sebaceous
cells.
ICD-O coding
8410/3 Sebaceous adenocarcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Y & XH4VR2 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Sebaceous
carcinoma
2B68.2 & XH4VR2 Malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual gland & Sebaceous
445

carcinoma
2B66.Y & XH4VR2 Other specified malignant neoplasms of other or unspecified parts of mouth &
Sebaceous carcinoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: sebaceous carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most arise in parotid (~70%) but may occur in sublingual, submandibular { 22430772 ; 29856905 }
or minor glands { 30116646 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with painful masses, varying degrees of facial nerve paralysis, and occasional skin
fixation. Rare patients present with tumour metastasis (head/neck or lung), or a non-tender mass
{ 22430772 }.
Epidemiology
Sebaceous adenocarcinoma is rare and presents two peak incidences: third and seventh/eighth
decades (range 6–93 years) { 22430772 ; 25848361 }. Tumours are slightly more common in
females { 29856905 }. Rare cases are associated with Muir Torre syndrome
{ 26577210 ; 29856905 }.
Etiology
Sebaceous adenocarcinoma associated with Muir–Torre syndrome (MIM number: 158320) shows
microsatellite instability and loss of expression of a DNA mismatch repair genes MLH1, MSH2, and
MSH6; these findings are not usually seen in sporadic sebaceous adenocarcinoma
{ 26577210 ; 26959146 ; 28681210 ; 29856905 ; 32907843 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Tumour sizes range from 6 to 95 mm and are frequently well-circumscribed or partially
encapsulated with pushing or locally infiltrating margins. Cut surfaces are yellow, tan-white, greywhite, white, or pale pink { 22430772 }.
Histopathology
The tumours are composed of invasive, multiple, variably sized nests and/or sheets with
hyperchromatic nuclei, abundant clear vacuolated to eosinophilic cytoplasm, and mild to marked
cellular pleomorphism. Necrosis is frequent. Perineural invasion is noted in ~20% of cases;
vascular invasion is rare. Oncocytes and foreign-body giant cells are rare { 22430772 }. A
lymphocytic infiltrate is commonly present.
Immunohistochemistry: Sebaceous/basal epithelial cells are positive for CK5/6, CK7, CK14, and
EMA { 21778832 }.
Differential This includes other salivary carcinomas with dense lymphoid reaction and sebaceous
differentiation { 8108090 }.
Cytology
In a background of plentiful mature lymphocytes, small epithelial tissue fragments are present
consisting of large cells resembling sebaceous cells, with abundant finely to coarsely vacuolated
cytoplasm and usually bland round nuclei with single conspicuous nucleoli. There is a
second population of basaloid cells. Some sebaceous cells are reported to show malignant nuclei
{ 12913846 }, but others report that the adenocarcinoma shows similar features to sebaceous
adenoma { 12913846 ; 31967730 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology

446

Loss of MSH2 expression is uncommon and may characterize tumours in Muir Torre syndrome
{ 26577210 ; 29856905 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: invasive neoplastic sebaceous cells of varying maturity
Desirable: tumour necrosis
Staging
According to UICC TNM8 staging guidance.
Prognosis and prediction
Tumours may recur and rarely metastasize. The 5-year overall survival rate is 62%, lower than
sebaceous adenocarcinoma of skin or orbit (84.5%) { 22430772 }. Oral tumours may have a better
prognosis with short term follow-up { 15599352 }.

#26555
Sebaceous Adenocarcinoma
Locally infiltrating, closely packed tumour nests with prominent sebaceous differentiation
surrounded by peripheral basal cells with mild to moderate cytologic atypia.

nests with prominent, central sebaceous
differentiation and composed of large,
vacuolated cells with moderately pleomorphic
nuclei. These cells are surrounded by
atypical basal cells. An infiltrate of
lymphocytes is seen.

#26556
Sebaceous Adenocarcinoma
Several tumour nests with prominent, central sebaceous differentiation composed of
large, moderately pleomorphic, vacuolated cells with an area of holocrine secretion
(center); nuclei are pleomorphic. These cells are surrounded by atypical basal cells. A few
infiltrating lymphocytes are noted.

Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Definition
Lymphoepithelial carcinoma (LEC) is a morphologically undifferentiated carcinoma with an
associated prominent, nonneoplastic lymphoplasmacytic cell infiltrate.
ICD-O coding
8082/3 Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Y & XH1E40 Other specified malignant neoplasms of parotid gland & Lymphoepithelial
carcinoma
2B68.2 & XH1E40 Other specified malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands &
Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
LEC arise mainly in major salivary glands (>90%) with parotid gland (>75%) predominating
{ 26547125 ; 32462279 ; 33447549 ; 33526225 }, as well in larynx (see 3.0.2.7: Lymphoepithelial
carcinoma of the larynx) and sinonasal cavities (see 1.2.2.4: Sinonasal lymphoepithelial
carcinoma).
Clinical features

448

Patients present with a mass of varying duration; pain (~5%) and nerve findings (~2.5%) are rare
{ 9241068 ; 9253628 ; 15389474 }. Cervical lymphadenopathy is noted in ~17% of cases. Most
patients show evidence of Epstein Barr Virus (EBV) infection (i.e. IgA antibodies to viral capsid
antigen), occasionally preceding actual tumour presentation { 1646614 ; 3759075 }.
Imaging: CT and MRI may show a homogeneous, enhancing soft tissue lesion with lobulated shape
and relatively well-defined margins. However, up to 50% may be heterogeneous, particularly those
which have metastasised to intra or extra-glandular lymph nodes { 28707954 }.
Epidemiology
In some ethnic groups (Inuit, southern Chinese, Japanese, northern Africans, and Mongolians)
{ 1334414 ; 1651284 ; 8543624 ; 8600040 ; 8756359 ; 9241068 } prevalence ranges from 3.692% of malignant tumours, but in non-endemic regions LEC only represents 0.3-0.7% of
malignancies { 17825603 ; 26547125 }. Wide age range is affected with median 46 years; nonendemic patients tend to be a decade older. No sex predilection is noted
{ 7825444 ; 16554915 ; 17306504 ; 19810095 ; 26547125 }.
Etiology
EBV is the major etiologic factor, more prominent in endemic populations.
Pathogenesis
EBV
related
LEC
pathogenesis
in
salivary
gland
is
presumed
{ 1334414 ; 1646614 ; 1651284 ; 2820863 ; 5018596 ; 7825444 ; 7944902 ; 7982672 ; 8756359 ;
9253628 ; 17306504 ; 24715369 }. Human immunodeficiency virus infection imparts increased risk
{ 19810095 }.
Some
cases arise in
the
setting
of
lymphoepithelial sialadenitis
{ 6882273 ; 18251590 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are unencapsulated with a lobulated, firm, tan-white cut surface, and vary from well
demarcated to infiltrative { 16554915 ; 25804344 ; 26547125 ; 33526225 }.
Histopathology
Histologic features of LEC are essentially identical to non-keratinizing nasopharyngeal carcinoma,
namely, nests of large anaplastic carcinoma cells within a lymphoid stroma. Variant basaloid
morphology has been described, showing a sclerotic stroma, limited lymphoid tissue, and
angulated cord or “syringoma-like” nests { 22926973 }. The adjacent parenchyma often shows a
lymphoepithelial sialadenitis { 17306504 ; 18251590 ; 25804344 }.
Differential LEC must be distinguished from large cell undifferentiated carcinoma, and metastasis
from nasopharynx { 25804344 }.
Cytology
Cellular smears show syncytial tissue fragments of markedly atypical undifferentiated epithelial
cells and spindle cells with high grade vesicular nuclei and prominent nucleoli in a
lymphoplasmacytic background, that may largely obscure the epithelial component, along with
necrosis. The features raises a differential diagnosis of high grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma and
sinonasal lymphoepithelial carcinoma and non keratinizing nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
{ 16554915 ; 31042496 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
In situ hybridization for Epstein Barr encoding RNA (EBER) is critical to establishing EBV status
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: undifferentiated morphology with lymphoid stroma; exclusion of other tumours with
associated lymphoid proliferation; exclusion of metastasis
Desirable: EBER positivity
Staging
TNM system as published by UICC
Prognosis and prediction

449

Five year survival is ~81% { 8902573 ; 9241068 ; 15389474 ; 24715369 ; 25804344 ; 26547125 }.
Nodal disease is present in ~17%; distant metastases are uncommon (~6%)
{ 1646614 ; 3294429 ; 6763017 ; 15389474 ; 16554915 ; 24715369 ; 26547125 }
but
more
frequent in endemic areas { 26547125 }. The latter is a key prognosticator.

453

Squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
Primary salivary squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is a malignant epithelial neoplasm with exclusive
squamous differentiation. The diagnosis can only be established after the exclusion of
metastatic SCC.
ICD-O coding
8070/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B67.1 Squamous cell carcinoma of parotid gland
2B68.1 Squamous cell carcinoma of submandibular or sublingual gland
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Approximately 80% of cases occur in parotid gland with the remainder primarily of submandibular
gland { 3667298 ; 33282162 }.
Clinical features
Patient presentations include a mass (with or without pain) and facial nerve palsy { 1642829 }
Epidemiology
Primary salivary SCC is very rare { 33282162 }, while majority of reported cases are cutaneous
SCC
with
secondary
invasion
or
metastasis
to
salivary
gland
{ 170839 ; 1642829 ; 3823246 ; 10622505 ; 16475198 ; 24339135 ; 33282162 }. SCC is more
common in men than in women; and it occurs mostly in 7th-8th decades of life.
Etiology
Radiotherapy is associated with increased risk of developing SCC { 6689907 ; 3667298 } with a
median time to development of 15.5 years { 6689907 } and range of 7 to 32 years { 889197 }. SCC
may arise independent of prior radiotherapy { 1642829 }.
Pathogenesis
Wnt/β-catenin signaling
SCC { 26928905 }.

demonstrated

in

rapidly

growing,

aggressive

salivary

gland

Macroscopic appearance
Most tumours are firm and infiltrative.
Histopathology
SCC is an infiltrating well- to moderately-differentiated carcinoma with associated desmoplasia.
Intracellular keratin, keratin pearl formation and intercellular bridges are seen. Less often, the
tumour is poorly-differentiated and may include spindle-shaped (sarcomatoid) cells. Acantholytic
(adenoid) features may uncommonly be present. Invasion includes infiltration of salivary gland
parenchyma, extraglandular fibroconnective tissues, neurotropism, lymphovascular and/or
osseous invasion. Direct invasion and/or metastasis to intraparotid lymph nodes may be present.
Salivary duct changes may include squamous metaplasia and/or dysplasia, and transition from
normal to dysplastic duct epithelium to squamous cell carcinoma may occasionally be identified.
Immunohistochemistry: SCC are immunoreactive for cytokeratins and p63/p40; negative for
androgen receptor, S100 protein, mammaglobin and DOG1. SCC may over-express p16 but are
negative for high-risk human papillomavirus RNA.
Differential SCC must be distinguished from high-grade transformation of adenoid cystic
carcinoma with squamoid pattern { 18059225 }. Lack of mucous and intermediate cells and

454

presence of keratinization are most helpful in differentiating SCC from a mucoepidermoid
carcinoma { 21243374 }.
Cytology
Smears vary depending on the degree of keratinization. Keratinized squamous cell carcinomas
show cohesive tissue fragments and dispersed spindle, polygonal and tadpole cells showing
varying degrees of keratinization with dense light blue (Giemsa stain) or orangophilic (Pap stain)
well defined cytoplasm. Nuclei can show pyknosis in the keratinized cells or larger central nuclei
with coarse chromatin and prominent nucleoli. Keratinous debris can be prominent in the
background. Poorly keratinizing carcinomas show predominantly large sheets and tissue fragments
consisting of cells with well-defined dense but nonkeratinized cytoplasm with large central
pleomorphic nuclei with coarse chromatin and one or more large and pleomorphic nucleoli.
Necrosis may be present in the background. Primary squamous cell carcinoma in the salivary gland
cannot be distinguished on FNAB or CNB or even excision from a metastatic carcinoma. The
clinical history is crucially important, and the presence of prominent lymphoid material intermingled
with the carcinoma may suggest a metastasis.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Absence of any history of other primary SCC
Desirable: Presence of sialodochal dysplasia
Staging
TNM system as published by UICC.
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis and predictive factors are unknown due to rarity. Overall survival (OS) of 50% at 5
years { 1642829 } and median disease free survival of 13 months were reported { 33282162 }.

of squamous metaplasia of the duct epithelium (lower right).

#26920

Squamous cell carcinoma, primary in parotid gland
The tumour includes a salivary duct lined in part by (normal) respiratory epithelial cells

SCC is keratinizing and extensively invasive with foci of well-, moderately- and poorlydifferentiated carcinoma.

Sialoblastoma
Definition
Sialoblastoma is a malignant primitive salivary gland neoplasm with resemblance to salivary gland
anlage structures.
ICD-O coding
8974/1 Sialoblastoma
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Z Malignant neoplasms of parotid gland, unspecified
2B68.Z Malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands, unspecified
Related terminology
Not recommended: embryoma, basaloid adenocarcinoma, congenital basal cell adenoma,
congenital hybrid basal cell adenoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Two thirds of tumours arise in the parotid gland, followed by submandibular gland, with only rare
reports in minor salivary gland { 17178498 ; 17638426 ; 24163547 ; 27284424 ; 31257547 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with painless face swelling, with rare cases showing nerve involvement or skin
ulceration { 1965332 ; 10078927 ; 14674095 ; 16818073 ; 24163547 }. Other tumours may be
coexistent (nevi and hepatoblastoma) { 9159361 ; 10890938 ; 16049999 ; 16514617 }. Elevated
alfa-feto protein has been reported { 16049999 }.

457

Imaging: This lesion is usually diagnosed in the neonatal period but is occasionally seen on
antenatal imaging. MRI may show a mass, hypointense to brain on T1W and mildly hyperintense
on T2W, containing foci of haemorrhage and necrosis. Heterogeneous, weak post-contrast
enhancement is described, as is invasion of adjacent structures including bone and muscle
{ 15278323 ; 19237343 }.
Epidemiology
The vast majority of tumours are identified at birth or within the few months of life, with only rare
reports in adults. There is no sex predilection { 24163547 ; 29728174 }.
Etiology
None
Pathogenesis
The tumour may originate from retained blastema cells rather than basal reserve cells
{ 27284424 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are lobulated, well defined, encapsulated or may show infiltration to the surrounding
tissue. The cut surface is usually gray or yellow and solid. Tumours range from 15 mm to 150 mm
in size. Focal hemorrhage or necrosis may be present { 3029739 ; 3211816 ; 3725707 }.
Histopathology
Sialoblastoma typically exhibits solid organoid nests composed of primitive basaloid epithelial
cells, separated by dense fibrous stroma. Tumour cells have indistinct cell borders, scant
cytoplasm, with a high nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio and round to oval vesicular nuclei with
occasional nucleoli. In addition, differentiated budding ducts lined by cuboidal to columnar
epithelium with intraluminal basophilic secretions or a cribriform pattern may be present.
Peripheral nuclear palisading is occasionally present. Tumour cells may exhibit apoptosis,
necrosis, and nuclear pleomorphism, with increased mitoses. Sialoblastoma is not graded.
Immunohistochemistry: Tumours are reactive with SOX10, p63, β catenin, and variably with
cytokeratins (including CK5/6, CK7 and CK19), S100, SMA, CD117, and calponin { 32695659 }.
Differential The primitive histologic features and the patients' young age help to differentiate
sialoblastoma from other basaloid salivary gland tumours such as basal cell
adenoma/adenocarcinoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma and adamantinoma-like Ewing sarcoma
{ 11210851 ; 18926196 ; 27327192 ; 29728174 ; 30285997 ; 32695659 }.
Cytology
Highly cellular smears show cohesive tissue fragments of basaloid cells with scant cytoplasm,
round to oval vesicular nuclei and occasional inconspicuous nucleoli, and may contain ductal cells
and dense metachromatic, magenta hyaline globular material with smooth rounded outlines. The
features raise a differential diagnosis with basaloid adenomas, adenoid cystic carcinoma and
cellular pleomorphic adenomas { 14674095 ; 26214398 }. FNAB in paediatric age groups requires
careful planning.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: primitive, solid, organoid nests, basaloid epithelial cells with vesicular chromatin, and
cuboidal to columnar ductal cells
Desirable: dividing fibrous stroma and peripheral palisading
Staging
TNM system as published by UICC
Prognosis and prediction
Local recurrence is seen in about a third of patients, while regional and distant metastases are
seen
in
about
15%
{ 27368441 }.
Death
from
disease
is
rare
{ 11090328 ; 16818073 ; 24163547 ; 27368441 ; 29728174 ; 31257547 }. A
less
aggressive
behaviour is suggested for tumours in the submandibular gland versus other anatomic sites.
Histologic features associated with a worse prognosis include tumour necrosis and increased
mitotic count and/or high Ki-67 proliferation index.

458

Salivary carcinoma, NOS and emerging entities
Definition
Salivary gland carcinoma, NOS (not otherwise specified) represents a heterogeneous spectrum of
carcinomas forming epithelial, ductal and/or glandular structures, representing a diagnosis of
exclusion, specifically exclusive of otherwise defined salivary gland carcinoma entities.
ICD-O coding
8140/3 Adenocarcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B67.Z Malignant neoplasms of parotid gland, unspecified
2B68.Z Malignant neoplasms of submandibular or sublingual glands, unspecified
2B60.Z Malignant neoplasms of lip, unspecified
2B65.Z Malignant neoplasms of palate, unspecified
2B66.Z Malignant neoplasms of other or unspecified parts of mouth, unspecified
Related terminology

461

Not recommended: unclassified adenocarcinoma; ductal adenocarcinoma; (papillary)
cystadenocarcinoma
Subtype(s)
Oncocytic adenocarcinoma; intestinal-type adenocarcinoma
Localization
More than 50% arise in the parotid gland { 7125074 ; 8944036 ; 21612974 }, 40% in minor glands,
most often in the hard palate, buccal mucosa, and lips { 8944036 ; 17577550 }.
Clinical features
Patients with tumours of major glands usually present with asymptomatic solitary firm or cystic
masses. Occasionally, they may be painful. Tumours in the palate often ulcerate and may erode
bony structures { 7125074 }.
Epidemiology
Due to repeated morphogenetic identification of new types of salivary adenocarcinoma
{ 27233253 ; 31094920 }, the entity adenocarcinoma, NOS, now accounts for only 5-10% of
salivary gland carcinomas { 7125074 ; 21612974 }. The average age is 58 years with a wide age
range { 12167424 ; 21612974 }. They are extremely rare in children.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours may be partly circumscribed, but usually have an irregular and infiltrative appearance.
The cut surface is commonly tan or yellow, sometimes with areas of necrosis and hemorrhage.
Histopathology
Tumours display a wide range of ductal or glandular proliferation with or without a cystic component
or necrosis. There is an architectural variety of growth patterns, including small confluent nests or
cords, large islands, and often assosciated densely cellular stroma. Tumour cells can be cuboidal,
columnar, polygonal, clear, mucinous, oncocytoid, or plasmacytoid. Ductal and glandular structures
are predominant in low- and intermediate-grade tumours, but less frequent in high-grade tumours
{ 15578883 ; 16673121 }.
Proof of an intraductal component supports salivary origin { 16673121 }.
Subtypes: These
include
oncocytic
{ 8944036 ; 20738418 }
and
{ 19695679 ; 21948243 ; 23108630 }, which can be positive for CK20 and CDX2.

intestinal-type

Immunohistochemistry: IHC is helpful in distinguishing adenocarcinoma, NOS, from acinic cell
carcinoma (NR4A3, DOG1) { 12608654 }, tumours with myoepithelial / basal-cell composition
(calponin, CK5/6, p63), and metastatic adenocarcinoma (site-specific markers) { 14976534 }.
Differential For the diagnosis of adenocarcinoma, NOS, to be rendered, other primary carcinoma
types must be excluded, including salivary duct carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma,
polymorphous adenocarcinoma, and high-grade transformation of other types of salivary
carcinoma { 14976534 }. Tumours with significant mucinous differentiation may be better regarded
as mucinous adenocarcinoma.
Cytology
Smears show adenocarcinoma with usually high grade nuclei in a necrotic background, and lack
features of other salivary gland carcinomas, resulting in a differential diagnosis of metastases from
colon and lung. Oncocytic and intestinal types are recognized { 7125074 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular alterations are heterogeneous and not diagnostic { 27334835 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Salivary carcinoma lacking morphological, immunohistochemical, and molecular features of other
salivary carcinomas. Exclusion of metastasis is mandatory.
Staging
TNM system as published by UICC.
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis is influenced by location, histological grade, and clinical stage { 7125074 ; 27166875 }.
High-grade adenocarcinoma, NOS is an aggressive malignancy. A study reported 15-year survival

462

rates for low-, intermediate-, and high-grade tumours of 54%, 31%, and 3%, respectively
{ 7125074 }. Survival of adenocarcinoma with a significant cystic component is more favourable
{ 8944036 }. The reports of intestinal-type adenocarcinoma suggest an aggressive behaviour
{ 19695679 ; 23108630 }.

components harboured NCAO4::RET.

464

#25854
Salivary carcinoma, NOS. Adenocarcinoma not otherwise specified, high grade
This submandibular gland tumour is highly invasive with a desmoplastic stroma and
perineural spread. The tumour grows as cords and ducts lined by pleomorphic cells with a
high mitotic rate. Necrosis is identified. Foci of hyalinization raise the possibility of a prior
pleomorphic adenoma but no obvious benign elements were identified and no fusions
were found by RNA sequencing. The tumour lacks apocrine morphology, and was
negative for androgen receptor (not shown). It is best regarded as high-grade
adenocarcinoma, not otherwise specified, a diagnosis of exclusion.

Mesenchymal tumours specific to the salivary glands

Sialolipoma
Definition
Neoplastic lipomatous proliferation within salivary glands with oncocytic (oncocytic lipoadenoma)
or non-oncocytic (sialolipoma) epithelial component.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E91.0 & XH8P28 Benign neoplasm of parotid gland & Lipoadenoma
2E90.3 & XH8P28 Benign neoplasm of other or unspecified parts of mouth & Lipoadenoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Lipoadenoma
Not recommended: adenolipoma, oncocytic sialolipoma
Subtype(s)
Oncocytic lipoadenoma
Localization
Conventional lipomas (see section 8.0.1.1) predominate in parotid glands (≥80%), while sialolipoma
and oncocytic lipoadenoma manifest in 50% in minor glands and in 40% in parotid glands
{ 11135044 ; 20952301 ; 23401010 ; 33187016 }. In the oral cavity, the lower lip and tongue are
the most common locations { 33187016 }.
Clinical features
Patients typically present with an asymptomatic swelling of slow growth. Salivary gland location is
verified by radiological or intraoperative localization and/or histological demonstration of a rim of
salivary parenchyma { 11135044 ; 23232852 ; 23401010 }.

465

Epidemiology
The mean age at presentation regardless of site is 51.5 years, with equal male to female ratio
{ 19347375 }. In minor salivary glands, the mean age of ten cases was 46.1 years (range: 11-71
years) with a 2.3:1 female to male ratio { 33187016 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Well-circumscribed tumours measuring on average 30-40 mm.
Histopathology
Combined lipoepithelial salivary lesions comprise a spectrum of morphological variants from
sialolipoma to oncocytic lipoadenoma. Sialolipoma combines lobules of salivary parenchyma within
dominating and evenly interspersed adipose tissue, rarely with focal sebaceous differentiation,
usually devoid of oncocytic epithelium. The term sialolipoma indicates that the adipocytic
component is regarded as neoplastic { 11135044 ; 20952301 ; 23232852 ; 33187016 }.
Subtype: The rare oncocytic lipoadenoma subtype typically demonstrates predominance of
epithelial elements with a varying oncocytic component, reminiscent of oncocytoma. A minor
sebaceous component may occur. The term oncocytic lipoadenoma indicates that the
oncocytic/epithelial component is regarded as neoplastic { 23232852 }.
Differential The entities include extrasalivary lipomatous tumours (see Chapter 8) and epithelial
salivary tumours with lipomatous metaplasia, especially pleomorphic adenoma/myoepithelioma
{ 11787848 }, and a fatty replacement (lipomatosis) in atrophic salivary parenchyma secondary to
chronic inflammation or old age { 11135044 ; 23232852 }.
Cytology
Cytological preparations may show mature adipocytic and salivary epithelial elements in varying
proportion
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: lipoma with benign salivary epithelial components
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
There are no reported recurrences. All types are cured by excision.

466

#27175
Sialolipoma
Isolated salivary gland elements are seen within a predominantly adipocytic proliferation.
A well-developed capsule was seen around the periphery.

#27216

Sialolipoma of parotid gland
Isolated salivary gland elements are seen within a predominantly adipocytic proliferation.
A well-developed capsule was seen around the periphery

#27703

Oncocytic lipoadenoma
The tumour consists of salivary gland lobules with oncocytic features and mature adipose
tissue. A well-developed capsule was seen around the periphery.

#27199

Oncocytic lipoadenoma of parotid gland
Well circumscribed encapsulated tumour composed of mature adipose tissue contains
lobules of parotid parenchyma with prominent oncocytic features.

6. Oral cavity and mobile tongue
Introduction
Non-neoplastic lesions
Necrotising sialometaplasia
Multifocal epithelial hyperplasia
Oral melanoacanthoma
Epithelial tumours
Papillomas
Squamous papillomas
Oral potentially malignant disorders & oral epithelial dysplasia
Oral potentially malignant disorders
Oral epithelial dysplasia
Proliferative verrucous leukoplakia
Submucous fibrosis
HPV-associated dysplasia
Squamous cell carcinomas
Oral squamous cell carcinoma
Verrucous carcinoma
Carcinoma cuniculatum
Tumours of uncertain histogenesis
Congenital granular cell epulis
Granular cell tumour
Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour
Melanotic neuroectodermal tumour of infancy

Introduction
The oral cavity and mobile tongue tumour classification shares similarities to the previous WHO
edition but with evolving molecular discoveries along with immunohistochemical advances,
classification of oral mucosal diseases continues to be refined.
The chapter now includes a section on non-neoplastic Lesions. Necrotizing sialometaplasia is
included, chiefly to highlight the clinical and histological features of this reactive, self-limiting entity
as it may mimic squamous cell and salivary carcinoma { 19415943 }. The condition forms a mass
lesion almost exclusively in the oral cavity so that it is covered here rather than with salivary gland
tumours. Multifocal epithelial hyperplasia has been moved to this section in view of the clinical
behaviour of spontaneous regression { 33501985 }. Melanoacanthoma is another new entity in this
edition as the clinical and histologic presentation needs to be distinguished from oral mucosal
melanoma { 27398186 }.
The epithelial tumours section is organized by tumour behaviour with squamous cell carcinoma
discussed last. Oral potentially malignant disorders and oral epithelial dysplasia (OED) have been
expanded to reflect advances in clinical, histological, and molecular findings. Dysplasia grading of
OED continues to be contentious, with different grading applied in different regions of the world.
The criteria for grading OED have been updated and include additional architectural features that
alone may confer a diagnosis of dysplasia [[Barnes, L , Eveson, JW , Reichart, P , Sidransky, D ,
eds. Pathology and Genetics of Head and Neck Tumours. Kleihues, P , Sobin, LH , series eds.
World
Health
Organization
Classification
of
Tumours. Lyon,
France: IARC
Press, 2005 ISBN:9283224175]] { 29606637 ; 30887394 } [[Odell E, Kujan O, Warnakulasuriya S,
Sloan P. Oral Diseases 2021]]. A three-tiered grading system of OED is maintained with
acknowledgement that defining dysplasia in this manner oversimplifies the inherent difficulties of
grading.
The clinical and histologic features of proliferative verrucous leukoplakia (PVL) have been better
defined and updated since the last edition { 33415517 }. The subtle histologic features of early PVL
which may easily be overlooked, are presented along with the panoply of histologic findings in all
stages of PVL. A section on oral submucous fibrosis is added with a description of histology and

469

clinical significance, since it is such an important OPMD in some parts of the world. Three
conditions have been removed from the table of OPMD. Candida infection is frequent in dysplastic
epithelium and infection is more frequent with higher grade but no good evidence has emerged that
candida is an independent risk factor for malignant transformation. Syphilis remains endemic in
many regions of the world { 30116697 } but syphilitic leukoplakia in tertiary syphilis appears
extremely rare. A risk of oral carcinoma in syphilis has remained { 8082966 } but syphilitic
leukoplakia appears to have all but disappeared. Its status as an OPMD was considered to be
largely of historical interest in the 2005 edition and so it has been removed from the list of OPMD.
Actinic keratosis is the key condition predisposing to lip vermilion squamous carcinoma. It has been
included in previous classifications primarily because ICD coding groups lip and oral cavity
together. However, its aetiology is distinct and anatomically the condition is extraoral. It has
therefore been removed from the list of OPMD, though its significance in lip cancer is not in
dispute.
Due to increased characterization and to be consistent with classification at other body sites, HPVassociated dysplasia (HPVOED) has been classified as a separate entity from conventional OED
{ 28407985 }. Despite strong p16 immunoexpression in HPVOED, incomplete data exists to
recommend this as a surrogate marker for HPV infection in the oral cavity { 32573035 }.
Both carcinoma cuniculatum and verrucous carcinoma are described separately from conventional
oral squamous cell carcinoma on the basis of distinctive presentation, histopatholoigcal
appearances and behaviour. The oral cavity is the most common location in the head and neck for
both these entities { 11443616 ; 30607347 }.
Under tumours of uncertain histogenesis, ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour has been
updated since the previous edition to reflect the molecular findings of a RREB1-MRTFB gene
fusion in 90% of cases { 29912715 }. The anterior dorsal tongue is still the most prevalent site, but
additional cases have been identified in the palate and mandible.
The previous edition included various soft tissue neoplasms in this chapter that are now included
in the soft tissue tumour chapter. Oral melanoma now appears in the melanocytic tumours chapter
and intraoral salivary gland lesions are included in the salivary gland chapter.
Non-neoplastic lesions

Necrotising sialometaplasia
Definition
Necrotising sialometaplasia is a reactive, self-limiting, inflammatory condition of salivary glands
usually resulting in necrosis or degeneration of salivary acini, and ductal squamous metaplasia.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA04 Diseases of salivary glands
Related terminology
Not recommended: Adenometaplasia
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The most common location is the posterolateral hard palate, but it can occur in other minor salivary
sites including tongue, oral floor, minor lacrimal glands, the upper airway, proximal esophagus,
trachea,
and
bronchi { 1923419 ; 3083385 ; 7741108 ; 19948640 ; 23793784 ; 24863247 ; 26634609 }. Major
salivary gland involvement is rare.
Clinical features
The classic palatal presentation is a single lateral ulcer near the junction of the hard and soft palate.
Bilateral or metachronous lesions may rarely occur. Swelling is typical and may precede the
development of a crater-like ulcer ranging in size from 10 - 15 mm, which can clinically mimic a

470

malignancy { 30776173 }. Most cases are asymptomatic; complaints of a burning sensation, mild
pain, or numbness are occasional { 1923419 }.
Epidemiology
Typically presents in the fourth decade of life with a male predilection { 1923419 }.
Etiology
Uncertain. Likely associated with trauma, vascular compromise, and/or hypoxia { 23837804 }.
Pathogenesis
Vascular compromise leading to ischemic infarction of seromucous glands and subsequent
regeneration { 12049830 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Ulcer ranging from 10-15 mm in diameter { 1923419 }
Histopathology
Pre-existing salivary lobular architecture is usually preserved { 19415943 }. Early lesions display
acinar infarction/necrosis accompanied by mucus spillage. Ulceration varies. As lesions mature,
involved ducts and acini undergo squamous metaplasia. Generally, cytologic features remain
bland but keratinocytic regenerative atypia with mitotic activity may be present. Remnants of ductal
cells, interspersed mucocytes, granulation reaction, and stromal subacute inflammation may
comingle. Recognition is most challenging when lobular preservation is poor, or when
pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia of intact, overlying mucosa merges with ductal squamous
metaplasia, simulating squamous carcinoma { 4716764 ; 19415943 }. Differential diagnosis should
include subacute necrotising sialadenitis { 10807718 ; 17142073 }.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Preservation of lobular architecture with squamous metaplasia of ducts and acini
Desirable: Infarction/necrosis of acini; pseudopepitheliomatous hyperplasia
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Heals spontaneously in 6-10 weeks, but healing may take as long as 6 months. Close follow-up is
needed to ensure proper healing. If healing does not occur, or if the lesion recurs, repeat biopsy is
indicated to rule out neoplasia or vasculopathy { 4716764 ; 22921832 }.

#24216
Necrotising sialometaplasia
The lobular architecture of minor salivary glands is preserved and also involves ducts.
Smooth contours and regenerative atypia of the lobules is evident amidst a reactive
myxocollagenous stroma.

471

#27257
Necrotising sialometaplasia
Necrosis of acini with loss of nuclei and cell borders with squamous metaplasia
maintaining a lobular configuration.

#27693
Necrotising sialometaplasia
Pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia and squamous metaplasia of residual ducts and
acini. The lobular architecture of the glands are preserved with the outlines of the residual
acini with necrosis and inflammation.

472

#27710
Necrotising sialometaplasia
Palatal ulcer lateral to midline.

#27677

Necrotising sialometaplasia
The image shows pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia and squamous metaplasia of
residual ducts and acini. The lobular architecture of the glands are preserved with the
outlines of the residual acini with necrosis and inflammation.

473

Multifocal epithelial hyperplasia
Definition
Multifocal epithelial hyperplasia (MEH) is a benign squamous epithelial proliferation caused by
human papillomavirus (HPV).
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
1E82.0 Focal epithelial hyperplasia of the oral mucous membranes
Related terminology
Acceptable: Heck's disease
Not recommended: focal epithelial hyperplasia
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The lesions predominantly affect the borders and tip of the tongue, the buccal mucosa along the
occlusal plane and the lips. The lower labial mucosa is more frequently affected than the upper
labial mucosa. When lesions involve the lip vermilion, they do not extend to perioral skin. The
keratinized hard palate and gingiva, floor of mouth and soft palate are rarely affected { 23061874 }.
Clinical features
Patients with MEH usually present with multiple, painless, sessile, smooth-surfaced papules that
are similar in colour to adjacent mucosa (papulonodular presentation). Early lesions can grow to
measure 10 mm in diameter. They can coalesce to form cobblestone plaques with increased
keratinisation. In the uncommon papillomatous presentation, the papules have a rough
pebbly surface { 23061874 }. Individual lesions are well-demarcated and tend to flatten when the
mucosa is extended, a helpful distinguishing feature.
Epidemiology
MEH is not limited to specific ethnic groups or geographical regions. It occurs in children and
adolescents, but has not been reported under 2 years of age. Adults are infrequently affected. A
male to female ratio of 3:4 is reported. Household transmission is common { 33501985 }.
Etiology
MEH is caused predominantly by HPV subtypes 13 and 32, although other genotypes have also
been detected { 33501985 }. Polymerase chain reaction and DNA or RNA in situ hybridisation for
HPV 13 and HPV 32 is positive { 23061874 ; 31473371 }.
Risk factors: Low socio-economic status, malnutrition and overcrowded living conditions are
significant contributing factors { 33501985 } that could explain the striking epidemiological
differences between developed and developing countries. Household spread of HPV 13 through
saliva and sharing of contaminated objects has been reported { 21697033 }. Medical conditions
that lead to immunosuppression are also associated with MEH. The condition is not transmitted
during vaginal birth or sexually transmitted since HPV 13 and 32 have not been reported in the
genital mucosa to date { 23061874 }.
Genetic factors: HLA-DR4 (DRB1*0404) has been associated with genetic susceptibility to the
disease { 15492185 ; 19165615 }.
Pathogenesis
Social conditions play an important role and are likely related to poor nutrition, which in turn leads
to cellular immunodeficiency during child growth. Infection with HPV 13 or 32, depending on tissuesite specificity and combined with local trauma, causes multiple papillomas
{ 19165615 ; 23061874 }. Trauma is exacerbated by poor dental status, sharp edges on teeth,
explaining the clinical distribution of the oral lesions.
Macroscopic appearance
Thickened epithelium with a cobblestone appearance.
Histopathology
MEH shows prominent exophytic acanthosis, broad and confluent rete ridges and normal epithelial
maturation. A papillary surface architecture is infrequently present. Occasional keratinocytes with
fragmented nuclei that may mimick a mitotic figure (“mitosoid bodies”) are present in the upper
epithelium, a viral cytopathic nuclear effect. Occasional koilocytes can be present within the

474

superficial epithelial layers { 23061874 ; 33501985 }. HPV subtypes 13 and 32 do not cause
functional inactivation of the RB gene, therefore p16 immunohistochemistry has no diagnostic
value.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant { 23061874 ; 33501985 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Routine detection of HPV in MEH is rarely required { 23061874 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: sessile or occasionally papilloma-like lesion; cobblestone surface appearance;
acanthotic epithelium, with mitosoid bodies in the upper epithelium.
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Most patients experience spontaneous regression when reaching puberty or with improved living
conditions { 33501985 }.

#26126: Multiple coalescing papules on the retrocommissural mucosa in an 8 year old boy.

#27287: Acanthotic epithelium with minimal superficial keratosis. Rete pegs show elongation and
widening with tendency to coalesce. Cell maturation is well preserved.

475

#27288: Characteristic mitosoid bodies representing cytopathic viral nuclear damage.

#26975: WSI image showing acanthotic epithelium with minimal superficial keratosis. Rete pegs
show elongation and widening with tendency to coalesce. The cell maturation is well preserved.
Characteristic mitosoid bodies representing cytopathic viral nuclear damage are noted.

476

Oral melanoacanthoma
Definition
Oral melanoacanthoma is a non-neoplastic process, which is distinct from the neoplastic cutaneous
melanoacanthoma. It is characterized by a dual proliferation of epidermal and melanocytic cells
with a characteristic clinical profile of rapid initial growth but indolent behaviour.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E90.3 Benign neoplasm of other or unspecified parts of mouth
Related terminology
Acceptable: melanoacanthosis
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The buccal mucosa is affected in approximately half of the cases, although it may occur on any
oral mucosal surface including palate, gingiva/alveolar mucosa, labial mucosa, or tongue
{ 19254138 ; 27398186 }.
Clinical features
Patients with melanoacanthoma present with a rapidly growing, pigmented lesion which is usually
smooth but can be slightly raised in appearance { 19254138 ; 12544093 }. It is most frequently
asymptomatic, but mild symptoms such as pain, itching or burning have been described
{ 12544093 }. Unifocal lesions are most common, with multifocal lesions in a minority of cases
{ 19254138 ; 27398186 }.
Epidemiology
Melanoacanthoma occurs most frequently in people with higher natural levels of skin pigmentation.
Females are more commonly affected. Ages affected range from childhood to the elderly with
average presentation in the third to fifth decades { 12544093 ; 19254138 ; 27398186 }.
Etiology
Although the etiology is unknown, multiple reactive factors have been proposed including chronic
trauma and reactions to oral care products such as toothpaste or mouthwash { 27398186 }. Oral
melanoacanthoma has been rarely reported in association with Laugier-Hunziker syndrome and
Addison’s disease { 29450847 ; 28658350 }.
Pathogenesis
Although the pathogenesis is not proven, it is thought that chronic or acute trauma may stimulate
melanocyte proliferation in oral melanoacanthoma in up to 75% of cases { 27398186 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Pigmented mucosal tissue
Histopathology
Epithelial and melanocytic proliferation with acanthosis and dendritic melanocytes are present
throughout the epithelium. Spongiosis and submucosal chronic inflammatory infiltrate are noted
and exocytosis of lymphocytes may also be seen { 12544093 }. Melanin deposition in the basal
layer is frequent. Langerhan cells may also be prominent. Nuclear pleomorphism,
hyperchromatism, or nesting of melanocytes is not present { 19254138 }. Immunohistochemistry
with S-100 protein, HMB-45, or Melan A highlight dendritic melanocytes { 19254138 ; 23356913 }.
Mucosal melanoma is the most important differential diagnosis { 27398186 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: dendritic melanocyte proliferation high in the epithelium; spongiosis; absence of atypia
and invasion of melanocytic nests
Desirable: epithelial acanthosis; melanin deposition at basal layer
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction

477

Oral melanoacanthoma frequently regresses after biopsy, even with incomplete removal
{ 32856618 ; 12544093 ; 23356913 }. Malignant transformation has not been described.

#27796: Diffuse melanin pigmentation in the buccal mucosa of rapid onset but otherwise
asymptomatic.

#27797: The epithelium is acanthotic and spongiotic.

478

#27798: Dendritic melanocytes within the intercellular spaces of the acanthotic epithelium may be
seen but S100 protein immunohistochemistry is helpful when they are scarce or lightly
pigmented.

#27809: Dendritic melanocytes within the intercellular spaces of the acanthotic epithelium may be
seen.

479

#27810: S100 protein immunohistochemistry is helpful when they are scarce or lightly pigmented.

#30190: Oral melanoacanthoma of the buccal mucosa, Acanthosis of the epithelium is apparent
along with melanin deposition at the basal layer and dendritic melanocytes within the epithelium.

480

#24773: Oral melanoacanthoma of the buccal mucosa, Acanthosis of the epithelium is apparent
along with melanin deposition at the basal layer and dendritic melanocytes within the epithelium.

#28400: Oral melanoacanthoma of the buccal mucosa, Acanthosis of the epithelium is apparent
along with melanin deposition at the basal layer and dendritic melanocytes within the epithelium.
Epithelial tumours
Papillomas

Squamous papillomas
Definition
Squamous papilloma is a benign, well-demarcated, exophytic neoplasm with finger-like surface
projections or cauliflower-like morphology.
ICD-O coding
8052/0 Squamous papilloma
ICD-11 coding
2E90.3 & XH50T2 Benign neoplasm of other or unspecified parts of mouth & Squamous cell
papilloma, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: squamous cell papilloma
Subtype(s)
Condyloma acuminatum
Verruca vulgaris
Localization
Any site in the oral cavity may be involved, but the soft and hard palate, tongue, lips and gingiva
are most commonly affected { 6154913 ; 25598510 }.
Clinical features
Most lesions are less than 10 mm in diameter, pedunculated, white with a rough, ‘cauliflower-like’
or ‘finger-like’ surface. Larger lesions up to 20 mm are rarely seen, but these are still well-defined
and regular in shape. Sessile lesions are less common, and may be pink in colour
{ 6154913 ; 25598510 }. Squamous papilloma is usually a solitary, well-defined lesion and the
occurrence of two or more lesions simultaneously is rare { 25598510 }. The clinical differential
diagnosis includes verruca vulgaris, condyloma acuminatum, verruciform xanthoma and giant cell
fibroma { 30693456 }. Immunocompromised individuals, particularly HIV-AIDS individuals
frequently present with multiple or florid lesions that may be difficult to distinguish from condyloma
acuminatum and multifocal epithelial hyperplasia. In such cases, lesions with overlapping clinical
481

appearance may be referred to as ‘benign HPV-associated oral lesions’ { 29480634 }. Patients on
long-term highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) also have increased number of HPV
associated oral lesions { 23278731 }.
Epidemiology
Squamous papillomas are common and can occur at any age from childhood to elderly, although
they are found most frequently in adults, with a mean age in the 4th to 5th decades with no sex
predilection { 6154913 ; 24655529 ; 25598510 }.
Etiology
HPV is the most important etiologic factor, and studies have shown an association with HPV 6 and
11 { 18354839 }. There is no association with high-risk, carcinogenic HPV types
{ 28639061 ; 31352627 }. The reported prevalence of low-risk HPV DNA in oral squamous cell
papillomas varies considerably, with some of this variability attributable to technical differences,
the characteristics of the HPV life cycle and the presence of nonpathogenic or passenger HPV in
normal
oral
mucosa
in
a
small
percentage
of
individuals
{ 25598510 ; 29909908 ; 30693456 }. Studies that used DNA in-situ hybridisation have reported
the presence of HPV DNA in 13% - 62% of squamous papillomas { 1648192 ; 1648194 }. Studies
that used the more sensitive technique of PCR (polymerase chain reaction) reported prevalence
rates between 21% and 68% { 7552864 ; 31352627 }.
Pathogenesis
HPV-induced proliferation of squamous epithelial cells with altered maturation. HPV low risk IHC
have yielded variable findings where 4% to 41% of cases showed intranuclear staining in the upper
epithelial layers { 30828618 }. Negative cases may be due to a low level of detectable viral antigen
expression { 25598510 }. HPV DNA ISH labels nuclei of upper epithelial layers in both koilocytic
and non-koilocytic cells { 1648192 ; 24703405 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Exophytic, pedunculated, or sessile papillary epithelial projections.
Histopathology
Squamous papilloma is an exophytic papillary proliferation of hyperplastic stratified squamous
epithelium with a narrow branching core of fibrovascular connective tissue with dilated capillaries
{ 25598510 ; 30693456 }. The papillary projections may be pointed or round { 2836773 }. The
majority of lesions show parakeratosis, but orthokeratotic or non-keratinized lesions may be seen
{ 6154913 }. The sessile type generally lacks the finger-like projections imparting a blunted
cauliflower appearance. The stratified squamous epithelium shows orderly maturation, with no
signs of dysplasia { 24703405 }. Koilocytes are infrequent in squamous papillomas compared to
verruca vulgaris and condyloma acuminatum. Basal cell hyperplasia with mild nuclear atypia and
increased mitotic activity may be present but can be attributed to HPV induced hyperplasia, or
trauma or inflammation { 6154913 ; 25598510 ; 25749203 }.
Condyloma acuminatum exhibits an exophytic broad based growth of acanthotic
stratified squamous epithelium with bulbous rete that may interconnect { 30693456 }. The papillary
fronds are more blunted than squamous papillomas with invagination of the parakeratin that fills
the crypts between the papillae. Koilocytes are usually present in the upper spinous layer.
Verruca vulgaris is uncommon intraorally, and the histologic features are identical to the
cutaneous verruca vulgaris with a papillary architecture and extensive keratinization
{ 25749203 ; 30693456 }. At the periphery of the lesion the rete ridges are inwardly facing. Coarse
keratohyalin granules and koilocytic change in the upper spinous layer are present but epithelial
maturation is maintained.
There can be histologic overlap between the benign papillomatous lesions of the oral cavity.
Squamous papillomas that are sessile and show koilocytes in the upper spinous layers may be
difficult to differentiate from verruca vulgaris and condyloma acuminatum { 11323946 ; 30693456 }.
More importantly, squamous papilloma should be distinguished from dysplastic papillary epithelial
proliferations and verrucous dysplastic lesions { 24703405 ; 30693456 }. Clinical information of an
irregular shaped lesion > 10 mm in size with the histologic finding of dysplasia should raise the
suspicion of atypical papillary, potentially malignant lesions arising in the setting of proliferative
verrucous leukoplakia.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant

482

Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: exophytic, pedunculated or sessile, papillary architecture; epithelial hyperplasia:
para/orthokeratosis; fibrovascular cores; no evidence of dysplasia
Desirable: superficial koilocytes
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Treatment is simple excision and recurrence is rare { 25598510 ; 31232385 }.
Immunocompromised
patients
may
require
multiple
excisions,
cryotherapy
and/or immunomodulatory agents.

fibrovascular connective tissue covered by hyperplastic stratified squamous parakeratinized
epithelium.

#26733: Papillary projections covered by stratified squamous parakeratinized epithelium with
basal cell hyperplasia and orderly maturation.

#27575: Condyloma acuminatum, Multiple, coalescing condylomata in a HIV+ male.

#28314: Squamous papilloma, lacking exophytic papillary projections, sessile papillomas have
blunted, rounded papilla. Koilocytes generally are absent. The architecture may be a reflection of
frictional forces in the oral cavity.

#28316: Ventral tongue squamous papilloma presenting as a solitary, white, somewhat sessile
lesion with finger-like fronds.

#26731: Bisected specimen of squamous cell papilloma of the oral mucosa showing a welldefined, pedunculated, exophytic proliferation of stratified squamous parakeratinized epithelium
that forms finger-like projections with a thin fibro-vascular connective tissue core.

Oral potentially malignant disorders
Definition
Oral potentially malignant disorders (OPMDs) is a heterogenous group of clinically-defined
conditions associated with a variable risk of progression to oral squamous carcinoma. Most
produce clinically visible lesions.
ICD-O coding
8077/0 Low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion
8077/2 High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Acceptable: epithelial precursor lesion
Not recommended: precancerous lesion; precancerous condition
Subtype(s)
See table 1
Localization
OPMDs can involve any intraoral site. Lesions presenting on the lateral/ventral tongue and floor of
mouth are more closely associated with cancer progression { 30887394 }.
Clinical features
Specific individual definitions are published { 33128420 }. Most high-risk OPMDs form red, white or
speckled lesions. 'Leukoplakia' is a clinical term used to describe white plaque of questionable risk
after having excluded other known diseases. Leukoplakia varies in thickness, and its surface
ranges from flat, thin, uniform and homogeneous in color, to granular, fissured, nodular or
verrucous, the latter types having a higher risk. Erythroplakia is defined as a red patch that cannot
be characterized clinically or pathologically as another definable lesions. Erythroplakia is a thin, or
slightly depressed, red patch of the oral mucosa and is less common than leukoplakia.
Erythroleukoplakia or speckled leukoplakia has both an erythroplakia and leukoplakia component.
Erythroplakia is much less common than leukoplakia but is much more likely to be associated with
high-grade dysplasia or carcinoma (>90%) { 30887394 }. Diagnosis of the common OPMDs is
partly by exclusion and therefore usually requires biopsy.
Epidemiology
Oral leukoplakia is considered to be the most common OPMD and the mean global prevalence of
leukoplakia ranges from 1-4% however a much higher prevalence is reported in south-eastern
Asia { 29738071 }. In contrast, erythroplakia is rare with prevalence <0.1% { 29480616 }.
Leukoplakia is more common in men but women have a higher malignant transformation risk
{ 33606345 }. Advanced age also is associated with an increased risk of cancer development.
Features of oral submucous fibrosis, proliferative verrucous leukoplakia and HPV-associated
dysplasia are given in their separate sections. Prevalence data are given in Table 2.
Etiology
Tobacco (smoked and smokeless), and to a lesser degree alcohol abuse, are strongly associated
with most OPMD. The use of areca nut, with or without tobacco causes submucous
fibrosis. However, for proliferative verrucous leukoplakia and many cases of OPMD, there is no
tobacco association and the etiopathogenesis is unknown, though genetic, oral microbiome,
immunity or other undiscovered factors may play a role { 31803979 }. HPV-associated oral
dysplasia is rare and covered separately.
Pathogenesis
The pathogenesis varies between OPMDs { 33128420 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The appearance varies between OPMD but most present as white, erythematous, or pigmented
macules, papules, or verrucous plaques, which can coalesce.
Histopathology
The histopathological features vary between OPMDs. In most OPMD the risk is considered to relate
to the presence and degree of oral epithelial dysplasia, ranging from minimal to severe. However,
some OPMD, particularly proliferative verrucous leukoplakia and leukoplakia with a verrucous
architecture have minimal dysplastic changes for their risk of malignant transformation.
Cytology

487

Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Individual diagnostic criteria are published separately { 33128420 }.
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
The transformation risk in most OPMDs is low. A metanalysis of leukoplakia found a mean global
annual transformation rate of 1.56% { 31803979 }. Both a large lesion size and erythroleukoplakia
are associated with increased potential for cancer { 16316774 }. The status of lichen planus as
potentially malignant is generally accepted but the degree of risk remains a matter of controversy
despite many studies. Lack of definitive clinical and histological diagnostic criteria and overlap in
histological features between lichen planus and dysplasia confound analysis. Wide variation in risk
is reported, higher in disease lacking typical features, sometimes called lichenoid lesions
{ 17112770 ; 33616234 }. The early stages of proliferative verrucous leukoplakia resemble lichen
planus both clinically and histologically (33185900} and it may be impossible to differentiate oral
lichen planus and mild dysplasia in some patients, though the distinction may become possible with
time { 27401683 ; 28060366 }. Similar uncertainty surrounds lupus erythematosus inside the oral
cavity, as opposed to on the lip vermilion border { 33128420 }.
