Oral epithelial dysplasia
Definition
Oral epithelial dysplasia (OED) is a spectrum of architectural and cytological epithelial changes
resulting from accumulation of genetic alterations, usually arising in a range of oral potentially
malignant disorders (OPMD), and indicating a risk of malignant transformation to squamous cell
carcinoma (SCC).
ICD-O coding
8077/0 Low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion
8077/2 High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Acceptable: epithelial precursor lesions
Not recommended: intraepithelial neoplasia; squamous intraepithelial lesions; carcinoma in situ
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
OED can involve any intraoral site, but its presence on the lateral/ ventral tongue, retromolar area,
and floor of mouth is associated with higher risk of malignant transformation than at other sites
{ 30887394 }. Gingiva and buccal mucosa are other frequently affected sites. The hard palate is
rarely affected.
Clinical features
OED itself has no clinical meaning or consistent presentation and its clinical features vary widely
depending on the OPMD. Erythroplakia and erythroleukoplakia are the presentations most likely to
be associated with dysplasia or carcinoma (>90%), on biopsy or subsequently { 30887394 }.
Epidemiology
Most OED arise in OPMD, each of which have distinct epidemiological features. The prevalence of
the various OPMDs is reported separately { 29738071 }.
Etiology

491

Most OEDs are associated with tobacco (smoked and smokeless), or areca nut, alone or in
combination, or to a lesser degree, alcohol abuse, but for many cases of OED the
etiopathogenesis remains unknown { 29606637 ; 31803979 }.
Pathogenesis
OED is associated with the progressive accumulation of genetic and epigenetic changes including
LOH/copy number alterations, hypermethylation, changes in RNA molecule expression
(mRNA and miRNA), and somatic mutations. These lead to chromosomal and genomic instability
at an early stage { 33577101 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Varied appearance depending on the OPMD.
Histopathology
OED comprises changes resulting from abnormal proliferation, maturation, and differentiation of
epithelial cells { 21501232 ; 31090138 ; 32447493 }. Table (see #25434Table #25434) lists the
architectural and cytological disturbances that are used to diagnose OED. Individually these
features are relatively non-specific, but the number and combination of features, which vary
between OPMDs, indicates epithelial dysplasia. OED can be diagnosed on the basis of
architectural or cytological features alone. Traditionally OED is divided into three grades of severity
and judging the number of thirds affected is one factor in assigning grade. Architectural and
cytological atypia usually increases in higher grade lesions, with mild dysplasia characterised by
cytological atypia limited to the basal third, moderate dysplasia by extension to the middle third,
and severe dysplasia by extending to the upper third. However, defining dysplasia grade only in
this manner oversimplifies the complexity of grading. Cytological atypia confined to the basal third
may be sufficient for a diagnosis of severe dysplasia depending on individual features present,
particularly bulbous rete processes, budding and disorganisation of basal cells, and marked
cytological atypia.
It has long been recognized that many OED have a lichenoid immune response composed of a
lymphohistiocytic band-like infiltrate with T-cell mediated epithelial cell destruction
{ 3862044 ; 28060366 ; 33415517 }. This histopathological finding can be confused with oral lichen
planus
in
which
the
presence
of
dysplasia
is
an
exclusion
criterion
{ 27401683 ; 29606637 ; 30887394 }. More difficult to assess is dysplasia grading when primarily
architectural features of OED are present in the absence of or with minimal cytological atypia. This
combination is commonly found in papillary and verrucous lesions { 29606637 ; 33415517 }.
Carcinoma in situ in the oral cavity is considered synonymous with severe dysplasia but the term
is not recommended.
While OED is considered to be an indicator of malignant transformation there are important caveats
to its clinical use. It has variable observer reproducibility (intra- and inter-observer)
{ 26216170 ; 32516857 }. Consensus grading after review by more than one pathologist may
enhance diagnostic reliability { 21499237 ; 26216170 ; 23761273 }. To improve reproducibility,
some authors advocate a binary system (low grade to high grade) for dysplasia grading
{ 18251935 ; 23217539 }, but this still requires additional validation against malignant
transformation.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Cytological or architectural atypia; nuclear hyperchromasia; high N:C ratios
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Not all OED progress to malignancy (3-50%) { 2939631 ; 19455705 ; 2939631 9; 31860085 } and
results of studies on prognostic value of dysplasia grade are ambivalent
{ 804458 ; 14720191 ; 23761273 }. OPMDs without evidence of OED can progress to SCC. The
predictive value of OED grading plays a significant role in stratifying malignant transformation risk.
Although the absence of OED does not preclude malignant transformation { 31860085 }, a higher
grade of OED is significantly associated with both earlier and higher risk of malignant

492

transformation { 19455705 ; 2939631 9; 31803979 ; 32447493 }. A recent population-based
cohort study that included more than 1,800 patients showed that the overall hazard ratios for
malignant transformation increased significantly with the grade of dysplasia, the overall hazard
ratios being 4.9 and 15.8 for mild dysplasia and severe dysplasia, respectively { 31860085 }. This
and other data { 34418233 }support a three-grade system to both differentiate lesions with different
risks of transformation and estimate time to transformation. The presence of low grades of OPMD
carry a very long-term risk of malignant transformation, extending for at least 18 years
{ 31860085 ; 34418233 } apparently not reducing with time { 33038723 }. There are no established
biomarkers to aid dysplasia grading or malignant transformation prediction though DNA ploidy and
loss of heterozygosity assays have shown promising results { 33577101 }.

#27705

confined to basal and parabasal layer with generalised premature keratinisation.

#27707
Oral epithelial dysplasia
Mild dysplasia: architectural features of dysplasia include a sharp lateral demarcation of
the epithelium with an abrupt change to orthokeratosis. Mild anisonucleosis and
hyperchromatism and a slightly disorganised basal cell layer with generalised premature
keratinisation is seen.

Proliferative verrucous leukoplakia
Definition
Proliferative verrucous leukoplakia (PVL) is a clinico-pathological variant of oral leukoplakia that is
multifocal, persistent and progressive with a high rate of recurrence, and a high risk of progression
to squamous cell carcinoma.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA01.00 Leukoplakia
Related terminology
Acceptable: proliferative leukoplakia, proliferative multifocal leukoplakia
Not recommended: atypical verrucoid epithelial proliferation; atypical verrucous hyperplasia;
papillary or verrucous hyperkeratosis with dysplasia
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Gingiva and alveolar ridge are the most frequently involved sites followed by the buccal mucosa,
tongue
{ 28209441 ; 31990082 ;
33185900} and hard palate { 24471527 ; 31630872 }.
Clinical features
Initial lesions in PVL patients are often indistinguishable from oral lichen planus and homogeneous
oral leukoplakias, forming smooth to fissured/verruciform or verrucous white or mixed white and
red patches usually without ulceration { 31630872 ; 33415517 }. Multiple non-contiguous lesions or
single lesion > 40 mm involving one site; or single lesion > 30 mm involving contiguous sites are
characteristic { 29337414 }. Thick, verrucoid marginal gingival leukoplakias that encircle the tooth
especially when multifocal, are characteristic of PVL { 32725623 }.
Epidemiology

497

PVL occurs predominantly in elderly women with no racial predilection and mean age of 66.8 years
{ 28209441 ; 29337414 }.
Etiology
Unknown. PVL does not seem to be correlated with the major risk factors for oral carcinoma
including tobacco (smoked and smokeless), alcohol abuse, and areca nut/betel quid chewing
{ 24471527 }. There is no apparent etiologic association between PVL and HPV, Epstein-Barr virus
or Candida albicans { 24115154 ; 29427033 ; 30190088 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown. Like other dysplastic lesions, tissue in PVL lesions show loss of heterozygosity and DNA
ploidy anomalies { 30190088 }.
Macroscopic appearance
White mucosa, ranging from flat to thickened, sometimes with a corrugated surface or exophytic
nodules.
Histopathology
Early lesions of PVL are unremarkable flat keratoses with architectural features that may be
atypical, possibly premature keratinisation, sharp lateral margins, increased keratin and no
cytological atypia; readily underdiagnosed as hyperkeratosis without dysplasia or lichen planus.
These gradually develop the typical verrucous morphology.
Corrugated hyperkeratotic lesions exhibit verruco-papillary or disproportionate flat hyper ortho/parakeratosis with minimal or no dysplasia. Skip areas of normal to abnormal to normal is a
common finding. A sharp abrupt transition from adjacent unaffected normal epithelium is usually
seen.
Proliferative bulky epithelial lesions demonstrate atypical, hyperkeratotic epithelial architecture
with/without dysplasia. Both an exophytic and endophytic growth pattern can be present and the
epithelium shows bulbous rete pegs that sometimes coalesce.
At all stages of PVL, lymphocytic infiltration in the epithelial-stromal interface can be mistaken for
lichen planus: intact basal cells and/or dysplasia preclude(s) that diagnosis
{ 29606637 ; 30887394 }. A strict, stepwise histopathologic continuum from the corrugated
hyperkeratotic to the proliferative epithelial stages and progression to malignancy does not always
occur (see #24428Table #24428){ 29606637 ; 33415517 }.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
- Clinical presentation consistent with PVL
- Early lesions: premature keratinisation, minimal cytological atypia
- Later lesions: verrucous, nodular or bulky architecture; variable dysplasia.
Desirable:
- Lichenoid host response
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
PVL is best managed by active patient surveillance with biopsy when clinically indicated
{ 26044786 ; 29606637 ; 31074011 }. Laser ablation, surgical excision, and radiotherapy are
associated with high recurrence rate and are often ineffective { 26044786 ; 28209441 }. Disease
progression is unpredictable. A systematic review of 17 studies reported progression to malignancy
in 43.87% (95% CI = 31.93–56.13) of PVL cases { 34009718 }.

Submucous fibrosis
Definition
Oral submucous fibrosis (OSF) is a chronic, insidious disease characterized by progressive fibrosis
of submucosal tissues of the oral cavity and the oropharynx with a risk of transformation to SCC.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA02.2 Oral submucous fibrosis
Related terminology
Not recommended: atrophia idiopathica (trophica) mucosae oris; idiopathic scleroderma of the
mouth; idiopathic palatal fibrosis, and sclerosing stomatitis
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Involves the oral cavity, oropharynx and may extend to the upper third of the esophagus
{ 16311067 }.
Clinical features
Fibrous banding in the buccal mucosae, lip, or palate leads to progressive limitation of mouth
opening. Associated features include burning mouth, depapillation of the tongue, blanching, and
leathery mucosa { 31915073 }. Clinical grades are described with varying severity (see
table #25763)
Epidemiology
OSF is exclusively described among populations in India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Nepal, Taiwan and
among the Pacific Islanders, but case reports have been described from Hunan and Hainan

502

provinces in Southern China, South Vietnam, Thailand and among migrants from the Indian
subcontinent to Europe, USA and South and East Africa [[DOI (BOOK) : 10.1007/978-2-319-149110, DOI (CHAPTER) : 10.1007/978-2-319-14911-0_8 Warnakulasuriya S, Tilakaratne WM, Kerr AR,
Contemporary Oral Oncology; Biology, Epidemiology, Etiology and Prevention, Oral Submucous
fibrosis, pp329-354, 2017 AG Switzerland, Springer Nature]]. A systematic review found OSF to be
a prevalent OPMD worldwide (4.96%; 95% CI = 2.28-8.62) with significant differences between
populations { 29738071 }.
Etiology
International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) has evaluated that areca nut is the main
etiological factor for OSF { 15635762 }. The relative risk (RR) estimates for OSF reported in casecontrol studies among areca nut chewers from India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, and Taiwan ranged from
1.8 to 172 { 27422417 }. Arecoline, a principal alkaloid in areca nut has been shown to induce
fibroblastic proliferation and increased collagen formation { 15946178 ; 16311067 ; 27289264 }
[[TLO Monograph 128 IARC 978-92-832-0195-3 ]].
Pathogenesis
The main pathological change in OSF is the increased accumulation of type 1 collagen within the
subepithelial tissues. Decreased levels of matrix metalloproteinases and increased amounts of
tissue inhibitors of matrix metalloproteinases have been reported { 22385081 }. Polyphenols of
areca nut such as flavonoids, catechin and tannins cause collagen fibres to crosslink, and thereby
make them resistant to degradation by collagenase. Lysyl oxidase (LOX), encoded by the LOX
gene is upregulated { 10426196 }. Cytokines such as TGF- β, induce transdifferentiation of
fibroblasts into myofibroblasts { 23284772 }. αγβ6-dependent TGF- β1 activation is also implicated
{ 26076896 ; 21171082 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Leathery mucosal texture and pale whitish mucosa, loss of lingual papillae.
Histopathology
A spectrum of histopathological changes is evident from early to advanced stage
{ 15078487 ; 19409103 ; 31205385 ; 31310693 ; 33008091 }.
Initially
the
epithelium is
hyperplastic and subsequently progresses to marked atrophy with loss of rete ridges. Epithelial
dysplasia starts to appear unpredictably with progression of the disease. Budding rete morphology
is a very early dysplastic feature. Changes in the submucosa at early stage are minimal with slightly
increased vascularity, inflammatory infiltrate, and increased fibrillar collagen and collagen fiber
bundles with interspersed fibroblasts. Later the collagen becomes homogeneous, starting
superficially with juxta-epithelial hyalinization. Advanced cases show loss of vascularity,
hyalinization of collagen, dense fibrosis extending to underlying tissues with muscle degeneration
and complete replacement of loose connective tissue (areolar and reticular tissue) by fibrous tissue.
Attempts have been made to classify OSF into histopathological stages but different parts of the
oral mucosa can be affected to varying degrees, so that histopathological grading may not be
representative { 5224185 ; 16091118 ; 19409103 ; 31915073 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: palpable fibrous bands in the oral mucosa, subepithelial hyalinisation and fibrosis
Desirable: Limitation of mouth opening, fibrosis extending to muscle; loss of tongue papillae;
epithelial atrophy
Staging
Staging is based on clinical features and histopathology. Clinical stages are based on mouth
opening (>35mm; 20-35mm and <20 mm), presence of an OPMD (leukoplakia, erythroplakia,
proliferative verrucous hyperplasia) and transformation to carcinoma { 21382138 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Oral submucous fibrosis is a fertile background for squamous carcinoma, developing in 4.2% (CI:
2.7%-5.6%) of patients and cases with oral epithelial dysplasia have a higher potential for
malignant transformation compared to cases without dysplasia { 33205543 }. There are no
established molecular predictive markers. LOH in genes within the 13 q14-q33, hypoxia, and
reactive oxygen species (ROS) may have some predictive value for transformation
{ 18221327 ; 27289264 }.

503

#26924: Fibrous bands of buccal mucosa.

#26925: Palatal fibrosis and shrunken uvula.

504

#27805: Epithelium shows dysplastic changes. The submucosa is fibrosed and contains a diffuse
inflammatory cell infiltrate.

#27806: Marked epithelial atrophy, flattening of rete ridges, juxta-epithelial hyalinisation and
homogenous collagen.

505

#27807: An advanced case showing fibrosis involving muscles, muscle degeneration and foci of
chronic inflammatory cells.

#28401: World map showing countries reporting arecanut consumption.

506

#28312: Oral mucosa covered by dysplastic epithelium in a verrucous keratosis with mild basal
cytological atypia amounting to mild dysplasia. The underlying tissue shows fibrosis extending
deeply around nerves and muscle fibres, with atrophy of the superficial muscle.

#28313: Buccal mucosa with epithelium showing keratosis, premature keratinisation and some
minimal anisonucleosis and hyperchromatism of the basal and suprabasal cells just amounting to
mild dysplasia. Beneath the epithelium fibrosis extends into fat.

Edward W. Odell

507

HPV-associated dysplasia
Definition
Human papillomavirus associated oral epithelial dysplasia (HPVOED) is characterized by
distinctive viral cytopathic changes caused by transcriptionally active high-risk HPV with a risk of
progression to squamous cell carcinoma.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Not recommended: HPV-associated squamous intraepithelial neoplasia, koilocytic dysplasia, oral
Bowen disease, oral bowenoid papulosis
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The ventral/lateral tongue and floor of mouth are the commonest sites followed by buccal mucosa.
Palatal
mucosa,
gingiva
and labial
mucosa
are
less
commonly
affected
{ 28799537 ; 31617604 ; 32632179 }.
Clinical features
Patients with HPVOED generally present as a white, red/white, or red patch, usually flat and
demarcated but sometimes slightly raised or nodular { 28799537 ; 31617604 }.
Epidemiology
There is a male predilection (M:F=6:1) with a peak in the sixth decade and a wide age range
{ 23522647 ; 28799537 ; 31617604 ; 31902664 ; 32632179 }.
Etiology
Epithelial infection by high-risk HPV subtypes, usually type 16, though others may be detected
{ 28799537 ; 28407985 ; 32573035 }. In limited series, most patients are tobacco smokers
{ 28799537 } and some are immunosuppressed.
Pathogenesis
Unclear in this context but assumed to follow pathways causing cervical dysplasia through
transcription of E6/7, p53 degradation and p16 overexpression { 12525422 }. Lesions vary, some
show viral integration through downregulation of HPVE2 { 30100997 } and others express HPVE4
and L1 indicating possible productive infection { 31617604 }. Apolipoprotein B enzyme catalytic
subunit-like protein-3B (APOBEC3B)-associated mutagenesis has also been implicated in its
pathogenesis { 32632179 }.
Macroscopic appearance
None
Histopathology
The epithelial surface is covered by a brightly eosinophilic layer of parakeratin in most cases
although orthokeratosis may be seen. There are marked architectural and cytological changes of
dysplasia characterized by a monotonous population of keratinocytes exhibiting basaloid
morphology and a high nuclear:cytoplasmic ratio. Some cases show pleomorphic cells and
koilocytes may be present superficially { 28799537 ; 31902664 ; 28407985 ; 31617604 }. Two
types of cells are characteristic for HPV cytopathic effect: karyorrhectic cells with condensed coarse
chromatin that resemble cells in mitosis, with a peri-cellular halo resulting from loss of attachment
to adjacent cells, and apoptotic cells with dense eosinophilic cytoplasm that in initial stages contain
residual chromatin { 28799537 ; 31902664 }. There is a continuum between these two cell types.
The specificity of karyorrhectic and apoptotic keratinocytes as a surrogate for HPVOED increases
with greater number of such cells { 28407985 ; 30100997 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Although there is strong and diffuse nuclear and cytoplasmic positivity for
p16 by immunohistochemistry (IHC), in a continuous band ("block positivity"), often sharply
demarcated from the adjacent non-dysplastic epithelium, this is not yet validated for diagnosis in
this setting { 31617604 }. Most studies suggest that positivity in more than 50% of the epithelial
thickness, excluding the keratin layer, suggests HPV-associated dysplasia however, studies have
not defined threshold for p16 interpretation { 31617604 }.

508

Differential The combination of dysplasia, viral cytopathic changes, and presence of high-risk HPV
distinguishes HPVOED from conventional OED, flat warts, and multifocal epithelial hyperplasia
{ 23599160 ; 25749203 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Strong and diffuse nuclear and cytoplasmic p16 IHC expression in the presence of OED with viral
cytopathic changes should be supported by testing for high-risk HPV by DNA or RNA in situ
hybridisation,
or
polymerase
chain
reaction
(PCR)
{ 28407985 ; 28799537 ; 30100997 ; 31902664 }. Standard consensus PCR alone without strong
p16 reaction and supportive histopathologic criteria is insufficient for the diagnosis of
HPVOED { 32573035 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Dysplasia with prominent karyorrhectic/apoptotic cells; presence of high-risk HPV
Desirable: Strong p16 IHC expression exceeding half epithelial thickness, excluding keratin;
evidence of E6/7 transcription
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Based on limited data, development of invasive squamous cell carcinoma occurs in 5% to 15% of
cases { 28799537 ; 28407985 }. To date, there is no validated grading criteria to predict
transformation. In the interim, it is recommended that HPVOED should be graded and managed
similar to conventional OED pending further evidence. In general, such HPVOEDs are amenable
to surgical excision. The role of prophylactic vaccination in the management of HPVOED is
unknown.

#26690: Dysplastic epithelium with a brightly eosinophilic keratin surface and basaloid
morphology.

509

#26696: Marked architectural and cytological changes of dysplasia with karyorrhectic and
apoptotic cells.

#26697:Epithelium shows full thickness intense cytoplasmic and nuclear immunoreactivity for
p16.

510

#26698: RNA in situ hybridisation for high-risk HPV shows punctate dot-like nuclear staining.

#30951: Marked architectural and cytological changes of dysplasia with karyorrhectic and
apoptotic cells.

#27097: Marked architectural and cytological changes of dysplasia with karyorrhectic and
apoptotic cells.

#27098: Strong and diffuse nuclear and cytoplasmic immunoreactivity for p16.

#27099: High-risk HPV DNA in-situ hybridisation demonstrating punctate and diffuse nuclear
positivity. Positive and negative controls to the left hand side.
Squamous cell carcinomas

Oral squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is a malignant neoplasm arising from the mucosal
epithelium of the oral cavity and showing variable squamous differentiation.
ICD-O coding
8071/3 Squamous cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B6E.0 Squamous cell carcinoma of other or ill-defined sites in the lip, oral cavity or pharynx
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Spindle cell; basaloid; acantholytic; adenosquamous; papillary; lymphoepithelial
Localization

512

OSCC can arise from any oral mucosal site. In European populations, the most commonly affected
sites are lateral/ventral tongue, floor of mouth, posterior buccal mucosa and gingiva/alveolar
mucosa { 19563504 ; 24813775 }. In South-Central Asia, OSCC most commonly affects the buccal
mucosa because of the prevalence of areca nut/betel quid habit { 29242026 }.
Clinical features
Patients with OSCCs may be completely asymptomatic, particularly at early stage, whereas
advanced tumours are associated with pain, alteration in sensation, restriction of tongue movement
or swallowing { 20400366 }. OSCCs may appear as white, red, or mixed, flat/nodular/mass lesions
of varying size. When present, advanced ulcers often have a raised and rolled margin, however
early OSCC can manifest as deceptively innocent appearing lesions. Other clinical findings
may include tissue fixation and induration, mobility of teeth, trismus, bone destruction and
pathological fracture, dependent on the localization of the neoplasm { 20400366 }. An unknown
proportion of OSCC arise in oral potentially malignant disorders, probably the majority, though
OSCC may arise in clinically normal mucosa { 33128420 }. A thorough visual and tactile oral cavity
examination remains the most effective method of screening. Several adjuncts to aid clinical
inspection have been developed, but all have less than ideal sensitivity and specificity for routine
clinical application { 26021841 ; 26946204 ; 29080605 ; 28958308 }.
Epidemiology
Worldwide, oral cancer (including the lips) is the 16th most common cancer, accounting for over
377,000 cases per annum, with males comprising 70% of cases { 33538338 }. However, these
overall figures obscure marked variation in incidence and mortality worldwide { 33967531 }. The
highest estimated incidence has been reported in Melanesia (Males ASR 22.2/per 100,000;
Females ASR 14.6 per 100,000) and South-Central Asia (Males ASR 13.3 per 100,000; Female
ASR 4.6 per 100,000). In India and Thailand, the ratio is reversed with a male:female ratios of 1:2
and 1:1.5 respectively { 24289546 }. On the other hand, reported incidence rates in Western Africa
are much lower (ASR of 2.0 per 100,000 or less for both genders. Trends in incidence are variable,
with marked reduction in lip cancers in the past decade, and whilst overall oral cavity cancer rates
are reducing in males, this is not the case in all populations, including India and the
UK. Furthermore, modest increases in incidence rates in females have been reported in many
populations { 24289546 }. Age standardised rates of mortality have remained constant over many
decades { 32067418 }. The highest cumulative risk of mortality has been reported in Melanesia
and South-Central Asian populations { 33538338 }. A worldwide increase in the incidence of oral
tongue cancer, particularly in individuals <45 years of age has been reported { 27696557 }.
Etiology
undefined
Pathogenesis
The majority OSCC arise in areas of pre-existing epithelial dysplasia or preceded by oral potentially
malignant disorders { 34050426 ; 30887394 } Most OSCC are genetically unstable and
characterized by significant chromosomal alterations and high somatic mutation burden.
Chromosomal losses at 3p, 8p, 9p, and 17p with gains at 3q, 5p, 8q and 11q are reproducibly
observed { 25631445 ; 26247464 }. Several large scale sequencing studies have defined the
mutational landscape for OSCC with somatic mutations being observed in a number of genes
including TP53, CDKN2A, FAT1, NOTCH1, KMT2D, CASP8, AJUBA, NSD1, HLA-A, TGFBR2,
USP9X,
MLL4,
HRAS,
UNC13C,
ARID2
and
TRPM3
{ 21798897 ; 21798893 ; 25631445 ; 24292195 ; 23619168 ; 28435450 }.
Macroscopic appearance
OSCC's are exophytic and/or endophytic, often ulcerated, firm, indurated tumours, with a tan or
white cut surface.
Histopathology
Most cancers in the oral cavity and mobile tongue are conventional keratinizing SCC. However,
other subtypes can rarely occur (See Table 1 #25606. Well-differentiated neoplasms contain large
nests, cords and islands of cells with pink cytoplasm, prominent intercellular bridging, with round,
often hyperchromatic, nuclei. Squamous pearls and dyskeratotic cells are also prominent. Higher
grade neoplasms may demonstrate marked nuclear and cellular pleomorphism, nuclear
hyperchromasia, mitotic figures (including atypical forms), and small islands or individuals cells can
be observed at the invasive front. Desmoplastic stroma with various degrees of inflammation can
be found around invading tumour cell nests and islands. Perineural and lymphovascular invasion

513

may occur, generally in poorly differentiated high-grade tumours. Adjacent mucosal epithelium may
show various grades of dysplasia. Grading alone does not correlate well with prognosis. However,
a number of specific features may have important biologic relevance (See Table 2 #25609).
Immunohistochemistry: In less-differentiated OSCC, immunohistochemical confirmation of
epithelial differentiation using keratins such as AE1/AE3, or squamous differentiation using CK5/6,
p63 and p40 may be needed { 24418859 }.
Cytology
Although confirmatory evaluation by histology of incisional or excisional biopsy of intraoral
carcinomas remains the gold standard, brush cytology may be helpful for screening and the
diagnosis of early OSCC { 28833675 ; 31752196 }. Fine needle aspiration biopsy is useful for
evaluating lymph node metastases.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Infiltrating malignant epithelial cells with squamous differentiation
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC)/American Joint
Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM classification, 8th Edition.
Prognosis and prediction
Conventional OSCC is aggressive with a propensity for local invasion and early lymph node
metastasis. The most significant prognostic factors are tumour size, depth of invasion, nodal status
and distant metastases. Stage at diagnosis and oral cancer mortality varies by site of cancer, with
low stage at diagnosis in lip cancers and highest mortality in tongue cancers { 30652378 }.
Conventional histologic grading corresponds poorly with clinical outcomes { 15644773 }.
Histological factors associated with a worse prognosis include a non-cohesive pattern of invasion,
tumour budding, perineural and lymphovascular invasion, and bone invasion (See Table
2 #25609). In addition, anatomical invasive depth (invading to intrinsic muscles) is an independent
predictive factor for delayed cervical lymph node metastasis after partial glossectomy for patients
with clinically node-negative tongue squamous cell carcinoma { 27186852 }. Narrow tumour bed
margins < 4 mm { 23271033 } and high grade dysplasia at the mucosal margins have been
reported to correlate with local recurrence { 11896817 }. Surgical clearance taken from margins of
the main resection specimen predicts local control better than sampling from the tumour bed
{ 26225798 ; 26225798 ; 31174238 ; 30912991 }. Positive lymph nodes, and particularly
extracapsular spread into the adjacent tissue { 32918710 }, and involvement of levels IV and V
correlate with adverse outcome.

Verrucous carcinoma
Definition
Verrucous carcinoma (VC) is a well-differentiated non-metastasizing squamous cell carcinoma
(SCC), that has a warty keratinised surface and specific architecture, lacks substantial cytologic
features of malignancy and is characterized by slow lateral spread and pushing invasion.
ICD-O coding
8051/3 Verrucous carcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B66.0 & XH5PM0 Squamous cell carcinoma of other or unspecified parts of mouth & Verrucous
carcinoma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: Ackerman tumour; verrucous hyperplasia
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Oral mucosa is the most common site for VC, accounting for 50-75% of all VC cases in the head
and neck { 11443616 } In the oral cavity, buccal mucosa, gingiva and tongue are most frequently
involved { 28850720 }.
Clinical features
VC presents as a slowly growing, and slightly exophytic white tumour. VC may erode bones and
can cause extensive destruction if left untreated.
Epidemiology
VC is rare, accounting for 2-16% of oral carcinomas { 20396919 }. It occurs predominantly in older
people, usually in the sixth decade or later, with a strong male predominance.
Etiology

520

VC has been etiologically related to the use of chewing tobacco or snuff. The habitual use of areca
nut/betel quid chew has been implicated in the high incidence of oral VC in India { 8002153 }. VC
is not associated with HPV infection { 22684225 ; 24071016 ; 24350715 }.
Pathogenesis
Little is known about pathogenesis of VC, but it’s molecular signature appears distinct from other
oral SCC { 26014678 }. Gene expression { 22690848 ; 22225903 ; 28621315 ; 25126189 } and
differentiation markers { 22374724 } differ from SCC of the same head and neck sites.
Macroscopic appearance
VC presents as a broad-based exophytic tumour with a warty, white or red surface, depending on
the amount of keratinization. On cut surface, it is firm, tan to white, and may show keratin-filled
clefts and a flat well-defined pushing interface with underlying tissue.
Histopathology
VC consists of a broad-based well differentiated exophytic and endophytic squamous epithelial
proliferation with marked surface keratinization and keratin plugging. VC invades the stroma evenly
with well-defined pushing borders, extending below the level of adjacent epithelium that may be
difficult to visualize on small biopsies. The thickened, bulbous rete often coalesce. A sharply
defined stroma-epithelial interface is generally seen often associated with a lymphoplasmacytic
inflammatory response { 30671763 }. VC lacks substantial cytologic features of malignancy.
Surface ulceration is uncommon and raises the concern for conventional SCC { 24071016 }.
Differential This includes conventional, and papillary SCCs and condyloma acuminatum. Epithelial
down growth extending deeper than the adjacent normal epithelium distinguishes VC from
dysplastic lesions with a verrucous morphology, but there are intermediate lesions that are difficult
to classify { 24223590 }. The term verrucous hyperplasia has been used for some of these lesions,
but such lesions lack clear diagnostic criteria { 24223590 } appear dysplastic rather than
hyperplastic and harbour copy number variation and carry a risk of malignant transformation
{ 29377391 }, suggesting they are part of the wider spectrum of dysplastic verrucous lesions
{ 33415517 }. Lack of substantial atypia distinguishes VC from conventional and papillary SCCs.
VC is characterized by a high frequency of initial misdiagnosis { 9570624 ; 21494762 }. A fullthickness biopsy with adjacent normal epithelium with sufficient stroma to evaluate for pattern of
invasion, and thorough sampling of the specimen is needed to make the diagnosis of VC and to
rule out co-existing SCC { 24223590 ; 25767335 ; 26225050 ; 27865210 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: broad-based well differentiated epithelial proliferation with marked papillary
parakeratosis and keratin plugging extending below the level of adjacent epithelium
Full-thickness biopsy to assess invasion; no to minimal cytologic atypia; pushing even border
without deep invasive growth
Desirable: examination of the whole tumour to exclude SCC
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC)/American Joint
Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM classification of the lip and oral cavity , 8th Edition.
Prognosis and prediction
VC has an excellent prognosis; the overall five-year survival rate is 77-86 %
{ 28850720 ; 11443616 ; 18620896 }. Surgery is treatment of choice; irradiation is less effective but
is
accepted
as
an
appropriate
treatment
for
some
VC
{ 7715385 ; 9231170 ; 11443616 ; 20493621 }. VC may be a precursor to SCC, and 20% of oral
cavity VC contain concomitant SCC { 6732584 }. Carcinomas with both patterns behave as
SCC. VC with focal dysplasia and/or minimal invasive SCC less than or equal to 2 mm in depth do
not adversely affect outcomes and should be treated as VC { 24947053 }.

521

#26021: Examination of the whole specimen showed verrucous carcinoma with a focus of
conventional squamous cell carcinoma.

#27572: A large, exophytic tumour with verrucous architecture blanketing the edentulous
mandibular alveolar ridge and extending to the involve the vestibule and buccal mucosa.

522

#28299: A broad-based well differentiated epithelial proliferation with marked papillary
parakeratosis and keratin plugging. VC invades the stroma evenly with well-defined pushing
borders, extending below the level of adjacent epithelium.

#28300: Bulbous coalescing rete and an expanded spinous layer are present. A
lichenoid immune response is often seen.

523

#28301: Lack of cytologic atypia with normal maturation is usually seen. Mitoses may be rarely
present in the basal/parabasal layer, but no atypical mitotic forms.

#27176: There is a broad pushing border of infiltration below the level of the adjacent epithelium
(taking the contour of the tongue into consideration). There are numerous papillary projections
with parakeratotic crypting and church-spire type keratosis. Pleomorphism is absent.

524

Carcinoma cuniculatum
Definition
Carcinoma cuniculatum (CC) is a rare well-differentiated locally destructive non-metastasizing
squamous cell carcinoma type characterized by a burrowing invasive pattern, keratin-containing
crypts and minimal cytological atypia.
ICD-O coding
8071/3 Squamous cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B6E.0 Squamous cell carcinoma of other or ill-defined sites in the lip, oral cavity or pharynx
Related terminology
Not recommended: epithelioma cuniculatum
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The gingivo-alveolar complex of the mandible is the commonest site, followed by that of the maxilla.
The tongue, buccal mucosa and lower lip are rarely affected { 30607347 }.
Clinical features
Pain, swelling, mucosal ulceration, tooth mobility and induration are common { 30607347 }. The
presence of sequestra or yellow keratinaceous discharge from oral or cutaneous fistulae may lead
to misdiagnosis as osteomyelitis or abscess { 21862267 ; 24035112 ; 25443811 }. Longstanding
symptoms in some individuals may reflect diagnostic delay and/or the slow-growing nature of this
tumour.
Imaging: On mucoperiosteal sites, CC presents radiologically as an osteolytic lesion with a poorlydefined moth-eaten outline { 30607347 }. Sequestration may be evident { 21862267 }.
Epidemiology
CC presents most commonly in the seventh and eight decades. There is no gender predilection.
Etiology
None
Pathogenesis
Pathogenic mechanisms are unknown and there is no association with HPV
{ 12424455 ; 19625845 ; 21643820 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Predominantly endophytic with a white to yellow-white appearance. Infiltrative keratin-filled crypts
may be seen.
Histopathology
CC is characterized by a primarily endophytic growth pattern of a burrowing labyrinthine network
of well-differentiated squamous epithelium forming interconnecting keratin-filled crypts
communicating with the surface. A minor exophytic component may be present. There is no more
than mild cytological atypia. Intraepithelial and stromal neutrophils are abundant, often with keratin
microbscesses. Microsequestra are frequently associated with bone invasion.
Differential Non-representative sampling may lead to misdiagnosis as cystic well-differentiated
squamous cell carcinoma, verrucous carcinoma, osteomyelitis, odontogenic keratocyst,
orthokeratinized odontogenic cyst or abscess { 30607347 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: correlation with imaging, well-differentiated heavily keratinizing squamous epithelium;
mild atypia; labyrinthine burrowing cohesive invasive pattern
Desirable: keratin microabscesses; stromal neutrophils; microsequestra; bone invasion on
mucoperiosteal sites
Staging

525

Staging according to Union for International Cancer control TNM classification of malignant tumours
8th edition.
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis is excellent following complete excision. Local recurrence is uncommon after correct
preoperative diagnosis and no metastases develop despite multiple interventions { 30607347 }.
There are no known predictive factors.

#26070: Macroscopic view of CC infiltrating bone between tooth roots. The white tumour
encloses necrotic bone. The apparently separate islands were contiguous in a different plane.

#26076: Characteristic infiltrative labyrinthine network of keratin-filled crypts.

#26078: Bone invasion with microsequestra .

#26081: No more than mild cytological atypia and keratin micorabscess formation .

#27577:
Panoramic radiograph showing a carcinoma cuniculatum in the posterior body of the
mandible producing a moth-eaten partial radiolucency with cortical preservation. The
appearance can resemble osteomyelitis, particularly when sequestra are detected
radiographically.

#26093: Interconnecting keratin-filled crypts invading bone with microsequestra formation. There
is no more than mild atypia.

#26187: The tumour invades bone as interconnecting keratin-filled crypts with microsequestra
formation. There is no more than mild cytological atypia. Note the lack of surface ulceration or
mass, carcinoma cuniculatum is primarily an endophytic lesion.
Tumours of uncertain histogenesis

Congenital granular cell epulis
Definition
Congenital granular cell epulis (CGCE) is a rare benign lesion occurring on the alveolar ridge of
the newborn, composed of sheets of cells with abundant granular cytoplasm.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
KC23 Neonatal disorders of the oral mucosa
Related terminology
Acceptable: congenital epulis, congenital epulis of the newborn, congenital gingival granular cell
tumor, Neumann tumor.
Not recommended: Congenital granular cell myoblastoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most lesions arise on the maxillary anterior alveolar process, with a maxillary:mandibular ratio of
2-3:1. Up to 20% are multifocal and rare extra-alveolar involvement has been reported
{ 17577543 ; 26712684 }, [[Karpal Singh Sohal, Jeremiah Robert Moshy, Sira Stanslaus
Owibingire, Ruhi Ameen Kashmiri. Congenital Granular Cell Epulis: A Systematic Review of Cases
from 2000-2017. Archives of Dentistry and Oral Health. 2018; 1(1): 56-65; 8355095]].
Clinical features
Patients present with pedunculated soft tissue mass with size ranging from <10mm to 200 mm,
although occasional tumours measuring 900 mm have been reported. Larger lesions may interfere
529

with feeding and, to a lesser extent, respiration. The mass may sometimes be detected on
ultrasonography from gestational week 26 { 26034711 }.
Epidemiology
Newborns are affected with an estimated incidence of 0.0006% { 20130770 } and a female:male
ratio of 7.7:1 [[Karpal Singh Sohal, Jeremiah Robert Moshy, Sira Stanslaus Owibingire, Ruhi
Ameen Kashmiri. Congenital Granular Cell Epulis: A Systematic Review of Cases from 2000-2017.
Archives of Dentistry and Oral Health. 2018; 1(1): 56-65]].
Etiology
The etiology is unknown. CGCT develops primarily during third trimester of pregnancy with no
relation to any risk factors such as premature delivery, maternal age, or pregnancy status [[Karpal
Singh Sohal, Jeremiah Robert Moshy, Sira Stanslaus Owibingire, Ruhi Ameen Kashmiri.
Congenital Granular Cell Epulis: A Systematic Review of Cases from 2000-2017. Archives of
Dentistry and Oral Health. 2018; 1(1): 56-65]].
Pathogenesis
The pathogenesis is unknown,but thought to result from a degenerative metabolic process affecting
mesenchymal stem cells { 6264813 ; 17577543 ; 19205730 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Firm soft tissue mass with a pedunculated base. The cut surface is typically tan-yellow,
homogeneous, and smooth { 18424116 ; 30888637 }.
Histopathology
A well-circumscribed lesion composed of sheets and nests of tightly packed polygonal cells with
abundant granular cytoplasm and well-delineated cell membranes. Nuclei are eccentric and dark
with inconspicuous nucleoli without mitosis. Thin-walled vessels are distributed throughout the
lesion. The overlying epithelium is atrophic without rete ridges. In addition, inflammation,
odontogenic epithelial rests, fibrosis and spindle cells may present.
Immunohistochemistry: The granular cells are positive for vimentin, NSE, laminin and PAS diastase
resistant, while negative for muscle markers and S100. Histiocytic markers are variably positive
{ 19205739 }. Rare cases exhibit S100 positivity { 8355095 ; 25783372 }.
Differential The entities include granular cell tumor, which shows S100 positivity and most
frequently occurs on the dorsal tongue { 8355095 ; 21393037 }.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: soft lump on maxillary or mandibular alveolar ridge; neonatal presentation;
sheets and nests of polygonal cells with eosinophilic, granular cytoplasm
Desirable: attenuated to atrophic stratified squamous epithelium of uniform thickness; negative
reaction for S100 protein.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
If progressive enlargement is not observed after birth, spontaneous regression may occur, and
such lesions should be followed up without excision. Conservative excision is indicated, if the
tumour interferes with feeding or respiration. Recurrence is not reported, even with incomplete
excision. { 8355095 ; 19205730 ; 25783372 }.

530

#25468
Congenital granular cell epulis
A newborn female with a sessile lobulated pink mass of the maxillary alveolar process
measuring several centimeters in diameter, the mass interferes with feeding.

#25469
Congenital granular cell epulis
A well-circumscribed lesion beneath an attenuated epithelium that lacks rete ridges. An
uninvolved zone of lamina propria separates the lesion from the epithelium. The lesion is
composed of tightly packed polygonal cells with abundant granular cytoplasm. Nuclei are
eccentric and dark staining with inconspicuous nucleoli and no evidence of mitotic activity.
Thin-walled vessels distributed throughout the lesion (inset).

531

#25519
Congenital granular cell epulis
Well-circumscribed lesion beneath an attenuated epithelium that lacks rete ridges. An
uninvolved zone of lamina propria separates the lesion from the epithelium. The lesion is
composed of tightly packed polygonal cells with abundant granular cytoplasm. Nuclei are
eccentric and dark staining with inconspicuous nucleoli and no evidence of mitotic activity.
Thin-walled vessels distributed throughout the lesion.

Granular cell tumour
Definition
Granular cell tumour (GCT) is a benign neuroectodermal tumour composed of large, round to oval
cells with abundant and distinctly eosinophilic granular cytoplasm.
ICD-O coding
9580/0 Granular cell tumour, NOS
9580/3 Granular cell tumour, malignant
ICD-11 coding
2E90.1 Benign neoplasm of tongue
Related terminology
Not recommended: Abrikossoff's tumor, granular cell myoblastoma, granular cell schwannoma.
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
GCT occurs in any subcutaneous or submucosal site, but of oral cases around 85% arise in the
tongue { 27918739 ; 31898368 }.
Clinical features
Patients with GCT present with a non-tender, slowly enlarging, sessile mucosal to yellowish
coloured submucosal mass, usually <10 mm but can reach up to 20 mm in diameter. Multifocal
tumours occur infrequently { 29661274 ; 32089415 ; 33253647 }.
Epidemiology
GCT can present at any age, but most occur in the 4th-6th decades with a female:male ratio of 3:1
{ 31898368 ; 32189915 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Sporadic oral GCT do not contain the PTPN11 and PTEN mutations { 29097601 } associated with
granular cell tumors in LEOPARD and Noonan syndrome { 19054014 ; 20537083 ; 22915371 } and
PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome { 12833416 }. Whole-exome sequencing has revealed either
mutations in the genes ATP6AP1, ATP6AP2 and ATP6V1A, components of the vaculolar APTase
(V-ATPase)
complex
{ 30166553 ; 33289181 }
or
mutations
in
genes
for

532

endosomal/lysosomal/autophagosomal networks { 33289181 }. Overexpression of transcription
factor E3 (TFE3) is observed in skin lesions, but not in oral tumours { 31788112 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Pale tan to yellowish white homogeneous submucosal nodule on cut surface
{ 26637199 ; 32189915 }.
Histopathology
GCT is an unencapsulated tumour composed of large polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic
granular cytoplasm and small, round nuclei in the subepithelial tissue. GCTs can grow in a syncytial
pattern or arranged in sheets surrounding and replacing muscle fascicles or nerves. Occasionally,
GCTs may be granular cell poor with extensive fibrosis. Pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia (PEH)
of the overlying mucosa is observed in 25-50% of cases { 19192059 ; 33085297 } raising a risk of
erroneous diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) in superficial biopsies. Criteria for
malignancy in GCT include at least 3 of the following: nuclear pleomorphism, cell spindling, mitotic
activity >2/2mm2, prominent nucleoli, high nuclear/cytoplasmic ratio, and necrosis. Cases with 1-2
criteria are classified as atypical { 669341 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The tumour cells are immunoreactive for S100 protein, CD57, SOX10,
inhibin, and calretinin. CD68 highlights the cytoplasmic granules composed of lysosomes
{ 26162589 ; 26637199 ; 29329562 }. A rare non-neural GCT subtype is S100 protein negative,
shows cell spindling, mitotic activity, rich vascularity, and absence of PEH. Both subtypes are
NKI/C3 positive { 27709427 }.
Differential GCT is relatively characteristic but differential diagnosis of other granular cell lesions
is aided by immunohistochemistry { 26637199 ; 32089415 }.
Cytology
Smears are usually cellular with sheets of large cells with usually central, round to mildly
pleomorphic nuclei and abundant granular cytoplasm and a small number of single cells often
resembling histiocytes in a granular background.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: granular cells
Desirable: PEH, S100+
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence
is
rare
following
excision
even
when
incompletely
excised
{ 31898368 ; 19192059 ; 33085297 ; 29661274 }. Malignant GCT is associated with metastases
and poor survival { 24669168 }.

#27727
Granular cell tumour

S100 protein immunostaining
positively stains the granular
cells.

533

#27793
Granular cell tumour
Sheets of large polygonal cells intermingled with skeletal muscle fibers. Cells
demonstrate abundant granular cytoplasm and small, round nuclei either eccentrically or
centrally located.

#27794
Granular cell tumour
Dramatic pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia of the overlying mucosa should not be
erroneously interpreted as squamous cell carcinoma and the unencapsulated mass of
granular cells immediately beneath the epithelium should not be overlooked.

534

#27811
Granular cell tumour
Sheets of large polygonal cells intermingled with skeletal muscle fibers. Cells
demonstrate abundant granular cytoplasm and small, round nuclei either eccentrically or
centrally located.

#27812
Granular cell tumour
The granular cells are immunoreactive with S100 protein.

535

#25536
Granular cell tumor
Dramatic pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia of the overlying mucosa should not be
erroneously interpreted as squamous cell carcinoma and the unencapsulated mass of
granular cells immediately beneath the epithelium should not be overlooked.

Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour
Definition
Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour (EMCMT) is a mesenchymal neoplasm characterized
by spindle-polygonal cells within myxoid-collagenous stroma and usually harbouring
a RREB1::MRTFB gene fusion.
ICD-O coding
8982/0 Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Not recommended: reticulated myxoid tumour
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
EMCMT occurs in the tongue, particularly in the anterior dorsal aspect
{ 7726361 ; 25404177 ; 27010880 ; 29912715 }. Less common locations include palate
{ 22769404 ; 29491357 } and mandible { 32372271 }.
Clinical features
Patients with EMCMT have a slow growing painless mass, with a duration ranging from months to
years { 7726361 }.
Epidemiology
There are fewer than 100 reported cases in the English literature. EMCMT occur over a broad age
range, but most commonly in young adults. There is no sex predilection.
{ 7726361 ; 25404177 ; 27010880 ; 29912715 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis

536

Approximately 90% of tumours are defined by fusion of RREB1 to MRTFB (formerly MKL2). This
fusion product has molecular pleiotropy and is not disease specific as it has been reported in other
tumours, including biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma { 33715240 }, mediastinal mesenchymal
neoplasms { 31991003 }, and oropharyngeal sarcoma { 29266774 }. A minority may have alternate
molecular drivers, including EWSR1 { 27010880 ; 29912715 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are generally circumscribed, lobulated, and range from 5 - 30 mm in diameter
{ 7726361 ; 29912715 }. On cut section they may be grey, tan and/or yellow { 7726361 }.
Histopathology
EMCMTs have a broad morphological spectrum. They are frequently multilobulated and comprised
of cells arranged in sheets, fascicles, and/or reticular patterns. Less commonly they may
be microcystic-cystic, or papillary { 7726361 ; 29912715 }.The margins are well demarcated but
usually unencapsulated with incorporation of surrounding muscle fibres at the periphery
{ 7726361 ; 25404177 ; 29912715 }. The cells range from spindle-stellate to polygonal, with
variable eosinophilic cytoplasm with round to ovoid, and monomorphic nuclei. Occasional atypia,
binucleation, and pseudonuclear inclusions may be identified. Mitotic activity is usually
inconspicuous. The intervening stroma may be myxoid to collagenous, and focally hyaline
{ 7726361 ; 25404177 ; 29912715 }.
Immunohistochemistry: IHC reveals polyphenotypic differentiation. Most tumours have diffuse
expression of glial fibrillary acidic protein. Other stains such as S100, desmin, smooth muscle
actin, myogenin, CD56, CD57, keratin, and epithelial membrane antigen are typically patchy and
weak { 7726361 ; 29912715 }.
Cytology
Clinically not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The presence of a RREB1::MRTFB gene fusion, in the appropriate context, may be diagnostically
helpful.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: sheets, cords and/or reticular arrangement of spindle-stellate to polygonal cells; variable
stroma that may be myxoid, hyaline or collagenous
Desirable: consistent immunohistochemistry
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Most tumours have an indolent clinical course, with occasional local recurrence
{ 7726361 ; 19716722 }. Rarely tumours may be locally aggressive { 32372271 }.

#24997
Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour
Spindle cells arranged in loose fascicles and whorls, with myxohyaline stroma.

537

#24998
ctomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour
Stellate cells with a reticular pattern and abundant myxoid stroma.

#24999
Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour
Locally aggressive features, such as bone involvement, are uncommon.

#25000
Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour
Most tumours express glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP).

#26089
Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour
Hyaline cartilage matrix is paradoxically rare and, when present, typically only focal.

#25015
Ectomesenchymal chondromyxoid tumour.
Multilobulated tongue mass. Spindle to stellate cells with a vague fascicular to whorled
architecture. The nuclei are ovoid and monomorphic with scattered atypia; mitotic activity
is not conspicuous. There Interspersed between the cells there is variable myxohyaline
stroma. a Anterior tongue massRNA sequencing confirmed the presence of a RREB1MRTFB gene fusion.
539

Melanotic neuroectodermal tumour of infancy
Definition
Melanotic neuroectodermal tumour of infancy (MNTI) is a biphasic tumour of small neuroblast-like
cells and larger melanin-producing epithelial cells.
ICD-O coding
9363/0 Melanotic neuroectodermal tumour of infancy
ICD-11 coding
2D42 & XH6C72 Malignant neoplasms of ill-defined sites & Melanotic neuroectodermal tumour
Related terminology
Not recommended: melanotic progonoma; retinal anlage tumour.
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
More than 90% of MNTI occur in the craniofacial bones, most commonly affecting maxilla (>60%),
followed by skull (~15%), and mandible (~8%) { 15290671 ; 25936939 ; 29275074 ; 31295620 }.
Rarely,
tumours
develop
in
the
genital
tract,
trunk,
and extremities
{ 14648795 ; 25936939 ; 28812465 }.
Clinical features
Typically, patients present with a sessile, painless, rapidly enlarging mass in the upper alveolus,
causing facial deformity and feeding disruption. The mass is usually bluish-black due to its melanin
content. Some tumours produce circulating vanillylmandelic acid (34%) and alpha-fetoprotein
{ 25936939 ; 29275074 ; 30170777 }.
Imaging
Imaging shows destruction of cortical bone with entrapment of developing tooth buds, extension
into the sinus, nasal cavity or orbit { 20080455 }.
Epidemiology
This rare tumour usually affects infants between 3 and 6 months of age, although it has been
diagnosed in utero, at birth, in older children, and adults. There is a slight male predilection
{ 15290671 ; 25936939 ; 29275074 ; 30170777 ; 31295620 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
MNTI is thought to be of neural crest cell origin and pathogenesis is unknown { 30170777 }. MNTI
shows morphologic similarity to melanotic medulloblastoma and more recently, has been shown to
share DNA methylation profile with high grade medulloblastoma (MB G3) despite its low grade
behaviour { 31953575 ; 33572349 }. Germline mutations in CDKN2A and BRAF p.V600E mutation
are also reported { 26122804 ; 27519597 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are pigmented and firm, unencapsulated and lobulated without necrosis or ulceration.
The mean size is 30 mm but may be up to 200 mm, with larger tumours in the skull
{ 25936939 ; 28291424 ; 30170777 ; 31295620 }.
Histopathology
Tumour cells are arranged in alveolar nests, cords, and trabeculae, infiltrating into dense
vascularized, fibrocollagenous stroma. Melanotic epithelial cells typically surround small
neuroblast-like cells, and may form tubuloglandular structures { 15290671 }. The small cells may
rarely produce a neurofibrillary matrix { 29275074 }. The tumour frequently destructively
infiltrates bone and contains entrapped odontogenic tissue. Mitoses and necrosis are generally
absent.
Immunohistochemistry
Seldom necessary for diagnosis. Both small and large tumour cells express vimentin and
synaptophysin, but are typically negative for chromogranin, neurofilaments, S100 protein, and
desmin. The large cells co-express pancytokeratin, HMB45 and Melan-A, confirming dual epithelial
and melanocytic features, but are negative for other melanoma markers. Rarely, membranous

540

expression of CD99, focal rhabdomyoblastic, and glial differentiation may be seen
{ 15290671 ; 17378694 ; 1847607 ; 29275074 }.
Differential This includes other malignant “small round blue” cell tumours, usually with worse
prognoses, such as Ewing sarcoma, rhadomyosarcoma and lymphoma. However, these
alternatives lack the characteristic biphasic melanin-producing keratin-positive epithelial cells and
the synaptophysin-positive neuroblast-like cells.
Cytology
Smears are cellular with discrete, biphasic populations of tumour cells comprising monomorphic
small cells with scant cytoplasm and round nuclei with fine chromatin, and large epithelioid cells
with vesicular nuclei and intracytoplasmic melanin pigment { 14648795 ; 26173836 ; 29027726 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:Biphasic small neuroblast-like cells and large, melanotic epithelial cells arranged in
lobules separated by fibrocollagenous stroma
Desirable:Age less than one year, jaw location, bone destruction, epithelial cells positive for keratin
and HMB45
Staging
Not clinically relevant
Prognosis and prediction
MNTI is a rapidly growing, locally destructive, paradoxically low grade, tumour of uncertain
malignant potential. Approximately 20-30% of tumours recur, usually within 6 months
{ 15290671 ; 29275074 ; 30170777 ; 30390348 ; 30871849 }, especially in patients younger than
5 months of age at diagnosis { 25936939 ; 30170777 }. Approximately 2% of MNTI behave in a
malignant fashion with metastases of the neuroblast-like cells, and results in death { 30170777 }.
Predictive factors are not well defined { 29275074 } but larger tumours, usually affecting the skull,
have a worse prognosis { 28291424 ; 31295620 }.

Melanotic neuroectodermal tumour of infancy

Aggregates of tumour cells separated by fibrous stroma. There are small, blue cells with
larger epithelial cells at the periphery, some of which contain melanin pigment.

7. Oropharynx (base of tongue, tonsils, adenoids)
Introduction
Benign oropharyngeal lesions
Hamartomatous polyps
Epithelial tumours
Squamous cell carcinoma
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-independent

Introduction
The oropharynx, an anatomic site rich in lymphoid tissue and harbouring the anatomically unique
reticulated tonsillar crypt epithelium, is one of the major head and neck sites of viral-related
carcinogenesis. The vast majority of oropharyngeal malignancies are squamous cell carcinoma
(SCC) and almost all SCC subtypes occur here { 27035614 }. It is now well established that a high
fraction of all SCC, particularly in the United States, Canada, and parts of Northern Europe (but
also to a lesser extent in most of the rest of the world) are driven by transcriptionally-active high
risk human papillomavirus (HPV) { 26823521 }. There is uniform agreement on the prognostic
benefit of positive HPV status in these patients and entirely specific ICCR, UICC, and AJCC staging
systems have been developed for them { 28128848 }. These distinctions render the pathologist and
p16/HPV testing critical in routine practice. To acknowledge this unique and extremely important
tumour type, while drawing a clear distinction from SCC of the oral cavity, larynx, nasopharynx, and
sinonasal tract, the 4th edition of the World Health Organization Tumour Classification of Head and
Neck Tumours book created a separate chapter for oropharynx, retained in the current edition
(topographically including tonsils, base of tongue, soft palate, uvula, and posterior wall).
Several topics are not duplicated in this chapter, even though they develop in the oropharynx with
some regularity. Squamous papilloma (discussed in larynx [see section 3.0.1.1] and oral cavity [see
section 5.1.1.1]), pleomorphic adenoma and other salivary gland tumours (discussed in salivary
gland [see section 4.0.2.5]), and extranodal lymphomas and haematopoietic tumours that develop
in Waldeyer’s ring (discussed in section 9). Neuroendocrine neoplasms (especially carcinomas),
many HPV-associated, are discussed in the respective neuroendocrine neoplasms sections (see
section 12). Finally, a new section is included on tonsillar hamartomas, a rare cause of tonsillar
tumour, and presented as part of the differential diagnosis.
Benign oropharyngeal lesions

Hamartomatous polyps
Definition
Lesions associated with the palatine tonsils composed of a haphazard proliferation of elements that
are normally found in the oropharynx.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DB35.3 Hamartomatous polyp
Related terminology
Acceptable: Lymphangiectatic fibrous polyp; Fibrovascular polyp; Polypoid lymphangioma;
Papillary lymphoid polyp; Benign hamartomatous polyp; Lymphangiectatic fibrolipomatous polyp
Not recommended: Tonsillar lipoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Palatine tonsil
Clinical features

544

Patients with hamartomatous polyp may complain of dysphagia, sore throat, globus, or obstructive
symptoms. Lesions may be present for weeks to years. The entity appears as a unilateral polypoid
mass and may be clinically worrisome for a neoplasm { 11048808 ; 29163996 ; 31304780 }.
Epidemiology
Hamartomas are rare, accounting for 2% or less of all tonsillar tumours. Lymphangiomatous polyps
comprise the overwhelming majority of lesions. Even lesions with predominance of lymphoid tissue
(lymphoid polyp) and those with prominent adipose tissue (fibrolipomatous polyp) are thought to
potentially belong to the overall spectrum of lymphangiomatous polyps. The age range of those
affected is broad, ages 3 to 63 years (median 25.5 years) with equal sex predilection
{ 11048808 ; 29392063 ; 21562903 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Polypoid, often pedunculated, mucosal surfaced mass. The size is variable, ranging from very small
to up to 80 mm { 11048808 ; 21562903 }.
Histopathology
The lymphangiomatous polyp has numerous small to medium-sized dilated vascular channels
containing proteinaceous fluid with lymphocytes. The overlying mucosa is generally intact,
displaying respiratory or squamous epithelium without dysplasia. Small collections of intraepithelial
lymphocytes can be seen. The stroma is variable and may consists of fibrous and lymphoid tissue.
Adipose tissue and muscle may also be seen in lesions termed fibrolipomatous polyp
{ 11048808 }.
Cytology
Not applicable
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not applicable
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: polypoid tumour of the palatine tonsils; submucosal proliferation of lymph-vascular
channels, containing proteinaceous fluid with lymphocytes, in a background of lymphoid or
connective tissue
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence is rare after surgical treatment. However, without treatment, the airway may become
critically obstructed { 11048808 }.

#25555
Lymphangiomatous Polyp-Hamartoma
Polypoid tumour with fibrous stroma and dilated vasculature intermixed with normal
lymphoid stroma.

Epithelial tumours
Squamous cell carcinoma

Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated
Definition
HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (OPSCC) is caused by transcriptionallyactive high-risk HPV and usually arises from the tonsillar crypts.
ICD-O coding
8085/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated
ICD-11 coding
2B6A.0 Squamous cell carcinoma of oropharynx & XH0EJ7 Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV
positive
Related terminology
Acceptable: HPV-positive SCC; HPV-related SCC; p16-positive SCC
Subtype(s)
Non-keratinizing;
keratinizing;
papillary;
adenosquamous;
ciliated;
lymphoepithelial;
sarcomatoid/spindle cell; basaloid
Localization
HPV-associated OPSCC arises from the tonsillar-bearing regions of the oropharynx (i.e. lingual
and palatine tonsils) where it takes origin from the specialized squamous epithelium lining the
tonsillar crypts { 28877058 }.
Clinical features
Patients may present with clinical signs related to local tumour effects, but most present with small
primary tumours that have metastasized to upper and mid-jugular chain lymph nodes (levels II and
III). Painless cervical lymphadenopathy is the most common presentation { 24652023 }. HPVassociated OPSCC imaging differs from HPV-independent in that primary tumours are more
commonly enhancing and exophytic with well-defined margins. They also more commonly present
with multiple positive nodes and the metastases are more commonly cystic (see
Table #26602) { 18383529 ; 23660291 }.
Epidemiology
The global incidence of HPV-associated OPSCC has risen over the past four decades, particularly
in North America and Northern Europe. Patients with HPV-associated OPSCC are typically male
(male-to-female ratio of 4:1), white, and of higher socioeconomic status. Over time, the prevalence

548

of HPV in OPSCC has been increasing among older adults causing an upward shift in the median
patient age to 55-58 years and closing the age gap with HPV-independent
OPSCC { 29710071 ; 29710393 }.
Etiology
HPV-associated OPSCC is caused by high-risk HPV with type 16 responsible for aproximately 90%
of all cases { 33232848 }. Sexual behavior is an established risk factor, with lifetime number of oral
sex partners as the most strongly associated factor { 17494927 ; 20022926 ; 26351338 }.
Individuals with HPV-associated OPSCC are less likely than patients with HPV-independent
OPSCC to be smokers (see Table #26602). However, a history of tobacco smoking is present in
60 to 75% of patients, and about 25% of patients are active smokers at presentation. Tobacco
smoking is associated with significantly higher oral HPV prevalence and thus may play a role in the
progression from oral HPV infection to OPSCC { 25291584 }.
Pathogenesis
HPV oncoproteins E6 and E7 inactivate the p53 and Rb by targeting them for protein degradation.
The role of other viral oncoproteins such as E5 is less clear, but they may play a role in escaping
immunosurveillance { 29854832 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The appearance of primary tumours is highly variable, ranging from small and imperceptible to
occasionally large and bulky. Lymph node metastases are often large and cystic.
Histopathology
The histologic progression through the sequential stages of surface dysplasia culminating in
carcinoma in situ and invasive growth that characterize HPV-independent SCC is not evident for
HPV-associated OPSCC. Instead, tumours originate from the tonsillar crypts and infiltrate beneath
the surface epithelium, growing as nests and lobules often with central necrosis. Invasive growth
may not elicit a desmoplastic stromal reaction, and since the reticulated tonsillar crypt epithelium
is a poor barrier to spread, SCC adjacent to organized lymphoid tissue can metastasize despite
appearing histologically only “in situ”. The tumour nests tend to be surrounded by a lymphoid
stroma that may permeate the tumour lobules. In the typical tumour, the tumour cells display high
nuclear to cytoplasmic ratios, oval to spindled nuclei, syncytial cytoplasm (indistinct cell borders)
without intercellular bridges, and usually lack significant cytoplasmic keratinization. These cellular
features are termed nonkeratinizing SCC. Histologic grading has been shown to lack prognostic
utility and is not advocated. Some tumours may show nuclear anaplasia or
multinucleation { 22743286 ; 31552619 }.
The morphologic spectrum of HPV-associated OPSCC also includes subtypes with papillary
{ 23797720 },
adenosquamous
{ 21305368 },
ciliated
{ 26457358 },
lymphoepithelial
(undifferentiated) { 20421782 }, sarcomatoid/ spindle cell { 24418859 }, and basaloid features
{ 18496144 }. The clinical behaviour of these subtypes mirrors that of HPV-associated OPSCC with
typical morphology. HPV-associated high grade neuroendocrine carcinomas (i.e. small and large
cell neuroendocrine carcinoma) can arise in the oropharynx, sometimes in association with an
HPV-associated SCC { 26735857 ; 29556964 ; 31093350 }. Despite the presence of HPV,
neuroendocrine carcinomas have aggressive clinical behavior necessitating distinction from HPVassociated OPSCC (see section 12.1.2). Recognition of a high grade neuroendocrine component
is facilitated by immunohistochemistry showing absent or diminished expression of the squamous
markers p63 and p40 and positive expression of neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin,
chromogranin, INSM1).
Cytology
Aspirates of metastatic lesions are seen as cohesive clusters and loose aggregates of cells with
scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei without prominent nucleoli. A cystic component
of inflammatory cells and keratinized debris is common. Due to cystic degeneration and possibly
fixation effects in cytology preparations. p16 immunostaining of cell blocks is not always reliable for
determining HPV status of the tumour. { 26764038 ; 30846180 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Diffuse (nuclear and cytoplasmic) immunohistochemical staining for p16 that is of moderate to
strong intensity is a highly reliable surrogate marker for high-risk HPV,and is widely advocated as
a standalone test for determining HPV status of primary and metastatic SCCs of oropharyngeal
origin { 29251996 }. In some scenarios, direct HPV detection assays such as RNA/DNA in
situ hybridization and/or PCR may be appropriate, as when required by clinical trials, in low overall
HPV-attributable fraction geographic regions, when p16 immunostaining is equivocal, or when

549

there is a discrepancy between p16 staining and morphology (e.g. p16 positive keratinizing SCC)
{ 22180304 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: SCC (usually nonkeratinizing); must be positive for high-risk HPV as determined by
surrogate marker p16 immunohistochemistry alone (70% nuclear and cytoplasmic cutoff) or by p16
immunohistochemistry plus HPV-specific testing
Desirable: p40/p63 (positive) and neuroendocrine marker (negative) immunohistochemistry (in
selected cases)
Staging
The Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) or AJCC staging systems may be used
{ 30500294 }. OPSCC staging is predicated upon p16/HPV status.
Prognosis and prediction
HPV-associated OPSCC patients have significantly better survival outcomes than those with HPVindependent OPSCC. (see #26602Table #26602) Approximately 20% of patients suffer disease
recurrence after treatment with 3 and 5 year overall survival rates of 86% and 80%, respectively
{ 26936027 }. The favorable prognosis may be tempered by the adverse effects of active cigarette
smoking { 18270337 ; 20530316 }.

Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated, ciliated adenosquamous subtype
Rare HPV-associated carcinomas can have glands with cilia, seen here with rounded,
smooth lumens and cells with terminal bar formation and prominent cilia.

554

#28027
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated, with nuclear anaplasia
Although not of clear prognostic significance, some HPV-associated squamous cell
carcinomas with show areas of tumour cell multinucleation and extreme nuclear
pleomorphism.

#28029
HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma, nonkeratinizing subtype with maturation
Some HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma have both nonkeratinizing and
keratinizing areas, which frequently show an unusual pattern of "reverse maturation",
where the keratinising areas are peripheral with artifactual clefting.

555

#28039
HPV-associated SCC, nonkeratinizing subtype
This nonkeratinizing HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma has large nests with
smooth edges in a lymphoid stroma and with tumour cells with high N:C ratios and round
to oval nuclei.

#28095
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated
Global IARC map showing the age standardized rates (ASR) for incidence of
oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma worldwide.

556

#28146
HPV-associated SCC, papillary subtype
This tumour shows prominent papillary surface growth with papillae lined by
nonkeratinizing epithelium.

#26625
