#28028
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated. p16 diffusely positive
The tumour shows strong, diffuse nuclear and cytoplasmic staining for p16 by
immunohistochemistry.

#28069
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated. nonkeratinizing subtype
Typical HPV-associated SCC showing a submucosal tumour with nonkeratinizing
morphology, large nests of cells exhibiting high N:C ratio, and little to no maturing
squamous differentiation.

Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-independent
Definition
HPV-independent oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (OPSCC) is a type of OPSCC that
lacks transcriptionally-active HPV.
ICD-O coding
8086/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-independent
ICD-11 coding
2B6A.0 & XA4J67 & XH4CR9 Squamous cell carcinoma of oropharynx & Squamous cell
carcinoma, keratinising, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: HPV-negative or p16-negative squamous cell carcinoma
Subtype(s)
559

Keratinizing; verrucous; basaloid, papillary; spindle cell/sarcomatoid; adenosquamous;
lymphoepithelial
Localization
HPV-independent OPSCC is more common in tonsillar than non-tonsillar sites but represents a
much greater fraction of non-tonsillar OPSCC because HPV-associated tumours predominate in
tonsillar tissue { 23342257 ; 28877058 ; 29161981 }. Most non-tonsillar tumours are located in the
soft palate.
Clinical features
Patients typically present with pain/sore throat, dysphagia and/or a neck mass
{ 28304083 ; 29161981 }.
Epidemiology
Patients are slightly older and less likely to be male and white compared to HPV-positive
OPSCC { 28304083 }. Recent data indicates that the age differences between HPV-independent
and HPV-associated OPSCC may be decreasing { 29710393 ; 31026209 }.
Etiology
HPV-independent OPSCC is caused by tobacco and alcohol use, which have a synergistic
effect. See also discussion in section 5.1.3 (oral cavity).
Pathogenesis
Accumulation of DNA damage in surface mucosa from exposure to carcinogens, mainly from
tobacco and alcohol use. TP53 is commonly mutated (see sections 3 and 5). Hypoxia signatures
may be increased compared to HPV-independent SCC at other head and neck sites { 33725617 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Primary tumours are larger (higher T-stage) and nodal disease burden is lower than for HPVpositive OPSCC { 28304083 }.
Histopathology
Most tumours exhibit keratinizing morphology { 16504868 ; 20596971 }. As such, tumour nests are
infiltrative and angulated, and often surrounded by prominent stromal desmoplasia. The cells are
polygonal with distinct cell borders. Squamous maturation including keratinization and intercellular
bridges are usually present. Other squamous cell carcinoma subtypes are rare (see section 3 for
description). Tumours are traditionally graded as well, moderately or poorly-differentiated based on
their resemblance to normal squamous epithelium. Well-differentiated OPSCC may be difficult to
distinguish from benign mucosa, whereas poorly-differentiated OPSCC may require
immunohistochemistry (p40/p63 and cytokeratin 5/6) to confirm squamous epithelial origin
{ 24418859 }.
Cytology
Smears of neck metastases show keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma with polygonal to spindle
to tadpole cells showing dense sky blue (Giemsa stain) to dark green to orangophilic (Pap stain)
cytoplasm. Considerable keratinized debris and necrosis is usually present with a granulomatous
inflammatory reaction in some cases Occasional cases have features that overlap with
nonkeratinizing squamous cell carcinoma.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Transcriptionally-active HPV is absent. p16 is typically not overexpressed but can be positive in a
small subset { 29438466 ; 33450095 ; 33739785 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: squamous cell carcinoma (usually keratinizing); HPV status must be negative (p16
negativity, using 70% nuclear and cytoplasmic cutoff, is an acceptable surrogate)
Staging
UICC T-staging is similar for HPV-independent and HPV-associated OPSCC except that T4 is
subdivided into T4a and T4b for HPV-independent OPSCC only. HPV-independent OPSCC nodal
staging is the same as for oral cavity and larynx/hypopharynx cancers (see sections 3 and 5).
Prognosis and prediction
The 3-year overall and progression-free survival rates are 55-60% and 40-45%, respectively
{ 20530316 }. The number of positive lymph nodes and presence of extranodal extension may be
prognostic { 33508706 ; 33595897 }.

560

#25128
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-independent, oropharyngeal
Carcinomas usually arise from surface mucosa but may extend into tonsillar lymphoid
tissue.

#25129
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-independent, keratinizing subtype
The tumour shows irregular, angulated nests of cells with dense, eosinophilic cytoplasm
with rare keratin pearls and associated desmoplastic stromal reaction.

561

#28032
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-independent, keratinizing subtype, p16 negative
Immunohistochemistry for p16 shows the tumour cells have only patchy, moderate
intensity reactivity in much less than 70% of tumour cells, a negative result (as a
surrogate of high risk HPV).

L#25123
Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated, keratinising subtype
Tumour originating from surface mucosa that is composed of nests of polygonal cells with
distinct cell borders and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm.
R#25127
Spindle cell carcinoma
Atypical spindle cells with a granulation tissue-like appearance undermine squamous
mucosa.

Introduction
The principles of the classification of individual odontogenic cysts, odontogenic tumours and
tumours of the jaws are unchanged from previous editions but there has been a major
reorganisation. The classification follows the new WHO structure in which more aggressive lesions
appear at the end of each section. New definitions of what should constitute an entity and subtype
have required several changes and the bone related lesions have been more logically divided into
fibro-osseous tumours and dysplasias, benign and malignant neoplasms.
The relatively short time since the last classification results in only one completely novel entity being
included. However, several other conditions that have been established in the literature for long
periods are new to the classification. The rationale for adding these conditions varies. Some, as in
the cysts, are for completeness of classification. Others are included to prevent misdiagnosis as
conditions with different treatment or clinical implications. For some, the intention has been to
propose consensus definitions and clear diagnostic criteria because the descriptions in the
literature are heterogeneous. Though consensus has been achieved on these diagnostic criteria
they must be regarded as proposals to be tested and may need to be adapted in future
classifications. Differentiating these poorly defined lesions will be critical in selecting them for
molecular analysis and interpreting the data.
Since the last edition, there has been a marked increase in the availability of molecular data.
Although these data have clarified the relationships between different lesions it is not yet detailed
enough to dictate changes to the classification. Only one lesion in this section is defined by its
molecular change, the rhabdomyosarcoma with TFCP2 rearrangement { 29758589 }.
Characteristic molecular changes are present in several bone and odontogenic tumours but are
not defining criteria.
The common hotspot mutations found in other human neoplasms do not seem to be present in the
odontogenic cysts and tumours, reflecting the fact that many are hamartomas, benign or low grade
malignant
neoplasms.
However,
some
common
themes
are
emerging
{ 28797453 ; 32035859 }. Dysregulated MAPK signalling, caused by BRAF p.V600E mutation is a
likely driver for ameloblastoma and is found in conventional, unicystic and peripheral subtypes and
many ameloblastic carcinomas. BRAF mutations seem common to other epithelial and mixed
odontogenic tumours that have ameloblastic differentiation. They can be found in both the epithelial
and mesenchymal component of ameloblastic fibroma and lesions previously classified as
ameloblastic fibrodentinoma and fibro-odontoma and in the mesenchymal component of some
odontogenic sarcomas. PTCH1 and Shh pathway alterations are associated with odontogenic
keratocyst and ameloblastoma. Wnt pathway signalling is implicated in odontoma formation and
mutations in the CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) gene are associated with ghost cell differentiation in cysts
and benign and malignant neoplasms. FOS rearrangement and c-FOS overexpression in
cementoblastoma confirm its relationship with osteoblastoma. MAPK/ERK pathway activation may
drive odontogenic myxoma. However, the role in pathogenesis in each of these changes remains
to be elucidated. Though such changes are often associated with neoplasia or malignancy in other
tissues, most of these pathways are key to odontogenesis { 9392510 ; 26589925 } and may act in
a context specific manner.
Oncogene and tumour suppressor gene alterations continue to raise the possibility that some
odontogenic cysts and hamartomas might be neoplasms. In the absence of a molecular definition
564

of neoplasia and in recognition of this situation in other benign conditions { 27059373 }, the
classification has been maintained based on progressive growth pattern and metastasis. As an
example, the status of odontogenic keratocyst remains contentious but is molecularly similar to
other conditions with alterations in oncogenes or tumour suppressor genes that promote growth
but fail to cause a neoplastic phenotype, such as fibrous dysplasia caused by GNAS1 mutation,
simple bone cyst with NFATC2 fusion and adenomatoid odontogenic tumour with KRAS mutation.
Odontogenic tumours
It has not proved possible to clarify the complex overlap between lesions variously termed amyloidrich subtype of odontogenic fibroma, non-calcifying variant of calcifying epithelial odontogenic
tumour, and their Langerhans cell-rich variants. Though some features favour classification as
odontogenic fibroma, both potential parent entities lack defined molecular markers to allow a
definitive classification to be made.
The new classification principles have required the addition of the descriptor ‘conventional’ to
ameloblastoma because this is not a grouping of subtypes and must be distinguished from other
ameloblastoma entities. The term ‘solid/multicystic’ previously performed this function but was
removed in the 2017 classification and is now replaced by ‘conventional’ to follow WHO practice in
other 5th edition classifications.
The adenoid ameloblastoma { 26297394 } has been accepted as an entity in the category of benign
odontogenic tumours. It has been recognised for several decades and has a distinctive appearance
but there is variation in the diagnostic criteria in the literature and lack of clarity about its behaviour.
This lesion may lack the BRAF mutations found in other ameloblastomas, though data is limited
and preliminary, and the nuclear beta-catenin immunostaining is consistent with its ghost cell
differentiation and may suggest a relationship with the ghost cell tumour group. A small number of
cases with a high proliferative fraction and aggressive behaviour are difficult to distinguish from
ghost cell odontogenic carcinoma and ameloblastic carcinoma with ghost cells. Further reporting
and molecular analysis of this and similar entities is encouraged to clarify their relationships and
classification.
The reclassification of ameloblastic fibro-odontoma as a developing odontoma was a contentious
change in the previous classification. Sufficient molecular data has not yet accrued to clarify the
situation and so the classification has remained unchanged pending further evidence. The change
in 2017 was made because the literature suggested that many developing odontomas were
misdiagnosed as ameloblastic fibro-odontoma, risking patient harm through overtreatment. While
the existence of a neoplastic ameloblastic fibro-odontoma was not disputed, it was thought to be
very rare and it was felt that the great majority of lesions with an ameloblastic fibro-odontoma
histological appearance were destined to mature into odontomas. Molecular analysis suggests
these intermediate lesions are mixed, with some lesions previously diagnosed as ameloblastic
fibrodentinoma or ameloblastic fibro-odontoma bearing BRAF p.V600E mutation. However, any
histological differences between mutation positive and negative lesions has not been defined and
it remains difficult or impossible to differentiate these possible entities from the much commoner
developing odontomas on histological grounds alone. Review of the literature for the classification
suggest that a significant number of lesions published as ameloblastic fibroma are also developing
odontomas and it seems likely that reclassification including some ameloblastic fibromas may be
required in the future. In the meantime, the requirement is that, without evidence to change it, the
classification must remain unchanged.
No changes have been made to the classification of the malignant odontogenic tumours, retaining
the simplified structure from the previous edition. Odontogenic carcinoma with dentinoid
{ 25409850 } has not been included because of possible overlap with other carcinomas and the
aggressive end of the behaviour spectrum of adenoid ameloblastoma. The possibility of clear cell
carcinoma with dentinoid has been removed due to lack of evidence of relationship to clear cell
odontogenic carcinoma. Odontogenic carcinosarcoma remains contentious and extremely rare.
Confusion over its status as an entity has centred around whether a specific line of sarcoma
differentiation must be present. Since none is present in odontogenic sarcomas this condition
cannot apply and the entity has been retained, accepting its extreme rarity.
Giant cell lesions and bone cysts
Since the last classification it has been found that the central giant cell granuloma is driven by
MAPK pathway activation caused by mutation in one of several genes { 31705763 }. Somewhat

565

surprisingly, peripheral giant cell granuloma harbours the same changes despite its limited growth
potential and occasional spontaneous involution. Both are retained as separate entities in view of
the differing presentation, growth pattern and treatment. In line with the classification of bone and
soft tissue tumours, the concept of secondary aneurysmal bone cyst is now replaced by one of
cystic haemorrhagic degeneration, because the cystic change frequently found associated with
fibro-osseous lesions of the jaw lack the USP6 rearrangement and are unrelated to the aneurysmal
bone cyst.
Fibro-osseous tumours and dysplasias
The fibro-osseous lesions have long been an area of diagnostic difficulty, caused primarily by the
fact that it has been considered impossible to differentiate them on histological grounds alone. The
current classification attempts to differentiate these lesions more clearly, but it must be appreciated
that the clinical presentation, radiographic appearance and growth pattern are essential factors in
diagnosis.
Molecular analysis of the fibro-osseous lesions is in its early stages and does not yet define any
entity. This classification introduces the subtype of familial florid cemento-osseous dysplasia based
on its earlier age of onset and frequency of marked expansion { 32315206 }. Very early data has
identified GDD1 mutation in one family, distinct from the mutation found in gnathodiaphyseal
dysplasia and its associated cemento-ossifying fibromas, but suggesting a related pathogenesis.
One further reason for defining the familial type as a separate subtype is to better define familial
gigantiform cementoma. This controversial and extremely rare disorder has been retained in the
classification separate from florid cemento-osseous dysplasia and syndromes with multiple
cemento-ossifying fibromas, pending molecular characterisation.
Segmental odontomaxillary dysplasia { 21684782 } is a relatively well-defined condition that may
be underdiagnosed and is prone to misdiagnosis as fibrous dysplasia. For this reason, it is included
in the classification for the first time.
Benign maxillofacial bone and cartilage tumours
In the previous classification the cemento-ossifying fibroma was grouped with the juvenile
trabecular ossifying fibroma and juvenile psammomatoid ossifying fibroma as three subtypes of an
ossifying fibroma entity. An additional paragraph noted its odontogenic nature in the benign
mesenchymal odontogenic tumours. In the current classification, changes to the definition of entity
and subtype have abolished the category of ossifying fibroma, making it necessary for cementoossifying fibroma to take its place in the benign mesenchymal odontogenic tumour section.
Early molecular evidence suggests that cemento-ossifying fibromas are heterogeneous
{ 34173971 ; 34188021 } making it possible that reclassification may be required in future. Lesions
resembling
cemento-ossifying
fibroma
can arise from
a
variety of causes
including CDC73 (HRPT2) { 16458039 } or GDD1 mutation or deregulation of the Wnt pathway
{ 29239811 }. The number of entities, clinical features, localisation and classification of this group
may have to be reconsidered in the light of molecular evidence, which is not yet well enough defined
to justify changes. Both the psammomatoid ossifying fibroma and juvenile trabecular ossifying
fibroma appear to have a distinct molecular pathogenesis, though based on limited data.
The term juvenile has been removed from the psammomatoid ossifying fibroma because the age
range is broad with peak incidence in the 2nd to 4th decades. The juvenile trabecular ossifying
fibroma retains its juvenile descriptor. Although the literature includes many cases in older patients,
stricter diagnostic criteria suggest this remains a lesion that arises primarily in children.
Malignant maxillofacial bone and cartilage tumours
The only completely novel entity in the classification is rhabdomyosarcoma defined
by TFCP2 rearrangement { 29758589 }. This rhabdomyosarcoma has a predilection for craniofacial
bones. Its variable morphology includes epithelioid and spindle cells with a confusing immunoprofile
of positive reaction for cytokeratins, sometimes p63 and ALK together with desmin, myogenin and
MyoD1 and occasionally S100. Though the appearances are suggestive in routine stains, it is
recommended that the diagnosis is confirmed by molecular methods as this entity may be easily
confused with completely unrelated malignant neoplasms.

566

Cysts of the jaws

Radicular cyst
Definition
Radicular cyst (RC) is an inflammatory odontogenic cyst associated with the root of a non-vital
tooth
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA09.8 Radicular cyst
Related terminology
Acceptable: periapical cyst; apical cyst
Not recommended: (Apical) periodontal cyst; inflammatory dental cyst; dental cyst
Subtype(s)
Residual cyst (residual radicular cyst) is a RC that is retained in the jaw after removal of the
causative tooth.
Localization
RC are located at the apex of a tooth root, or on the lateral aspect of the root associated with a
lateral root canal. The most common site is the anterior maxilla, where 40-50% arise, followed by
the lower molar area { 16918602 ; 21850702 }.
Clinical features
RC are always associated with a non-vital tooth. Patients may complain of swelling, but RC are
often symptomless and found incidentally on radiological investigation. There may be a history of
dental pain or abscess.
Imaging: RC form a round or oval, well-demarcated, corticated radiolucency, usually 10-20 mm
diameter, occasionally larger. Residual cysts are found at a site of previous tooth extraction.
Epidemiology
RC is the most common jaw cyst, accounting for about 60% of all odontogenic cysts. There is a
peak incidence in the 4th and 5th decades and a slight male predilection { 16918602 ; 21850702 }.
Etiology
Caused by chronic inflammation at the apex of a non-vital tooth, following pulp necrosis, usually
due to dental caries.
Pathogenesis
Inflammation stimulates cell rests of Malassez to proliferate and form a cystic cavity that enlarges
as a result of osmotic pressure, causing peripheral bone resorption.
Macroscopic appearance
Usually a sac-like cystic mass.
Histopathology
RC is lined by non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium, that is proliferative with a
characteristic arcading pattern. Hyaline (Rushton) bodies, mucous (goblet) cells or small areas of
keratinization are not uncommon { 27957265 ; 25859536 }. The wall is composed of inflamed
fibrous tissue, often with foamy histiocytes. Deposits of cholesterol crystals (clefts) with foreign
body giant cells are often seen and may form luminal nodules. Residual, and long-standing cysts
are less inflamed and have a more regular thin epithelium.
Cytology
Smears of aspirated cysts may show squamous cells but more commonly show degenerative cyst
fluid with cholesterol clefts and debris.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Non-vital tooth; cyst lined by non-keratinising epithelium.
Staging
Not relevant

567

Prognosis and prediction
Extraction of the causative tooth or root canal treatment remove the cause and enucleation of the
cyst is rarely followed by recurrence { 17931389 }. Residual cyst may follow extraction alone.

L#23634
Radicular cyst
Periapical radiograph showing a well-demarcated, corticated radiolucency at the apex of
an upper incisor tooth.
R#23635
Radicular cyst
Panoramic radiograph showing a residual radicular cyst at a site of a previous tooth
extraction. It appears as a corticated radiolucency.

L#23636
Radicular cyst
The cyst is lined by hyperplastic epithelium showing a characteristic arcading pattern.
R#23637
Radicular cyst
A cluster of hyaline (Rushton) bodies forms a small nodule in the epithelial lining,
indicating an odontogenic cyst.

568

L#23638
Radicular cyst
Cholesterol clefts disrupt the epithelial lining and protrude into the cyst lumen.
R#25274
Radicular cyst
A typical radicular cyst. In places the lining shows proliferative epithelium with an arcading
pattern. Cholesterol clefts are seen in the lumen.

Inflammatory collateral cysts
Definition
Inflammatory collateral cysts (ICC) are inflammatory cysts on the buccal or distobuccal aspect of
the roots of partially or recently erupted teeth.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.Y Other specified cysts of oral or facial-neck region
Related terminology
Acceptable: mandibular infected buccal cyst; inflammatory paradental cyst
Subtype(s)
Paradental cyst (PC); Mandibular buccal bifurcation cyst (MBBC)
Localization
PC comprise 60% of all ICC and are found on the distobuccal aspect of mandibular third molars.
MBBC (35%) are found on the buccal aspect of mandibular first or second molars and are often
bilateral (up to 25%). Rare cases of ICC (4%) arise at other sites { 15128056 ; 2760852 }.
Clinical features
PC are associated with chronic pericoronitis but are often symptomless at presentation. MBBC may
be infected with pain, tenderness and suppuration. The tooth is vital and often tilted buccally with
deep periodontal pockets.
Imaging: ICC appear as corticated radiolucencies overlying the buccal or distobuccal aspect of the
tooth. The lamina dura is intact, and the follicular space surrounding a partially erupted tooth is
normal { 12180213 }.
MBBC may extend to the lower border of the mandible { 15128056 }, and often show a periosteal
reaction with laminated new bone formation
Epidemiology
ICC comprise about 5% of odontogenic cysts and are more common (70%) in males. The average
age is 30 years for PC and 9 years (first molar) or 17 years (second molar) for MBBC
{ 1065342 ; 15128056 }.
Etiology

569

Inflammation in the pericoronal tissues. Cyst formation may be exacerbated by food impaction or
by an enamel projection on the buccal aspect of the tooth { 1065342 ; 12180213 }.
Pathogenesis
ICC are “pocket” cysts caused by dilation of the pericoronal tissues and lined by sulcular or
junctional epithelium derived from the reduced enamel epithelium { 1065342 ; 26116019 }.
Macroscopic appearance
If removed intact, the cyst appears as a sac-like mass attached to the buccal or distobuccal aspect
of the coronal one third of the tooth root
Histopathology
Indistinguishable from radicular cyst. An inflamed wall lined by hyperplastic non-keratinised
epithelium. Cholesterol deposition and foamy histiocytes are common. The cyst may be open and
continuous with the pericoronal or gingival tissues.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: associated with partially or recently erupted vital tooth; radiolucency distinct from dental
follicle; intact lamina dura; non keratinised epithelium
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Cysts are enucleated. Third molars associated with PC are removed. Molars associated with MBBC
can be conserved and usually erupt normally.
#23640
Inflammatory collateral cyst
The mandibular buccal bifurcation cyst
overlies the buccal aspect of a mandibular
second molar tooth. It appears as a welldemarcated corticated radiolucency. Note
that the periodontal space and lamina dura
are intact and normal.

L#23641
Inflammatory collateral cyst
This paradental cyst is composed of an inflamed fibrous wall lined by hyperplastic
epithelium. The cyst appears as an open pocket.
R#27539

570

Inflammatory collateral cyst
The epithelial lining is squamous and non-keratinised and shows secondary inflammatory
changes with arcading rete hyperplasia and has no specific features.

L#27802
Inflammatory collateral cyst
This cyst appears as a well-demarcated and corticated radiolucency at the distobuccal
aspect of an impacted third molar tooth. The follicular space surrounding the crown is
intact and normal, seen as a narrow radiolucent rim around the crown posteriorly,
excluding dentigerous cyst.
R#25277
Paradental cyst
Paradental cyst is composed of an inflamed fibrous wall lined by proliferating epithelium,
often showing an arcading pattern. The cyst is open at one aspect where the lining may
be conntinuous with the pericoronal or gingival tissues.

Surgical ciliated cyst
Definition
Surgical ciliated cyst is a benign cyst caused by the traumatic implantation, usually surgical, of
respiratory epithelium in the gnathic bones.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.Y Other specified cysts of oral or facial-neck region
Related terminology
Acceptable: surgical ciliated cyst of the maxilla; postoperative maxillary cyst (if maxillary
location); (Respiratory) implantation cyst
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The gnathic bones, most common in posterior maxilla { 6936541 ; 12524623 }. Very rare in the
mandible.
Clinical features
Surgical ciliated cysts may be asymptomatic or present with swelling, pain or tenderness
{ 3143772 ; 27478654 }.

571

Imaging: Radiographs show a well-demarcated unilocular radiolucency of the jaws. Approximately
60% of cases are at least partially surrounded by a corticated margin. Occasional large lesions in
the maxilla may fill the sinus or be multilocular { 3466125 ; 6936541 }.
Epidemiology
Surgical ciliated cysts are rare { 27478654 ; 31093791 }. There is no gender predilection and the
most common age range is the 5th to 6th decades.
Etiology
Cysts develop from entrapped sinus or nasal mucosa in the jaw bones following trauma or surgery.
These inciting factors include, but are not limited to Caldwell-Luc or Le Fort I procedures, sinus
surgery,
maxillary
fracture,
midface
osteotomy
or traumatic
tooth
extraction
{ 11887151 ; 15953915 ; 25109584 }. Mandibular cases are generally caused by implantation of
sinus epithelium by contaminated instruments, or, transfer of epithelium with
autologous nasal osteocartilagenous
grafts
for
chin
augmentation
{ 11887151 ; 15953915 ; 25109584 ; 33096262 }.The cyst usually develops after a long latent
period, the reported delay being up to 20 years after the causative surgery { 27478654 }.
Pathogenesis
It is thought that the implanted epithelium forms a cyst cavity due to persistent mucous secretion.
In this respect some regard the lesion as a type of mucous retention cyst { 3466125 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions are often fragmented, but show a cyst wall of variable thickness. The lumen may contain
mucinous material.
Histopathology
The cyst is lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar (respiratory-type) epithelium. Areas of
squamous metaplasia or simple cuboidal cells may be seen { 3125266 }. Mucous (goblet) cells are
common. The cyst wall is composed of loose fibrous connective tissue, which may be inflamed.
Areas of subepithelial fibrosis or hyalinisation are frequent { 3143772 ; 3466125 }.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: a history of previous surgery; radiolucent well demarcated cyst; respiratory epithelial
lining
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Treatment is simple enucleation and recurrences are rare.

L#23074
Surgical ciliated cyst
Cyst lined by bilaminar cuboidal epithelium with focal cilia.

572

R#23263
Surgical ciliated cyst
Cyst lined by fully differentiated respiratory epithelium.

L#27261
Surgical ciliated cyst
Sagittal CT imaging of a surgical ciliated cyst of the maxilla from a patient with a history of
traumatic tooth extraction.
R#27674
Surgical ciliated cyst
The cyst is lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with areas of squamous
metaplasia. Mucous (goblet) cells are seen. The cyst wall is composed of loose fibrous
connective tissue.

Nasopalatine duct cyst
Definition
Nasopalatine duct cyst (NDC) is a developmental non-odontogenic cyst arising in the incisive canal.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.1 Developmental nonodontogenic cysts of oral region
Related terminology
Acceptable: Incisive canal cyst
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
NDCs present in the midline of the anterior hard palate.
Clinical features
Most NDCs present as sessile swellings posterior to the maxillary incisors or may be entirely
asymptomatic. More deeply sited cysts present as a swelling on the labial alveolus or bulging
into the nasal floor { 23285406 ; 29248496 }. Rarely, a NDC will develop within the soft tissue of
the incisive papilla. Typical diameter is 10 - 20 mm but they may reach several centimetres
{ 1995816 ; 18587308 ; 25579059 }.
Imaging: Radiology shows a corticated, symmetrical radiolucency in the anterior hard palate
between the roots of the incisors, which may be displaced. Unless there are other reasons for loss

573

of vitality, adjacent teeth are vital with intact lamina dura. Radiographic superimposition of the
anterior nasal spine often produces a characteristic heart or pear shape radiolucency in occlusal
view. Diagnosis requires a size greater than 6mm, the diameter of the normal incisive canal
{ 1995816 }, slightly more in older patients { 31640721 }.
Epidemiology
Fewer than 5% of all cysts of the jaws are NDC, but it accounts for as many as 80% of all nonodontogenic cysts { 8170660 ; 16827841 }. It occurs most frequently in patients aged 30–60 years,
with a male-to-female ratio of about 3:1 { 29248496 }.
Etiology
NDC is categorised as a developmental cyst and arises from respiratory and squamous epithelial
vestigial remnants of an embryonic nasopalatine duct present in some individuals { 27860365 }.
Pathogenesis
In one third of cases, inflammation from non-vital teeth or periodontal ligament may be a factor by
stimulating the epithelial remnants to proliferate { 18587308 ; 25579059 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Fibrous curettings and cyst lining
Histopathology
About 90% of NDCs are lined by non-keratinised stratified squamous epithelium, with focal
cuboidal, columnar, or ciliated areas. About half contain respiratory epithelium, but less
than 10% are lined entirely by it { 1995816 ; 29248496 }. Cysts with respiratory-type epithelium
tend to be more superiorly placed in the incisive canal { 18587308 }. The cyst wall contains
prominent neurovascular bundles and occasionally mucous glands or cartilage.
Cytology
Smears of aspirated cysts show degenerative cyst fluid with debris and macrophages some of
which contain hemosiderin. Epithelium is absent or scant.However these findings are non-specific.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: epicentre at incisive canal; lining of non-keratinised squamous or respiratory epithelium.
Desirable: neurovascular bundle in the cyst wall.
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
NDCs do not normally recur after enucleation.
#27262

Nasopalatine duct cyst
The cyst wall is lined by
respiratory type epithelium
with thick walled vessels,
fat and nerves.

#27263

Occlusal radiograph showing typical
radiographic appearances of a
corticated radiolucency just behind
and around the apices of the upper
central incisors.

#27804
Nasopalatine duct cyst
Three areas of the wall from the same cyst showing a range of lining epithelium, from
bilaminar cuboidal to low squamous and respiratory, intermediate appearances are often
seen.

#26582
Nasopalatine duct cyst
The cyst is lined in part by respiratory-type epithelium. Fat and thick walled arteries are
present in the wall, along with some nerve bundles. This particular example has been
removed without significant crush artefact, but many are fragmented at surgery.

Gingival cysts
Definition
Gingival cysts are developmental odontogenic cysts arising in attached gingiva or alveolar ridge
mucoperiosteum of adults or infants.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.0 Developmental odontogenic cysts
Related terminology
Acceptable: Gingival cyst of the newborn; Bohn's nodules (in infants)
Not recommended:Bohn’s nodules, Alveolar cyst
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
In adults, most cysts arise in the gingiva of the mandibular premolar/canine region
{ 26233969 ; 29156092 } or less frequently in anterior maxillary gingiva, almost always on the
buccal aspect of the alveolus. In infants, the cysts occur anywhere on the edentulous alveolar ridge
of the mandible or maxilla { 6172766 ; 4506677 }.
Clinical features
Gingival cyst of the adult (GCA) usually forms a single, painless, well-circumscribed nodule of the
attached gingivae, sometimes with a bluish-grey translucent appearance. Radiographs are not
helpful to detect GCA, despite occasional superficial erosion of the underlying alveolar bone.
Gingival cysts of the infant (GCI) present as small (< 2 mm) white nodules on the alveolar mucosa
and are often multiple { 14155470 }.
Epidemiology
GCA accounts for less than 0.5% of odontogenic cysts { 30758760 }. The majority of the patients
are in the fifth and sixth decades of life { 12146543 ; 29156092 ; 30758760 }. GCI is found in as
many as 90% of neonates, but is rare after the age of 3 months { 6679627 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Nodular mucosa with smooth surface, whitish or translucent.
Histopathology

576

GCA is lined by a thin, flat or cuboidal squamous epithelium 1-3 cells thick without rete ridges.
Plaque-like thickenings that protrude into the lumen or the connective tissue wall and clear cells
are usually present. The connective tissue wall may contain islands of epithelium resembling the
epithelial plaques. GCIs are rarely biopsied but are lined by thin keratinized epithelium that may
have a connection to the surface epithelium.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Gingival cysts of adults: site in attached gingiva; thin epithelial lining
Gingival cysts of infants: site in alveolar ridge; patient age less than 3 months
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
GCA do not recur after conservative excision { 29156092 }. GCIs rupture or involute and resolve
spontaneously.
#24457
Gingival cyst of the adult
A small, well-circumscribed, translucent nodule in
the left lower attached gingiva, buccal to the first
premolar.

L#27510
Gingival cyst of the adult
The cyst is lined by non-keratinized eptihelium with two plaque-like thickenings that
protrude into the lumen.
R#27641
Gingival cyst of the adult
Gingival mucosa with an underlying cyst cavity with a thin inconspicuous epithelial lining.

577

L#24477
Gingival cyst
Cystic cavity covered by cuboidal epithelium.
R#24478
Gingival cyst
Cystic cavity covered by cuboidal epithelium (PAS staining)

Dentigerous cyst
Definition
A developmental odontogenic cyst of the jaws surrounding the crown of an unerupted tooth, the
lining attached to the cementoenamel junction.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.0 Developmental odontogenic cysts
Related terminology
Acceptable: Follicular cyst
Subtype(s)
Eruption cyst
Localization
Dentigerous cysts develop most frequently on third molars and maxillary canines, but any
unerupted tooth or odontoma may be affected { 23278191 ; 28153133 ; 30175860 ; 31858303 }.
An eruption cyst is a superficial dentigerous cyst over an erupting tooth in a child, usually a
deciduous first molar { 28160586 }.
Clinical features
Most dentigerous cyst are small and asymptomatic and discovered on routine dental radiographs
or when investigating the failure of a tooth to erupt. Larger cysts cause expansion of the jaw and
may reach several centimetres in diameter. Infection, generally associated with oral
communication, may result in symptoms of pain and swelling.
Imaging: Radiographically, the cyst forms a well-defined, unilocular radiolucency with a corticated
border. Large cysts may appear pseudolocular, expand the jaw or displace or resorb adjacent
teeth. The cyst is closely associated with the crown of an unerupted tooth and appears to
originate from its cemento-enamel junction, though the cyst may surround the tooth crown, lie
lateral to it or surround parts of the root in addition, depending on direction of
enlargement { 23278191 ; 28153133 ; 30175860 ; 31858303 ; 33881736 }
Epidemiology
The most common developmental cyst of the jaws, representing about 25% of all jaw cysts. The
age range is broad (5-83 years) with a peak incidence in the second and third decade. There is a
gender predilection with men affected 1.7 times more frequently than women
{ 23278191 ; 28153133 ; 30175860 ; 31858303 }.
Etiology

578

A developmental cyst, the dentigerous cyst, develops by the accumulation of fluid between reduced
enamel epithelium and the crown of a tooth { 28060370 ; 28153133 ; 30175860 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown. Dentigerous cyst does not harbour the BRAF p.V600E mutations found in
ameloblastomas { 27084044 ; 33443847 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Fibrous, tan, friable cyst wall and extracted tooth. While the relationship to tooth is usually lost with
extraction of the associated tooth, adherent tags of cyst lining often remain at the cemento-enamel
junction.
Histopathology
The dentigerous cyst has a thin, non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelial lining, often
bilaminar in areas. Cuboidal, columnar and ciliated cells may be present, as can mucous or
sebaceous metaplasia { 16050487 ; 23278191 ; 28060370 }. Hyaline (Rushton) bodies and focal
keratinisation may also occur { 25210349 ; 25859536 }. When not inflammed, the cyst wall is
composed of loose, often myxoid, fibrous connective tissue and may contain occasional
odontogenic epithelial rests { 23278191 }.
Differential Inflamed dentigerous cysts show hyperplasia and thickening of the lining epithelium
and often foamy macrophages and cholesterol clefts associated with foreign body giant cells
{ 23278191 ; 30175860 }, and come to resemble radicular cysts histopathologically.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: well-defined radiolucency associated with the crown of an unerupted tooth; epithelium
and cyst wall attached to the cementoenamel junction of unerupted tooth
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Dentigerous cyst does not recur after enucleation and removal of the associated unerupted tooth.
Decompression prior to enucleation may be indicated for large lesions { 28579245 }.

#27264
Dentigerous cyst
Thin, regular squamous epithelial lining.

579

L#27265
Dentigerous cyst, inflammed
The thick and hyperplastic epithelial lining of an inflamed dentigerous cyst in relationship
to the dense acute and chronic inflammation.
R#27523
Dentigerous cyst
Typical pericoronal radiolucency around the lower third molar, with attachment of the cyst
lining at the cementoenamel junction. The associated tooth is displaced posteriorly.

L#26940
Dentigerous cyst
There is a thin non-keratinised epithelial lining.
R#28336
Dentigerous cyst
Cyst with a thin, sometimes bilaminar, epithelium. There is focal respiratory metaplasia,
often seen in maxillary cysts, and focal inflammation with cholesterol crystals.

Orthokeratinized odontogenic cyst
Definition
Orthokeratinized odontogenic cyst (OOC) is a developmental cyst lined by orthokeratinized
stratified squamous epithelium
ICD-O coding
580

None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.0 Developmental odontogenic cysts
Related terminology
Not recommended: Orthokeratinized variant of odontogenic keratocyst
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most OOC (80%) are found in the mandible and 65% in the angle/ramus region
{ 6166916 ; 20121617 ; 21062939 }.
Clinical features
OOC usually presents as a painless swelling { 20121617 ; 21062939 } but many are incidental
findings during radiographic examination. Rare cases of multiple or bilateral OOC have been
reported, but there is no evidence of any association with the naevoid basal cell carcinoma (Gorlin)
syndrome { 24737103 ; 31119532 }.
Imaging: OOC are well-demarcated, spherical unilocular radiolucent lesions, often with a corticated
margin. Occasional cases (less than 10%) are multilocular { 21062939 }. Up to 70% of all lesions
are associated with an impacted tooth, with a radiological similarity to dentigerous
cyst{ 20121617 }.
Epidemiology
OOC is rare and comprises about 1% or less of odontogenic cysts { 32868122 ; 30758760 }, arising
over a wide age range with an average of about 35 years and a peak in the third and fourth decades.
About 65% occur in males { 6166916 ; 20121617 ; 21062939 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
OOC is a developmental cyst that most likely arises from remnants of dental lamina { 9568510 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The pathologist usually receives multiple fragments of cyst wall. The contents are cream or yellow
coloured with a cheesy or buttery texture.
Histopathology
OOC is lined by thin regular orthokeratinized stratified squamous epithelium with a prominent
granular cell layer and inconspicuous or low cuboidal basal cells. It is heavily keratinized, often with
lamellae of keratin filling the lumen. The wall is fibrous and may show areas of inflammation. There
may be parakeratinized or non-keratinized areas, but these form a small part of the lining and are
often associated with inflammation { 6166916 }. Rare examples include sebaceous glands in the
lining { 18022486 }. Microcysts may be seen in the wall in 5% of cases { 6166916 ; 9568510 }.
Cytology
Examination of an aspirate may show lamellae and fragments of orthokeratin.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: site tooth bearing areas of jaw; thin, regular epithelial lining with orthokeratinization
Desirable: prominent granular cell layer. Flat basal cells
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence is rare (less than 5%) following enucleation { 21062939 }.

581

#23195
Orthokeratinized odontogenic cyst
A thin regular lining of orthokeratinized stratified squamous epthelium. The keratin is
lamellated and extends into the lumen.

L#23312
Orthokeratinized odontogenic cyst
Radiology shows a well-demarcated and corticated unilocular radiolucency
R#25287
Orthokeratinised odontogenic cyst
The cyst is lined by thin regular orthokeratinised stratified squamous epithelium with a
prominent granular cell layer. The surface is heavily keratinised and keratin is seen in the
lumen

582

Lateral periodontal cyst and botryoid odontogenic cyst
Definition
Lateral periodontal cyst (LPC) is a developmental odontogenic cyst lined by non-keratinized
epithelium with characteristic thickenings.
Botryoid odontogenic cyst (BOC) is a less common multilocular subtype of LPC.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.0 Developmental odontogenic cysts
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Botryoid odontogenic cyst (BOC)
Localization
LPC and BOC arise mainly in the mandibular canine‐premolar alveolar bone, accounting for 70%
and 85% respectively, most of the remainder arise in the maxilla { 26233969 ; 29156092 }.
Multifocal occurrence has been reported.
Clinical features
LPC and BOC are asymptomatic in almost 90% of the cases and usually identified incidentally on
radiographs. They cause buccal alveolar bone expansion in 50% of cases { 29156092 }.
Imaging: LPC is a well demarcated, often corticated unilocular radiolucency closely related to the
lateral surface of the root of an erupted tooth { 6935584 }. Cortical bone expansion and perforation
may be evident on cone beam imaging in half of cases. Tooth roots are almost never
resorbed. Most lesions are smaller than 10 mm in size. BOC has a multilocular appearance.
Epidemiology
LPCs and BOCs are rare, accounting for less than 1% of odontogenic cysts
{ 1501155 ; 30758760 }. The majority present in the fifth to seventh decades of life { 29156092 }
with a male preponderance { 29156092 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
LPC and BOC are developmental and thought to arise from odontogenic epithelial rests of dental
lamina, reduced enamel epithelium or rests of Malassez.
Macroscopic appearance
Intact LPC presents as a small, tan, soft tissue cystic sac containing clear fluid. BOC is multilocular.
The luminal surface of both may show focal thickenings { 28153133 }.
Histopathology
LPC has an uninflamed fibrous wall lined by a thin, non-keratinized squamous or cuboidal
epithelium 1-3 cells thick. Focal, whorled, plaque-like epithelial thickenings are present, identical to
those found less frequently in gingival cyst of the adult. Clear cells are often found in the thickenings
{ 28153133 }. BOC is multilocular with the same appearances { 20952285 }.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: site on the lateral aspect or between the roots of vital erupted teeth; characteristic
whorled epithelial plaques; multilocular (BOC only).
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence of LPC is exceedingly rare after enucleation. BOC recurs after enucleation in almost
22% of cases and additional treatment may be indicated for recurrent BOC { 29156092 }.

583

#24934
Lateral Periodontal Cyst
Cyst with uninflamed fibrous wall lined by a thin, cuboidal epithelium a few cells thick, with
focal, whorled, plaque-like epithelial thickenings containing clear cells.
#24935
Lateral periodontal cyst
High power showing clear cells in the epithelium

Cyst showing multiple cystic spaces with the same histology as lateral periodontal cyst

Calcifying odontogenic cyst
Definition
Calcifying odontogenic cyst (COC) is a developmental odontogenic cyst characterized
histologically by ghost cells, which often calcify.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.0 & XH3R33 Developmental odontogenic cysts & Calcifying odontogenic cyst
Related terminology
Acceptable: calcifying cystic odontogenic tumor, gorlin cyst
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
COC usually arise in the tooth bearing segments of the jaws, almost equally in the maxilla and
mandible. In the maxilla there is a strong predilection for the anterior segments while mandibular
lesions are more evenly distributed { 2005490 ; 18221328 ; 29738629 }. Approximately 10% of
cases are extraosseous with a predilection for the anterior mandibular gingiva { 27669959 }.
Clinical features
Most patients are asymptomatic, though intrabony lesions typically cause cortical expansion.
Extraosseous lesions present as submucosal swellings { 29738629 ; 33591801 }.
Imaging: COCs generally form well-defined unilocular lesions, about a third uniformly radiolucent
and the remainder a mixed radiolucency. Cortical expansion is common { 29738629 ; 33591801 }.
Epidemiology
COC shows a worldwide distribution { 29738629 } but is rare, accounting for less than 1% of
odontogenic cysts { 16918602 }. There is no gender predilection and peak incidence in the second
and third decades.
Etiology
Unknown

586

Pathogenesis
COC has mutations in CTNNB1, which encodes β catenin, shared with adamantinomatous
craniopharyngioma and pilomatrixoma { 10192393 ; 27158066 ; 30598409 } and implicating the
Wnt pathway { 14578169 ; 28658279 }. No other pathogenic mutations in other oncogenes or
tumor suppressor genes were detected in a 50 gene panel { 27158066 }. Wnt is also involved in
odontomas, with which COC is often associated. It is not yet known whether the mutations cause
a replication error phenotype.
Macroscopic appearance
Cystic cavity architecturally unless the cyst is curetted.
Histopathology
Unilocular cyst with a stratified epithelial lining of varying thickness, often loose resembling stellate
reticulum and with palisaded and hyperchromatic columnar ameloblast-like basal cells. Cysts may
show intraluminal and/or mural epithelial proliferation producing ameloblastoma-like areas. Ghost
cells are characteristic, rounded or stacked flattened pale eosinophilic cells within the epithelium
with distinct outlines and karyolysis resulting in a 'ghost like appearance'. These are variable in
morphology and number, and often calcify. Masses of ghost cells often pass into the connective
tissue of the cyst wall, eliciting a foreign body reaction and inducing dentinoid (dentine-like matrix
or mineralised tissue without tubular structure) { 2005490 ; 18221328 }. Though characteristic,
ghost cells form in other odontogenic tumours and do not alone justify a diagnosis of
COC. COCs can occur with odontomas or other mixed odontogenic tumours { 15472661 }.
Differential Includes dentinogenic ghost cell tumour { 27669959 } and ghost cell odontogenic
carcinoma.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: cystic architecture; numerous ghost cells
Desirable: palisaded hyperchromatic basal cells; dentinoid
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
COCs are treated by conservative surgical removal, enucleation and/or curettage. A systematic
review reported an 8% recurrence rate { 29738629 }.

#24298
Calcifying odontogenic cyst

587

Typical radiographic presentation as a pericoronal mixed radiolucent/radiopaque lesion of
the right posterior mandible and ramus.

#24300
Calcifying odontogenic cyst
Cytologic detail of ghost cells devoid of nuclei but with maintenance of cell outlines.

#24304
Calcifying odontogenic cyst

588

Areas of the cyst wall containing ghost cells and a homogeneous eosinophilic dentinoid
product with cell inclusions.

#27270
Calcifying odontogenic cyst
Cystic architecture with prominent basal cells and numerous homogeneous eosinophilic
ghost cells.

Glandular odontogenic cyst
Definition
Glandular odontogenic cyst (GOC) is a developmental cyst in which the epithelial lining resembles
glandular tissue.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.0 Developmental odontogenic cysts
Related terminology
Not recommended: sialo-odontogenic cyst; mucoepidermoid odontogenic cyst
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
GOC arises in tooth-bearing areas of the jaws, about 75% of cases in the mandible. Mandibular
and maxillary lesions have a propensity for the anterior regions.
Clinical features
GOC usually presents as a slowly expanding painless swelling.
Imaging: GOC are well-defined corticated unilocular or multilocular radiolucencies. Tooth
displacement or root resorption is seen in up to 25% of cases. Lesions may reach a large size and
cross the midline in the mandible { 12775659 ; 28744957 }.
Epidemiology
GOC comprise less than 0.5% of odontogenic cysts { 16918602 }. There is no gender predilection.
The average age at presentation is about 50 years with a peak in the 5th to 7th decades { 28744957 }.

589

Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
GOC is a developmental cyst thought to arise from cell rests of the dental lamina. No pathogenic
mutations were detected in a panel of 50 oncogenes and tumour suppressor genes screened by
next-generation sequencing { 28866361 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Often multicystic or lobular. The cyst wall may have thickenings or papillary projections.
Histopathology
GOC are often multilocular cysts and are lined by epithelium of variable thickness from a thin layer
of flattened squamous or cuboidal cells to stratified squamous epithelium, often with whorled
epithelial thickenings, or plaques similar to those in lateral periodontal cyst. Cuboidal or low
columnar cells on the luminal surface (“hobnail” cells) are found in all cases. Other typical features
include intraepithelial microcysts, apocrine metaplasia, clear cells, papillary projections (tufting),
cilia and mucous cells { 16043383 ; 21915706 ; 28744957 }. Not all features are present in every
case and a higher number of features allows more confident diagnosis { 21915706 }.
Differential GOC may show similar features to central mucoepidermoid carcinoma (CMEC) and
this must be considered in the differential diagnosis { 21915706 ; 18248589 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
GOC is generally considered to be negative for MAML2 gene rearrangements { 24647913 }.
However, recent studies have shown rearrangements in lesions that meet the histological criteria
for GOC. These lesions may have been CMEC at the outset, but it raises the possibility that some
CMEC might develop from a pre-existing GOC and MAML2 rearrangement studies must
be interpreted with caution { 29121421 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
- Radiolucent cystic lesion of tooth-bearing area of the jaw;
- Often multilocular
Desirable:
- lining of variable thickness with epithelial thickenings, plaques or papillary projections;
- luminal columnar or cuboidal ("hobnail") cells;
- microcysts or duct-like structures;
- mucous or clear cells.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
GOCs, especially large and multilocular lesions, recur after enucleation in about 22% of
cases { 15789313 ; 28744957 }.

#24137

590

Glandular odontogenic cyst
The epithelial lining shows microcysts and duct like structures. There is a superficial layer
of columnar cells, in places with cilia.

#24138
Glandular odontogenic cyst
The lining shows whorled epithelial thickenings and papillary projections. Superficial
columnar cells can also be seen.

#27609

591

Glandular odontogenic cyst
An extensive multilocular lesion filling the entire body of the mandible and crossing the
midline.

#25289
Glandular odontogenic cyst
The cyst is multicystic and is lined by stratified squamous epithelium of variable thickness.
Features of note include epithelial thickenings and plaques with whorling,superficial
columnar ("hobnail") cells and mucous cells and gland-like spaces.

Odontogenic keratocyst
Definition
The odontogenic keratocyst (OKC) is a developmental odontogenic cyst that is characterized
histologically by a thin parakeratinzed stratified squamous epithelial lining with palisaded and
hyperchromatic basal cells and clinically by a tendency to recur after treatment.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.0 Developmental odontogenic cysts
Related terminology
Acceptable: keratocystic odontogenic tumour
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
OKCs are unique to the jaws with a mandible:maxilla incidence ratio of 4:1. There is a strong
predilection for the posterior mandible and ramus { 1065842 ; 16918602 ; 21159911 }.
Clinical features
Most patients are asymptomatic and just under half have detectable swelling. OKCs' insidious
growth pattern produces large cysts at diagnosis with significant bony destruction but minimal bone
expansion { 1065842 ; 11068078 ; 16918602 ; 21159911 ; 21850702 }. Pathologic fracture is
often a risk. OKCs are a component of the nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome which is
autosomal dominant and most often results from mutation of the PTCH1 gene. Syndromic OKCs

592

are typically multiple and occur in younger patients { 2442330 }. Occasionally OKCs can
arise extraosseously in the gingiva where they can be distinguished from gingival cysts because of
their distinct histologic features { 18812605 }.
Imaging: OKCs form well-circumscribed radiolucencies with just over half showing cortication in the
border. Approximately three quarters are unilocular and one quarter multilocular. Most show at
least some cortical expansion radiographically but tend to grow anteriorly and posteriorly initially,
producing only minor expansion for their total length. Root resorption is occasionally observed.
Approximately 35% are associated with unerupted teeth { 21159911 }.
Epidemiology
OKCs are the third most commonly diagnosed odontogenic cyst and they have a worldwide
distribution { 21159911 }. They occur in all ages but show a strong peak in the second or third
decade and a second smaller peak in the elderly. There is a slight male predominance
{ 1065842 ; 11068078 ; 16918602 ; 21159911 ; 21850702 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
OKCs arise from remnants of the dental lamina. The most notable molecular finding in OKCs are
frequent mutations of the tumour suppressor gene PTCH1(9q22.3-q31), in syndromic and up to
80% of sporadic OKCs { 8528259 ; 21270459 ; 31162759 }. A next generation sequencing panel
for the members of the sonic hedgehog pathway reported an inactivating mutation rate of 93% with
biallelic inactivation in 80% { 25458233 }. Other genetic alterations including mutations
in PTCH2, NUFU and BRAF p.V600E have also been reported, but at a much lower frequency and
not always confirmed { 25458233 ; 31987674 ; 29272070 } and sequencing showed no pathogenic
changes in commonly mutated oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes { 29272070 }. These
findings indicate constitutive activation of SHH signaling plays a major role in OKC pathogenesis,
raising the possibility of treatment with small molecule inhibitors of SHH signalling { 30610186 }.
The possible role of fibroblasts in OKC fibrous capsules in promoting or regulating cyst growth via
interaction with epithelial cells has also been reported { 24972872 ; 24581331 }.
Macroscopic appearance
OKCs are architecturally cystic unless submitted as multiple smaller curetted fragments. The lumen
may contain yellowish-white thick keratin.
Histopathology
The histological features of OKC are diagnostic, unlike commoner odontogenic cysts. The cyst is
lined by a thin stratified squamous epithelium approximately 4-8 cells thick, typically without rete
ridges. The surface typically shows corrugation with parakeratin; a small proportion shows focal
orthokeratosis. The characteristic basal cells are hyperchromatic, palisaded, and range from
cuboidal to columnar with some limited reverse nuclear polarity but rarely subnuclear vacuolization.
Cysts are usually uninflamed but when they are secondarily inflamed the inflammatory reaction
induces metaplastic change in the epithelium and these characteristic features are lost. Nonspecific changes can be seen including cholesterol deposition, Rushton bodies, mucous or
sebaceous cells and even cartilage in the wall of the cyst { 264648 ; 11598579 }. Some OKCs
display basal budding with small rounded rete ridges. Many also have remnants of dental lamina
and microcysts in their walls and this tends to be more prominent in syndromic OKCs { 2441019 }.
OKCs have an increased proliferative capacity as evidenced by increased mitoses and other
indices of cell proliferation { 32733563 }. A controversial “solid” variant of OKC has been reported
but care must be taken to distinguish this from keratin-producing ameloblastoma and well
differentiated squamous cell carcinoma { 14720200 }. Odontogenic carcinoma can arise rarely from
the epithelial lining of OKCs and this should be considered if solid areas have developed.
OKC treated by decompression before enucleation develop metaplastic changes and lose their
unique diagnostic features { 12796876 }.
Cytology
Smears show polygonal anucleate squamous cells with well defined cytoplasmic margins,
squamous cells with pyknotic nuclei and fragments of keratinous debris that may appear lamellar
{ 31951108 ; 31110417 ; 24648668 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular analysis is not normally required but finding PTCH1 mutations may help in differentiating
ambiguous cystic lesions or possible malignant neoplasms that may mimic OKCs.

593

Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
site
in
jaws;
stratified
squamous
epithelial
lining
with
surface
parakeratin; palisaded hyperchromatic basal cells.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
OKCs have a risk of recurrence following traditional enucleation, between 20-30% though a wide
range is reported { 11077375 ; 29264656 ; 30795995 }. This risk can be much reduced by more
aggressive or adjunctive treatment including curettage, resection, peripheral ostectomy,
cryotherapy or chemical cautery of the cavity, and excision of the overlying mucosa. Prior to
definitive cystectomy, many cysts are decompressed/marsupialized. This has led to a significantly
diminished recurrence rate { 29264656 ; 26003518 }. Recurrence rates are comparable for
syndromic and nonsyndromic OKCs { 20115971 }.

Odontogenic keratocyst
Some odontogenic keratocysts display basal budding.

despite the longitudinal extent there is minimal expansion and displacement of teeth.

Odontogenic tumours
Benign epithelial odontogenic tumours

Adenomatoid odontogenic tumour
Definition
Adenomatoid odontogenic tumour (AOT) is a benign encapsulated epithelial odontogenic tumour
that contains rosette or duct-like structures and has an indolent behaviour.
ICD-O coding
9300/0 Adenomatoid odontogenic tumour
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH2SD0 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Adenomatoid odontogenic tumour
2E83.1 & XH2SD0 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Adenomatoid odontogenic tumour
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
AOT appears almost exclusively within the jawbones with two-thirds of cases in the maxilla, most
commonly in the anterior regions.Three quarters of cases are associated with an unerupted
permanent tooth, usually the maxillary canine, and expand the follicle. The rarer peripheral variant
occurs mainly in the anterior maxillary gingiva { 30256456 }.
Clinical features
Most cases are asymptomatic and all have limited growth potential. Large AOTs present as bonyhard swellings with cortical expansion but not perforation. Peripheral AOTs appear as small gingival
nodules { 18088735 }.

597

Imaging: AOTs arising in a tooth follicle form well-defined, unilocular radiolucencies around or
alongside the crown of an unerupted tooth, often extending apically past the cementoenamel
junction. Extrafollicular AOT appears as a unilocular radiolucency located between, above or
superimposed upon the roots of erupted teeth { 19686935 }. Tooth displacement and root
resorption have been reported and two-thirds of cases exhibit discrete radiopaque foci
{ 17617830 ; 18088735 ; 30256456 ; 32680811 }. The peripheral variant may cause superficial
erosion of the underlying alveolar bone.
Epidemiology
AOT accounts for less than 10% of odontogenic tumours. Two-thirds of cases occur in females.
Although AOTs have been reported over a wide age range, more than 80% are diagnosed in the
second and third decades { 17617830 ; 18088735 ; 30256456 }.
Etiology
Unknown. Multiple AOTs can occur in patients with Schimmelpenning syndrome (OMIM#163200)
{ 17366580 ; 31402313 }, caused by postzygotic RAS mutations { 22683711 }.
Pathogenesis
KRAS p.G12V and p.G12R mutations are detected in approximately 70% of sporadic AOT and are
independent of the clinicopathological features { 26979257 ; 30643167 }. MAPK pathway activation
occurs in AOT irrespective of KRAS mutations { 30294831 ; 30643167 }. Additionally, copy number
loss at 6p15 and 7p15.3 has been detected in one AOT { 26979257 }. No other mutations in
oncogenes or tumour suppressor genes have been detected in AOTs { 26979257 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Most AOTs are smooth, rounded, symmetrical masses with a firm consistency{ 19686935 }. On cut
surface, the lesions present a brownish or yellowish colour and range from solid to cystic growth
patterns. Follicular AOTs may be removed with the affected tooth.
Histopathology
AOTs are encapsulated and contain variably sized nodules of spindle, cuboidal and columnar
epithelial odontogenic cells with minimal stroma{ 19686935 }. Within the nodules are rosette or
duct-like structures, which when frequent produce the adenomatoid or gland-like appearance. In
some tumours, the duct-like spaces can be inconspicuous. Between the epithelial cells and in the
center of the rosette-like structures, eosinophilic amorphous secretory material similar to enamel
matrix is present { 18088735 }. Anastomosing strands of epithelial cells in a plexiform pattern are
often present and more prominent at the periphery. Small foci of calcification, dentinoid matrix and
hemorrhage are variably seen { 18088735 ; 32680811 }. AOTs may contain cysts lined by
nonkeratinizing stratified epithelium, resembling a dentigerous cyst when AOT develops in a dental
follicle { 32680811 }.
Differential AOT-like areas have been recognized within other odontogenic tumours, including
odontomas, adenomatoid odontogenic hamartoma, adenomatoid dentinoma, and adenoid
ameloblastoma { 9768421 ; 16476044 ; 26297394 } and AOT may contain areas resembling
calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour with clear cells { 15695124 }. These histological overlaps
makes radiological and clinical correlation essential for definitive diagnosis.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: site in alveolar processes of jaws; epithelial nodular structure; rosettes of spindled to
columnar epithelial cells; duct-like structures; minimal stroma
Desirable: encapsulation; young patient; association with tooth follicle
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction

598

AOT has almost no risk of recurrence following conservative enucleation.

of a permanent pre-molar with intraluminal

#27275: Tumour showing prominent epithelial proliferation organized in solid nodules and strands
formed by cuboidal odontogenic epithelial cells, with round arrays of back to back columnar cells.

#27633: Cropped panoramic radiograph showing an adenomatoid odontogenic tumours with cyst
formation on the upper first premolar, the corticated radiolucency causing tooth root
displacement. There is no visible internal mineralisation.

#27666: Cone beam imaging of adenomatoid odontogenic tumour, left axial, centre
3D reconstruction, right panoramic reconstruction, showing a relatively well defined radiolucency
containing small speckles of mineralisation.

600

#24490: Extrafollicular adenomatoid odontogenic tumour demonstrating a fibrous capsule
delimiting odontogenic epithelium organized in solid nodules and strands of varying size
containing duct-like structures and focal calcification in a hemorrhagic stroma.

#24738: A thick fibrous capsule surrounds odontogenic epithelium organized in cell-rich nodules
of varying size containing rosettes and duct-like structures. At higher magnification, small foci of
eosinophilic enamel matrix secretion (so-called "tumor droplets") may be seen associated with
ameloblast-like cells.

Squamous odontogenic tumour
Definition
Squamous odontogenic tumour (SOT) is a benign, slow-growing epithelial odontogenic tumour with
squamous differentiation.
ICD-O coding
9312/0 Squamous odontogenic tumour
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH4PV9 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Squamous odontogenic tumour
2E83.1 & XH4PV9 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Squamous odontogenic tumour

601

Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
SOT affects the tooth-bearing parts of the jaws. Most are solitary lesions with a predilection for the
anterior maxilla and posterior mandible. Multifocal and peripheral SOT have been reported
{ 2674829 ; 8515987 ; 27632187 }.
Clinical features
SOT usually present as an asymptomatic swelling, a minority are associated with pain, tenderness,
mobility of teeth or bone expansion.
Imaging: SOT typically presents as a triangular or semicircular unilocular radiolucency with welldefined borders along one or more tooth roots. Cortication of margins is variable. Root
displacement is common but root resorption is rare. Cortical bone perforation is seen in less
common multilocular and aggressive lesions.
Epidemiology
The mean age at diagnosis is 34.8 years, with an almost equal prevalence in men and women
{ 29311021 ; 32717216 }. Multifocal SOT tend to present in a younger age group and show marked
predilection for African Americans { 27632187 }.
Etiology
Unknown but a familial incidence has been reported { 2674829 ; 33246182 }.
Pathogenesis
A role for NOTCH receptors and their ligands in the cytodifferentiation of SOT is proposed
{ 20554499 } and mutations in the ameloblastin gene (AMBN) have been found { 15288841 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Most SOT are submitted as tan-gray or brown curettings of soft tissue.
Histopathology
SOT comprises variably sized and shaped islands of cytologically bland well-differentiated
squamous epithelium in a fibrous or myxoid stroma. The peripheral cells of the islands are flat to
cuboidal with very infrequent mitoses. The central cells have a tendency for cystic degeneration,
individual cell keratinization, and calcification. Mucous metaplasia, sebaceous differentiation and
ghost cell-like areas may be present as minor changes { 27632187 ; 32717216 }. SOT does not
exhibit peripheral palisading, reverse nuclear polarity, cellular atypia or pleomorphism.
Differential The entities should include acanthomatous and desmoplastic ameloblastoma, and
well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. SOT-like proliferation of epithelium may develop in
the lining of odontogenic cysts and is a close histological mimic of SOT { 285403 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: location in tooth bearing areas of jaw; closely packed islands of cytologically bland
epithelium; uniform squamous differentiation without significant keratinisation; no peripheral
palisading and stellate reticulum
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Unifocal SOT rarely recurs after conservative surgery. Multifocal or recurring SOT, especially of
the maxilla, may require a more radical approach.

602

#24358: Islands of benign, squamous epithelium in a mature fibrous stroma.

#24359: The peripheral cells are flattened, and central cells show areas of microcystic
degeneration and individual cell keratinization.

603

#24479: Proliferation of epithelium in the wall of an odontogenic cyst can be a close mimic for
squamous odontogenic tumour, but the context is not compatible with that diagnosis.

#24489: Proliferation of epithelium in the wall of an odontogenic cyst can be a close mimic for
squamous odontogenic tumour, but the context is not compatible with that diagnosis.

604

#24494: Well-differentiated, bland appearing islands of squamous epithelium in a fibromyxoid
stroma

Calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour
Definition
Calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour (CEOT) is a benign epithelial odontogenic tumour
characterised by amyloid, which may calcify.
ICD-O coding
9340/0 Calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH4PT4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour
2E83.1 & XH4PT4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour
Related terminology
Acceptable: Pindborg tumour
Subtype(s)
Clear cell calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour; cystic/microcystic calcifying epithelial
odontogenic tumour; non-calcifying / Langerhans cell–rich calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour
Localization
Tooth-bearing areas of the jaws, approximately 60% in the mandible with a predilection for the
body. Over 85% of CEOT arise centrally, the remainder at adjacent extraosseous
sites { 28601296 }.
Clinical features
Many CEOT are asymptomatic, larger examples cause slow growing localised expansion of the
jaw and tooth mobility.
Imaging: Radiographically, 75% of CEOT have mixed radiodensity, but the extent of calcification
increases with age. 30% of CEOT appear multilocular and around half have cortical perforation.
Cortication is variable. About half of cases are associated with an unerupted tooth. On CT scan,
the lesion has diffuse high attenuation. On MRI scan, the lesion is hyperintense on T1 weighted
images and hypointense in T2 weighted images { 10864713 }.
Epidemiology
Central lesions occur across a wide age range (8-83 years) with maximum incidence in the
4th decade and equal incidence in males and females { 28601296 }.
The clear cell variant arises in a slightly older patient population, with a female predominance.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis

605

Unknown. Mutations
in
tumour
suppressor
genes PTEN and CDKN2A and
oncogenes JAK3 and MET { 29127140 }, and in ameloblastin (AMBN) { 19661317 ; 24118390 }
and PTCH1 { 20371205 } have been reported in CEOT.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions are solid with variable calcification and, rarely, cystic spaces.
Histopathology
The histological features are variable. There are clear cell, cystic/microcystic and noncalcifying/Langerhans cell rich histological subtypes. Lesions with mixed CEOT and adenomatoid
odontogenic tumour (AOT) features should be classified as AOT subtypes.
Most CEOT comprise sheets, cords or nests of polyhedral epithelial cells with distinct cell borders
and prominent intercellular bridges. Nuclear pleomorphism may be present, and sometimes
extreme, but mitoses are very rare. Classical CEOT contain deposits of amorphous lightly
eosinophilic amyloid composed of ameloblast-associated proteins including Odontogenic
Ameloblast-associated protein (ODAM). This stains with congo red and is birefringent on
polarization. The amyloid may calcify forming large masses or small round concentric densely
basophilic calcifications with Liesegang rings. CEOT may extend into adjacent medullary spaces
in an infiltrative pattern worrying for malignancy. Foci resembling CEOT may be found in
odontomas and the follicles of unerupted teeth.
Subtypes: The clear cell subtype contains a variable proportion of cells with PAS-positive, diastase
labile glycogen accumulation. Stains for mucins are negative. Most also contain areas of more
conventional CEOT { 28601296 ; 32642935 }. Macro- and microcystic variants have been reported,
some with areas of focal pseudo glandular change in an otherwise conventional CEOT
{ 29164474 }. The natural history of these variants is not known.
The non-calcifying/Langerhans cell-rich subtype is an area of diagnostic uncertainty, with
morphological and molecular features suggesting it is a subtype of odontogenic fibroma rather than
CEOT { 30291007 }. Whilst the lack of calcification could reflect relative immaturity of the lesions,
this subtype appears to be distinct, as they often contain significant numbers of Langerhans cells.
Some cases have other features of CEOT focally but the majority consist of small, scattered islands
of epithelium in a collagenous and amyloid-rich background, resembling the amyloid-rich subtype
of odontogenic fibroma { 30291007 ; 33737014 }.
Cytology
Smears are paucicellular with scattered tissue fragments of mildly pleomorphic squamous cells
with abundant eosinophilic (Pap stain) amorphous material and often plentiful concentric
calcifications in a cystic or bloody background { 16334032 ; 17017443 }. Nuclear pleomorphism
may result in a misleading suggestion of a malignant neoplasm. The role of FNAB cytopathology
is contentious with some arguing it assists in distinguishing benign from malignant lesions and often
specific diagnoses in the jaw when working in concert with clinical/site and radiological findings.
{ 31951108 }
Diagnostic molecular pathology
EWSR1/ATF1 gene rearrangements may aid differentiation of the clear cell variant of CEOT from
clear cell odontogenic carcinoma { 23715163 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Radiology - unilocular (more common) or multilocular lesion, which may be radiolucent or of mixed
radiodensity.
Histological features - sheets, islands and cords of polyhedral cells with distinct cell borders, very
few or no mitoses; amyloid present.
Desirable: prominent intercellular bridges; nuclear and cellular pleomorphism
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
The recurrence rate is about 13% and varies with treatment modality, being much higher in those
treated by curettage { 28601296 }. No histological parameters predict recurrence. Malignant
transformation develops rarely { 33744097 }. The non-calcifying/Langerhans cell-rich subtype
behaves as odontogenic fibroma with minimal or no risk of recurrence { 30291007 }.

606

#24901

Islands of odontogenic epithelium, with amyloid and focal calcification.

mild nuclear pleomorphism.

#24903
