#27277
Calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour
Panoramic radiograph showing a calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour in the left body
and parasymphysis of the mandible, generally a well-defined radiolucency with some
small internal mineralisations.

608

Ameloblastoma, unicystic
Definition
Unicystic ameloblastoma (UAM) is an intraosseous ameloblastoma with a single cyst cavity.
ICD-O coding
9310/0 Ameloblastoma, unicystic
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Ameloblastoma, NOS
2E83.1 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Ameloblastoma, NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Luminal; Intra-luminal; Mural
Localization
Most
UAMs develop
in
the
posterior
body
of
mandible
and
ramus
{ 3150441 ; 9861335 ; 29858565 }.
Clinical features
UAM is usually an asymptomatic jaw swelling.
Imaging: Most UAM are unilocular, well-demarcated radiolucencies, often accompanied by
unerupted teeth, frequently mandibular third molars { 6587306 ; 9861335 }. Root resorption and
cortical bone perforation are often present { 11688034 }.
Epidemiology
UAM accounts for 5-22% of all ameloblastomas { 9861335 }. Patients with UAM associated with
impacted teeth have a mean age of 16 years; those without of 35 years { 9861335 }. Approximately
50% of cases are diagnosed in the second decade { 3150441 ; 29858565 }, slightly more frequently
in males.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
UAM is probably caused by dysregulated MAPK signaling pathways, with BRAF p.V600E the most
common activating mutation in all subtypes { 27084044 ; 30216733 }. SMO mutation activating the
Hedgehog pathway also occurs { 30216733 }.
Macroscopic appearance
UAM is a single cyst. Substantial extensions of the lining epithelium may fill the lumen.
Histopathology
Single cyst lined by epithelium with palisaded columnar basal layer with reverse polarity and stellate
reticulum-like upper layers in most of the lining constitutes the luminal subtype. Additional plexiform
epithelial masses may extend into the lumen only, constituting the intra-luminal type. Additional
islands of epithelium extending into the wall constitute the mural subtype { 9861335 }. Extensive
sampling is required for accurate definition of subtype. Similar but focal changes may be seen in
dentigerous and radicular cysts, especially in areas of inflammation.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
BRAF p.V600E immunohistochemistry and genetic testing have potential but are not
yet validated for diagnosis { 30216733 ; 33377543 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: single cyst; ameloblastoma-like epithelial lining
Desirable: detection of luminal and mural extension for subtype diagnosis
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Up to 30% of UAM may recur after enucleation { 16782308 }, as definitive diagnosis prior to initial
treatment is not possible. Conservative marsupialisation followed by enucleation is proposed for

609

luminal and intraluminal UAM but carries risk of recurrence, demanding long-term follow up
{ 12089689 ; 31562035 }.
Mural UAM appears to be intermediate between UAM and conventional ameloblastoma. A
diagnosis of mural UAM might require consideration of more extensive surgery as for conventional
ameloblastoma, depending on size, extent of intra-mural proliferation and radiological
findings { 12089689 ; 33403703 }. However, accurate diagnosis often only follows definitive
removal, allowing a period of follow up to confirm recurrence before more aggressive
treatment. BRAF p.V600E mutation confers a lower rate of recurrence than other MAPK-pathwayrelated mutations { 24993163 ; 29388014 ; 30216733 } and may be a better predictor than division
into unicystic and conventional types { 33403703 }. Mutation status of all UAM subtypes is
similar. Early data suggests BRAF-targeted therapy is effective in mutation-positive
UAM { 34599642 } and conventional ameloblastoma { 27209484 }.

sometimes called 'parachute cells' because of their shape are distinctive. Subepithelial
hyalinisation in a narrow band is a common finding.

#25157
Ameloblastoma, unicystic
The cyst lumen is lined by odontogenic epithelium with focal basal cell palisading. The
upper epithelial layers resemble stellate reticulum. Intra-luminal epithelial proliferation in a
plexiform pattern is present.

Ameloblastoma, extraosseous/peripheral
Definition
Extraosseous ameloblastoma (EA) is the soft tissue counterpart of intraosseous ameloblastoma.
ICD-O coding
9310/0 Ameloblastoma, extraosseous/peripheral
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Ameloblastoma, NOS
2E83.1 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Ameloblastoma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: soft tissue ameloblastoma, ameloblastoma of mucosal origin, ameloblastoma
of the gingiva
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
EA occurs only in soft tissues overlying tooth-bearing areas or edentulous ridges of the jaws, with
a mandible:maxilla ratio of 1.6:1 to 3.3:1 { 11120479 ; 8683419 ; 3295656 ; 16827840 }. The most
frequent subsites
are
the mandibular
premolar
and
maxillary
molar
regions
{ 3295656 ; 8683419 ; 11120479 ; 16827840 ; 29680843 }; the lingual aspect of the alveolar ridge
is often involved.
Clinical features
EA is an asymptomatic, slow-growing, firm epulis with mean diameter < 20 mm
{ 11120479 ; 29680843 }.
Imaging: On imaging, superficial erosion of the underlying bone may be seen, but a central
component precludes a diagnosis of EA.
Epidemiology
EA accounts for up to 10% of all ameloblastomas { 11120479 }. The mean patient age is 52 years
(range 9 to 92 years), with 64% of cases occurring during the fifth to seventh decades { 11120479 }.

612

The male-to-female ratio is 1.4:1 { 11120479 }; however, a greater male predilection has been
reported in some Asian series { 11120479 ; 29680843 }.
Etiology
Like other ameloblastomas, EA is probably caused by dysregulated MAPK signaling pathways.
Pathogenesis
BRAF p.V600E mutation has been reported in 10 of 16 peripheral ameloblastoma cases
{ 28658279 ; 29388014 ; 30811720 ; 33231757 ; 33827932 }, with NRAS p.Q61R occurring as an
alternative in 1 of 3 cases { 28658279 }.
Macroscopic appearance
A firm to somewhat spongy mucosal mass, solid or with tiny cystic spaces
{ 11120479 ; 29680843 }.
Histopathology
Histopathological features and growth patterns are as conventional ameloblastoma { 11120479 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Ber-EP4 expression on immunohistochemistry may aid in distinguishing
intraoral basal cell carcinoma from EA { 29247621 }.
Differential The differential diagnosis includes peripheral odontogenic fibroma, peripheral
squamous odontogenic tumour, odontogenic gingival epithelial hamartoma, and intraoral basal cell
carcinoma { 11120479 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Site in gingiva or edentulous alveolar mucosa; no intraosseous component;
histopathologic features as conventional
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
EA has a more indolent behaviour than conventional ameloblastoma { 11120479 }. Recurrence
rates after conservative supraperiosteal excision range from 9% to 20%, although some may result
from incomplete removal { 33162201 } and long-term follow-up is recommended. Malignant
transformation is exceedingly rare { 8426721 }.

#23287
Extraosseous ameloblastoma
Mucosa with ameloblastic tumour islands within the lamina propria, characterised by
hyperchromatic peripheral basal cells and looser epithelium centrally.

613

#23981

Extraosseous ameloblastoma
A nodule on the gingiva, without radiographic evidence of underlying bone involvement.

#24130

Extraosseous ameloblastoma
Gingival mucosa with peripheral ameloblastic tumor islands in the lamina propria.

614

Ameloblastoma, conventional
Definition
Ameloblastoma (AM) is a benign but locally infiltrative epithelial odontogenic neoplasm of the
jawbones characterised by ameloblast-like cells and stellate reticulum.
ICD-O coding
9310/0 Ameloblastoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Ameloblastoma, NOS
2E83.1 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Ameloblastoma, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: solid/multicystic ameloblastoma
Not recommended: classic intraosseous ameloblastoma
Subtype(s)
Follicular; plexiform; acanthomatous; granular cell; basal cell; desmoplastic
Localization
Up to 87% of AM arise in the mandible, predominantly in the posterior molar area
{ 30614154 ; 33403703 }. Maxillary AMs are usually posteriorly sited. The exception is the
desmoplastic subtype, which has an almost equal jaw distribution and a preponderance for the
anterior regions { 31810564 }. Extremely rarely, AMs are also found in the sino-nasal region
{ 29846904 }.
Clinical features
AM presents as a painless, slow-growing mass that, if untreated, reaches a large size, displaces
and loosens teeth, expands and perforates the cortices, may cause paraesthesia and ultimately
causes disfigurement and risks adjacent vital structures { 28142213 ; 26505234 }.
Imaging: On two-dimensional radiographs, AM usually presents as a multilocular radiolucency
showing soap-bubble or honeycomb patterns (72%) and well-defined corticated margins (54%)
{ 33403703 }. Three-dimensional imaging (preferably contrast-enhanced computed tomography)
reveals internal bony architecture, content, size, expansion and cortical perforation more accurately
{ 26166034 ; 29791200 }. Impacted teeth are associated with 18% of AM { 33403703 }.
Bone formation in desmoplastic AMs may producing a fine honeycomb mixed radiolucent
appearance resembling a fibro-osseous lesion { 31810564 }.
Epidemiology
Excluding odontomas, AM is the most common odontogenic neoplasm in all ethnic groups,
representing approximately 1% of all head and neck neoplasms in Europe and the USA, probably
with the highest incidence in African and Afro-Caribbean populations { 21781186 }. The incidence
of AM is approximately 0.5 new cases/1,000,000 population worldwide and there is no gender
predilection. The peak incidence of diagnosis is in the fourth and fifth decades of life, with a patient
age range of 8–92 years { 16916667 ; 20549076 ; 22157674 }. The diagnosis is generally made at
a somewhat lower age in women, especially in populations of African heritage { 25350592 }. For
BRAF p.V600E-mutant cases, the reported mean patient age at diagnosis is about 34 years,
compared with about 54 years for BRAF wild-type cases { 24993163 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
AM is caused by dysregulated MAPK and Hedgehog signaling pathways, probably in SOX2positive
dental
epithelial
stem
cells/ameloblast
progenitor
cells
{ 20978072 ; 20978073 ; 24057668 ; 24148099 ; 25398365 }. BRAF p.V600E mutation is the most
common activating mutation in the MAPK pathway, followed by mutually exclusive mutations in
RAS genes (KRAS, NRAS, HRAS) and FGFR2 { 24374844 ; 24859340 ; 24993163 ; 30216733 }.
Dysregulation of the Hedgehog signaling pathway may participate, especially in maxillary AMs,
where mutatiom of SMO is the most frequent mutation to co-occur with the MAPK pathway

615

mutation { 24859340 ; 24993163 }. Epigenetic factors are largely unknown, but mutational
signatures in mandibular AM appear to correlate with smoking { 30917298 }.
Macroscopic appearance
AM range from solid and yellowish-white to multicystic tumours with little intervening solid tissue.
{ 4812754 }. AM may extend into adjacent cancellous bone.
Histopathology
In the commonest histological subtype, the follicular subtype, there are islands of epithelium
resembling the epithelial component of the enamel organ in a fibrous stroma. Peripheral cells are
columnar-to-cuboidal (ameloblast-like), with hyperchromatic nuclei arranged in a palisading pattern
with reverse polarity, and often subnuclear vacuolation { 5458275 ; 30036443 }. The central
epithelium is reminiscent of stellate reticulum, with loosely arranged angular cells and often
undergoes cystic change. The second commonest subtype is the plexiform, composed of
anastomosing strands of ameloblastomatous epithelium with an inconspicuous stellate reticulum,
less prominent ameloblast-like cells and cyst-like degeneration in the stroma rather than the
epithelium. Mitoses are usually scattered but posterior maxillary ameloblastoma may have high
cellularity and frequent mitoses.
Several other subtypes are recognised { 22157674 }. The acanthomatous subtype has squamous
differentiation centrally in islands but maintains the reverse polarization of the nuclei in peripheral
columnar cells. In the granular cell subtype the central epithelium develops abundant eosinophilic
granular cytoplasm. The basal cell subtype comprises islands and strands of basaloid cells with
scanty cytoplasm and peripheral palisading, reminiscent of basal cell carcinoma { 27272180 }. The
desmoplastic subtype has more widely dispersed islands with a spiky outline, cuboidal to flat
peripheral cells and central spindle shaped cells in a densely collagenous stroma, sometimes
with osteoplasia. Many AM contain more than one histological subtypes { 12570061 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
BRAF p.V600E mutation immunohistochemistry and genetic testing have potential but are not
yet validated for diagnosis { 24993163 ; 30216733 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: location in jaws; islands/strands of odontogenic epithelium bounded by
cuboidal/columnar cells with palisaded, hyperchromatic nuclei; reverse polarity (less marked in
plexiform pattern); loose central epithelium resembling stellate reticulum; no cytological atypia
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
AM recurs if inadequately removed. The standard of care is complete excision with negative
margins { 30036443 }, irrespective of the histopathological subtype { 19297131 }. This requires
removal of a bone margin of at least 10mm beyond the radiographic margin to ensure removal of
AM permeating medullary bone, usually a segmental resection, mandibulectomy or maxillectomy,
depending on size { 27272180 }. More conservative surgery has a high recurrence rate (60–80%)
and long follow-up (1-2 decades) is mandatory { 7633291 }. Mandibular tumours are more likely
to harbour BRAF p.V600E mutation and the mutation is associated with later recurrence than wild
type AM { 24374844 ; 24859340 ; 24993163 ; 30216733 }. Furthermore, single versus multiple
mutations and geographic regions also seem to stratify patients for recurrence risk { 29388014 }.
BRAF inhibitor treatment has been proposed, alone or in combination with MAPK/ERK kinase
(MEK) inhibitors { 24749150 ; 25425580 ; 33231757 ; 33737992 }. Early data shows effectiveness
in selected cases { 34599642 } and in disseminated AM { 25475564 }.

616

#24390
Ameloblastoma, conventional
Plexiform histological pattern with anastomosing cords and strands of tumour.

#24395
Ameloblastoma, conventional
The most commonly identified and relative frequency of oncogenic mutations in
ameloblastoma (AM) and ameloblastoma, unicystic (UAM) located in the mandible or
maxilla {24374844; 24993163; 24859340; 30216733}

#27487
Ameloblastoma, conventional
Mixed patterns of larger rounded pale staining follicular pattern, focally acanthomatous,
and the spiky darker staining islands with more cellular central areas of the desmoplastic
histological pattern.

#27488

Large ameloblastoma of the mandible causing facial deformity.

#27529

Panoramic radiograph of an ameloblastoma forming a typical radiolucent, multilocular,
soap-bubble pattern lesion with cortical expansion, tooth displacement and root
resorption.

#27530

3D CT reconstruction highlights expansion and multilocularity of the tumour.

#27531
Ameloblastoma, conventional
Positive immunohistochemical staining for V600E-mutant BRAF in ameloblastoma
epithelium; staining is cytoplasmic.

#27532

Ameloblastoma of follicular pattern, with acanthomatous change in the large cyst.
Peripheral epithelial cells in the islands have hyperchromatic nuclei in a palisading

#27799
Ameloblastoma, conventional
High power of follicular pattern showing peripheral ameloblast-like cells with palisaded
nuclei, reverse polarity and cytoplasmic vacuolation, and looser stellate reticulum-like
cells in the centre of the follicles.

Adenoid ameloblastoma
Definition
Adenoid ameloblastoma (AA) is an epithelial odontogenic neoplasm characterized by cribriform
architecture and duct-like structures. Dentinoid is often present.
ICD-O coding
9300/0 Adenoid ameloblastoma
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Ameloblastoma,
NOS
2E83.1 & XH1SV4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Ameloblastoma, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: Adenoid ameloblastoma with dentinoid
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
No site predilection defined
Clinical features
Usually a painless swelling, mean diameter 40 mm { 26297394 }, sometimes with pain and
paraesthesia { 31925581 }.
Imaging: Most AA are radiolucent and unilocular with well-defined boundaries, but multilocularity,
internal mineralisation, cortical perforation, root resorption and paranasal sinus involvement may
occur { 29799420 } [[Sonone A, Hande A, Chaudhary M, Bonde R, Sheorain A, Agni N.
doi:10.1111/j.1752-248X.2010.01109.x, Journal: Oral Surgery (ISSN:1752-2471), Volume 4 pages
77-81, 2011]].
Epidemiology
621

Incidence peaks in the 4th decade with slight male predilection (M:F=1.3:1) but with wide age range
of 15-82 years { 34282559 }. Most AA have occurred in South America and Asia.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
BRAF p.V600E mutation, typical of other ameloblastomas, appears absent based on limited data
[[E. A. Bilodeau and R. R. Seethala. Adenoid ameloblastoma: a series of 5
cases. DOI:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oooo.2019.02.196, Journal: Oral Surg Oral Med Oral Pathol
Oral Radiol Endod. Volume 128, page e78, 2019]]; { 34549835 }, as are the the KRAS p.G12V and
KRAS p.G12R mutations typical of adenomatoid odontogenic tumour { 34549835 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Predominantly multicystic with solid zones and variable hard tissue.
Histopathology
AA is characterized by a partly cribriform arrangement of basal ameloblast-like cells demonstrating
reversed nuclear polarity and a minor component of suprabasal stellate reticulum-like epithelium.
Basal cells may be multi-layered with transition to a round or ovoid morphology. Distinctive features
are duct-like structures formed by cuboidal to columnar cells, some of which contain mucin,
and focal whorled cellular condensations reminiscent of morules. Two-thirds of cases contain
varying amounts of dentinoid. Clear cells are often closely associated with dentinoid and ghost-cell
keratinization may be a minor feature.
Immunohistochemistry: There is variable staining for CK14, CK19, p40, p16 and p53. CK7 is weak
to negative { 29799420 }. Nuclear β-catenin positivity co-localises with morules and Ki-67 indices
are usually high { 26297394 ; 29449727 } [[E. A. Bilodeau and R. R. Seethala. Adenoid
ameloblastoma: a series of 5 cases. DOI:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.oooo.2019.02.196, Journal: Oral
Surg Oral Med Oral Pathol Oral Radiol Endod. Volume 128, page e78, 2019]].
Differential There are overlapping histomorphologic features with adenomatoid odontogenic
tumour and dentinogenic ghost cell tumour but AA is distinguished by the combination of cribriform
pattern, duct-like structures, whorls/morules and dentinoid. Lack of EWSR1 rearrangement aids
exclusion of clear cell odontogenic carcinoma when clear cells are prominent. Criteria to distinguish
aggressive AA from odontogenic carcinoma with dentinoid are not yet defined { 26297394 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Ameloblastoma-like component; duct-like structures; whorls/morules; cribriform
architecture.
Desirable: Dentinoid, clear cells, focal ghost-cell keratinisation.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
AA
is
locally
infiltrative
with
a
recurrence
rate
of
up
to
70%
{ 15529131 ; 20596984 ; 22923903 ; 26297394 ; 34282559 }. Cytological atypia, hypercellularity,
p53 positivity, and high Ki-67 counts are associated with recurrence but the borderline with
carcinoma { 25409850 } is not yet defined.

Characteristic cribriform architecture with pseudocysts, duct-like structures and 'whorls'
or morules.

dentinoid are present.

Metastasizing ameloblastoma
Definition
Metastasizing ameloblastoma (MA) is an ameloblastoma that has metastasized despite benign
histopathological appearance.
ICD-O coding
9310/3 Ameloblastoma, metastasizing
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH96J9 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Ameloblastoma, metastasizing
2E83.1 & XH96J9 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Ameloblastoma, metastasizing
Related terminology
Acceptable: benign metastasizing ameloblastoma
Not
recommended: malignant
ameloblastoma;
malignant
adamantinoma; metastatic
ameloblastoma; atypical ameloblastoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The primary tumour develops more frequently in the mandible than in the maxilla
{ 32659412 ; 33394372 }. The most common sites of metastasis are the lungs followed by cervical
lymph nodes { 33394372 }, with distant metastasis in approximately 68% of MAs { 32659412 }.
625

Infrequently reported metastatic sites include bone, brain, liver, and pericardium
{ 15891309 ; 24152624 ; 29747056 ; 30775320 ; 33394372 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms vary by site; some patients are asymptomatic. Pulmonary metastases may cause dry
cough, hemoptysis, and dyspnea { 28944145 ; 32132332 }. Metastasis may be diagnosed
concurrently with the primary tumour or after a variable, often long, latent period (range 0 to 45
years; mean 11 years) { 20970910 ; 33394372 }. Metastases retain the benign ameloblastoma
growth pattern.
Epidemiology
Incidence data is only available combined with ameloblastic carcinoma, which is more common,
with combined annual cumulative incidence of 1.79 per 10 million persons per year { 25692490 }.
MA has a mean age at diagnosis of 45 years and a slight male predominance
{ 32659412 ; 33394372 }.
Etiology
MA harbours BRAF p.V600E mutation as conventional ameloblastoma { 25475564 }. Risk factors
for metastasis include large size, rapid enlargement, protracted clinical course, inadequate
removal, and multiple recurrences at the primary site { 32659412 }.
Pathogenesis
Metastasis may result from haematogenous spread during surgical treatment of the primary
{ 32659412 }. Alternatively, metastasis could be driven by additional genetic alterations without
histopathologic changes { 32659412 } but no putative molecular markers of metastatic behavior
have been identified { 31169204 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Features are identical to those of conventional ameloblastoma.
Histopathology
MA exhibits identical histopathological features to conventional ameloblastoma, with no specific
features predicting metastasis. Among reported MA cases, the plexiform histological pattern is most
common { 20970910 ; 33394372 }.
Cytology
Hypercellular smears show tissue fragments of small basaloid cells with peripheral palisading, and
distinct larger cells with larger cells with more open chromatin, similar to ameloblastoma. Nuclei
tend to be larger and more pleomorphic, with a tendency to molding and a high mitotic rate. There
may be occasional stromal fragments with spindle cells with elongated nuclei and abundant clear
cytoplasm { 9250285 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Benign conventional ameloblastoma in primary tumour and metastasis; no cytological
atypia or features of malignancy in primary tumour and metastasis.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
MA has overall mean survival of 5 years on systematic review { 33394372 }. Surgery is the
mainstay of treatment; radiotherapy and chemotherapy have no proven benefit
{ 21459020 ; 32659412 }. BRAF-targeted therapy has been reported in limited cases
{ 25475564 ; 31340860 }. Recurrence occurs in about one-fourth of cases and is associated with
increased mortality { 32659412 }.

626

#24139
Metastasizing ameloblastoma
Brain metastasis from a maxillary
ameloblastoma.

Benign mixed epithelial &
mesenchymal odontogenic tumours

Odontoma
Definition
Odontomas are mixed odontogenic hamartomas that mature from soft tissue
to predominantly dental hard tissues with a small amount of residual odontogenic epithelium
and ectomesenchyme.
ICD-O coding
9280/0 Odontoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face & Odontoma, NOS
2E83.1 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw & Odontoma, NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Complex odontoma (CxO); Compound odontoma (CdO)
Localization
CxOs develop mainly in the posterior body of the mandible, CdOs in the anterior maxilla
{ 21840103 ; 27678223 }.
Clinical features
Asymptomatic slow-growing lesions, often associated with unerupted teeth. The diameter is
typically 10-30 mm but larger lesions expand the jaws, displace and resorb teeth and prevent their
eruption. Odontomas as large as 80 mm in diameter can be encountered { 15663443 ; 23479585 }.
Odontomas occasionally erupt or suffer trauma and may become infected. Odontomas may be
multiple.
Imaging: Radiographically, developing odontomas are predominantly radiolucent with a corticated
periphery, sometimes with small radiopacities. Small lesions resemble a developing tooth crypt.
With maturation, CxO slowly develops irregular disorganised masses of calcified material of
enamel and dentine radiodensity. CdO develops into tooth-like structures of varying size and
shape. Mixed patterns may occur. Both types retain a narrow peripheral radiolucent zone with a
smooth or lobular outline.
Epidemiology
Among referral series of odontogenic tumours, odontoma is the second most common lesion,
frequently occurring in the second and third decades, without distinct gender predilection
{ 21840103 ; 22439972 ; 30448231 ; 32684315 }, but many small odontomas are treated outside
hospital or left untreated.
Etiology
Unknown

627

Genetic factors: Multiple odontomas arise in odontoma-dysphagia (MIM #164330),
Schimmelpenning-Feuerstein-Mims
(MIM #163200),
familial
adenomatous
polyposis
(MIM #175100) and encephalocraniocutaneous lipomatosis (MIM #613001) syndromes.
Pathogenesis
WNT/β-catenin pathway activation in embryonic SOX-2 positive dental stem cells can drive
odontoma formation { 26411543 ; 30862786 }.
Odontoma does not harbour BRAF p.V600E mutation { 32966885 ; 32939809 } unlike ameloblastic
fibroma { 24993163 ; 26306423 ; 32939809 ; 32966885 } and histopathologically similar lesions,
previously classified as ameloblastic fibro-odontoma (AFO){ 26306423 ; 32939809 ; 32966885 }
and ameloblastic fibro-dentinoma (AFD){ 24993163 ; 32966885 }.
Macroscopic appearance
CxO comprises amorphous masses of dentine and enamel, CdO comprises multiple tooth-like
structures of varying size. Both may be surrounded by capsule-like soft tissue with a smooth
surface.
Histopathology
CxO consists of a disorganized mass of mature tubular dentine intermixed with rounded zones of
enamel matrix where enamel has been lost on decalcification, with areas of dental pulp and
cementum. CdO contains multiple rudimentary teeth exhibiting dentin and enamel matrix. Dentine
in odontomas usually shows some irregularity of tubule structure. A variable amount of loose
connective tissue and capsule may be present resembling dental papilla or follicle. These may
contain varying amounts of immature odontogenic epithelium in islands and strands
{ 21840103 ; 27678223 }. These epithelial residues may be prominent and may contain ghost cells,
granular cells and other features resembling other odontogenic tumours, but these are
hamartomatous and of no significance.
Developing odontomas are initially soft tissue, mostly dental papilla-like with prominent epithelial
strands and there may be limited or no evidence of dental hard tissue induction. They resemble
ameloblastic fibroma closely and may be difficult to differentiate histologically. Lesions previously
diagnosed as AFD and AFO also resemble ameloblastic fibroma with less prominent and less well
organised induction of dental hard tissue matrix or hard tissue than in
odontomas. Radiographic appearance, patient age and lesion size may help distinguish these
lesions { 33991318 }.
AFD and AFO were previously classified as developing odontomas. However, a proportion of both
AFD { 24993163 ; 32966885 } and fewer AFO { 26306423 ; 32939809 ; 32966885 } harbour BRAF
p.V600E mutation suggesting a relationship with ameloblastic fibroma, rather than with odontomas,
which lack this mutation. Histological and molecular overlap make it unclear whether AFD and AFO
are separate entities, intermediate lesions with a spectrum of behaviour that ultimately result in
formation of odontoma, or are a mixture of developing odontomas and ameloblastic fibromas.
Odontomas are occasionally the origin of cysts of dentigerous type.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Complex odontoma
Essential: consistent radiographic features; conglomerate mass of enamel and dentin.
Desirable: dental pulp, cementum
Compound odontoma
Essential: consistent radiographic features; multiple, small tooth-like structures
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Odontomas do not recur after enucleation, though recurrence may follow incomplete removal of
growing lesions { 15663443 }.

628

Microscopic section showing radiating sheets of dentin enclosing spaces containing

#25158

Sheets of dentin enclosing lacunae of enamel matrix. The key-hole and radiating space

#28259

632

Primordial odontogenic tumour
Definition
Primordial odontogenic tumour (POT) is composed of variably cellular fibrous tissue with areas
similar to dental papilla, surrounded by epithelium resembling the internal epithelium of the enamel
organ.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E83.1 & XH43L1 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Odontogenic tumour, benign
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
POT arises in the tooth-bearing segments of the jaw, between two and three times more frequently
in mandible than maxilla.
Clinical features
Most cases are asymptomatic. Some POTs produce marked cortical expansion.
Imaging: Well-defined radiolucency associated with unerupted teeth, most commonly the
mandibular third molar, producing an apparent pericoronal radiolucency. POT may displace and
resorb roots of adjacent teeth { 32040459 }.
Epidemiology
The age range is 2-19 years (mean: 11.4 years) with a slight male predilection
{ 32040459 ; 32593436 ; 32981867 ; 33079412 ; 33433252 }.
Etiology
Unknown. No mutations were identified in 151 cancer- and 42 odontogenesis-associated genes in
3 POT by next-generation sequencing { 29908099 }.
Pathogenesis
The expression profile of dentinogenesis-associated genes suggests inhibition of dentin formation.
No BRAF p.V600E mutations were found { 29908099 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Solid, well-circumscribed sometimes multilobulated whitish mass. The crown of a tooth may be
embedded, but is easily detached { 24807692 }.
Histopathology
POT comprises loose or myxoid fibrous tissue containing variable numbers of fusiform and stellate
fibroblasts and areas with the appearance of cellular odontogenic mesenchyme. The periphery is
covered by a single layer of columnar or cuboidal epithelium, with scant superficial layers of stellate
reticulum-like cells partially enclosed by a thin fibrous capsule. Epithelial cords may appear to be
within the lesion as a result of lobular infolding. Extracellular concentric mineralized structures
within the epithelium have been reported, but there is no evidence of odontoblastic differentiation
or mature dentine.
Immunohistochemistry: Mesenchymal cells are positive for vimentin and subepithelial expression
of CD34 and CD138 have been demonstrated in several cases. Ki67 index is low (<5%). The
epithelial component is positive for pan-cytokeratins (AE1/AE3), CK5 and CK14, whereas
cytokeratin 19 is usually expressed by columnar cells. Amelogenin, ameloblastin and dentin
sialophosphoprotein (DSPP) have also been detected. Other markers are variably expressed
{ 24807692 ; 28390134 ; 28543986 ; 29090496 ; 29908099 }.
Differential POT resembles a mesenchymal proliferation very similar to the dental papilla of a
developing tooth.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology

633

Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: mass of myxoid dental papilla-like tissue; entire periphery covered by columnar or
cuboidal enamel epithelium
Desirable: focal stellate reticulum-like cells externally; lobular clefted surface
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
POT
does
not
recur
after
conservative
excision { 32040459 ; 32593436 ; 32981867 ; 33079412 ; 33433252 }.

#26560

Well-defined unilocular radiolucency surrounding and displacing the second premolar and
producing slight root resorption of first permanent molar.
.

Primordial odontogenic tumour
Tumour composed of variably cellular fibrous tissue, covered at the periphery by cuboidal
to columnar epithelium with scant superficial stellate reticulum-like cells.

Ameloblastic fibroma
Definition
Ameloblastic fibroma (AF) is a rare, benign, mixed odontogenic tumour comprising cellular
mesenchymal tissue resembling dental papilla and an epithelial component resembling early
developing enamel organ, without dental hard tissue or matrix.
ICD-O coding
9330/0 Ameloblastic fibroma
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH06Y3 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Ameloblastic fibroma
2E83.1 & XH06Y3 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Ameloblastic fibroma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
AFs are 3 times more frequent in the mandible than maxilla { 24055148 ; 28776760 }. Posterior
mandible is the most common location, the incisors-canine region being infrequent in both jaws,
though large lesions (~10%) can occupy up to a jawbone quadrant.
Clinical features
Most AFs are slow-growing and eventually cause asymptomatic jawbone expansion (78%). Rare
cases range from an incidental radiological finding to large enough to cause facial deformity
{ 24055148 ; 28776760 }.
Imaging: Radiographically, AFs are well-defined and corticated, unilocular (60%) lesions with mean
diameter 50 mm. Larger lesions are multilocular. AFs are usually associated with unerupted and
displaced teeth (80%), most frequently the first or second permanent molars. Cortical perforation
and tooth root resorption occur in a minority (22%) { 28776760 }.

636

Epidemiology
AF accounts for up to 2% of all odontogenic tumours { 22082131 ; 23766099 }. The mean patient
age is 15 years (range 7 weeks to 61 years) { 24055148 ; 28776760 }, with 80% in patients younger
than 22 years { 24055148 }. The male-to-female ratio is 1.4:1.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
AF has been considered a neoplasm of both epithelial and mesenchymal components. AF harbours
BRAF p.V600E mutation in 46% of cases { 24993163 ; 26306423 ; 32939809 ; 32966885 }, usually
limited to the mesenchymal component and only occasionally in both components { 32966885 }.
This mutation is also detected in cases previously classified as ameloblastic fibro-odontoma (AFO)
(34%) { 26306423 ; 32939809 ; 32966885 } and ameloblastic fibro-dentinoma(AFD)(60%)
{ 24993163 ; 32966885 } suggesting that at least some may be related to AF or other BRAFmutated odontogenic tumours. While mutations at other oncogenes and tumour suppressor genes
have not been detected { 32966885 }, AF shows allelic loss at 17p13 { 22082131 }.
Macroscopic appearance
A solid tumour, well circumscribed slightly myxoid mass with a smooth outer surface.
Histopathology
The mesenchymal component is myxoid and evenly hypercellular and resembles the dental papilla
of the tooth bud. The epithelial component forms long narrow cords and strands of bilaminar
cuboidal to columnar palisaded cells with occasional thickenings. These may contain a minor
component of stellate reticulum-like tissue, resembling the follicular stage of the enamel organ. A
collagenous capsule may be present. No dental hard tissue is normally present but extensive
sampling may reveal small foci.
Based on histopathological features alone, it may not possible to distinguish between the
neoplastic AF and early-stage hamartomatous odontomas before they differentiate and mature
{ 24055148 ; 23001451 }. In general, AF lacks architectural organisation and is more evenly
and densely hypercellular.
Lesions previously diagnosed as AFD and AFO are histologically intermediate between AF and
odontomas and have previously been classified as developing stages of odontoma. The status of
these tumours has been debated for decades and the current classification as odontoma is not
consistent with BRAF p.V600E mutation, occasional cases with locally aggressive biological
behaviour, large size and recurrence that appear neoplastic { 27563600 }. AFD and AFO resemble
AF but with additional induction of dental hard tissue matrix (AFD) or dentine and enamel (AFO). It
is unclear whether these intermediate lesions are a mixture of odontomas and AFs with hard tissue
induction, or form a spectrum of behaviour and currently this cannot be resolved histopathologically.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Diagnostic relevance of BRAF mutation is not yet defined, but would appear useful to exclude
developing odontoma in selected cases.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Consistent radiographic features; bland hypercellular, dental papilla-like mesenchyme;
dispersed bilaminar strands of cuboidal or columnar odontogenic epithelium
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
AF does not recur after meticulous enucleation, applied to small, asymptomatic tumours in young
patients { 9231165 ; 24055148 }. Recurrence occurs in 19% of cases with more conservative
removal { 28776760 }. Extensive, destructive and recurrent tumours should be treated
radically. BRAF-targeted inhibitors for selected cases are not yet assessed. Long term follow-up
is required.
Sarcomatous transformation is rare, but 24% of ameloblastic fibrosarcoma arise in benign AF or
recurrent AF { 28776760 }. Carcinosarcoma develops even more rarely { 17395065 }.

637

#24382
Ameloblastic fibroma
Ameloblast-like epithelial cells with tall, hyperchromatic, palisading nuclei with reverse
polarity arranged in bi-layered, parallel strands in a hypercellular stroma that resembles
dental papilla.

#24383
Ameloblastic fibroma
Some of the epithelial strands widen into rounded expansions, the larger of which may
contain a small amount of loose stellate reticulum-like epithelium. Occasionally, if these
enlargements are wide enough, they resemble an early enamel organ.

#27281

Typical ameloblastic fibroma showing numerous mostly bilaminar strands of odontogenic

Dentinogenic ghost cell tumour
Definition
Dentinogenic ghost cell tumour (DGCT) is a benign but locally infiltrative odontogenic tumour
characterized by ameloblastoma-like sheets and islands of epithelium with prominent ghost cell
keratinization and varying amounts of dentinoid in the stroma. Both the ghost cells and dentinoid
may mineralise.
ICD-O coding
9302/0 Dentinogenic ghost cell tumour
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH12N4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Dentinogenic ghost cell tumour
2E83.1 & XH12N4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Dentinogenic ghost cell tumour
Related terminology
Not recommended: calcifying ghost cell odontogenic tumour; (epithelial) odontogenic ghost cell
tumour; dentinoameloblastoma; solid/neoplastic variant of calcifying odontogenic cyst
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
DGCT arises with almost equal incidence in the mandible and maxilla, with a predilection for the
posterior region in both jaws { 26341683 ; 29738629 }. Up to 25% of all DGCTs are peripheral,
located in the gingiva or alveolar ridge mucosa { 18221328 ; 27669959 ; 29738629 }.
Clinical features
DGCT presents as a slow growing tumour with cortical expansion and occasional cortical
perforation { 26341683 ; 29738629 }. Mean diameter at presentation is 40 mm, but occasional

640

DGCT reach 100 mm and cause gross facial deformity { 29753697 }. Mild pain can be
present. Peripheral DGCT presents as a gingival nodule.
Imaging: Radiographically, the majority of DGCT are unilocular, well-defined, mixed radiolucent
tumours, about a quarter appear multilocular and about one-third have focally irregular or poorly
defined borders { 26341683 ; 29738629 }. Tooth displacement and root resorption are frequent in
larger tumours. A few cases have been reported in association with odontoma
{ 18221328 ; 26341683 }.
Epidemiology
DGCT is rare, and the second most common of the ghost cell-rich odontogenic tumours, all of
which have a predilection for Asian males { 18221328 ; 29738629 }. In Asia, DGCT comprised
0.3%-0.5% of all odontogenic tumours { 17156974 ; 22439972 }. Males are almost twice as
frequently affected as females { 26341683 ; 29738629 }. Mean age at presentation is between 40
and 47 years (range > 1 year – 84 years) and most cases are diagnosed between the 3rd and
5th decades { 26341683 ; 29738629 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
BRAF p.V600E mutation, found in other ameloblastoma-like odontogenic tumours, is absent
from DGCT { 31987674 }. CTNNB1 gene (beta-catenin) has been associated with calcifying
odontogenic cysts and ghost cell keratinisation but the status of DGCT is
unclear { 17178501 ; 29575443 }.
Macroscopic appearance
DGCT are solid and friable with variable amounts of mineralisation, focal areas of hemorrhage and
occasional small cysts. Cortical plates may be perforated.
Histopathology
DGCT are composed of nests, islands and sheets of odontogenic epithelium
resembling conventional ameloblastoma, with cuboidal to columnar hyperchromatic basal cells that
have reverse nuclear polarity at least focally { 26341683 }. Tissue resembling stellate reticulum
usually lies centrally in islands and may undergo cystic degeneration. Frequently, nests or “eddies”
of basaloid cells are observed within the cellular areas, and occasional aggregates of clear cells
may be present. DGCT is characterised by epithelial cells with aberrant ghost cell keratinization,
forming large rounded or stacked flattened cells with homogeneous, pale, eosinophilic cytoplasm
with a pale or clear central hole where the nucleus is missing. The number of ghost cells is variable,
ranging from scattered individual cells to small clusters and to large masses but they are a
prominent feature and ghost cells can replace the epithelial islands entirely. Ghost cells often
undergo mineralisation. Clusters of ghost cells frequently pass into the fibrous connective tissue
where
they
may
induce
a
foreign
body
reaction.
Dentinoid material
is
frequent and characteristic, especially
in
close
association
with
the
epithelial
component { 30911844 }. The dentinoid forms irregular eosinophilic masses that often entrap
individual or groups of epithelial cells and ghost cells. Mineralisation of dentinoid is frequent and
may be followed by remodelling to produce a mixed dentinoid and bone-like
appearance. Mineralisation of dentinoid, and to a lesser degree that of the ghost cells, define the
radiological features of the tumour. The relative amounts of ameloblastomatous epithelium, ghost
cells and dentinoid material vary from tumour to tumour. In general, mitotic figures are scarce, but
cellular pleomorphism can be present, however, it should not be interpreted as evidence of
malignant transformation. The proliferation fraction on Ki-67 immunohistochemistry ranges
from 2% to 29%.
Immunohistochemistry: Immunohistochemical stains with cytokeratin CK14 and CK19 highlight the
epithelial component { 30911844 }.
Differential This includes calcifying odontogenic cyst, adenoid ameloblastoma, ghost cell
carcinoma and the poorly defined odontogenic carcinoma with dentinoid { 25409850 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant

641

Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: solid tumour; conventional ameloblastoma-like epithelium; ghost cells; dentinoid
Desirable: scarce mitotic figures; mild cellular pleomorphism possible in a minority
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
DGCT has a recurrence rate of up to 73% following conservative surgery (enucleation or curettage)
and 33% following radical surgery (marginal or segmental resection), occurring from 1 to 20 years
after treatment. Long-term follow-up is recommended. It is unclear whether multiple recurrences
might be associated with malignant transformation in view of other histologically similar entities in
the differential diagnosis.
Peripheral DGCTs have a much more indolent behaviour and rarely recur after conservative
excision { 18221328 ; 19716497 }.

Benign mesenchymal odontogenic tumours (mail to whole section contributors)

Odontogenic fibroma
Definition
Odontogenic fibroma is a neoplasm of mature fibrous or fibromyxoid connective tissue with variable
amounts of inactive-appearing odontogenic epithelium, with or without associated mineralisations.
ICD-O coding
9321/0 Odontogenic fibroma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH1MT3 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull and upper
face & Odontogenic fibroma, NOS
2E83.1 & XH1MT3 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Odontogenic fibroma, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: central odontogenic fibroma
Not recommended: central odontogenic fibroma WHO/non-WHO types
Subtype(s)
Odontogenic fibroma, amyloid subtype
Odontogenic fibroma, granular cell subtype
Hybrid odontogenic fibroma with central giant cell granuloma
Localization
Although conflicting results have been reported, the maxilla appears slightly more frequently
affected { 32988809 }. Most maxillary central odontogenic fibromas occur anterior to the first molar
while slightly more than half of mandibular tumours are found posterior to the first molar
{ 32988809 }.
Clinical features
Small odontogenic fibromas (OdF) are usually asymptomatic while larger tumours present with
localised swelling, loosening of teeth and associated pain. OdF on the anterior maxillary alveolus
often have a characteristic depression rather than expansion.
Imaging: Small OdF typically present as well-defined corticated radiolucencies closely associated
with roots of erupted teeth. Larger tumours may have a multilocular appearance and produce
divergence or resorption of associated tooth roots. Around 10% of tumours have a mixed
radiolucent-radiopaque appearance { 32988809 }.
Epidemiology
Central OdFs are more common in females (2.2:1) and develop over a wide age range with a mean
at presentation of 34 years { 32988809 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Consistency varies from soft to firm depending on the collagen content. Internal mineralisations
may produce a “gritty” consistency on section.
Histopathology
OdFs are composed of moderately cellular bland fibrous tissue with moderate to dense collagen
content, accompanied by varying amounts of dispersed inactive-appearing odontogenic epithelial
nests and cords, sometimes completely absent. There may be minor hard tissue formation in the
form of mineralised dentinoid or round dense basophilic cementum-like mineralisations,
both associated with the odontogenic epithelium.
Several rare subtypes of central odontogenic fibroma have been described.
OdF, amyloid subtype is characterised by amyloid deposits with Langerhans cells often present in
the epithelial elements { 30291007 }. This subtype accounts for approximately 16% of all cases
644

{ 32988809 }. These non-calcifying Langerhans cell-rich lesions have also been considered to be
part of the spectrum of calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour and their correct classification
remains unclear. The site, gender predilection and biological behaviour of this subtype resemble
better those of the OdF { 30291007 ; 33737014 }.
OdF, granular cell subtype is composed of granular cells with variable amounts of odontogenic
epithelium and accounts for around 5% of lesions. Completely granular central odontogenic
fibromas are sometimes referred to as a granular cell odontogenic tumour but are best regarded
as a subtype of odontogenic fibroma { 32988809 }.
OdF, ossifying subtype, demonstrating odontogenic fibroma components intermingled with
ossifying fibroma like tissue has also been reported { 21751042 ; 32988809 }.
OdF with hybrid features of giant cell granuloma and odontogenic fibroma
{ 9067742 ; 21751042 ; 32988809 } have been described.
Differential This should include sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma. This shows infiltration but
diagnosis can be challenging because of epithelial neurotropism of benign odontogenic epithelium
in OdF { 25270252 }. Small radiolucent lesions surrounding the crowns of impacted teeth showing
histological features of OdF should be diagnosed as hyperplastic dental follicles and are not
neoplasms. The peripheral odontogenic fibroma (POF) arises extraosseously on the gingiva,
usually anteriorly, and shares some histological features and the name with OdF { 20674403 }, but
there is little evidence that POF are neoplastic { 6576600 } and they appear unrelated to
intraosseous OdF. POF are much commoner than OdF with a similar female predominance with a
peak incidence in the second to fourth decades of life { 21751042 }. POF are treated by surgical
excision. A recurrence rate of up to 50% has been reported { 20674403 }, but this is almost
abolished if excision extends to periodontal ligament.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: site in tooth bearing segments of the jaws; well-defined lesion radiologically; bland
fibrous connective tissue of varying cellularity
Desirable: Odontogenic epithelial nests or cords
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
OdF almost never recur after enucleation and curettage.

#25397
Central odontogenic fibroma

645

Appearance of the conventional pattern, cellular connective tissue associated with
strands of odontogenic epithelium.

#25398

amyloid subtype

Fibrous tissue with scattered odontogenic
epithelial islands associated with amyloid
deposits presenting as homogeneous
eosinophilic ovoid masses.

#27292

granular cell pattern

Granular stromal cells
associated with
odontogenic epithelial
islands.

#25394

646

Cellular connective tissue with strands of odontogenic epithelium.

#27293
Central odontogenic fibroma
Well defined partly corticated radiolucency with areas of radiopacity in the left mandibular
body with tooth displacement.

Cementoblastoma
Definition
Cementoblastoma (CB) is a benign odontogenic neoplasm that forms a rounded mass of
cementum on the root of a tooth.
ICD-O coding
9273/0 Cementoblastoma
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH4VL1 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Cementoblastoma, benign
2E83.1 & XH4VL1 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Cementoblastoma, benign
Related terminology
Acceptable: benign cementoblastoma
Not recommended: true cementoma; cementoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
CB develops on the apical third of a tooth root, most frequently in the posterior mandible, usually
on the permanent first molar. Teeth in the mandibular premolar and maxillary molar regions are the
second most frequently affected. Involvement of deciduous teeth is rare.
Clinical features
CB causes a slow-growing characteristically painful expansion of the jaw. The associated tooth is
vital in about 80% of cases. Root resorption occurs in around two thirds of cases. Cortical bone
perforation and tooth displacement are rare { 11925541 ; 28869132 }.
Imaging: Radiographically, there is a well-defined circumscribed radio-opaque or mixed-density
mass expanding from the root of the associated tooth. A characteristic radiolucent rim continuous
with the periodontal ligament is usually present { 11925541 }.
Epidemiology
At around 3% of all odontogenic tumours, CB is a relatively rare tumour. There is a wide age range,
with highest frequency in the second and third decades. Males and females are equally affected
{ 11925541 ; 28869132 ; 32684315 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis

647

Cementoblastoma harbours FOS rearrangement and shows c-FOS overexpression { 33653978 },
features in common with osteoid osteoma and osteotoblastoma { 31490237 } with which it shares
histological similarity. FOS would be expected to affect maturation and proliferation of
cementoblasts in the same way as osteoblasts, but cementoblastoma appears to have a more
indolent growth pattern than osteoblastoma.
Macroscopic appearance
Calcified mass, usually 20 - 30 mm in diameter, adherent to and fused with a tooth root.
Histopathology
CB resembles osteoblastoma histologically. The bulk of the lesion is composed of a mass of dense
cellular cementum resembling bone, often with prominent reversal lines, fused with the resorbed
surface of the tooth root. At the periphery there are radiating finger-like trabeculae of newly formed
matrix, often associated with plump cementoblasts and cementoclasts and vascular immature
fibrous tissue.
Differential This should include cemento-osseous dysplasia.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: mass fused to a tooth root; densely mineralised; radiating peripheral matrix; plump
cementoblasts; no fibro-osseous component.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
CB does not usually recur after extraction of the associated tooth and curettage. Recurrence follows
incomplete removal or attempted conservation of the tooth { 11925541 }.

L#25905

A low power photomicrograph showing cementoblastoma arising from and fused to a
resorbed tooth root.

mandibular fist molar tooth.

L#27520

permanent molar, a typical radio-opaque lesion with radiolucent rim in continuity with the
periodontal ligament.

partly or completely filled by multilayered eosinophilic and slightly enlarged osteoblasts.

Cementoblastoma showing origin in association with the root of a vital tooth. There is a
central mass of dense cementum-like material with less mature cemental projections at
the periphery.
R#27161
Cementoblastoma
There is a mass of dense cellular cementum showing prominent reversal lines,
completely fused with the resorbed surface of the tooth root.

649

Cemento-ossifying fibroma
Definition
The cemento-ossifying fibroma (COF) is a benign odontogenic fibro-osseous neoplasm arising in
the jaws and characterised by production of bone and cementum-like calcifications in a fibrous
stroma.
ICD-O coding
9274/0 Cemento-ossifying fibroma
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH52T0 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face
& Cemento-ossifying fibroma
2E83.1 & XH52T0 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower
jaw & Cemento-ossifying fibroma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Ossifying fibroma, conventional type
Not recommended: Cementifying fibroma; ossifying fibroma; ossifying-odontogenic
fibroma; periodontoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The mandible is involved much more frequently than the maxilla, with a predilection for the
mandibular premolar and molar region { 22776732 ; 31950477 ; 3864113 }. Whether nonodontogenic lesions of a similar histology that arise in craniofacial bones outside the jaws are
related is unclear { 34173971 }.
Clinical features
COF typically causes painless jaw expansion. Growth is slow but COF can reach a considerable
size if left untreated { 22776732 ; 26614954 ; 31950477 }. Most patients have a solitary lesion but
rare cases of multiple lesions can arise sporadically or as a component of the hyperparathyroidismjaw tumour syndrome, a rare autosomal dominant disorder characterized by parathyroid adenoma
or carcinoma and bilateral kidney cysts, hamartomas, or Wilms tumours. COFs are present in 30%
of patients with this syndrome { 12434154 ; 24678936 }.
Imaging: Radiographs show a well-defined corticated radiolucency in early stages. Variable
degrees of central opacification develop with time, some COF becoming densely sclerotic, but all
retain an unmineralised periphery { 22776732 ; 3864113 }. Mandibular lesions may cause
characteristic downward bowing of the inferior border. Maxillary lesions may displace the sinus floor
{ 27387498 }. Other findings include cortical thinning, tooth displacement, and root resorption
{ 22776732 ; 29157551 }.
Epidemiology
COF occurs over a broad age range, with a peak in the 3rd to 4th decade, and has a female
predilection (F:M as high as 5:1) { 25409854 ; 27387498 ; 31950477 ; 33878496 }. The tumour
arises in Caucasians primarily, followed by patients of African descent { 27387498 }.
Etiology
COF is presumed to be odontogenic in origin based on its almost exclusive location in tooth-bearing
areas of the jaws { 34184157 } and variable production of dense cementum-like bone, though the
latter is not specific to COF or other types of ossifying fibromas { 5212309 }.
Genetic factors: COF, often multiple and in young patients, are a feature of hyperparathyroidismjaw tumour syndrome (MIM: 145001) { 12434154 ; 23453027 } and gnathodiaphyseal dysplasia
(MIM: 166260) { 4014312 ; 11547842 ; 25409854 }.
Pathogenesis
Inactivating mutations in the tumour suppressor gene CDC73 (HRPT2) have been identified in a
few sporadic cases of COF { 16458039 } and mutations in this gene are the underlying cause of
hyperparathyroidism-jaw
tumour
syndrome
(OMIM
#145001)
{ 12434154 ; 23453027 }. CDC73 encodes parafibromin, which exerts antiproliferative activity via
interaction with cyclin D1 { 23453027 }. Because CDC73 mutations occur in only some COF, they
may play a role in tumour progression rather then in initiation { 23453027 }. Assessment of hotspot

650

mutation in 50 oncogenes and tumour suppressor genes by next-generation sequencing did not
reveal any pathogenic variants in the COF evaluated { 29239811 }. COF show copy number
variation, mostly on chromosomes 7 and 12 but with considerable heterogeneity { 34188021 }.
COF in gnathodiaphyseal dysplasia (OMIM #166260) accompany long bone fragility and cortical
thickening of tubular bones { 11547842 } caused by GDD1 (TMEM16E or ANO5) mutations. These
mutations
are
different
from
those
in
familial
cementoosseous dysplasia { 28176803 ; 23047743 ; 27216912 }.
In addition, gene transcription studies have implicated deregulation of the Wnt/β-catenin pathway
in the molecular pathogenesis of COF { 29239811 } and a number of other probably nonpathogenic genetic changes have been detected { 24664543 ; 29239811 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Single rounded mass or multiple large pieces of bony tissue, with some smooth surfaces
{ 33878496 }.
Histopathology
COF consists of variable proportions of fibrous and mineralised tissue, more heavily mineralised
centrally and with a thin fibrous capsule or well demarcated margin from surrounding normal
bone. The fibroblastic stroma may have areas of hypercellularity and show nuclear
hyperchromasia but no pleomorphism and mitoses are infrequent { 31950477 }. Stromal vascularity
is generally low.
A fibro-osseous appearance of condensation of woven bone from stroma must be present at least
focally. COF exhibits considerable variation in the amount and type of mineralisation, even within
a single lesion. Woven to lamellar bone, osteoid, and dense acellular or
paucicellular basophilic rounded cementum-like calcifications may all be present { 25409854 }, the
last forming round droplets, irregular bulbous, and fine brush-stroke forms { 31950477 }. The bony
trabeculae may form thick anastomosing strands or fuse into large sheets centrally. Osteoblastic
rimming is frequent and there is bone remodelling in the lesion with infrequent osteoclasts
{ 33878496 }. Haemorrhagic cystic degeneration resembling aneurysmal bone cyst formation
occurs rarely { 33878496 ; 34184157 }. Histological features alone are not diagnostic and areas
resembling COF may be seen in psammomatoid and less frequently in trabecular ossifying fibroma,
and COF may contain ossicles like psammomatoid ossifying fibroma as a minor feature.
COF in syndromes have features as non-syndromic COF. COF in gnathodiaphyseal dysplasia are
at the more fibrous end of the spectrum with predominantly basophilic rounded islands and droplets
of acellular bone { 4014312 ; 11547842 ; 25409854 }.
Differential This includes fibrous dysplasia, which has a more uniform woven or lamellar
architecture but may contain islands of basophilic bone and, in the jaws, show osteoblast rimming.
However the sharp demarcation of COF and cortical expansion rather than replacement distinguish
it from fibrous dysplasia. Histologically COF also resembles focal cemento-osseous dysplasia and
COF-like expansion may occur in florid cemento-osseous dysplasia, particularly its familial form.
It should be noted that the so-called peripheral ossifying fibroma is not regarded as an
extraosseous ossifying fibroma, but an unrelated reactive gingival hyperplasia with mineralisation
that shows only some histological similarity to neoplastic cemento-ossifying fibromas
{ 2926546 ; 30693455 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None for sporadic COF.
Syndromic forms can be identified clinically or
by CDC73 (HRPT2) mutations in COF or other tissues {12434154, 16458039, 27658992,
33505612}. COF does not exhibit the GNAS mutations seen in fibrous dysplasia.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: location in tooth bearing region of jaws; benign fibro-osseous histology; well demarcated
Desirable: slow continuous growth
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
COF is usually treated by enucleation and curettage as it shells out readily and recurs rarely
{ 27311848 }. There is a single reported case of possible malignant transformation {25741469,
31062892}.

651

#23684
Cemento-ossifying fibroma
Well-defined tumour mass with cellular fibrous stroma and focal cementum-like dense
bone calcifications. This particular example shows abundant stroma and only a focal
mineralized component.

#27332

Panoramic radiograph showing a well-defined, expansile radiolucency in the posterior
mandible with associated cortical thinning and tooth root displacement.

#27333
Cemento-ossifying fibroma
A lesion in mid-phase maturation with less cellular fibrous stroma and dense lamellar
bone mineralisation.

#27335

Cemento-ossifying fibroma
Trabeculae of heavily mineralised but sparsely cellular woven bone in a fibrous stroma.

A fragment of tooth is entrapped within the center of this ossifying fibroma. There is a
fibrous connective tissue stroma associated with dystrophic calcifications and bone.

654

Odontogenic myxoma
Definition
Odontogenic myxoma is a benign neoplasm histologically resembling odontogenic
ectomesenchyme and characterized by sparse spindle or stellate cells in a myxoid stroma.
ICD-O coding
9320/0 Odontogenic myxoma
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH48L4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face &
Odontogenic myxoma
2E83.1 & XH48L4 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw &
Odontogenic myxoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Odontogenic fibromyxoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Odontogenic myxoma (OM) arises in the jaws { 21266837 ; 31551163 }. Two thirds of OMs are
located in the mandible and one third in the maxilla { 17996487 }, usually in relation to a tooth,
typically a premolar or molar { 17149953 }. Maxillary lesions tend to obliterate the maxillary sinuses
as an early feature, and expansion is an early and consistent feature. Rare cases have been
reported to occur extraosseously in the gingiva { 23722914 }.
Clinical features
OM is characterized by insidious and oligosymptomatic slow permeative growth, causing bone
destruction, expansion { 17149953 ; 19027311 }, mobility or absence of tooth, eventual cortical
perforation and soft tissue infiltration { 21180454 ; 21624835 }. On palpation, OM is firm in
consistency and not tender { 30967732 }.
Imaging: Radiographically OMs are unilocular or multilocular radiolucencies with defined or diffuse
borders, better defined by CT or MRI { 23972779 }. Unilocular OM are more frequent in children
and in the anterior region of the jaw. { 12435020 ; 22442596 }. Fine straight internal trabeculation
is characteristic { 17507265 }. Odontogenic myxoma of the maxilla is less frequent and has a
tendency to spread to the maxillary sinus { 29683236 }. Root displacement and resorption may
occur, but as late features.
Epidemiology
OM accounts for <10% of all odontogenic tumours and is the third most frequent after odontoma
and ameloblastoma { 16916667 ; 17996487 ; 23965450 ; 25937364 }. OM affects mostly young
adults between the second and third decades of life, but with wide age range { 28469823 }. In many
studies, odontogenic myxoma is as much as twice as common in females as males, but not in all
populations { 17507265 ; 17996487 ; 32048275 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
Unknown. No pathogenic mutations were found in 50 oncogenes and tumour suppressor genes
commonly mutated in human cancers { 28597929 }. OMs show MAPK/ERK pathway activation and
inhibition may reduce tumour growth { 31713744 }. OM is not associated with Carney syndrome
{ 21444236 }, but has been reported occasionally with Gardner syndrome, nevoid basal cell
carcinoma syndrome, and tuberous sclerosis { 24163866 ; 27994429 ; 30531170 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Unencapsulated gelatinous white-grey loose tissue { 32506377 }.
Histopathology
OM is composed of predominantly loose myxoid stroma with a sparse population of stellate to
spindle cells. Small inactive rests of odontogenic epithelium are present in a small minority of OM
and are few in number { 29683236 }. The tumour is generally unencapsulated with

655

permeative spread in medullary bone { 32048275 }. Lesions with more collagenous stroma are
often referred to as odontogenic fibromyxomas. Immunohistochemistry does not aid diagnosis.
Differential The most significant differential diagnosis for OM is dental follicle or developing dental
papilla, which are histologically identical but clinically and radiographically distinct { 28508997 }.
Myxoid neurofibroma may have overlapping histology but will generally show positive
immunohistochemical staining for S100 protein { 28508997 }. Other differential diagnoses include
chondromyxoid fibroma, odontogenic fibroma, or low-grade myxofibrosarcoma, particularly in
OM with a more collagenized stroma { 28508997 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: arising in tooth-bearing segments of jaws; myxoid stroma with variable collagenization;
sparse stellate or spindle shaped cells
Desirable: scattered inactive odontogenic rests
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Lack of capsule and permeative spread produce uncertainty over extent, and account for
recurrence rates for OM ranging from 10 to 43% { 31551163 }. It is unclear whether recurrence
increases with more conservative treatment methods { 31551163 ; 32048275 }. OM in the maxilla
appear to be more likely to recur than mandibular lesions { 31551163 }. MAPK/ERK pathway
inhibition may have potential to reduce tumour growth { 31713744 }. If malignant transformation is
possible in odontogenic myxoma, it is exceedingly rare and may represent misdiagnosis of low
grade myxoid malignancies { 10992937 }.

#27521
Normal dental follicle resembling odontogenic myxoma
The histological appearances of dental follicle and papilla are almost identical to
odontogenic myxoma but a clue may be given by the size, shape and presence of
epithelial remnants running along some surfaces.
#27522
Odontogenic myxoma

656

This slightly under-exposed panoramic radiograph shows the characteristic straight crisscrossing internal bony septa found in some lesions.

#27620
Odontogenic myxoma
An odontogenic myxoma from the mandible comprising loose myxoid tissue with a sparse
cell population.

#27624
Odontogenic myxoma
Odontogenic myxoma from the mandible at high power showing loose myxoid tissue
stroma with scattered spindle and stellate cells without atypia and forming minimal or no
collagen.

657

#27628
Odontogenic myxoma
This myxoma has prominent collagen formation but excess basophilic ground substance
remains between the fibres. Such lesions are sometimes termed fibromyxoma.

#24764
Odontogenic myxoma
Tumour from the maxilla which is unencapsulated and composed of loose myxoid tissue
with sparse dispersed spindle cells. No odontogenic epithelial rests are present in this
example.

Malignant odontogenic tumours

658

Sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma
Definition
Sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma (SOC) is a primary intraosseous carcinoma of the jaws with
bland cytology, markedly sclerotic stroma and local aggressive infiltration, but no metastatic
potential.
ICD-O coding
9270/3 Sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B5J & XH4M89 Malignant miscellaneous tumours of bone or articular cartilage of other or
unspecified sites & Odontogenic tumour, malignant
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most cases have affected the posterior mandible. Maxillary SOC tend to lie anteriorly.
Clinical features
SOC presents as swelling which occasionally grows rapidly. Most cases have been asymptomatic
with only a few mandibular examples characterized by paraesthesia.
Imaging: Radiographically, SOC is a poorly defined radiolucency with cortical bone destruction and
occasional tooth mobility. Tumour extension beyond radiographic margins has been reported in
many cases.
Epidemiology
There is a very slight female predilection with age at diagnosis ranging from 31 to 73 years with the
majority of cases arising in the 5th to 7th decades { 33715971 }. SOC may be under recognized or
have been described under another name { 21418400 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Dense fibrous tissue fragments
Histopathology
A dense, fibrocollagenous sclerotic stroma is the hallmark of SOC, and contains malignant
epithelial cells forming single-file thin cords, strands, small nests and infrequent islands of more
epidermoid cells { 18753945 }. Only mild cellular atypia has been observed in most cases. SOC
are infiltrative and lack encapsulation. Perineural or intraneural invasion is frequently seen but
lymphovascular invasion is rare. Mitotic activity has been inconspicuous and necrosis is rare.
Mucus-negative clear cells may be present but glandular differentiation has also been reported
{ 33715971 }. Calcifications including, cementum-like bone, bone or dentinoid have been observed
in half of the cases.
Immunohistochemistry: There is no specific immunohistochemical marker for SOC. The epithelial
component is highlighted by CK 5/6, CK14, CK19 and p63.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
All cases studied have been negative for EWSR1 rearrangement.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: thin cords and nests of epithelium; sclerotic stroma
Desirable: mild atypia; perineural/intraneural infiltration often prominent
Staging
While there is no defined UICC staging guidance, the use of International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting minimum data set reporting is encouraged { 30500289 }.
Prognosis and prediction

659

Prognosis up to now has been excellent with only local recurrence in a few cases despite perineural
invasion. No metastasis has been reported.

#27295
Sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma

Left, a cellular example with less sclerotic stroma showing haphazardly arranged thin
cords, strands and small nests of epithelium blending with loose and dense collagenous
stroma. Mild inflammation is present in the background which can cause some problem
distinguishing the epithelial component.

#27296

660

Sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma
Typical appearance with extensive sclerosis of the stroma and dispersed, sometimes
inconspicuous epithelial islands and strands.

#27298
Sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma
Right, CK19 immunostain highlights the epithelial component.

L#27765

661

Radiographic appearance showing a relatively low grade appearance with well-defined
and partly corticated outline.

This whole slide shows the typical appearance with extensive sclerosis of the stroma,
remodeled bone, and dispersed, sometimes inconspicuous epithelial islands and strands.

Ameloblastic carcinoma
Definition
Ameloblastic carcinoma (AC) is a primary odontogenic carcinoma histologically resembling
ameloblastoma.
ICD-O coding
9270/3 Ameloblastic carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B5J & XH4M89 Malignant miscellaneous tumours of bone or articular cartilage of other or
unspecified sites & Odontogenic tumour, malignant
Related terminology
Not recommended: malignant ameloblastoma; dedifferentiated ameloblastoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Intraosseous
jaw
carcinoma
with
mandibular
predilection,
usually
posteriorly
{ 28600599 ; 26768075 ; 31444935 }. Most AC arise de novo but there may be pre-existing
ameloblastoma { 6366686 ; 17368379 ; 25436009 }. Extraosseous AC is extremely rare
{ 11120479 }.
Clinical features
Jaw or orofacial swelling, sometimes with pain and paraesthesia { 28600599 ; 31444935 } and later
with tooth mobility, extension into oral mucosa and trismus { 19800270 ; 26768075 ; 31444935 }.
Rarely, AC is associated with hypercalcemia of malignancy { 11113817 }.
Imaging: Contrast-enhanced CT shows a poorly defined expanding lesion with medullary and
cortical bone destruction { 19800270 }. MRI delineates soft tissue or marrow extension. Moderate
18-fluorodeoxyglucose uptake on PET-CT is used to detect metastasis { 20841465 ; 25436009 }.
Epidemiology
AC accounts for ≤2% of all odontogenic tumours { 15660091 ; 17156974 ; 30448231 }, and 30% of
malignant odontogenic tumours { 22385300 }. A wide age range is affected with a median age of
49 years { 28600599 } with male predilection { 17448710 ; 25013517 ; 28600599 }.
Etiology
Unknown. Some develop in longstanding untreated ameloblastoma { 26768075 }, or locally
recurrent ameloblastoma after surgery and/or irradiation { 6366686 ; 24906942 ; 11113817 }.
Pathogenesis
BRAF
p.V600E
mutations
have
been
reported
in
between
25-40%
{ 25854168 ; 26306423 ; 30889301 } and 100% { 33176823 } of AC.
Macroscopic appearance
Solid mass, irregular borders, limited cystic change {20841465, 26238174}.
Histopathology
AC resembles ameloblastoma and has variable features of malignancy. The architecture ranges
from ameloblastoma-like follicular or plexiform patterns to solid, compact sheets of basaloid
epithelium or anastomosing strands with loss of stellate reticulum. Peripheral palisading, nuclear
reverse polarity and stellate reticulum must be evident, at least focally. The threshold for diagnosis
of malignancy is poorly defined but AC usually show some of: at least moderate cellular or nuclear
atypia, increased mitotic activity, increased nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, hyperchromatic vesicular
nuclei, crowding of basal cells and expansion of the basal cell compartment with disordered polarity
and expansion of atypical cells into stellate reticulum. Central necrosis in islands is a helpful finding,

662

but may be subtle and apoptotic. Extension into mucosa or medullary space infiltration do not
distinguish benign ameloblastoma from AC. Increased mitotic activity or Ki67 proliferative fraction
cannot define AC and emphasis is placed on identifying a combination of architectural and
cytomorphologic abnormalities.
Minor differentiation patterns include squamous, clear cell and spindle cell pseudosarcomatous.
Combinations
are
possible and
these
features
vary
in
extent
{ 17448710 ; 19800270 ; 24906942 ; 27195964 ; 30928327 }. Dispersed invasion and perineural
infiltration are uncommon in AC { 27195964 ; 30500289 ; 33176823 } in contrast to other
odontogenic malignant neoplasms { 18753945 ; 23715163 }.
Benign ameloblastoma may be present as a precursor lesion { 23099227 ; 24906942 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Relative to ameloblastoma, AC shows expression of p53
{ 27195964 }, SMA and SOX2 { 19150605 ; 24603057 } but these are not validated as arbiters
of malignancy.
Differential This includes benign ameloblastoma, especially maxillary examples that can show high
cellularity. Basaloid AC must be separated from basal cell ameloblastoma, basaloid squamous cell
carcinoma and adamantinoma-like Ewing tumour { 26034869 ; 31895100 }. AC with spindle cell
changes warrants exclusion of spindle cell/sarcomatoid squamous cell carcinoma, carcinosarcoma
and other spindle cell malignancies { 26238174 ; 30928327 }. AC with clear-cell features must be
separated from clear cell odontogenic carcinoma { 23715163 }. A primary intraosseous carcinoma
lacking ameloblastoma features may be better categorised as primary intraosseous carcinoma.
Cytology
There are scant references in the literature. FNAB may detect possible metastasis in known cases
{ 20841465 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
BRAF p.V600E mutation { 25854168 ; 33176823 } and TP53 mutation { 24906942 } are of
undefined diagnostic value. EWSR1 translocation indicates clear cell odontogenic carcinoma
rather than AC with clear cell phenotype { 23715163 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: histological resemblance to ameloblastoma; cytological atypia
Desirable: intraosseous jaw tumour; tumour necrosis, BRAF mutation (in subset); associated
ameloblastoma precursor
Staging
While there is no defined UICC staging guidance, the use of International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting minimum data set reporting is encouraged { 30500289 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Radical surgical resection is the treatment of choice, with radiotherapy for non-surgical candidates,
recurrent/metastatic disease and/or inadequate surgical margins { 30393089 ; 26796877 }. AC
recurs locally in ≤40% { 19800270 ; 25436009 } and metastases to cervical nodes in ≤13% of
patients { 28600599 ; 30509051 ; 33176823 }. Distant metastasis develops in a third, most
commonly in lung, followed by brain, liver and bone { 17942338 ; 28600599 }, reducing prognosis
{ 19800270 ; 31444935 }. The 5-year overall survival rate is 70%, better after complete surgical
resection { 28600599 }. Origin in benign precursor { 6366686 ; 17368379 ; 25436009 } and
histopathological patterns do not affect clinical course { 29362117 }. Novel treatment
considerations include proton beam therapy { 30240599 }, and BRAF targeted agents
{ 26796877 }.

663

#25565
