Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma
Definition
Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma (COdC) is an odontogenic carcinoma characterized by sheets,
nests, or cords of clear cells in a fibrocellular or hyalinized stroma.
ICD-O coding
9341/3 Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B5J & XH5DZ4 Malignant miscellaneous tumours of bone or articular cartilage of other or
unspecified sites & Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: clear cell odontogenic tumour; clear cell ameloblastoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
All develop in the jaws, three quarters of cases in the mandible, most frequently in the posterior
body and lower ramus { 31482399 }.
Clinical features
The clinical manifestations are non-specific with destructive jaw swelling, with or without pain,
paraesthesia and tooth mobility, later with ulceration to the mucosa. Less commonly, patients
develop lymphadenopathy (19%) or distant metastases (11%).

668

Imaging: Radiographically, COdC are expansile, ill-defined radiolucencies often exhibiting tooth
root resorption { 31482399 }.
Epidemiology
Over 100 cases of clear cell odontogenic carcinoma have been reported in the literature with a
mean age at presentation of 53 years and with 63% of cases in females { 31227275 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
EWSR1 rearrangements have been reported in over 80% of cases { 23715163 }. ATF is the most
common fusion partner, less frequently CREB1 and CREM { 23715163 ; 30047065 ; 33616852 }.
Clear cell odontogenic carcinomas are morphologically and immunohistochemically similar to
salivary clear cell carcinoma, which share EWSR1 translocation, suggesting a common
pathogenesis { 21484932 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Intraosseous tumour with homogeneous, tan-white cut surface, indistinct borders and sometimes
soft tissue extension.
Histopathology
COdC are composed of lobular sheets, nests, or trabeculae of monomorphic, polygonal cells with
clear to slightly eosinophilic cytoplasm and eccentric nuclei embedded in a hyalinised or
fibrocellular stroma { 4077550 }. Peripheral palisading may or may not be present, and the tumour
may have a heterogeneous appearance with areas of more basaloid epithelium without cytoplasmic
clearing. The clear cells are negative for mucin, but they are PAS-positive and diastase-sensitive,
indicating glycogen. The mitotic index is low and some COdC are bland and relatively indolent.
Necrosis and angiolymphatic invasion are uncommon, but perineural invasion is reported in onethird of tumours { 21290202 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Clear cell odontogenic carcinomas are positive for cytokeratins (AE1/3,
CK5/6, CK19), p63, and EMA, but they are negative for myoepithelial markers (SMA, calponin,
S100 protein) { 21290202 ; 31482399 }.
Differential The differential diagnosis includes other clear cell-rich gnathic neoplasms, including
other odontogenic tumours (clear cell calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumour, amyloid-rich central
odontogenic fibroma), salivary tumours, both metastatic and intraosseous primary
(mucoepidermoid carcinoma, clear cell carcinoma, epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma), and
metastatic neoplasms (renal cell carcinoma, melanoma) { 32642935 }. Odontogenic carcinoma
with dentinoid has overlapping morphologic features, some cases having a clear cell phenotype
but these form dentinoid and appear to be a distinct entity { 25409850 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
EWSR1 rearrangement facilitates diagnosis, but is precluded by specimen decalcification.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: location in jaws; prominent clear cell phenotype; infiltrative margin
Desirable: EWSR1 translocation
Staging
While there is no defined UICC staging guidance, the use of International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting minimum data set reporting is encouraged { 30500289 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Primary management is complete surgical resection. The role of adjuvant therapy is not clearly
defined. Prognosis is variable with a local recurrence rate of nearly 40% { 31482399 }. Metastases
have been reported to cervical lymph nodes, but uncommonly to the lungs and bone and may occur
after many years. Approximately 11% of patients succumb to the disease { 31227275 }.

669

#27496
Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma
The tumour is composed predominantly of optically clear cells.

#27497
Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma
Clear cell tumour nests in a prominent fibrocellular stroma.

670

#27498
Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma
The tumour demonstrates cords of cells with clear to faintly eosinophilic cytoplasm in a
hyalinized stroma.

#25615
Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma
Carcinoma with extensive bony erosion of the mandible reaching the inferior alveolar
nerve bundle.

671

Ghost cell odontogenic carcinoma
Definition
Ghost cell odontogenic carcinoma (GCOC) is an odontogenic carcinoma with ghost cells and
sometimes dentinoid deposition.
ICD-O coding
9302/3 Ghost cell odontogenic carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2B5J & XH2BX2 Malignant miscellaneous tumours of bone or articular cartilage of other or
unspecified sites & Ghost cell odontogenic carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: dentinogenic ghost cell carcinoma; malignant calcifying ghost cell odontogenic
tumour; malignant calcifying odontogenic cyst; calcifying ghost cell odontogenic carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
GCOC arises within the jaws and is almost twice as common in the maxilla as the mandible. The
ramus and molar area are the most affected region in the mandible { 29738629 ; 30746377 }.
Clinical features
GCOC may present with non-specific signs and symptoms suggestive of malignancy, including
pain, swelling, ulceration, tooth mobility and paraesthesia. Soft tissue infiltration may be present
{ 30746377 }.
Imaging: GCOC typically present as a poorly demarcated radiolucency, in half of cases associated
with internal mineralisation. Root resorption and displacement are common.
Epidemiology
GCOC is a rare tumour with less than 50 cases reported, of which about half occurred in Asian
patients { 30746377 }. GCOC is more common in men than women (3.2:1). It can arise at any age,
with a peak incidence in patients aged 30- 60 years and a range from 10 to 89 years { 30746377 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
GCOC may arise de novo or arise in a calcifying odontogenic cyst (COC) or a dentinogenic ghost
cell tumour (DGCT) benign precursor { 18221328 ; 33245488 }.
Mutation of CTNNB1 (β-catenin) has been reported in GCOC { 26500725 }, similar to that found in
other tumours with ghost cells and/or dentinoid material and clear cells, including odontogenic
carcinoma with dentinoid { 31606421 }, dentinogenic ghost cell tumour (DGCT) { 17178501 } and
calcifying cystic odontogenic tumours { 14578169 ; 27158066 }. Notably, mutations in APC, also
part of the WNT/β-catenin pathway, have also been reported in one case of GCOC { 26173781 },
in similarity with odontogenic carcinoma with dentinoid { 31606421 }. Additional mutations in genes
in the SHH and ATM pathways and loss of RB1, FHIT, PTEN, ATM and CHEK2 gene regions are
reported, amongst other changes { 26173781 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Appearances vary from solid to multicystic, often with a gritty consistency on section.
Histopathology
The histological features are those of DGCT and calcifying odontogenic cyst (COC) with cytological
evidence of malignancy. The main component is odontogenic epithelial sheets, islands and strands
resembling an ameloblastoma with palisaded columnar hyperchromatic basal cells with reverse
polarity, at least focally, and additional aberrant keratinization as ghost cells. Ghost cells may
mineralise. The ghost cells may be dispersed, isolated or present in clusters
{ 18221328 ; 30746377 }. In higher grade lesions the malignant epithelial cells are basaloid or nondescript and ameloblastoma-like features may be absent. Dentinoid may be present adjacent to
the epithelial cells. The amount of dentinoid and ghost cells are variable. Evidence of malignancy
includes mitotic activity, pleomorphism, hyperchromatism, necrosis and an infiltrative growth
pattern. Benign precursor COC or DGCT may be present.

672

Differential GCOC should be differentiated from similar but benign odontogenic tumours,
including DGCT and COC and other odontogenic tumours that may contain ghost cells as a minor
component, such as ameloblastoma and adenoid ameloblastoma. The diagnosis of GCOC requires
cytological evidence of malignancy which is not present in these other tumours. There is histological
overlap with adenoid ameloblastoma and odontogenic carcinoma with dentinoid { 25409850 }. It is
possible these entities are related and differentiating criteria are not well defined.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: poorly demarcated lesion radiologically; ameloblastoma-like epithelium; prominent ghost
cells; cytological evidence of malignancy
Desirable: mixed radiolucency; root resorption; dentinoid formation
Staging
While there is no defined UICC staging guidance, the use of International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting minimum data set reporting is encouraged { 30500289 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Complete surgical excision is the treatment of choice. The aggressive nature of GCOC is reflected
by a high recurrence rate of about 63% reported in 44 patients. Distant metastases developed in
three patients and nine patients died { 29738629 }. Long-term follow-up is required.

carcinoma

peripheral palisading
associated with ghost
cells and apoptotic
necrosis.

Images from a more solid carcinoma comprising sheets of malignant hyperchromatic
epithelium with scattered clusters of ghost cells. There is no epithelial architecture,
peripheral palisading or evidence of ameloblastic differentiation.
R#25393
Ghost cell odontogenic carcinoma
Malignant epithelium with an ameloblastomatous appearance with ghost cells and areas
of necrosis

674

Primary intraosseous carcinoma, NOS
Definition
Primary intraosseous carcinoma NOS (PIOC) is a central jaw carcinoma that cannot be categorized
as any other type of carcinoma and derives from odontogenic cysts, rests of odontogenic
epithelium, the reduced enamel epithelium surrounding impacted teeth or other benign precursors.
ICD-O coding
9270/3 Primary intraosseous carcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B5J & XH4M89 Malignant miscellaneous tumours of bone or articular cartilage of other or
unspecified sites & Odontogenic tumour, malignant
Related terminology
Not recommended: Primary intraosseous squamous cell carcinoma, cystogenic type; Primary
intraosseous squamous cell carcinoma, solid type; Primary intra-alveolar epidermoid
carcinoma; Primary odontogenic carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
PIOC is more frequent in the posterior body and ramus of the mandible and, less often, in the
anterior maxilla. Cases arising in cysts are more frequent in the mandible. Intraosseous origin must
be established for diagnosis, excluding extension of gingival or alveolar squamous cell carcinoma
or antral carcinoma into the jaws { 22290811 }. Metastases must be excluded. In the mandible
these usually localise below the inferior alveolar nerve, in contrast to PIOC which develops above
it { 22290834 }.
Clinical features
PIOC may be asymptomatic, discovered incidentally on radiographs with or without tooth root
resorption and cortical erosion. More advanced lesions are characterized by painful cortical
expansion, loosening of teeth, non-healing extraction sites, labial or facial paraesthesia
and pathologic fractures. Approximately 40% of patients have metastasis at the time of
presentation { 11448352 }.
Imaging: Poorly defined osteolytic area with or without tooth root resorption and cortical erosion.
There may be radiological evidence of a benign precursor and PIOC arising in cysts may produce
multilocular or scalloped radiolucency.
Epidemiology
POIC is rare and data is mostly in case reports. A systemic review identified 257 reported cases in
2020, the majority apparently arising from odontogenic cysts, more frequently residual and radicular
cysts and less often dentigerous and odontogenic keratocysts { 33044723 }. There is a male
predilection of 2:1 and the mean age at diagnosis is 55-60 years, though a wide age range is
described, with occasional cases in young patients.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Those that arise in odontogenic cysts may be preceded by dysplasia.
Macroscopic appearance
Non-specific unless a cyst or other precursor is present.
Histopathology
PIOC are squamous carcinomas, comprising islands and small nests of atypical squamous
epithelium with prickle cells and minimal keratinisation. Cytological atypia ranges from quite bland
to severe. Most PIOCs are moderately differentiated and necrosis is rare.
Immunohistochemistry: CK19 positivity on immunohistochemistry supports an odontogenic
epithelial origin, but has low specificity.
Differential Peripheral palisading or plexiform patterns may occur, but if prominent suggest
ameloblastic carcinoma. Imaging, histopathology and clinical information are essential to exclude

675

metastasis, which is considerably more common, other malignant odontogenic tumours, mucosal
and sinonasal primary carcinomas invading bone and intraosseous salivary gland cancers.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: site in jaws; destructive central jaw lesion; absence of a communication with the surface
mucosa or antrum; exclusion of metastatic disease by staging and immunohistochemistry
Desirable: CK19 positive on immunohistochemistry; historic radiological evidence of
odontogenic precursor
Staging
While there is no defined UICC staging guidance, the use of International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting minimum data set reporting is encouraged { 30500289 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis is generally poor. Outcomes are similar to stage IV oral squamous cell carcinoma with
overall five year survival of around 45%, Radical resection with neck dissection is the most frequent
treatment, with adjunctive radiotherapy providing a benefit. Recurrence is frequent (up to 60%) and
is associated with poor outcome. Distant metastasis is rare but typically to the lung. Cases arising
in cysts have a more prolonged clinical course, but 5 year survival is around 40%. If dysplasia is
found incidentally in a cyst after enucleation, close follow up is appropriate.

#27308
Primary intraosseous carcinoma NOS
This radiolucency associated with the lower third molar presented in an 82-year-old male
and had increased in size when compared to a panoramic radiograph taken 7 years
before and had developed a poorly defined periphery. There was complete bony
destruction of buccal cortex, root resorption and paresthesia along the distribution of right
inferior alveolar nerve.

676

#27309
Primary intraosseous carcinoma, NOS
Typical squamous carcinoma that arose in an odontogenic cyst.

#27534
Primary intraosseous carcinoma
This primary intraosseous carcinoma is arising from a pre-existing odontogenic cyst. A
search for definite cytological atypia, mitotic activity and invasion may be required as
hyperplastic islands of epithelium often develop in odontogenic cysts' walls and those
PIOC that arise in cysts are often well differentiated.
677

Odontogenic carcinosarcoma
Definition
Odontogenic carcinosarcoma (OCS) is a malignant mixed odontogenic tumour in which the
epithelial component as well as the ectomesenchymal component show features of malignancy.
ICD-O coding
9342/3 Odontogenic carcinosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B5J & XH4LP1 Malignant miscellaneous tumours of bone or articular cartilage of other or
unspecified sites & Odontogenic carcinosarcoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: Malignant odontogenic mixed tumour; ameloblastic carcinosarcoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
All cases have involved the posterior mandible, with one equivocal case in the maxilla
{ 1987921 ; 30554629 }.
Clinical features
Most patients present with pain and swelling and mean tumour diameter at diagnosis of
approximately 60 mm. Paresthesia or anesthesia has also been reported.
Imaging: Radiographically, most patients present with highly destructive poorly marginated
radiolucencies. Cortical perforation is common, followed by soft tissue extension { 26131431 }.
Epidemiology
Cases have been reported from Japan, Saudi Arabia, Venezuela, S Korea, France, Germany,
Brazil and the USA. No age incidence is defined though the mean age of female patients is
significantly lower than males. Males are affected twice as frequently as females { 30220320 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Most cases have been associated with a previously existing odontogenic neoplasm, often
ameloblastic fibroma or ameloblastic fibrosarcoma { 17395065 ; 30220320 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The neoplasm is often tan, lobulated and fleshy { 17395065 ; 21195529 }.
Histopathology
Histopathological features are based on few cases, but resemble ameloblastic fibroma or
fibrosarcoma. Both the epithelial and ectomesenchymal components must show convincing
cytologic features of malignancy. The epithelial component ranges from fine strands to larger
islands, often with peripheral palisading. The ectomesenchymal component is hypercellular with
cytologic atypia. Mesenchymal hard tissues such as dentinoid, bone and cartilage may be produced
but the histological appearances are not required to mimic osteosarcoma or other defined
sarcomas. Both components show a high mitotic index and Ki-67 proliferative fraction
{ 17395065 ; 30554629 }.
Immunohistochemsitry: The epithelial component reacts to a variety of cytokeratins, particularly
CK19. The ectomesenchymal component is invariably vimentin positive but may also show
reactivity to desmin and muscle specific actin { 30554629 }.
Differential This must exclude a malignant epithelial odontogenic tumour with a spindle cell
component of epithelial origin, such as ameloblastic carcinoma, a significant diagnostic pitfall
{ 30928327 }. Formation of matrix or hard tissue in an odontogenic carcinoma does not necessarily
indicate sarcoma { 11903826 ; 25409850 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
678

Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: origin in tooth bearing segment of jaws; carcinoma and sarcoma components; Significant
cytologic atypia in both components; exclusion of spindle cell carcinoma.
Desirable: evidence of pre-existing odontogenic tumour
Staging
While there is no defined UICC staging guidance, the use of International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting minimum data set reporting is encouraged { 30500289 }.
Prognosis and prediction
The treatment of choice is wide surgical excision. Almost two thirds of patients suffer recurrence
and just over 40% metastasis, most often to lung. Disease associated mortality is about 50%
{ 30220320 }.

#24516
Odontogenic carcinosarcoma
Islands of highly cellular and poorly
differentiated epithelium lying in
a stromal component producing
abundant dentinoid.

#24517
Odontogenic carcinosarcoma
The epithelial component shows high cellularity, some pleomorphism and
hyperchromatism and frequent mitoses.

679

#24518
Odontogenic carcinosarcoma
The ectomesenchymal component shows hypercellularity, some pleomorphism,
hyperchromatism, increased mitoses and extensive dentinoid.

Odontogenic sarcomas
Definition
The odontogenic sarcomas (OSs) are a group of malignant mixed odontogenic neoplasms in which
only the ectomesenchymal component shows microscopic features of malignancy.
ICD-O coding
9330/3 Ameloblastic fibrosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B5J & XH0XD5 Malignant miscellaneous tumours of bone or articular cartilage of other or
unspecified sites & Ameloblastic fibrosarcoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Ameloblastic fibrosarcoma; ameloblastic fibrodentinosarcoma; ameloblastic fibro-odontosarcoma
Localization
Approximately 75-80% arise in the mandible with a predilection for the posterior region
{ 28492759 ; 32508466 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with locally aggressive expansile lesions, with or without pain. Many OSs arise
from malignant transformation of a benign precursor.
Imaging: Early lesions may appear benign radiographically, features reflecting a benign precursor.
However, with time the lesion typically develops ill-defined margins with cortical destruction. Most
lesions are unilocular or multilocular radiolucencies, but are occasionally mixed radiolucent/opaque
{ 19150219 ; 28776760 }. Tooth displacement, impaction, and root resorption also are possible
{ 28776760 }.

680

Epidemiology
Only about 100 cases have been reported { 28776760 ; 32508466 }. Males are affected about 1.5
times more frequently than females. The average age at diagnosis is in the upper third decade
{ 25921823 ; 28776760 }.
Etiology
Unknown. However, over half OSs arise from malignant transformation of a pre-existing benign
neoplasm, such as ameloblastic fibroma.
Pathogenesis
OSs have BRAF p.V600E activating mutations { 31899815 } in the mesenchymal component
{ 32966885 }. Single cases have shown NRAS p.Gln61Lys mutation (mutually exclusive with BRAF
p.V600E mutation), and EGFR exon 20 insertions, MDM2 amplification, or aneuploidy
{ 7614208 ; 31899815 ; 33867182 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Not reported.
Histopathology
OSs contain an epithelial and ectomesenchymal component. The epithelial component is
cytologically bland and benign. It varies considerably in quantity and morphology, ranging from thin
dental lamina-like strands to larger islands of odontogenic epithelium with peripheral cells showing
nuclear palisading and reverse polarity but not stellate reticulum. The malignant ectomesenchymal
component is characterized by hypercellularity, varying degrees of cytologic atypia, and increased
mitotic activity { 21074695 ; 28492759 }. In approximately 10% of cases, dysplastic dentine with or
without enamel matrix is produced (ameloblastic fibro-dentinosarcoma or ameloblastic fibroodontosarcoma).
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The mutations identified (see pathogenesis) are not yet clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: origin in tooth bearing segment of jaws; mixed odontogenic neoplasm; cytologically
bland epithelial component; cytologically malignant ectomesenchymal component
Desirable: evidence of benign precursor
Staging
While there is no defined UICC staging guidance, the use of International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting minimum data set reporting is encouraged { 30500289 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Most cases are treated by surgical resection with an overall recurrence rate of 35% and 21%
disease-related mortality { 28776760 }. Metastases are usually distant but affect less than 5% of
cases { 32508466 }.

#27535
681

Odontogenic sarcoma
Radiographic image showing poorly marginated osteolytic radiolucency of the right
posterior mandible.

#27536
Ameloblastic fibrosarcoma
Mixed odontogenic tumour in which the epithelial component is cytologically bland but the
ectomesenchymal component displays hypercellularity.

#27537
Odontogenic sarcoma
At high power, the ectomesenchymal component is hypercellular with cytologic atypia and
a high mitotic index.

682

Giant cell lesions and bone cysts

Central giant cell granuloma
Definition
Central giant cell granuloma (CGCG) is a localized, benign but sometimes aggressive osteolytic
jaw lesion consisting of a proliferation of osteoclasts in a mononuclear and fibrous vascular stroma.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA01.20 Giant cell granuloma, central
Related terminology
Not recommended: Central giant cell lesion; reparative giant cell granuloma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
CGCG develops almost exclusively in the jaws and is more frequent in the anterior mandible.
Clinical features
CGCGs generally present as well-defined, asymptomatic, slow growing, expansile radiolucencies.
A minority of lesions are aggressive and may exhibit rapid growth, root resorption, tooth
displacement, cortical perforation and recurrence after curettage { 3462363 ; 12378481 }.
Imaging: Corticated unilocular or multilocular radiolucency, sometimes with fine honeycomb or
wispy trabecular opacity. Cortical expansion often prominent, with perforation. Tooth root resorption
infrequent. MRI is helpful in delineating soft tissue involvement and multicentricity can be verified
with fusion PET/CT scan.
Epidemiology
The incidence of CGCG is 1.1 per million in Europe { 15350025 }. Most cases occur in females and
in patients aged < 30 years { 29751369 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Genetic factors
Multiple giant cell lesions occur in syndromes, including neurofibromatosis type 1, osteoglophonic
dysplasia, Noonan, cardiofaciocutaneous, oculoectodermal, LEOPARD, SchimmelpenningFeuerstein-Mims and Jaffe–Campanacci, most of which are caused by MAPK-pathway gene
mutations { 31705763 }.
Pathogenesis
CGCG arise from osteoclast precursors in the mononuclear stroma, induced by RANKL,
VEGF and b-FGF. The last amplifies the resorptive action of parathyroid hormone.
Sporadic CGCG have low tumour mutation burden, lack fusions { 30385747 } and are driven by
mutually exclusive somatic mutations in KRAS (more often affecting codon 12), FGFR1 (either
p.C381R or p.N330I) and TRPV4 (p.M713V/I) that occur in 70% of cases leading to MAPK-pathway
activation { 30385747 }. TRPV4 codes a polymodal Ca2+ permeable channel and is mutated in
hereditary channelopathies characterized by peripheral nervous system and skeletal changes.
Recently, CGCG have been described as one feature of a polysystemic syndrome due to
germline TRPV4 mutation, expanding the spectrum of TRPV4 channelopathies { 33685999 }.
Unlike giant cell lesions of long bones, H3F3A mutations are not present { 25442495 ; 30385747 }.
Macroscopic appearance
CGCG have a fleshy, reddish-brown, haemorrhagic appearance.
Histopathology
CGCG is an unencapsulated proliferation of mononuclear spindle-shaped and polygonal cells
intermingled with osteoclast multinucleate giant cells in a vascular background, with haemorrhage
and haemosiderin pigment. The lesion may have a lobular architecture, lobules separated by
fibrous septa that may contain thin osteoid and woven bone. The giant cells in CGCGs show
reactivity to osteoclast and macrophage markers { 18581286 ; 21029180 ; 25409853 }. The
mononuclear stromal cells are the proliferating component and mitoses may be readily found.

683

Differential This includes cherubism in young patients and cystic haemorrhagic degeneration
producing an aneurysmal bone cyst-like appearance in fibro-osseous lesions. Brown tumour of
hyperparathyroidism is a close histological mimic only definitively excluded by serology.
Cytology
Mononuclear and osteoclast-type multinucleate giant cells, hemosiderin pigment.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: located in jaws; clustered osteoclast giant cells; haemorrhage; spindle cell stroma
Desirable: lobular structure
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Most CGCGs do not recur after thorough curettage. A more radical surgical approach may be
necessary for aggressive or recurrent lesions. To limit the extent of resection of large lesions,
intralesional or systemic pharmacological agents such as corticosteroids, calcitonin, interferon or
denosumab may be considered, but the effects are inconsistent { 29751369 }.
#27757

A typical giant cell granuloma in the posterior body
and ramus of mandible showing a well delineated but
variably corticated apparently multilocular
radiolucency. Faint internal septa of osteoid and thin
bone are present, posteriorly producing a honeycomb
appearance.

trabecular bone and osteoid.

#24335

intermixed with mononuclear cells.

L#27770

Low-power view showing small osteoclast-like giant cells in a haemorrhagic background
and a relatively well demarcated margin to surrounding bone.
R#28337

Incisional biopsy through an expanded periosteal bone layer, showing an underlying
lobular lesion of cellular spindle and mononuclear cells, with numerous small
multinucleate cells and haemorrhage.

685

Peripheral giant cell granuloma
Definition
Peripheral giant cell granuloma (PGCG) is a reactive localized proliferation of mononuclear cells
and osteoclasts in a vascular stroma located in the soft tissues of gingiva or alveolar ridge mucosa.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA0D.Y Other specified disorders of gingival or edentulous alveolar ridge
Related terminology
Acceptable: Giant cell epulis
Not recommended: Peripheral reparative giant cell granuloma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
PGCG arises in the gingiva or alveolar ridge, more commonly of the mandible than maxilla
{ 29569293 ; 25240995 ; 17428697 }.
Clinical features
A sessile or pedunculated lump with a smooth, ulcerated or nodular surface and often a
characteristic red, maroon or purple colour. PGCG may reach several centimetres in diameter and
erosion of the underlying bone is found in almost one-third of cases { 17428697 ; 25240995 }.
Imaging: Radiographic assessment should exclude a central giant cell granuloma
Epidemiology
Unknown. PGCG is the commonest oral giant cell lesion. PGCG is more prevalent in women than
in men (1.2:1) and arises over a wide age distribution.
Etiology
Local irritation of the mucoperiosteum or the coronal part of the periodontal ligament by dental
calculus, plaque deposits, retained tooth roots or other chronic irritation.
Pathogenesis
Similar to the central giant cell granuloma, about 70% of PGCG harbour KRAS mutations, mainly
in codons 146 and 12, and 10% have FGFR1 p.C381R or FGFR1 p.N330I mutations { 30385747 }.
Notably, when associated with dental implants, PGCG also harbours KRAS mutations
{ 31758745 }. MAPK pathway activation is observed in PGCG irrespective of the presence of
mutations { 30385747 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Fleshy red-brown tissue due to vascularity and haemorrhage. When formalin-fixed it has a solid
consistency with dark brown coloration.
Histopathology
PGCG resembles central giant cell granuloma, with an unencapsulated proliferation of
mononuclear spindle shaped and polygonal cells with osteoclast multinucleate cells in a vascular
loose fibrous tissue. Foci of haemorrhage, haemosiderin pigment and scattered deposits of
immature bone are frequent. An inflammatory component of fibrous or pyogenic granuloma-like
hyperplasia may be present reflecting local irritant causes.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: located on gingiva or alveolar ridge; cellular mononuclear stroma; clustered osteoclasts;
haemorrhage; no origin from underlying intraosseous giant cell granuloma
Desirable: exclusion of hyperparathyroidism (very rarely associated with localised lesions)
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction

686

Recurrence after conservative surgical removal may be 16%. Curettage to periodontal ligament or
peripheral osteotomy reduce recurrence, especially when underlying bone is eroded { 29569293 }.
Any source of irritation should be removed.

#27354: The lesion forms a red or
maroon, gingival enlargement in
the left maxilla between two teeth.

#27153: This WSI shows an
unencapsulated proliferation of
mononuclear spindle shaped and
polygonal cells with innumerable
osteoclast-type multinucleated cells
in a loose fibrous connective tissue.

#27355: Mononuclear and osteoclast-type multinucleate giant cells in a vascular stroma
containing some haemosiderin, all covered by oral epithelium. The lesion is microscopically
identical to a central giant cell granuloma.

687

#27356: A complete lesion, a pedunculated polyp containing a cellular lobular mass of giant cells
with haemorrhage.

Cherubism
Definition
Cherubism is a rare autosomal dominant inherited bone disorder characterized by symmetrical
expansion of the maxilla and mandible by an osteoclast-like giant cell rich lesion.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
LD24.22 Cherubism
Related terminology
Not recommended: Familial fibrous dysplasia; juvenile fibrous dysplasia
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Both jaws are affected bilaterally, with the mandible more commonly affected than the maxilla
{ 23298620 }.
Clinical features
Slow, symmetrical expansion of the jaws develops in early childhood, usually before the age of 6
years. Maxillary expansion in severe cases may cause orbital displacement with scleral exposure,
producing the characteristic facial appearance of putti children or 'cherubs' in Renaissance
paintings. Females are usually more severely affected. Tooth displacement and impaction, root
resorption and agenesis are typical. Partial or complete regression occurs by adulthood
{ 25409853 }.
Association with odontogenic tumours is reported { 28721660 }.

688

Imaging: Radiologically, affected bones show a symmetrical well-defined radiolucent/hypodense,
multilocular ‘soap bubble’ like appearance with thinned cortex { 32620450 }. The fibrous tissue is
progressively replaced by new bone, leading to sclerosis.
Epidemiology
The incidence is unknown. Two thirds of cases are familial with complete penetrance in males, 6070% penetrance in females and variable expressivity; the others arise as de novo germline
alterations { 32620450 ; 28644570 }.
Etiology
Autosomal
dominant SH3BP2 mutations,
located
on
chromosome
4p16.3
{ 10364527 ; 10364528 }, have been identified in about 90% of cases of cherubism { 32620450 }.
The majority of mutations occur in exon 9, within a proline-rich sequence 6 amino acids long
{ 11381256 }.
Pathogenesis
The SH3BP2 mutations result in excessive generation of abnormally large osteoclasts
with constitutive activity { 30236129 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Similar to other giant cell lesions, red and haemorrhagic tissue with areas of cystic change.
Histopathology
The microscopic features are not specific and resemble those of other giant cell-rich lesions of the
jaws, including giant cell granuloma, giant cell lesions in Noonan and Jaffe Campanacci syndromes
{ 31705763 }, and brown tumour of hyperparathyroidism. Typically, abnormally large
multinucleate osteoclast-like giant cells lie in a monotonous loose or oedematous spindle cell
stroma with eosinophilic cuff-like perivascular deposits { 25409853 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: only posterior jaws affected; bilateral symmetrical lesions; giant cell lesions on histology
Desirable: detection of SH3BP2 germline mutation.
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Most cases regress after puberty, but some show continued growth with little regression. Surgery
should be performed only in severe cases, where it will provide a functional benefit { 23298620 }.

#24432: Panoramic radiograph showing bilateral multilocular radiolucencies in the posterior
mandible.
689

#24434: Much of lesions is oedematous fibrous tissue with sparse giant cells and sometimes
prominent vessels.

#27357: Vascular fibrous tissue with scattered multinucleated osteoclast-like giant cells,
haemorrhage and haemosiderin deposits.

690

#27936: CT coronal reformat showing massive bilateral enlargement of both mandibular rami,
with cortical thinning and perforation and an internal honeycomb structure of osteoid or woven
bone trabeculae.

#24431: Vascular fibrous tissue with scattered multinucleated osteoclast-like giant cells,
haemorrhage and haemosiderin deposits.

Aneurysmal bone cyst
Definition
Aneurysmal bone cyst (ABC) is a cystic or multicystic, expansile, osteolytic neoplasm composed
of blood-filled sinusoidal spaces lined by fibrous septa that contain osteoclast-type giant cells.
ICD-O coding
9260/0 Aneurysmal bone cyst.

691

ICD-11 coding
FB80.6 & XH23E0 Aneurysmal bone cyst & Aneurysmal bone cyst
Related terminology
Not recommended: Primary aneurysmal bone cyst; secondary aneurysmal bone cyst
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Over 60% of jaw ABCs occur in the mandible, usually posteriorly. Maxillary lesions have a more
uniform anatomic distribution. Other sites in the craniofacial complex can be affected { 31950466 }.
Clinical features
ABC presents as an enlargement of the jaw that is frequently painful. Teeth remain vital, however,
tooth mobility and displacement are common. ABCs can cause root resorption. Maxillary tumours
can extend to the sinuses and nose, and orbital involvement can lead to exophthalmos.
Imaging: Radiographically, there is a well-delineated, unilocular or multilocular radiolucency,
characteristically markedly expansile. Computed tomography (CT) imaging may show bone septa
compartmentalizing the lesion. CT and MRI studies may demonstrate characteristic fluid levels,
sometimes with perforation of the cortex and extension to adjacent soft tissue { 31950466 }.
Epidemiology
ABC is rare with an estimated annual incidence of approximately 0.15 per million { 10379320 },
1.5% of which arise in the jaws. All age groups are affected but over 80% occur in younger patients
usually during the first two decades of life. Both genders are equally affected, however, a male
predilection has been reported in ABCs of the jaws { 24931106 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
Fusion of USP6 and CDH11 is seen in 70% of cases { 15509545 }, affecting the stromal fibroblast
population only. Other fusion partners include CNBP, COL1A1, CTNNB1, EIF1, FOSL2, OMD,
PAFAH1B1, RUNX2, SEC31A, SPARC, STAT3, THRAP3, and USP9X { 30582658 }. Fusion with
any partner leads to upregulation of USP6. The biologic consequence of these genetic fusions is
the upregulation of the USP6-coding region that regulates several cellular processes such as
intracellular trafficking, matrix degradation and cell transformation { 31950466 ; 32011035 }. USP
translocation is not present in jaw giant cell granuloma { 24742829 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Haemorrhagic spongy tissue comprising multiple cysts separated by fibrous septa of various
thickness. Solid areas may be present and a rare completely solid form of ABC is recognized.
Histopathology
ABC is composed of blood filled or empty sinusoidal spaces lined by macrophages and bland
fibroblasts that are separated by fibrous septa containing scattered multinucleate osteoclastic giant
cells. Woven bone in septa can appear prominently basophilic and has been referred to as “bluebone” and this is characteristic if present, but not diagnostic. The solid variant can feature cellular
areas, that can be mitotically active, and few inconspicuous cystic spaces.
Differential The entities include ABC-like cystic haemorrhagic degeneration in a range of other
neoplasms including osteoblastoma, fibrous dysplasia, cemento-ossifying fibroma, other fibroosseous lesions and osteosarcoma.
Cytology
In a recent study in long bone ABC, concordance of fine needle aspiration cytology and diagnostic
biopsy was approximately 42% { 33181515 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Translocation involving the USP6 and several 5' partners including CDH11. Helpful in diagnosis of
the solid variant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: compatible radiological features; multicystic; fibrous septa with haemorrhage and
osteoclasts
Desirable: woven bone in septa; USP6 gene rearrangement
Staging
None

692

Prognosis and prediction
There is a risk of recurrence after curettage and en bloc resection may be necessary for large
destructive tumors { 16755186 }. The recurrence rate is approximately 10% with soft tissue
extension.

#24293
Aneurysmal bone cyst
Low power image showing blood filled sinusoidal spaces.

#24301
Aneurysmal bone cyst
Higher power of a blood filled sinusoidal space lined by multinucleated giant cells. The
features are relatively non-specific but the diagnosis was supported by cytogenetic
analysis showing rearrangement of the USP6 gene.

#27603

CT scan, axial view, showing a circumscribed, partially fluid filled mass with prominent
expansion in the right posterior mandible.

#27604
Aneurysmal bone cyst
Panoramic radiograph showing ABC in the posterior body and ramus of the mandible with
typical scalloped outline, cortical erosion and limited cortication.

694

Simple bone cyst
Definition
Simple bone cyst (SBC) is an intraosseous cystic cavity lined by a fibrous membrane
without epithelium, either empty or containing blood, serous or serosanguinous fluid
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
FB80.5 Solitary bone cyst.
Related terminology
Acceptable: Solitary bone cyst
Not recommended: Traumatic bone cyst; haemorrhagic bone cyst; unicameral bone cyst
Subtype(s)
Cystic haemorrhagic degeneration in florid cemento-osseous dysplasia has been termed SBC and
may account for up to a third of apparent jaw SBCs { 17448841 } but the age group is older and
shows a female predilection { 21217632 } and these cysts are typically more expansile and may
recur after treatment. Such cysts are probably not conventional SBC but mutational status is not
defined.
Localization
The mandible is most frequently affected with a predilection for the body (30792085). Bilateral or
multiple SBCs are fairly frequent (30792085; 9503460).
Clinical features
The vast majority of cases are asymptomatic and discovered on routine examination. Expansion is
unusual and limited.
Imaging: Radiographically SBC are well-delineated unilocular radiolucencies that 'scallop' between
the roots of associated vital teeth (9503460; 26850870). Multilocular presentation is rare and
typically only present in larger lesions. Cortical perforation, displacement of teeth or root resorption
are not observed and expansion is rare.
Epidemiology
SBCs are found predominantly in patients in their 2nd and 3rd decades, with no gender differences
{ 30792085 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Over half of SBC in the peripheral skeleton have been shown to harbour
either FUS::NFATC2 or EWSR1::NFATC2 fusion { 32991339 ; 33316098 }, one of the genetic
changes seen in round cell sarcoma with EWSR1::non-ETS fusions { 34081036 }. Jaw SBC have
not yet been analysed.
Macroscopic appearance
Sparse scrapings of fibrous tissue with bone fragments.
Histopathology
SBC has no epithelial lining and samples contain nondescript thin strips of loosely collagenous or
myxoid connective tissue with thin lace-like fragments of immature bone at the periphery.
Haemosiderin, cholesterol and sparse osteoclasts may be present. Spiky needle-like collagen
fibres may lie peripherally and can become densely mineralised, a useful diagnostic feature when
biopsy tissue is sparse { 22034879 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: typical radiological features; empty of fluid filled cavity at operation.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction

695

Healing is induced by bleeding following surgical exploration and curettage { 26850870 }.
Occasional spontaneous healing is reported { 26850870 ; 12058266 }. Occasional cases recur.

L#27358
Simple bone cyst
Scrapings from the wall of a simple bone cyst featuring bland fibrous tissue and bone
fragments.
R#27359
Simple bone cyst
Scraping from the wall of a simple bone cyst featuring delicate connective tissue and
irregular fragments of bone.

on the left. Note the lesion extending up in a rounded pattern between the roots of the
associated teeth
R#25866

of bone and minimal connective tissue

696

Bone and cartilage tumours
Fibro-osseous tumours and dysplasias

Cemento-osseous dysplasia
Definition
Cemento-osseous dysplasia (COD) is a non-neoplastic fibro-osseous lesion arising only in the
tooth-bearing areas of the jaws .
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA07.3 Disturbances in tooth formation, Florid cemento-osseous dysplasia 2E83.1 Benign
osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw & Periapical cemento-osseous
dysplasia of lower jaw
Related terminology
Not recommended: Osseous dysplasia; cemental dysplasia; cementoma
Subtype(s)
Periapical
cemento-osseous
dysplasia
(PCOD); Focal
cemento-osseous
dysplasia
(FocCOD); Florid cemento-osseous dysplasia (FCOD); Familial florid cemento-osseous dysplasia
(FFCOD).
Localization
PCOD predominantly involves the periapical bone of the anterior mandibular teeth, FocCOD a
single, usually posterior mandibular tooth and FCOD involves multiple tooth-bearing quadrants
{ 32565263 ; 29267533 }. FFCOD, almost exclusively, has the distribution of FCOD { 32315206 }.
Clinical features
COD are usually asymptomatic, non-expansile lesions associated with the roots of vital teeth. Some
cases of FocCOD and up to 25% of FCOD may expand the jaw, either directly or following cyst
formation, sometimes markedly { 27161103 }.FocCOD, FCOD and FFCOD are prone to develop
osteomyelitis in their late sclerotic stage, with pain and discharge { 27161103 ; 27422424 }. COD
may remain in edentulous jaw after tooth extraction. FFCOD has earlier age of onset than FCOD,
often affecting tooth eruption and is prone to marked expansion, usually in the anterior mandible
but sometimes multifocal { 32315206 } leading to confusion with familial gigantiform cementoma.
Imaging: Radiographic features are characteristic and often diagnostic. The early stage of all
subtypes of COD produce well-defined periapical radiolucencies continuous with periodontal
ligament, enlarging slowly before developing internal, usually central, radiopacities and patchy
sclerosis beyond a well-defined radiolucent rim. Later, lesions become radiodense with lobulated
dense mineralisation { 32565263 ; 29267533 }. FocCOD is more variable { 9394387 }.
Epidemiology
COD is the most common benign fibro-osseous lesion of the jaws with strong predilection for
middle-aged women { 25409854 ; 33878496 }, most commonly African and African-American
females, but with significant incidence in Asian { 24326826 } and Caucasian { 32565263 } females.
FFCOD has less marked racial and sex predilection { 32315206 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown for PCOD, FocCOD and FCOD. One family with FFCOD have been shown to carry a
mutation in the Anoctamin 5 gene (ANO5) that is proposed to destabilise this transmembrane ion
channel { 30996299 }. The mutated locus is distinct from that in gnathodiaphyseal dysplasia, in
which there are multiple cemento-ossifying fibromas { 27068316 }.

697

Macroscopic appearance
Multiple variably sized, gritty fragments of haemorrhagic bone and soft tissue.
Histopathology
All COD subtypes exhibit identical microscopic features dependant on the degree of maturation.
There are varying proportions of osteoid and woven bone, mature lamellar bone and rounded,
densely basophilic cementum-like mineralizations in a cellular fibrous connective tissue stroma of
loosely arranged collagen and prominently dilated, thin-walled vessels. Osteoblastic rimming is
rare. There is often focal high cellularity. Maturing lesions show increasing calcification with often
prominent reversal lines and reduced vascularity. Late stage lesions are characterised by
coalescence of calcified tissue to form hypocellular, sclerotic masses with limited stroma
{ 9377196 ; 30887390 ; 32565263 ; 33878496 }.
Differential Approximately 10% of cases may develop expansile cystic degeneration resembling
solitary bone cyst { 21683027 }, usually in the mandible in late disease. Expansile areas in
FFCOD may be difficult to distinguish from cemento-ossifying fibroma { 32315206 } if the
background disease is not appreciated.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: radiological features are almost always diagnostic
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Radiological diagnosis is usual, with periodic follow-up for expansion, cyst formation and
osteomyelitis.
Surgical intervention
indicated
exclusively
for
symptomatic
cases
{ 32565263 ; 27422424 } as osteomyelitis is a common complication. Cysts may recur following
surgery.

#27312
Cemento-osseous dysplasia
Irregular woven bone trabeculae and cementum-like particles in a cellular fibrovascular
connective tissue background.

698

#27314
Cemento-osseous dysplasia
Periapical form, with discrete mixed radiopaque and radiolucent lesions involving the
anterior tooth root apices.

#27315
Focal cemento osseous dysplasia
Focal cemento-osseous dysplasia, a similar but solitary lesion surrounded by well-defined
osteolytic rim.

699

#27316
Florid cemento-osseous dysplasia
Florid cemento-osseous dysplasia, a mixture of radiopaque and radiolucent lesions
involving the anterior and posterior mandibular alveolar bone centred on tooth root
apices.

#27318
Cemento-osseous dysplasia
Woven bone and osteoid in a focally hypercellular stroma.

700

#25859
Cemento-osseous dysplasia
Typical fragmented nature of biopsy specimens.

Segmental odontomaxillary dysplasia
Definition
Segmental odontomaxillary dysplasia (SOD) is a non-hereditary unilateral developmental disorder
characterized by segmental maxillary and soft tissue enlargement with dento-osseous
abnormalities and occasionally homolateral cutaneous manifestations.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA07.3 Disturbances in tooth formation
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Unilateral maxillary enlargement, almost equally right or left, in the premolar/molar region of the
maxilla.
Clinical features
Painless maxillary buccal and palatal enlargement, usually in the first or second decades, with
failure of tooth eruption and dental abnormalities clinically and radiographically. Enlargement
appears stable and non-progressive, starting in childhood and continuing until puberty. There is
frequently associated fibrous gingival hyperplasia { 2371053 }. The maxillary overgrowth extends
to the sinus, reducing its volume radiographically { 11027387 }.
Dental abnormalities include missing permanent teeth, usually premolars, retained primary molars
beyond the normal time of exfoliation, enamel and dentine hypoplasia and altered tooth shape and
size. Facial manifestations, often subtle, include hypertrichosis, hyper- or hypopigmentation
including Becker nevus, increased frequency of melanocytic nevi, erythema, and lip clefting
{ 29389339 ; 32893658 }.
There is ill-defined overlap of features with some related presentations and syndromes including
hemimaxillofacial dysplasia { 3477765 } and hemimaxillofacial enlargement, asymmetry of the face,
tooth abnormalities, and skin findings (HATS syndrome) { 25372264 }.
Imaging: Panoramic and periapical radiographs show a poorly defined area of abnormal bone,
often with a characteristic vertical trabeculation, and coarse radiopacities.
Epidemiology
SOD is rare and slightly more frequent in males.
Etiology

701

Unknown, proposed causes include prenatal trauma, endocrine abnormalities and bacterial or viral
infection.
Pathogenesis
The possibility that somatic PIK3CA or ACTB mutations contribute to the overgrowth has been
raised { 32500425 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Bone trephine or sample with tooth fragments.
Histopathology
Gingival biopsies may reveal fibrous hyperplasia with occasional dystrophic calcifications. Bone
biopsies reveal irregular bone spicules with multiple accentuated reversal lines. The bone appears
inert, without osteoblastic or osteoclastic activity and histological features are probably the end
stage of a much earlier developmental process. External root resorption of teeth with dysplastic
dentin and fibrosis of the pulp have been described { 29389339 }.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Localised swelling; soft tissues, bone and teeth abnormal; characteristic radiographic
features
Desirable: Inert bone histologically with defects of tooth formation (only if fibrous dysplasia requires
exclusion)
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
SOD shows slow or minimal enlargement until puberty. Surgery may be required for cosmetic and
functional purposes.

#24958
Segmental odontomaxillary dysplasia
Abnormal woven and lamellar bone architecture with accentuated and multiple reversal
and prominent resting lines associated with fibromyxoid tissue. Note retraction
artefact and lack of bone deposition.

702

#27319
Segmental odontomaxillary dysplasia
Fibrous and bony enlargement of the the maxillary alveolar mucosa (left side of image).
Retained primary molar on the same side.

#27320
Segmental odontomaxillary dysplasia
Sequential tomograms reveal absence of premolars, retained primary molars with root
resorption of the second molar and vertical trabeculation in the affected area of the
maxilla.

703

#27321
Segmental odontomaxillary dysplasia
Coarse radiopacities affecting the left side of the maxilla extending to the zygomatic bone
and floor of the orbit.

Fibrous dysplasia
Definition
Fibrous dysplasia (FD) is a genetically based disorder of bone growth in which bone is replaced by
abnormal bone and fibrous tissue.
ICD-O coding
8818/0 Fibrous dysplasia
ICD-11 coding
FB80.0 Fibrous dysplasia of bone.
Related terminology
Acceptable: Craniofacial fibrous dysplasia (extending across more than one bone)
Subtype(s)
Monostotic FD; polyostotic FD; craniofacial FD
Localization
Monostotic FD disease affects a single bone (term not usually employed for the maxilla or facial
bones) while polyostotic FD affects multiple bones. The term craniofacial FD applies
when multiple adjacent bones in the craniofacial region are affected, extending across suture
lines. The craniofacial bones are affected most commonly, typically involved in approximately 10%
of patients with monostotic FD and in up to 100% with polyostotic FD. Maxilla and paranasal
regions are more frequently affected than mandible { 22981804 }.
Clinical features
Patients generally present with painless swelling of bones or disease may be detected incidentally.
FD of the jaws causes oro-facial asymmetry. Tooth eruption is normal and teeth remain aligned but
with arch distortion as the jaw expands. Tooth structure is normal. Maxillary FD may cause nasal
obstruction and chronic sinusitis. Skull base FD may compress cranial nerves resulting in visual
impairment or hearing loss. Growth of FD tends to slow or stop with skeletal maturation. Survey for
extent and possible syndromic association may be necessary { 31196103 }. In McCune-Albright
syndrome, cafe-au-lait skin pigmentation and endocrine abnormalities are present { 18489744 }.

704

Imaging: The radiologic appearance varies with the stage of development and age of the patient.
Early lesions may appear radiolucent gradually becoming more radiopaque over time, merging
almost imperceptibly with unaffected bone at the periphery. Abnormal bone extends around tooth
roots with minimal displacement. The lesions of FD are characteristically described as having a
ground-glass or fingerprint appearance in the late stage { 14697371 ; 30887390 ; 31196103 }.
Epidemiology
FD is an uncommon disease and is usually diagnosed in children and young adults. The
monostotic form accounts for 80% to 85% of all cases and shows no gender
predilection. Polyostotic FD is more frequent in females { 14697371 }.
Etiology
Genetic factors
FD occurs with endocrinopathies in McCune-Albright syndrome { 8336219 ; 22981804 } and with
myxomas in Mazabraud syndrome { 29030278 }.
Pathogenesis
Fibrous dysplasia is caused by postzygotic activating missense mutations in the GNAS gene
(20q13.32), which encodes the alpha subunit of the stimulatory G protein (Gsα) { 22245114 }. The
result is arrest of differentiation of proliferating cells of osteoblastic lineage to mature osteoblasts
{ 29282319 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Sample type depends on sampling method. Lesional bone resembles normal bone, but may be
compressible and is gritty on sectioning { 30887390 }.
Histopathology
A lesion consists of bone with variable amounts of fibrous tissue. The fibrous component is
composed of spindle cells without atypia which can be quite cellular but becomes less so and more
fibrous with age. Mitoses are uncommon but small numbers may be observed in children. The bone
is woven in nature and forms irregular curvilinear trabeculae, commonly likened to Chinese
characters or C, S and Y-shaped trabeculae, which are not rimmed by osteoblasts. The immature
bone merges almost imperceptibly with the surrounding lamellar bone and it may be difficult to
distinguish lesional from non-lesional bone. Jaw FD develops osteoblast rimming and lamellar
differentiation as it matures. Cartilage may form, but is rare in the jaws { 8734416 }.
Differential
Histological appearances are characteristic but not completely specific and
differential diagnosis includes other jaw fibro-osseous lesions including juvenile trabecular,
psammomatoid and cemento-ossifying fibromas, cemento-osseous dysplasias and, in small
specimens, aneurysmal bone cyst or giant cell lesions. Low grade central osteosarcoma may
resemble FD closely but is rare in the jaws { 20975343 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Definitive diagnosis of FD requires detection of somatic GNAS mutation, by next generation or
Sanger sequencing { 31196103 } or mutation-specific restriction enzyme digest PCR { 17493233 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: clinical, radiological, and histomorphological correlation; bone replaced by fibrous tissue
and woven bone
Desirable: GNAS mutation analysis
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
FD follows a benign course and growth reduces in adulthood. Treatment is focused on preventing
function loss from complications such as optic neuropathy or hearing loss, and minimizing
disfigurement, pathologic fracture risk, and pain { 27493082 ; 31196103 }. Hypophosphatemia may
be used to measure activity and extent of skeletal lesions { 31196103 }. FD carries a low risk of
sarcomatous transformation and the development of cystic haemorrhagic degeneration resembling
aneurysmal bone cyst and giant cell lesions { 32619461 }. FD patients taking bisphosphonates
have an elevated risk of medication related osteonecrosis of the jaw { 27493082 ; 31196103 }.

705

#24474
Fibrous dysplasia
Late stage disease showing lamellar maturation but retaining irregularly shaped
trabeculae and often lacking osteoblastic rimming.

#27323

Radiograph of an area affected by fibrous dysplasia, note the ground glass appearance
and loss of lamina dura and cortex definition without tooth displacement.

706

#27324

CT , axial view, shows an even mixed radiolucency lesion of fibrous dysplasia with
marked expansion of the maxilla but no tooth displacement.

#27325

Early stage or active area of the disease with a hypercellular fibrous tissue and
condensing woven bone trabeculae, with a focus of giant cells.

#27326

Maturing area of the lesion with a less cellular stroma and woven bone, note the
osteoblast rimming which is common in maturing fibrous dysplasia of the jaws, unlike
fibrous dysplasia of long bones.

Typical example showing areas of active fibroblastic proliferation with early bone

708

Juvenile trabecular ossifying fibroma
Definition
Juvenile trabecular ossifying fibroma (JTOF) is a benign fibro-osseous neoplasm, characterised by
trabeculae of cellular osteoid and woven bone, rapid growth, and a predilection for the jaws of
children and adolescents.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
LD24.2Y & XH6M86 Other specified bone diseases with disorganised development of skeletal
components & Ossifying fibroma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Trabecular ossifying fibroma; trabecular juvenile ossifying fibroma.
Not recommended: Juvenile (active or aggressive) ossifying fibroma.
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
JTOF predominantly affects the jaws, with almost equal distribution between the maxilla and
mandible { 11925539 ; 31285096 }. There is variation in diagnosis, and it is not clear
whether JTOF in extragnathic craniofacial bones { 31285096 } is the same entity.
Clinical features
Usually painless jaw expansion with rapid growth and tooth displacement
{ 23052375 ; 31285096 }. Maxillary lesions may cause nasal obstruction and epistaxis
{ 25409854 }.
Imaging: Radiographic examination shows a well-defined radiolucency or mixed radiolucentradiopaque lesion often with focal cortication { 31285096 ; 33878496 }. The radiolucency is more
often unilocular than multilocular { 31285096 }. Tooth displacement occurs in over half of cases
{ 31285096 }. Less frequent findings include cortical perforation and tooth root
resorption { 31285096 }.
Epidemiology
JTOF is rare { 31950477 } and arises predominantly in children and adolescents (mean 11.3 years),
with <20% of cases affecting individuals older than 15 years { 31285096 ; 31950477 }. However,
variation in diagnosis may distort the age distribution and most typical JTOF probably arise before
the age of 10 years. There is no sex predilection { 31285096 ; 31950477 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
MDM2 and RASAL1 amplification have been detected in 7 of 10 JTOFs { 24925056 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Yellowish white, gritty fragments of variable size or an intact tumour mass; the cut surface may
exhibit brown curvilinear strands { 25409854 ; 23052375 }.
Histopathology
Unencapsulated tumour with elongate strands, trabeculae and sheets of osteoid densely rimmed
by osteoblasts and in a fibroblastic stroma { 25409854 }. Newly formed osteoid blends
imperceptibly with the surrounding stroma. Over time, the osteoid strands undergo central
mineralization to form anastomosing trabeculae of woven bone. The stroma exhibits zones of
variable cellularity, often hypercellular, and scattered aggregates of osteoclastic giant cells are
usually evident.
Differential Appearance may be worrisome for osteosarcoma. Garland-like, curvilinear strands of
oedema, haemorrhage, osteoclasts, and pseudocystic degeneration form the characteristic brown
curvilinear strands seen macroscopically in JTOF; this feature has not been noted in cementoossifying or psammomatoid ossifying fibromas { 23052375 }. Haemorrhagic cystic degeneration
may resemble aneurysmal bone cyst { 22104705 ; 23305772 ; 31285096 }. A trabecular woven
bone pattern may be seen in other types of cemento-ossifying fibroma and other fibroosseous lesions; this feature alone, is insufficient for diagnosis.

709

Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: onset in childhood or adolescence; rapid expansion; well demarcated radiographically;
very cellular benign fibro-osseous lesion with prominent osteoid
Desirable: site in jaws
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Approximately 21% of cases recur { 31285096 }, from 4% following resection to 53% following
enucleation or curettage. Enucleation plus curettage or peripheral ostectomy may mitigate
recurrence risk while avoiding surgical disfigurement { 31285096 }. JTOF may attain a large size
{ 34184157 }
but,
malignant
transformation
has
not
been
reported
{ 25409854 ; 26740351 ; 29027609 }.

#27752
Juvenile trabecular ossifying fibroma
In actively growing lesions, disorganised sheets of osteoid may raise concern for
osteosarcoma.

A more fibrous area of cellular plump fibroblasts with thin trabeculae of woven bone.

#27755

Anastomosing trabeculae of woven bone containing plump osteocytes and lying in a very
cellular fibroblastic stroma.

Mandibular JTOF in a child aged 12 years, CT coronal and axial reformats left, panoramic
radiograph right, showing well-defined scalloped margin, focally perforated cortex and
internal mineralisation, no root resorption but early root growth deflection of the second
premolar.

L#27456
