Psammomatoid ossifying fibroma
Definition
Psammomatoid ossifying fibroma (PsOF) is a benign fibro-osseous neoplasm of the craniofacial
skeleton characterised histologically by spherical ossicles.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
LD24.2Y & XH6M86 Other specified bone diseases with disorganised development of skeletal
components & Ossifying fibroma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Juvenile psammomatoid ossifying fibroma
Not recommended: Juvenile (active or aggressive) ossifying fibroma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
PsOF arises in the jaws and craniofacial bones, 90% occurring around the paranasal sinuses and
orbit { 8630892 ; 1843064 ; 11925539 ; 31285096 }.
Clinical features
Usually asymptomatic, rapidly enlarging bony swelling, although pain is possible { 31285096 }.
Additional findings may include nasal obstruction, epistaxis, proptosis, and visual disturbance
depending on site { 26740351 ; 31950477 }.
Imaging: Radiographically a well-defined mass with variable opacification, early lesions being
predominantly radiolucent, later developing ground-glass opacification with central radiolucency,
homogeneous
ground-glass
opacification
or
discrete
calcifications
{ 25457891 ; 26740351 ; 31950477 }. The radiolucent component is more often unilocular than
multilocular { 31285096 }. There may be tooth displacement, cortical thinning or perforation, and
intracranial extension { 1843064 ; 31285096 }.

712

Epidemiology
PsOF is rare { 31950477 }. Peak incidence is in the second to fourth decades with a broad range
{ 11925539 ; 25409854 ; 31285096 } and male:female ratio approximately 1.4:1 { 31285096 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Breakpoints at Xq26 and 2q33 with reciprocal translocation or insertion were reported in 3 apparent
PsOF
(reported
as
'cemento-ossifying
fibromas
of
the
orbit')
{ 8625058 }. MDM2 and RASAL1 amplification was present in 2 of 3 cases { 24925056 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Large, intact bone pieces with a yellow, gritty cut surface { 1843064 ; 25409854 ; 33878496 }.
Histopathology
An unencapsulated lesion containing multiple spherical ossicles in a hypercellular stroma of bland
ovoid and spindle cells. The spherules are usually small and relatively uniform with osteoid rims
and wispy eosinophilic collagen fibres at the periphery and no osteoblastic rimming { 33878496 }.
Ossicles fuse together in late disease, forming lumpy trabeculae. Although described as
psammomatoid, the ossicles are much larger than psammoma bodies, not as sharply defined and
not lamellar. Mitotic figures may be seen occasionally, and multinucleated giant cells are an
infrequent finding.
Differential The small densely basophilic cementum-like droplet calcifications in cemento-ossifying
fibroma (COF) are more similar to psammoma bodies than the ossicles in PsOF { 1843064 } and
should not lead to misdiagnosis of COF as a PsOF. PsOF may contain minority areas of more
typical fibro-osseous architecture with woven bone trabeculae and dense basophilic bone.
Secondary changes include myxoid change and haemorrhagic cystic degeneration resembling
aneurysmal bone cyst { 11925539 ; 30090219 ; 31285096 }.
Cytology
Cytology has not been shown to be specific.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: well-demarcated radiographically; site in craniofacial bone or jaw; benign fibroosseous lesion histologically; predominant ossicle pattern
Desirable: onset in the second to fourth decades
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
PsOF recurs in approximately 30% of cases following surgical excision { 1843064 ; 31285096 } but
no features predict recurrence. Malignant transformation has not been reported
{ 25409854 ; 32257561 }.
#27341
Psammomatoid ossifying fibroma
Typical facial bone lesion showing early
maturation with minimal internal
mineralisation, marked expansion and
cortical thinning.

713

#27343
Psammomatoid ossifying fibroma
Numerous closely packed spherical ossicles or psammomatoid mineralisations in a
cellular fibrous stroma.

L#27346
Psammomatoid ossifying fibroma
Spherical ossicles with a wispy eosinophilic collagenous periphery.
R#26747
Psammomatoid ossifying fibroma
H and E stained slide demonstrating psammomatoid calcifications within hypercellular
fibrous stroma.

714

Familial gigantiform cementoma
Definition
Familial gigantiform cementoma (FGC) is a rare benign fibro-osseous lesion of the jaws,
characterised by early onset and multifocal grossly expansile growth.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2E83.0 & XH6W94 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of skull or face
& Gigantiform cementoma
2E83.1 & XH6W94 Benign osteogenic tumours of bone or articular cartilage of lower jaw
& Gigantiform cementoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: gigantiform cementoma
Not recommended: florid osseous dysplasia, expansive osseous dysplasia
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Disease affects the jaws, with multifocal involvement in two to four quadrants { 25409854 }.
Clinical features
There is rapidly progressive, painless expansion, resulting in significant deformity, orbital distortion
and possible airway compromise { 10077086 ; 11312460 ; 25409854 }. Many cases of florid
cemento-osseous dysplasia have been diagnosed as FGC, particularly cases of the familial form
{ 32315206 }, but the degree of expansion is usually less marked and patients are generally older
{ 27161103 }. Presentations of gnathodiaphyseal dysplasia and large cemento-ossifying fibromas
are also often mistaken for FGC.
Imaging: Radiographic examination shows well-defined, markedly expansile radiolucencies with
associated calcifications; lesions often cross the midline { 11312460 ; 21830054 ; 25409854 }.
Features of cemento-osseous dysplasia should be absent.
Epidemiology
FGC is extremely rare { 26945411 }. Onset is in early childhood, with continuous growth into early
adulthood { 25409854 }. Cases have been described in various countries, with 55% from Asia.
Etiology
Unknown. Autosomal dominant inheritance with a high level of penetrance and variable expressivity
{ 25409854 } is proposed; other cases are sporadic { 11312460 ; 25284619 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Lobular mineralized masses { 22378756 }.
Histopathology
The histopathologic features are similar to those of cemento-ossifying fibroma and other fibroosseous lesions. Immature bony trabeculae and cementum-like calcifications are seen within a
hypercellular fibroblastic stroma. The cementum-like calcifications appear as hypocellular,
spheroidal to curvilinear structures, which may be surrounded by radiating Sharpey fibers as
visualized under polarized light { 25409854 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: onset in childhood or adolescence; multifocal/multiquadrant involvement; very marked
expansion; benign fibro-osseous histology analogous to cemento-ossifying fibroma
Desirable: rapid progression followed by slower growth later in life; family history
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction

715

Surgical management is complicated by extensive disease and rapid regrowth, complete resection
is often not practical { 26945411 }. Surgical recontouring or partial excision typically results in
regrowth, which may occur at an accelerated rate. Although the condition is characterized by a
rapidly progressive growth phase, spontaneous stabilization may occur later in life { 25284619 }.

#23286
Familial gigantiform cementoma
Cementum-like calcifications appearing as spheroidal to curvilinear deposits of
hypocellular, basophilic material.

#27771
Familial gigantiform cementoma
Tumour in a 3-year-old female, a massive expansion of the jaws causing marked facial
deformity.

716

Benign maxillofacial bone and cartilage tumours

Osteoma
Definition
Benign bone forming neoplasm consisting of mature bone, restricted almost exclusively to the jaws
and craniofacial bones.
ICD-O coding
9180/0 Osteoma
ICD-11 coding
2E83.Z & XH4818 Benign osteogenic tumours of unspecified site & Osteoma NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: Exostosis
Subtype(s)
Surface (periosteal) and central (endosteal) subtypes.
Localization
In the jaws, the mandible is more often affected than the maxilla { 18154576 }. Sinuses are a site
of predilection, most commonly the frontal sinus followed by the other sinuses
{ 21820784 ; 25663319 }.
Clinical features
Central osteomas are often asymptomatic while surface osteomas may present with a slow growing
swelling, facial distortion or altered dental occlusion. Sinus/orbital tumours may present with
headache or pain { 21820784 ; 26044223 }.
Imaging: Osteomas are dense radiopacities with well-defined margins.
Epidemiology
Osteomas occur over a wide age range but mostly in the 3rd to 5th decades, with an equal gender
distribution { 25663319 ; 26044223 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Genetic factors
Osteomas may be a manifestation of Gardners syndrome, an autosomal dominant disorder
associated with a germline mutation in the APC gene { 31070935 } and may be the first
manifestation.
Pathogenesis
Unknown, osteomas have a simple karyotype { 31741049 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Bony mass, surface osteomas may be widely pedunculated to underlying bone. Central osteomas
merge with surrounding intramedullary bone.
Histopathology
Osteomas are composed mostly of lamellar bone, compact or trabecular, in which osteoblasts and
osteoclasts are usually inconspicuous. Some contain osteoblastoma-like areas that are thought to
represent a reactive remodelling process and do not imply more aggressive behaviour
{ 19792048 }. Up to 40% of sinus/orbital osteomas contain such areas { 25663319 }. Fibro-osseous
lesions, sclerosing osteomyelitis and ossification in a fibrous epulis may all mimic an osteoma
{ 26044223 }.
Central (endosteal) subtype of osteoma are also known as bone islands. Unlike surface osteomas,
bone islands arise more frequently in long bones and are a mixture of hamartomas and true
neoplasms. Rare extra-osseous osteoma occurs in soft tissue { 26044223 }. Osteomas in
Gardner's syndrome often arise near the mandibular angle and may be multiple.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

717

Essential: compatible radiological features; surface origin (surface type); mature lamellar bone,
solid cortical or medullary patterns
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Growth is slow. Resection is performed for symptomatic osteomas; recurrence is unusual
{ 18154576 ; 21820784 }.

#27362
Osteoma. surface type, Osteoma of maxillary sinus with a dense smooth swelling of
compact mature bone with focal more cellular osteoblast like areas.centrally.
#27170
Osteoma
Surface type compact osteoma with multiple fragments of tissue, showing both
osteoclastic and osteoblastic activity.

Osteochondroma
Definition
Osteochondroma is a benign neoplasm forming a bony projection with a cartilaginous cap, with
continuity between the marrow cavity of the tumour and underlying bone.
ICD-O coding
9210/0 Osteochondroma
ICD-11 coding
2E83.Y & XH5Y87 Benign osteogenic tumour of other specified site & Osteochondroma
Related terminology
Not recommended: Osteochondromatous exostosis
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Osteochondromas are rare in the craniofacial bones, usually involving the mandibular condyle
and coronoid process { 22727110 }.
Clinical features
Craniofacial osteochondromas often cause swelling, asymmetry, trismus and TMJ dysfunction
{ 21367506 ; 22727110 }, but may be chance radiographic findings. Osteochondromas may be
multiple, but multiple lesions usually affect long bones.
Imaging: Uneven
enlargement
or
protrusion
from
the
condyle
radiographically { 22727110 ; 28110943 }, unlike the diffuse condylar enlargement of condylar
hyperplasia.
Epidemiology

718

Osteochondroma of the jaw manifests in the 2nd to 4th decades, slightly older than at other sites, and
unlike other sites, often continues to grow slowly after puberty { 7629631 }. A slight female
predominance is seen { 21367506 }.
Etiology
Trauma may be an aetiologic factor and rarely prior radiotherapy{ 21367506 }.
Pathogenesis
Homozygous deletions of the EXT1 gene are associated with sporadic osteochondromas,
supporting a neoplastic rather than developmental condition { 33622860 }. Germline mutations
of EXT-1 and EXT-2 are
associated
with
Hereditary Multiple
Osteochondroma(HME)/Multiple Osteochondroma (MO) syndrome but the facial bones are not
usually affected { 18271966 ; 33622860 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Bony mass, sessile or pedunculated, with a cartilage cap.
Histopathology
Osteochondroma is a cartilage capped bony projection from the external bone surface showing
cortical and medullary continuity with the parent bone. There is a surface layer of fibrous tissue, a
middle layer that mimics growth plate cartilage with endochondral ossification, merging with
underlying mature trabecular bone { 28110943 ; 31842965 }. Binucleate cells, nodularity and
cystic/
mucoid
changes
may
be seen,
but
neither
mitoses
nor
marked pleomorphism { 22555180 }.
Differential While a cartilaginous cap above 20mm in thickness in adults must raise the suspicion
of secondary chondrosarcoma, occurrence in the facial bones is very rare { 33622860 }. Criteria for
histologic distinction from condylar hyperplasia are poorly defined { 28110943 ; 31842965 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: pedunculated or broad based bony exostosis; cartilaginous cap with growth plate-like
architecture; continuity with underlying bone cortex and marrow
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence following excision is rare, unless incompletely removed { 22727110 ; 33622860 }.

#27370
Osteochondroma
Cartilaginous cap covered by periosteum at the surface, merging with underlying
trabecular bone.

719

Osteoblastoma
Definition
Osteoblastoma is a benign but aggressive bone forming tumour with large osteoblasts forming
sheets and prominent osteoblastic rimming on woven bone, and greater than 20mm in diameter.
ICD-O coding
9200/1 Osteoblastoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E83.Z & XH4316 Benign osteogenic tumour of unspecified site & Osteoblastoma NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
10% of osteoblastomas are found in the craniofacial bones, most often in the body of the mandible.
They may be intra-osseous or periosteal { 16129654 }.
Clinical features
Osteoblastoma may be asymptomatic or present with localised swelling and pain { 17052641 }.
Imaging: Radiographs show a circumscribed round to oval lesion varying from radiolucent to radioopaque, usually without a sclerotic border or periosteal reaction, 20mm or greater { 17052641 }.
The clinical and radiologic appearance is sometimes more aggressive and may mimic malignancy
{ 21181315 }.
Epidemiology
A rare tumour that occurs mostly in the 2nd to 3rd decades with a slight female predominance, unlike
long bone lesions { 17052641 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Recurrent rearrangement of FOS or, less frequently, FOSB define osteoblastoma and osteoid
osteoma { 29858576 } and are found in cementoblastoma, suggesting a pathogenic relationship
{ 33653978 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Curettings of red to tan tissue reflecting vascularity. If resected, bone expansion with well defined
borders is typical { 8119712 }.
Histopathology
Osteoblastoma is composed of anastomosing trabeculae of bone and osteoid, rimmed by plump
osteoblasts, sometimes with densely mineralised foci and set in a loose richly vascular fibrous
stroma. Mitoses are rare { 16129654 }. Lack of nuclear atypia, permeative growth into surrounding
bone and atypical mitoses distinguish osteoblastoma from osteosarcoma { 8119712 }. In some
cases, the osteoblasts may appear larger and epithelioid but this does not indicate a clinically
aggressive course. A tumour size greater than 40mm and an anatomic site that makes removal
difficult are better predictors of aggressive clinical behaviour { 21181315 ; 25409851 }
Differential This includes osteoid osteoma in a lesion less than 20 mm in diameter.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The translocations of FOS and FOSB identified in osteoblastoma are extremely rare in
osteosarcoma, and FOS immunohistochemistry may be a helpful diagnostic tool in this setting, but
only if there has been short decalcification { 31768625 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: well demarcated radiologically; 20mm diameter or larger; bone tumour trabeculae of
woven bone; prominent plump osteoblasts; vascular stroma.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence may follow curettage or incomplete removal { 25409851 }.

720

#27592
Osteoblastoma
Circumscribed edge and characteristic "blue bone" appearance, the spaces in osteoid are
filled by multilayered large eosinophilic osteoblasts.

#27594
Osteoblastoma
Radiographic appearance of osteoblastoma in panoramic radiograph.

721

#27595
Osteoblastoma
CT showing a well defined but irregular outline and radiolucent rim with dense patchy
mineralisation centrally.

#27596
Osteoblastoma
Immunohistochemistry showing strong nuclear positivity for FOS transcription factor in
osteoblastoma.

722

#27602
Osteoblastoma
Irregular interconnected trabeculae of osteoid, partially mineralised and with resting and
reversal lines. There is a prominent sometimes multilayered osteoblast rim with scattered
osteoclasts.

#31914
Osteoblastoma
Prominent osteoblastic rimming with a layer of large osteoblasts several cells thick on the
bone surface.

723

#27189
Osteoblastoma
There are numerous fragments of tissue, with anastomosing trabeculae of bone and
osteoid, rimmed by plump osteoblasts set in a richly vascular fibrous connective tissue
stroma.

Chondroblastoma
Definition
Chondroblastoma is a benign tumour of bone composed of chondroblasts forming sheets and
islands of eosinophilic chondroid matrix, with or without chicken-wire calcifications.
ICD-O coding
9230/0 Chondroblastoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E82.Z & XH4NK2 Benign osteogenic tumour of unspecified site & Chondroblastoma NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Chondroblastomas (CB) involve predominantly the epiphyseal area of long bones. When in the
head and neck, CB are located mainly around the temporomandibular joint and the squamous part
of the temporal bone { 15875910 ; 21504272 }.
Clinical features
The most frequent clinical symptom is pain while hearing loss, tinnitus and vertigo may be present
when tumours are located in the temporal bone, and trismus if around the temporomandibular joint
{ 15875910 ; 20614285 }.
Imaging: Chondroblastomas of the temporal bone have a well-demarcated, lobulated radiolucent
appearance with foci of calcifications { 15875910 }. Cystic haemorrhagic degeneration may
produce aneurysmal bone cyst-like changes with fluid levels on MRI { 6470260 ; 10471886 }.
Epidemiology
Chondroblastomas of the head and neck region are rare with about 100 cases reported in the
literature { 32603862 }. While most chondroblastomas occur in the second decade of life, patients
with skull tumours are slightly older, presenting in their third or fourth decade of life { 20614285 }.
A slight male predominance (1.3:1) has been reported for temporal chondroblastomas
{ 32603862 }.
Etiology
724

Unknown
Pathogenesis
H3-3A and H3-3B mutations drive chondroblastoma, with the H3-3B:c.110A>T p.Lys37Met (K36M)
mutation being most common { 24162739 ; 27174990 ; 33779999 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Well-defined lesions with a thin sclerotic rim and a soft to gritty consistency. Haemorrhagic areas
may be present.
Histopathology
The tumour cells are uniform, eosinophilic and polygonal with well-defined borders and nuclear
grooves. The cells are intermingled with an amorphous eosinophilic matrix with osteoclast-like giant
cells and varying amounts of 'chicken-wire' calcification { 5051664 ; 14880556 }. Cystic
haemorrhagic degeneration is frequent. Tumours harbouring mutations in the H3-3A or H3-3B gene
resulting in expression of H3 p.K37M (K36M) mutant protein, show consistent
nuclear immunoreactivity with a mutant-specific antibody K36M { 26844533 ; 33779999 }.
Differential This includes aneurysmal bone cyst, clear cell chondrosarcoma, chondromyxoid
fibroma and Langerhans cell histiocytosis.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Detection of the H3-3B:c.110A>T p.K37M (K36M) mutation is highly specific for chondroblastoma
{ 26457357 ; 33779999 }, and may be useful in selected cases.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: sheets of chondroblasts; scattered osteoclast; eosinophilic chondroid matrix.
Desirable: fine network of 'chicken-wire' calcification; histone H3 mutation or H3 p.Lys37Met
(K36M) immunopositivity
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Up to 50% of cases recur, metastasis has been reported only rarely (<1% of cases) { 3604981 }.

L#24839
Chondroblastoma
H3-3B diffuse nuclear expression in the chondroblastic cells. H3-3B immunostain.
R#27371
Chondroblastoma
Oval to polygonal chondroblasts associated with an eosinophilic cartilaginous matrix.

#27646
Chondroblastoma
Expansile and well delineated tumour in the wall of the right sphenoidal sinus. CT, lower
coronal reconstruction.

#24837
Chondroblastoma
Polygonal chondroblasts will well-defined borders associated with chondro-osteoid matrix.

Chondromyxoid fibroma
Definition
Chondromyxoid fibroma (CMF) is a benign lobulated chondroid neoplasm with a zonal architecture
composed of chondroid, myxoid and myofibroblastic areas.
ICD-O coding
9241/0 Chondromyxoid fibroma
ICD-11 coding
2E82.Z & XH89S0 Benign osteogenic tumour of unspecified site & Chondromyxoid fibroma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most craniofacial CMFs occur in the jaw and sinonasal bones, but any craniofacial bone can be
affected
{ 9540086 ; 9596266 ; 12946671 ; 14614877 ; 16165913 ; 21544896 ; 26929898 ; 29324473 }.
Clinical features
Depending on the site, there may be tinnitus, visual disturbances, headaches, hearing loss, and
sinonasal congestion { 9596266 ; 26929898 ; 31754576 ; 32209521 }.
Imaging: Radiographically, craniofacial CMFs are similar in appearance to CMFs in other locations.
They are primarily lytic, often with a sclerotic rim and frequently demonstrate cortical thinning or
erosion { 9596266 ; 20614285 }. Matrix mineralization occurs in approximately 10% and is usually
focal { 9596266 }. A subset of craniofacial CMFs develop on the surface of bone { 29324473 }.
Epidemiology
CMF is rare and 5% of all cases involve craniofacial bones { 9596266 ; 20614285 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
The majority of CMFs harbour a recombination of the glutamate receptor gene (GRM1) with several
5′ partner genes, which represent strong promoters, leading to high GRM1 expression. This is

727

considered the driver event for approximately 90% of CMFs { 24658000 }. GRM1 expression is
absent or very low in other cartilaginous tumours { 24658000 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lobular grey white tumour with smooth surface.
Histopathology
CMF consists of a lobular proliferation of spindled and stellate cells with abundant eosinophilic
cytoplasm and a chondromyxoid background { 9596266 }. The lobules show a hypocellular center
and a hypercellular periphery with intermingled osteoclast-like giant cells. In a third of cases there
are scattered coarse calcifications but hyaline cartilage is only occasionally seen { 9596266 }.
Cytologic atypia may occur, however, mitoses are uncommon { 9596266 }. In approximately 10%
of cases, cystic haemorrhagic degeneration produces aneurysmal bone cyst-like changes
{ 9596266 }.
Immunohistochemistry: IHC is typically not required. CMF shows variable staining with SMA, S100,
EMA, and SOX9, and is negative for cytokeratins and GFAP { 2263591 ; 19801163 ; 29324473 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not usually needed for diagnosis. If required, upregulation of GRM1 expression can help
distinguish CMF from its mimics { 24658000 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Lobulated lesion; zonal distribution of spindled and stellate cells; chondromyxoid matrix;
condensation of lesional cells at the periphery of lobules, admixed with multinucleate giant cells.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis is excellent, even for recurrent tumours. Recurrence is more common in craniofacial
CMFs,
due
to
challenges
obtaining
clear
surgical
margins
{ 20614285 ; 25217119 ; 26929898 ; 29324473 }.

#24628
Chondromyxoid fibroma
Foci of coarse calcification.

#27372
Chondromyxoid fibroma
CT head, axial view, showing an expansile, osteolytic tumour with matrix mineralization, a
sclerotic margin and cortical thinning, involving the ethmoid bone and left frontal sinus.

#27373
Chondromyxoid fibroma
Stellate cells with prominent eosinophilic cytoplasmic processes embedded in a myxoid
matrix.

729

#27377
Chondromyxoid fibroma
Lobular tumour with cellular condensation at the periphery of the lobules.

#24631
Chondromyxoid fibroma of the mandible
Multiple bits of bone with a tumour composed of stellate cells with prominent eosinophilic
cytoplasmic processes embedded in a myxoid matrix.

Desmoplastic fibroma of bone
Definition
Desmoplastic fibroma of bone (DFB) is a locally aggressive fibroblastic/myofibroblastic tumour
composed of benign spindle cells embedded in a collagenous background, mimicking desmoidtype fibromatosis.
ICD-O coding

730

8823/1 Desmoplastic fibroma
ICD-11 coding
2F7B & XH6YK5 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of bone and articular cartilage & Desmoplastic
fibroma
Related terminology
Not recommended: Desmoid tumour of bone; fibromatosis of jaw
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The jaws are a site of predilection. In the jaws, 82% of DFB affect the mandible, 70% the posterior
body and angle { 16360612 ; 25113037 ; 27816276 ; 32215029 ; 33204607 }. The remainder
involve the maxilla, usually anteriorly.
Clinical features
Most individuals (66%) present with asymptomatic swelling or facial asymmetry. Other signs and
symptoms include pain (15%), trismus with limited mouth opening (11%), mobile teeth (7%),
infection (3%), and bleeding (3%) { 16360612 ; 25113037 ; 27816276 ; 32215029 ; 33204607 }.
Imaging: Radiography shows a well-defined radiolucency without mineralization, either unilocular
or multilocular, with pushing borders, expansion, cortical erosion, tooth displacement and root
resorption,
often
with
soft
tissue
extension
{ 16360612 ; 25113037 ; 27816276 ; 32215029 ; 33204607 }.
Epidemiology
DFB
presents
over
a wide
age
range
(0.5
to
70
years)
{ 16360612 ; 25113037 ; 27816276 ; 32215029 ; 33204607 }, with 35% occurring in the 1st
decade, and almost 70% before age 30 years, mean age at diagnosis 20 years. DFB in the jaws
is slightly more common in females (M:F 1:1.3), and in non-Hispanic Caucasians (51%), compared
with those of African origin (24%), Hispanics (16%) and Asians (9%).
Etiology
Unknown
Genetic factors
DFB occurs in tuberous sclerosis, more commonly involving the maxilla (60%)
{ 15619647 ; 31078508 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Firm white tumour with coarse whirled architecture, focal myxoid areas and no necrosis. Soft tissue
extension may be present.
Histopathology
DFB is composed of uniform benign spindle cells with slender tapering nuclei, arranged in
intertwining fascicles, without atypia or pleomorphism and only rare mitoses. The cells often have
indistinct cytoplasmic borders that blend with adjacent collagen, which can become keloid-like.
Myxoid areas, cysts and osteoclasts are present rarely. Thin-walled vessels interposed between
spindle cell fascicles may be present. Due to the infiltrative margin, entrapped residual bone
trabeculae may be present.
Immunohistochemistry: DFBs express vimentin (92%), smooth muscle actin (80%), muscle specific
actin
(63%),
S100
(7%),
and
have
low
Ki-67
expression
{ 16360612 ; 25113037 ; 27816276 ; 32215029 ; 33204607 }. Almost all DFBs lack nuclear ßcatenin expression, only two cases with CTNNB1 mutation have been positive.
Differential
This
is
by
exclusion
of
desmoid
tumour
(nuclear
ßcatenin; CTNNB1 or APC mutation), fibrous dysplasia (GNAS mutation), low-grade fibrosarcoma,
low-grade central osteosarcoma (MDM2, CDK4, SATB2), low-grade myofibroblastic sarcoma
(SMA, desmin, nuclear ß-catenin), synovial sarcoma (TLE1; SS18 rearrangement), spindle cell
rhabdomyosarcoma of jaw (TFCP2 rearrangement), and myoepithelial tumours (S100,
CAM5.2; EWSR1 rearrangement, INI1 loss).
Cytology
Not clinically relevant

731

Diagnostic molecular pathology
Only a few DFB have activating CTNNB1 point mutations { 16153468 ; 31838586 ; 31901437 }, in
contrast to DF of soft tissue, in which up to 75% have nuclear ß-catenin expression and
somatic CTNNB1 or constitutional APC mutations.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: intraosseous site; benign spindle cell lesion; no cytologic atypia or pleomorphism; dense
collagenous background; exclusion of alternative fibrous tumours.
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
DFB frequently
recurs
after
curettage
(31%)
or
enucleation
(25%)
{ 16360612 ; 25113037 ; 27816276 ; 32215029 ; 33204607 }. Recurrence after resection is
approximately 10% and chemotherapy may be considered if excision is not feasible. Radiotherapy
is not recommended.

Malignant maxillofacial bone and cartilage tumours

Osteosarcoma of the jaw
Definition
Osteosarcoma constitutes a group of malignant bone neoplasms in which the cells produce
immature bone.
ICD-O coding
9180/3 Osteosarcoma, NOS
9187/3 Low-grade central osteosarcoma
9192/3 Parosteal osteosarcoma
9193/3 Periosteal osteosarcoma
9194/3 High-grade surface osteosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B51.0 Osteosarcoma of bone or articular cartilage of jaw
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Conventional osteosarcoma; small cell osteosarcoma; telangiectatic osteosarcoma; low-grade
central osteosarcoma; parosteal osteosarcoma; periosteal osteosarcoma; high-grade surface
osteosarcoma; radiation-induced osteosarcoma
Localization
Conventional osteosarcoma most commonly arises in the metaphysis of long bones but the fourth
most common site of origin is the jawbones (particularly the mandible), accounting for about 6% of
cases. However, any craniofacial bone can be involved { 24035127 ; 24246156 }.
Clinical features
The most frequent clinical manifestation is local swelling, which can be associated with pain and
ulceration. Other signs include loosening of the adjacent teeth and pathological fracture
{ 30608407 ; 31154837 ;}.
Imaging: Presentation depends on the amount of mineralized matrix formed by the tumour. Most
osteosarcomas present as mixed radiolucent and sclerotic tumours, high-grade lesions show
osteodestructive growth, aggressive periosteal reactions (Codman triangle, sunburst appearance)
and soft tissue extension. Widening of the periodontal ligament space of the involved teeth due to
tumour infiltration may be observed. CT scans are particularly useful for assisting biopsy and
734

staging. MRI is important for assessing the intraosseous tumour extension and soft-tissue
involvement { 31642708 }.
Epidemiology
Osteosarcoma is rare, with an overall annual incidence of approximately 4-5 cases per million
population. The jawbones account for approximately 6% of cases and remarkably, patients are
usually 10–20 years older than those developing osteosarcoma in the extragnathic sites. There is
no gender predilection { 24246156 ; 25811167 ; 28109463 }.
Etiology
Unknown. Risk factors include local radiotherapy { 20219593 ; 33184778 ; 32343457 }, a history of
retinoblastoma, or underlying Paget's disease of bone (osteitis deformans) { 12570058 }.
Genetic factors
Osteosarcoma is associated with Li–Fraumeni syndrome, Werner syndrome, Bloom syndrome and
Rothmund-Thomson syndrome but the jaws seem exceptionally rarely involved { 32959210 }.
Pathogenesis
The pathogenesis and cell of origin of osteosarcoma are unknown. Few recurrent mutations have
been described and include TP53 and RB1 as the most commonly mutated genes. The hallmark
of osteosarcoma is chromosomal instability resulting in highly complex chromosomal aneuploidy
and both inter- and intratumoural heterogeneity. It is most likely caused by chromoanagenesis
(chromothripsis / chromoplexy) following an unknown trigger, likely involving chromosome
segregation errors during cell division. Some genes such as MDM2, CDK4, RAS and TUG1 in
addition to RNAs as ZEB2-AS1, CircECE1, Circ0085539 have been implicated. Germline
mutations in different RECQ helicases underlie Werner, Bloom and Rothmund Thomson
syndromes
conferring
an
increased
risk
of
developing
osteosarcoma
{ 20196171 ; 26248895 ; 33747256 ; 33841645 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Depending on the type, amount, and mineralization of the lesional matrix, osteosarcoma can
present with tan-white and solid or a softer more grey, glistening cut surface. Areas of
haemorrhage, necrosis, and cystic change are frequently observed.
Histopathology
Conventional osteosarcoma is the most common and aggressive subtype, whereas periosteal
osteosarcoma is of intermediate grade and low-grade central and parosteal osteosarcomas are
low-grade subtypes.
Conventional osteosarcoma is defined by highly atypical cells producing an immature and often
lace-like neoplastic osteoid. The extent of matrix can vary significantly, ranging from focal,
immature osteoid to heavily mineralized sclerotic bone. The tumours generally show an aggressive
and osteodestructive growth permeating marrow spaces. As a result, pre-existing trabeculae
become entrapped by tumour infiltrates and neoplastic bone. The periosteum forms a rigid barrier
for tumour permeation and may remain as a pseudocapsule around some tumours that have
destroyed the cortex. Frequently, fibroblastic components and / or neoplastic cartilage can also be
found, and the predominant matrix defines the tumours as osteoblastic, chondroblastic, and
fibroblastic type. Chondroblastic osteosarcoma is proportionally more common in the jaw bones
and can histologically mimic chondrosarcoma, which is far less frequent in the maxillofacial bones
{ 6573939 ; 30608407 }. Gnathic osteosarcoma less commonly shows a pleomorphic high-grade
morphology although cytologic atypia is nevertheless present, but often subtle. Jaw tumours are
often well delineated and show a cambium-like proliferative cell layer at the periphery of the lesion.
Small-cell and telangiectatic variants have been reported but are exceptionally rare.
Periosteal subtypes generally demonstrate a predominant chondroblastic differentiation, with
intermediate-grade atypia but are exceedingly rare in the head and neck region.
Low grade medullary and parosteal subtypes generally demonstrate more subtle atypia; mitotic
activity can be scarce. They consist of either irregular or interlacing bone trabeculae embedded in
a fibroblastic stroma with only minimal atypia. The stromal component can predominate and is of
low to moderate cellularity.
Giant cell rich osteosarcoma is a morphologic subtype that should be kept in mind and
distinguished from other giant cell rich lesions affecting the jaws.

735

Chemotherapy-related changes can mask original tumour morphology and extent and include
frankly necrotic tumour, partly degenerate cells, a framework of tumour osteoid and chondroid
matrix with empty lacunae, interlacing trabeculae of reactive bone and loose sparsely cellular
collagenous tissue with scattered capillaries and chronic inflammatory cells.
Immunohistochemistry: Positive staining with antibodies against MDM2 and CDK4 might aid in
distinguishing
low-grade
osteosarcoma
from
benign
fibro-osseous
mimics
{ 20601938 ; 21336260 }. SATB2 immunohistochemistry may be helpful useful to detect
osteoblastic differentiation but despite sensitivity it is not completely specific
{ 26644288 ; 27465835 }.
Cytology
Although there are some studies showing the utility of FNAC in osteosarcomas, biopsy is the gold
standard method for diagnosis { 26229255 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: consistent imaging features; tumour cells producing immature and often lace-like
neoplastic osteoid; permeative invasion; entrapment of host bone
Desirable: Necrosis; atypical mitosis; pleomorphism; high cellularity
Staging
AJCC/UICC staging system is used for craniofacial osteosarcoma, based on tumour grade, size
and regional and distant metastasis. Other systems based on anatomic location, intra- versus
extracompartmental, the MSTS system and the less known Spanier system based on periosteal
breach are not commonly used in jaws, although the latter parameter is prognostically
significant { 28195881 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Osteosarcomas of the jaws metastasize far less frequently (in 6–21% of cases), and later in the
course of disease, than do their peripheral counterparts. These prognostically favourable
characteristics are restricted to tumours of the jaws. Extragnathic osteosarcomas of the skull or
facial bones behave as aggressively as tumours of the peripheral skeleton. Histological grade of
malignancy, and stage of the disease are the most important prognostic factors. Surgical resection
with clear margins is the treatment of choice. Low-grade osteosarcomas can be cured by complete
resection without additional treatment modalities. The role of (neo)adjuvant treatment for
osteosarcomas of the jawbones is controversial. However, a recent meta-analysis showed that
chemotherapy might improve survival in patients with positive margins, high-grade tumours, and
recurrent
tumours
{ 6573939 ; 12237918 ; 17467326 ; 19382187 ; 21288615 ; 23131392 ; 24246156 ; 30608407 ; 3
0706580 }.
The prognosis for osteosarcoma secondary to Paget's disease or irradiation is much worse
[[Flanagan, bridge and O'Donnell. Secondary osteosarcoma, page 419- 421in WHO classification
of tumours fifth edition soft tissue and bone tumours]].

739

Chondrosarcoma family
Definition
Conventional chondrosarcoma is a malignant bone neoplasm that produces cartilaginous matrix.
ICD-O coding
9220/3 Chondrosarcoma, grade 2
9220/3 Chondrosarcoma, grade 3
ICD-11 coding
2B50.Z & XH6LT5 Chondrosarcoma of bone and articular cartilage of unspecified sites &
Chondrosarcoma, grade 2
2B50.Z & XH0Y34 Chondrosarcoma of bone and articular cartilage of unspecified sites &
Chondrosarcoma, grade 3
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Conventional (grade 1; grade 2-3); Periosteal; Dedifferentiated; Clear cell.
Localization
Conventional chondrosarcoma affects the maxilla and the nasal septum more frequently than the
mandible, but may occur in any maxillofacial bone { 8635057 ; 10862026 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms are non-specific and depend upon location. Involvement of the nose can result in nasal
obstruction. At other sites asymptomatic or painful swelling is the most common presentation
{ 24213203 }.
Imaging: Osteolytic tumour, ranging from well defined to aggressive destruction with varying
opacity producing stippled pattern or irregular masses best seen on CT. Dedifferentiation may
produce a purely lytic area or soft tissue extension best localised on MRI. Chondrosarcoma has a
high T2-weighted signal intensity.
Epidemiology
Maxillo-facial chondrosarcoma is almost exclusively of conventional type, and accounts for only
approximately 3-4% of all chondrosarcomas { 8635057 ; 27042949 }[[ISBN 1-881041-93-X; Unni
KK, Inwards CY, Bridge JA, Kindblom LG, and Wold LE; AFIP atlas of tumor pathology, fourth
series, fascicle 2; Tumors of the Bones and Joints; Chapter 4: Cartilaginous Lesions; pp. 37-118;
2005 ARP Press, Silver Spring, Maryland, USA]]. All age groups can be affected with a slight
predilection for middle-aged men { 10862026 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Genetic factors
Chondrosarcomas in the maxillofacial skeleton as manifestations of Ollier disease and Maffucci
syndrome with malignant transformation are exceedingly rare { 7573751 }.
Pathogenesis
Conventional, periosteal and dedifferentiated chondrosarcomas, but not clear cell
chondrosarcoma, harbour somatic mutations in the isocitrate dehydrogenase genes type 1 and 2
(IDH1, IDH2) { 21598255 ; 23598960 ; 25432631 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lobular tumour with a glistening blue-grey or white, solid cut surface. The dedifferentiated subtype
also shows a fleshy component.
Histopathology
Chondrosarcoma generally has a lobular morphology and osteodestructive growth with entrapment
of pre-existing trabecular bone and/or cortical permeation. Well-differentiated tumours resemble
hyaline cartilage with oval to polygonal cells in lacunar spaces surrounded by basophilic matrix.
The nuclei are small and uniform, with round to oval outlines and evenly distributed dense
chromatin. With increasing grade, the nucleoli become discernible due to open chromatin. Nuclear
atypia, increased cellularity with irregular distribution, decreased volume of cytoplasm, myxoid
background, and mitoses are also associated with higher tumour grade { 890662 }.

740

Subtypes: Conventional chondrosarcoma is a malignant bone neoplasm that arises in the
medullary cavity and produces cartilaginous matrix. Periosteal chondrosarcoma develops on the
surface of bone. Dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma shows abrupt transition into a high-grade, noncartilaginous sarcoma. Clear cell chondrosarcoma is a low-grade malignancy of lobules of cells
with abundant clear cytoplasm.
Differential Since chondromas in the maxillofacial bones are exceptionally rare, tumours with pure
cartilaginous differentiation should always be considered to be chondrosarcoma until proven
otherwise. Chondroblastic osteosarcoma is far more common than chondrosarcoma, especially in
the jawbones, requiring a thorough search to exclude small foci of neoplastic osteoid formation.
Accordingly, a diagnosis of maxillofacial chondrosarcoma should not be made solely on a core
needle biopsy. The high-grade component of dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma most frequently
resembles undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma, but can vary. Clear cell chondrosarcoma shows
lobules of cells with abundant pale (attributable to glycogen) or slightly eosinophilic cytoplasm and
frequently includes woven bone formation. In the temporomandibular joint, synovial
chondromatosis should be ruled out, especially when the lesion shows the typical multinodular
architecture and lacks infiltrative growth.
Immunohistochemistry: Has limited diagnostic value, the tumor cells usually stain for S-100 protein,
SOX9, ERG, and podoplanin { 18820665 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Detection of IDH1/2 mutations can be helpful in distinguishing conventional, periosteal and
dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma from chondroblastic osteosarcoma in selected cases but is
generally not required { 21598255 ; 23598960 }. Detection of IDH1/2 mutation may be less likely
in chondrosarcomas of facial skeleton, whilst the skull base tumours reportedly have a higher
detection rate { 30296521 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: consistent imaging features; tumour with cartilaginous matrix; entrapped pre-existing
bone; lack of neoplastic bone or osteoid formation
Staging
AJCC/UICC staging system may be used for craniofacial chondrosarcoma { 24213203 } and at this
site tumours are of lower stage and grade than peripheral chondrosarcomas { 27042949 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Surgical resection is the treatment of choice in the maxillofacial bones. Histologic grade and
complete resection are the most important prognostic factors. The prognosis of dedifferentiated
chondrosarcoma is usually poor.

#25444
Chondrosarcoma Grade 2
Cartilaginous tumour formation showing permeative and osteodestructive growth.

742

Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma
Definition
Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma (MCS) is a high-grade, biphasic malignant cartilaginous neoplasm
characterized by sheets of small blue round to spindle-shaped cells interspersed with lobules of
hyaline cartilage.
ICD-O coding
9240/3 Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma.
ICD-11 coding
2B50.Z & XH8X47 Chondrosarcoma of bone and articular cartilage of unspecified sites &
Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The most common sites in the head and neck are the jaws, mandible more frequently, followed by
the skull { 3697943 ; 6825046 ; 6861090 ; 15233236 ; 17681487 ; 29582189 }. Within the
sinonasal tract, the maxillary sinus is most commonly affected, followed by ethmoid and nasal
cavity { 12792311 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with swelling, pain, and possible pathological fracture.
Imaging: An aggressive lytic and destructive mass is seen radiologically, sometimes with soft tissue
extension and calcifications.
Epidemiology
MCS comprise 2-10% of all chondrosarcomas and 20-30% arise in the head and neck
{ 14416919 }. Peak incidence is in the third decade (age range 7 and 80 years). There is no sex
predilection { 25529371 ; 26975384 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Almost all
cases harbour
a
specific HEY1::NCOA2 fusion gene, much
more
rarely IRF2BP2::CDX1. Small cell areas show TP53 loss, Rb pathway alterations and homozygous
loss of CDKN2A { 22674453 ; 23415961 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Gray, gray-white or pink tumour, soft, firm or hard with nodules of cartilage and bone.
Histopathology
MCS has a characteristic biphasic pattern. Large areas are composed of small, uniform, round to
spindle-shaped cells, which resemble those of Ewing sarcoma, often with a perivascular
arrangement and hemangiopericytoma-like pattern, necrosis and frequent mitoses. Admixed
lobules of highly differentiated cartilage may be limited or extensive and can exhibit calcification
and immature ossification.
Immunohistochemistry: IHC shows positivity for membranous CD99, and nuclear SOX9. NKX3-1
{ 31972596 } has been suggested to be highly specific and there may be aberrant muscle marker
expression.
Cytology
Small cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasm ratio, singly and in clumps in a basophilic extracellular
matrix.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
HEY1::NCOA2 recurrent
translocation
is
present
in
almost
all MCS
{ 22034177 ; 23252872 }, IRF2BP2::CDX1 is
reported
much
more
rarely
{ 23185413 }. IDH1 or IDH2 mutations do not occur { 21598255 ; 24680178 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: prominent primitive component of round-to-spindled cells punctated with islands of
mature cartilage

743

Desirable: HEY1::NCOA2 fusion in diagnostically challenging cases
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
MCS has a high local recurrence rate with metastasis after a long disease free interval. MCS in
general has 5 and 10-year survival rates of 51 and 43% respectively, depending on the site, surgical
resectability, type and duration of therapy { 26975384 }. Jaw tumors generally have a better
prognosis { 6402853 ; 6825046 ; 13883114 }.

destruction and patchy internal mineralisation. CT, coronal reconstruction.

distribution in both the small cell and chondroid components.

#24607
Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma
The characteristic histologic feature is a bimorphic pattern: small, uniform, round to
spindle-shaped cells with a hemangiopericytoma-like vascular pattern in the stroma and
admixed cartilaginous tissue that may mineralise.

Rhabdomyosarcoma with TFCP2 rearrangement
Definition
Rhabdomyosarcoma
with TFCP2 rearrangement (TFCP2-RMS)
is
rhabdomyosarcoma, characterised by fusion of TFCP2 to EWSR-1 or FUS.
ICD-O coding
8900/3 Rhabdomyosarcoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding

747

a

high

grade

2B66.Y & XH0GA1 Other specified malignant neoplasms of other and unspecified parts of mouth
& Rhabdomyosarcoma, NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most TFCP2-RMS arise in bone, less frequently in soft tissue { 31383960 ; 33382123 }. There is a
striking predilection for craniofacial bones, in decreasing order of frequency the mandible, maxilla
and skull bones, from where TFCP2-RMS commonly infiltrates into the soft tissues of the mouth,
nose and the neck.
Clinical features
Large rapidly growing swelling with pain and signs related to compression. Tumour size varies
widely with median diameter 60mm { 31383960 }.
Imaging: Imaging shows large, ill-defined, infiltrating and destructive masses, commonly centered
on bones and invading soft tissue.
Epidemiology
TFCP2-RMS affect young adults (median age 25 years, range 11-86 years)
{ 31383960 ; 33382123 }. 30% of cases arise in patients less than 18 years old. Males and females
are equally affected.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
TFCP2-RMS are defined by TFCP2 rearrangement, fused in 5’ either to FUS or EWSR1, producing
expression profiles distinct from other rhabdomyosarcomas { 29431183 }. The translocation may
be unbalanced. TFCP2, located in 12q13.12, encodes the Late Simian Virus 40 factor (LSF)
transcription factor which regulates the expression of an enzyme involved in the regulation of DNA
synthesis { 8157699 }. The breakpoint occurs in 5’ of exon 2 of TFCP2, preserving the DNAbinding domain of the protein { 29431183 }. TFCP2-RMS harbour complex, commonly aneuploid
genomic profiles { 29758589 ; 30477883 } with multiple copy number alterations across their
genomes.
Homozygous
deletions
of CDKN2A are
present
in
virtually
all
cases. TERT overexpression has been observed { 29758589 ; 29431183 }. Occasional alterations
include MDM2 amplification { 31383960 }, mutations of TP53 { 33382123 } and chromotrypsis in
1p and 3p { 29758589 ; 30477883 }. Intragenic deletions of ALK are present in roughly half of
cases, preserving the kinase domain of the protein, and correlate with ALK upregulation at
transcript and protein levels. These alterations may lead to ALK activation through alternative
transcription initiation { 26444240 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Non-specific, solid, white-grey tumour with necrosis.
Histopathology
TFCP2-RMS are usually biphasic with spindle cell and epithelioid areas in solid sheets or fascicles
with scant accompanying stroma. More rarely, TFCP2-RMS are purely epitheloid, spindle or round
cell. Tumour nuclei are large, displaying brisk mitotic activity and dotted with conspicuous nucleoli.
Overall, tumour nuclei are monotonous but may show focal hyperchromatism and pleomorphism.
Necrosis is common.
Immunohistochemistry: Virtually all cases are positive for cytokeratins AE1/E3 and desmin. All, by
definition, express myogenin and/or MYOD1, MYOD1 being more sensitive. There is occasional
expression of p63, CK7, SATB2, P53, CD30, CD4, S100 protein, caldesmon and smooth muscle
actin and INI-1 expression is retained { 29758589 ; 32504289 ; 32556562 ;}.
Differential Particularly in older adults, this includes metastastic carcinoma due to keratin
expression and epithelioid features { 30948206 }. Alveolar RMS commonly expresses cytokeratins
and ALK but harbours FOXO1 fusions. Triton tumour or anaplastic lymphoma may be suspected
when S100 is positive, especially if purely epithelioid. Sarcomas with PATZ1 fusions are rare and

748

have variable appearance and often express myogenin, MYOD1 and S100 but are associated
with PATZ1 translocations.
Cytology
No data available
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The diagnosis should be confirmed by molecular testing using FISH with a break-apart probe for
TFCP2 or by sequencing.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: high grade epithelioid, spindle cell or mixed tumour; MYOD1 or myogenin expression
Desirable: location in bone; TFCP2 rearrangement or typical immunophenotype with co-expression
of AE1/E3 and ALK
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
TFCP2-RMS are aggressive and associated with poor outcome, presenting at a locally advanced
stage with bone and soft tissue destruction { 31383960 ; 33382123 }. Distant metastasis is present
at diagnosis or follow-up in 65% of cases, usually to bone, lungs or lymph nodes
{ 31383960 }. Estimated median survival is 14 months but follow-up data are limited
{ 31383960 ; 33382123 }.
Most TFCP2-RMS recur after surgery and progress despite aggressive multimodal therapy. There
has been insufficient time to evaluate specific chemotherapy regimens { 31383960 ; 32556562 }.
ALK
inhibitors
have
proved
ineffective
alone,
but
have
some
effect
with irradiation { 31470995 ; 31965718 }.

eosinophilic cytoplasm along with

9. Tumours of the ear
Introduction
Tumours of the external auditory canal
Chondrodermatitis nodularis chronica helicis
Cystic chondromalacia
Exostosis (osteoma) of the ear
Ceruminous adenoma
Ceruminous adenocarcinoma
Squamous cell carcinoma of the external auditory canal
Tumours of the middle and inner ear
Otosclerosis
Cholesteatoma
Middle ear papilloma
Vestibular schwannoma
Middle ear neuroendocrine tumour (MeNET)
Endolymphatic sac tumour
Middle ear squamous cell carcinoma
Middle ear adenocarcinoma

Introduction
The ear is comprised of three distinct regions/compartments, including the external ear, the middle
ear/temporal bone, and the inner ear. The classification of ear tumours remains similar to the prior
edition yet is evolving based on emerging molecular discoveries linked to histomorphology and
immunohistochemistry.
The most common tumour of the external ear region is the (cutaneous) squamous cell carcinoma
(SCC). Site specific external canal neoplasms develop from ceruminal glands and include
ceruminous adenoma and ceruminous adenocarcinoma, both including salivary gland type
neoplasms (e.g., ceruminous pleomorphic adenoma, ceruminous adenoid cystic carcinoma, and
ceruminous mucoepidermoid carcinoma). Tumour-like conditions that enter into the differential
diagnosis have been included (such as chondrodermatitis nodular chronica helicis and auricular
pseudocyst). Exostosis (osteoma) has been added due to the unique findings within the external
auditory canal.
Selected tumours of the middle and inner ear have new terminology and expanded definitions.
Meningioma has been combined with other extraneuraxial sites, and included in the sinonasal tract
section. Aggressive papillary tumour (APT) included in the previous edition is included within middle
ear adenocarcinoma, although it remains controversial whether it is an independent tumour or a
subtype of endolymphatic sac tumor (ELST). APT arises in the middle ear without involvement of
the apical petrous temporal bone and, therefore, unrelated to ELST { 18633929 ; 32940914 }. APTs
may represent a heterogenous spectrum of distinct tumours unified under the designation APT with
histologic, immunohistochemical and molecular alterations (MKRN1::BRAF fusion) distinctly
different from middle ear neuroendocrine tumour (MeNET) and ELST { 32940914 }. The
emerging DEK::AFF2 carcinoma represents a distinct nonkeratinizing SCC which may affect the
middle ear/temporal bone { 32366754 } but is more common in the sinonasal tract { 33002918 },
and shows histologic features of SCC (thus, for this edition, is considered a SCC subtype).
The tumour formerly classified as “middle ear adenoma” is also a controversial tumour. This tumour
has been referred to as carcinoid tumour { 16148713 } and neuroendocrine adenoma of the middle
ear { 12011260 }. Given their predominant neuroendocrine features, the designation of middle ear
neuroendocrine tumour (MeNET) has been proposed in line with recent WHO neuroendocrine
terminology harmonization across all anatomic sites { 30140036 }. A recent publication
{ 34041698 } identified tumours composed of cells comparable to normal intestinal L cells
resembling hindgut NETs, proposed middle ear NET and recommend classification within the
broader category of neuroendocrine neoplasms. Previous studies proposed admixtures of epithelial
non-neuroendocrine elements, making them possibly mixed neuroendocrine and nonneuroendocrine neoplasms (MiNENs), but diffuse INSM1-immunoreactivity suggests a single cell
type. Given their site specificity, however, the tumour has been catalogued within this section for
this edition.

752

#26370
Anatomy of the Ear
Figure including the Pinna, External Auditory Canal, Middle Ear and Inner Ear
Other source (Dr Katherine R Hulme, Department of Tissue Pathology and Diagnostic
Oncology, NSW Health Pathology, Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, Sydney, Australia.
Katherine.Hulme@health.nsw.gov.au)
Tumours of the external auditory canal

Chondrodermatitis nodularis chronica helicis
Definition
Chondrodermatitis nodularis chronica helicis (CNCH) is a non-neoplastic inflammatory
degenerative process of the external ear characterized by necrobiotic changes in the dermis and
perichondrium.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
AA12 Chondrodermatitis nodularis
Related terminology
Acceptable: chondrodermatitis nodularis helicis
Not recommended: Winkler disease, chondrodermatitis chronica auriculae
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The helix is affected twice as often as the antihelix, but any part of the auricle may be affected
{ 17524165 ; 27399947 }, with the right > left { 13079290 ; 13124280 ; 21276202 ; 24206803 }.
Clinical features
Patients present clinically with an intensely painful nodule, usually nocturnal and magnified by any
ear pressure { 13079290 ; 13124280 ; 21276202 ; 24206803 ; 30505787 }. CNCH begins as a
reddish, round, indurated nodule which develops a central crater containing crust-like to horny
material, with atrophic adjacent skin. Drainage of purulent material is common. Dermoscopy shows
a global configuration (daisy pattern) of thick white lines, radially arranged and converging on a
central rounded lump { 29926164 ; 30775149 }.
Epidemiology

753

CNCH is a relatively common external ear skin lesion { 13079290 ; 17524165 ; 27399947 }, more
common in males than females (~2:1) { 17524165 ; 21276202 ; 24206803 ; 27399947 }. Most
patients are in the 7th decade { 13079290 ; 17524165 ; 21276202 ; 24206803 ; 27399947 }. Young
patients may have an underlying systemic disease causing ischemic necrobiosis { 15811116 }.
Etiology
Exposure to sunlight or cold predispose { 24206803 }.
Pathogenesis
Dermal microtrauma (helmets, headsets, actinic damage, frostbite) combined with the relatively
tenuous aural vascularity { 19413613 ; 29805936 } leads to subepidermal injury, and with reduced
repair capacity and collagen degeneration in the elderly, the lesion becomes persistent
{ 13079290 ; 15811116 }. Systemic diseases associated with microangiopathy may predispose
individuals to CNCH { 13079290 ; 15811116 ; 21276202 }.
Macroscopic appearance
A small central crater containing necrotic material, usually between 4 - 15 mm.
Histopathology
A central cup-shaped crater is filled with necrotic debris, fibrin, and a variable number of
inflammatory cells. A para- and hyperkeratotic crust is usually present, surrounded by acanthotic
epithelium often with acute inflammation. An inverted funnel-shaped defect is present in the dermis,
with the collagen underlying the crater appearing homogeneous and eosinophilic, admixed with
fibrin. There is superficial cartilage erosion with loss of cartilage basophilia, associated with fibrosis
and chondrocyte dropout causing smudgy cartilage degeneration. Rarely, devitalized cartilage may
extruded through the crater.
Differential CNCH is very commonly confused clinically with skin neoplasms (squamous or basal
cell carcinoma), and rarely with relapsing polychondritis or granuloma annulare.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: cup-shaped crater with necrotic debris and fibrin extending to involve the superficial
cartilage
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrences
(up
to
35%)
usually
develop
within
a
year
{ 15149500 ; 19116517 ; 24684884 ; 27399947 }.

#22780
Ear chondrodermatitis nodularis helicis chronica
Central ulceration down to the cartilage is surrounded by a hyperplastic squamous
epithelium. Note adjacent solar elastosis.

#22781
Ear chondrodermatitis nodularis helicis chronica
Fibrinoid-type necrotic debris extends down to the ear cartilage. There is adjacent
granulation-type tissue and hyperplastic squamous epithelium.

#22783

Ear chondrodermatitis nodularis helicis chronica
Biopsies are frequently superficial, with areas of hyperkeratosis and scale crust. Deeper
and additional levels or deeper sections generally aid in diagnosis.

of a male patient.

Cystic chondromalacia
Definition
Cystic chondromalacia is a degenerative pseudocystic lesion of the auricular cartilaginous plate.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
AA41.Y Other specified acquired deformity of pinna & benign idiopathic cystic chondromalacia of
the pinna
Related terminology
Acceptable: auricular pseudocyst
Not recommended: auricular seroma; otoseroma; intracartilaginous cyst; auricular endochondral
pseudocyst.
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
Ear helix, scaphoid fossa, concha, or triangular fossa { 30470633 }.
Clinical features
Patients usually present with a unilateral, fusiform, painless, slightly fluctuant swelling
{ 1556479 ; 3755327 ; 5904038 ; 6508627 ; 15068513 }; rare bilateral lesions may be
asynchronous { 2354120 ; 3083365 ; 6508627 ; 15068513 }. Lesions present rapidly (<12 weeks),
lacking overlying skin changes. A rare association with atopic eczema is reported, perhaps related
to rubbing or repeated microtrauma { 2354120 }.
Epidemiology
Auricular pseudocyst tends to be more common in young males (mean, 35 years), with a 9:1
male:female
ratio,
although
either
sex
and
all
ages
may
be
affected
{ 1556479 ; 3755327 ; 5904038 ; 6508627 ; 15068513 ; 30564784 ; 30921629 }. An increased
incidence is reported in Chinese patients, though reported in all ethnic groups
{ 1556479 ; 3083365 ; 5904038 ; 6508627 ; 15068513 ; 23575386 ; 30564784 }.
Etiology
Undefined, possibilities include ischemic necrosis (related to repetitive minor trauma), abnormal
release of lysosomal enzymes by chondrocytes, and an embryologic fusion defect { 3755327 }.
Trauma may be accounted for from contact sports, movement disorders (such as [Friedreich]
ataxia,
spasticity
or
dyskinesia),
or
earphone
or cellphone use
{ 1556479 ; 3755327 ; 5904038 ; 6508627 ; 15068513 ; 23473281 ; 26693094 }.
Pathogenesis
Interleukin-1 (IL-1), a mediator of inflammation, induces IL-6 (stimulates chondrocyte proliferation)
and stimulates proteases and prostaglandin E2 production by chondrocytes, leading to decreased
extracellular matrix formation, and thus may contribute to intracartilaginous cleft development
{ 15341921 ; 21178846 }. Lactate dehydrogenase isoenzymes are elevated compared to
autogenous blood { 23575386 } suggesting disrupted cartilage origin { 23575386 ; 26693094 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions range up to 40 mm (mean 20 mm). Unroofing procedures may show a central
intracartilaginous cleft. Incision or aspiration may yield viscous, clear to olive-oil coloured fluid
{ 3755327 ; 15235361 }.
Histopathology
The cartilaginous plate contains a central cleft without epithelium (“pseudocyst”). The contour of
the cleft may be irregular, associated with a rim of fibrous tissue or granulation tissue with plump
fibroblasts. Mucoid material, hemosiderin deposits, granulation tissue, and fibrosis are variably
present
dependent
on
lesion
duration
or
prior
procedure
{ 1556479 ; 3755327 ; 5904038 ; 6508627 ; 12619465 ; 15235361 ; 30470633 ; 25536139 ; 2525
5358 }.
Differential The differential includes relapsing polychondritis, chondrodermatitis nodularis chronica
helicis, and traumatic perichondritis.
Cytology
757

Fine needle aspiration is sometimes performed, showing acellular smears of granular
proteinaceous material { 10945913 }. Fluid LDH levels may be supportive in the correct clinical
setting { 30921629 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Central ear cartilage degenerated with spectrum of haemorrhage, granulation tissue,
and fibrosis
Staging
Not performed
Prognosis and prediction
Cosmetic concerns often prompt therapeutic intervention, while avoiding infection, auricular
chondritis and further deformity. Recurrence is seen in around 1-5% { 15068513 }.

#22733
Ear cystic chondromalacia
A clinical photograph of the left ear demonstrates a region of fusiform swelling along the
scapha below the helix.

The cartilage is splayed by an area of central degeneration, filled with blood and
granulation tissue.

#22776
Ear cystic chondromalacia
Frequently, a "de-roofing" of the lesion will be performed clinically, with only the outer
cartilage plate and some associated fluid and fibrosis identified.

#22777
Ear cystic chondromalacia
Higher power shows disrupted cartilage plates, with the central area degenerated, with
fibrin, organizing thrombus, fibrosis, and granulation-type tissue. Note the "central"
location of the findings.

759

#24193

Ear cystic chondromalacia
A cleft is noted within the cartilage, where mucinous material may be noted.

#22222
Ear Auricular pseudocyst
A whole slide demonstrate fragments of auricular cartilage that have central degeneration
with granulation-type tissue filling the space.

760

Exostosis (osteoma) of the ear
Definition
Slowly progressive benign growth of bony cortex secondary to chronic irritation { 13904891 }.
ICD-O coding
9180/0 Osteoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
AA40.Y Other specified acquired deformity of external auditory canal & Osteoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Bony cortex of the external auditory canal (EAC) { 30521295 } and rarely the middle ear
{ 16243740 }.
Clinical features
Patients with exostoses of the EAC and middle ear are generally asymptomatic but can present
with conductive hearing loss { 21310100 ; 25087466 }. Patients with exostosis of the EAC can
present with recurrent otitis externa, otalgia and cerumen impaction { 21310100 }. Exostosis or
bone spurs arising in the external auditory canal (EAC) are also called Surfers’ Ear { 30521295 }.
Imaging: EAC exostoses are best assessed on high resolution CT and usually present as bilateral,
broad-based convex thickenings of cortical bone, medial to the isthmus. This is in comparison with
EAC osteoma which is usually unilateral, may be pedunculated and originates in the lateral half of
the EAC.
Epidemiology
Exostosis of the EAC affects 6.3 per 1000 individuals { 12075223 }. The frequency is higher in
those with regular aquatic exposure such as surfers, divers, swimmers, kayakers and other
maritime activities { 30521295 ; 32033062 }. Exostosis of the EAC has been identified in prehistoric
man and is used anthropologically to identify cultures with aquatic lifestyle { 27834109 }. On the
other hand, exostosis of middle ear is extremely rare and has been described in dry/desert areas
in both modern and ancient societies { 16243740 }.
Etiology
Exostosis most likely to occur due to chronic irritation of the bone, particularly in the EAC where
the cutaneous lining is very thin. The incidence is proportional to frequency and duration of irritant
exposure { 25772761 }. Multiple osteomas may be associated with Gardner's syndrome
{ 31070935 }.
Pathogenesis
Chronic irritation due to cold temperature, water, pH and physical irritants leads to vasodilatation,
inflammation and stimulation of osteoblastic activity leading to bone formation { 13904891 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Exostosis appear as broad-based bony proliferation resembling normal bone cortex; though in most
instances pathologists receive multiple fragments.
Histopathology
Histologically, exostosis are composed of lamellar bone. Anastomosing trabeculae are present with
interspersed vascular tissues { 31070935 }. Of note, marrow spaces are absent.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Normal/mature appearing lamellar bony cortex in the appropriate radiologic context.
Staging
Not applicable
Prognosis and prediction
Exostosis of the EAC and the middle ear are benign lesions usually managed conservatively with
good outcome.

761

#27305
Exostosis
This ear exostosis demonstrates thick trabeculae of bone beneath an intact squamous
epithelium.

#27306
Exostosis
The bone is dense and thick, identified beneath an intact squamous epithelium. If on a
pedicle, osteoma may be used as the diagnosis.

762

#27307
Exostosis
This high power demonstrates lamellar bone with thick, anastomosing trabeculae with
reversal lines.

#28633
Ear osteoma
Axial CT showing an osteoma in the lateral aspect of the left external auditory canal, with
a soft tissue plug lying medial in the meatus.

#24423
Exostosis of the External Auditory Canal
Section showing anastomosing trabeculae of woven bone with intervening stroma.

Ceruminous adenoma
Definition
Benign neoplasm of apocrine cerumen producing glands of external auditory canal (EAC).
ICD-O coding
8420/0 Ceruminous adenoma
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XH7AL8 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory system &
Ceruminous adenoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: Ceruminoma; ceruminomata; adenoma ceruminalis; cylindroma; eccrine
cylindroma; aural hidradenoma
Subtype(s)
Ceruminous pleomorphic adenoma; ceruminous syringocystadenoma papilliferum
Localization
Tumours arise from dermal ceruminal glands confined to the outer half of the EAC. Direct extension
of pleomorphic adenoma from adjacent structures should be excluded { 15104293 ; 21792797 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms include an outer EAC mass with intact tympanic membrane, conductive hearing loss,
otorrhea or discharge and otalgia { 6243462 ; 6300574 ; 14102073 ; 15104293 }. Imaging

764

identifies an EAC soft tissue density mass and excludes direct extension from adjacent sites
{ 16168591 ; 23351401 }.
Epidemiology
Ceruminous
adenoma
represents
<1%
of
all
primary
ear
neoplasms
{ 6243462 ; 6300574 ; 15104293 }, with an equal sex distribution and wide age range (12 to 85
years), with a peak in the 6th decade { 6243462 ; 6300574 ; 15104293 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Unilateral polypoid grey skin covered 10 - 40 mm mass { 6243462 ; 6300574 ; 15104293 }.
Histopathology
Ceruminous adenomas are circumscribed, usually unencapsulated and lacking invasion. Surface
pagetoid-type extension, ulceration, and pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia { 6402199 } may be
seen.
They are predominantly glandular, often with cystic change, and extending into the surrounding
stroma. Solid, back-to-back glands, and papillary patterns may be identified. A dual cell population
(inner/luminal epithelial cells and outer basal/myoepithelial cells) is bland, with low-moderate
cellularity. Luminal cells are cuboidal to columnar, with ample eosinophilic cytoplasm, showing
apical snouting (apocrine). Isolated tumour cells show cytoplasmic golden-yellow/brown pigment
granules. Myoepithelial cells are often spindled with cleared cytoplasm. Mitoses may be increased
(up to 4 per 2 mm2), especially around ulceration or previous trauma/biopsy. Atypical mitoses and
comedonecrosis are absent. Concurrent cholesteatoma may occur.
Subtypes: Tumours with chondromyxoid stroma represent ceruminous pleomorphic adenoma
{ 32443830 },
often
with
metaplastic
elements
{ 2545327 ; 7517760 ; 10349387 ; 16398681 ; 18314029 ; 24908278 }.
Ceruminous syringocystadenoma papilliferum shows papillary projections lined by a dual cell
population,
with
a
variably
dense
stromal
plasma
cell
infiltrate
{ 15104293 ; 16168591 ; 28490727 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Neoplastic cells react strongly and diffusely with pancytokeratin; luminal
cells express CK7, EMA, and CD117; basal cells express myoepithelial/basal cell markers
{ 2545327 ; 15104293 ; 22623086 }. GATA3 is usually positive in luminal or apocrine cells.
Differential The most important differential is ceruminous adenocarcinoma; exclude direct
extension of salivary gland tumours; paraganglioma and middle ear neuroendocrine tumour is
distinctive.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not performed
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Lateral EAC location; low/moderately cellular bland glandular proliferation of biphasic
population; luminal apocrine morphology with decapitation.
Desirable: Cytoplasmic golden-yellow-brown pigment granules
Staging
Not performed
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrences develop when incompletely excised { 6243462 ; 6300574 ; 15104293, 32443830}.

Ear ceruminous adenoma
A well circumscribed proliferation of ceruminous glands showing apocrine change. Note

Ceruminous adenocarcinoma
Definition
Malignant epithelial neoplasm derived from ceruminous apocrine-type glands of the external
auditory canal (EAC).
ICD-O coding
8420/3 Ceruminous adenocarcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2D40 Adenocarcinoma of unspecified site
Related terminology
Not recommended: ceruminoma; cylindroma.
Subtype(s)
Ceruminous adenoid cystic carcinoma (CerAdCC); Ceruminous mucoepidermoid carcinoma
(CerMEC)
Localization
Tumours are unilateral, identified lateral to the bony-cartilaginous junction of the EAC, although
temporal bone invasion may be seen { 20596983 }. Direct extension from adjacent organs (parotid
gland) must be excluded.
Clinical features
Patients present with otalgia, an EAC mass, and hearing changes most commonly, with fullness,
discharge,
and
bleeding
less
frequently
{ 1328435 ; 5021609 ; 6096419 ; 6243462 ; 6291744 ; 6300574 ; 14102073 ; 20596983 ; 234531
17 ; 26954854 }. Perineural invasion is common in CerAdCC, but not uniformly associated with
otalgia { 32389593 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies are encouraged to exclude direct extension from adjacent sites and help
define the extent of the tumour { 6291744 ; 20596983 }.
Epidemiology

771

Representing <1% of all ear neoplasms { 6291744 ; 20596983 }, ceruminous adenocarcinomas are
more
common
than
ceruminous
adenomas
{ 6291744 ; 198620 ; 6243462 ; 6300574 ; 5021609 ; 1328435 ;
15104293 }, presenting on
average about a decade earlier. Overall, there is a slight female predominance with most patients
presenting
in
the
sixth
decade
(range 21-92
years)
{ 6243462 ; 6291744 ; 6300574 ; 14102073 ; 20596983 ; 23453117 ; 26954854 ; 32389593 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are polypoid, firm, solid, nodular masses often with superficial ulceration. Overall, tumours
are small (mean, 15 mm) { 20596983 }.
Histopathology
Histologically, tumours are separated into ceruminous adenocarcinoma, not otherwise specified
(NOS), adenoid cystic carcinoma (CerAdCC) and mucoepidermoid carcinoma (CerMEC)
{ 1328435 ; 6291744 ; 20596983 },
with CerAdCC the
most
common
{ 198620 ; 6096419 ; 6291744 ; 6300574 ; 7745330 ; 20596983 }. Tumours show destructive
infiltration into the surrounding soft tissues, while bone and cartilage invasion is less common.
Pagetoid spread into the surface epithelium may be seen. Perineural and lymphovascular invasion
and comedonecrosis are exclusive to carcinoma. The tumours are cellular, with tumour subtype
determined by architectural and cytological features.
Ceruminous adenocarcinoma, NOS shows solid and cystic patterns, with easily identified
pleomorphism in cells arranged in back-to-back gland formations with irregular lumina. Papillary
structures are uncommon. The neoplastic cells are low columnar to cuboidal with an increased
nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio, variable amounts of eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm with frequent
cytoplasmic extensions, snouts or blebs (apocrine differentiation). The nuclei are oval,
hyperchromatic to vesicular with easily identified nucleoli. Ceroid pigment granules are absent in
carcinoma { 5021609 ; 6243462 ; 20596983 }. There may be desmoplastic stroma at the advancing
edge. Mitotic figures, including atypical forms, are frequent.
CerAdCC demonstrates a perforated or cribriform, sieve-like, tubular-trabecular (cylinder), and
solid architecture. The cribriform pattern has ovoid spaces filled with blue glycosaminoglycan
material or eosinophilic replicated basement membrane material. An epithelial-stromal clefting
artifact aids in the diagnosis { 20596983 }. The neoplastic basaloid cells contain roughly pegshaped nuclei with coarse to heavy granular chromatin. The cytoplasm is scant
{ 198620 ; 4371368 ; 6243462 ; 6291744 }. Small duct-like lumen lined by small cuboidal cells are
noted.
CerMEC is histologically identical to MEC, showing intermingled epidermoid and transitional cells
with mucocytes { 1328435 ; 20596983 ; 23318008 }.
Immunohistochemistry: IHC may aid in highlighting the biphasic nature of the tumours, which at
least focally have inner luminal epithelial cells subtended by basal/myoepithelial cells
{ 1789146 ; 7690238 ; 20596983 ; 22623086 }. CerMEC will also show CEA(m) in the glandular
component. Neuroendocrine markers are non-reactive.
Differential It is imperative to exclude direct extension from salivary gland primary tumours, as
management and outcome will be different. Generally, separation from benign ceruminous
neoplasms is most significant, while selected skin adnexal primaries and metastatic neoplasms
may occasionally enter the differential.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant, with only rare single cases reported.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: External auditory canal origin; destructive infiltration by malignant glandular cells
associated with benign ceruminous glands
Staging

772

Without standardized staging, ICCR minimum data set reporting is encouraged
{ 30500288 } [[Thompson LDR, Gupta R, Sandison A, Wenig BM. (2018) Ear and Temporal Bone
Tumours, Histopathology Reporting Guide, 1st edition. International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting; Sydney, Australia. ISBN: 978-1-925687-22-4]].
Prognosis and prediction
There is a significant recurrence risk (up to 50% usually within 3 years) and distant metastatic
disease
potential
{ 6300574 ; 20596983 ; 23318008 ; 23453117 ; 26954854 ; 27040415 ; 32389593 }.
Tumours
with positive margins, skull base or brain invasion, solid pattern CerAdCC, and distant metastases
behave
poorly
{ 1328435 ; 6291744 ; 6243462 ; 6300574 ; 7745330 ; 18677277 ; 20596983 ; 23318008 ; 26954
854 }. Overall survival is about 50% at 5 years { 1328435 ; 20596983 ; 23453117 ; 26954854 }.
Higher stage tumours { 30500288 } have a much lower overall survival (5-year survival of 30%)
compared to 85% for stage 1 tumours { 23453117 }. Regional metastatic disease is low
{ 27040415 }. When metastatic disease develops, it is usually to lungs and liver
{ 198620 ; 6096419 ; 6291744 ; 6300574 ; 7745330 }.
