Squamous cell carcinoma of the external auditory canal
Definition
A carcinoma showing squamous differentiation arising from the keratinising squamous epithelium
lining the external auditory canal (EAC).
ICD-O coding
8070/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2C31 & XA1RS6 & XH0945 Squamous carcinoma of skin of head and neck
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Origin within the EAC instead of extension from adjacent pinna or periauricular skin sites
{ 30069837 ; 32311775 }.
Clinical features
Chronic otorrhea, otalgia, bleeding, hearing loss, otitis externa or otitis media, and facial palsy are
nonspecific symptoms { 33216374 }, an EAC mass, while rare, is more suggestive of the diagnosis
{ 11734117 ; 22926989 ; 30069837 }.
Imaging: High resolution CT of the temporal bone and contrast enhanced MRI are
complimentary modalities in the preoperative assessment of EAC SCC { 27639864 }. CT allows
detection of bone erosion and delineates the surgical anatomy whilst MRI demonstrates the extent
of soft tissue, intracranial and nodal involvement. PET-CT is also useful in the detection of nodal
involvement, distant metastases and to monitor for residual / recurrent disease.
Epidemiology
EAC SCC affects 1.6/1 million individuals, accounting for 0.2% of head and neck malignancies
{ 10912706 ; 23845289 }. It presents in the 6th-7th decades with equal sex distribution
{ 4842612 ; 33568243 }.
Etiology

779

Associated with long standing chronic suppurative otitis media and irradiation
{ 18948828 ; 21765382 ; 31890889 }. Rarely, sinonasal-like papillomata may transform into SCC
{ 22470050 ; 25564042 ; 31890383 ; 34021464 }.
Pathogenesis
The genetic profile is complex with chromosomal arms 3q, 5p and 8q amplification; 3p
deletions; TP53, CDKN2a, NOTCH and FAT alterations, similar to SCC at other sites
{ 32500594 }. Solar UV damage may be seen in pinna SCC. Genetic alterations from carcinogens
like tobacco, alcohol, and viral aetiologies are not seen.
Macroscopic appearance
Ulceration of an EAC mass is common
Histopathology
The morphologic appearance and immunohistochemical profile is similar to SCCs of other
cutaneous sites. Carcinoma in situ may be seen in the adjacent epithelium, while concurrent
sinonasal-type papilloma may also be noted. Cholesteatoma and chronic otitis may be seen in
some cases { 10828803 ; 31130732 }. Most tumours are well-differentiated and keratinizing
{ 23507994 ; 23845289 ; 25401454 ; 25654948 ; 28858932 }.
Poorly
differentiated,
nonkeratinising tumours may be seen.
Immunohistochemistry: Immunoreactivity with pancytokeratins, p40, p63, and CK5/6 is useful in
separating the tumours from other lesions in the differential diagnosis.
Differential Distinction from cholesteatoma, pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia, cutaneous basal
cell carcinoma and cutaneous melanoma is important. Direct extension to the EAC from a SCC of
the pinna or another nearby cutaneous site should be excluded. Documenting direct extension from
the salivary gland, nasopharynx or sinonasal tract tumours is important for management. In poorly
differentiated non-keratinizing tumours, translocation driven tumours (BRD4::NUTM1, DEK::AFF2,
EWSR1::FLI1, EWSR1::ERG) should be considered { 32366754 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: infiltrating squamous proliferation with architectural and cytologic features of carcinoma.
Keratin production may or may not be present. Origin from the pinna should be excluded.
Desirable: immunohistochemical confirmation of squamous lineage will be useful in poorly
differentiated tumours.
Staging
UICC TNM staging for head and neck cutaneous SCC, although modifications are suggested
{ 30500288 }.
Prognosis and prediction
These tumours are locally aggressive with nodal metastases. Factors like large tumour size, depth
of invasion >8 mm, lymphovascular or perineural involvement, incomplete resection, and nodal
metastases
portend
a
poor
prognosis
{ 19381105 ; 23845289 ; 24843224 ; 30500288 ; 32367152 ; 33216374 }.

780

#24420

auditory canal
Posterior aspect of a pinnectomy with
external auditory canal, tympanic membrane
and the malleus. A firm grey to white tumour
is seen involving the ear canal with
extension into the soft tissues.

Tumours of the middle and inner ear

Otosclerosis
Definition
Otosclerosis is usually an inherited disturbance in bone metabolism affecting the otic capsule (or
bony labyrinth) of the inner ear.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
782

AB33 Otosclerosis
Related terminology
Acceptable: localised bone dysplasia
Subtype(s)
Clinical otosclerosis; histological otosclerosis
Localization
Bony changes can occur at many locations in the inner ear. Plaques of softer, more vascular bone
replace endochondral bone of the otic capsule, mainly located anterior to the oval window and on
stapes footplate (80%), at the round window (30%), pericochlear (21%) and anterior part of internal
auditory canal (19%) { 26276418 }.
Clinical features
Otosclerosis is one of the commonest causes of conductive hearing loss (when involvement of the
annular ligament of the stapes causes fixation) { 20195188 }, although patients may have
sensorineural (with involvement of cochlea and part of the labyrinth) or mixed hearing loss. It is
usually bilateral (80%), although with initial asymmetrical hearing loss in one ear before the other
is affected. It is traditionally regarded as a middle ear disease, although inner ear involvement is
recognized with sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and vertigo { 30540696 }. Otoscopy may
reveal circumscribed reddening on the promontory { 32215901 }.
Imaging: Demineralised otospongiotic bone adjacent to the cochlea and anterior to the stapes
footplate is identified on CT scan { 32215901 }.
Epidemiology
Clinical otosclerosis mainly affects Caucasians (prevalence 0.3-0.4%) { 11568664 } with a reported
incidence in White Europeans of 0.1-2.1% { 30540696 } while it is rare in Africans and Asians (0.030.1%). Incidence in Japanese and South American populations is half that of Caucasians
{ 19318139 }. It accounts for up to 9% of all hearing loss and 22% of conductive hearing loss.
Prevalence of histological otosclerosis in large autopsy series is 8-11%. Age at presentation is
variable, typically in the 3rd decade (range 1st-6th decade) { 5298472 ; 2692453 ; 14414297 }. The
disease
is
more
often
diagnosed
in
females
than
males
(F:M
2:1)
{ 2825424 ; 16985478 ; 30540696 }.
Etiology
It is considered an autosomal dominant disease with variable penetrance (average 40%), a family
history
is
reported
in
up
to
70%
cases
{ 5298472 ; 9102432 ; 13242969 ; 14414297 ; 3054069 6"> 3054069 6 }. Several gene loci on
different chromosomes have been identified, but without an established causal link
{ 24215213 ; 30968248 }. Although unclear, measles virus has been identified with inflammation
postulated as a trigger { 3054069 ; 18235207 }. Oestrogen deficiency may cause otosclerosis in
menopausal women and hormone replacement has been shown to improve symptoms
{ 26276418 }. Worsening
of
symptoms
during
pregnancy has been
reported
{ 3054069 6"> 3054069 6 }. Studies investigating autoimmune causes, HLA association, and role
of inflammation have been inconclusive and contradictory.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Otosclerosis is rarely biopsied. Specimens comprise all or part of stapes bone, which may show
plaque tissue in the anterior footplate.
Histopathology
Histologically active and inactive/sclerotic areas are seen. Active areas (otospongiosis) have zones
of active bone resorption composed of spongy immature bone with numerous enlarged plump
osteoblasts, osteoclastic giant cells, and dilated vascular marrow spaces containing cellular
connective tissue { 20195188 }. Otospongiotic bone contrasts with the well-developed lamellar
bone beneath the outer periosteum, endochondral middle layer, and endosteal layer of the otic
capsule. A sharply demarcated edge between normal and otosclerotic bone is a prominent feature.
Otosclerotic bone sometimes reaches the endosteum of the cochlear capsule. Sometimes it may
lead to a fibrous reaction deep to the spiral ligament.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant

783

Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Well demarcated plaques of immature bone from middle ear
Staging
Not performed
Prognosis and prediction
Stapedectomy with prosthetic implant aids with hearing restoration, although progressive loss is
seen in untreated cases.

#26657
Otosclerosis
Axial CT of the petrous bones demonstrates bilateral pericochlear lucency consistent with
retro-fenestral otospongiosis

#28620
Ear otosclerosis
Axial high resolution CT imaging shows bilateral otic capsule demineralization and new
bone formation obstructing both oval windows.

784

#28635
Ear otosclerosis
Endoscopic image of the left ear showing otosclerosis. TM, tympanic membrane; m,
malleus handle; O, otosclerotic focus; Fn, facial nerve; Rw, round window niche; pe,
pyramidal eminence.

Cholesteatoma
Definition
Cholesteatoma is a non-neoplastic but destructive, middle ear accumulation of keratinising
stratified squamous epithelium and keratinous debris with fibrosis and an inflammatory reaction.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
AB12 Cholesteatoma of middle ear
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Congenital cholesteatoma; acquired cholesteatoma
Localization
Congenital cholesteatomas unilaterally arise in the middle ear medial to an intact tympanic
membrane in the anterio-superior quadrant closely followed by the posterior-superior quadrant
{ 31955213 }. Acquired forms present in a posterior mesotympanic or posterior
epitympanic location { 26747599 }. Bilateral disease is rare. External auditory canal presentation is
reported, but more likely represents a distinctly different entity: keratosis obturans
{ 25422282 ; 23235551 ; 21839307 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with unrelenting or recurrent foul-smelling otorrhea, and tinnitus with progressive
hearing impairment (due to eroded ossicles) { 28059056 }. Petrosal involvement can result in facial
paralysis, vertigo, diplopia, and blurred vision { 31876216 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies (CT and diffusion-weighted, - especially non-echoplanar MRI) are
complimentary studies that demonstrate a nonenhancing soft tissue mass in Prussak space with
scutum erosion (about 80% of cases) or an erosive mass in the posterior mesotympanum, medial
to the ossicles (about 15%). Involvement of ossicles, tegmen tympani, mastoid or facial nerve canal
may
be
seen.
Importantly,
surrounding
granulation
tissue
may
enhance
{ 28969854 ; 28634512 ; 10845037 }.
785

Epidemiology
Worldwide incidence approaches 5 million cases, in decline due to widespread use of ventilation
tubes { 21739009 ; 25866816 }. Reported annual incidence is 3-14 per 100,000 children and 9.2
per 100,000 adults, with male to female ratio 1.4:1 { 10478597 ; 12835944 }. Caucasians show
the highest prevalence while the lowest prevalence is in Inuit, Native American, and Asian
populations { 20360335 }. Patients with syndromes involving dysmorphic ears and/or cleft palate,
develop cholesteatoma disproportionately more often than the general population
{ 23860365 ; 23931986 ; 25151219 ; 28941966 }.
Etiology
Congenital cholesteatoma develops medial to an intact tympanic membrane from persistent
embryonic epithelial rests that proliferate { 30069838 ; 27171804 }. Inferred to form via multiple
mechanisms, acquired cholesteatoma results from traumatic or iatrogenic tympanic membrane
perforation and subsequent migration of squamous epithelium into the middle ear. Retraction
pockets caused by negative pressure in the middle ear and subsequent accumulation of
desquamated keratin results in cholesteatoma { 25866816 ; 11078064 }. Metaplastic
transformation of middle ear mucosa into keratinizing epithelium is another suggested pathway to
development { 25123251 }.
Pathogenesis
Congenital cholesteatoma is thought to develop from the epidermoid formation, persistent
embryonic epithelial rests. Pathogenesis of acquired cholesteatoma is incomplete, with the most
widely accepted theory being that of tympanic membrane retraction into the middle ear space,
resulting in accumulation of desquamated keratin with trapped bacteria leading to infection,
increased
inflammation
and
release
of
cytokines
that
results
in
epithelial
proliferation { 17097438 ; 17697435 ; 19467409 ; 2926259 }.
Macroscopic appearance
When intact, there is a white, ovoid, compressible mass surrounded by a thin wall. Disrupted
lesions show an irregular, shaggy friable surface with edematous edges. Spilled sac contents
display flaky, greasy or cheese-like keratinaceous debris { 30069838 }.
Histopathology
Keratinising stratified squamous epithelium, granulation tissue, and keratinaceous debris
characterized as perimatrix, matrix, and cystic contents are noted. The peripheral perimatrix
consists of granulation tissue with the subepithelial area consisting of cellular, thickened, dense
fibrous connective tissue. The epithelial matrix consists of keratinizing, cytologically bland, thin to
atrophic stratified squamous epithelium arranged in convoluted formations, lacking rete ridges, and
showing a prominent granular cell layer with abundant keratohyaline granules. Cystic contents are
comprised of abundant layers of laminated, free-flowing or aggregated, desquamated, anucleate
keratin. Haemorrhage, hemosiderin laden macrophages, and when ruptured, a foreign body giantcell reaction are noted { 30069838 }.
Differential Separation from cholesterol granuloma, otic polyp, and squamous cell carcinoma is
generally straight forward.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Keratinising squamous epithelium with keratin debris, prominent granular layer, and
inflammation
Desirable: Origin from the middle ear
Staging
Clinical staging reflects severity, difficulty of complete excision, and restoration of normal function.
Stage I – Cholesteatoma localized in the primary site such as attic or tympanic cavity
Stage II – Involvement of 2 or more sites
Stage III – Cholesteatoma with extracranial complications or pathologic conditions
Stage IV – Cholesteatoma with intracranial complications { 28059056 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Long term follow-up is necessary as recurrences are common { 28953605 }, especially with
ossicular involvement or mastoid bone disease { 28543174 ; 24882923 ; 27565391 ; 28274504 }.

Cholesteatoma

Middle ear papilloma
Definition
A benign surface epithelial neoplasm defined by its resemblance to sinonasal papilloma showing a
multilayered epithelium with mucocytes and transmigrating neutrophils, separated into three
distinct subtypes.
ICD-O coding
8140/0 Middle ear papilloma
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Z & XH3DV3 Benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory system, unspecified &
Adenoma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: Schneiderian papilloma; papilloma; transitional papilloma; fungiform papilloma;
eosinophilic papilloma
Subtype(s)
Inverted sinonasal-type papilloma; oncocytic sinonasal-type papilloma; exophytic sinonasal-type
papilloma.
Localization
Most temporal bone sinonasal-type papilloma (TBSP) are identified unilaterally in the middle ear,
but external auditory canal (EAC), mastoid bone, eustachian tube, and skull base are commonly
concurrently
affected
{ 8615588 ; 11802052 ; 12759269 ; 12737292 ; 25959041 ; 31428494 ; 34021464 }. About 50% of
patients show concurrent sinonasal tract / nasopharyngeal involvement. Must be distinguished from
cutaneous-type papilloma { 20596973 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with hearing loss, otorrhea, a mass, otalgia, tinnitus and otitis media, while nerve
symptoms (motor or sensory) are also reported. Tympanic membrane rupture is frequent and
concurrent eustachian tube involvement is common { 34021464 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies document disease extent for surgical planning, while frequently
confirming
concurrent
sinonasal
tract
disease
{ 20875193 ; 21817933 ; 26698908 ; 29764790 ; 22565660 ; 25564042 ; 34021464 }.
Epidemiology

790

TBSP is very rare. There is an equal sex distribution and a broad age range (19-81, median 56
years) at presentation { 34021464 }. When exclusively TBSP disease, females are more commonly
affected, but patients are less likely to develop carcinoma (19% versus 50%) compared to patients
with concurrent sinonasal tract disease. Males develop carcinoma at a 1.7:1 ratio versus females
{ 34021464 }.
Etiology
In a few reported cases, low risk human papillomavirus (HPV) is identified
{ 19863319 ; 20073603 ; 25724573 }.
Pathogenesis
Embryologically, Schneiderian membrane derived epithelium lines the tympanic cavity and
eustachian tube. Whether primary or secondary, tumour development may be viral or by another
mechanism. Neither EGFR nor KRAS mutations have been documented in TBSP
{ 29145573 ; 27234382 }. Emerging data suggests oncocytic-type papilloma may be distinct
{ 32940914 }. In rare cases, transcriptionally active high risk HPV is detected
{ 25724573 ; 20981655 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions are fragmented, multi-lobulated, grape-like, or polypoid masses within and often filling the
middle ear. Although physically exophytic, the majority are inverted type histologically.
Histopathology
The vast majority are the inverted subtype, showing a markedly thick, inverted (endophytic) growth
of nonkeratinizing transitional cells. The inverted areas are contained by an intact basement
membrane. Intraepithelial microcysts containing macrophages, mucin, and cell debris are present,
along with transepithelial inflammatory cells. Tumour cells lack pleomorphism, tumour necrosis,
atypical mitoses, and bone destruction. Mitotic figures are usually easily identified. Marked
pleomorphism, tumour necrosis, destructive infiltration of bone, and atypical mitoses are features
of malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma, identified in about a third of patients
{ 34021464 }.
Tall, columnar stratified cells with abundant oncocytically altered cytoplasm are seen in the rare
oncocytic papilloma category.
Differential Tumours must be separated from middle ear neuroendocrine tumour, cholesteatoma,
and squamous cell carcinoma.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: multilayered transitional epithelial proliferation with transepithelial inflammatory cells,
lacking pleomorphism, necrosis and increased mitotic figures.
Staging
Not performed
Prognosis and prediction
There is a very high (80%) local recurrence risk, along with a significant malignant transformation
risk (33%), the latter especially when concurrent sinonasal tract papilloma/carcinoma is identified
{ 34021464 }.
About
10%
of
patients
die
of
disease
{ 11802052 ; 23306579 ; 26698908 ; 34021464 }. Radical rather than conservative treatment is
associated with lower risk of local recurrence.

This T1 weighted coronal post contrast fat suppressed image demonstrates destructive
growth into the mastoid bone.

#24086
Middle ear papilloma
This axial T2 weighted fat suppressed image demonstrates masses in both the sinonasal
tract and temporal bone, with carcinoma identified in both locations.

#24088
Middle ear papilloma
Spicules of bone are being remodeled by the inverted neoplastic islands of transitional
epithelium in this middle ear papilloma.

#24089
Middle ear papilloma
A multilayered transitional epithelium shows an inverted growth into the stroma of the
middle ear. There is no pleomorphism or tumour necrosis.

793

#24090
Middle ear papilloma
Transepithelial inflammatory cells are seen in this multilayered epithelium. There is an
overall inverted growth to the tumour.

#27664
Middle ear inverted papilloma
Fragments of temporal bone are noted in the periphery of this inverted papilloma. The
histologic features of sinonasal-type inverted papilloma are present.

794

Vestibular schwannoma
Definition
A benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour arising from the eighth cranial nerve composed nearly
entirely of differentiated neoplastic Schwann cells.
ICD-O coding
9560/0 Schwannoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2F00.Y & XH98Z3 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory system &
Schwannoma,NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: Acoustic neuroma
Not recommended: neurilemmoma; neurioma
Subtype(s)
Ancient schwannoma; cellular schwannoma; epithelioid schwannoma; microcystic/reticular
schwannoma
Localization
Vestibular or acoustic schwannoma (AS) derives from the vestibular division of the 8th cranial
(vestibulocochlear) nerve { 33826821 }, and accounts for >90% of cerebellopontine angle (CPA)
tumours, with cochlear division tumours uncommon. Bilateral tumours strongly associate
with neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2) { 19545378 ; 19652604 }.
Clinical features
The majority of acoustic schwannomas are unilateral, but when bilateral, it defines NF2
{ 19652604 ; 33445724 }. Symptoms relate to the specific nerve-branch involved, mass effect,
and/or tumour progression, usually combined. The majority of patients (>90%) present with
ipsilateral sensorineural hearing loss, frequently unrecognized by the patients. Other symptoms
include vertigo, loss of balance (66%) and asymmetric tinnitus (70%) { 8971818 ; 9007856 }. Facial
numbness, pain, hydrocephalus and cerebellar tonsil herniation are rare, usually seen in
longstanding,
large
tumours
{ 8971818 ; 9007856 ; 20844798 ; 20042905 }.
Multiple
schwannomas are a feature of neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2) and schwannomatosis. In contrast
to NF2, schwannomatosis is not associated with vestibular schwannomas { 22210082 }.
Imaging: Imaging studies must be performed to document tumour size, location, and extent,
usually showing a funnel-shaped widening of the internal auditory canal (IAC)
{ 29764781 ; 29764780 ; 31469788 ; 31504802 ; 32234257 }.
Epidemiology
AS is the most common tumour occurring at the cerebellopontine angle, with a incidence 1/100,000
person years incidence, lowest in blacks and highest in Chinese patients
{ 14967754 ; 16443943 ; 24786799 ; 30032646 }, with an increased incidence over time, reflecting
improved imaging, improved kindred screening, and more rigorous protocols for unilateral hear loss
investigation { 31504802 ; 33826821 }. Patients commonly present in the fifth to sixth decades of
life, but generally <30 years when NF2 associated. Females are affected slightly more often than
males { 28441508 ; 28045630 ; 29315189 ; 30032646 ; 31504802 }.
Etiology
Exposure to ionising radiation may increase the risk of tumour development { 3173432 }, with
conflicting data about occupational or recreational exposure to loud noise over extended periods
{ 23725662 ; 24786799 ; 26996581 ; 2736213 ; 28213724 }. Mutations in tumour suppressor
gene NF2 (somatic or germline) are common.
Pathogenesis
There is a causal relationship between schwannoma tumourigenesis and loss of merlin
(schwannomin) expression, the protein product of the NF2 gene located at 22q12 { 9403715 }. The
types of mutation in the NF2 gene appear to affect disease severity and mortality { 33445724 }.
Inactivating
mutations
have
been
detected
in
50-75%
of
sporadic
cases
{ 8889506 ; 23921927 ; 26360373 ; 28409725 }.
Macroscopic appearance

795

Size is variable, with most <15 mm showing a globular to mushroom shaped mass, frequently
attached to the nerve with a smooth surface. Stretched nerve may be seen on the surface.
Sectioned tumours reveal firm, light tan glistening tissue, cystic spaces, white/yellow areas and/or
patchy haemorrhage.
Histopathology
Tumours are encapsulated, showing compact hypercellular Antoni A areas with occasional nuclear
palisading, alternating with loosely arranged Antoni B foci. Cellular zones demonstrate spindled
cells with modest eosinophilic cytoplasm, no discernible cell borders, and elongated tapered to
buckled nuclei. Microcystic to reticular areas may be seen. Mitoses are inconspicuous. Perivascular
hyalinization is characteristic. Extensive, multifocal longitudinal nerve involvement is a clue to NF2
association { 18422762 ; 28365909 }. Ancient change shows scattered, atypical pleomorphic often
hyperchromatic nuclei. Cellular schwannoma shows more closely packed cells with occasional
mitoses.
Immunohistochemistry: There is strong diffuse S100 protein and SOX10 immunoreactivity
{ 22495377 ; 25724000 }.
Differential
Separation from meningioma, neurofibroma, solitary fibrous tumour, and
paraganglioma is usually straight forward.
Cytology
Squash preparations are typically cellular with cohesive syncytial fragments of spindle cells. Within
the fragments, variably wavy and bent tumour cell nuclei with tapered edges and fibrillary cytoplasm
are seen { 2677811 ; 22297471 ; 17922589 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not routinely performed
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Tumour arising from 8th cranial nerve; dense spindled cell areas with loose hypocellular
myxoid areas; spindled cells with buckled, elongated, fusiform nuclei, occasionally palisaded
Desirable: S100 protein/SOX10 in selected cases.
Staging
Not applicable for tumour classification, but clinical tumour staging may be useful in management
{ 29204572 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Indolent tumours, recurrence may develop in up to 10%, often due to incomplete removal. Long
term sequelae include meningitis, facial nerve paralysis, bilateral hearing loss, and vestibular
dysfunction,
higher
in
patients
>65
years
{ 9092842 ; 9092841 ; 8971819 ; 9007856 ; 31129783 ; 33826821 }.

796

Alternating areas of hyper- and hypocellularity are seen in this schwannoma.

Interlacing fascicles of spindled cells are seen in this schwannoma. The nuclei are
pointed and elongated.

are wavy or buckled.

associated with pointy nuclei.

Middle ear neuroendocrine tumour (MeNET)
Definition
Middle ear neuroendocrine tumour (MeNET) is a neoplasm arising from the middle ear mucosa
showing epithelial and neuroendocrine differentiation.
ICD-O coding
8249/3 Neuroendocrine tumour
ICD-11 coding
2C21.Y & XH8DS0 Other specified malignant neoplasm of middle ear & Neuroendocrine tumour,
NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: middle ear adenoma, neuroendocrine adenoma of the middle ear, carcinoid
tumour, adenomatous tumour of the middle ear, adenocarcinoid, amphicrine tumour
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Any part of the middle ear may be affected with extension into the mastoid, eustachian tube, or
external
auditory
canal
via
ruptured
tympanic
membrane
{ 12011260 ; 22623086 ; 24766278 ; 30001283 ; 34041698 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms include hearing changes, fullness or ear pressure, and tinnitus. Otoscopy usually
reveals
a
gray-white
or
fibrotic
mass
behind
an
intact
tympanic
membrane { 12011260 ; 22623086 ; 24766278 ; 30001283 ; 34041698 }.
Imaging: CT and MRI delineate extent and tumour location, including ossicular chain involvement
{ 12011260 ; 11214650 ; 28680503 }. Functional imaging (such as 68Ga DOTATATE) targets
somatostatin receptors (especially SSTR2 and SSTR5 located on the tumour cell membrane) which
can be used for detection or follow-up monitoring { 15709214 ; 17515757 ; 33273780 ; 30001283 }.
Epidemiology
Accounting for <2% of all middle ear tumours, both sexes are equally affected, most commonly the
fifth
decade,
but
over
a
broad
age
range
{ 12011260 ; 19303146 ; 22623086 ; 24766278 ; 30001283 ; 34041698 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Still under investigation, there is evidence of origin from L-cells within the middle ear
mucosa { 1031337 ; 12011260 ; 34041698 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The tumour is white, grey, or red-brown, with soft or rubbery consistency. It is unencapsulated,
frequently entrapping and destroying the ossicles { 12011260 }.
Histopathology
Tumours exhibit cuboidal to columnar cells with distinct cell borders and eosinophilic cytoplasm.
The small central hyperchromatic nuclei rarely contain nucleoli. The tumours show many patterns
that infiltrate the stroma, including a glandular configuration in a fibrous stroma, back-to-back,
pseudorosette, trabecular, and sheet-like patterns, along with discohesive single cell infiltration;
many have prominent swirling of tumour cell ribbons. Mucins can be demonstrated within the gland
lumen. Some tumours show significant nuclear pleomorphism and increased mitotic activity.
Immunohistochemistry: The neoplastic cells co-express keratins (AE1/AE3, Cam5.2) and
neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin) along with transcription factors INSM1,
islet-1 (ISL1) and SATB2; they may co-express CDX2 and GATA3 focally but are negative for
TTF1. The most common hormones produced are glucagon, pancreatic polypeptide, and PYY, but
serotonin is also expressed { 12011260 ; 24766278 ; 29438167 ; 34041698 }. Current studies
suggest a single cell rather than a dual cell proliferation as traditionally identified, with no
myoepithelial cells { 22623086 ; 34041698 }. The Ki67 labelling index is usually low (<2%), but
reported >20%, the latter correlating with aggressive behaviour, suggesting grading similar to all

801

neuroendocrine tumours: G1, G2, and G3 { 30140036 ; 34041698 }, with cutoffs under
investigation.
Cytology
Rarely clinically applied, but reported { 29731914 ; 33044790 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: A middle ear epithelial neoplasm arranged in variable patterns, with neuroendocrine
differentiation.
Desirable: Epithelial and neuroendocrine immunoreactivity.
Staging
None, but expert-derived proposals have been introduced { 30001283 ; 30500288 }[[Thompson
LDR, Gupta R, Sandison A, Wenig BM. (2018) Ear and Temporal Bone Tumours, Histopathology
Reporting Guide, 1st edition. International Collaboration on Cancer Reporting; Sydney, Australia.
ISBN: 978-1-925687-22-4]].
Prognosis and prediction
About 15-20% of patients develop recurrence { 12011260 ; 28547535 ; 33605503 }, specifically
when the ossicles remain. In well documented, non-manipulated, non-irradiated tumours primarily
of the middle ear, metastasis is very rare and may involve cervical nodes, bone, and liver
{ 22777694 ; 28547535 ; 33044790 ; 34041698 }, more frequent with a high Ki-67 proliferation
index.

#25354
Middle ear mixed epithelial neuroendocrine tumor
The neoplastic proliferation has expanded below the middle ear epithelium. There are
various growth patterns with cords and small glands.

Various growth patterns with cords and small glands.

morphology

that were formerly thought to be epithelial non-neuroendocrine components

#25360

The cells have ample cytoplasm surrounding round nuclei with salt-and-pepper nuclear

Endolymphatic sac tumour
Definition
Endolymphatic sac tumour (ELST) is an epithelial neoplasm associated with the endolymphatic
sac/duct, often demonstrating an association with von Hippel-Lindau syndrome (VHLS).
ICD-O coding
8140/3 Endolymphatic sac tumour
ICD-11 coding
2C21.Y & XH85C2 Other specified malignant neoplasm of middle ear & Endolymphatic sac
tumour
Related terminology
Not recommended: papillary endolymphatic sac tumour; aggressive papillary adenoma; aggressive
papillary middle-ear tumour; low grade papillary adenomatous tumour of temporal bone;
endolymphatic sac papillary tumour; low grade papillary adenocarcinoma; endolymphatic sac
carcinoma; low grade carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
ELST are believed to arise in association with the endolymphatic sac/duct
{ 16322231 ; 20614260 }. Bilateral tumours are nearly always VHLS-associated { 29482989 }.
Clinical features
Ipsilateral progressive hearing loss, tinnitus, facial nerve palsy, and vertigo are common presenting
findings
{ 15796386 ; 21451430 ; 23070752 ; 25650230 ; 25867206 ; 29482989 ; 30291511 ; 32068496 }.
A
long
symptom
duration
(4
years)
suggests
slow
tumour
growth
{ 2804921 ; 7630290 ; 14710902 ; 15035284 ; 23070752 ; 30291511 }.
Imaging: Comprehensive evaluation includes a combination of MRI and CT. T1-weighted MRI
shows a hyperintense (hypervascular) heterogeneous mass, while CT shows a permeative,
destructive,
multilocular
lytic
lesion
centered
on
the
endolymphatic
sac { 9051037 ; 11942507 ; 15190140 ; 15796386 ; 17544692 ; 17991063 ; 20351605 ; 2116776
1 ; 22367728 ; 30466638 ; 31197530 ; 32068496 }.
Epidemiology
Data from the International ELST Registry { 25867206 } show a 3.6% prevelance of ELSTs in
VHLS. VHL germline mutations were identified in 39% of apparently sporadic ELSTs, showing
ELSTs are the initial presentation of VHLS in 32% of patients. These findings support genetic
testing in all patients with ELSTs { 20351605 ; 20495761 ; 21451430 ; 25650230 ; 25867206 } and
all
VHLS
patients
should
be
radiographically
screened
for
ELSTs
{ 15035284 ; 15190140 ; 23070752 }. There is a wide age range at presentation (10 to 88 years),
but the majority are between 30 and 40 years, although younger in VHLS patients { 30291511 }.
There
is
a
female
to
male
ratio
of
1.6:1
{ 2804921 ; 15035284 ; 15190140 ; 23070752 ; 30291511 }.
Etiology
Germline mutations of the VHL tumour suppressor gene are usually detected in these patients,
although sporadic cases may lack such abnormalities { 11085513 ; 15035285 ; 18423895 }.
Somatic and germline mutation analysis of ELSTs has been performed, with many VHL mutations
and
allelic
deletions
identified
{ 9214679 ; 10932304 ; 11085513 ; 15796386 ; 16322231 ; 20351605 ; 20495761 ; 20850701 }.
Pathogenesis
VHLS is an autosomal-dominant inherited disease, caused by germline mutations in the tumour
suppressor gene VHL (3p25). When inherited, a second mutation affecting the wild-type allele will
result in a cell with no functional VHL protein, at risk for tumour development
{ 16322231 ; 20151405 ; 21386872 }.
Macroscopic appearance

809

Tumours range from millimeters up to 100 mm (mean 30 mm), usually larger in older patients.
Tumours are unencapsulated, destructive lesions, with bone invasion and remodeling
{ 7936748 ; 20614260 ; 30291511 }.
Histopathology
The tumours are papillary and/or cystic, commonly with bone “invasion” or remodeling. A single
layer of low cuboidal to columnar epithelial cells are arranged in simple, coarse, broad
interdigitating papillary projections with fibrovascular cores, showing limited branching, found within
cystic spaces. Cystic spaces contain serum, secretions, and/or erythrocytes. Fibrosis may be
present. The acinar spaces filled with inspissated material with similarity to thyroid colloid. The
cytoplasm is ample, clear, vacuolated with indistinct cell membranes. The nuclei are uniformly
small, round, and hyperchromatic with coarse nuclear chromatin and small nucleoli
{ 2804921 ; 7936748 ; 9145719 ; 16778477 ; 18423895 ; 20614260 ; 21167761 ; 30291511 }.
Pleomorphism, increased mitotic figures, and necrosis are inconspicuous.
Immunohistochemistry: The neoplastic cells are positive for pancytokeratin, CK7, HIF-1α, EMA,
GLUT1, CAIX (membranous), and PAX-8 (nuclear), with limited S100 protein, GFAP, and vimentin,
and negative for TTF-1, thyroglobulin, PSA, CD10, P504S, p63, synaptophysin, GATA3, and RCC
{ 7630290 ; 7936748 ; 9023246 ; 11455007 ; 15035285 ; 16778477 ; 18423895 ; 30291511 }.
Differential The major differential diagnoses include metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma
(CCRCC), papillary or follicular thyroid carcinoma, along with choroid plexus papilloma, papillary
ependymoma, papillary meningioma, paraganglioma and middle ear mixed epithelial
neuroendocrine tumour { 25944396 ; 30291511 }. Metastatic CCRCC, especially when in a VHLSassociated patient, can be quite challenging, but immunohistochemistry studies aid in distinction
{ 30291511 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Endolymphatic sac/duct location
Simple, coarse, broad interdigitation papillary projections lined by low cuboidal to columnar
epithelial cells with clear or slightly eosinophilic cytoplasm surrounding round nuclei with coarse
chromatin
Desirable: In selected cases, reactivity with pancytokeratin, CAIX and PAX8, while non-reactive
with TTF1, GATA3, and RCC
Staging
Not applicable.
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis is dependent on the extent of the disease, with recurrences common when
incompletely
excised.
Metastases
are
not
reported
{ 15354011 ; 17315832 ; 17544692 ; 21451430 ; 22367728 ; 23685713 ; 24662627 ; 25650230 ;
29923043 ; 32068496 }. Overall outcome is often dependent on other stigmata of VHLS
{ 2804921 ; 16778477 }.

816

Middle ear squamous cell carcinoma
Definition
A carcinoma showing squamous differentiation arising from metaplastic squamous epithelium lining
the middle ear.
ICD-O coding
8070/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2C21.1 Squamous cell carcinoma of middle ear
Related terminology
Not recommended: epidermoid carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) arising primarily in the middle ear (MeSCC) is rare. Direct
extension from SCC of the pinna, nasopharynx, and/or the sinonasal tract is more common, but
not herein discussed { 32366754 }.
Clinical features
Presenting findings include hearing loss, otorrhoea, otalgia, bleeding, tinnitus, vertigo, and facial
nerve palsy { 20513031 ; 26472473 ; 23553471 ; 24121777 ; 28692593 }.
Epidemiology
MeSCC
affects
approximately
1
per
6
million
individuals
{ 19373882 ; 25081186 ; 26472473 ; 28692593 }. Mean age at presentation is 60 years (range 2189 years) with equal sex distribution { 15925819 ; 20513031 ; 24121777 }. MeSCC is rarely
bilateral { 10077254 ; 11667995 }.
Etiology
Predisposing factors include chronic otitis media and irradiation { 25932267 ; 31890889 }. Viral
etiology
and
genetic
alterations
(such
as DEK::AFF2) have
been
described
{ 21493437 ; 32366754 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Late presentation is common with tumour filling the middle ear and extending into mastoid air
spaces { 15925819 ; 24121777 ; 25081186 ; 26472473 ; 28692593 }.
Histopathology
The histologic features and immunohistochemical profile are similar to SCC at other sites, with
pleomorphic epithelial cells arranged in sheets with pavemented appearance, increased nuclear to
cytoplasmic ratio, and coarse nuclear chromatin. Most tumours are keratinising and well to
moderately differentiated. Non-keratinising tumours may be EBV-associated or harbour genetic
alterations (such as DEK::AFF2 rearrangements) { 21493437 ; 32366754 ; 34049316 }.
Immunohistochemistry: Immunoreactivity for pancytokeratin, p40, p63, and CK5/6 is noted.
Differential Direct extension from external ear, nasopharynx, or sinonasal tract tumours should be
excluded with clinical and radiological correlation, with EBER, NUT immunohistochemistry, or
molecular
testing
for
genetic
rearrangements
aiding
in
classification { 21493437 ; 28060373 ; 34049316 ; 34108636 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically performed
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Infiltrating squamous epithelial proliferation with malignant cytological and architectural
features with or without keratin production; clinical exclusion of direct extension from adjacent sites.
Staging

817

Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification for
head and neck cutaneous SCC, although expert-derived modifications are suggested
{ 30500288 }[[Thompson LDR, Gupta R, Sandison A, Wenig BM. (2018) Ear and Temporal Bone
Tumours, Histopathology Reporting Guide, 1st edition. International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting; Sydney, Australia. ISBN: 978-1-925687-22-4]].
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis is generally poor due to advanced disease at presentation and delayed diagnosis
{ 31890889 }. Outcome is related to stage at presentation { 23507994 } and reported 5 year survival
is between 25% for high volume disease and 83% for low volume disease
{ 23202152 ; 23553471 ; 24121777 ; 24492135 ; 26472473 }. Direct intracranial extension carries
a poor prognosis. Nodal metastases are uncommon, affecting levels 2 and 3 neck lymph nodes
when identified { 20513031 }, while distant metastases are rare.

#25132
Middle ear squamous cell carcinoma
A tumour arising within a background of cholesteatoma. Destruction of the malleus by
infiltrating nests of squamous cells with moderate cytologic atypia.

#27489
Middle ear squamous cell carcinoma
A fragment of bone is noted along with a desmoplastic stromal reaction to an invasive
squamous cell carcinoma, showing abundant keratinization and eaily identified
pleomorphism.

820

#27399
Ear Squamous cell carcinoma
There is a malignant squamous epithelial proliferation identified in the context of a
cholesteatoma. Destructive stromal invasion is noted by an atypical squamous epithelium.

Middle ear adenocarcinoma
Definition
Middle ear adenocarcinoma is a locally aggressive primary epithelial malignancy arising from the
middle ear showing glandular differentiation.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2C21.0 Adenocarcinoma of middle ear
Related terminology
Acceptable: papillary adenocarcinoma of middle ear; low grade adenocarcinoma
Not recommended: Endolymphatic sac tumour (ELST) (distinct tumour category); aggressive
papillary tumour (APT) of middle ear
Subtype(s)

821

None
Localization
The tumour extensively fills the middle ear and/or tympanic cavity, expanding into the petrous bone
apex, sometimes involving the cerebellopontine angle and cerebellum { 18633929 }.
Clinical features
Reported
mostly
as case
reports
{ 8692411 ; 11307613 ; 14738614 ; 17167601 ; 18633929 ; 32940914 }, symptoms are often of a
long duration and non-specific including hearing loss, otalgia, vestibular dysfunction, and
otorrhoea. Association with von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) syndrome should be determined.
Epidemiology
The age range at presentation is broad (16-55 years; median 33 years; mean 34 years), but due to
long symptom duration, onset age may be younger. Females are affected slightly more often than
males { 8692411 ; 11307613 ; 14738614 ; 17167601 ; 18633929 ; 32940914 }. Isolated cases
have been associated with VHL syndrome.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
VHL mutations have been described in association with aggressive papillary tumours involving
middle ear but that involved the endolymphatic sac. Mouse models of histologically similar tumours
showed EGFR mutations { 26027747 }. Oncocytic and papillary tumours have been reported to
have MKRN1::BRAF fusion { 32940914 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The middle ear cleft and mastoid air cells are usually filled with a bone destructive tumour.
Histopathology
Tumours show a cystic-papillary glandular pattern with complex architecture including papillae lying
loosely or infiltrating fibrous connective tissue. The papillae are usually lined by a single layer of
low cuboidal to columnar epithelial cells with uniform nuclei, eosinophilic cytoplasm, and indistinct
cell borders. Thyroid follicle-like areas may be present similar to those seen in endolymphatic sac
tumour. Oncocytic, mucinous, polymorphous adenocarcinoma, and even salivary duct carcinomalike appearances
suggest
a
wide
morphologic spectrum
in
these
tumours
{ 32940914 ; 34097084 }.
Immunohistochemistry and Differential Tumour cells are positive for CK7 and EMA, and
sometimes S100 protein. PAX8 and carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX) are negative, opposite to ELST
{ 30291511 ; 32940914 }, which is the principle differential consideration. Metastatic carcinomas
(lung, thyroid, colon) should be excluded.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not diagnostically useful
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: imaging confirmation of middle ear localization; bone destructive adenocarcinoma, often
with papillary growth; exclusion of metastases.
Staging
Without standardized staging, ICCR minimum data set reporting is encouraged
{ 30500288 }[[Thompson LDR, Gupta R, Sandison A, Wenig BM. (2018) Ear and Temporal Bone
Tumours, Histopathology Reporting Guide, 1st edition. International Collaboration on Cancer
Reporting; Sydney, Australia. ISBN: 978-1-925687-22-4]].
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis depends on the size of the lesion at presentation and the adequacy of the surgical
excision.

#28388

There is a very complex papillary architecture, with mucinous producing cells. A
concurrent cholesteatoma is noted (left upper).

#28389

Cuboidal to columnar epithelial cells are noted lining the papillae. There is easily identified
mucin production.

#28390

The columnar cells show basally located nuclei and abundant mucinous material in the
cytoplasm. Pleomorphism is limited.

#27779
Adenocarcinoma of middle ear
Tumour showing a papillary glandular pattern with complex architecture including papillae
lying loosely or infiltrating fibrous connective tissue. The papillae are lined by a single
layer of columnar epithelial cells with uniform nuclei and vacuolated to eosinophilic
cytoplasm.

10. Soft tissue tumours

Introduction
Many different soft tissue tumours arise in the soft tissues and mucosa-covered surfaces of the
head and neck region. Some mesenchymal neoplasms more commonly arise in other parts of the
body, some such as spindle cell lipoma, have a predilection for the head and neck region, while
others are unique to specific head and neck sites. The most significant change from the 4th edition
of the classification is to remove most soft tissue tumours from each anatomical site and collate the
diagnostic information in one chapter for all relevant head and neck sites. Selected tumours specific
to, or most often found in specific sites, are covered in site-specific chapters (see Table 1). Most of
the bone lesions are presented in the Odontogenic and Maxillofacial Bone Tumours chapter
(Chapter 7).

825

The 5th edition of the WHO Classification of Tumours of Soft Tissue and Bone [cross-ref to website]
provides a more detailed account of each soft tissue tumour. In this chapter, the authors provide
the key aetiological, pathogenetic, diagnostic, and prognostic information on each neoplasm.
Where appropriate, information specific to head and neck neoplasms is emphasised. The
classification remains primarily morphological but immunocytochemistry and other molecular
techniques are often useful to confirm a diagnosis (e.g. solitary fibrous tumour) or to resolve a
differential diagnosis. Molecular techniques are increasingly valuable to identify targets for specific
treatments.

13.2.0.6

Adipocytic tumours

Lipoma family
Definition
Lipomas are a family of benign mesenchymal tumours composed predominantly of adipocytes
admixed with other cellular constituents and include conventional lipoma, spindle cell
lipoma/pleomorphic lipoma, dysplastic lipoma, fibrolipoma, chondrolipoma, angiolipoma and
intramuscular lipoma.
ICD-O coding
8850/0 Lipoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E80.0 Lipoma
Related terminology
None.
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
Conventional lipoma in the head and neck is less common than at many other body sites and rarely
involves intraoral, hypopharyngeal or laryngeal sites { 32851988 }. The buccal mucosa, lower lip
and tongue are the most commonly affected sites { 32851988 ; 31041916 }. Spindle
cell/pleomorphic and dysplastic lipomas mainly occur in the posterior neck, and less frequently in
the oral cavity, scalp and face { 31291740 }. Dysplastic lipoma occurs preferentially in upper back,
shoulders, and posterior neck { 30001242 }.
Clinical features
Lipoma typically presents as a slowly growing asymptomatic, circumscribed mass less than 5cm
diameter { 21447447 }; some cases are associated with lipomatosis { 18644522 ; 19192164 }.
Occasionally, multiple lesions of SCL/PL are seen; these cases may be familial
{ 9422314 ; 12522375 }. 15% of dysplastic lipomas are multifocal and occasionally they occur postretinoblastoma { 28807343 ; 30001242 }.
Epidemiology

826

Lipomas are more common in men than women. They occur in all age groups but most commonly
in the 5th-6th decades of life and are rare in children { 24800932 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
HMGA2 rearrangements
are
associated
with
conventional
lipoma
{ 10747931 ; 15593017 ; 28139834 }. Rb1 deletion is detected in the majority of spindle
cell/pleomorphic lipoma { 12494468 }. Dysplastic lipoma harbours Rb1 deletions and a subset of
cases occur post-retinoblastoma { 28807343 ; 30001242 }. A vascular anomaly is considered the
underlying mechanism in intramuscular lipoma and may explain its tendency to recur.
Macroscopic appearance
Lipomas are well circumscribed and encapsulated with a yellowish cut-surface. Intramuscular and
facial skin lipomas may show infiltrating borders { 25219904 }. SCL is variably yellow, greyishwhite, and myxoid, depending on the proportion of the constituent elements.
Histopathology
Conventional lipoma displays adipocytes lacking atypia.
Spindle cell lipoma and pleomorphic lipoma (SCL/PL) represent the morphological spectrum of a
single neoplasm. Spindle cell lipoma (SCL) is a benign adipocytic tumour composed of variable
amounts of mature adipocytes, bland spindle cells, and ropey collagen. Pleomorphic lipoma (PL)
also contains pleomorphic and multinucleated floret-like giant cells { 25319950 }. Mitoses are rare
but single atypical-looking mitoses may be present as well as lipoblasts
{ 28546131 ; 28843712 }. Skeletal muscle involvement is seen in 36% of facial spindle cell lipomas
{ 25219904 }.
Dysplastic lipoma shows striking anisocytosis of adipocytes but little or no atypia. They lack the
stromal component and atypical pleomorphic cells of spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomas
{ 30001242 }.
Fibrolipoma combines mature fat cells with prominent intermingled paucicellular collagenous
component. Chondrolipoma is characterized by foci of mature cartilage. Angiolipoma contain a
variable vascular component with intermingled or confluent capillary-size vessels frequently
associated with fibrin microthrombi. Intramuscular lipoma shows infiltrating mature adipocytes
within skeletal muscle with frequent thick-walled vessels.
Degenerative-related changes have resulted in misclassification of submucosal lingual lipomas as
atypical lipomatous tumors, but all lack MDM2 amplification { 29748845 }. Rare (low-fat) tumours
may lack adipocytes.
Cytology
The smears from SCL show a mixture of adipocytes and uniform spindle cells often set in a myxoid
stroma. Fragments of brightly eosinophilic collagen fibres are present. PL shows multinucleated
giant cells with hyperchromatic nuclei and a moderate amount of cytoplasm {11748578 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
MDM2 assessment may rule out atypical lipomatous tumors in traumatized lesions { 29748845 }.
Demonstration of loss of RB1 can be helpful in selected cases to confirm
SCL/PL{ 12494468 ; 21563233 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Neoplasm composed either exclusively of mature adipocytes or combined with stromal
elements.
Desirable: absence of MDM2 amplification (traumatized and atypical-looking lesions).
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Lipomas are cured by simple excision. Recurrence is rare. Non-destructive recurrence has been
reported in 15-20% dysplastic lipomas following non-radical resection. Malignant transformation
has not been observed.
SCL/PL is a benign tumour that is adequately treated with conservative excision. Local recurrence
is rare, even with incomplete resection { 1192370 ; 7459800 ; 998247 }.

collagen bands.

Liposarcoma family
Definition
Liposarcomas are a heterogeneous group of malignant adipocytic neoplasms with type-dependent
features and include atypical lipomatous tumour/well-differentiated LS (ALT/WD-LS);
dedifferentiated liposarcoma (DD-LS); myxoid/round cell liposarcoma (MLS/RCLS); pleomorphic
liposarcoma (PLS); and myxoid pleomorphic liposarcoma (MPLS).
ICD-O coding
8850/1
Atypical
lipomatous
tumour
8851/3 Liposarcoma, well-differentiated, NOS
8858/3 Dedifferentiated liposarcoma
8852/3 Myxoid liposarcoma
8854/3 Pleomorphic liposarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2F7C & XH0RW4 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of connective or other soft tissue & Atypical
lipomatous
tumour
2B5H & XH7Y61 Well-differentiated lipomatous tumour, primary site & Liposarcoma, welldifferentiated
2B59 & XH1C03 Liposarcoma, primary site & Dedifferentiated liposarcoma
2B59.Y & XH3EL0 Liposarcoma, other specified primary site & Myxoid liposarcoma
2B59.Y & XH25R1 Liposarcoma, other specified primary site & Pleomorphic liposarcoma
2B59.Y & XH3EL0 & XH25R1 Liposarcoma, other specified primary site & Myxoid liposarcoma &
Pleomorphic liposarcoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: atypical lipoma, lipoma-like liposarcoma (for ALT/WD-LS); pleomorphic myxoid
liposarcoma (for MPLS)
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
80% liposarcomas in this site involve the subcutaneous connective tissue of the face, neck, and
scalp { 23728920 }. MLS may metastasize to unusual sites, including the head and neck region
{ 22473934 }
Clinical features
Patients often present with a mass. Symptoms are site- and size-dependent { 12379747 }.
Epidemiology
Liposarcomas comprise 2-9% of sarcomas in the head and neck. The mean age is 60 years. Males
(76%) outnumber females and white people (>80%) the blacks and asians. The most common
subtype is ALT/WD-LS, followed by myxoid, pleomorphic and dedifferentiated LS { 23728920 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis

831

WD-LS/DD-LS: amplified sequences of 12q14-15, including MDM2, TSPAN31, HMGA2, CDK4,
YEATS4, CPM, FRS2, STAT6 { 31501988 ; 24457460 }.
MLS: the driver mutation is FUS-DDIT3 or EWSR1-DDIT3 { 7566973 ; 8637704 }.
PLS:
complex
numerical
chromosome
aberrations,
including
loss
of RB1, P53 and NF1 { 31501988 }.
MPLS: complex numerical chromosome aberrations, including loss of RB1; mutations
in KMT2D { 26647907 }.
ASCLT/APLT:
deletion/losses
of
13q14,
including RB1,
RCBTB2,
DLEU1 and ITM2B { 31501988 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Liposarcomas form (multi)nodular fatty (ALT/WD LS) or grey-white firm tumours with a variable
fatty component (DDLS, PLS, ASCLT/APLT). Myxoid/gelatinous areas can be observed in all
lesions. Necrotic areas are mainly found in high-grade lesions { 31950475 }.
Histopathology
ALT/WD-LS consists of atypical adipocytes with enlarged hyperchromatic nuclei. Irregular septa
contain atypical stromal cells with hyperchromatic nuclei. Lipoblasts may be present { 12379747 }.
MDM2 and CDK4 provide strong immunohistochemical support for the diagnosis { 22301498 }
DD-LS: variable, spindle cell sarcomatous pattern often combined with a WD-LS component.
Lipoblastic,
rhabdomyoblastic
or
osteogenic
differentiation
may
occur
{ 12379747 ; 22301498 ; 24457460 ; 31950475 }.
MLS are arranged in lobules consisting of a variable number of pre-lipoblasts and lipoblasts in a
myxoid background with a characteristic plexiform capillary network. Adipocytes may be present.
The lobules often show peripheral condensation of primitive cells. High grade MLS show >5% solid
sheets of undifferentiated mainly round cells { 31950475 }. DDIT3 immunohistochemistry is a
surrogate marker for the fusion gene { 33465826 }.
PLS is a high-grade sarcoma defined by the presence of atypical lipoblasts in an otherwise highgrade sarcoma with spindle cell- and/or epithelioid cell features. A dedifferentiated liposarcoma
may be excluded by expression of MDM2/CDK4 or amplification of MDM2. RB can be absent
{ 31950475 }.
MPLS shows mixed features of MLS and PLS with atypical and bland looking lipoblasts, variable
pleomorphic stromal cells and myxoid matrix and less vascularity { 31501988 ; 19194281 }.
ASCLT/APLT shows additional to classical features of spindle cell and pleomorphic lipoma, atypical
stromal and fat cells including lipoblasts. Infiltration into the surrounding tissues occurs variably
{ 27879515 ; 28877053 ; 33782225 }
Cytology
Cytological features of the subtypes of liposarcoma reflect their histological
composition { 19899126 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
ALT/WD/DD-LS: MDM2 amplification
MLS/RCLS: FUS/EWSR1-DDIT3 fusions
ASCLT/APLT, MPLS, PLS: RB1 deletion
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
ALT/WD-LS: atypical stromal cells in irregular septa, adipocytes may show (subtle) atypia
DD-LS: non-adipogenic sarcoma and atypical fat cells
MLS: prelipoblasts (round or spindle cells) and lipoblasts, chicken-wire vasculature, myxoid
background; round cell hypercellular areas >5% in high-grade lesions
PLS: pleomorphic lipoblasts in an otherwise high-grade sarcoma “NOS”
ASCLT/APLT: spindle cell/pleomorphic lipoma morphology with atypia of stromal cells and fat cells
including lipoblasts
MPLS: mixed features of PLS and MLS.
Desirable: appropriate immunocytochemistry or molecular features (selected cases)
Staging
Not clinically relevant in ALT/WD and ASCLT/APLT. For other subtypes the American Joint
Committee on Cancer (AJCC) and Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM systems
can be applied.
Prognosis and prediction

832

Liposarcomas of the head and neck are usually early stage and low-grade. Survival is significantly
better for liposarcoma of the head and neck than other sites { 23728920 }. However, prognosis
depends on subtype and localization.
ALT/WD-LS: may recur if incompletely excised { 24800932 }.
ASCLT/APLT: recurrence is rare { 33782225 ; 27879715 }.
DD-LS: often recurs and shows metastatic potential in a small subset { 24800932 }
MLS: recurrence and metastatic disease depends on grade. Most tumours behave as low-grade
sarcomas. When metastasized, the outcome is worse { 22190864 }
PLS, MPLS: often aggressive behavior with metastases and fatal outcome
{ 24800932 ; 31501988 ; 31950475 }. Superficial lesions often have a good prognosis { 31950475 }

L#27329
Dedifferentiated liposarcoma
This field shows the abrupt transition from well-differentiated liposarcoma to a nonlipogenic sarcoma.
R#27330
Myxoid liposarcoma
This type of liposarcoma is composed of prelipoblasts and (univacuolar) lipoblasts set in a
myxoid matrix. Note the typical peripheral condensation of cells in the lower part of the
field.

#27331
Pleomorphic myxoid liposarcoma
This field shows atypical uni- and plurivacuolated lipoblasts and pleomorphic cells in a
prominent myxoid matrix.

833

Fibroblastic and myofibroblastic tumours

Nodular fasciitis
Definition
Nodular fasciitis (NOF) is a transient neoplasm typically found in subcutaneous tissue, composed
of bland myofibroblasts, and usually harbouring USP6 rearrangements.
ICD-O coding
8828/0 Nodular fasciitis
ICD-11 coding
FB51.2 & XH5LM1 Pseudosarcomatous fibromatosis & Nodular fasciitis.
Related terminology
Not recommended: pseudosarcomatous fasciitis.
Subtype(s)
Intravascular fasciitis; cranial fasciitis
Localization
NOF typically develops in the subcutis; dermal and deep localization is rare. Any anatomical site
can be involved, including face, neck, oral cavity, orbit, parotid and ear. Intravascular fasciitis
occurs
in
small
to
medium-sized
vessels
{ 6462780 ; 20716998 ; 19469872 ; 31950474 ; 20716998 ; 27686647 ; 33000481 }.
Clinical features
Nodular fasciitis grows rapidly and usually has a preoperative duration of not more than 2–
3 months. It usually measures ≤ 3 cm on excision { 31950474 }. Extension into adjacent structures
does not exclude the diagnosis { 32192385 ; 9923933 ; 27686647 ; 31950474 }.
Epidemiology
Up to 25% of NOF occur in the head and neck region, affecting all age groups with a peak incidence
in the third and fourth decades. There is no sex predilection. Intravascular and cranial fasciitis are
rare. Cranial fasciitis develops predominantly in infants aged <2 years, more commonly in boys
{ 31950474 ; 6462780 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
The identification of recurrent USP6 gene rearrangements as driver mutations has established the
clonal neoplastic nature { 12550774 ; 21826056 ; 28752842 }. Several fusion partners lead by
promotor switch to transcriptional activation of USP6. Activated pathways include NF-kB, Wntcatenin, JAK1-STAT3 { 32635781 }.
See additional information in STB5.
Macroscopic appearance
NOF is unencapsulated and circumscribed or infiltrative. The cut surface varies from myxoid to
fibrous, occasionally with central cystic change { 31950474 }.
Histopathology
NOF (including subtypes) is composed of plump, spindle-shaped cells lacking nuclear
pleomorphism. Mitotic figures may be plentiful without atypical forms. There is typically a tissue
culture–like architecture. Cellular areas often show a storiform growth pattern. The stroma may be
variably myxoid with microcystic stromal changes or fibrous, sometimes with keloidal collagen.
There are extravasated erythrocytes, lymphocytes, and osteoclast-like giant cells. Small vessels
may be numerous, occasionally resembling granulation tissue. Extension into adjacent structures
may be present { 1928550 ; 6462780 ; 31950474 }.
Lesional cells express SMA and MSA; focal desmin positivity is occasionally found
{ 1928550 ; 31950474 }.
Cytology
Bland spindle cells with unipolar curved/bipolar processes, lacking relevant atypia. A tissue culture–
like appearance and myxoid stroma can be appreciated { 30311731 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
In selected cases, USP6 rearrangement may confirm the diagnosis { 28752842 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: bland myofibroblastic proliferation with tissue culture–like growth pattern.
834

Desirable: assessment of USP6 rearrangement in selected cases
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence is rare. Metastatic disease is exceptional { 31950474 ; 27113271 }.

L#26417
The lesion is often circumscribed.
R#26418
This field shows the tissue culture appearance of nodular fasciitis.

#26419
Bland looking myofibroblastic cells are loosely arranged in a fibromyxoid matrix.

Desmoid fibromatosis
Definition
Desmoid fibromatosis is a locally aggressive, non-metastasizing myofibroblastic neoplasm with
infiltrative growth and propensity for local recurrence.
ICD-O coding
8821/1 Desmoid-type fibromatosis
ICD-11 coding
2F7C & XH13Z3 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of connective or other soft tissue & Desmoidtype
fibromatosis
(aggressive
fibromatosis)

835

2F7C & XH6116 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of connective or other soft tissue & Abdominal
(mesenteric) fibromatosis
Related terminology
Acceptable: Aggressive/deep fibromatosis; desmoid tumour
Subtype(s)
Extra-abdominal desmoid
Localization
Soft tissues of the neck/mandibular region are commonly involved. Less frequently affected are
the scalp, face, paranasal sinuses, nasopharynx, oral cavity and larynx { 31950474 ; 31059930 }.
Multifocality may be seen { 29705714 }.
Clinical features
Patients usually report no pain. Lesions can be large (> 5 cm) and become symptomatic by forming
a mass and from invasion of bone, nerves and vasculature { 31950474 ; 24206198 ; 31059930 }.
Epidemiology
Fibromatosis of the head and neck is rare, comprising up to 15% of all extra-abdominal desmoids in
adults and 35% in children { 31950474 ; 31059930 ; 27988199 }. Patients have a median age of
37–39 years. The disease is more common in women than men.
Etiology
There are multiple causes including genetic events and external factors such as
trauma { 31950474 }.
Pathogenesis
Desmoid fibromatosis derives from mesenchymal progenitor cells. Mutations in CTNNB1 or less
frequently APC, stabilize beta-catenin which activates the Wnt/APC/beta-catenin pathway
{ 29705714 ; 20841474 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Fibromatosis forms a poorly delineated, firm, grey-white lesion with a coarse trabecular cut surface
{ 24206198 ; 31950474 }.
Histopathology
Neoplasms are infiltrative and composed of ill-defined long fascicles of uniform myofibroblasts with
slender, tapering, stellate-shaped or oval nuclei with an open chromatin. Mitotic figures are variably
present and often scarce. There is inconspicuous cytoplasm. The background is most often
collagenous, sometimes showing coarse bundles, but can be also strikingly myxoid blurring the
classical architecture. Parallel with the fascicles are small vessels with perivascular oedema.
Immunohistochemically, SMA and nuclear beta catenin are variably expressed, with the latter
showing ~80% positivity { 31950474 ; 29705714 ; 24206198 }.
Cytology
FNA shows clusters of fibroblasts without atypia.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The finding of hot spot somatic mutations in exon3 of CTNNB1 may be helpful in small biopsies or
in selected cases if morphology is inconclusive { 31950474 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: long fascicles of monomorphic myofibroblasts with small tapered nuclei and
inconspicuous cytoplasm
Desirable: Nuclear expression of beta-catenin; CTNNB1 hot spot or alternatively APC mutation
Staging
Not applicable.
Prognosis and prediction
The course is variable and unpredictable with progression, stable disease and spontaneous
regression. An individual approach is mandatory. A wait and see policy is advocated in
asymptomatic patients. Systemic treatments comprise antihormonal or non-steroidal antiinflammatory therapies, tyrosine kinase inhibitors and chemotherapy. Recurrence after surgery
does not correlate with margin status. The question of whether tumours
harbouring CTNNB1 pSer45Phe mutations are associated with a greater risk for local recurrence
is controversial { 31950474 ; 29705714 ; 31059930 ; 32004793 }.

836

#26409 There are long fascicles of uniform myofibroblasts in bundles with tapered nuclei.
Lymphocytes are sometimes obvious at the margin.

#26411 Tumours may show desmin and/or SMA expression in the myofibroblasts.
#26413 Nuclear expression of beta-catenin is characteristic.

Solitary fibrous tumour
Definition
Solitary fibrous tumour is a fibroblastic tumour characterized by a prominent, branching, thinwalled,
dilated
(staghorn)
vasculature
most
commonly
associated
with NAB2STAT6 rearrangement.
ICD-O coding
8815/0 Solitary fibrous tumour, benign
8815/1 Solitary fibrous tumour, NOS
8815/3 Solitary fibrous tumour, malignant
ICD-11 coding
2F7C & XH7E62 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of connective or other soft tissue & Solitary
fibrous tumour NOS
2B5Y & XH1HP3 Other specified malignant mesenchymal neoplasms & Solitary fibrous tumour,
malignant.
Related terminology
Acceptable: Extrapleural solitary fibrous tumour
Not recommended: Fibrous mesothelioma; haemangiopericytoma; giant cell angiofibroma, benign
solitary fibrous tumour; glomangiopericytoma
Subtype(s)
Lipomatous solitary fibrous tumour; giant cell–rich solitary fibrous tumour
Localization
These occur most commonly in the orbit, followed by nasal cavity alone (~50%) and/or paranasal
sinuses, however any site including salivary glands, larynx and oral cavity may be affected

837

{ 26924782 ; 31206727 ; 28838212 ; 32529350 ; 29282671 ; 22002440 ; 20614325 ; 28877055 ;
26026772 }.
Clinical features
Patients with an SFT usually present with a painless, slow growing mass. There may be visual
changes, pain, and proptosis for orbital tumours { 32529350 }; obstructive symptoms, epistaxis,
headaches, and discharge for sinonasal tumours { 29282671 }; hoarseness, foreign body
sensation, breathing difficulty and voice changes for laryngeal lesions { 20614325 }. Paraneoplastic
syndrome has been reported { 16025074 }.
Computed tomography shows well-circumscribed tumours that strongly enhance with contrast.
Tumours show enhancement with gadolinium contrast on T1-weighted magnetic resonance
{ 17337142 ; 23413243 }.
Epidemiology
About 15% of SFT develop in the head and neck { 11920476 ; 26924782 ; 31206727 }. Males are
affected more often (M:F 6:1) for laryngeal tumours, with an equal sex distribution and a broad age
range at presentation (20-80 years; median ~ 50 years) for other sites
{ 22002440 ; 29282671 ; 32529350 ; 31206727 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
SFT almost always show a recurrent NAB2::STAT6 gene fusion { 24513261 ; 24807787 }.
The NGFI-A binding protein 2 (NAB2) fuses with signal transducer and activator of transcription
6 (STAT6) as a result of a paracentric inversion at chromosomal region 12q13.
Macroscopic appearance
In cavities, there is usually a polypoid, firm-fibrous and gray-white mass, possibly attached by a
stalk. In soft tissue, the same firm masses are circumscribed, but unencapsulated if
benign. Malignant tumours tend to be infiltrative and may have necrosis. The median size is
smallest for larynx (25 mm), intermediate for salivary gland (40 mm), and largest for sinonasal tract
sites (50 mm) { 20614325 ; 32529350 ; 23413243 ; 29282671 ; 22002440 }.
Histopathology
SFT are nonencapsulated masses containing a patternless distribution of hypo- and hypercellular
areas, intermingled with loose to dense keloid-like collagen. Sheets of uniform, bland, syncytial
spindled cells are separated by dilated, angulated vascular spaces. Mitoses, while uncommon, aid
in risk stratification. Myxoid change, fibrosis, and interstitial mast cells may be seen. Biologically
aggressive features include increased cellularity, infiltrative borders, marked pleomorphism,
necrosis, and increased mitoses { 26924782 ; 28838212 ; 31206727 ; 32529350 }.
The lipomatous subtype contains mature adipocytes; the giant cell-rich subtype has tumour giant
cells; both of these types are rare in the head and neck { 28877055 }.
Cells show nuclear reactivity with STAT6, a highly specific and sensitive marker
{ 24030747 ; 24625420 ; 24977739 ; 25873501 ; 26811389 }, along with CD34, bcl-2 and CD99.
Desmin, S100 protein, SOX10, actins, and ß-catenin are usually negative
{ 32529350 ; 29282671 ; 22002440 ; 24977739 ; 25873501 ; 29282671 }. STAT6 and CD34 may
be lost in malignant tumours { 27189111 ; 30623305 }.
Within sinonasal tract, the differential diagnosis includes glomangiopericytoma, peripheral nerve
sheath tumours, leiomyoma, desmoid-type fibromatosis, and biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma;
within salivary gland, orbit, and oral cavity: myoepithelioma, pleomorphic adenoma, and nodular
fasciitis.
Cytology
Smears are cellular with loosely cohesive clusters around blood vessels or dispersed cells with
bare nuclei { 32686237 ; 15192959 }. Round, oval and spindled cells have scant cytoplasm with
isomorphic ovoid nuclei { 15192959 }. Wispy collagenous stroma may be identified
{ 32686237 ; 15192959 ; 28469319 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
As FISH is difficult to interpret due to the small size of the inversion, STAT6 immunohistochemistry
and NAB2::STAT6 fusion analysis are more reliable { 24030747 ; 30623305 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: spindled to ovoid cells arranged around a branching and hyalinized vasculature; variable
stromal collagen deposition; nuclear STAT6 expression by immunohistochemistry.
Desirable: demonstration of NAB2-STAT6 gene fusion (in selected cases).

838

Staging
Multivariable risk stratification models are used instead of staging { 31206727 ; 32529350 }.
Prognosis and prediction
The majority are benign. Multiple risk models with variable clinicopathologic features (age, size,
cellularity,
mitoses,
necrosis,
pleomorphism)
can
predict
aggressive
behavior
{ 22575866 ; 26924782 ; 28731041 ; 28838212 }, although only one is optimized for orbital
tumours { 32529350 }, and none for other head and neck sites. Recurrences are seen (<10%) often
with positive margins { 29282671 }, and metastases are rare. Secondary TERT promoter mutations
and TP53 deletions or mutations { 22026427 ; 26022454 ; 29985536 } may be associated with
more aggressive disease. Lungs, bones, and liver are the most frequent sites for metastasis.

image.
