Low grade myofibroblastic sarcoma
Definition
Low-grade myofibroblastic sarcoma is a rarely metastasizing mesenchymal neoplasm, often having
fibromatosis-like features, which tends to arise in the head and neck region.
ICD-O coding
8825/3 Low-grade myofibroblastic sarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B53.Y & XH2668 Other specified fibroblastic or myofibroblastic tumour, primary site & low-grade
myofibroblastic sarcoma.
Related terminology
Acceptable: Myofibrosarcoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The most common site is the tongue, followed by the larynx and gingiva { 33302922 }. Rarely these
tumours arise in the nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses. They are mostly deeply seated, however,
an origin in subcutaneous and submucosal sites does not exclude the diagnosis. Origin in the bone,
notably gnathic bones, can occur { 31950474 ; 33302922 ; 9777985 ; 15173943 ; 24132324 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a slowly growing painless mass. The average tumour size is 40 mm
{ 9777985 ; 33302922 }.
Epidemiology

843

Patients can be affected at any age with a median of 42 years { 33302922 ; 9777985 ; 15173943 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
Complex genetic aberrations have been identified, however the pathogenesis is unknown
{ 10362810 ; 31950474 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The tumours are poorly marginated, grey-white and firm with a fibrous cut-surface { 9777985 }.
Histopathology
The pattern is that of a cellular, fibromatosis-like or fibrosarcoma-like lesion composed of fascicles
or broad sheets of cells, with or without focal herringbone or storiform arrangement. Checkerboardlike infiltration of the adjacent skeletal muscle is a key diagnostic feature. The atypical
myofibroblastic cells have enlarged tapered hyperchromatic nuclei and a scant or moderate amount
of cytoplasm. Mitotic figures are variably present. The background can be collagenous or myxoid.
Transformation into high-grade sarcoma has been reported { 9777985 ; 24132324 ; 15173943 }.
There is a myofibroblastic immunophenotype showing variable expression of SMA, desmin,
calponin, and CD34. H-caldesmon is reported to be focally expressed in few cases. Expression of
beta-catenin does not rule out this tumour type { 9777985 ; 15173943 ; 31950474 ; 17711447 }.
At the ultrastructural level, the neoplastic cells are spindle-shaped, with oval often indented nuclei.
The cytoplasm contains numerous endoplasmic reticulum cisternae and subplasmalemmal
bundles of actin filaments, with or without focal densities, sometimes associated with
subplasmalemmal attachment plaques. Pinocytic vesicles and fibronexus junctions are present in
some cases { 15764578 ; 16612972 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant. { 31950474 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: diffusely infiltrative growth, often between skeletal muscle fibres; cellular fascicles of
spindle cells with pale eosinophilic cytoplasm; at least focally moderate nuclear atypia; variable
expression of SMA and/or desmin.
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Local recurrences are common, but metastatic spread to lung, soft tissue and bone, often after a
long interval, occurs rarely { 9777985 }.

#26414
Myofibroblastic sarcoma showing infiltration of the skeletal muscle

844

#26415
Myofibroblastic sarcoma
Infiltration into the voluntary muscle leads to a typical checkerboard pattern

#26416
Myofibroblastic sarcoma
The myofibroblastic cells are arranged in long fascicles bearing a resemblance to
desmoid fibromatosis.

Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour
Definition
Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour (IMT) is a distinctive, rarely metastasizing neoplasm
composed of myofibroblastic and fibroblastic spindle cells accompanied by an inflammatory
infiltrate of plasma cells, lymphocytes, and/or eosinophils.
ICD-O coding
8825/1 Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour
ICD-11 coding
2B53.Y & XH66Z0 Other specified fibroblastic or myofibroblastic tumour, primary site &
Myofibroblastic tumour NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: Inflammatory pseudotumour.
Subtype(s)
None.
845

Localization
Approximately 15-25% of IMTs develop in the head and neck { 7611533 ; 31690781 }, most
commonly the larynx (true vocal cords), sinonasal tract (maxillary sinus), and oral cavity
{ 26276782 ; 24756612 ; 22014665 ; 18022129 ; 8635024 ; 26276782 ; 24756612 }. Other sites
include trachea, pharynx, orbit, skull base, salivary glands, neck, and skin
{ 7611533 ; 21042957 ; 31690781 }.
Clinical features
Patients with laryngeal tumours present with hoarseness and dysphonia { 18022129 ; 8635024 },
whereas those with sinonasal and skull base tumours experience nasal obstruction, pain, visual
changes, and cranial nerve palsies { 24756612 }. Constitutional symptoms are uncommon
{ 22014665 ; 30410939 ; 31950474 ; 24756612 }.
Imaging shows a non-specific lobulated soft tissue mass with variable calcification, post-contrast
enhancement, and local invasion { 22974570 ; 24756612 ; 30410939 ; 27153788 }.
Epidemiology
A wide age range of patients is affected (0 to 81 years). Head and neck IMTs commonly affect
adults
{ 26276782 ; 24756612 ; 27443585 ; 30410939 ; 31690781 }.
No consistent
sex
predilection is observed { 21042957 ; 22014665 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
Up to 60% of tumours have an anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) gene (2p23) rearrangement with
a wide variety of fusion partners. Among head and neck IMTs, ALK fusion with TIMP3 has been
most commonly detected, and ROS1 fusion can be rarely seen { 31215130 ; 34001695 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The tumours are usually < 50 mm, firm and fleshy with a tan-white, whorled to myxoid cut surface,
occasionally showing haemorrhage and calcification.
Histopathology
Tumours are submucosal and may be ulcerated, ranging from circumscribed to infiltrative. Three
principal patterns may co-exist: a myxoid fasciitis or granulation tissue-like pattern, a hypercellular
spindle
cell
pattern,
and
a
hypocellular
fibrous
(fibromatosis-like)
pattern
{ 7611533 ; 17414097 ; 31950474 }. The myofibroblasts have eosinophilic cytoplasm and
vesicular, open chromatin with several small nucleoli. A ganglion-like appearance, intranuclear
cytoplasmic inclusions, or granular cell change may be seen. The spindle cells are associated with
variable inflammatory infiltrate and fibromyxoid stroma. Necrosis is infrequent. Mitotic activity is
variable but generally low, without atypical forms { 7611533 ; 24756612 ; 17414097 }.
By immunohistochemistry, most head and neck IMTs, especially in patients <40 years of age,
express
ALK,
correlating
with ALK gene
rearrangements
{ 26469330 ; 26647767 ; 27443585 }. ROS1 may show cytoplasmic reactivity in ROS1-rearranged
IMT { 26647767 ; 25612511 }. The spindle cells generally show a patchy, variably intense reactivity
for SMA, MSA, calponin, and desmin, with focal keratin reactivity in 30% of cases
{ 7611533 ; 31950474 }.
The pathologic differential diagnosis of IMT is broad and depends on the predominant histologic
pattern and site. In particular, distinction from spindle cell squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is
crucial; finding areas of squamous dysplasia or differentiated SCC is helpful { 33723761 }.
Laryngeal IMT should not be confused with vocal cord polyp { 26276782 ; 31215130 }. IgG4expressing plasma cell infiltration in IMT is usually much lower than in IgG4-related disease
{ 19718789 ; 26469330 ; 30451733 }.
Cytology
The cytology of IMT features bland spindle cells with oval nuclei and small nucleoli, with ganglionlike cells possible, in a background of lymphocytes and plasma cells { 20730898 ; 27087029 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular assays for ALK, ROS1 and other gene rearrangements may be used, particularly if
targeted therapy is being considered { 26647767 ; 30002191 ; 31690781 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Loose or compact fascicular proliferation of spindle cells with myofibroblastic differentiation, fibrous
or myxoid stroma, and associated chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate.
Desirable:

846

Demonstration of ALK or ROS1 expression by immunohistochemistry; rearrangement of ALK or
other implicated receptor tyrosine kinases by molecular methods (in selected cases).
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
The natural history of IMT is variable. Laryngeal IMTs generally follow a benign course
{ 18022129 ; 8635024 ; 26276782 }, while sinonasal and oral cavity IMTs are more aggressive
{ 21042957 ; 29643133 ; 24756612 }. Overall, approximately 25% of IMTs recur, related to
anatomic site and resectability { 24756612 ; 27443585 ; 30410939 ; 7611533 }. Distant
metastases are rare (<5%) and may be associated with ALK-negative IMT. Death from disease is
unusual (about 10%), but reliable prognostic indicators are lacking { 17414097 }. Targeted
therapies (i.e., ALK inhibitors) may improve outcome { 28787259 ; 29286567 ; 31690781 }.

L#24430
Laryngeal inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour
In the myxoid pattern, plump myofibroblasts are set in an oedematous background with
blood vessels and a chronic inflammatory infiltrate rich in lymphoyctes and plasma cells.
R#24435
Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour of the tongue
Lesional cells are arranged in fascicles in the hypercellular pattern. This example shows
limited chronic inflammation, and the tumour cells infiltrate skeletal muscle. This tumour
harboured a CARS::ALK fusion gene.

#24436
Laryngeal inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour
Collagenous stroma predominates in the hypocellular pattern, with a relatively limited
proliferation of elongated spindle cells and associated chronic inflammatory cells.

847

#24461
Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour of the oral mucosa
The spindle-shaped myofibroblasts have oval nuclei and vesicular chromatin with small,
prominent nucleoli; scattered intranuclear pseudoinclusions can be seen.

#24463
Hypopharyngeal inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour after crizotinib therapy
A large inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour of the hypopharynx is grossly lobulated, tan,
and fleshy with areas of necrosis (yellow, left) after neoadjuvant treatment with crizotinib.
This tumour harboured a ROS1 gene rearrangement, an unusual feature for head and
neck inflammatory myofibroblastic tumours.

#24714

The submucosal myofibroblastic spindle cell proliferation is diffusely, strongly positive for

The polypoid tumor demonstrates a submucosal fascicular proliferation of spindle cells

#23577

The myxoid pattern shows a storiform to fascicular proliferation of spindled to plump
myofiboblasts in an edematous, loose background with a background of lymphocytes and
plasma cells.

Vascular tumours

Haemangioma
Definition
Haemangioma is a benign vascular neoplasm.
ICD-O coding
9120/0 Haemangioma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E81.0Y & XH5AW4 Other specified neoplastic haemangioma & Haemangioma NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: Lobular capillary haemangioma; capillary haemangioma; cavernous haemangioma
Not recommended: Pyogenic granuloma, pregnancy epulis, histiocytoid haemangioma, epithelioid
haemangioma.
Subtype(s)
Capillary haemangioma; cavernous haemangioma; lobular capillary haemangioma.
Localization
The oral cavity (gingiva and lips) and sinonasal tract (anterior septum) mucosa are the most
commonly
affected
head
and
neck
sites
{ 1595597 ; 1642875 ; 6307911 ; 7435775 ; 11685962 ; 16955784 ; 17478135 ; 19203815 ; 2041
7014 ; 23184353 ; 30673134 },
with
rare
reports
in
other
organ
sites
{ 29106877 ; 11115277 ; 25439553 ; 22503446 ; 21766313 ; 22503446 ; 21766313 }.
Clinical features
Oral lesions present as a solitary, red haemorrhagic papule, while unilateral epistaxis and/or an
obstructive
painless
mass
is
seen
in
the
sinonasal
tract
{ 7435775 ; 10827405 ; 16539297 ; 16859060 ; 17478135 ; 19203815 ; 20417014 ; 23184353 ; 2
5207171 ; 28153757 ; 28389160 ; 29019747 ; 29977856 }. A compressible mass is noted in major
salivary gland tumours { 25439553 ; 22503446 ; 21766313 ; 11115277 }.
Imaging studies show an intensely enhancing tumour surrounded by a hypoattenuated peripheral
rim, often with remodelling { 1642875 ; 20007721 ; 23703148 }.
Epidemiology
Mucosal haemangiomas account for about 10% of head and neck haemangiomas and about 25%
of non-epithelial sinonasal tract neoplasms { 4362952 ; 7435775 ; 16955784 ; 23184353 }.
Haemangiomas occur in patients of all ages with a median of 40 years. Incidence peaks in boys
and adolescent males, and pregnant women. Haemangiomas of the head and neck account for
about 7% of all benign tumours in children. The sex distribution is equal in patients aged >40 years
{ 9425482 ; 4362952 ; 6307911 ; 1642875 ; 7435775 ; 23184353 }. Salivary gland haemangiomas
almost exclusively develop in neonates and children, with females affected more frequently than
males (~2:1) { 8419878 ; 11115277 ; 29106877 ; 25439553 ; 22503446 ; 21766313 }.
Etiology
Lobular capillary haemangioma is associated with mucosal injury, hormonal factors (pregnancy
and oral contraceptive use){ 21745433 ; 22713445 ; 16955784 ; 30628110 }, and selective BRAF
inhibitors { 25251629 ; 27116335 }.
Pathogenesis
The pathogenesis is unknown for head and neck tumours. Nevertheless, the hypothesis that
infantile (congenital) haemangiomas are proposed to originate from ectopic placenta cells recently
has been supported by their abundant expression of the C19MC cluster of miRNA genes
{ 22374805 ; 27660822 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The mean size is <15 mm but examples as large as 80 mm have been reported
{ 16955784 ; 23184353 }. Gross appearances range from a diffuse flat mass to a bulging, polypoid
nodule.
The
lesions
are
soft,
often
with
secondary
surface
changes
{ 9425482 ; 4362952 ; 6307911 ; 1642875 ; 7435775 }.
Histopathology
Haemangiomas of the sinonasal tract are primarily divided into capillary and cavernous types.
Other variants are reported rarely { 1642875 ; 7435775 ; 16955784 ; 1497117 ; 23184353 }.
Lobular capillary haemangioma is a circumscribed proliferation of capillaries with plump endothelial
cells surrounded by pericytes in a fibromyxoid stroma, arranged in one or more lobules which may
850

show high cellularity. Each lobule has a large central vein surrounded by small capillaries, with an
overlying collarette of epithelium (often ulcerated or atrophic). Mitoses are often identified and lack
atypia.
Cavernous haemangiomas are composed of multiple, large cystic, thin-walled, blood-filled spaces
lined by endothelial cells and separated by scant connective tissue stroma and may be considered
as vascular malformations.
The neoplastic cells show CD34, CD31, ERG, FLI-1, and factor VIII-RAg immunopositivity, with
variable expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors { 16955784 ; 1497117 ; 23184353 }.
HHV8 is negative.
Tumours should be distinguished from granulation tissue, reactive or malformative vascular polyps,
papillary endothelial hyperplasia, glomus tumour, angiofibroma, glomangiopericytoma, lymphoma
and angiosarcoma.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
For LCH
Essential: polypoid, lobular, circumscribed anastomosing network of capillaries with plump
endothelial cells and often a proliferation of pericytes in a fibromyxoid stroma arranged in one or
more lobules.
Desirable: In selected cases, vascular immunohistochemistry markers.
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Multiple recurrences (up to 45%) are more common in children, and may develop if the lesional
bed is not completely eradicated { 17478135 ; 23184353 }. Spontaneous regression is documented
in
pediatric
and
post-partum
patients
{ 22852124 ; 23075363 ; 23184353 ; 25181659 ; 27144115 ; 27271813 ; 29019747 ; 29215342 ;
30319828 ; 30673134 }.

tract hemangioma, showing a bright signal intensity.
R#23242

vessels and pericytic cells, associated with stromal fibrosis and haemorrhage.

#23245

#28866

A circumscribed vascular lesion composed of cavernous spaces located in the dermis
and subcutis of the neck.

853

Epithelioid haemangioma
Definition
Epithelioid haemangioma is a benign vascular neoplasm of well formed blood vessels with plump,
epithelioid endothelial cells, a variable eosinophil infiltrate. It is often associated
with FOS and FOSB fusions.
ICD-O coding
9125/0 Epithelioid haemangioma
ICD-11 coding
2E81.0Y & XH10T4 Other specified neoplastic haemangioma & Epithelioid haemangioma
Related terminology
Not recommended: angiolymphoid hyperplasia with eosinophilia; histiocytoid hemangioma.
Subtype(s)
Cellular epithelioid hemangioma; atypical epithelioid haemangioma.
Localization
Sites involved include the cutaneous and superficial soft tissues of the forehead, preauricular region
and scalp { 4008683 ; 22015125 ; 26135557 ; 26685720 ; 29266025 }.
Clinical features
Epithelioid hemangiomas form slowly growing indolent erythematous-violaceous nodules, often
pruritic or painful { 30277937 }, and are multifocal in about 15% of cases { 26685720 ; 29266025 }.
Epidemiology
These lesions develop over a broad age distribution with a peak in the 4th decade without a sex
predilection { 7364594 ; 23845664 ; 24676051 ; 26685720 ; 26135557 }.
Etiology
Vessel damage or trauma are suggested as etiologic factors for cutaneous lesions, while molecular
alterations are detected in soft tissue tumours { 26135557 }.
Pathogenesis
Chromosomal translocations leading to FOS or FOSB rearrangements drive tumourigenesis in a
significant proportion of tumours { 25043949 ; 26173738 ; 26135557 ; 29150442 }.
Macroscopic appearance
They form pale pink to red-brown nodules, sometimes coalescing into plaques.
Histopathology
There is a circumscribed, nodular to lobular proliferation of variably sized vessels lined by
distinctive, plump epithelioid endothelial cells. The vessels may show a gradient of maturation
towards the periphery, ranging from lobular to irregular distribution. The endothelial cells are
epithelioid with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, often with intracytoplasmic vacuoles (blister
cells). The hobnailed monolayer of endothelial cells creates a bumpy or tombstone-like
appearance. Lymphocytes and eosinophils are invariably present, but to a variable degree. Mitotic
figures are inconspicuous. Atypical tumours are infiltrative, solid, with an increased cellularity,
tumour necrosis and less spaces, but are rare in the head and neck { 25043949 }.
Immunohistochemical expression of FOSB may be seen (about 50% of cases) even when the gene
fusion is absent { 28009608 ; 29527734 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant, although reported { 12685201 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: lobular architecture with vasoformation; lining endothelial cells are epithelioid, with
eosinophilic cytoplasm; loose stroma, often with eosinophils.
Desirable: demonstration of FOS or FOSB rearrangements or expression in selected cases.
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Tumours may show loco-regional growth { 24676051 ; 26685720 }.

854

L#26913
Epithelioid hemangioma
An erythematous lower eyelid lesion has caused significant swelling.
R#26915
Epithelioid haemangioma of the pinna
Low power demonstrates a rich vascular proliferation with lymphocytic infiltration below
an intact epidermis.

#26916
Epithelioid haemangioma
The epidermis is intact. There is a proliferation of vessels with endothelial cells projecting
into the lumen. Note the eosinophils and lymphocytes.
#26918
Epithelioid haemangioma
Numerous intracytoplasmic vacuoles (blister cells) are noted in the endothelial cells.
There is a background scattering of lymphocytes and eosinophils.

855

Lymphangioma
Definition
Lymphangioma is a benign vascular lesion composed of a localized collection of dilated lymphatic
channels.
ICD-O coding
9170/0
Lymphangioma,
NOS
9173/0 Cystic lymphangioma
ICD-11 coding
LA90.12 & XH9MR8 Lymphatic malformations of certain specified sites & Lymphangioma
Related terminology
Acceptable: cystic hygroma, vascular malformation; lymphatic malformation, lymphangiomatous
polyps.
Subtype(s)
Microcystic, macrocystic (depends on cyst diameter, cut-off 10 mm) and mixed { 30819600 }
Localization
While the skin and subcutaneous tissue of the head and neck region is the most common
localization for lymphangiomas, they are only occasionally reported in the oral cavity and salivary
glands. Intraoral lymphangiomas arise on the dorsum of the tongue, followed by palate, buccal
mucosa, gingival and lips { 31823215 }.
Clinical features
Clinical behaviour varies with erratic growth, progression, or even spontaneous regression during
the first two decades of life. Symptoms are related to size and perturbation of surrounding
structures { 22420724 ; 30819600 }.
Epidemiology
Lymphangiomas are relatively uncommon and usually diagnosed in infancy and early childhood.
However, there is a broad age range { 30677207 ; 22658544 ; 30819600 }.
Etiology
These lesions are caused by developmental errors in the vascular system due to mosaic somatic
mutations arising during embryogenesis, or more rarely germ-line mutations { 31950476 }.
Pathogenesis
Activating
mutations
result
in
hyperactive
PI3K/AKT
signaling,
mainly
in PIK3CA { 22658544 ; 21793738 ; 31950476 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions are sponge-like and not always delineated. They can be pedunculated or, more rarely,
sessile.
Histopathology
Variably sized, irregular, thin-walled fluid-filled spaces are lined by lymphatic endothelium and
surrounded by a stroma of fibrous, smooth muscle and adipose tissue along with lymphocytes
{ 22420724 }. Red blood cells and organized thrombi may be seen in the lumina post trauma.
Communication with the venous system is sometimes seen. The endothelial cells show expression
of CD31 and rarely CD34 { 11048808 }. They also express D2-40 (against podoplanin), Prox1,
VEGFR-3 and Lyve1 { 19098468 ; 24283866 ; 22420724 }. The walls of the lymphatic vessels
and/or the surrounding layer show expression of smooth muscle actin { 11048808 ; 21605441 }.
Cytology
Aspiration produces lymphocyte rich yellow fluid { 30819600 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Mutational analysis for PIK3CA in selected cases may be helpful.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Variably sized mainly thin-walled lymphatic channels often with aggregates of
lymphocytes.
Desirable: Expression of vascular markers and D2-40.
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Lymphangiomas are benign, but because of their propensity for involvement of deeper tissue
planes, recurrence after surgical resection may occur { 22420724 }.

856

L#27201

This papillary mucosal lesion shows accumulation of dilated lmphatic vessels and focally
small clusters of lymphocytesR
R#27202
Lymphangioma
D2-40 highlights the lymphatic nature of the vessels

#28867
Lympangioma
This lesion located next to the parotid gland of a newborn consists of cystically dilated
lymphatic channels.

Epithelioid haemangioendothelioma
Definition
Epithelioid haemangioendothelioma (EHE) is a malignant fusion-gene associated neoplasm
composed of epithelioid endothelial cells in a myxohyaline stroma.
ICD-O coding
9133/3 Epithelioid haemangioendothelioma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B5Y & XH9GF8 Other specified malignant mesenchymal neoplasms & Epithelioid
haemangioendothelioma
Related terminology
Not recommended: malignant epithelioid haemangioendothelioma
Subtype(s)
857

EHE with WWTR1::CAMTA1 fusion
EHE with YAP1::TFE3 fusion
Localization
These are rare tumours of the head and neck region arising typically in soft tissue of the neck, oral
cavity, salivary glands or bone { 24986479 ; 26298095 ; 16301153 ; 21584898 ; 10846129 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a slowly growing mass, sometimes painful due to vascular occlusion. Other
symptoms are site- and size-dependent. There is a propensity for lymph node metastases
{ 31950476 ; 31463731 ; 26298095 ; 16301153 }.
Epidemiology
Age ranges from childhood to the elderly, with young to middle aged adults being mainly affected
{ 22420724 ; 24986479 ; 31950476 ; 31463731 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
As a specific driver mutation, the WWTR1::CAMTA1 fusion gene due to a chromosomal
translocation t(1;3)(p36;q25) occurs in most EHE {11342784, 21584898, 21885404, 25961935}.
Rare cases display a YAP1::TFE3 fusion due to a t(X;11)(p11;q13) translocation
{ 23737213 ; 24986479 }.
Macroscopic appearance
These tumours form a (multi)nodular mass typically showing a pale, solid cut surface, sometimes
with haemorrhage { 24986479 ; 26298095 ; 31950476 }.
Histopathology
Bland looking epithelioid-, spindle- and histiocytoid-appearing endothelial cells are arranged in
short cords and strands in a myxohyaline stroma { 33776722 }. They show abundant hyaline
cytoplasm with subtle intracytoplasmic lumina. Striking nuclear atypia is seen in approximately 30%
of tumours. Mitotic activity is usually low. Multicellular vascular channels are sometimes present
and may be more prominent in YAP1::TFE3 associated lesions. Immunohistochemically, CD31
and ERG are consistently expressed. CAMTA1 and TFE3 are expressed corresponding to the
fusion genes { 7093931 ; 23737213 ; 21584898 ; 24986479 ; 31950476 ; 31463731 }.
Differential diagnoses are extensive and include epithelioid haemangioma, epithelioid
angiosarcoma, myoepithelial tumours, sclerosing epitheloid fibrosarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma,
carcinomas,
pseudomyogenic
haemangioendothelioma,
chordoma,
chondrosarcoma,
extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma { 7093931 ; 31950476 ; 31463731 ; 24986479 }.
Cytology
Cytological examination may show clusters of epithelioid and spindle cells with round to ovoid
nuclei, occasional vacuolated cytoplasm and scant mitotic figures { 32797671 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular identification of the WWTR1::CAMTA1 or YAP1::TFE3 fusion may be helpful in selected
cases.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: infiltrative lesion composed of strands, cords, and nests of bland looking epithelioid,
spindle and histiocytoid cells with typical glassy cytoplasm and subtle intracytoplasmic lumina
in myxohyaline background
Desirable: immunohistochemical positivity for CD31, ERG and CAMTA or TFE3
Staging
According to the UICC and American Joint Commitee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM staging systems.
Prognosis and prediction
Clinical behaviour and prognosis depend on the primary site. Most cases behave in an indolent
manner, but there is a risk of metastatic disease and a significant mortality rate (up to 45%)
{ 31950476 ; 31463731 }.

858

L#26421
Epitheloid hemangioendothelioma
The bland looking epithelioid cells are arranged in cords and nests showing typical glassy
cytoplasm. Note the subtle vascular lumina
R#26424
Epitheloid hemangioendothelioma
Cells are more spindly and loosly arranged in a fibrous matrix. Some cells show typical
lumina (so-called blister cells)

#26434
Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma
Most cases express CAMTA1 due to the WWTR1-CAMTA1 fusion

Kaposi sarcoma
Definition
Kaposi sarcoma (KS) is a locally aggressive vascular endothelial proliferation associated with
human herpesvirus 8 (HHV8).
ICD-O coding
9140/3 Kaposi sarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B57.Z & XH36A5 Kaposi sarcoma of unspecified primary site & Kaposi sarcoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: angiosarcoma multiplex; granuloma multiplex haemorrhagicum.
Subtype(s)
Classic indolent KS; endemic African KS; AIDS-associated KS; iatrogenic KS.
Localization

859

Within the head and neck, the oral cavity is the most common site for AIDS-associated KS, while
70% of patients with cutaneous KS also have oral lesions. Oral lesions are the initial site of infection
in 20% of cases, affecting the hard palate more commonly than gingiva or tongue
{ 21644423 ; 23008762 }. Involvement of other head and neck sites is rare
{ 10030245 ; 10618601 ; 16496108 ; 18605999 ; 27178524 ; 29508130 }.
Clinical features
KS forms multiple reddish to purple macules that progress to plaques or nodules, which may
ulcerate.
Epidemiology
Oral KS is the most common tumour identified in HIV/AIDS patients { 21644423 ; 23008762 },
highest in males who have sex with males (MSM) { 32862546 }. While KS can develop at any stage
of
HIV
infection,
it
is
more
common
in
advanced
immunosuppression
{ 11423260 ; 25709361 ; 26261737 }, with a 500-fold elevated risk of KS in HIV patients over the
general population { 32051269 }. AIDS-associated KS develops in the 4th or 5th decades and is
seen in primarily HIV-positive MSM, especially in North American and European countries, while
African and Chinese patients do not show a similar male predominance
{ 22807383 ; 21644423 ; 23008762 ; 19404695 ; 32862546 }.
Etiology
HHV8 is found in KS endothelial cells in all disease forms { 7997879 ; 7700310 }.
Pathogenesis
A serological correlation exists between HHV-8 infection and KS, with infection required but
insufficient for disease induction, which develops by a complex interplay of genetic, immunological,
and environmental factors { 11423257 ; 11536246 }.
Macroscopic appearance
KS forms purplish, haemorrhagic nodules, frequently coalesced. See STB5 for additional clinical
details.
Histopathology
Early lesions are characterised by a subtle proliferation of small, slit-like vascular spaces with
extravasated erythrocytes and a lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate. The plaque stage demonstrates an
increased vascular proliferation along with tumour cell spindling and prominent intra- and
extracellular hyaline globules. The advanced nodular stage comprises of unencapsulated
infiltrating fascicles of spindle cells with atypia and easily identified mitoses. Rare histologic
variants include solid, lymphangioma-like, telangiectatic, desmoplastic, lymphangiectatic,
ecchymotic, and anaplastic { 19839366 ; 23312917 ; 24034072 }. Nearly all cases show a strong
and
diffuse
nuclear
HHV8
reaction
{ 11474291 ; 14990970 ; 15023037 ; 18494611 ; 19874352 ; 22314185 ; 22372906 }.
The
differential
diagnosis
includes
lobular
capillary
hemangioma,
Kaposiform
haemangioendothelioma, angiosarcoma, and spindle cell carcinoma, all of which are HHV8
negative.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant, although reported { 22807383 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: proliferation of small slit-like vessels lined by mildly atypical cells with surrounding bland
spindle cells; extravasated erythrocytes and lymphoplasmacytic patchy infiltrates.
Desirable: hyaline globules; nuclear HHV8 expression by immunohistochemistry in selected cases.
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis is immune status dependent. Combined HAART and systemic chemotherapy improves
morbidity and mortality { 15280789 }. Intralesional therapies do not seem to affect disease course
{ 25345840 }.

L#23240

R#23715

neoplastic cells, although spindled cells are seen.

#23716

dilated lumen. Extravasated erythrocytes are noted.
#23717

endothelial cells are negative, a finding that helps to confirm the diagnosis.

#24792: Nodular Kaposi sarcoma of the palate

862

Angiosarcoma
Definition
Angiosarcoma is a malignant neoplasm that variably recapitulates the morphological and
immunohistochemical features of endothelial cells.
ICD-O coding
9120/3 Angiosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B56.Y & XH6264 Angiosarcoma, other specified primary site & Haemangiosarcoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: epithelioid haemangioendothelioma, malignant haemangioendothelioma,
malignant angioendothelioma, haemangiosarcoma and haemangioblastoma .
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
The oral cavity and nasal cavity/maxillary sinus are the most frequently affectedmucosal sites in
the
head
and
neck
region,
most
commonly
with
a
single
site
of
disease { 20614274 ; 3294314 ; 11520569 ; 28478092 }.
Clinical features
Presenting signs and symptoms are non-specific and usually of short duration (mean ∼10 months).
For sinonasal tumours, common symptoms are recurrent epistaxis and obstruction { 20614274 }
along with nasal discharge, enlarging mass, sinusitis, epiphora, pain, diplopia, and headaches
{ 17987727 }. Infiltration often leads to invasion of vital structures and bone erosion. On T2weighted MRI, tumours show contrast enhancement or a bright signal. Angiography reveals tumour
extent and feeder vessel(s) which may facilitate pre-surgical embolization { 20614274 }. Lymph
node and distant metastasis are uncommon at initial presentation.
Epidemiology
A male predominance of the tumours has been noted { 28723012 }. Tumours develop most
commonly in the elderly (oral cavity) and patients in the 5th decade (sinonasal tract).
Etiology
Rarely, radiation or environmental exposure to vinyl chloride and coal dust have been associated
with these tumours {3294314, 28723012}. Also, telangiectasia, trauma, and chronic oedema have
been considered as possible etiologic factors { 25698587 ; 12360047 }.
Pathogenesis
Angiosarcomas are a genetically heterogeneous group of tumours, often incorporating mutations
affecting the MAPK pathway (KRAS, HRAS, NRAS, BRAF, MAPK1, and NF1) { 26440310 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The tumours can be as large as 80 mm (mean 39 mm); paranasal sinus tumours are typically larger
than sinonasal cavity tumours (68 vs. 22 mm). The tumours are nodular to polypoid, soft, friable,
purple to red, and often ulcerated, with associated haemorrhage and necrosis
{ 20614274 ; 9630175 ; 12640107 }.
Histopathology
Angiosarcomas develop below an intact, uninvolved epithelium, with vasoformative neoplastic cells
expanding into soft tissue and bone, frequently accompanied by necrosis, haemorrhage and
secondary ulceration . The tortuous, irregular, freely anastomosing vascular channels can create
cleft-like spaces, rudimentary vessels, capillary-sized vessels, as well as cavernous spaces filled
with erythrocytes and lined by plump, enlarged, atypical, spindled or epithelioid endothelial cells
protruding into the vascular spaces in multiple layers or papillae. The nuclei are enlarged and
pleomorphic with heavy chromatin distribution, irregular nuclear contours, and prominent nucleoli.
Mitoses, including atypical forms, are easily identified {20614274, 17987727}. Extensive
hemorrhage and clot formation may obscure the tumour.
Tumours express CD31, in a typical cell membranous fashion, and ERG, while CD34, FLI1, D2-40
and actin are inconsistently expressed. Focal keratin expression (particularly in the epithelioid
pattern) can be misleading and suggest carcinoma { 21959309 ; 33964187 ; 12640107 }
Cutaneous angiosarcoma can look rather bland and thus may be mistaken for a benign vascular

863

tumour { 29766535 }; mucosal melanoma, spindled squamous cell carcinoma and myoepithelial
carcinoma must also be considered. Grading is not applied to sinonasal angiosarcoma.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
No identified genetic findings aid in diagnosis at present.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Anastomosing vascular spaces lined by atypical endothelial cells; infiltrative growth;
endothelial multilayering; marked nuclear atypia; brisk mitotic activity.
Desirable: expression of endothelial markers (preferably CD31 and ERG) by
immunohistochemistry.
Staging
Staging is not applied to these tumours.
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrences occur in ~40% of cases and the overall survival rate is ~60% { 25698587 }. Old age,
metastatic disease and poor performance status are predictors of poor outcome. Metastatic spread
occurs most commonly to the lung, liver, spleen, and bone (marrow). Specific etiologic factors may
be associated with shorter survival { 17987727 }.

#23163: A large, fleshy, hemorrhagic mass is present adjacent to the gingiva, histologically
diagnosed as an angiosarcoma.

#23247: An epithelioid proliferation of atypical cells comprises this angiosarcoma, with numerous
neolumen identified.
#23248: Freely anastomosing vessels are lined by highly pleomorphic endothelial cells
associated with extravasated erythrocytes.

864

L#23249: The neoplastic cells show profound pleomorphism, with cytoplasmic vacuoles and
eosinophilic globules, associated with neolumen formation and erythrocyte extravasation.
R#23251: The neoplastic cells show a strong and diffuse cytoplasmic reaction with CD34.

L#23252: The neoplastic cells show a strong and diffuse nuclear reaction with ERG.
R#23253: The neoplastic cells show a strong and diffuse cytoplasmic reaction with CD31.
Pericytic (perivascular) tumours

Myofibroma
Definition
Benign mesenchymal neoplasm of perivascular myoid cells that forms a spectrum with
myopericytoma.
ICD-O coding
8824/0 Myopericytoma
ICD-11 coding
2E84.Y & XH0953 Benign fibrogenic or myofibrogenic tumour of other specified sites & Myofibroma
Related terminology
Not recommended: congenital generalized fibromatosis
Subtype(s)
Infantile myofibroma; infantile myofibromatosis; solitary myofibroma; adult myofibroma.
Localization
Head and neck, upper extremities, trunk and rarely deep soft tissue and bones are the main sites
affected by myofibromas { 22486319 }. Wide-spread visceral involvement characterizes the
generalized form.
865

Clinical features
Myofibroma presents as a small well circumscribed but not encapsulated superficial dermal or
subcutaneous violaceous nodule <10 mm to several centimeters in size { 22486319 }. Three
clinicopathological settings are recognized: solitary, multicentric and generalized. Multicentric
disease more commonly affects females.
Epidemiology
Myofibroma may be present at birth, appear in the first 2 years of life, or arise in adults with a male
predominance.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Somatic and germline PDGFRB mutations are responsible for solitary and familial
myofibroma/tosis, respectively { 28505006 ; 27776010 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Myofibroma forms a well circumscribed but not encapsulated dermal or subcutaneous violaceous
nodule (one to several centimeters) with firm, fibrous consistency.
Histopathology
Biphasic growth of alternating bundles of primitive basophilic small rounded to plump spindled cells
and eosinophilic myoid cells associated with prominent thin-walled pericytoma-like vessels with
frequent myointimal nodular proliferations (vascular balls) { 16330949 ; 9591720 ; 8067513 }. The
stroma is characteristically myxohyaline and may show ischaemic-type necrosis and calcification.
Mitotic activity is low but highly mitotically active lesions may be encountered. Atypical mitoses are
lacking. The neoplastic cells express alpha smooth muscle actin, but h-caldesmon is absent or only
focally positive. Desmin is negative.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Identification of somatic or germline PDGFRB mutations may be helpful in selected cases. Infantile
myofibromatosis lacks ETV6::NTRK3 and LMNA::NTRK1 gene fusions found in infantile
fibrosarcoma { 10895816 ; 26863915 }. SRF::RELA fusions are detected in subset of cellular
myofibroma but not in conventional myofibroma { 28248815 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Alternating bundles of primitive dark-stained rounded to plump cells and mature
eosinophilic myoid cells associated with prominent thin-walled pericytoma-like vessels, myointimal
nodular proliferations (vascular balls) within a myxohyaline stroma.
Desirable
(in
selected
cases): PDGFRB mutations
in
myopericytoma
and
myofibroma; SRF::RELA gene fusions in cellular/atypical myofibroma.
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Myofibroma does not recur after excision. The generalized form is frequently fatal due to extensive
visceral involvement and limited surgical possibilities. Atypical histology (increased cellularity, high
mitotic activity, absent myoid nodules, infiltrating borders, intravascular growth, perineural invasion,
entrapment of nerves) does not seem to adversely influence outcome in the absence of atypical
mitoses and cytological atypia { 24921644 }.

#27436: Myxoid myointimal
proliferation (vascular ball) closely
juxtaposed to sclerotic areas.

#27435: Well-define tumour with prominent micronodular myointimal proliferations (vascular balls)
surrounded by cellular areas with primitive ovoid to spindled cells and gaping vessels.

R#27438: Cellular myofibroma with monomorphic primitive ovoid to spindled cells interrupted by
pericytoma-like ectatic thin-walled vessels.

L#27439: Alternating fascicles of dark stained primitive-looking and eosinophilic (myoid) cells.
R#27440: Ischemic-type necrosis may be present.

L#27441: Alternating eosinophilic myoid and dark-stained primitive cellular fascicles.
R#27444: Diffuse cellular areas lacking zonation should not be mistaken for sarcoma.

L#27447: CT scan shows contrast-enhancing well-defined mass in the median neck closely
associated with the thyroid gland.
R#24602: Sporadic infantile solitary myofibroma from a 12-year-old girl (Arcus palatoglossus)
showing fascicular growth of spindle cells with alternating myoid and darker staining cells. This
tumor had a PDGFRB mutation.

Smooth muscle tumours

Leiomyoma
Definition
Leiomyoma is a benign tumour with smooth muscle differentiation. Angioleiomyoma shows, in
addition, vascular differentiation.
ICD-O coding
8890/0 Leiomyoma NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E86.1 Leiomyoma of other or unspecified sites
Related terminology
Unacceptable: myoma.
Subtype(s)
Angioleiomyoma.
Localization
The most common sites for leiomyoma in the head and neck region are the oral cavity (lips, tongue,
buccal mucosa, palate), sinonasal tract, and mandible { 24250094 ; 26094018 ; 26682845 }. They
are extraordinarily rare in the sinonasal tract predominantly with involvement of the nasal cavity
and more rarely of the paranasal sinuses. In the sinonasal tract they mostly present as
angioleiomyoma { 26047608 ; 12653572 ; 24384850 }.
Clinical features
At most sites tumours cause soft tissue swellings, while mucosal lesions may cause nasal or airway
obstruction, bleeding and rarely pain { 26047608 ; 12653572 ; 24384850 }.
Epidemiology
Only about 1% of all leiomyomas are located in the head and neck region { 24250094 }. Of these,
about 12 - 15% present as angioleiomyomas { 26047608 ; 1122481 ; 12653572 ; 24250094 }.
Adults are most often affected with an equal sex distribution.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis

869

There is no evidence that leiomyomas of the head and neck region share the driver mutations
(MED12, HMGA2 rearrangements)
seen
in
genital
and retroperitoneal leiomyomas { 22223266 ; 28591699 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Leiomyomas are polypoid, nodular, and usually sharply demarcated, with a white to tan trabecular
cut surface covered by a capsule or normal mucosa { 30885006 }.
Histopathology
Leiomyomas are subepithelial with infrequent mucosal ulceration. Bland, spindled tumour cells are
arranged in intersecting fascicles. Cells are oval to elongate with cigar-shaped nuclei, without
atypia and with eosinophillic fibrillary cytoplasm. Mitoses are absent to sparse.
Angioleiomyoma, the most common smooth muscle tumour in the head and neck region, shows a
prominent vasculature surrounded by smooth muscle cells intimately associated with vessels
{ 24384850 }. Calcification, ossification, fatty metaplasia, or myxohyaline degeneration may be
seen, probably indicating longstanding lesions. A fatty component is more common in males and
older patients. While having a smooth muscle component, angioleiomyoma is formally classified
as a pericytic (perivascular) tumour (see STB5).
Immunohistochemically, lesions express smooth muscle markers (alpha-SMA, MSA, desmin, and
caldesmon)
but
are
negative
for HMB45,
SOX10,
and
S100
{ 26047608 ; 12653572 ; 24111893 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
No known markers of genetic relevance.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Tumour with smooth muscle differentiation with very rare or absent mitoses and
lacking nuclear atypia and necrosis.
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Leiomyomas have an excellent prognosis.

L#23501: Computed tomography demonstrating a well circumscribed mass in the anterior nasal
cavity (white arrow).
R#23502: Vascular channels are surrounded by a spindle cell proliferation in this nasal cavity
angioleiomyoma.

870

L#23503: An angioleiomyoma shows the spindled smooth muscle proliferation intimately
associated with vessel walls.
R#23504: Angioleiomyoma, A collapsed vessel lumen is surrounded by a thickened wall in which
the smooth muscle cells continue into the stroma as part of an angioleiomyoma (also known as a
vascular leiomyoma). Inflammatory cells are noted.

L#23505: Leiomyoma, Interlacing fascicles of smooth muscle cells are seen in this leiomyoma.
The nuclei are bland.
R#23506: Leiomyoma, Fascicles of spindled leiomyoma cells show cytoplasmic vacuoles, some
adjacent to bland nuclei.

L#23507: Leiomyoma, The neoplastic cells of leiomyoma show a strong and diffuse cytoplasmic
reaction with smooth muscle actin.
R#23508: Leiomyoma. The neoplastic cells of leiomyoma show a strong and diffuse cytoplasmic
reaction with desmin.

871

#23510
Leiomyoma
A whole slide image of a vascular (angio) leiomyoma of the sinonasal tract, focally
showing fatty metaplasia.

EBV-associated smooth muscle tumour
Definition
EBV-associated smooth muscle tumour is a tumour with smooth muscle differentiation that is
associated with EBV infection in the setting of immunosuppression.
ICD-O coding
8897/1 Smooth muscle tumour of uncertain malignant potential
ICD-11 coding
2F7Y&XH00B4 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of other specified site & Smooth muscle tumour
NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: Smooth muscle tumour in immunocompromised patient
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
EBV-associated smooth muscle tumour in the head and neck region is very rare, occurring in the
larynx,
oropharynx
and
eye/orbit
more
frequently
than
the nasal
cavity
{ 30120720 ; 20690046 ; 33891274 }. Some patients show multifocal disease throughout the body,
thought to result from independent monoclonal events related to EBV infection rather than from
metastasis { 16330945 }.
Clinical features
Patients usually have had kidney transplantation, HIV infection, malnutrition, or other
disease/events causing immunodeficiency prior the emergence of the tumour. Dysphonia,
hoarseness, dyspnea, cough and pain may result from airway narrowing.
Epidemiology
The age range is wide (8 to 59 years), with no sex predilection, and tends to occur in patients with
Asian ethnicity { 33891274 }. Patients with primary (inherited) immunodeficiency tend to be children
{ 30120720 ; 29535735 }.
Etiology
The etiology involves EBV infection in the setting of T-lymphocyte immunosuppression.
Pathogenesis

872

Mechanisms of the role of EBV infection in tumourigenesis are not fully understood. The EBV type
III latency pattern is suggested to be involved, with activation of the mTOR/AKT pathway
{ 19706821 ; 23682851 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Laryngeal mucosal tumours are polypoid.
Histopathology
The tumour comprises intersecting fascicles of spindle cells with ample eosinophilic cytoplasm and
blunt ended nuclei. Some tumours also contain epithelioid cells with open nuclear chromatin in boxshaped to oval nuclei { 33891274 }. Nuclear atypia or increased mitotic activity can be seen;
necrosis is uncommon except in HIV-positive patients. By immunohistochemistry, tumour cells are
positive for SMA, h-caldesmon, and smooth muscle myosin heavy chain. Strong nuclear positivity
of EBV-encoded RNA (EBER) by in situ hybridization allows distinction from other SMA-expressing
tumours { 25027306 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Demonstration of EBV infection usually by EBER in situ hybridization.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: clinical history of immunosuppression; tumour with smooth muscle differentiation;
evidence of EBV infection
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
EBV-associated smooth muscle tumour shows indolent behaviour and can be removed
conservatively without radical resection. Prognosis is mainly dependent on the immune condition
of the patient. Some posttransplant cases respond to reduced immunosuppression.

L#23689

R#23690

neoplasm of the larynx.

to cytoplasmic ratio and open-vesicular nuclear chromatin.

hybridization.

874

Smooth muscle tumour of uncertain malignant potential
Definition
Smooth muscle tumours of uncertain malignant potential (STUMPs) show morphological features
that exceed criteria for leiomyoma or its subtypes, yet are insufficient for a diagnosis of
leiomyosarcoma, and behave in a malignant fashion in only a minority of cases.
ICD-O coding
8897/1 Smooth muscle tumour of uncertain malignant potential
ICD-11 coding
2F7Y & XH00B4 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of other specified site & Smooth muscle tumour
NOS
Related terminology
None.
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
Rare examples of smooth muscle tumour of uncertain/undetermined malignant potential (STUMP)
have been reported in the sinonasal tract { 12653572 ; 27879303 }, but leiomyoma and
leiomyosarcoma are much more common { 2242259 ; 29270859 }.
Clinical features
Sinonasal tumours present with nasal obstruction and facial pain.
Epidemiology
Limited to single case reports, patients with STUMP range from 20 – 44 years, with males and
females affected { 12653572 ; 27879303 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
Unknown for Head and Neck tumour sites specifically.
Macroscopic appearance
STUMP typically form an ulcerated submucosal mass with a fleshy pale-tan appearance.
Histopathology
These tumours form interlacing fascicles and bundles of smooth muscle cells with blunt-ended
nuclei and ample eosinophilic cytoplasm, show slightly increased cellularity, mild to
moderate pleomorphism and a mitotic count of ≤ 4 per 2 mm2 { 12653572 ; 27879303 }. Tumour
cells blend with the musculature of the tunica media of vessels { 12653572 }. Erosion or invasion
of surrounding bone or cartilage may be present { 12653572 }. Isolated pleomorphism is generally
seen in symplastic leiomyoma { 12401830 }.
Immunocytochemistry is positive for SMA, MSA, and desmin, with no nuclear expression of ßcatenin, HMB45, SOX10, S100 protein or keratins { 27137987 }.
The differential diagnosis includes leiomyoma, leiomyosarcoma(LMS), biphenotypic sinonasal
sarcoma, glomangiopericytoma, peripheral nerve sheath tumour, PEComa and meningioma. The
modest cellularity, moderate pleomorphism and lower mitotic count is usually sufficient to make the
distinction from sarcoma.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Smooth muscle tumour with mild to moderate pleomorphism, increased cellularity,
mitotic count of ≤ 4 per 2 mm2; no tumour necrosis.
Desirable: desmin, SMA, and/or caldesmon (in selected cases).
Staging
Not performed.
Prognosis and prediction
STUMP, in contrast to leiomyoma, are more likely to recur locally { 12653572 ; 27879303 }.

875

L#28541
Smooth muscle tumour of uncertain malignant potential. Submucosal cellular neoplasm
composed of spindle-shaped cells with deeply eosinophilic cytoplasm characterized by
interlacing and/or intersecting fascicles or bundles some at acute angles.
R#28542
Smooth muscle tumor of uncertain malignant potential, Areas of the tumor showing
features of benign smooth muscle tumor (i.e., leiomyoma) including well-organized
fascicles composed of spindle-shaped cells with uniform appearing nuclei some with
blunt-ends (cigar-shaped nuclei) and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm.

#28543
Smooth muscle tumour of uncertain malignant potential. Less organized appearing
fascicles composed of mild to moderate to focally marked nuclear pleomorphism
with hyperchromatic nuclei, and eosinophilic cytoplasm are seen. Despite these atypical
nuclear features mitotic figures and tumour necrosis are not present. The extent of
cellularity and degree of nuclear pleomorphism were not focal or limited to focal areas of
the neoplasm but included extensive areas of the neoplasm and, therefore, were beyond
those that might be ascribed to findings seen in symplastic leiomyomas.
R#28544
Smooth muscle tumour of uncertain malignant potential. High magnification shows an
admixture of cells including more differentiated cells characterized by uniform nuclei with
blunt ends (cigar-shaped nuclei) and evenly dispersed nuclear chromatin admixed with
less differentiated cells characterized by nuclear hyperchromasia and moderate to
marked nuclear pleomorphism. All cells have brightly eosinophilic appearing cytoplasm.
Mitoses and tumour necrosis were not identified.

Leiomyosarcoma
Definition
Leiomyosarcoma (LMS) is a malignant neoplasm composed of cells showing smooth muscle
differentiation.
ICD-O coding
8890/3 Leiomyosarcoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B58.Y & XH7ED4 Leiomyosarcoma, other specified primary site & Leiomyosarcoma NOS
2B58.Z & XH7ED4 Leiomyosarcoma, unspecified primary site & Leiomyosarcoma NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
Subcutaneous and deep neck soft tissues are involved more frequently than mucosal locations; of
the latter sinonasal tract and oral cavity are the most commonly affected { 23434173 ; 12653572 }.
Organ specific cases are rare { 21729441 ; 23434173 ; 24220466 ; 29395283 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms are those of a mass lesion { 23434173 ; 29270859 }. Metastatic LMS to the head and
neck are rare { 23434173 ; 2242259 ; 27015437 }.
Epidemiology
Primary head and neck LMS are very rare, representing 0.01% of all tumours
{ 2242259 ; 12653572 ; 23434173 ; 29270859 ; 29588301 }. There is an equal sex distribution
over a wide age range (20 to 86 years), most commonly in the 5th to 6th decades
{ 2242259 ; 12653572 ; 23434173 ; 29270859 }.
Etiology
Radiation exposure, immunosuppression and tumour predisposition syndromes (Li-Fraumeni,
retinoblastoma)
are
predisposing
factors
{ 20803265 ; 21837677 ; 23674091 ; 29220301 ; 29270859 }. Sinonasal LMS may arise after
radiotherapy { 29395283 }.
Pathogenesis

878

Specific evaluation of head and neck LMS is limited { 8431912 }, but LMS show extensive genomic
instability,
with
near-universal
inactivation
of TP53 and RB1 { 21125665 ; 26692951 ; 29100075 ; 29321523 }. Head and neck LMS do not
show the driver mutations of MED12 seen in genital leiomyosarcoma { 22768200 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are polypoid, firm to fleshy, usually poorly defined and unencapsulated. Cut sections show
a whorled and whitish or tan-grey appearance with areas of haemorrhage, cystic degeneration,
and necrosis { 23434173 ; 29270859 }.
Histopathology
LMS are composed of spindle cells arranged in compact interlacing fascicles. Tumour nuclei are
oval to elongated (cigar-shaped) and frequently blunt-ended, with a perinuclear vacuole
compressing the nucleus. Atypia varies, with enlarged nuclei and hyperchromasia, and sometimes
obvious nucleoli. Cytoplasm is eosinophilic or clear. An epithelioid cytomorphology is rarely seen.
Osteoclastic and pleomorphic giant cells may occur. Tumours may have a myxoid background and
scattered inflammatory cells, and rarely show dystrophic or psammomatous calcification. Mitotic
activity is usually brisk (>4 MF/2 mm2) and comedo necrosis is common
{ 12653572 ; 2242259 ; 29270859 }. High grade transformation may be present. Neoplastic cells
show strong reactivity with SMA, desmin and h-caldesmon { 29270859 }. RB1 is commonly lost,
and tumours do not express EBER { 2242259 ; 23434173 ; 29270859 }. The differential diagnosis
is primarily with leiomyoma, smooth muscle tumours of uncertain malignant potential, biphenotypic
sinonasal sarcoma, perivascular epithelioid cell tumour, spindle cell squamous cell carcinoma,
malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, and metastatic tumours.
Cytology
Plump spindled cells with cigar-shaped nuclei, fibrillar granular cytoplasm with perinuclear
vacuoles,
atypia
and
possibly
mitoses
may
be
seen
in
FNA
smears
{ 21325797 ; 29395283 ; 31043260 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: fascicles of eosinophilic spindled cells with blunt-ended nuclei with variable
pleomorphism and mitoses.
Desirable: immunolabelling for SMA, desmin and/or caldesmon (in selected cases).
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification for
sarcoma.
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis is often poor (20% 5-year overall survival) due to late presentation and difficulties
achieving tumour-free margins { 16369174 ; 29270859 }. High grade tumours have a worse
survival than low grade tumours (53% vs. 88%) { 22777866 }. Distant metastasis, often to the
lungs, occurs in up to 50%, often after a prolonged interval { 16369174 ; 23434173 ; 29270859 }.

Skeletal muscle tumours

Rhabdomyoma
Definition
Rhabdomyoma is a benign soft tissue neoplasm showing skeletal muscle differentiation.
ICD-O coding
8900/0 Rhabdomyoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E86.2
&
XH8WG9
Rhabdomyoma
&
Rhabdomyoma
NOS
2E86.2
&
XH4729
Rhabdomyoma
&
Fetal
rhabdomyoma
2E86.2 & XH4BG5 Rhabdomyoma & Adult rhabdomyoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: extracardiac rhabdomyoma; rhabdomyomatous hamartoma
Subtype(s)
Adult type (ARM), fetal type (FRM)
Localization
ARM and FRM arise predominantly in the head and neck with parapharyngeal space, salivary
glands, larynx, mouth and soft tissue of the neck most commonly affected { 31950473 }.
Clinical features
Patients usually present with a slowly growing mass, but rapid growth does not exclude the
diagnosis { 31950473 }. ARM may be multifocal { 2974590 ; 32773112 ; 8505039 }. Symptoms are
site dependent and include hoarseness, airway obstruction and dysphagia { 31950473 }.
Epidemiology
The age range of ARM patients is broad with a median age of 60 years; the male-female ratio is
3:1. The median age of FRM is 4.5 years, ranging from congenital to 60 years; the male-female
ratio is 5:3 { 31950473 ; 8319954 ; 8505039 }.
Etiology
Some cases are syndrome-related (FRM: nevoid basal cell carcinoma; ARM: Birt-Hogg-Dubé
syndrome) { 16294371 ; 29744825 }.
Pathogenesis
Mutations in the sonic hedgehog pathway genes are often involved { 23780909 ; 16294371 }.
The FLCN gene can be altered in some cases { 29744825 }.
Macroscopic appearance
ARMs are circumscribed, lobulated, soft and tan to red-brown with a fleshy cut surface. FRMs are
circumscribed, soft, grey-white with a glistening cut surface. The median tumour size for both is 30
mm { 8505039 ; 8319954 }.
Histopathology
ARM is composed of large polygonal cells having abundant eosinophilic, granular or vacuolated
cytoplasm (spider cells) with focal cross striation or crystalline (rod-like or "jack-straw-like")
inclusions. The small, round nuclei are centrally or peripherally located with vesicular chromatin
and distinct nucleoli { 8505039 ; 31950473 }.
FRM shows immature rhabdomyoblasts including primitive round to spindle cells and myotubelike rhabdomyoblasts with eosinophilic cytoplasm and cross-striations set in a myxoid matrix
{ 8319954 }.
FRM can be confused with embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma. Features favouring FRM are superficial
location, circumscription without infiltrative margins, lack of cellular atypia, absence of a cambium
layer, and paucity of mitotic figures { 19421668 ; 31950473 ; 8319954 }.
Cytology
ARM shows large polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic or vacuolated cytoplasm and
peripherally located nuclei { 29131558 ; 21053579 }. In FRM bland spindle cells and
rhabdomyoblasts with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm are seen { 19421668 }
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
ARM: large polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round centrally or peripherally located
nuclei with nucleoli.
883

FRM: rhabdomyoblasts in different stages without atypia and few mitotic figures.
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Recommended treatment is complete excision. Recurrence is often attributed to incomplete
removal. There is no metastatic potential { 19421668 ; 31950473 ; 8505039 }.

L#26876
