Rhabdomyosarcoma family
Definition
Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS) is a family of malignant mesenchymal tumours with skeletal muscle
differentiation and includes embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma (ERMS); alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
(ARMS); pleomorphic rhabdomyosarcoma; spindle cell/sclerosing rhabdomyosarcoma
(Sp/ScRMS).
ICD-O coding
8900/3
Rhabdomyosarcoma,
NOS
8910/3
Embryonal
rhabdomyosarcoma
8912/3
Spindle
cell
rhabdomyosarcoma
8920/3
Alveolar
rhabdomyosarcoma
8901/3 Pleomorphic rhabdomyosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B55.0 & XH0GA1 Rhabdomyosarcoma of the oral cavity or pharynx & Rhabdomyosarcoma, NOS
2B55.0 & XH83G1 Rhabdomyosarcoma of the oral cavity or pharynx & Embryonal
rhabdomyosarcoma,
NOS
2B55.0 & XH7099 Rhabdomyosarcoma of the oral cavity or pharynx & Alveolar
rhabdomyosarcoma
2B55.0 & XH7NM2 Rhabdomyosarcoma of the oral cavity or pharynx & Spindle cell
rhabdomyosarcoma
2B55.0 & XH5SX9 Rhabdomyosarcoma of the oral cavity or pharynx & Pleomorphic
rhabdomyosarcoma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: myosarcoma; malignant rhabdomyoma
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
Within the head and neck (excluding parameningeal disease), RMS affects the orbit/eyelid,
nasopharynx, ear and temporal bone, and sinonasal tract in order of frequency, recognizing
differences
in
histologic
type
and
age
at
presentation
{ 1247965 ; 17346806 ; 23743294 ; 28875443 ; 30055718 }.
Clinical features
Diagnosis is often delayed as symptoms are non-specific. Metastatic disease is frequently present
at diagnosis (25-30%) { 28521080 }.
Epidemiology
Approximately 35-40% of all RMS are located in the head and neck { 20718921 }, and RMS is the
most common sinonasal sarcoma { 7838548 ; 12110339 ; 23743294 ; 25186315 ; 28875443 }.
See STB5 for additional details.
Etiology
ERMS (but not other subtypes) is associated with radiation and several tumour predisposition
syndromes { 20186103 ; 27345568 ; 27617148 ; 31697451 ; 33372952 }.
Pathogenesis
Most
ARMS
harbour
a PAX3
or
PAX7::FOXO1 fusion { 8098985 ; 8187070 ; 8275086 }. PAX3 and PAX7 are transcription factors
with essential roles in myogenesis { 26424495 }, with fusion proteins activating many downstream
target genes, including MET, ALK, MYCN, and MYOD1 { 8643596 ; 20663909 ; 30617281 }. Less
common
fusions
include PAX3 with FOXO4, NCOA1,
and FGFR1 { 12183429 ; 21666686 ; 24436047 }. Sp/ScRMS separate into three genetic
groups: NCOA2 and/or VGLL2 rearrangement; MYOD1 mutation; no identifiable genetic alteration
{ 23463663 ; 30181563 }.
Skull/mandibular
intraosseous
SpRMS
shows EWSR1
or FUS::TFCP2 { 33382123 }. Up to 50% ERMS have RAS pathway mutations { 30617281 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours are usually polypoid, fleshy, gelatinous to firm, poorly circumscribed and infiltrative
masses { 10420698 ; 25267746 }. Necrosis and haemorrhage may be seen. Botryoid ERMS shows
multiple grape-like polypoid masses. Mastoid tumours tend to be smaller (< 30 mm) than extremity
counterparts.

886

Histopathology
ERMS:
ERMS shows primitive round to spindle cells, with scant cytoplasm and hyperchromatic nuclei, and
may have scattered rhabdomyoblasts. Botryoid ERMS typically shows linear aggregates of tumour
cells
close
to
the
mucosa
(cambium
layer),
with
a
cellularity
gradient
{ 666648 ; 9724344 ; 17443282 ; 18985676 ; 26646016 }.
ARMS:
ARMS typically shows fibrovascular septa separating cellular nests of small sized monomorphic
round cells with scant cytoplasm, which tend to coalesce in the centre with a dyscohesive periphery.
The solid variant
lacks
fibrovascular
septa,
with
sheets of
tumour
cells
{ 28521080 ; 24113309 ; 25186315 ; 16006807 ; 28875443 }.
Sp/ScRMS:
Sp/ScRMS consists of a fasciculated proliferation of spindle cells with elongated nuclei and pale
indistinct cytoplasm, with interspersed spindled or polygonal rhabdomyoblasts with abundant,
brightly eosinophilic cytoplasm { 31950473 }.
The skeletal muscle–specific proteins myogenin (MYF4) and MYOD1 are variably expressed both
within and between tumours, the proportion of positive cells reflecting the degree of differentiation
{ 16861966 }. Occasional expression of keratin, neuroendocrine markers, S-100 protein and ALK
may be observed { 2455782 ; 16006807 ; 18973919 ; 23307059 ; 28875443 ; 33382123 }. A panel
of markers is essential to avoid misdiagnosis.
The major differential diagnoses depend on pattern of tumour, while metastatic tumours must be
excluded { 18606509 }. Fetal rhabdomyoma, Ewing sarcoma, haematolymphoid tumours,
melanoma, carcinoma, and neuroendocrine tumours must be excluded.
Refer
to
section
7.5.2.4
for
details
of
Intraosseous
rhabdomyosarcoma
with EWSR1 or FUS::TFCP2 fusion.
Cytology
Smears are highly cellular with primitive uniform, round, spindled, and stellate cells with scattered
rhabdomyoblasts, sometimes in a tigroid background { 16680771 ; 17636492 ; 24599626 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
PAX3, PAX7, NCOA2, or VGLL2 rearrangement and MYOD1 mutation (in selected cases).
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
ERMS: primitive round to spindle cell morphology
ARMS: monomorphic primitive-appearing round cells dyscohesively arranged in a nested to solid
pattern
Sp/ScRMS: spindled morphology with/without sclerotic collagenous matrix
Desirable (in selected cases):
Rhabdomyoblasts; postive immunohistochemistry for Myogenin/MYOD1; detection of supporting
genetic alterations
Staging
Staging is via the Intergroup Rhabdomyosarcoma Study Group (IRSG) stratification, as head and
neck
sites
are
precluded
from
UICC
and
AJCC
staging
protocols
{ 9724344 ; 15059650 ; 18521303 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Risk stratification predictive of outcome has been addressed with surgicopathological staging and
fusion-based classification (IRSG grouping system) { 24326270 ; 30617281 ; 30373318 }. Overall,
RMS are considered systemic disease, with specific sites showing a relatively poor prognosis (5year survival rate of 40–45%) { 25186315 ; 22777866 ; 16025051 ; 28875443 }. Age and tumour
stage (most adult patients are stage IV at presentation) are the most important risk factors, with
children
and
females
having
a
better
outcome
than
adults
or
males
{ 31456361 ; 23743294 ; 23424037 }. Fusion-positive ARMS patients have a worse outcome than
fusion negative ARMS and ERMS, with a worse prognosis for PAX3- versus PAX7-FOXO1 fusions
{ 24326270 ; 30617281 ; 30373318 ; 23526739 ; 20351326 ; 22454413 ; 22447499 ; 28035744 ;
31456361 }.

887

#23547
Sinonasal rhabdomyosarcoma
Computed tomography demonstrates a large, destructive sinonasal tract mass (between
black and white arrows), present for several months.

L#23549
Sinonasal alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
A dyscohesive appearance shows neoplastic cells falling from the adjacent fibrous tissue
septa into the center of the alveolar structures. The neoplastic cells have a very high
nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio.R
R#23550
Sinonasal alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
The neoplastic cells are small with slightly cleared cytoplasm. They are present
surrounding and entombing the native minor mucoserous glands.

888

#23551
Sinonasal alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
On high power, the rhabdoid appearance of the cells can be seen, with opaque, eccentric
cytoplasm. Mitotic figures are easily identified.

#23552
Sinonasal alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
There is an intact respiratory epithelium. The stromal is "busy," filled with small cells
showing a high nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio and rhabdoid features. Sometimes this
appearance may mimic inflammatory sinonasal polyps.

889

L#23553
Ear embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma
A pleomorphic spindled cell population is noted adjacent to the cartilage. Strap cells are
present, a finding that helps to confirm a rhabdomyosarcoma diagnosis.
R#23554
Oral embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma
Short fascicles are composed of spindled cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm showing focal
cross striations. There is easily identified pleomorphism.

L#23555
Sinonasal alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
The neoplastic cells demonstrate a strong and diffuse nuclear reaction with myogenin.
R#23556
Sinonasal alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
The neoplastic cells of rhabdomyosarcoma may show aberrant co-expression with
CAM5.2, a finding that may cause diagnostic problems if a panel of
immunohistochemistry studies is not performed.

890

L#23557
Sinonasal alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
The neoplastic cells of rhabdomyosarcoma may show aberrant co-expression with
synaptophysin, a finding that may cause diagnostic problems if a panel of
immunohistochemistry studies is not performed.
R#23559
Sinonasal alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma
Aspiration smears demonstrate multinucleated tumour cells along with dispersed single
cells, showing eccentric cytoplasm.

L#24780
Spindle cell rhabdomyosarcoma
Spindle-shaped tumour cells have hyperchromatic nuclei and are arranged in fascicular
patterns.
R#24782
Spindle cell rhabdomyosarcoma with FUS: TFCP2 fusion, there is diffuse expression of ALK.

891

L#23192:
Sinonasal Alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma,
The stroma is filled with nests of neoplastic cells in an alveolar architecture. Delapidated growth is
noted by neoplastic cells with rhabdoid differentiation and eccentric eosinophilic cytoplasm.
R#26502
Rhabdomyosarcoma spindle cell
Near complete replacement of the soft tissues of the pharynx by this spindle cell
rhabdomyosarcoma.
Chondro-osseous tumours
Chondroma

Chondroma
Definition
Chondroma is a benign soft tissue neoplasm, unrelated to bone or synovium, and composed of
chondrocytes that produce hyaline or myxoid cartilage.
ICD-O coding
9220/0 Chondroma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E89.1 & XH0NS4 Benign tumours of uncertain differentiation, soft tissue & Chondroma NOS.
Related terminology
Acceptable: chondroma of soft parts; extraskeletal chondroma
Subtype(s)
Chondroblastoma-like soft tissue chondroma
Localization
These tumours usually arise in the hands and feet (80%). Origin in head and neck is uncommon
{ 19349148 }. Case reports describe lesions in the preauricular region and auricle
{ 24653926 ; 22844293 }, cheek { 15955665 ; 8229872 }, parotid gland { 16487372 }, lip
{ 29115679 }, tongue { 30845089 ; 15617977 ; 6590720 }, neck and parapharyngeal space
{ 8587816 ; 17008056 } and dura { 25250651 }.
Clinical features
These are painless firm slowly growing masses { 26297064 }. Lesions have a high signal on T2
weighted MRI. There may be ring like or spiculated calcification.
Epidemiology
Patients are usually middle-aged (average: 34.5 years), and males are affected more frequently
than females (M:F ratio: 1.5:1) { 76505 }.
Etiology
Unknown

892

Pathogenesis
Cytogenetic analyses reveal several different aberrations, including rearrangements of 12q13-q15
and trisomy 5, and in a set of tumours with rearrangements of 12q13-q15, a truncated or full-length
HMGA2 transcript was found { 8402563 ; 10748295 ; 11793371 ; 14614053 ; 25250651 }. In about
50% of the cases, FN1 gene rearrangements were reported, with FGFR1 and FGFR2 as fusion
partners { 31273315 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions are well-circumscribed, solid nodular masses, 10-20 mm in size, with grey blue, sometimes
myxoid cut surface.
Histopathology
Chondromas for nodular tumours which are sometimes thinly encapsulated, composed of mature
hyaline or myxoid cartilage, with lobules delineated by fibrous septate. Cartilage may be
hypercellular and the usually small, hyperchromatic nuclei may be large with coarse chromatin,
small nucleoli and up to moderate pleomorphism. Calcification or endochondral ossification may
occur. Degenerative changes include fibrosis, mucinous change and cyst formation { 24653926 }.
The chondroblastoma – like subtype is hypercellular with epithelioid chondrocytes showing grooved
or cleaved nuclei and individual cell calcification { 11342780 }. Mitoses are few or absent.
Cytology
Cytology is rarely performed. Smears may show variably sized clusters and single cells in
a myxofibrillary matrix. Nuclei are oval to spindled with moderate variation in size, finely dispersed
chromatin and small, indistinct nucleoli. Cytoplasm may be abundant and vacuolated and cell
margins indistinct. No multinucleation or mitoses are seen { 11213511 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: soft tissue mass composed of nodules of cartilage; hyaline or myxoid matrix that may
calcify; cytologically bland chondrocytes; few mitoses
Staging
Not clinically relevant
Prognosis and prediction
These are benign tumours with no recurrence after complete excision.

#30262
Chondroma arising in maxillary alveolus
Biopsies are often fragmented as seen here. Islands of hyaline cartilage may be present
in mucosa beneath intact epithelium.

893

L#30263
Chondroma arising in maxillary alveolus
Nodules of hyaline cartilage are present separated by fibrous septae. Chondrocytes
contain small hyperchromatic nuclei.
R#30264
Chondroma arising in maxillary alveolus
Higher power view of chondrocytes with hyperchromatic nuclei showing no binucleate
forms and no mitotic figures.

L#30265
Chondroma in nasal septum.
At some sites such as larynx and nasal septum chondroma may erode into adjacent bone
mimicking malignancy.
R#30668
Chondroma of tongue, A nodule of fibrohyaline cartilage is present in the stroma beneath
intact epithelium.
894

Peripheral nerve sheath tumours

Neurofibroma
Definition
Neurofibroma is a benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour consisting of differentiated Schwann
cells, fibroblasts, perineurial-like cells, and residual interspersed axons set in a fibromyxoid matrix.
ICD-O coding
9540/0 Neurofibroma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2F38 & XH87J5 Benign neoplasm of other or unspecified sites & neurofibroma NOS
2F24 & XH87J5 Benign cutaneous neoplasms of neural or nerve sheath origin & Neurofibroma
NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Ancient neurofibroma; cellular neurofibroma; plexiform neurofibroma; atypical neurofibroma
Localization
Neurofibromas most commonly affect the skin but deep neck lesions are seen (carotid space,
brachial plexus, posterior/paraspinal). Any head and neck site may be affected
{ 25503638 ; 27402223 ; 24315214 ; 23601771 ; 7127260 }. Intraorally, the tongue is the most
common
location
{ 25654048 ; 33196600 ; 31675118 ; 31598201 ; 31111316 ; 29931661 ; 25503638 }.
Diffuse
neurofibromas arise in skin and subcutis, and plexiform neurofibromas superficially or deep along
nerves with the orbitotemporal region most commonly affected { 33196600 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a slowly growing, often circumscribed and sometimes painful mass. Motor or
sensory symptoms develop when major nerve trunks are affected. Disfigurement and compression
of vital structures may be seen. Multiple and plexiform neurofibromas are frequently associated
with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) { 22606860 ; 9267819 ; 22420725 }.
Epidemiology
About 15% of neurofibromas arise in the head and neck region { 33196600 ; 33723760 }. It is the
most common benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour affecting patients of all ages, but most
commonly in the second to fourth decade { 22420725 ; 22327363 ; 33196600 }. All ethnic groups
are affected without a sex predilection { 25654048 }. Plexiform neurofibroma, specifically in NF1
patients, presents in childhood { 33196600 ; 33723760 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Inactivating mutations of the tumour suppressor NF1 encoding neurofibromin are the sole recurring
abnormality identified in the Schwann cell population { 22420725 ; 33723760 }, recognizing that
microenvironmental
factors
also
play
a
role
in
tumour
development
{ 30728335 ; 29596064 ; 27171146 }.
Specific criteria for atypical NF/atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biologic potential
are usually seen in the NF1 setting and are strongly associated with deletions
of CDKN2A/CDKN2B { 21987445 ; 26857854 ; 30722027 ; 29774626 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions are nodular with a tan-white, glistening cut surface. Occasionally, an associated nerve can
be identified { 22327363 ; 22420725 }. Plexiform neurofibromas show a complex, tortuous and
convoluted shape { 33723760 }. Diffuse neurofibromas are plaque-like lesions { 22327363 }.
Histopathology
Neurofibromas are localized intra- and/or extraneural. They are most often nodular and
uncommonly diffuse or plexiform. Lesions are non-encapsulated and characterized by random
distribution of spindly cells in a myxoid to collagenous stroma. Nuclei are wavy and tapered. The
cytoplasm is inconspicuous. Mitotic figures are usually absent. Entrapped ganglion cells may also
be seen. The collagen bundles resemble shredded carrots. Mast cells are frequently found
{ 22327363 ; 33723760 ; 33588442 }.

895

Diffuse neurofibromas show Meissnerian corpuscles and can entrap preexistent structures
{ 22327363 }.
Plexiform neurofibroma shows involvement of several cross-sections of peripheral nerve fascicles
surrounded by perineurium in a localized area.
Immunohistochemically, neurofibromas show a heterogeneous and variable expression of S100
protein and SOX10, interspersed with fibroblasts or perineurial cells positive with CD34 and EMA,
respectively. Neurofilament
protein
highlights
entrapped
axons
{ 22420725 ; 22495377 ; 22327363 }.
The differential diagnoses include schwannoma, traumatic neuroma, palisaded
encapsulated neuroma, mucosal neuroma of MEN, desmoplastic melanoma and
dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (when superficially located) { 22327363 }.
Increased mitotic figures, increased cellularity, and pleomorphism may suggest malignant
transformation { 33588442 }.
Cytology
FNA specimens are often paucicellular due to the collagen matrix, but a mucin-rich background
and small spindled cells with thin wavy nuclei can aid in diagnosis { 10459049 ; 21938166 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
There is no established role for molecular analyses in NF.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: non-encapsulated low cellularity spindle cell neoplasm associated with variably
fibromyxoid background with shredded-carrot-like collagen
Desirable: expression of S100, SOX10, CD34, EMA, neurofilament protein in appropriate cell
population (in selected cases)
Staging
none
Prognosis and prediction
Tumours are consistently benign, but neurofibromas in NF1 patients have a potential for malignant
transformation { 22327363 ; 23036231 ; 25929351 ; 22420725 }.

L#23526
Sinonasal neurofibroma
A sagittal computed tomography demonstrates a soft tissue density mass (white arrow)
within the nasal cavity below the ethmoid sinus.
R#23527
Sinonasal neurofibroma
Short intersecting fascicles of spindled with with buckled nuclei and a background fibrous
stroma.

896

L#23528
Sinonasal neurofibroma
Spindled cells with buckled nuclei set within a collagenized background stroma. Mast
cells are easily identified.
R#23529
Sinonasal neurofibroma
The spindled cells are S100 protein (shown), SOX10 and NFP positive.

Schwannoma
Definition
Schwannoma is a nerve sheath tumour composed entirely or nearly entirely of differentiated
neoplastic Schwann cells.
ICD-O coding
9560/0 Schwannoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2F38 & XH98Z3 Benign neoplasm of other or unspecified sites & Schwannoma (neurilemmoma).
Related terminology
Not recommended: Neurilemmoma, neurinoma
Subtype(s)
Ancient schwannoma; cellular schwannoma; plexiform schwannoma; epithelioid schwannoma;
microcystic reticular schwannoma
Localization
Extracranial, non-vestibular schwannoma arise anywhere in head and neck region. Intraorally, the
tongue is most commonly affected { 22606860 ; 25654048 ; 24384632 ; 31485983 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a slowly growing sometimes painful mass. Other signs depend largely
on size,
nerve
of origin,
and
localization
{ 22606860 ; 7127260 ; 9267819 ; 22420725 ; 33723760 }. CT and MRI show unilateral, well
circumscribed, expansile, oval, round or fusiform tumours, with mottled central lucency and
peripheral post-contrast enhancement, often with cystic changes. Bone erosion and remodeling is
occasionally present { 7127260 ; 22621416 ; 22954739 ; 24717879 }.
Epidemiology
Up to 40% of schwannomas occur in the head and neck { 33723760 ; 24384632 }. The majority of
cases affect adults in their second to fifth decades, with equal sex incidence
{ 9267819 ; 24384632 }.
Etiology
Most lesions are sporadic { 22327363 ; 22606860 }. Multiple and plexiform schwannomas
suggest association with neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2) or schwannomatosis { 33723760 }.
NF2 is an autosomal dominant genetic condition caused by mutations in the NF2 tumour
suppressor gene characterized by development of schwannomas (including bilateral vestibular
schwannomas), meningiomas, ependymomas, and low-grade gliomas { 33723760 }.
897

Schwannomatosis is characterized by spinal and peripheral schwannomas and meningiomas
usually without vestibular schwannomas { 33723760 ; 26848914 }, usually sporadic (up to 85%),
but also inherited { 33588442 }.
Pathogenesis
NF2 inactivating mutation is the most common genetic finding in sporadic or familial schwannomas
{ 33588442 }.
Schwannomatosis is associated with SMARCB1 (INI1) and LZTR1 mutations { 33723760 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions are nodular with a tan-white, glistening cut surface ranging up to 75 mm. Occasionally, an
associated nerve can be identified { 22327363 ; 22420725 ; 24384632 }. Plexiform schwannomas
appear tortuous to convoluted { 33723760 }.
Histopathology
Schwannomas are commonly well circumscribed, encapsulated, spindle cell tumours, occasionally
encroaching on adjacent structures. Most show compact hypercellular Antoni A areas with
occasional typical nuclear palisading (degenerative/ancient change), (Verocay bodies), alternating
with loosely arranged myxoid Antoni B foci. The elongated slightly polymorphic spindle cells
possess oval, tapered or buckled nuclei and poorly defined eosinophilic cytoplasm. Intranuclear
cytoplasmic inclusions, nuclear pleomorphism, and mitoses may exist. Antoni B zones contain a
cobweb-like network of cell processes together with lipid-laden histiocytes and thick-walled,
hyalinized blood vessels. Microcystic/reticular changes may be seen. Peripheral lymphoid
aggregates are common { 9267819 ; 22327363 }.
The cellular variant shows predominantly hypercellular Antoni A pattern, forming long fascicles.
Mitotic activity and necrosis may be present { 9267819 }.
Plexiform schwannomas have a thin fibrous capsule and an intraneural multinodular/plexiform
architecture. Cell morphology resembles classical schwannomas { 33723760 }.
Epithelioid schwannomas are (multi)nodular and encapsulated, and characterized by epithelioid
cells arranged in trabeculae, loose nodules and cohesive nests in collagenous, myxohyaline or
myxoid stroma. Degenerative features and mitoses may be present { 1746681 }
Diffuse
immunoreactivity
for
S-100
protein
and
SOX10
is
usual
{ 9267819 ; 22420725 ; 22495377 ; 22327363 ; 25724000 }. Absence of INI1 is observed in 40%
of epithelioid schwannomas and a mosaic staining pattern is typical for schwannomatosis
{ 28368924 ; 33723760 }. EMA outlines perineurial cells peripherally and neurofilament protein
may highlight entrapped axons at the periphery. CD34 is usually positive only in subcapsular and
septal areas { 33588442 }.
The differential diagnosis includes nodular fasciitis, neurofibroma, solitary fibrous tumour,
(angio)leiomyoma, desmoid-type fibromatosis, meningioma, glomangiopericytoma, biphenotypic
sinonasal sarcoma, mucosal melanoma, solitary circumscribed neuroma, mucosal neuroma of
MEN, malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, synovial sarcoma, spindled squamous cell
carcinoma, and leiomyosarcoma { 9267819 ; 11073336 ; 22002440 ; 25472697 ; 19644540 }.
Cytology
Aspirate smears are typically cellular with cohesive syncytial fragments of bland spindle cells.
Within the fragments, variably wavy/convoluted nuclei with tapered edges and fibrillary cytoplasm
are
seen.
Degenerative
atypia
may
be
present
{ 17277622 ; 23737794 ; 29413661 ; 30457183 ; 30620436 ; 31043260 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Hypercellular spindled cell areas and loose hypocellular myxoid areas; spindle cells with
buckled, elongated, fusiform nuclei, occasionally palisaded
Desirable: Hyalinized blood vessel walls; S100 protein/SOX10 in selected cases
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Schwannomas are benign. Multifocality may mimic recurrence or malignancy
{ 22327363 ; 9267819 ; 22420725 ; 22606860 ; 24384632 }.

898

L#23560: Sinonasal schwannoma, A coronal T1 fat suppressed MR image shows a mass
expanding into the skull base. It is not uncommon for schwannoma to grow through foramina.
R#23561: Schwannoma of the parapharyngeal space, There is a well circumscribed mass in the
parapharyngeal space showing increased intensity on this axial T2 FSE image.

L#23562: Sinonasal schwannoma, Hypo- and hypercellular areas are seen in this schwannoma.
R#23563: Sinonasal schwannoma, There is a hypercellular spindled cell population below an
intact respiratory epithelium. The nuclei are wavy to buckled.

L#23564: Oral schwannoma, Fascicles of spindled neoplastic cells are seen adjacent to vessels
that have a peritheliomatous hyalinization.
R#23565: Sinonasal schwannoma, There is an Antoni A area juxtaposed to an Antoni B area,
with myxoid change noted in the stroma.

899

L#23567: Sinonasal schwannoma, The spindled cell proliferation is intimately associated with
inflammatory cells as well as vessels with hyalinization.
R#23568: Sinonasal schwannoma, Ancient change, identified by remarkably pleomorphic
appearing nuclei, is a finding of degeneration in schwannoma.

L#23569: Sinonasal schwannoma, The neoplastic cells show a strong and diffuse, nuclear and
cytoplasmic S100 protein immunoreactivity.
R#23570: Sinonasal schwannoma, The neoplastic cells show a strong and diffuse, nuclear
SOX10 immunoreactivity.

L#25208: Schwannoma, This Diff Quick stained smear is cellular, demonstrating cohesive
syncytial fragments of bland spindle cells. The tumour cells are wavy, with convoluted nuclei.
R#25370: Oral schwannoma, A clinical photograph of a submucosal lateral tongue nodule.

#25371: Oral schwannoma, The cut surface shows a very well demarcated pale tumor
below an intact tongue epithelium.

Neuromas
Definition
Neuromas comprise a diverse group of peripheral nerve sheath tumors, some reactive and
hyperplastic, that include Traumatic neuroma (TN); Solitary circumscribed neuroma (SCN);
MEN2B-mucosal neuroma; Oral pseudoperineurioma (OPP); and PIK3CA-related overgrowth
spectrum (PROS) associated neuromas (PIK3CA-neuroma).
ICD-O coding
9570/0 Solitary circumscribed neuroma
ICD-11 coding
2F24 & XH90Y8 Benign cutaneous neoplasms of neural or nerve sheath origin & Solitary
circumscribed neuroma
8C12.5 Traumatic neuroma, not otherwise specified
2F7A.0 MEN2B-mucosal neuroma
8C4Y & XH4UE6 Other specified disorders of nerve root, plexus or peripheral nerves & Neuroma,
NOS
LD2C & XH4UE6 Overgrowth syndromes & Neuroma, NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: (Solitary circumscribed neuroma) Palisaded encapsulated neuroma
Not recommended: (Traumatic neuroma) Amputation neuroma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
TN occurs in areas of nerve injury including bones. SCN frequently affects facial skin and
palate/gingiva. Most OPP occur on the tongue. PIK3CA-neuromas affect tongue, lips and cheek.
Clinical features
Single (TN, SCN) or multiple (PIK3CA-neuroma; rarely in SCN) nodules, < 10 mm with the
exception of TN. Many but not all TN are painful, all other neuromas are asymptomatic. Multiple
mucosal neuromas unrelated to MEN2B and PROS have been described { 26708403 }.
Epidemiology
TN: Middle aged adults, more frequent in females.
SCN: 5th-7th decades of life, twice more common in men.
OPP: Young females.
Mucosal neuromas: Usually the first sign in MEN2B and in PIK3CA-orofacial overgrowth.
Etiology
TN are the result of accidental or iatrogenic nerve injury or amputation. Although considered
hyperplastic lesions, trauma maybe a possible cause for oral SCN while cutaneous lesions have
been associated with acne. MEN2B- and PIK3CA-related neuromas are caused by
germline RET and somatic PIK3CA mutations, respectively. Solitary OPP are considered the result
of trauma. Multiple OPP can be encountered in PROS.

901

Pathogenesis
TN: Exuberant proliferation of disorganized peripheral nerve fascicles as part of nerve regeneration
after nerve injury
SCN: Intraneural fascicular Schwann cell proliferation
MEN2B- and PIK3CA-neuromas: Enlargement of peripheral nerve bundles as part of RET germ
and PIK3CA somatic mutations, respectively.
OPP: Perineurial proliferation as part of apparent nerve injury or nerve enlargement as seen in
PROS
Macroscopic appearance
The size of TN varies while SCN, PROS-neuromas are small smooth nodules.
Histopathology
TN: Disorganized Schwann cell fascicles surrounded by perineurium sprouting from severed nerve
{ 30011306 }.
SCN: Fungating, round, multinodular or plexiform in the superficial dermis/lamina propria
characterized by intersecting Schwann cell fascicles with nerve axons frequently revealing cracking
artifact { 20237984 }.
OPP: Nerve fascicles showing complete or incomplete perineurium featuring thin, internally
located, perineurial cell pseudo-onion bulbs { 23645379 }.
PIK3CA-neuroma. Enlarged nerves with frequently hyperplastic perineurium or OPP. May share
features with MEN2B necessitating clinicopathologic correlation { 28665924 ; 32770747 }.
Cytology
Not described.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
PIK3CA variants in the majority of PIK3CA-associated neuromas.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
TN: Haphazardly arranged nerve fascicles
SCN: Fungating, multilobular/plexiform circumscribed, partially encapsulated fascicular
proliferation
OPP: Pseudo-onion bulbs
PIK3CA-neuromas: Enlarged nerves, thickened perineurium and OPP
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis is excellent for all types described.

L#25645: Traumatic neuroma, Sprouting fascicles of Schwann cells haphazardly arranged in a
fibromyxoid stroma.
R#25647:Solitary circumscribed neuroma, Fungating neural lesion characterized by intersecting
Schwann cell fascicles with cracking artifacts.

902

#25648: Oral pseudoperineurioma,
Fibrous hyperplastic lesion with
fascicles of nerves in the stroma.

#25650: Oral pseudoperineurioma,Pseudo-onion bulb perineurial cell proliferation. Single to
mostly 2 layers of perineurial cells surrounding individual axons.

L#25651: Oral pseudoperineurioma, Pseudo-onion bulb perineurial cell proliferation. A single to
mostly 2 layers of perineurial cells surrounding individual axons in a collagenous hyperplastic
stroma (H&E stain).
R#25655:Oral pseudoperineurioma, EMA highlights multiple pseudo-onion bulbs composed of
perineurial cells within a nerve partially surrounded by perineurium.

903

L#25659: PIK3CA-neuroma, Nodular lesions on the lip.
R#25660: PIK3CA-neuroma, Unilateral enlargement of the tongue with prominent papillae

#25662: PIK3CA neuroma, Biopsy of the labial lesion revealed findings consistent with oral
pseudoperineurioma.

Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour
Definition
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST) is a malignant spindle cell neoplasm with
limited Schwannian differentiation, often arising from a peripheral nerve, from a pre-existing benign
nerve sheath tumour, or in a patient with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1).
ICD-O coding
9540/3 Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B5E & XH2XP8 Malignant nerve sheath tumour of peripheral nerves or autonomic nervous
system, primary site & Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour
2B5E & XH4V81 Malignant nerve sheath tumour of peripheral nerves or autonomic nervous
system, primary site & Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, epithelioid
2B5E & XH2VV8 Malignant nerve sheath tumour of peripheral nerves or autonomic nervous
system, primary site & Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour with rhabdomyoblastic
differentiation
Related terminology
Not recommended: malignant schwannoma; neurofibrosarcoma; neurogenic sarcoma.
Subtype(s)
Epithelioid MPNST
Localization

904

The head and neck region is the second most common location after the trunk and extremities
{ 27158754 ; 3082508 }, most commonly in the soft tissue of the neck { 24685259 ; 28762137 }.
Clinical features
MPNSTs typically present between 20-50 years of age, with younger ages for NF1-associated
tumours. Patients present with an enlarging mass that may be painful. MPNST arising within a
nerve is often associated with neuropathic symptoms.
Epidemiology
MPNST is rare, accounting for 5-10% of all soft tissue sarcomas in the head and neck { 30779403 }.
Etiology
Subsets are associated with radiation (~10%) { 7377756 } or NF1 (~50%).
Pathogenesis
MPNSTs have complex karyotypes and frequent, concurrent inactivating mutations involving three
pathways: NF1, CDKN2A/CDKN2B,
and
PRC2
core
components
(EED or SUZ12)
{ 25240281 ; 25119042 ; 25305755 ; 30722027 }. Up to 80% of high-grade MPNSTs exhibit
complete loss of PRC2 activity and complete loss of H3K27me3 by immunohistochemistry
{ 26645727 ; 25240281 ; 26585554 }.
Epithelioid MPNSTs are molecularly distinct, characterized by frequent SMARCB1 gene
inactivation { 30864974 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Most MPNSTs are >50 mm. Intraneural tumours show fusiform enlargement of the involved nerve.
MPNST shows a tan-white, fleshy cut surface, often with haemorrhage and necrosis.
Histopathology
MPNST shows interlacing fascicles of spindle-shaped cells, often with alternating hypercellular and
hypocellular areas. A haemangiopericytoma-like vascular pattern is characteristic, and denser
perivascular growth is common. There are often areas of geographical necrosis and high mitotic
activity. Nuclei are hyperchromatic and spindled to serpentine, while the cytoplasm is pale and
eosinophilic. Occasional cases show prominent pleomorphism. Approximately 15% of MPNSTs
show heterologous differentiation, which may be rhabdomyoblastic ("Triton tumour"), osseous,
cartilaginous, or vascular, as well as epithelial (glandular or squamous).
MPNST show limited staining for S100 (<50% of tumours), SOX10 (<70%), and GFAP (20–30%)
{ 23929265 }. Complete loss of staining for H3K27me3 is seen in 50-80% of all MPNSTs, with loss
being
more
frequent
in
high-grade
and
radiation-induced
MPNST { 28776579 ; 26645727 ; 26585554 ; 29482987 }.
Epithelioid MPNST is composed of lobules of epithelioid cells with abundant eosinophilic
cytoplasm, sometimes embedded in an abundant myxoid or hyalinized stroma { 1746681 }. It is
not associated with NF1 and rarely arises ex-schwannoma { 25602794 }. Unlike conventional
MPNST, epithelioid MPNST shows strong and diffuse staining for S100 and SOX10, with retained
H3K27me3 and loss of SMARCB1 expression { 30864974 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
In diagnostically challenging cases, DNA methylation profiling can identify a specific tumour
signature of MPNST { 33479225 }. Other supporting molecular data include mutations
in NF1 and SUZ12 or EED.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
-Spindle cell sarcoma with fascicular growth, alternating hypercellular and hypocellular areas, and
tapered or buckled nuclei
-Epithelioid MPNST: epithelioid morphology and diffuse positivity for S-100 and SOX10
Desirable:
-Association with a pre-existing large nerve, neurofibroma, NF1, or radiation
-Variable positivity for S-100, SOX10, or GFAP
-Loss of H3K27me3 by immunohistochemistry
-Epithelioid MPNST: Loss of SMARCB1 expression
Staging
These tumours are staged using the UICC TNM system.
Prognosis and prediction

905

MPNST shows aggressive behavior with poor prognosis. For head and neck MPNST, 2 and 5-year
disease-specific survival are 50% and 30%, respectively { 28762137 }. Adverse prognostic factors
include tumour size > 50 mm, local recurrence, positive surgical margins, and high-grade
morphology (including Triton tumour). NF1-associated and radiation-induced MPNST are
associated with 5-year survivals of approximately 35% and 23%, respectively
{ 27158754 ; 31078939 }. Epithelioid MPNST are less aggressive { 25602794 }.

L#26767
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour. Interlacing fascicles of spindle-shaped cells
with alternating hypercellular and hypocellular areas.
R#26768
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor. Fascicles are densely cellular and tumour cell
nuclei are hyperchromatic and spindled to serpentine, with palely eosinophilic cytoplasm.

#26769
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour. Complete loss of staining for H3K27me3 is a
helpful diagnostic feature, occurring in 50-80% of all cases.

906

Tumours of uncertain differentiation

Phosphaturic mesenchymal tumour
Definition
Phosphaturic mesenchymal tumours (PMTs) are distinctive neoplasms that cause tumour-induced
osteomalacia (TIO), usually through production of fibroblast growth factor-23 (FGF23).
ICD-O coding
8990/0 Phosphaturic mesenchymal tumour, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2F7C & XH9T96 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of connective or other soft tissue &
Phosphaturic
mesenchymal
tumour,
benign
2F7C & XH3B27 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of connective or other soft tissue &
Phosphaturic mesenchymal tumour, malignant
Related terminology
Acceptable: Phosphaturic mesenchymal tumour, mixed connective tissue type
Subtype(s)
Phosphaturic mesenchymal tumour, malignant
Localization
PMTs may arise in any soft tissue or osseous location { 14707860 }. After the appendicular
skeleton, the head and neck region is the second most common site; about half the cases arising
in the paranasal sinuses, followed in frequency by the mandible and maxilla; uncommon sites are
the
floor
of
mouth,
pharynx,
larynx,
tongue,
and
temporal
bone
{ 31301876 ; 31505461 ; 30206408 }.
Clinical features
Most PMT are small lesions and patients commonly present with a long history of muscle
weakness, fatigue, bone pain and progressive fractures { 33151412 ; 28450684 }. Radionuclide
scans (especially 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT) are useful in detecting occult PMTs { 33709632 }.
Rare ‘non-phosphaturic’ PMTs are also reported. { 31301876 ; 29514106 }.
Epidemiology
PMTs are exceedingly rare; the true incidence is unknown. Less than 500 cases have been
reported { 14707860 ; 31301876 ; 30206408 }. Tumours affect middle-aged adults with equal
gender
distribution
and
are
rarely
detected
in
children
or
the
elderly
{ 31301876 ; 28614212 ; 26009620 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
PMTs cause TIO through production of phosphatonins, most often FGF23. FGF23 inhibits renal
reabsorption of phosphates inducing phosphaturia, hypophosphatemia, and TIO. Binding of
FGF23 to its receptor, fibroblast growth factor receptor 1 (FGFR1) activates signalling pathways
that upregulate FGF23 production in an autocrine/paracrine loop which potentially drives PMT
tumourigenesis. { 25319834 ; 27443518 ; 31301876 }. Roughly 50% of PMTs harbour fibronectin
1 FN1::FGFR1 fusions, or less often, FN1::FGF1 fusion { 25319834 ; 27443518 }. Upregulation of
Klotho expression may underly the pathogenesis of fusion-negative tumours { 31792355 }. ‘Nonphosphaturic’
PMT
are
likely
found
earlier
or
produce
less
FGF23
{ 31301876 ; 27443518 ; 29514106 }.
Macroscopic appearance
PMTs usually are non-descript soft tissue or bone masses. Some may be calcified,
haemorrhagic or contain fat { 14707860 ; 28614212 }.
Histopathology
Classical PMTs are composed of bland, ovoid, spindle to stellate cells embedded in a richly
vascularized stroma and contain the pathognomonic basophilic, smudgy matrix with ‘grungy’ or
‘flocculent’ calcification { 14707860 ; 28614212 ; 31301876 } (See Table 1 #24513). Head and
neck PMTs tend to contain adipose tissue, show minimal/absent calcified matrix, are more myoid
(particularly
sinonasal),
and
contain
prominent
thick-walled
blood
vessels
{ 14707860 ; 28614212 }. Alveolar PMT may induce epithelial (odontogenic) proliferation
{ 30206408 ; 32049812 }.

907

Exceptionally rare malignant PMT show cytologic anaplasia, increased mitotic rate and necrosis,
usually with a sarcomatous appearance { 14707860 ; 26759148 }.
The immunophenotype of PMT is non-specific, including variable expression of CD56, ERG1,
SATB2, SSTR2A, CD99, and D2-40. Chromogenic insitu hybridization for FGF23 mRNA is much
more
sensitive
and
specific
than
FGF23
immunohistochemistry
{ 25025444 ; 31301876 ; 14707860 ; 28614212 ; 30206408 ; 26464698 ; 24060005 ; 28858396 }
(see Table 2 #24514).
Cytology
Not relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
FN1::FGFR1 or infrequently FN1::FGF1 fusions are identified in > 50% cases and are confirmatory
but not essential for diagnosis { 33151412 ; 27443518 ; 25319834 ; 31301876 ; 31792355 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Clinicopathologic constellation of a bland spindle cell tumour with basophilic, smudgy
matrix, with /or without grungy calcification, and /or vascularized stroma, along with clinical
evidence of phosphate wasting and /or osteomalacia that is reversed by complete removal of the
tumour.
Desirable: FGF23 overexpression in tissues or elevated levels in the serum (in selected cases).
Staging
Not clinically relevant
Prognosis and prediction
The vast majority of PMTs are clinically and histologically benign. Complete tumour excision brings
dramatic resolution of osteomalacia { 14707860 ; 30206408 }. Exceptionally rare instances of
malignant PMT may metastasize and cause death. FGFR1 fusions may be a potential marker for
targeted use of FGFR1-3 inhibitors { 32905668 }

911

Myxoma
Definition
Myxoma is a benign myofibroblastic proliferation within an extensively myxoid ground substance
without other epithelial or mesenchymal (nerve sheath) elements and not affecting gnathic bone.
ICD-O coding
8840/0 Myxoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2E90.6 & XH6Q84 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx & Myxoma, NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: peripheral odontogenic myxoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Myxomas affect the periauricular soft tissues (external auditory canal), sinonasal tract, and larynx
{ 8116795 ; 22306211 ; 8334970 ; 21938690 ; 25606843 ; 26915162 ; 29988276 ; 31032655 }.
They appear to arise in the soft tissue but may expand to involve adjacent bone. When multiple
myxomas are present (skin of the external ear canal, eyelids), Carney complex may be present
{ 7640202 ; 8116795 ; 16267413 }.
See section 7.4.2.2 for Odontogenic myxoma.
Clinical features
Myxoma manifests as a slow-growing site-specific swelling { 8116795 ; 22306211 }. Symptoms
may be related to local tumour extension into adjacent structures (middle ear, palate, orbit, and/or
nasal
cavity)
{ 8116795 ; 8334970 ; 16267413 ; 19221255 ; 22306211 ; 26915162 ; 32066550 ; 32525735 ; 33
829881 }. Endoscopic and radiographic images demonstrate a mass, often well delimited.
Sinonasal tract tumours may show an intraosseous, unilocular/multilocular radiolucency
{ 6302014 ; 21168253 ; 21938690 ; 24429059 ; 15364495 ; 15701983 ; 27398121 ; 30484069 ; 3
1032655 }.
Epidemiology
Myxomas are rare, reported most commonly in children, although all ages may be affected, with
an
equal
sex
distribution
{ 8116795 ; 8334970 ; 6302014 ; 15364495 ; 21168253 ; 21880053 ; 22306211 ; 24429059 ; 216
24835 ; 15701983 ; 26915162 ; 30484069 ; 32525735 ; 33829881 }.
Etiology
Carney complex, if present, is commonly associated with germline inactivating mutations in
the PRKAR1A gene, and shows myxomas, spotty pigmentation, endocrine overactivity, and
schwannoma { 7640202 ; 8116795 ; 16267413 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours show a gray-white, glistening surface with gelatinous to firm consistency. They range up
to 50 mm, but are usually < 20 mm { 8116795 }.
Histopathology
Myxomas are circumscribed, but unencapsulated, composed of a loose hypocellular proliferation
of spindled, stellate or round cells within a myxoid stroma containing arciform thin blood vessels
and scattered fine collagen bands. Pleomorphism, mitotic figures, and tumor necrosis are absent
{ 8116795 ; 8334970 ; 6302014 }. Epithelial basaloid buds from the surface may be seen.
Odontogenic epithelium is absent in sinonasal tract tumors { 24429059 ; 30484069 }.
Immunohistochemistry is non-contributory, with variable SMA and nuclear ß-catenin reactivity
{ 30484069 ; 21624835 }.
In the differential diagnosis, angiomyxoma is a unique entity (CD34 reactive), and odontogenic
myxomas are separated based on anatomic tumour centering. Aural polyp and myxoid laryngeal
polyp may have some histologic overlap, but also have inflammatory components.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology

912

Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Circumscribed myxoid mass with scattered stellate and spindled cells in a capillary richmyxoid
matrix;
not
arising
in
gnathic
bones.
Desirable: nuclear ß-catenin staining (in selected cases).
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
These
lesions
are
benign,
but
recurrence
are
common
{ 7640202 ; 8116795 ; 30484069 ; 21624835 ; 24429059 ; 6302014 }. External auditory canal
tumours should provoke screening for Carney complex { 7640202 ; 16267413 ; 22306211 }.

spindled to stellate cells within a myxoid
matrix.

A collagenous and myxoid stroma are the predominant finding, with only isolated, bland
spindled neoplastic cells.

Note the myxoid stroma and polypoid appearance.

Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
Definition
Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma (EMC) is an aggressive malignant mesenchymal tumour of
uncertain differentiation, characterised by NR4A3 gene rearrangement, abundant myxoid matrix,
multilobular architecture, and uniform cells arranged in cords, clusters, and reticular networks.
Despite the name, there is no evidence of cartilaginous differentiation.
ICD-O coding
9231/3 Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B5F.2 & XH9344 Sarcoma, not elsewhere classified of other specified sites & Myxoid
chondrosarcoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: NR4A3-rearranged myxoid sarcoma (provisional)
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
Most common head and neck sites are orbit, intracranial and sinonasal tract. Orbit is the third most
common site after meninges and lower extremities. Case reports describe tumours in eyelid,
parapharyngeal space and buccal space { 29381948 ; 31909032 }.
Clinical features
Patients may present with an enlarging deep seated soft tissue mass associated with pain, diplopia
or headache. Intracranial tumours may cause symptoms related to raised intracranial pressure.
Epidemiology
EMC is rare (<1% of all soft tissue sarcomas). The male to female ratio is 2:1 and it usually affects
adults in 4th-5th decades. Orbital EMC is extremely rare { 27162461 }. Few cases are described
in the paediatric age group { 28638563 }. Intracranial EMC have been reported to show a female
predominance (M:F 1: 3) and a slightly younger age at presentation (median 39 years)
{ 29381948 }.
Etiology
Unknown
914

Pathogenesis
Molecular characterization of the t(9;22) and t(9;17) variant translocation show NR4A3 gene
fusions
with
either EWSR1 at
22q12.2
or TAF15 at
17q12
{ 10602520 ; 8634690 ; 10602519 ; 10537274 }. The NR4A3 fusions, present in > 90% of EMCs
{ 15998372 ; 12378528 ; 12598313 }, are specific to EMC and may therefore be considered a
hallmark of this disease. See STB5 for additional details.
Macroscopic appearance
EMC forms well circumscribed, lobulated, thinly encapsulated tumours, with soft to rubbery, tan /
white cut surface +/- microcalcifications and haemorrhage { 4261659 }.
Histopathology
EMC is multinodular with fibrous septa forming hypocellular lobules with abundant pale-blue
myxoid or chondromyxoid matrix. Well-formed hyaline cartilage is virtually never seen and the
stroma is strikingly hypovascular. Characteristically cells form cords, small clusters, and complex
trabecular or cribriform arrays. They contain modest amounts of deeply eosinophilic to vacuolated
cytoplasm and uniform round / oval nuclei; delicate elongated cytoplasmic processes are common.
Chromatin is evenly distributed, often with a small, inconspicuous nucleolus. Mitoses are
infrequent. Some tumours have prominent rhabdoid cytoplasmic inclusions. Rare cases are
hypercellular with decreased myxoid matrix and higher-grade, often epithelioid cytomorphology.
S100 positive in up to 20% of cases and KIT (CD117, c-KIT) positive in up to 30%. Synaptophysin
and NSE may be positive { 10895826 ; 11679947 ; 10955458 }. Tumours with rhabdoid
features are often negative for SMARCB1 (INI1). Tumours are very rarely keratin positive and are
negative for GFAP and muscle markers.
Cytology
Aspirates may comprise whitish fluid. Smears may be cellular or paucicellular with myxo-chondroid
background matrix. Two cell populations are observed oval and spindled occurring singly or in
cords and clusters, with pale blue cytoplasm +/- nuclear grooves and small inclusions.
Multinucleated cells may be present. Differential diagnosis includes other ‘round blue cell tumours’
including rhabdomyosarcoma, synovial sarcoma and Ewings.{ 30407856 }
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Identification of the NR4A3 gene rearrangement may be helpful in selected cases.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Multilobular tumour; generally bland cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm disposed in
strands or cords in a predominantly myxoid stroma.
Desirable: NR4A3 rearrangement (in selected cases)
Staging
The American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) or Union for International Cancer Control (UICC)
TNM system may be used.
Prognosis and prediction
EMC is associated with longterm overall survivals of 82-90% 5-year, 65-70% 10-year and 58% 15
-year in large retrospective series { 18951519 ; 10366145 }. However, there is a high rate of distant
metastases and death from disease. Lung metastases are common at presentation in high grade
tumours.{ 18951519 ; 10366145 ; 1451062 ; 1451062 } Poor prognostic indicators include older
age at presentation, large tumour size and proximal location { 10366145 , 10955458 }. Adverse
histology includes increased cellularity and pleomorphism { 9781944 ; 10469211 } and rhabdoid
cells { 10955458 ; 11493979 }. Worse outcomes are associated with variant non-EWSR1 gene
fusions rather than with EWSR1-NR4A3 { 24746215 }.

915

L#28043
Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
A gross photograph demonstrates a multinodular appearance with a myxoid, pale tanwhite appearance.
R#28044
Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
The tumour shows a multinodular appearance with fibrous septa forming hypocellular
lobules with abundant pale-blue chondromyxoid matrix.

L#28045
Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
The tumour cells form cords, small clusters, and complex trabecular and cribriform arrays.
R#28046

The basaloid cords of neoplastic cells are set within a myxoid backgorund, with fibrous
septa noted.

L#28047
Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
This EWSR1 breakapart probe demonstrates that EWSR1 is involved, but it does not
confirm the partner.
R#27228
Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
There is a multinodular appearance with fibrous septa forming lobules with abundant
pale-blue myxoid matrix. The cells are arranged in cords and small clusters with cells that
have mild variation.

Synovial sarcoma
Definition
Synovial sarcoma (SS) is a spindle cell sarcoma showing variable epithelial differentiation and
characterised by a specific SS18::SSX1, SSX2 or SSX4 fusion gene.
ICD-O coding
9040/3 Synovial sarcoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B5A.Y & XH9B22 Synovial sarcoma, other specified primary site & Synovial sarcoma NOS
Related terminology
Not recommended: Synovial cell sarcoma; synovioma.
Subtype(s)
Synovial sarcoma, monophasic; synovial sarcoma, biphasic; synovial sarcoma, poorly
differentiated
Localization
Most SS arise in the deep soft tissue of the extremities; 7% occur in the head and neck region
{ 19514087 }. Poorly differentiated SS most often arises in deep soft tissue of extremities, although
this subtype can be seen at a wide range of anatomic sites including head and neck; these have
higher metastatic potential than conventional SS.
Clinical features
Patients with synovial sarcomas present with palpable, deep-seated swellings, with or without pain
or tenderness.
Epidemiology
Synovial sarcoma may occur at any age, with 70% affecting patients less than 50 years. SS are
the most common non-rhabdomyosarcoma soft-tissue sarcomas in children and young adults
{ 19514087 ; 24633301 ; 33791777 }.
Etiology

917

Etiologic factors have not been identified, although exceptionally an association with prior
radiotherapy has been reported { 29336183 }.
Pathogenesis
In nearly all cases, a specific chromosomal translocation t(X;18)(p11;q11) acts as driver mutation
leading to formation of a SS18::SSX1 or SSX2 fusion genes. This translocation has not been found
in any other human neoplasm { 25614489 }. Molecular variants involving SSX4, SS18L1,
or NEDD4 are very rare { 32559641 }. Tumour mutational burden is usually low in these tumours
{ 32387103 }. Nevertheless, further driver mutations may accompany clonal evolution of synovial
sarcomas { 33436720 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions are yellow or grey to white, and well-circumscribed when slow growing.
Histopathology
Several monophasic histopathologic patterns (i.e. spindle-cell, calcifying/ossifying, myxoid, and
poorly differentiated) and biphasic patterns with glandular or solid epithelial cell nests can be
distinguished. Rarely, an overwhelming (>90 %) epithelial glandular component mimicking
adenocarcinoma with only rare, spindled areas has been described { 33819897 }. Poorly
differentiated tumours may contain areas with frequent mitoses and necrosis.
TLE1 nuclear immunoreactivity has been noted in as many as 95% of cases. Variable positivity for
CD99, BCL2, and CD56 as well as patchy to focal reactivity with epithelial markers, cytokeratins
(CK7), and BerEP4 is found. The tumours are usually negative for S100 and WT1. Two newly
developed antibodies, SS18::SSX fusion-specific antibody (E9X9V, reactive against the
breakpoint) as well as an SSX-specific antibody (E5A2C, reactive against the SSX C-terminus)
show strong diffuse nuclear staining with high sensitivity and specificity (>95%) in SS { 32141887 }.
Cytology
Synovial sarcoma (SS) can only be suggested in cytology with certainty when it presents with a
biphasic pattern. Monophasic SS is a diagnostic consideration when a uniform spindle cell
population is present, characterized by bipolar or fusiform nuclei with fine granular chromatin and
scant cytoplasm { 14696140 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular identification of the fusion gene is recommended to avoid misdiagnosis of the tumor as
metastatic adenocarcinoma, other sarcomas displaying epithelial differentiation, or a carcinoma
with a sarcomatous component, all of which require different clinical management { 33819897 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Biphasic pattern: contains both epithelial and spindle components, that may merge or
be distinct. Monophasic pattern: pure epithelial pattern is rare; pure spindle cell pattern contains
sheets of spindle cells with dark chromatin and scant cytoplasm.
Desirable: Keratin immunoreactivity; demonstration of t(X;18)(p11;q11).
Staging
The American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) or Union for International Cancer Control (UICC)
TNM system may be used.
Prognosis and prediction
SS may metastasise to lungs, bone and regional lymph nodes. The prognosis depends mainly on
staging, grading, resectability, use of radiation therapy, site of primary tumour, and presence of
metastases { 15364967 ; 18997619 ; 20716627 }. Tumours with > 20% poorly differentiated areas
show more aggressive behaviour { 9888710 }. In a small subset of synovial
sarcomas, targetable BRAF p.V600E mutations have been described { 32238877 }.

920

GLI1-altered soft tissue tumour
Definition
GLI1-altered soft tissue tumour is a mesenchymal neoplasm, of uncertain histogenesis,
characterized by an epithelioid morphology and GLI1 alterations.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
Not available
Related terminology
Acceptable: epithelioid neoplasm with GLI1 rearrangement
Not recommended: haemangiopericytoma of tongue; pericytoma with t(7;12)
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Tumours may involve soft tissue, bone, or viscera. Approximately 40% occur in the head and neck,
most arising in the tongue with less common locations including submandibular gland and soft
tissues of the neck { 15111311 ; 29309307 ; 31189998 ; 31934916 }.
Clinical features
Patients typically present with a slow growing painless mass { 8163277 ; 31567194 }.
Epidemiology
This is a rare tumour occurring over a broad age range, but most commonly presenting in young
adults (median 37, range 1 to 83 years). There is no overt sex predilection
{ 15111311 ; 31934916 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
GLI1-altered soft tissue tumours have a heterogeneous molecular pathogenesis, and can be
broadly divided into two groups. About two thirds of cases are defined by gene fusions
{ 15111311 }. The remaining third arise from amplification involving GLI1, often with coamplification of neighboring genes { 31189998 }. Tumours with GLI1-rearrangment can be further
subdivided based on their fusion partner. The most common fusion product is ACTB::GLI1,
with PTCH1::GLI1, MALAT1::GLI1,
and DERA::GLI1 representing
rare
events
{ 15111311 ; 31189998 ; 31934916 ; 33067875 }. Various GLI1 exons have been reported as
breakpoints { 15111311 ; 29309307 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours range from 8 to ≥ 100 mm but tend to be smaller in the head and neck region. They are
generally circumscribed and, on cut section, are white-yellow, fleshy, and may be solid and/or cystic
{ 15111311 ; 29309307 ; 31567194 ; 33067875 ; 32132020 }.
Histopathology
Morphologically there is a spectrum of architectural and cytologic findings. Tumours are frequently
multilobulated, with margins that may be well demarcated or infiltrative { 15111311 }. Architecturally
the cells are generally arranged in nests and sheets; occasionally they may have fascicles,
pseudorosettes, cords and/or reticular patterns { 15111311 ; 29309307 ; 31189998 ; 31934916 }.
The cells are small-medium and range from epithelioid to ovoid to spindled, with variable
eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm { 29309307 ; 26980027 ; 31189998 }. The nuclei are round to ovoid
and relatively monomorphic, with pin-point nucleoli; mitotic activity is variable, ranging from 0 - 5
per mm2 { 29309307 ; 31189998 ; 31934916 }. A prominent capillary-sized vasculature is often
present.
The
intervening
stroma
may
be
focally
myxoid
or
hyalinized
{ 15111311 ; 29309307 ; 31189998 ; 31934916 }. Protrusion into vascular spaces is common
{ 15111311 ; 31934916 }. Necrosis is rarely encountered { 31934916 }. The morphology and
ultrastructural findings are suggestive of pericytic differentiation { 15111311 }, but this is not entirely
supported by immunohistochemistry.
These neoplasms lack a consistent immunophenotype. While positive for vimentin these tumours
show variable, often in a patchy/focal or weak distribution, immunoreactivity for smooth muscle
actin,
S100,
CD10,
CD56,
CD99,
and
epithelial
membrane
antigen
{ 15111311 ; 29309307 ; 26980027 ; 31189998 ; 31934916 ; 32132020 }. Focal expression of h-

921

caldesmon, and keratin has been reported { 31567194 ; 31189998 }. Tumours are generally
negative for SOX10, HMB45, GFAP, CD34 and p63 { 29309307 ; 31189998 }. The lack of
expression of chromogranin and synaptophysin { 31934916 } may assist in distinction from
neuroendocrine neoplasms. Tumours with GLI1 amplification often have concomitant
overexpression of CDK4, MDM2 and STAT6 { 31934916 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The presence of a GLI1-rearrangement or amplification, in the appropriate clinical and
histomorphology context, is diagnostically helpful. GLI1-rearrangement is not disease-defining and
has been reported in plexiform fibromyxoma { 27101025 } and gastroblastoma { 28731043 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Nests and sheets of epithelioid-ovoid cells; prominent capillary vasculature; and,
monomorphic round-ovoid nuclei.
Desirable: Demonstration of GLI1-rearrangement, or amplification.
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Most tumours pursue an indolent clinical course when completely excised. A subset is complicated
by local or distant recurrence. Distant metastasis has been reported in approximately 20% of cases
and includes spread to lung, lymph node, soft tissue and bone, and brain
{ 29309307 ; 31189998 ; 31567194 ; 31934916 }.

with monomorphic nuclei. Extension
into vascular spaces is common.

permeates the tumour.

present in some cases.

Undifferentiated sarcoma
Definition
Undifferentiated sarcoma is a heterogenous group of neoplasms without any identifiable line of
differentiation as analysed by currently available techniques.
ICD-O coding
8805/3 Undifferentiated sarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B5F.2 & XH73J4 Sarcoma, not elsewhere classified of other specified sites & Giant cell sarcoma
2B5F.2 & XH0947 Sarcoma, not elsewhere classified of other specified sites & Malignant fibrous
histiocytoma
2B5F.2 & XH6HY6 Sarcoma, not elsewhere classified of other specified sites & Undifferentiated
sarcoma
2B5F.2 & XH85G7 Sarcoma, not elsewhere classified of other specified sites & Small cell sarcoma

923

Related terminology
Not acceptable: Malignant fibrous histiocytoma.
Subtype(s)
Spindle cell sarcoma, undifferentiated; pleomorphic sarcoma, undifferentiated; round cell sarcoma,
undifferentiated
Localization
Lesions arise in the sinonasal tract, upper aerodigestive system, parotid gland region and soft
tissue of the neck { 12778019 ; 25186315 ; 18853446 ; 23812657 ; 28629789 }. They are usually
subcutaneous or submucosal, but occasionally arise in bone { 12778019 }.
Clinical features
There are no characteristic clinical features, although rapid growth is common. Rarely, regional or
distant metastases are reported{ 12778019 }.
Epidemiology
Undifferentiated sarcoma occurs in adults without sex predilection. Because of reporting
differences,
the
incidence
cannot
be
reliably
determined
{ 18343096 ; 25186315 ; 18853446 ; 23812657 ; 28629789 }.
Etiology
While unknown in most tumors, radiation is a risk factor { 12778019 ; 18853446 }.
Pathogenesis
Undifferentiated
sarcoma
shows
extensive
and complex
genetic
aberrations
{ 30889380 ; 20217954 }. Genomic profiling may reveal that some seemingly undifferentiated
sarcomas
can
be
classified
more
specifically
{ 19290004 ; 15221942 ; 12640106 ; 21412072 }. See [[STB5]] for additional details.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumors present as multilobulated gray-white, fleshy masses. Cut surface often shows
hemorrhagic, myxoid, and/or necrotic changes { 207408 ; 23812657 ; 28629789 }.
Histopathology
Tumors are composed of an admixture of spindle, epithelioid and pleomorphic cells set in a variably
collagenized extracellular matrix. Cellularity varies. Pleomorphism, frequent mitotic figures
including atypical forms, histiocyte-like cells, and foamy cells, as well as giant tumor cells with
enlarged, polylobulated nuclei are commonly observed. Areas of tumor necrosis are often seen.
Specific features of known tumor types are absent. The three subtypes listed above probably
represent histological patterns, rather than distinctive subtypes.
Immunohistochemically, there may be limited smooth muscle actin reactivity, but h-caldesmon,
desmin, S100 protein, and epithelial markers are not expressed. Histiocytic antigens don't aid
interpretation.
Undifferentiated sarcoma is a diagnosis of exclusion. All other potential mimics should be ruled out,
including rhabdomyosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, liposarcoma, malignant peripheral nerve sheath
tumor, high-grade myxofibrosarcoma, high-grade osteosarcoma, other distinctly characterized
round cell sarcomas carcinoma, melanoma and lymphoma { 20217954 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: pleomorphic high-grade morphology; exclusion of other entities
Desirable: absence of distinctive molecular aberrations (in selected cases)
Staging
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
Patients are reported with intermediate survival of 60% to 70% { 1624288 ; 25186315 }. Previous
radiation is reported as an adverse prognostic factor for disease-free survival { 18853446 }.

undifferentiated malignancy.

It is not uncommon to have a mixture of epithelioid, spindled, and polygonal cells,
showing easily identified pleomorphism.

Undifferentiated small round cell sarcomas of bone and soft tissue

Ewing sarcoma
Definition
Ewing sarcoma is a primitive small round cell sarcoma defined by fusions involving members of the
FET and ETS gene families.
ICD-O coding
9364/3 Ewing sarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B52.3
Ewing
sarcoma
of
soft
tissue
2B52.Y Ewing sarcoma of bone and articular cartilage of other specified sites
Related terminology
Not recommended: primitive neuroectodermal tumour, peripheral neuroectodermal tumour
Subtype(s)
Adamantinoma-like Ewing sarcoma
Localization
Overall, 1-9% of Ewing sarcomas arise in head and neck sites, of which approximately 40% involve
skull and facial bones, 30% are centered in soft tissue, and 9% affect the mandible
{ 19841575 ; 28056517 }. However, most cases of the adamantinoma-like variant reported to date
involve the head and neck, including salivary glands, thyroid gland, and sinonasal tract
{ 26034869 ; 30285997 ; 31950471 }.

926

Clinical features
Patients with Ewing sarcoma present with a palpable mass or mass effect, including pain, swelling,
bleeding, or pathologic fracture.
Epidemiology
Approximately 50% of head and neck Ewing sarcoma arise in patients <18 and 85% in the first 4
decades of life, with a slight male predominance similar to other sites outside the head and neck
{ 28056517 ; 31774563 }
Etiology
Ewing sarcoma generally arises sporadically, and no risk factors are known.
Pathogenesis
Ewing sarcomas arise as a result of reciprocal translocations between members of the FET gene
family, predominantly encompassing EWSR1 or FUS, and the ETS gene family, most commonly
including FLI1, ERG, ETV1, ETV4,
or FEV { 3163261 ; 8022439 ; 8162068 ; 7700648 ; 8834175 ; 9121764 ; 12907633 ; 17620387 }.
These genes encode transcription factors contributing to oncogenic transformation via widespread
gene activation and repression { 27246176 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Ewing sarcoma specimens are solid with a soft, fleshy gray-white cut surface.
Histopathology
Ewing sarcoma is composed of hypercellular sheets and nests of uniform small round cells with a
scant amount of eosinophilic to clear cytoplasm, indistinct cytoplasmic borders, and occasional
rosette formation. Tumour nuclei are generally round to oval with delicate stippled chromatin and
inconspicuous nucleoli. A subset of cases, known as atypical Ewing sarcoma, can show larger cells
with hyperchromatic, angulated nuclei, coarse chromatin, and prominent nucleoli.
Adamantinoma-like Ewing sarcoma is a subtype of Ewing sarcoma that shows lobulated
architecture, peripheral palisading, and myxoid to hyalinized stroma; there is consistent
immunohistochemical evidence of squamous differentiation although keratin pearls are not seen in
every case.
Immunohistochemistry
Ewing sarcoma consistently shows diffuse membranous positivity for CD99 and nuclear positivity
for NKX2.2 { 1848471 ; 29661713 ; 26847175 }. While NKX2.2 is the more specific marker overall,
it stains several mimics in the head and neck, particularly tumours with neuroendocrine
differentiation { 28616785 }. FLI1 and ERG are expressed in cases with corresponding gene
fusions, although their specificity is limited { 11117787 ; 19841938 ; 22766791 }. Neuroendocrine
marker positivity can be seen in 50% of Ewing sarcoma, and 30% show some cytokeratin
expression { 33610950 ; 16006796 }. The adamantinoma-like variant of Ewing sarcoma
consistently demonstrates diffuse cytokeratin, p63, and p40 positivity { 16006796 }.
Differential diagnosis
Ewing sarcoma overlaps with many head and neck small round blue cell tumours, including
olfactory neuroblastoma, malignant melanoma, lymphoma, and various carcinomas. A broad
immunohistochemical panel including CD99 or NKX2.2 can facilitate the correct diagnosis although
molecular confirmation is frequently necessary. Although less common in the head and neck,
tumours previously known as Ewing-like sarcomas, including those with CIC and BCOR fusions,
should also be ruled out via immunohistochemistry and molecular studies
{ 28337592 ; 28777154 }.
Cytology
Fine-needle aspirates demonstrate a hypercellular population of monotonous round cells frequently
including a mix of smaller and larger cells and occasionally showing rosette formation { 22180234 }
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Genetic confirmation is recommended for diagnosis in the head and neck given the overlap with
other small round blue cell tumours. 85-90% of cases show t(11;22)(q24;q12) resulting
in EWSR1::FLI1 fusion { 3163261 } and 5-10% of cases show t(21;22)(q22;q12) resulting
in EWSR1::ERG fusion { 10561219 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Small round cell morphology

927

Membranous CD99 expression
Desirable:
Molecular confirmation of EWSR1 or FUS rearrangement
Staging
AJCC/UICC TNM staging for the appropriate anatomic site can be applied.
Prognosis and prediction
5 year survival is 70% for localized disease but just 30% with metastases at presentation
{ 26304893 }. Tumours in the head and neck have better survival compared to other sites,
probably as they are smaller and have a lower metastatic rate { 28056517 }. 100% necrosis after
chemotherapy is associated with better survival { 27482030 }.

11. Haematolymphoid proliferations and neoplasia
Introduction

Follicular lymphoma

T/NK cell tumours
T-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma
Primary mucosal CD30-positive T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder
Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma
Hodgkin Lymphoma
Hodgkin lymphoma
Histocytic and dendritic cell tumours
Juvenile xanthogranuloma
Erdheim Chester disease
Rosai-Dorfman disease
Langerhans cell histiocytosis
Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma

Introduction
With the abundance of lymph nodes and other lymphoid structures, including those of the Waldeyer
ring, in the head and neck, it is not surprising that this anatomic region is one of the most common
sites where nodal and extranodal haematolymphoid neoplasms are first detected in adults as well
as children { 26107683 ; 34388659 ; 31187530 }. While many such neoplasms are systemic, some
have a strong predilection to arise primarily in the head and neck region. Of these, we note in
particular paediatric-type follicular lymphoma, large B-cell lymphoma with IRF4 rearrangement,
plasmablastic lymphoma, and extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma.
This chapter starts with a number of reactive haematolymphoid lesions and related disorders that
may mimic haematolymphoid neoplasia. EBV-positive mucocutaneous ulcer is now regarded as a
self-limited lymphoproliferative disorder that arises in immunocompromised individuals. A new
entity included in this category is IgG4-related disease, which often presents in this region of the
body. Listing IgG4-related disease in this volume is anticipated to help standardize its diagnostic
criteria worldwide.
The majority of lymphomas of the head and neck are of B-cell lineage { 14631680 }. Differential
diagnostic considerations for these should rest on combinations of morphologic and
immunophenotypic findings, often diverging on the basis of whether the neoplastic cells are
small/intermediate in size or large. For tumours that do not fall in this group, the possibility that they
may be myeloid sarcoma, extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma, T lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma,
or any of the rare histiocytic or dendritic cell tumours should be entertained.

931

Reactive haematolymphoid and related lesions

Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia
Definition
Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia is a non-neoplastic proliferation of lymphoid tissue in nodal or
extranodal sites.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
MA01.Z Enlarged lymph nodes, unspecified
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia may involve cervical lymph nodes, Waldeyer's ring, salivary glands,
and/or mucosal surfaces. Localization may vary by etiology.
Clinical features
Patients present with lymphadenopathy, enlarged tonsils or salivary glands, and/or mucosal
ulceration. Symptoms vary by cause. Manifestations may be more severe in immunodeficient
patients.
Epidemiology
Varies by underlying disease (See Table #24644).
Etiology
Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia and lymphadenitis in the head and neck can be caused by viruses
(EBV { 31347374 ; 30773381 ; 20154586 }, CMV { 30773381 ; 26857321 }, HSV { 27905140 },
HIV { 31060959 ; 32171271 }), bacteria (Bartonella henselae { 26551620 }, pyogenic bacteria
{ 29594802 ; 30626342 ; 26857321 }),
mycobacteria
{ 31060959 ; 32171271 ; 31002716 ; 29594802 }), autoimmune diseases (systemic lupus
erythematosus) { 8853165 }, Sjögren syndrome { 11130833 }, rheumatoid arthritis { 17064872 }),
and diseases of unknown etiology (e.g. IgG4-related disease { 20061932 }), histiocytic necrotizing
lymphadenitis [Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease]
{ 3217625 },
Kawasaki
disease
{ 25882057 ; 23020240 }, Kimura disease { 8936520 ; 15087670 }, and atypical marginal zone
hyperplasia of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue { 15256428 ; 26794707 ; 21956819 }).
Pathogenesis
Exposure to the inciting agent causes reactive changes in lymphoid tissue or mucosa.
Macroscopic appearance
Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia produces enlarged lymph nodes, expanded extranodal lymphoid
tissue or ulceration. In salivary glands, small or large cysts may form due to ductal obstruction.
Histopathology
Depending on the etiology and disease phase, lymphoid tissue may show follicular hyperplasia,
paracortical hyperplasia, monocytoid B-cell hyperplasia, immunoblastic reaction, granuloma
formation (caseous or non-necrotizing), and/or suppurative inflammation. Certain reactive
conditions, especially infectious mononucleosis { 22627742 }, Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease
{ 3217625 }, and atypical marginal zone hyperplasia { 15256428 }, can mimic lymphoma. EBV+
mucocutaneous ulcer is a neoplastic rather than reactive lesion, but is an important differential
diagnosis for reactive lymphoid hyperplasia and lymphoma { 20154586 }.
Cytology
Aspiration biopsies of reactive lymphoid proliferations show a mixture of lymphoid cells, mimicking
the reactive germinal centre, including immunoblasts and normal appearing lymphocytes. Tingible
body macrophages may also be present. Occasional reactive lymphoid proliferations may show
clonal B-cell populations by flow cytometry and should be interpreted with caution { 15080297 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Occasional cases may show such distortion of normal architecture or cytologic atypia as to raise
the question of lymphoma. In such cases, gene rearrangement studies can be useful to show
absence of a clonal B- or T-cell population. EBV+ mucocutaneous ulcer can show B- and/or T-cell

932

gene rearrangements, and the results should be interpreted in the context of other clinicopathologic
features.
Genetic techniques can assist with identification of certain infectious agents { 29594802 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Reactive lymphoid tissue changes with no evidence of lymphoma or other neoplasm.
Desirable:
Identification of an infectious agent by immunohistochemistry, special stains, microbiology culture
or molecular techniques; or, recognition of an underlying autoimmune disease, immunodeficiency
or other disease by clinical or laboratory criteria.
Staging
Not clinically relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Outcome is dependent on the underlying disease and available therapy, but most cases due to
infection resolve spontaneously or respond to antibiotics. Outcome in patients with autoimmune
diseases or immunodeficiency is variable.
#24644 Lymphoid hyperplasia and lymphadenitis in the head and neck

Outcome
