EBV+ mucocutaneous ulcer
Definition
EBV+ mucocutaneous ulcer (EBVMCU) is a self-limiting lymphoproliferative disorder with a
polymorphous lymphoid infiltrate including Hodgkin-like cells that typically involves mucosal and
cutaneous sites in immunocompromised patients.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
ME60.Z & XH3SG2 Skin lesion of unspecified nature & EBV positive mucocutaneous ulcer
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None

937

Localization
EBVMCU involves mucosal sites including oral mucosa, tonsils, palate, gastrointestinal tract, and
skin
{ 20154586 ; 19889052 ; 22769419 ; 25007145 ; 28395107 ; 30418184 ; 32561847 ; 33739792 }.
Rarely, regional lymph nodes may be involved { 20154586 ; 28395107 }.
Clinical features
Well-circumscribed, often painful ulcers in mucosal or cutaneous sites without systemic symptoms,
lymphadenopathy,
hepatosplenomegaly,
or
bone
marrow
involvement
{ 20154586 ; 19889052 ; 22769419 ; 25007145 ; 28395107 ; 30418184 ; 32561847 ; 33739792 }.
Multifocal lesions within one anatomic region may be present in a subset of patients
{ 20154586 ; 30418184 }.
Epidemiology
The incidence of EBVMCU is unknown. It typically occurs in immunosuppressed patients including
in the elderly and secondary to autoimmune, iatrogenic, HIV, and posttransplant settings
{ 20154586 ; 19889052 ; 22769419 ; 25007145 ; 28395107 ; 30418184 ; 32561847 ; 33739792 ;
22236092 ; 24334644 }.
Etiology
EBV is present is all cases and likely etiologically associated.
Pathogenesis
The pathogenesis is not well understood and is likely related to alterations in cytotoxic T-cell
repertoire and functionality in response to EBV in immunosuppressed patients. IGH gene
rearrangements are detected in 50% of cases; rare TCR gene rearrangements may be present
{ 20154586 }.
Macroscopic appearance
EBVMCU causes a shallow ulcer without mass formation.
Histopathology
Mucosal and cutaneous lesions show well-circumscribed ulcers at the base of which is a
polymorphous infiltrate ranging from small to large lymphoid cells, variable numbers of
immunoblasts, plasma cells, and Hodgkin-like cells. Vascular destruction, thrombosis and necrosis
is often prominent; the overlying epithelium may show pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia
{ 20154586 ; 25007145 ; 28395107 ; 19889052 ; 22236092 ; 30082493 ; 30418184 ; 32561847 }.
Small cell infiltrates predominate in some cases { 33739792 }.
Immunohistochemistry shows a mixture of CD20- and CD3-positive lymphoid cells. CD30 is
commonly expressed whereas CD15 is rare. CD8 T-cells may predominate in some cases. Small
T-cells
typically
form
a
boundary
with
uninvolved
adjacent
tissue
{ 20154586 ; 25007145 ; 28395107 ; 30418184 }.
The differential diagnosis includes EBV-positive diffuse large B-cell and classic Hodgkin
lymphomas. The circumscription and localization of EBVMCU is easily distinguished from
lymphomas although the diagnosis may be challenging in small or needle core biopsies.
{ 20154586 ; 25007145 ; 28395107 ; 30082493 ; 30418184 ; 32561847 ; 33739792 }.
Cytology
Cytologic features show a polymorphous lymphoid infiltrate with necrosis.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Well-circumscribed ulcer in mucosal and cutaneous sites.
Desirable: Demonstration of EBV infection, for instance by EBER.
Staging
Staging may be necessary to separate EBVMCU from EBV-positive large B-cell and Hodgkin
lymphomas.
Prognosis and prediction
Majority of cases regress spontaneously or respond to withdrawal of immunosuppression (if
feasible) or localized therapy. Occasional cases may have a relapsing and remitting course without
progression { 20154586 ; 25007145 ; 28395107 ; 22769419 ; 19889052 ; 24456137 }.

938

#25550: Low magnification of tonsil shows a well-circumscribed lesion with extensive mucosal
ulceration.

L#25552: High magnification shows a mixed inflammatory infiltrate with atypical Hodgkin-like
cells.
R#25553: EBER in situ hybridization highlights a range of EBV-positive cells.

IgG4-related disease
Definition
IgG4-related disease (IgG4RD) is a systemic, immune-mediated and mass-forming lesion
characterized by IgG4+ cell-rich lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, storiform fibrosis, obliterative
phlebitis, and typically elevated serum IgG4.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
4A43.0 IgG4 related disease
Related terminology
Not recommended: IgG4-related autoimmune disease; IgG4-related sclerosing disease;
Kuttner tumour; pseudotumour
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
IgG4RD affects single or multiple organs simultaneously or metachronously (Table 1). In the head
and neck region, the orbit and submandibular salivary gland are most frequently affected, followed
by cervical lymph nodes, skin and soft tissue. Upper aerodigestive tract (sinonasal area, oral cavity,
laryngopharyngeal
mucosa),
ear,
skull
base
and
thyroid
can
be
affected.
{ 30612117 ; 27658185 ; 26686205 ; 33392765 ; 33167472 ; 25204708 ; 24115385 }
Clinical features

939

The presentation is subacute. Fatigue and weight loss are common but fever is unusual. Preexisting allergic symptoms are common. Clinical manifestations in the head and neck region include
mass lesions (submandibular mass) and localized effects in the affected sites such as nasal
obstruction, facial swelling, pain, epistaxis, destruction of nasal structures, dysphonia, dyspnea,
stridor,
hearing
loss,
vestibular
dysfunction
and
headache
{ 26194097 ; 26686205 ; 33192112 ; 33167472 ; 33325728 ; 24682733 }.
Serum IgG4 is elevated in the majority of cases, which may correlate with the number of organs
involved and disease activity, but is not very specific for IgG4RD. Tests for serum IgG4 to IgG ratio
and IgG4/IgG RNA ratio may perform better { 32546500 ; 24651618 ; 22536256 ; 32552502 }. Mild
peripheral eosinophilia and elevation of IgE, ESR, antinuclear antibodies, positive rheumatoid
factor, and hypocomplementemia are common, but disease-specific markers such as ANCA, SSA,
SSB and dDNA are negative.
Imaging
Imaging studies are useful to confirm the primary site of the disease and to detect multiorgan
involvement. 18F-FDG avidity may reflect disease activity { 33516733 }.
Epidemiology
IgG4RD is uncommon, typically affecting patients in late adulthood (fifth and sixth decades) with a
male to female ratio of 1.6:1 for the head and neck region and 4:1 for other sites. Pediatric cases
exist but are rare { 32546500 ; 27012661 }. No environmental or racial factors have been found.
Etiology
IgG4RD is associated with chronic antigen stimulation by self (annexin A11, laminin-511, galectin3, prohibitin) or unidentified antigens in genetically susceptible individuals with impaired
immunologic tolerance { 29852256 ; 28765476 ; 30089633 ; 25932630 ; 31104539 ; 30081155 }.
Pathogenesis
Recent studies suggest that chronic antigen stimulation and a breach of immune tolerance leads
to oligoclonal expansion of circulating plasmablasts and CD4+ cytotoxic T cells. Increased follicular
helper T2 cells promote plasmablast differentiation, IgG4 isotype switching, and contribute to
germinal center formation in the affected sites { 28166682 ; 24815737 ; 27411315 }. CD4+
cytotoxic T cells, IgG4+ plasmablasts and the locally recruited M2 macrophages in the affected
sites express profibrotic cytokines leading to fibroblastic activation and collagen production
{ 32939060 }. IgG4 probably plays an anti-inflammatory role as an unsuccessful attempt to dampen
the aberrant immune response.
Macroscopic appearance
The involved salivary gland shows a lobulated tan cut surface. Mucosal lesions in the upper
aerodigestive tract feature an exophytic granular or nodular mass usually with an intact surface.
Histopathology
Lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate rich in IgG4+ plasma cells and storiform fibrosis are cardinal features
of IgG4RD, where the former often predominates in the early “inflammatory phase” of the disease
and the latter in the late “fibrotic phase”. Elevation of both the number of IgG4+ cells (Table #24693)
and ratio (IgG4+/IgG+ >40%) is required to render this feature specific { 22596100 }. Obliterative
phlebitis is characterized by occlusion of the venous lumen by a fibroinflammatory infiltrate, but this
feature is less frequently encountered in the head and neck region { 23068303 ; 21107087 }.
Necrosis, abscess, neutrophilic infiltration, necrotizing vasculitis and granulomas are uncommon in
IgG4RD. However, perifollicular granulomas may be seen { 32889888 }.
The salivary gland lesions are non-circumscribed, with patchy atrophy of acini, periductal fibrosis,
dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, reactive lymphoid follicles and eosinophilic infiltrate. Focal
involvement of the gland is the norm. The lobular architecture is accentuated by fibrosis of the
interlobular septa.
Lesions in the upper aerodigestive tract show a mucosa expanded with IgG4+ cell-rich
lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate and storiform fibrosis, but obliterative phlebitis is uncommon.
Destruction of the surrounding bone is common and perineural extension can be seen
{ 21546462 }. Inner ear lesions can result from extension of middle ear disease or involvement by
skull base pachymeningitis { 33325728 ; 31841234 }.
Eosinophilic angiocentric fibrosis is a rare entity characterized by eosinophilic vasculitis,
lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate and concentric perivascular stromal fibrosis { 25065665 ; 30289267 }.
Some cases show increased IgG4+ plasma cells and serum IgG4 { 21502911 ; 32342944 }.
Whether it may represent an IgG4RD is still controversial because of its lack of response to steroid
and inconsistent elevation of IgG4+/IgG+ cells ratio in the reported cases { 33064012 ; 27281484 }.

940

Cytology
Fine needle aspirate of lesions involved by IgG4RD are usually low to moderately cellular. In the
salivary gland, there may be scattered ductal structures, often enveloped by collagen bundles,
paucity or absence of acini, and isolated fragments of fibrous stroma in a background rich in a
lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate { 11791589 ; 27666423 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate with increased IgG4+ cells (Table 1) and IgG4+/IgG+ ratio >40% with
storiform fibrosis
Exclusion of known entities such as ANCA-associated vasculitis, rheumatoid arthritis, multicentric
Castleman disease, Rosai-Dorfman disease, inflammatory myofibroblastic tumour, chronic
infection.
Desirable:
Obliterative phlebitis
Clinically compatible features: IgG4RD in other body sites, elevated serum IgG4 and response to
steroid/rituximab
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
IgG4RD follows a remitting and relapsing course. Although highly responsive to steroids, it can
result in organ dysfunction and even death if not properly diagnosed and treated
{ 25155229 ; 26453089 ; 17414116 }. Localized disease such as IgG4-related sialadenitis may be
controlled by surgery with or without steroids. Multi-organ disease, high baseline IgG4, IgE and
peripheral eosinophilia are risk factors for relapse. Maintenance therapy with steroids,
immunomodulators and biological agents like rituximab has been advocated { 33689549 }.
Increased incidence of malignancy, including carcinoma and lymphoma, has been observed in
patients with IgG4RD { 25155229 ; 31872350 ; 26472416 ; 32188869 ; 28544157 }, but the causal
relationship with cancer is still controversial { 25881845 ; 24492683 ; 32552502 }.

Myeloid tumours

Extramedullary myeloid sarcoma
Definition
Myeloid sarcoma is an extramedullary tumor mass composed of myeloid blasts and often maturing
myeloid elements that effaces tissue architecture.
ICD-O coding
9930/3 Myeloid sarcoma

947

ICD-11 coding
2A60.39 & XH3l40 Myeloid sarcoma & Myeloid sarcoma
Related terminology
Obsolete: chloroma; Not recommended: granulocytic or monocytic sarcoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Myeloid sarcoma in the head and neck region is rare, occurring in 0.1-1% of patients with AML,
and accounting for 5-10% of all cases of myeloid sarcoma { 17170724 ; 23530613 ; 25213180 }.
Any site in this region can be involved, but the oral cavity is most common { 23530613 }.
Clinical features
Most often patients have a history or coexistent evidence of acute myeloid leukemia (AML), or less
often other myeloid neoplasms. Rarely, myeloid sarcoma is the initial manifestation of disease.
Patients may present with a mass, painful throat, bleeding from nose or mouth, jaw pain, or
disturbed vision.
Epidemiology
The epidemiological features are similar to patients who develop de novo AML, or patients who
develop a blast phase of an underlying myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS), myeloproliferative
neoplasm (MPN), or myelodysplastic/myeloproliferative neoplasm (MDS/MPN).
Etiology
The etiology is similar to that of AML. The specific reasons for presentation as an extramedullary
mass are unknown, but adhesion molecules (e.g. integrin alpha 7) and extracellular matrix
interactions are suggested as being involved { 32033262 }.
Pathogenesis
Myeloid sarcoma involving the head and neck region shares cytogenetic and molecular
abnormalities with AML, such as monosomy 7, t(8;21), inv(16), KMT2A rearrangement
and NPM1 mutation { 23530613 ; 28574302 ; 31682773 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Involved organs are commonly enlarged, have a fish-flesh cut surface, and may appear green when
the neoplastic cells exhibit granulocytic maturation and express myeloperoxidase.
Histopathology
Myeloid sarcoma forms a mass that diffusely replaces tissue architecture. A single file pattern is
common, particularly for cases with monocytic differentiation. Mitotic figures are often numerous.
The neoplastic cells have thin nuclear membranes, fine (blastic) chromatin, and usually pinpoint
nucleoli. Tumours with granulocytic differentiation often show a subset of partially mature neoplastic
cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm (e.g. eosinophilic myelocytes and metamyelocytes). Tumours with
monocytic differentiation often have a subset of cells with folded nuclei (e.g. promonocytes).
Immunophenotypic analysis using flow cytometry and/or immunohistochemistry shows that the
neoplastic cells are often positive for CD11c, CD13, CD33, CD34 (often absent in immature
monocytes),
CD43,
CD68,
CD117
(KIT),
myeloperoxidase
and
lysozyme
{ 17170724 ; 23530613 ; 26185307 }. CD4, CD19 (cases with t(8;21)), CD56, CD99, CD123,
CD163 (more mature neoplastic cells) and TdT (often weak or only in a subset of cells) can be
expressed in subsets of cases { 17170724 ; 26185307 }.
Cytology
Wright-Giemsa or Diff-Quik touch imprints or smears highlight cytoplasmic granules in maturing
neoplastic cells { 31051716 }. Auer rods, or morphologic features of myelodysplasia in the
background may be identified. Unstained touch imprints can be assessed by
traditional cytochemistry methods for myeloperoxidase and esterases.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Detection of myeloid sarcoma is equivalent to a diagnosis of AML { 26185307 ; 31682773 }.
Comprehensive evaluation that includes conventional cytogenetic analysis and molecular
assessment for translocations and gene mutations is needed for correct classification and planning
therapy.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: effacement of tissue architecture by a mass composed of myeloid blasts, with or without
maturing elements, that are positive for multiple granulocytic and/or monocytic markers.
Desirable: cytogenetic and molecular analysis (essential for de novo myeloid sarcoma).
Staging

948

Evaluation of peripheral blood and bone marrow for AML or other myeloid neoplasms (e.g. MDS,
MPN, MDS/MPN) is mandatory.
Prognosis and prediction
Patients with myeloid sarcoma require AML-type therapy, guided by targets identified by
cytogenetic and molecular analysis. Prognosis is similar to AML patients without evidence of
myeloid sarcoma { 29043233 }. Patients who present with isolated myeloid sarcoma appear to
have a better prognosis { 18623376 ; 25213180 }. Long-term remission has been achieved in a
subset of patients { 29043233 }.

L#24806
Myeloid sarcoma involving gingiva
Myeloid sarcoma with monocytic differentiation infiltrating the gingiva. In many areas the
neoplastic cells exhibit a single file pattern.
R#24807
Myeloid sarcoma involving gingiva
This myeloid sarcoma shows monocytic differentiation; the neoplastic cells are strongly
positive for CD68.

L#24808
Myeloid sarcoma involving orbital fat
The neoplastic cells have thin nuclear membranes and immature chromatin. Rare
eosinophilic metamyelocytes are present in this field.
R#24809
Myeloid sarcoma involving orbital fat
The neoplastic cells are positive for myeloperoxidase supporting granulocytic
differentiation.

949

B cell lymphomas

Extranodal marginal zone lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue
(MALT lymphoma)
Definition
Extranodal marginal zone lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT lymphoma) is
an indolent mature B-cell neoplasm with architectural and cytological features similar to reactive
mucosa-associated lymphoid tissues occurring in certain extranodal sites.
ICD-O coding
9699/3 Extranodal marginal zone lymphoma
ICD-11 coding
2A85.3 Extranodal marginal zone B-cell lymphoma, primary site excluding stomach or skin
Related terminology
Acceptable: MALT lymphoma, extranodal marginal zone lymphoma (EMZL)
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Head and neck MALT lymphoma most frequently affects ocular adnexa (60%) and salivary glands
(30-40%), and less commonly, thyroid (2%) { 31757436 }. Involvement of Waldeyer's ring
{ 12712477 ; 18234544 }, oral cavity { 18158571 ; 17604660 }, larynx { 33198051 }, and sinonasal
tract { 26094364 ; 16320028 ; 19378323 ; 10555004 } is rare.
Clinical features
Most patients present with painless enlarging masses. Salivary gland lesions occasionally cause
pain or facial nerve paralysis. Thyroid MALT lymphomas present with a neck mass which can cause
dysphagia, dyspnea or hoarseness { 31584614 }. Laryngeal tumors may cause hoarseness and
cough { 33198051 ; 25280040 }. B symptoms are rare.
Epidemiology
The head and neck region is the second most common primary site for MALT lymphoma, after the
gastrointestinal tract. Most patients are middle-aged and older adults (median age, 7th decade),
with a slight female preponderance among ocular adnexal lymphoma cases { 17255761 }. Salivary
gland MALT lymphoma and thyroid MALT lymphoma have a female predominance (male:female
ratio, 1:2 to 1:3){ 24417667 }, due to associations with Sjögren syndrome
{ 24733777 ; 21075560 ; 21867583 } and Hashimoto thyroiditis { 31584614 }, respectively. The
incidence rate of ocular adnexa and salivary gland MALT lymphoma is 1.4 and 0.9-1.0 per
1,000,000 person-years in the United States, respectively; it is lower for other head-and-neck sites
{ 24417667 }.
Etiology
MALT lymphoma often arises in association with chronic antigenic stimulation related to infection
or autoimmune disorder. Patients with Sjögren syndrome have a 5-20 fold increased risk of
developing lymphoma { 25316606 ; 18984412 }. Hepatitis C infection may be a predisposing factor
in some salivary gland { 17071937 } and ocular adnexal MALT lymphomas, especially in Italy
{ 32088088 }. Chlamydia psittaci is reported as a possible etiologic agent in some series of ocular
adnexal lymphoma { 15100336 }, but not in others { 17454602 }. Small numbers of MALT
lymphoma
cases
arise
in
association
with
IgG4-related
disease
{ 18580683 ; 11717546 ; 29285637 }. Hashimoto thyroiditis is associated with a 40-80 fold
increased risk of thyroid lymphoma { 10800981 }. Occasional laryngeal MALT lymphomas occur in
patients with autoimmune disease { 25280040 }. Rare MALT lymphomas arise in iatrogenically
immunosuppressed patients { 29957733 }.
Pathogenesis
Chronic inflammation leads to development of clonal B-cell populations that may acquire additional
genetic
abnormalities
and
progress
to
overt
lymphoma
{ 20408860 ; 25316606 ; 29674500 ; 30065018 }.
Immunoglobulin heavy (IGH) and light (IGL) chain genes are clonally rearranged, and show
mutated variable regions. Genetic changes vary by site; they result in NFκB activation. Trisomy 3
and 18 are common in head and neck MALT lymphomas { 17052360 }. A subset of cases shows
t(14;18)(q32;q21)(IGH/MALT1). t(11;18)(q21;q21)(BIRC3/MALT1) is found in MALT lymphomas in

950

some sites, but is rare in head and neck MALT lymphomas { 15356642 ; 18158571 }.
Approximately half of thyroidal MALT lymphomas harbor t(3;14)(p14.1;q32)/FOXP1-IGH, but this
translocation is rare to absent outside the thyroid. Inactivating mutations, deletions and promoter
hypermethylation of TNFAIP3 (A20) (negative regulator of NFκB) are described, mainly in
translocation-negative
MALT
lymphomas
{ 19006194 ; 19907439 }.
Mutated TBL1XR1 and GPR34 are
common
in
salivary MALT
lymphomas; TET2, TNFRSF14 and PIK3CD are commonly mutated in thyroid MALT lymphomas
{ 29674500 ; 30065018 }. MYD88 L265P mutation is rare in MALT lymphoma and should raise the
possibility of lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma, particularly in cases with marrow involvement and
serum M-component { 22944768 ; 22808296 ; 28682481 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Tumors are non-circumscribed, firm and tan-colored. Interspersed cysts formed by dilated ducts
are common in salivary gland MALT lymphoma.
Histopathology
MALT lymphomas are composed of small lymphocytes and marginal zone cells with small-tomedium-sized, slightly irregular nuclei, smooth chromatin and pale cytoplasm { 31912792 }.
Monocytoid cells with abundant pale cytoplasm are sometimes present and are conspicuous in
salivary gland MALT lymphomas. Occasional large lymphoid cells are interspersed. Plasmacytic
differentiation occurs in one-third of cases, sometimes with Dutcher bodies, and infrequently with
amyloid deposition { 22180478 ; 18158571 }. Remnants of reactive follicles are common,
sometimes with follicular colonization. Well-formed lymphoepithelial lesions are commonly found in
salivary gland and thyroid MALT lymphomas, and occasionally in lacrimal gland MALT lymphomas.
In Waldeyer's ring, a diagnosis of MALT lymphoma may be difficult to establish because crypt
epithelium is normally heavily infiltrated by lymphoid cells.
Neoplastic cells express B-lineage markers (CD20, CD22, PAX5), usually IgM, occasionally IgG or
IgA, and rarely IgD. They are typically negative for CD5, CD10, BCL6, CD23, cyclin D1, SOX11,
and EBV (except for rare EBV+ cases in immunosuppressed patients) { 29957733 }. IRTA1, a
marker of marginal zone cell differentiation, is positive in the majority of cases { 22716304 }.
Expression of CD43 or TBX21 (T-bet) on B-cells, demonstration of immunoglobulin light chain
monotypia by B cells (usually by flow cytometry) or, in cases with plasmacytic differentiation, in
plasma cells, can aid in the diagnosis of lymphoma. Salivary gland MALT lymphomas frequently
express stereotypic B-cell receptors { 32182401 ; 28682481 }, often with rheumatoid factor activity
{ 32182401 }. A small subset of MALT lymphomas (<4%) that express CD5 may be associated with
more
widespread
disease
and
more
aggressive
behavior
{ 17255761 ; 11697516 ; 22406370 }. Foci of solid or sheet-like growth of large B-cells,
unassociated with follicular dendritic cell meshworks, represent progression to diffuse large B-cell
lymphoma.
Differential diagnosis includes other B-cell lymphomas and reactive hyperplasia, including atypical
marginal zone hyperplasia (see section 9.0.1.1){ 15256428 }.
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration biopsy, when combined with flow cytometry, can be a valuable tool in
establishing a diagnosis.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Immunoglobulin heavy (IGH) chain genes are clonally rearranged.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Architectural effacement or distortion by a small B-cell proliferation lacking CD5 or CD10
expression.
Desirable:
Demonstration of monotypic lymphoid and/or plasma cells (or clonally-rearranged immunoglobulin
genes), lymphoepithelial lesions, and remnants of underlying reactive lymphoid follicles.
Presence of lymphoepithelial sialadenitis (LESA) and/or Sjögren syndrome in salivary gland cases,
and of Hashimoto thyroiditis in thyroid cases.
MYD88 wild-type.
Staging
Most lymphomas are localized at presentation { 24521680 }. Lymph nodes and other extranodal
sites may be involved { 31733094 ; 17255761 }.

951

Lymphoid neoplasms are staged according to the Lugano classification, which has been adopted
by the eighth edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
Spread to lymph nodes and other MALT sites is not uncommon, but death due to lymphoma is rare
{ 21075560 ; 24733777 }. Ferry JA, Fung CY, Lucarelli MJ et al. Ocular adnexal lymphoma: Longterm outcome, patterns of failure and prognostic factors in 174 patients. J Hematopathol
2021;14:41-52} Prognosis is more favorable with localized disease and low-risk MALT Lymphoma
International Prognostic Index (MALT-IPI) score { 31733094 }. Large cell transformation is
associated with more aggressive behavior { 10577857 }.

L#24505 MALT lymphoma, parotid gland

L#24511

Much of the tissue is replaced by an interfollicular, vaguely nodular and diffuse

the image.
R#24512
MALT lymphoma, tongue
The marginal zone B cells are diffusely positive for IRTA1.

#27178
Salivary MALT-type lymphoma
There are numerous lymphoepithelial islands

mononuclear cells, characteristic of a MALT-type
lymphoma.

Mantle cell lymphoma
Definition
Mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) is a B-cell neoplasm usually composed of monomorphic small
irregular lymphoid cells that express monotypic immunoglobulin, B-cell antigens and usually CD5.
Over 95% of cases carry t(11;14)(q13;q32) involving IGH and CCND1 resulting in cyclin D1
overexpression.
ICD-O coding
9673/3 Mantle cell lymphoma
ICD-11 coding
2A85.5 Mantle cell lymphoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: Centrocytic lymphoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
MCL most often involves lymph nodes, but extranodal involvement occurs commonly. The head
and neck region is the second most common extranodal site involved by MCL, in about 2-4% of
patients { 23341329 }. Waldeyer ring, most often the tonsils, is the most frequent site, but MCL also

954

involves the nasopharynx, orbital region, salivary glands and skin of the head and neck
{ 24851070 ; 34137303 }. About 40% of patients with head and neck involvement have stage I or
II disease { 23341329 }.
Clinical features
Patients may present with pain or difficulty in swallowing due to a mass. Orbital and skin lesions
may present with a nodule.
Epidemiology
The incidence of MCL is 0.5 cases per 100,000 person-years { 21945518 }. MCL is most common
in older adults with a median age in the seventh decade. The male-to-female ratio is 2-3 to 1
{ 18615506 ; 21945518 ; 24763513 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
The t(11;14)(q13;q32) resulting in cyclin D1 overexpression is thought to be a founding or early
event { 33783838 }. Cyclin D1 drives the cell cycle at the G1 to S phase transition. Rare cyclin D1negative
cases
may
have CCND2 or
rarely CCND3 translocations,
partnered
with IGH, IGK or IGL { 23255553 ; 30538135 }. Conventional cytogenetic analysis has shown
many additional abnormalities and a complex karyotype in most MCL cases { 11764078 }. MCL
also shows numerous copy number changes { 32584970 }. MYC rearrangements occur in a small
subset of cases and are likely late events in pathogenesis { 32273477 ; 33528622 }. The most
common gene mutations in MCL involve ATM (40-75%), TP53 (20-40%), CDKN2A (2030%), KMT2D (15%) and NOTCH1/2 (5-15%) { 32598477 }. In sum, these many abnormalities
impair the cell cycle, apoptosis and DNA repair { 33783838 }.
Macroscopic appearance
MCL has a fish-flesh cut surface.
Histopathology
MCL most often shows a diffuse pattern (~75%), but vaguely nodular (~25%) and rarely pure
mantle zone (~1%) patterns occur { 16173960 }. In conventional MCL, the lymphoma cells are
uniform and small with irregular nuclear contours. Intermixed single epithelioid histiocytes are seen
in about 75% of cases, and “naked” germinal centers can be present. Aggressive variants of MCL
represent 15-20% of cases. Blastoid variants are composed of intermediate-size cells with
immature chromatin and a high mitotic rate mimicking lymphoblasts. Pleomorphic variants are
composed of intermediate-size and large cells with more prominent nucleoli mimicking, in part,
large cell lymphoma.
The neoplastic B cells of MCL are typically positive for surface immunoglobulin, pan B-cell antigens,
CD5, BCL-2, cyclin D1 and SOX11, and negative for CD10, CD23, CD200, BCL-6, and LEF-1
{ 16173960 ; 31054894 }. A small minority of cases lack CD5, or express CD10 and/or BCL6
{ 33528622 }. A few cases lack expression of cyclin D1 or SOX11, but not both
{ 16123218 ; 17934069 }. Cyclin D1-negative MCL cases have a similar immunophenotype,
including SOX11 expression { 23255553 }.
Cytology
In conventional cases, smears show monomorphic small lymphoid cells often associated with
epithelioid histiocytes.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
CCND1::IGH rearrangment, t(11;14)(q13;q32), is characteristic of MCL and is found in >95% of
cases.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Typical morphologic features with consistent immunophenotype, including expression of
cyclin D1 and/or SOX11.
Desirable: t(11;14)(q13;q32) CCND1::IGH, CCND2 or CCND3 rearrangement.
Staging
Lymphomas are staged according to the Lugano classification, which has been adopted by the
8th edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification { 25113753 }.
Prognosis and prediction
The MCL International Prognostic Index (MIPI) is used to predict prognosis { 26926679 }. The
tumour proliferation rate, most easily assessed by Ki-67, is the most important pathologic predictor
of prognosis and is often added to the MIPI { 26926679 ; 33783838 }

955

L#28754
Mantle cell lymphoma involving tonsil
The lymphoma has a nodular pattern, mimicking follicular lymphoma. Note intact but
compressed crypts lined by squamous epithelium.
R#28755
Mantle cell lymphoma involving tonsil
Neoplastic cells are small to medium-sized and have round to irregular nuclei, coarse
chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli and scant cytoplasm. Mitotic figures are readily
identified.

L#28757
Mantle cell lymphoma involving tonsil
The nodules are occupied by densely packed CD20+ B cells.
R#28758
Mantle cell lymphoma involving tonsil
Immunohistochemical analysis shows that the neoplastic cells are positive for BCL-2.

956

L#28759
Mantle cell lymphoma involving tonsil
Immunohistochemical analysis shows that the neoplastic cells are positive for cyclin D1.
R#28760
Mantle cell lymphoma involving tonsil
Immunohistochemical analysis shows that the neoplastic cells are negative for BCL-6.

#27218
Mantle cell lymphoma
There is an intact squamous epithelium over
an atypical lymphoid proliferation, showing a
sheet-like distribution of lymphoid cells with
ample cytoplasm and elongated nuclei. A
monocytoid appearance is focally noted.
Mitoses are easily identified.

Follicular lymphoma
Definition
Follicular lymphoma (FL) is a neoplasm composed of a mixture of centrocytes and centroblasts
that usually has a follicular growth pattern (at least a partially).
ICD-O coding
9690/3 Follicular lymphoma
ICD-11 coding
2A80.Z Follicular lymphoma, unspecified
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Typical FL in the head and neck region is most often seen in older individuals and usually involves
cervical lymph nodes and Waldeyer ring { 9704731 }. Rarely, FL can involve salivary glands, the
ocular adnexal region and other locations { 9704731 ; 11583402 ; 17786716 ; 16984613 }.

957

Clinical features
Most patients present with painless enlarging lymph nodes or masses (see table #26080).
Epidemiology
FL accounts for about 20-30% of all lymphomas { 32586570 } and the incidence of this disease has
increased in recent years { 31548815 }. The highest incidence rates are reported in the USA and
Western Europe; the incidence is lower in developing countries compared with developed countries
{ 27354024 }. FL affects predominantly adults, with a median age of 65 years. The incidence is
similar in men and women. FL is 2-3 times more common in White populations as compared with
Black populations { 32586570 }.
Etiology
Lifestyle and occupational risk factors have been associated with higher risk of FL { 25174024 }.
Pathogenesis
FL is thought to derive from FL-like B-cells that undergo the t(14;18)(q32;q21) BCL2 rearrangement
in the bone marrow and circulate in the blood { 17043145 }. These circulating B cells are long-lived
due to BCL2 overexpression. The circulating t(14;18) BCL2 rearranged B-cells undergo several
passages through the germinal center, where they acquire additional genetic hits via somatic
hypermutation eventually leading to FL { 25384217 }. Localized and systemic FL differ in their gene
expression profiles { 31697802 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Involved tissues have a fish-flesh appearance which may be vaguely nodular.
Histopathology
Typical FL cases show numerous and crowded follicles of uniform size consisting of centrocytes
and centroblasts. These follicles lack a starry sky pattern or polarization. Rare cases exhibit a
predominantly diffuse pattern of growth but these are usually encountered in inguinal lymph nodes.
FL are graded according to the number of large transformed cells (centroblasts) per area by
counting at least ten different neoplastic follicles at high power [[LYM4+]]. In grades 1 and 2 FL
cases, the number of centroblasts is <90 per mm2 (<150 per 10 high power fields, where each field
is 0.159 mm2 in area). Cases with more than 90 centroblasts per mm2 are grade 3A, if centrocytes
are still present. Purely follicular FL 3B cases consist exclusively of centroblasts and are rare. If a
diffuse growth pattern is present in addition, these cases should be classified as diffuse large Bcell lymphoma (DLBCL) with grade 3B follicular component.
As a rule, the tumour cells are positive for CD20 and germinal centre markers such as CD10, BCL6,
LMO2 and HGAL. BCL2 is expressed in 85% of FL 1/2 and 60% of FL3A. FL3B is more often
negative for CD10 and BCL2 and may co-express MUM1. The proliferation index in typical FL
grades 1 and 2 is low, but FL3 may show high Ki67 indices up to 70-100% { 11986240 }.
See table for differential diagnosis #26080.
Cytology
Smears show a mixture of centrocytes and centroblasts. Ancillary studies can be performed on
FNA fluid specimens or cell blocks.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
FL involving head and neck lymph nodes or tonsils in systemic disease harbours the
t(14;18)(q32;q21) in 85% of cases and has a characteristic mutation pattern involving genes such
as KMT2D, TNFRSF14, CREBBP, and EZH2. In FL 3A, the frequency of the t(14;18) is lower
(60%). BCL6 may be rearranged in BCL2 translocation positive or negative cases. FL 3B is often
negative for the BCL2 gene translocation but may show BCL6 rearrangement { 21659362 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Presence of an atypical lymphoid proliferation of follicular center cells with a matching
immunophenotype and/or genotype.
Desirable: Follicular pattern
Staging
The Lugano system is used to stage FL { 25113753 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Stage and the FL International Prognostic Index (FLIPI) are major prognostic determinants. More
recently, gene expression profiling and the establishment of clinicogenetic risk models have proved
useful in the prognostication of patients { 29475724 ; 26256760 }.

958

Tumour grade has some prognostic impact. FL grades 1, 2 and 3A are clinically indolent whereas
FL grade 3B often exhibits more aggressive behaviour and usually requires DLBCL-like
chemotherapy.

961

Paediatric-type follicular lymphoma
Definition
Pediatric-type follicular lymphoma (PTFL) is a neoplasm that usually occurs in children and young
adults, most often involving head and neck lymph nodes, and which has distinctive morphologic,
immunophenotypic and genetic features. Cases of PTFL genetically differ from common follicular
lymphoma in that they have a high frequency of MAPK pathway gene alterations and
lack BCL2 rearrangements.
ICD-O coding
9690/3 Paediatric-type follicular lymphoma
ICD-11 coding
2A80.4 Paediatric type follicular lymphoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Usually, lymph nodes of the head and neck region are involved { 22855608 }. Lymph nodes from
other non-head and neck sites can be involved, but much less often { 27325104 ; 31686194 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with isolated, asymptomatic lymphadenopathy. As currently defined, disseminated
disease excludes a diagnosis of PTFL.
Epidemiology
Patients are usually children or young adults with a median age in the second decade. There is a
marked male predominance of > 10:1. PTFL can occur in adults.
Etiology
There are no known risk factors
Pathogenesis
Mutations in MAPK pathway genes occur in about 50% of cases; usually MAP2K1 but
uncommonly MAPK1 or RRAS { 27325104 ; 28533310 }. TNFRSF14 is mutated in ~50% of cases,
and IRF8 p.K66R occurs in about 15% of cases and may be specific for PTFL { 28533310 }.
Mutations of chromatin modifying genes usually present in common FL, such
as KMT2D, CREBB and EZH2, are infrequent in PTFL { 27325104 ; 28533310 }. PFTL
lack BCL2, BCL6, MYC, or IRF4 rearrangements (22855608; 27325104 ; 28533310 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The involved nodes show a fish-flesh cut surface.
Histopathology
Lymph node architecture is partially or completely replaced by large expansile follicles of lymphoma
that may be round or serpiginous { 22855608 ; 27325104 ; 31686194 }. These follicles often have
a prominent starry sky pattern and may be surrounded by under-developed mantle zones, or mantle
zones may be absent. The follicles are usually composed of intermediate-size cells with blastoid
features and multiple nucleoli. A subset of PTFL cases have typical centroblasts. Mitotic figures are
usually numerous. As currently defined, areas of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma exclude a diagnosis
of PTFL.
Flow cytometry immunophenotypic studies show a monotypic B-cell population, CD10 positive and
negative for CD5. Immunohistochemical analysis shows that these neoplasms express pan B-cell
markers (e.g. CD20, PAX-5) and germinal center B-cell associated markers including CD10, BCL6, LMO2, stathmin and LLT-1, and are negative for T-cell antigens { 22855608 ; 31686194 }.
FOXP1 is positive in most cases and has been proposed as a useful marker to distinguish PTFL
from reactive follicular hyperplasia { 31686194 }. BCL-2 is negative or weakly and variably positive
in 40-50% of PTFL cases. Ki-67 is usually above 30%.
Cytology
Smears show sheets of intermediate-size cells with blastoid nuclear features and/or centroblasts.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
PTFL cases carry clonal immunoglobulin heavy chain and light chain rearrangements, and no
evidence of clonal T-cell receptor gene rearrangements.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

962

Essential: Follicular pattern, expression of germinal center B-cell antigens, absence of diffuse large
B-cell lymphoma or disseminated disease; monotypic B-cell population by flow cytometry or
monoclonal immunoglobulin gene rearrangement shown by PCR analysis.
Desirable: MAPK pathway gene mutations (usually MAP2K1) and TNFRSF14 mutations.
Staging
The Lugano system is used to stage lymphomas and has been adopted by the Union for
International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification { 25113753 }. Patients with PTFL present
with localized disease.
Prognosis and prediction
Most patients achieve complete remission following surgical excision of the involved lymph node
and additional therapy is not required.

#26074
Paediatric-type follicular lymphoma
The atypical follicles are large and expanded, often serpiginous and with iregular
contours. A starry sky pattern is often preserved.

#26077
Paediatric-type follicular lymphoma
The neoplastic follicles are negative for BCL2.

963

Large B-cell lymphoma with IRF4 rearrangement
Definition
Large B-cell lymphoma with IRF4 rearrangement (LBCL-IRF4) is a de novo large B-cell lymphoma
with a follicular, follicular and diffuse or purely diffuse growth pattern characterized by strong
expression of IRF4/MUM1 and, in most cases, associated with an IRF4 rearrangement to an
immunoglobulin gene partner (IGH, IGK, IGL).
ICD-O coding
9698/3 Large B-cell lymphoma with IRF4 rearrangement
ICD-11 coding
2A81.Y & XH6SU8 Other specified diffuse large B-cell lymphomas & Large B-cell lymphoma with
IRF4 rearrangement
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Most cases of LBCL-IRF4 arise in Waldeyer ring or head and neck lymph nodes. Less commonly,
the neoplasm can present in the gastrointestinal tract.
Clinical features
Most patients present with localized lymph node or tonsillar enlargement (stage I/II) in the head
and neck region. Systemic (“B”) symptoms are present in a minority of patients { 32239695 }.
Epidemiology
LBCL-IRF4 is rare. Although there is a wide age range from 4 to 79 years, the median age at
presentation is in the 2nd decade, and LBCL-IRF4 is more frequently encountered in patients aged
<18 years. Males and females are affected almost equally { 21487109 ; 32239695 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
A translocation involving IRF4, usually partnered with IGH, is the hallmark of LBCL-IRF4. LBCLIRF4 has a gene expression profile distinct from diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of activated B-cell
or germinal centre B-cell types. There is overexpression of both IRF4 and BCL6, as well as NFkB
target
genes,
indicating
disruption
of BCL6-IRF4-PRDM1 pathways
{ 21487109 ; 31738823 }. Recurrent mutations of BCL6, usually in the IRF4-binding site,
and IRF4 mutations, as well as mutations of NFkB-related genes such as CARD11,
CD79B and MYD88 are found { 21487109 ; 31738823 }. Copy number alterations include loss of
17p13/TP53 and gains of chromosomes 7 and 11q { 23073988 }.
Macroscopic appearance
These lymphomas produce a firm fleshy homogeneous mass.
Histopathology
LBCL-IRF4 may present with a follicular, follicular and diffuse, or entirely diffuse growth pattern;
most cases are at least partially diffuse. When follicular, the neoplastic follicles are large, expansile,
devoid of mantle zones and closely packed. The neoplastic lymphoid cells have centroblastic
morphology or, less frequently, are of intermediate-size with blastoid morphology. Mitotic figures
are often infrequent and tingible body macrophages are usually lacking { 32452165 }.
The neoplastic cells are mature B-cells, positive for CD20, CD79a and PAX5. BCL6 is positive,
IRF4/MUM1 is strongly expressed and staining for BLIMP1/PRDM1 is negative
{ 21487109 }. Positivity for CD10 and BCL2 is variable but usually present in about half of cases
{ 21487109 }. Approximately 30% of LBCL-IRF4 are positive for CD5 { 31738823 }. These
neoplasms usually have a high proliferation index.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Conventional
cytogenetic
analysis
shows
t(6;14)(p25.3;q32)/IGH-IRF4 or
possibly IGK (chromosome 2p12) or IGL (chromosome 22q11) partners. IRF4 rearrangements
can be identified by FISH in most, but not all cases. In 10% of cases, the rearrangement is cryptic
and undetectable using commercially available break apart FISH probes { 31738823 }.

964

Rearrangements involving BCL6 may be identified but not BCL2 or MYC rearrangements
{ 21487109 ; 31738823 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Large B-cell lymphoma with mature B-cell immunophenotype and strong uniform expression of
both BCL6 and IRF4. IRF4 rearrangement by FISH If IRF4 rearrangement cannot be
demonstrated, the diagnosis can be established only if typical pathological features are present in
the appropriate clinical setting (children or young adults, localized disease, head and neck or
intestinal region)
Desirable:
Absence of BCL2 and MYC gene rearrangements.
Staging
The Lugano system for staging of lymphomas is used { 25113753 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Patients are treated with combination immunochemotherapy, plus or minus local radiotherapy, with
a favourable outcome { 21487109 ; 32239695 }. A small number of patients have been reported
who had tonsillar LBCL-IRF4 with purely follicular disease and tonsillectomy alone resulted in
complete and sustained remission. Currently, however, there is insufficient evidence for deescalation of treatment for patients with this disease { 23108024 }.

This lymphoma shows partly follicular and partly diffuse growth pattern.

966

Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
Definition
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma not otherwise specified (DLBCL) is a diffuse proliferation of
neoplastic large B lymphoid cells.
ICD-O coding
9680/3 Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma NOS
ICD-11 coding
2A81.Z Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma NOS
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
About 60% of head and neck DLBCL cases involve lymph nodes; many of these patients have
systemic disease. About 40% of DLBCL cases are localized to head and neck, with nearly even
split in terms of lymph node and extranodal involvement. Any extranodal site may be
involved. Waldeyer ring, particularly palatine tonsil, is the most common site
{ 26113048 ; 28772206 }. DLBCL is the most common type of lymphoma involving the head and
neck region { 22700993 ; 26118762 ; 28772206 }.
Clinical features
Patients with DLBCL at any site often present with a mass. Site-specific symptoms include altered
vision, sinus or nasal cavity obstruction (often mimicking upper respiratory infection),
oropharyngeal ulcer with or without pain or bleeding, enlarged tonsil(s) or hoarseness. A subset of
patients has B-type symptoms.
Epidemiology
Lymphomas represent about 5-10% of all malignant neoplasms that involve the head and neck,
and DLBCL is the most common type of lymphoma. DLBCL can involve nodal or
extranodal sites. Waldeyer ring, particularly palatine tonsil, is the most common site
{ 26113048 ; 28772206 }. Most patients are in the sixth or seventh decade of life, and the male-tofemale ratio in most studies is 1.2-1.6 to 1 { 28772206 }. However, DLBCL involving the thyroid
gland is more common in women { 17429099 ; 24273125 }.
Etiology
The etiology is largely unknown. In salivary and thyroid glands, DLBCL is often preceded by
extranodal marginal zone lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue, associated with the
autoimmune
diseases
Sjogren syndrome
and
Hashimoto
thyroiditis,
respectively
{ 22367111 ; 32153241 }.
Pathogenesis
DLBCL is biologically heterogeneous, and gene expression profiling can divide cases into
germinal-centre B-cell (GCB), activated B-cell (ABC) and unclassified subtypes. More recent
studies of DLBCL using high throughput methods to assess gene expression, copy number
alterations and gene mutations have shown activation of various cellular pathways { 31648986 }
and several genetic subtypes of DLBCL have been proposed { 32289277 ; 33249557 }. Molecular
studies focused on DLBCL of the head and neck are few. Translocations that occur in
nodal DLBCL, such as those involving MYC, BCL2 and BCL6, also occur in head and neck
DLBCL, more commonly in nodal cases.
Macroscopic appearance
DLBCL often forms a soft fish-flesh mass. Necrosis may be visible.
Histopathology
Head and neck DLBCL is composed of centroblasts or, less often, immunoblasts arranged in a
diffuse pattern. Some aggressive neoplasms exhibit a starry-sky pattern. Mitotic figures are
common. Coagulative necrosis may be present.
Immunophenotypic studies show that DLBCL cells are positive for pan B-cell antigens and negative
for pan T-cell antigens. Ki-67 often shows a high proliferation index ranging from 60-90%. About
50-75% of cases are of non-GCB type, usually positive for MUM1/IRF4 and FOXP1. About 25-50%
of cases are of GCB type usually positive for CD10, BCL-6, LMO2 or GCET1
{ 22700993 ; 28772206 }. In situ hybridization for Epstein-Barr virus encoded RNA (EBER) is

967

negative. Large B-cell lymphoma with IRF4 rearrangement should be considered, especially if the
lymphoma cells are positive for MUM1/IRF4 and CD10.
Cytology
Cytologic preparations typically show sheets of large, discohesive cells with lymphoid morphology
that may be associated with necrosis.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular studies are not usually required for diagnosis. Monoclonal immunoglobulin gene
rearrangements are usually present, and T-cell receptor genes are germline.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: head and neck region; diffuse proliferation of large malignant lymphoid cells; B-cell
lineage.
Staging
The Lugano system is used to stage lymphomas and has been adopted by the Union for
International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification { 25113753 }. Most patients with
extranodal DLBCL of the head and neck present with stage IE disease; some patients have
involvement of regional lymph nodes (stage II).
Prognosis and prediction
Patients are treated with systemic immunochemotherapy, often with involved-field radiotherapy
{ 17429099 ; 31793408 }. Clinical stage and the International Prognostic Index are useful for
assessing prognosis. Patients with extranodal DLBCL of the head and neck have a better
prognosis than patients with solely nodal disease or those with extranodal DLBCL at other sites
{ 24273125 ; 29861074 }.

nasopharynx
The neoplasm diffusely replaces the
mucosa. Normal epithelium is
present at the top of the field.

#25558

lymphoma of the
nasopharynx

some centrocytes replace

#25559
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the nasopharynx
The neoplasm is strongly positive for CD20 supporting B-cell lineage.

#25560
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the thyroid gland
The neoplasm diffusely replaces the parenchyma. One residual thyroid follicle can be
appreciated in this field.

#25561
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of tonsil
The neoplasm diffusely replaces the tonsillar parenchyma. Squamous epithelium from the
surface of the tonsil is also present in the field.

#25562
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the tonsil
Oil magnification showing the centroblastic features of the lymphoma cells. Histiocytes
and mitotic figures are also present in the field.

#25563
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the tonsil.
The lymphoma cells are strongly positive for MUM1/IRF4 and CD10 was negative (not
shown) supporting a non-germinal center B-cell immunophenotype.

Burkitt lymphoma
Definition
Burkitt lymphoma (BL) is an aggressive B-cell neoplasm composed of uniform, intermediate-size
cells with multiple small nucleoli, basophilic cytoplasm, high proliferation index, and a high (9095%) frequency of MYC translocations. Currently there is no gold standard for diagnosis, and a
multiparametric approach to diagnosis is needed.
ICD-O coding
9687/3 Burkitt lymphoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2A85.6 & XH3FE9 Burkitt lymphoma, including Burkitt leukaemia & Mature B-cell lymphomas
Related terminology
Acceptable: Burkitt cell leukaemia (9826/3)
Subtype(s)
Endemic BL; sporadic BL; immunodeficiency-associated BL
Localization
In endemic BL, jaws and other facial bones are involved in 50-70% of patients
{ 2358947 ; 31115699 }. Sporadic BL can involve the orbit, Waldeyer ring, gingiva and the thyroid
gland { 12560151 ; 27257042 }. Immunodeficiency-associated BL most often involves lymph
nodes, and uncommonly involves the parotid or thyroid glands. Sporadic and immunodeficiencyassociated BL uncommonly involve facial bones. Patients with all BL subtypes are at high-risk for
involvement of the central nervous system. Sporadic and immunodeficiency-associated BL patients
can present with acute leukaemia; this occurrence is rare in endemic BL patients.
Clinical features
Patients with BL commonly present with extranodal disease, a high tumour burden and a short
duration of symptoms { 31229156 ; 31115699 ; 32144513 }. BL can destroy bones (shown by

971

imaging studies) and compress nearby structures. Site-specific symptoms include altered vision,
nasal obstruction, enlarged tonsil(s), dental/jaw pain often with loss of teeth, thyroid gland mass
{ 2358947 ; 31115699 ; 32144513 }. BL patients uncommonly present with lymphadenopathy;
lymph node involvement is most common in immunodeficiency-associated BL patients.
Generalized B-type symptoms occur in a subset of patients.
Epidemiology
The incidence of endemic BL is 30-60 cases per million persons { 22260300 ; 2374749 }. Most
patients are children, with a median age between 4 and 7 years. Boys are affected more often then
girls, in a 3 to 1 ratio. Sporadic BL occurs in industrialized nations and is much less common than
endemic BL, with an incidence of 1-3 cases per million persons { 22260300 ; 2374749 }. The maleto-female ratio is 3-4 to 1. Patients with sporadic BL are usually younger adults, with a median age
of 25-40 years. The incidence of immunodeficiency-associated BL is 200-250 cases per million
{ 22260300 ; 32735838 }. The male to female ratio is 4-5 to 1. Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)
infection is the most common cause of immunodeficiency in BL patients { 32735838 }.
Etiology
Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) is present in >95% of endemic BL cases { 15685227 ; 22260300 }. These
neoplasms
are
also
linked
to
holoendemic
malaria
implicating
a
role
for Plasmodium falciparum infection { 26276629 } . Other infectious agents (e.g. HIV, Arboviruses)
may play a role via chronic antigenic stimulation and extrinsic activation of B-cell receptor signaling
{ 26712879 }. Germline DNA genetic variants in TCF4 and CHD8 have been identified in endemic
BL patients in East Africa { 33051549 }. Using traditional detection methods such as in situ
hybridization for EBV small encoded RNA (EBER), EBV can be detected in 20-30% of sporadic
and 20-40% of immunodeficiency-associated BL cases (see below). Using more sensitive
techniques, however, up to 67% of sporadic and immunodeficiency-associated BL cases may be
positive for EBV { 32483241 }.
Pathogenesis
All subtypes of BL carry MYC translocations which likely occur as mistakes in immunoglobulin (Ig)
heavy chain switching or Ig variable region gene somatic hypermutation { 26712879 }. EBV may
be essential early in BL pathogenesis by allowing B-cells with genetic defects to evade apoptosis,
but the virus may be subsequently lost (“hit and run”) with the acquisition of stable (epi)genetic
changes by the lymphoma cells. The presence of ID3 and TCF4 mutations in BL result in tonic BCR
signaling { 24492847 }. These mutations are nearly mutually exclusive with EBV infection
suggesting a dual mechanism of BL pathogenesis: mutational versus viral driven { 26468873 }.
Gene expression and microRNA profiling have shown similarities between BL subtypes and have
shown that BL cases differ from other lymphoma types { 21701491 ; 21245480 ; 26113842 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The gross specimen often shows a mass. The cut surface has a fish-flesh appearance. Necrosis
is common and hemorrhage can be present. The neoplasm can surround contiguous lymph
nodes.
Histopathology
The morphologic features of BL subtypes are similar. Normal structures are replaced by a diffuse
proliferation of lymphoma cells; a starry-sky pattern is very common. The lymphoma cells are
monomorphic, intermediate (medium)-size cells with 2-4 basophilic nucleoli. The cells have
moderate, basophilic cytoplasm that often squares off with the cytoplasm of other cells ("cookie
cutter" appearance). Apoptosis is common and often marked. Coagulative necrosis is common.
Rare cases are associated with a prominent granulomatous reaction { 33948980 }.
The lymphoma cells are of B-cell lineage, positive for monotypic light chain, IgM, pan B-cell
antigens, CD10, CD38, CD81, and Bcl-6, and are negative for pan T-cell antigens, CD23, CD44,
CD138, LMO2 and TdT. Bcl-2 is usually negative, but rarely focally and/or weakly positive
{ 21718280 }. MYC is positive in >90% of cells in most cases and the proliferation index is high,
with Ki-67 90-95% { 29954940 }.
Cytology
Smears show intermediate-size lymphoma cells often associated with many apoptotic cells.
In Romanowsky stains, many cytoplasmic vacuoles are present.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
MYC/8q24 translocations occur in 90-95% of BL cases. The t(8;14)(q24;q32) MYC::IGH occurs in
80% of cases, and t(2;8)(p12;q24) IGK::MYC and t(8;22)(q24;q11) MYC::IGL occur in 20% of
cases. 5-10% of cases of BL lack MYC translocations. Conventional cytogenetic or molecular

972

methods may "miss" some cases that truly carry MYC translocations. Cryptic insertions
of MYC into IGH have been described { 31073073 }. Alternative mechanisms that
dysregulate MYC likely occur in a small subset of cases { 31748534 }. BL cases, regardless of
subtype, have similar gene expression and microRNA profiles { 21701491 ; 30617194 }. Next
generation sequencing has shown mutations of ID3 and TCF3 that activate B-cell receptor
signaling
in
70%
of
sporadic
BL
{ 24492847 }.
Mutations
of CCND3, TP53, RHOA, SMARCA4 and ARID1A occur in 10-40% of sporadic BL cases. EBVpositive BL cases have fewer mutations.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Intermediate-size lymphoma cells, germinal center B-cell lineage, high proliferation (Ki67) index, absence (or minimal expression) of Bcl-2.
Desirable: MYC translocation, starry-sky pattern
Staging
The predominantly extranodal distribution of BL makes Ann Arbor staging suboptimal. The system
proposed by Murphy and revised in 2015 is used routinely for children { 25940716 }.
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis of patients with sporadic BL, if treated with immunochemotherapy, is excellent. The
overall survival
rate approaches
90% in
children
and 60-70% in
adults
{ 31229156 ; 32144513 ; 33502927 }. A recently proposed BL International Prognostic Index
subdivides patients into low-, intermediate- and high-risk groups with overall survival of 96%, 76%
and 59%, respectively { 33502927 }. The prognosis of endemic BL patients has been less good, in
part because of inadequate resources { 29769263 ; 32841337 }. Results have improved following
introduction of more intensive regimens in African nations. Patients with HIV-associated BL can be
successfully treated with immunochemotherapy
if HIV
infection
is controlled
{ 31229156 ; 32735838 }.

The lymphoma involves the submucosa forming a dense infiltrate.

975

Plasmacytoma
Definition
Solitary plasmacytoma is a localized, mass forming intramedullary or extramedullary tumour
composed of monoclonal plasma cells without evidence of plasma cell myeloma or additional
plasma cell tumours.
ICD-O coding
9731/3 Plasmacytoma of bone
ICD-11 coding
2A83.2 & XH4BL1 Solitary plasmacytoma & Plasmacytoma NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: Extramedullary plasmacytoma; plasmacytoma of bone
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Solitary extraosseous plasmacytoma (SEP) typically infiltrates the mucosal tissues of the upper
respiratory tract (nasopharynx, nasal cavity, paranasal sinuses, oropharynx, larynx, and oral
cavity{ 10357398 ; 15586381 }, while solitary bony lesions affect the mandibular marrow-rich areas
(body, angle, ramus) more than the maxilla { 22134311 }. Cervical lymph node dissemination
occurs in about 15% { 11190797 }.
Clinical features
SEP typically present as a soft tissue mass (80%){ 9852535 }, followed by airway obstruction
(35%), epistaxis (35%), local pain (20%), proptosis (15%), nasal discharge (10%), and cranial nerve
palsy (5%). Jaw and teeth pain, teeth migration and swelling are seen in solitary bony lesions
{ 9117760 }. Approximately 20% of the patients have a detectable, low quantity monoclonal serum
or urine paraprotein (M protein), most commonly IgA { 10629591 ; 12780789 ; 15009059 }.
Multifocal bone involvement is absent by imaging studies { 19421229 ; 8315427 ; 21757591 }.
Epidemiology
About 80% of all SEP arise in the head and neck; SEP accounts for 4% of all non-epithelial tumors
in the head and neck { 10357398 }. Males are more often affected. Peak incidence is around the
sixth decade of life { 6180279 ; 12780789 ; 10357398 ; 9852535 }. Rare cases have been reported
in a post-transplant setting { 19762536 }.
Etiology
Etiology is unknown, although in 10% of SEP cases, Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) has been detected
in immunocompetent patients { 7675768 ; 25556356 ; 16308165 }.
Pathogenesis
Pathogenesis and genetic features appear to be similar to other plasma cell neoplasms but have
not been extensively studied in head and neck sites { 23368088 ; 18326524 ; 20880115 }.
Macroscopic appearance
SEP form soft, gelatinous, tan to yellow and sometimes hemorrhagic soft-tissue masses.
Histopathology
SEP shows a subepithelial diffuse infiltrate of neoplastic plasma cells combined with a scant
vascularized stroma, hemorrhagic areas { 7675768 ; 6180279 }, and, in a few cases, amyloid or
immunoglobulin deposits. For solitary bone plasmacytoma and SEP, clonal plasma cells can be
classified based on their mature and immature features. Immature plasma cells (plasmablastic or
anaplastic features) usually exhibit a higher nuclear/cytoplasmic ratio, dispersed chromatin, often
with prominent nucleoli, more abundant cytoplasm, and a hof region.
The immunophenotype is similar to other plasma cell neoplasms with CD38, CD138, and MUM1
positivity and can be differentiated from reactive plasma cells by light chain restriction.
Occasionally, they may display CD20 positivity with negativity for most B-cell markers. Rare
positive staining for cytokeratin and EMA is reported { 1469918 }, potentially leading to
misdiagnosis of carcinoma. Unlike solitary bone plasmacytoma and plasma cell myeloma, SEP
usually lacks Cyclin D1 expression and shows a weaker positivity for CD56 { 15586381 }. MIB1
proliferation index is usually low and if elevated, may help in predicting a more aggressive course
{ 25842207 }.
Differential diagnosis

976

Large B cell lymphoma, carcinoma, melanoma, extramedullary myeloid sarcoma, and olfactory
neuroblastoma can be challenging to distinguish from cases of moderately immature SEP. In EBVpositive plasmacytoma, it is mandatory to exclude plasmablastic lymphoma which has a high Ki67
proliferative index (often >60%) { 25193957 } and is usually associated with an
immunocompromised state. When a clear distinction may be difficult, a descriptive diagnosis such
as plasmablastic neoplasm, indeterminate between plasmablastic lymphoma and anaplastic
plasmacytoma is allowed. In the differential diagnosis between SEP and lymphomas with extensive
plasma cell differentiation (e.g., mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue marginal zone lymphoma,
lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma), the finding of aggregates of small B lymphocytes, clonal B cells (if
flow cytometry is performed), remnants of germinal centers and expression of IgM favor B-cell
lymphoma.
Cytology
Cytology specimens may include plasma cells with variable degrees of immaturity and anaplasia.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Pathogenesis and genetic features appear to be similar to other plasma cell neoplasms but have
not been extensively studied in head and neck sites. Molecular subtyping follows that for multiple
myeloma { 23368088 ; 18326524 ; 20880115 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Mass-forming infiltrate of plasma cells, which may exhibit variable degrees of immaturity and
anaplasia
Immunohistochemical demonstration of light chain restriction
No evidence of plasma cell myeloma or plasma cell lesions elsewhere
Staging
Thorough staging is required to rule out evidence of other plasmacytomas or plasma cell myeloma.
Prognosis and prediction
About 25% of patients experience local recurrence or spread to regional lymph nodes { 1272069 }.
Many patients have a relatively indolent course, and a ten-year disease-free survival rate is about
70% { 12780789 ; 12057071 }. Possible EBV-driven transformation of plasmacytoma into
plasmablastic lymphoma has been reported { 25193957 }. About 10% of patients progress to
plasma cell myeloma { 10357398 ; 25439696 }.

R#23781Plasmacytoma

plasmacytoma.

CD79a by immunohistochemistry.

L#23789 Plasmacytoma, There is a monotonous proliferation of plasmacytoid cells in this field,
with focal binucleation.

occasional nucleoli.
R#24954 Plasmacytoma, there is a monotonous proliferation of plasmacytoid cells in the
submucosa.

Plasmablastic lymphoma
Definition
Plasmablastic lymphoma (PBL) is an aggressive large B-cell lymphoma composed of a diffuse
proliferation of large cells resembling immunoblasts or plasmablasts, lacking mature B-cell markers
and expressing plasma cell differentiation antigens.
ICD-O coding
9735/3 Plasmablastic lymphoma
ICD-11 coding
2A81.2 Plasmablastic lymphoma
Related terminology
None

979

Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The most frequent site of involvement in the head and neck region is the oral cavity, although cases
in the nasal and respiratory sinuses have been reported. The gastrointestinal tract, soft tissues,
skin, bone, and lung are rare extranodal sites that may be encountered. Nodal presentations are
reported
(<10%)
more
frequently
in
post-transplant
settings
(30%)
{ 12786898 ; 15166665 ; 16327436 ; 9242574 }.
Clinical features
A tumour mass in the oral cavity is the most common presentation. However, patients usually show
advanced-stage disease at diagnosis. Disseminated disease is seen in 75% of HIV-positive, 50%
of post-transplant patients, and 25% of patients without apparent immunodeficiency
{ 15166665 ; 25636338 }. Some cases in HIV–positive patients may have overlapping features with
multiple myeloma such as lytic bone lesions and paraprotein { 20348882 }.
Epidemiology
PBL is an uncommon type of large B-cell lymphoma that occurs predominantly in adults with
increased frequency in immunocompromised states (e.g., HIV infection, post-transplant patients,
autoimmune diseases, and immunosenescence) { 17944927 ; 9028965 ; 22958176 }.
Etiology
In HIV-positive and post-transplant patients, Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), with a latency type I and
occasional LMP1 expression, is frequently observed { 25636338 }. By definition, HHV8 is absent
{ 9028965 ; 15166665 ; 16327436 ; 9242574 }.
Pathogenesis
PBL seems to originate from a terminally differentiated B-cell that has switched to a plasma cell
program. MYC rearrangement is detected in 50%, and these cases are commonly associated with
EBV infection (74%) { 23599149 ; 20962620 }. PBL has a mutational profile that differs from that of
diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of ABC subtype and multiple myeloma with frequent mutations in
genes of the MAPK (25-50%) (NRAS, KRAS) and JAK-STAT (40%) (STAT3, JAK2, SOCS1)
signaling pathways { 27687004 ; 33225311 ; 33951889 }. The miRNA expression profile of
PBL has revealed a number of EBV-encoded miRNA that suppress transcription of tumour
suppressor genes (PTEN, PBX2) and lipid metabolism controller genes (PPARGC1A,
PLIN2/adipophilin) suggesting a pathogenetic role of EBV-encoded miRNA in lymphomagenesis of
EBV positive cases { 29296171 }. EBV positive PBL have molecular properties and pathogenetic
mechanisms distinct from EBV negative PBL, similar to what is observed in other lymphoma types
{ 28419429 ; 31403034 ; 26468873 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Involved organs replaced by masses with a fish-flesh appearance, often associated with areas of
haemorrhage and necrosis.
Histopathology
PBL may display a variety of morphological features. Monomorphic PBL shows diffuse, cohesive
sheets of large cells most resembling immunoblasts with inconspicuous plasmacytic features and
sometimes with “starry sky” pattern. Some cases may have cells with more overt plasmacytic
differentiation showing round eccentric nuclei, coarser chromatin, ample cytoplasm, and paranuclear hof. Areas of geographic necrosis are seen with low numbers of tumour-infiltrating T cells.
PBL shows a terminally differentiated B-cell/plasma cell phenotype (CD38+, CD138+,
IRF4/MUM1+) without expression of common B-cell markers (CD45, CD20, PAX5), although
CD79a positivity is reported in 40% { 25636338 ; 20418245 ; 23599149 }. In oral cavity PBL, CD56
is usually negative, but in other sites it is frequently positive (25%). In situ hybridization for EBER
is commonly observed (60-75%).
Differential diagnosis
Key features distinguishing between PBL and plasma cell neoplasms are the clinical presentation,
high proliferation index and EBV positivity in the former. Possible transformation of plasmacytoma
into plasmablastic lymphoma has been hypothesized to be triggered by EBV reactivation, and this
event demands a precise diagnosis { 25193957 }. When a clear distinction may be difficult, a
descriptive diagnosis such as plasmablastic neoplasm, indeterminate between plasmablastic
lymphoma and anaplastic plasmacytoma can be used. The key feature to discriminate PBL from
large B-cell lymphomas, like diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) with plasmablastic features

980

and EBV+ DLBCL, NOS is essentially the maintenance of the B-cell program, namely strong
expression of CD20, CD79a, and PAX5 in the latter. The cohesive growth, “starry sky pattern” and
high proliferation index can suggest Burkitt lymphoma, but PBL can be distinguished by CD20 and
PAX5 negativity.
Cytology
No literature has been published.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
There are no specific diagnostic molecular tests.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Lymphoma with immunoblastic or plasmablastic morphology
Immunophenotypic features of terminal B-cell differentiation (loss of conventional B-cell markers;
expression of plasma cell markers)
Lack of ALK expression
HHV8 negativity
Staging
The predominantly extranodal distribution and high stage at presentation of PL makes Lugano
staging suboptimal.
Prognosis and prediction
Overall survival is short (6-11 months), despite therapeutic intervention.The presence
of MYC translocation has been associated with an inferior prognosis { 25636338 ; 24832164 }.

T/NK cell tumours

T-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma
Definition
T-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma (T-ALL/LBL) is a neoplasm of precursor T lymphocytes.
ICD-O coding
9837/3 T-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma
ICD-11 coding
2A71 & XH7T28 Precursor T-lymphoblastic neoplasms & Precursor T-cell lymphoblastic leukaemia
Related terminology
Not recommended: Precursor T-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma
Subtype(s)
Cortical; medullary; early thymic precursor (ETP) ALL/LBL (see Table #25340).
Localization
T-ALL/LBL occurs in the mediastinum, lymph nodes, and rarely extranodal sites such as orbit,
tonsils or nasopharynx, etc.
Clinical features
Patients with anterior mediastinal (thymic) masses present with dyspnea, superior vena cava
syndrome, and pleural and/or pericardial effusions { 31567129 }.
Epidemiology
T-ALL/LBL mostly affects children and young adults, less often older adults. The male:female
ratio is 2.5-3:1.{ 26991119 ; 16173961 ; 31567129 ; 26585702 }. T-LBL accounts for 1-2% of nonHodgkin lymphoma (NHL) overall. It is the second most common paediatric NHL in children and
adolescents, comprising 15-20% of paediatric NHL cases { 26991119 ; 16173961 } and 85-90% of
lymphoblastic lymphomas. T-ALL represents approximately 15% of paediatric ALL and 25% of
adult ALL [[Borowitz M, Chan J, Bene M, Arber D. T lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma In:
Swerdlow S, Campo E, Harris N, et al., eds. WHO Classification Tumours of Haematopoietic and
Lymphoid Tissues. Updated 4th ed. Lyon: IARC; 2017:209-12.]]. Most patients in the USA are
white, but Asians and blacks are also affected { 26991119 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
T-ALL/LBL shows diverse genetic changes { 23023710 }. The karyotype is abnormal in ~60% of
cases, with structural and numerical changes, including translocations and partial losses of
chromosomes and others { 21689091 ; 31567129 }. Translocations often involve TCR genes (3550%) (TCRα/δ at 14q11, TCRß at 7q34, TCRγ at 7p14) { 21112032 ; 18691165 ; 26276771 }, or
less commonly MLL (KMT2A) and ABL1 genes { 27451956 }. The del(9p21)/CDKN2A/2B is seen
in 60-70% of cases, causing cell cycle disruption.
At the molecular level, NOTCH1 mutation is most common (~60% of cases, targeting MYC),
followed by FBXW7 mutation (~30%, negative regulator of NOTCH1) { 33467425 }. Other common
mutations include PHF6 (~ 20% of cases), PTEN (~15% of cases), LEF1 (~10%) and ILR7 (~10%
of cases). Mutations typically associated with acute myeloid leukaemia (AML), such as FLT3-ITD,
EZH2,
RAS
and
RUNX1 have
been
described
more in
ETP-ALL/LBL
{ 26276771 ; 26991119 ; 21112032 }.
Mutations
unique
to
T-LBL
involve PAPPA,
NFIL3 and ZNF91 { 25160903 }.
Genetic and epigenetic changes lead to activation of NOTCH, JAK-STAT and/or PI3-AKT-Mtor
pathways { 31570389 ; 33152759 ; 21112032 ; 18691165 ; 26216196 ; 28872614 ; 33227818 }
Macroscopic appearance
Not relevant
Histopathology
Normal architecture is typically effaced by a diffuse proliferation of medium-sized neoplastic cells
with round to convoluted nuclei, fine chromatin, small or absent nucleoli and scant cytoplasm, with
frequent mitoses, often with 983angible body macrophages imparting a starry-sky
pattern.{ 31567129 ; 26991119 }
Large
cells showing
prominent
nucleoli
may
be
seen. { 21451365 } Cases with rearrangement of PDGFRA, PDGFRB or FGFR, or PCM1-JAK2,
often associated with eosinophilia, are classified separately.
983

Neoplastic cells variably express T-cell-associated markers (CD1a, CD2, CD3, CD5, CD7), CD10,
CD34, CD43, CD45, and CD99 and usually express TdT. CD33 and CD117 are detectable in
a subset of cases. TdT is absent in 3-4% of cases at diagnosis { 23702731 }. CD79a is expressed
in 12% of cases. { 21451365 } Lymphoblasts can be CD4+/CD8+ (double-positive), CD4-/CD8(double-negative) or positive for CD4 or CD8 only. Surface CD3+ cases usually express TCRαβ,
and rarely TCRγδ.{ 31567129 } Ki67 is high (often >90%). The immunophenotype simulates
stages of intrathymic T-cell differentiation. { 31567129 ; 28538509 ; 21112032 ; 7564526 } T-LBL
has a more mature (cortical) immunophenotype than T-ALL, with some overlap (see
Table #25340). { 22994934 }.
Lymphoblastic lymphoma/leukaemia of natural killer type (NK-ALL/LBL) (provisional entity), like TALL/LBL, expresses CD3 epsilon (CD3-ε) and is therefore CD3+ by IHC. NK-ALL/LBL lacks clonal
TCR rearrangement.
The differential diagnosis includes indolent T-lymphoblastic proliferation (It-LBP), a rare self-limited
disorder characterized by a polyclonal proliferation of TdT+ T cells, usually small in size, distorting
but not obliterating normal architecture.{ 10435569 ; 11224614 ; 24618611 ; 23574769 }. ItLBP usually involves head and neck sites and rarely occurs in association with Castleman disease,
follicular
dendritic
cell
sarcoma
and
other
neoplasms.{ 23574769 ; 23060347 ; 30222603 ; 29143359 }. LMO2 is usually positive in TALL/LBL but negative in It-LBP. { 32526041 }
Cytology
Lymphoblasts are typically agranular, with delicate chromatin, round or notched nuclei, and
occasionally vacuolated cytoplasm.{ 31567129 }
Diagnostic molecular pathology
TCR ß and/or γ chain genes are almost always clonally rearranged; concurrent clonal IGH is found
in up to about 20% of cases.{ 19147408 ; 10419594 ; 10361104 }
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Diffuse proliferation of T-lineage lymphoblasts; negative for myeloperoxidase, without strongly
expressed B-lineage markers.
Desirable:
Demonstration of monoclonal TCR rearrangement; abnormal cytogenetic or/and mutational profiles
consistent with those of T-ALL/LBL.
Staging
While staging is not typically used for risk stratification of T-ALL/LBL, almost all patients have stage
III or IV disease { 10627444 ; 26585702 } using Lugano or Murphy staging { 7414342 }. Bone
marrow involvement is common and central nervous system involvement occurs in 5-10% cases
{ 10627444 ; 28427520 ; 16173961 }.
Staging requires history; physical examination; laboratory and imaging studies. Ancillary testing
such as flow cytometry, karyotyping and molecular genetic analysis may be required to assess
peripheral blood, CSF and bone marrow involvement { 26991119 ; 28427520 }. Although arbitrary,
marrow involvement of ≥25% is usually used as threshold to diagnose T-ALL { 7414342 }.
Prognosis and prediction
T-ALL/LBL is treated with ALL-type intensive chemotherapy; high-risk patients receive allogeneic
stem cell transplantation { 28427520 }. Complete remission rate is 76–93%. 5-year event-free
survival is 75-90% in paediatric patients but 50-70% in older patients. Relapse occurs in one-third
of patients and predicts a poor outcome { 26991119 ; 10627444 ; 26585702 }. ETP ALL-LBL is still
considered an aggressive disease in adult patients { 26991119 ; 26747249 ; 28605290 }.
Minimal measurable disease (MMD) detectable post-induction/consolidation is a significant risk
factor for relapse { 28427520 ; 26679753 }. Mutations of NOTCH1/FBXW7 combined with wildtype RAS /PTEN is associated with a favorable outcome { 29051182 ; 24166518 }. Activation of
PI3K and MLL (KMT2) disruption predict an unfavourable outcome. Targeted therapy modulating
NOTCH‐, PI3K‐Akt‐Mtor‐, JAK‐STAT‐ and MAPK‐pathways and immunotherapy may prove
valuable. Mortality is associated with tumour progression, complications of therapy, and rarely,
second malignancies { 10627444 ; 26585702 }.

ETP: early T precursor phenotype: defined as expression (>25%) of one or more of myeloid or stem

is negative (<3%) and CD5 weak (<75%). Cases with CD5 expression >75% are considered nearly
ETP type with absence of CD1a and CD8 (<5%). ETP overlaps with pro or pre-T ALL-LBL described
before. { 19147408 }
