Primary mucosal CD30-positive T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder
Definition
CD30-positive T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder (TLPD) is a neoplastic proliferation of large,
CD30-positive T cells arising in the oral cavity or occasionally other mucosal sites in the head and
neck. This entity constitutes a clinicopathological spectrum of lymphoproliferative lesions,
analogous to the spectrum observed in primary cutaneous CD30-positive TLPD.
ICD-O coding
9718/3 Primary mucosal CD30-positive T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder
ICD-11 coding
Not available
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None

986

Localization
The proliferation typically occurs in the oral cavity or tongue, but similar lesions have been
described
in
the
nasopharynx,
conjunctiva,
and
orbit
{ 8833302 ; 22388754 ; 8332573 ; 24332333 }.
Clinical features
Patients with CD30-positive TLPD typically present with a mass lesion, often with ulceration.
Spontaneous regression may occur { 9415340 }. Clinical history and staging are important for
excluding secondary involvement by a systemic lymphoma.
Patients with both mucosal and skin involvement should be classified as mucosal involvement by
primary cutaneous CD30-positive TLPD { 22388754 ; 24749749 ; 33269496 }.
Epidemiology
There is a male predominance, with a male-to-female ratio of 2:1. The disorder primarily affects
adults, with a mean patient age in the sixth decade of life { 22388754 ; 24332333 }.
Etiology
The etiology is unknown. Presentation following dental implants has been reported { 26261101 }.
Pathogenesis
Clonal T-cell receptor gene rearrangements have been detected in most cases
{ 22388754 ; 24332333 }. Occasional cases carry rearrangements of DUSP22 on 6p25.3, similar
to the rearrangements observed in some primary cutaneous CD30-positive TLPDs and ALKnegative anaplastic large cell lymphomas { 22388754 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The tumour typically appears as a nodule, mucosal ulceration, or both { 24332333 }.
Histopathology
Primary mucosal CD30-positive TLPD demonstrates a morphological spectrum similar to that
observed in primary cutaneous cases. The neoplastic cells are large atypical lymphoid cells with
pleomorphic nuclei and abundant cytoplasm. Cells resembling the hallmark cells of anaplastic large
cell lymphoma often are seen. Most cases show a diffuse or sheet-like growth pattern. A mixed
inflammatory background may be present, including areas with prominent eosinophils or
neutrophils { 14750239 ; 22388754 ; 24332333 }. Some cases of traumatic ulcerative granuloma
with stromal eosinophilia may represent the indolent end of the spectrum of CD30-positive TLPD
{ 16938660 }.
By definition, CD30 is positive, and staining is strong and uniform. The large lymphoid cells typically
show a T-cell phenotype, but often demonstrate loss of one or more pan–T-cell antigens. CD4 is
expressed more frequently than CD8. Cytotoxic markers (i.e. TIA1, granzyme B, and perforin) are
often expressed, and EMA may be positive. CD56, ALK, and EBV are negative { 24332333 }. The
EBV-positive cases that have been reported in children most likely represent chronic active EBV
infection instead { 26420252 }.
This disorder must be distinguished from reactive inflammatory conditions of the oral cavity and
from secondary involvement by systemic anaplastic large cell lymphoma.
Cytology
The cytological features have not been described.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
T-cell receptor gene rearrangement studies may be helpful in establishing clonality
{ 22388754 ; 24332333 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Infiltrate of large, atypical, pleomorphic lymphoid cells in a mucosal site
Variable inflammatory background
Strong and uniform staining for CD30
Demonstration of T-lineage markers and/or molecular evidence of T-cell clonality
Exclusion of systemic lymphoma
Staging
Patients should have disease limited to the presenting mucosal site (stage IE by
Lugano staging). Clinical staging is essential to exclude mucosal involvement by systemic
anaplastic large cell lymphoma { 22388754 }.
Prognosis and prediction

987

Most cases show complete resolution with local therapy (excision with or without radiotherapy),
with or without the addition of systemic chemotherapy { 18387994 ; 22388754 ; 24332333 }.
Occasional cases show spontaneous regression.

L#26653

There is extensive infiltration of the mucosa with superficial ulceration.
R#26654
Primary CD30-positive T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder of the buccal mucosa
The tumour is composed of sheets of large neoplastic cells with abundant pale
eosinophilic cytoplasm.

L#26655
Primary CD30-positive T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder of the tongue
There is marked ulceration with deep infiltration of the muscle.
R#26656
Primary CD30-positive T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder of the tongue
The neoplastic cells are present within an inflammatory background containing numerous
eosinophils.

988

Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma
Definition
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma (formerly nasal-type) is an EBV-associated NK or cytotoxic T-cell
lymphoma occurring in an extranodal site.
ICD-O coding
9719/3 Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma
ICD-11 coding
2A90.6 Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: Lethal midline granuloma; malignant midline reticulosis; polymorphic
reticulosis; angiocentric immunoproliferative lesion; angiocentric lymphoma
Subtype(s)
Nasal lymphoma, involving the nasal and/or paranasal region; extranasal lymphoma, arising from
the skin, gastrointestinal tract, testis, or soft tissue
Localization
About 85% of ENKTL occur in the upper aerodigestive tract with the nasal cavity being the most
common site of involvement { 16380410 ; 23232851 ; 24438142 ; 25554090 }.
Clinical features
Most patients present with nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and necrotizing lesions of the nose or hard
palate. Extensive inflammation associated with a tumour results in periorbital edema, proptosis,
and diplopia.
Epidemiology
ENKTL affects adults with a median age of 43-53 years and male predominance
{ 21854649 ; 32105608 ; 32658985 ; 32891786 }. The disease rarely affects children and
adolescents { 26712870 }. The prevalence of ENKTL varies depending on ethnicity and geographic
location. ENKTL accounts for 0.7%–17% of malignant lymphoma cases in East Asian countries
{ 18251781 ; 20806229 ; 21854649 ; 29661730 ; 30311404 } and for 0.5-7.8% in central and south
American countries { 22263570 ; 23086753 }, but less than 0.1% of US populations
{ 19029440 ; 27618563 ; 27778143 }.
Etiology
The etiology of ENKTL is poorly understood other than its association with EBV
exposure. Epidemiologic data suggest that genetic or environmental factors predispose some
individuals to the disease. GWAS study in an Asian population identified SNPs, on IL18RAP
(rs13015714) on chromosome 2q12.1, HLA-DRB1 (rs9271588) on chromosome 6p21.3, and HLADPB1 (rs9277378) on chromosome 6 that were associated with an increased risk of ENKTL
{ 27470079 ; 31879220 }.
Pathogenesis
ENKTL demonstrates complex genomic alterations; the most consistently detected chromosomal
aberration is deletion of 6q21-25 { 16049916 ; 19965620 ; 29097495 } which harbours tumour
suppressor genes, including PRDM1, PTPRK, and FOXO3 { 19194464 ; 21690554 ; 25612622 }.
Recurrent mutations identified in ENKTL include those of STAT3, JAK3, BCOR, ARID1A, TP53,
MGA and DDX3X { 11733360 ; 22705984 ; 25980440 ; 26192917 }. Deregulation of signaling
pathways and genes (NF-κB, BIRC5, MYC, RUNX3, AURKA, EZH2 and PD-L1), epigenetic
alterations
and
miRNAs
deregulation
have
been
identified
{ 21052088 ; 21294123 ; 21610143 ; 21921041 ; 23529930 ; 23963825 ; 25760809 ; 27737703 ;
28119527 ; 28349165 ; 29386059 ; 30299211 ; 32183952 }. Recently, three ENKTL molecular
subtypes have been proposed. TSIM-subtype is associated with JAK/STAT activation, NK-origin
and PD-L1 overexpression. MB-subtype shows MYC overexpression and poor outcome, while
HEA-subtype is characterized by epigenetic changes, NF-κB activation and T-cell origin
{ 32183952 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Not applicable
Histopathology
ENKTL exhibits polymorphous infiltration of neoplastic cells mixed with inflammatory cells. The
cytomorphology of tumour cells varies and they are most often composed of medium sized cells or
mixture of small and large lymphocytes with hyperchromatic and irregular nuclei. Angiocentric

989

growth and extensive necrosis of tumour tissue are found in most cases of ENKTL. In the nasal
cavity, tumours are often accompanied by pseudoepitheliomatous proliferation of overlying
squamous mucosa.
Cellular lineage of tumour cells is either NK or T cell { 25554090 ; 23232851 ; 22314189 }. The
typical immunophenotype of ENKTL is CD20-, sCD3-, cCD3-+, CD4-, CD5-, CD8-, and CD56+,
and expression of cytotoxic molecules such as TIA-1, granzyme B and perforin. A minority of cases
can be surface CD3+, CD8+, or CD56-. T cell receptor antigen is usually negative and frequently
lost even in T-lineage ENKTL { 25554090 ; 27015135 }. Therapeutic targets including CD30, PDL1,
and
CD38
are
commonly
expressed
{ 29052238 ; 26770954 ; 31653980 ; 27696202 ; 32766875 ; 28188133 ; 25865943 }
Differential diagnosis
EBV-negative extranodal cytotoxic T cell lymphoma should be diagnosed as PTCL. NOS.
Nodal cytotoxic T cell lymphoma with EBV association is diagnosed as EBV-positive nodal PTCL.
See [[LYM5]] for additional details of differential diagnosis.
Cytology
ENKTL rarely presents in effusion and shows anaplastic tumour cells with fine to coarse azurophilic
granules { 32020785 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
EBER in situ hybridization reveals EBV infection in virtually all tumour cells. The T cell receptor
gene is clonal in up to 40% of cases { 15195017 ; 22314189 ; 27015135 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Extranodal location; EBV positivity in cytotoxic T- or NK tumour cells
Staging
Most patients present with Lugano stages I and II (76%) { 32066865 ; 33054461 }.
Prognosis and prediction
The 5-year overall survival rate of nasal lymphoma and extranasal lymphoma is 54% and 34%
respectively { 26873565 ; 32105608 ; 33054461 }. Patients above 60 years, stage III or IV disease,
distant lymph-node involvement, and extranasal lymphoma, and a higher level of circulating EBVDNA, have a significantly worse prognosis { 26873565 ; 32105608 ; 33718104 }.

L#26043
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
The nasal tumour invades adjacent tissue and penetrates overlying skin.
R#26044
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
The tumour in the nasal cavity extends to the orbit, resulting in proptosis.

990

#29313
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
The tumour frequently demonstrates prominent necrosis.
#29330
Extranodal NK/T cel lymphoma
In the nasal mucosa, extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma can be associated with florid
pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia of the overlying squamous epithelium.

#29334
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
The tumour cells display characteristic angiocentric growth pattern with infiltration and
destruction of a muscular blood vessel wall.
#29335
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
The tumour cells are positive for CD3.

991

L#29336
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
The tumour cells are positive for CD56.
R#29339
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
The tumour cells are positive for granzyme B.

L#29340
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
Almost all tumour cells are positive for Epstein-Barr virus encoded RNA by in situ
hybridization.
R#29342
Extranodal NK/T cell lymphoma
The lymphoma shows medium to large tumour cells with irregular and often
hyperchromatic nuclei.

Hodgkin Lymphoma

Hodgkin lymphoma
Definition
Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) is a clonal, malignant lymphoproliferation derived from germinal center B
cells, in which a minority of neoplastic cells with characteristic immunophenotype termed
Hodgkin/Reed-Sternberg (H/RS) cells in classic HL (CHL) and LP cells in nodular lymphocytepredominant HL (NLPHL) reside in a mixed inflammatory background.
ICD-O coding
9650/3 Hodgkin lymphoma, NOS
9663/3 Classic Hodgkin lymphoma, nodular sclerosis
9652/3 Classic Hodgkin lymphoma, mixed cellularity
9651/3 Classic Hodgkin lymphoma, lymphocyte-rich
9653/3 Classic Hodgkin lymphoma, lymphocyte depletion
9659/3 Hodgkin lymphoma, nodular lymphocyte-predominant type
ICD-11 coding
2B30.1Z Classical Hodgkin lymphoma, unspecified
2B30.10 Nodular sclerosis classical Hodgkin lymphoma
2B30.11 Lymphocyte-rich classical Hodgkin lymphoma
2B30.12 Mixed cellularity classical Hodgkin lymphoma
2B30.13 Lymphocyte depleted classical Hodgkin lymphoma
2B30.0 Nodular lymphocyte predominant Hodgkin lymphoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Hodgkin disease
Subtype(s)
Classic HL:
- Nodular sclerosis - Mixed cellularity - Lymphocyte-rich - Lymphocyte depletion
HL, nodular lymphocyte predominant type
Localization
HL is usually a node-based disease. Less than 1% of cases involve extranodal sites in the head
and neck region { 20564093 ; 7503365 ; 15981804 ; 33478377 }. Most commonly, Waldeyer ring
is affected, including nasopharynx and tonsils, frequently associated with cervical
lymphadenopathy. HL in the parotid usually arises from intraparotid lymph nodes { 25929348 }. HL
rarely occurs in the thyroid gland { 20564093 ; 16258502 }.
Clinical features
Waldeyer ring involvement often causes local symptoms including difficulties in swallowing or
obstructed nasal respiration { 12373352 }. Less than 20% of patients with HL manifesting at these
sites have B symptoms.
Epidemiology
HL accounts for 15-25% of malignant lymphomas, with NLPHL representing 3-8% of all HL. HL
overall shows slight male preponderance, more marked (2.4:1) for NLPHL { 8922237 ; 10071266 }.
Thyroid CHL shows a female predominance and frequent history of thyroiditis
{ 20564093 ; 16258502 }.
Etiology
The etiology of HL is unknown, but 20% to almost 100% of cases of CHL are associated with
Epstein Barr-virus (EBV) { 8836402 ; 33686198 }.
Pathogenesis
H/RS cells are germinal-centre B cells with somatically hypermutated IG genes, absence of
immunoglobulin expression, and lack of a B cell gene expression programme, whereas LP cells
retain their B cell phenotype { 10637266 ; 12393731 ; 27496304 }. H/RS cells show constitutive
activation of NF-κB and other pro-tumourigenic signaling pathways and produce a variety of
cytokines and chemokines responsible for the inflammatory infiltrate and generation of a protective
microenvironment { 21368758 ; 21483001 ; 27496304 }.
Macroscopic appearance
CHL nodular sclerosis may show gross nodularity and fibrotic bands, whereas other subtypes
exhibit a whitish, homogeneous cut surface.

993

Histopathology
The main feature of CHL is the presence of characteristic H/RS cells and variants in a mixed
inflammatory infiltrate. The cellular composition of the background varies depending on the subtype
{ 27496304 }. Classic RS cells are bi- or multinucleate large cells with nuclei with open chromatin
and large, eosinophilic nucleoli. The mononuclear variant is called a Hodgkin cell. In nodular
sclerosis CHL, the lacunar cell is characterized by clear halos surrounding the frequently lobulated
nuclei with less prominent nucleoli. CHL in Waldeyer ring shows a higher frequency of mixed
cellularity and lymphocyte rich subtypes as compared to primary nodal disease
{ 7503365 ; 15981804 ; 20564093 }. H/RS cells in CHL express CD30 and CD15 in almost 100%
and 75-85% of cases, respectively. They are positive for MUM1 and weakly express PAX5,
whereas other B cell markers are either negative or weakly positive in occasional tumor cells. LMP1
is expressed in EBV+ cases. The background lymphoid population is dominated by T cells, except
for lymphocyte rich CHL which exhibits small B cells in a nodular arrangement
{ 10961891 ; 30407610 }.
NLPHL is characterized by rare, large atypical LP cells with frequently lobulated nuclei in a
background of small lymphocytes usually arranged in a macronodular fashion { 30407610 }. LP
cells in NLPHL show a retained B cell program and express CD20, CD79a, and B cell transcription
factors PAX5, Oct-2, BOB.1 and MEF2B, and variably EMA. The background usually is dominated
by small B cells. LP cells are rosetted by PD1+/CD57+ follicular T helper cells { 30407610 }.
Differential
H/RS-like cells can occur in a variety of NHL and reactive conditions. In Waldeyer ring, infectious
mononucleosis and EBV-associated mucocutaneous ulcer are important differential diagnoses of
EBV+ CHL { 29518976 }. NLPHL needs to be distinguished from lymphocyte rich CHL and small B
cell lymphomas { 10961891 ; 30407610 }.
Cytology
FNA specimens show H/RS or LP cells, respectively in a mixed inflammatory background, but
primary diagnosis requires a tissue biopsy { 11842377 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular studies are not required and of limited diagnostic use.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
CHL: H/RS cells with typical immunophenotype (CD30+, CD15+/-, PAX5+ (weak), CD20-/+, ALK) in an appropriate inflammatory background.
NLPHL: LP cells with preserved B cell phenotype in a usually nodular background with rosetting of
CD57+/PD1+ T cells.
Staging
The Lugano staging system is accepted by the UICC for TNM classification.
Prognosis and prediction
With modern therapy regimens, 80-90% of patients with HL can be cured { 29194581 }. Stage
according to the Lugano classification is the most important predictor of outcome { 25113753 }.

#25974
Nodular lymphocyte-predominant HL
Low power view shows a vaguely nodular pattern in an intraparotid lymph node.

L#25977

CD20+ LP cells are rosetted by CD20-ve cells in a nodular background rich in small B
lymphocytes.
R#25980
Nodular lymphocyte-predominant HL
LP cells are rosetted by PD1+ follicular T helper cells.

L#25982
Classic HL, mixed cellularity
Normal nasopharyngeal tissue is replaced by a diffuse lymphoid proliferation with an
inflammatory background and dispersed H/RS cells.
R#25983
Classic HL, mixed cellularity
Diffuse inflammatory background with dispersed H/RS cells.

995

L#25984
Classic HL, mixed cellularity
The H/RS cells express CD30.
R#31157
Nodular lymphocyte-predominant HL
Nodular pattern with predominance of small lymphocytes and scattered large atypical LP
cells.

L#31158
Nodular lymphocyte-predominant HL
Oct-2 immunostaining highlights the nuclei of the LP cells in the nodular background.
R#28278
Hodgkin lymphoma lymphocyte predominant
The parotid salivary gland is pushed aside by the neoplastic proliferation. This lymphocyte
predominant proliferation contains many LP cells along with a mixed inflammatory cell
infiltrate, including eosinophils.

996

Histocytic and dendritic cell tumours

Juvenile xanthogranuloma
Definition
Juvenile xanthogranuloma (JXG) is a clonal infiltrate of non-Langerhans cell histiocytes with a
favorable outcome except in uncommon cases with systemic dissemination.
ICD-O coding
9749/1 Juvenile xanthogranuloma
ICD-11 coding
2B31.0 Juvenile xanthogranuloma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Xanthogranuloma; solitary xanthogranuloma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
JXG are generally confined to the skin, with a predilection for head and neck (HN) (42%), upper
trunk, and proximal extremities { 12717244 ; 15613853 ; 32468628 }. Solitary extra-cutaneous
JXG are rare with 40-50% being localized in HN, mostly eye and ocular adnexa (iris, conjunctiva,
eyelid, choroid, orbit), oral cavity (gingival, buccal mucosa, midline hard palate) nasal cavity and
paranasal sinuses, subglottis, and ear { 12717244 ; 26744443 ; 26189188 }. Solitary spinal or
intracranial JXG (mostly extra-axial, in the skull base) or within skeletal muscles, are rare
{ 25281434 ; 29488239 ; 26744443 }.
Systemic
JXG shows
multivisceral
involvement
{ 16462582 ; 19515049 }.
Clinical features
Cutaneous JXG is a papulo-nodular lesion, measuring from 5-10 mm up to 100 mm
{ 7577591 ; 12717244 ; 29488239 }. JXG is generally asymptomatic and evolves from a raised,
pink to darker brown lesion with progressive flattening. JXG may disappear within months or years
with scarring or anetoderma { 12717244 ; 15613853 ; 32468628 }. In adults, lesions are large,
solitary, and persistent { 16319479 }. Solitary ocular JXG and those associated with skin lesions
(reported
in
less
than 0.4%
of
cutaneous
JXG)
are generally unilateral
{ 8609257 ; 26189188 ; 29488239 }. CNS JXG can cause seizures, hydrocephalus or diabetes
insipidus (in case of pituitary involvement) { 8765620 ; 12717244 ; 15613853 }. JXG arising within
the upper respiratory tract or oral cavity may appear as a verrucous, pedunculated or fibroma-like
lesion.
Epidemiology
JXG is a rare disease typical of young children. The incidence is unknown and is probably
underestimated. According to Kiel’s tumour registry, JXG represent 0.5% of all paediatric tumours
{ 12717244 ; 32468628 }. About 5% of patients show multiple cutaneous lesions { 12717244 }.
Systemic JXG occurs in <5% of cases { 12717244 ; 15613853 ; 32468628 }. More than 90% of
JXG arise within the first year of life (20% are congenital) and show a predilection for males
(male:female ratio 1.4:1) { 15613853 }. JXG in adults is rare. JXG may be present in 13-15% of
patients affected by neurofibromatosis 1 { 15078345 }. A higher prevalence of juvenile
myelomonocytic leukemia in NF1 patients affected by JXG is debated { 7632061 ; 28189268 }. JXG
is also reported in Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome { 23152840 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Pathogenetic mechanisms in JXG are poorly understood. Rare cases with mixed LCH and JXG
phenotype suggest a common cell(s) of origin, similar to LCH and ECD
{ 28566492 ; 28512190 }. Expression of fascin and coagulation factor XIIIa support a histogenetic
link with dermal macrophages { 17786242 ; 34620874 }. Recent studies suggest a pathogenetic
role of the MAPK/ERK pathway. Activating mutations of NRAS, KRAS, ARAF, MAP2K1 and
a RNF11::BRAF fusion have been reported in 7 out of 12 systemic JXG { 28752840 }. Further
somatic activating mutations including CSF-1R (in 10%) or NTRK1 fusions (in 12%) and, less
frequently, mutations in CSF-3R, KIT, ALK, MET, JAK3, RAF1, as well as RAF fusions have been
reported in 55 JXG (20 in HN) { 31768065 }. PIK3CD mutations, identified in few cases, suggest a
role of PI3K pathway in JXG pathogenesis { 25202140 ; 26637772 }. BRAF p.V600E mutations
997

recently described in systemic JXG with CNS involvement, raise the possibility that this group
might represent variants of Erdheim-Chester disease { 31685033 }. JXG in monozygotic twins
suggests genetic transmission { 21091658 ; 31768065 }.
Macroscopic appearance
JXG appears as a pale yellow-tan, dome-shaped lesion. Early lesions appear as pink macules.
Histopathology
JXG are non-encapsulated, well circumscribed lesions centered in the papillary dermis, with
extension into subcutis in larger lesions. Overlying skin may ulcerate. The infiltrate is composed
of histiocytes intermixed with variable numbers of lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells,
neutrophils, and mast cells. Dilated vascular channels may be present.The histological features
may vary according to the temporal evolution. In early lesions small oval-spindle histiocytes with
eosinophilic cytoplasm predominate, whereas large xanthomatous histiocytes and multinucleated
Touton giant cells, characterized by foamy cytoplasm and multiple nuclei in a ring-like arrangement
are more frequent in older JXG. Mitoses may be seen { 12717244 ; 24582118 }.
Histiocytes are positive for CD68, CD163, factor XIIIa, CD4, CD14 and fascin. CD1a
and langerin are negative; S-100 protein is often negative but may show variable light staining.
Differential
Early JXG with rare xanthomatous cells are differentiated from Langherans’ cell histiocytosis by
negative immunostaining for S100 and CD1a. In older children, negative staining for
ALK differentiates JXG from ALK-related histiocytosis and epithelioid fibrous histiocytoma, while
lack of characteristic follicolosebaceous induction, peripheral entrapment of collagen and
haemosiderin deposits exclude a lipidized fibrous histiocytoma.
"Benign cephalic histiocytosis" is a rare self-healing asymptomatic papular eruption predominantly
in the head and neck of young children, histologically and immunohistochemically
undistinguishable from JXG { 15613853 ; 23551579 }.
Cytology
Cytology is rarely performed. Smears show mononuclear or multinucleated histiocytes with
reniform/oval nuclei intermixed with variable numbers of lymphocytes, neutrophils, and eosinophils.
Giant Touton cells or foreign body cells may be present. In older lesions fibroblasts are also seen
{ 21491610 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
none
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Histiocytes intermixed with variable numbers of inflammatory cells.
Positivity of histiocytes for CD4 and CD68/CD163 and negativity for CD1a/langerin.
Desirable:
Touton giant cells
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
JXG has an excellent prognosis and may resolve spontaneously. Ocular JXG may be complicated
by glaucoma, uveitis and blindness. JXG in the CNS and systemic JXG may behave aggressively
with an overall mortality rate of 5–10% in systemic JXG { 12717244 ; 26637774 ; 32383820 }.
#26571

Juvenile xanthogranuloma
Classical morphology displaying small
histiocytes with ovoid nuclei and pink
cytoplasm admixed with xanthomatous
cells and Touton giant cells.

#26572
Juvenile xanthogranuloma family
In some cases the classical morphology, including xanthomatous cells and Touton giant
cells is lacking, but there is a JXG phenotype. The histiocytes infiltrate through the orbital
muscle. This case harbored a CSF1R in-frame deletion.

L#26573
Juvenile xanthogranuloma family
CD163 is diffusely expressed in an orbital soft tissue JXG.
R#26574
Juvenile xanthogranuloma family
Factor XIIIa is diffusely expressed in an orbital soft tissue JXG.

999

#26575
Juvenile xanthogranuloma family
The histiocytes in JXG family lesions have ample pink cytoplasm and bland round to
ovoid nuclei with variable, mild nuclear indentation, without grooves and complex folding,
distinguishing them from those in LCH.

Erdheim Chester disease
Definition
Erdheim-Chester disease (ECD) is a systemic histiocytic neoplasm characterized by multi-organ
proliferation of mature histiocytes in a background of fibrosis.
ICD-O coding
9749/3 Erdheim–Chester disease
ICD-11 coding
2B31.Y & XH1VJ3 Other specified histiocytic or dendritic cell neoplasms & Erdheim–Chester
disease
Related terminology
Not recommended: Lipogranulomatosis; lipoid granulomatosis; lipid (cholesterol) granulomatosis;
polyostotic sclerosing histiocytosis
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
ECD is a multisystem disease and can affect virtually any organ system but the lymph nodes,
spleen, and liver are generally spared { 32187362 ; 28553668 ; 29396850 ; 31472931 } (#26435).
Bilateral symmetric osteosclerosis of the lower extremity long bones is characteristic of ECD and
presents in 80-95% of cases (#25581). Perinephric (55-65%) and periaortic (50-80%) infiltrations
are also frequent. ECD involvement of the head and neck is characterized by xanthelasma-like
lesions (#25583) around the eyes, face, and neck (33%), uni- or bilateral orbital infiltration (30%),
and sclerosis of facial sinuses (~47% by imaging studies) (#25581). Rare cases with intraoral
involvement have been described in the course of the disease process rather than at initial
presentation { 29192649 ; 29097410 ; 29188284 ; 28553668 ; 33582038 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms are variable depending on site and extent of multisystem involvement. Patients mostly
present with lower extremity bone pain and fatigue. ECD can manifest with multisystem
involvement with cerebellar signs, diabetes insipidus, panhypopituitarism, pulmonary and cardiac
disease,
urinary
tract
obstruction,
and
retroperitoneal
fibrosis
{ 32187362 ; 28553668 ; 29396850 ; 31472931 }. Patients with involvement of maxilla/mandible
may show tooth loss { 33582038 }, while sinus involvement may mimic chronic sinusitis with
thickening of the walls of paranasal sinuses and facial pain. Orbital lesions may present as
exophthalmos or diploplia { 30337283 }. Underlying clonal haematopoiesis or overt
myeloid neoplasms (mostly chronic myelomonocytic leukaemia) can be present in as many as
1000

42.5%
and
15.8%
of
cases,
respectively
{ 28679734 ; 31221777 ; 31624111 ; 31475353 ; 33067622 }. Those patients associated with
clonal haematopoiesis and/or myeloid neoplasms present at older age than those without. ECD
can be associated with Langerhans cell histiocytosis or Rosai–Dorfman disease, as well as
autoimmune diseases { 24894769 ; 26966089 ; 30923093 ; 31123032 }.
Epidemiology
ECD is a rare but probably an underdiagnosed disease. It is a disease of middle-aged adults (a
mean age of ~50 years) with an age range of 15–80 years at diagnosis and a male predominance
(~70%) { 32187362 ; 28553668 ; 29396850 ; 31472931 ; 29192649 }. It is extremely rare in
children, in whom it is frequently associated with Langerhans cell histiocytosis
{ 19755920 ; 26466952 ; 30265230 }.
Etiology
ECD is a clonal neoplastic disorder with no known genetic predisposition
{ 22879539 ; 26566875 ; 29192649 ; 31768065 } although, an underlying clonal hematopoiesis
has been described in over two-thirds of the cases, a much higher rate than reported in the general
population { 33067622 }.
Pathogenesis
Gain-of-function alterations activating mostly the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathway
and cell-proliferation related pathways other than MAPK have been identified in nearly all cases
{ 22879539 ; 26566875 ; 29192649 ; 30262559 ; 31768065 } (See table #26437). Additionally, a
characteristic cytokine signature responsible for local activation and recruitment of histiocytes has
been identified { 21205927 }.
Functional studies have shown that some cases derive from haematopoietic/myeloid progenitors
{ 28566492 }. The high frequency of myeloid neoplasms and clonal haematopoiesis associated
with ECD further support the clonal myeloid origin in at least a proportion of ECD patients. A model
with sequential acquisition of driver mutations leading to development of ECD and myeloid
neoplasms from underlying clonal haematopoiesis has been proposed. However, ECD can occur
irrespective
of
the
presence
of
clonal
haematopoiesis
{ 33067622 ; 29192649 ; 28679734 ; 31624111 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Most of the samples obtained for diagnosis correspond to biopsies of skin or bone. They are usually
firm and sclerotic. Cut surface of lesions may be yellow in appearance.
Histopathology
The infiltrate is composed of histiocytes with foamy and/or eosinophilic cytoplasm admixed with
chronic inflammatory cells and marked fibrosis. The lesional histiocytes may be sparse and may
have distorted shapes. Eosinophils are rare to absent. Touton giant cells can be seen but are not
always present { 29192649 }.
Histologic features of ECD xanthelasma-like lesions can closely resemble classic xanthelasma.
ECD xanthelasma-like lesions infiltrate the reticular dermis more frequently, display more
multinucleated and Touton giant cells, and show less extensive fibrosis than classic xanthelasma
lesions { 26785805 ; 29192649 }.
Immunohistochemistry:
The immunophenotype is non-specific with the expression of CD163, CD68, CD14, FXIIIa, and
CD4. CD1a and Langerin (CD207) stains are consistently negative. There is variable staining for
S100 protein in up to one third of the cases although extensive expression is not seen { 29192649 }.
Immunostaining with BRAF p.V600E antibody for the detection of BRAF p.Val600Glu mutation can
be useful, although some cases showing discordant results with the molecular techniques have
been reported { 29192649 }. Histiocytes are positive for phospho-ERK in most cases thus
confirming activation of the MAPK pathway, however this staining is sensitive to fixation conditions
and may have false negativity.
Cytology
Cytology samples are mostly of low cellularity due to associated fibrosis. They may show bland
foamy histiocytes including
occasional
binucleated
and
multinucleated cells without
grooves admixed with chronic inflammatory cells. However, the diagnosis of ECD typically requires
a biopsy of involved tissues { 22279491 ; 23447119 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology

1001

Demonstration of a corroborating genomic alteration may aid in diagnosis and treatment
(#26437). A BRAF p.Val600Glu
mutation
is
present
in
50–60%
of
cases
of
ECD { 22879539 ; 26566875 ; 29192649 ; 31768065 }. Patients
without BRAF p.Val600Glu
frequently
have
other
mutations
mostly
on MAP2K1, KRAS,
or NRAS { 22879539 ; 26566875 ; 29192649 ; 31768065 }. BRAF indels or BRAF fusions
may
also be present, which may require RNA sequencing in patients without point mutations in kinases
{ 31768065 }. It should be noted that mutant allele frequency is low (< 5%) in a quarter of cases,
which may pose a diagnostic difficulty { 30262559 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Collections of histiocytes with bland cytologic features, foamy and/or eosinophilic
cytoplasm, and non-Langerhans cell phenotype in the appropriate clinical and radiological context.
Desirable: Corroborating genetic alteration.
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Most patients require systemic therapy. Traditionally, drugs such as peginterferon and cladribine
have been used as initial treatments. However, in recent years, MAPK pathway alterations have
been recognized and targeted therapies with kinase inhibitors are used more frequently
{ 29396850 ; 22300602 ; 32187362 }. Recent studies reported a 43–100% response rate and
almost no disease progression with the use of BRAF and/or MEK inhibitors
{ 30867592 ; 28667012 ; 29188284 }. Prognosis depends on site and extent of the involvement.
CNS involvement and multisystemic disease are poor prognostic factors { 21239701 }.

L#25581
Erdheim-Chester disease
Whole body PET-CT scan (maximum intensity projection) showing involvement of
mandible and long bones of legs

Xanthelasma-like lesions are commonly located on the inner canthus of the eyelids.

1005

Rosai-Dorfman disease
Definition
Rosai-Dorfman disease (RDD) is a histiocytic disease with characteristic morphology, S-100
protein positivity, and often genetic mutations in the mitogen-activated protein kinase/extracellular
signal-related kinase (MAPK/ERK) pathway.
ICD-O coding
9749/3 Rosai–Dorfman disease
ICD-11 coding
EK92 Histiocytoses of uncertain malignant potential
Related terminology
Acceptable: Destombes-Rosai-Dorfman
disease;
sinus
histiocytosis
with
massive
lymphadenopathy
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
RDD most often involves lymph nodes, particularly in the cervical region. About 30-40% of patients
have extranodal disease. In the head and neck, the nasal cavity, paranasal sinuses, orbit and oral
cavity are most commonly involved. Less common sites include the salivary glands, soft tissues
and facial bones { 8491488 }.
Clinical features
Most patients present with painless, slow growing lymphadenopathy or an extranodal mass. About
one third of patients have B-type symptoms.
Epidemiology
RDD has a worldwide distribution. The median age of patients is in the third decade with a wide
age range { 2180012 }. In the United States, there are about 100 new cases/year { 29720485 }.
The disease affects all races but is more common in African Americans.
Etiology
Etiology is unknown, but at least a subset of cases appears to be neoplastic. Assessment of the
human androgen receptor gene (HUMARA) in a small number of cases has shown that RDD is
polyclonal { 8547085 }.
Pathogenesis
The pathogenesis is incompletely understood. A subset of cases (~50%) carries gene mutations in
the MAPK/ERK pathway and appears to be neoplastic { 28664935 ; 29720485 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Lymph nodes are enlarged and matted often forming multinodular masses.
Histopathology
Lymph nodes show expanded sinuses filled by large histiocytes with plate-like cytoplasm
containing central hypochromatic nuclei and distinct, round nucleoli. The histiocytes usually show
emperipolesis manifested by multiple small lymphocytes within the histiocyte cytoplasm. Between
sinuses the lymph node contains many small lymphocytes and plasma cells. At low power
magnification, the impression is that of light and dark zones or a striated appearance. Neutrophils
and less often microabscesses can be present. Fibrosis can be marked imparting a storiform
pattern. Cytologic atypia, multinucleation and mitoses are uncommon. Eosinophils are absent.
In lymph nodes, rare RDD cases can be associated with lymphoma of various types { 30323237 }.
Lymphoma-associated RDD is usually small and focally involves lymph nodes.
At extranodal sites, the striated low appearance is less common and emperipolesis can be rare or
absent. The mixed inflammatory infiltrate including plasma cells, lymphocytes, and neutrophils, with
variable numbers of histiocytes, can make the diagnosis of RDD challenging. Entities in the
differential diagnosis of extranodal RDD include infectious processes, IgG4-related sclerosing
diseases, and other histiocytic disorders.
The
histiocytes of
RDD are
strongly
positive
for
S100
protein
and
OCT2
{ 2180012 ; 28664935 ; 33177341 }. The histiocytes are also commonly positive for p-ERK or
cyclin D1 indicating activation of the MAP/ERK pathway { 30323237 ; 31172509 }.
RDD histiocytes are weakly positive or negative for histiocyte-associated antigens such as CD4,
CD11c, CD68 and CD163 { 28664935 }, and are negative for CD1A, CD207/langerin and pan B-

1006

cell and T-cell antigens. Background small lymphocytes represent a mixture of T- and B-cells.
Plasma cells are polytypic and IgG4-positive cells can be numerous.
Cytology
Aspiration smears show many lymphocytes, plasma cells and large histiocytes often with
emperipolesis.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Up
to
50%
of
RDD cases
have
mutations
in
the
MAPK/ERK pathway,
including KRAS, NRAS, MAP2K1, ARAF, CSF1R and
rarely BRAF V600E
{ 28664935 ; 29720485 ; 31172509 ; 32601132 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Large histiocytes with characteristic morphology; S100 protein positive.
Desirable: Emperipolesis; MAPK/ERK pathway mutations
Staging
No staging system has been developed. Most patients have unilateral or bilateral lymphadenopathy
or localized extranodal disease.
Prognosis and prediction
Most patients have asymptomatic disease and observation is adequate. About 50% of patients
undergo complete resolution. A subset of patients has persistent disease or RDD can relapse,
requiring therapy that may include complete excision, radiation therapy or chemotherapy. In this
patient subset, MEK (encoded by MAP2K1) inhibitors may be helpful. Rare RDD patients have
familial disease. Workup to exclude H syndrome (SLC29A3 mutation) or autoimmune
lymphoproliferative syndrome (TNFRSF6 mutation) may be indicated { 20140240 }.

L#25616Rosai-Dorfman disease involving a lymph node. At low magnification the lymph node
has striated appearance due to distended pale sinuses and intervening darker cords of
lymphocytes and plasma cells.
R#25617Rosai-Dorfman disease involving lymph node. A large histiocyte with numerous
intracytoplasmic lymphocytes (emperipolesis) is present in a distended sinus

1007

#25618Rosai-Dorfman disease
involving lymph node.
Numerous characteristic histiocytes
are positive for S100 protein. The
reaction product also outlines
unstained small lymphocytes within
the histiocyte cytoplasm.

L#25620Rosai-Dorfman disease involving lymph node. The histiocytes are positive for
phosphorylated ERK indicating that the MAP-ERK pathway is activated, likely due to a mutated
gene in the pathway.
R#25621 Rosai-Dorfman disease involving parotid gland. In this field, Rosai-Dorfman disease
infiltrates and distorts the acinar tissue of the parotid gland. Although there are many
characteristic histiocytes, emperipolesis is relatively minimal, as is common at extranodal sites of
disease.

Langerhans cell histiocytosis
Definition
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a clonal neoplastic proliferation of myeloid dendritic
cells expressing LC phenotype.
ICD-O coding
9751/1 Langerhans cell histiocytosis, NOS
9751/3 Langerhans cell histiocytosis, disseminated
ICD-11 coding
2B31.2 & XH1J18 Langerhans cell histiocytosis & Langerhans cell histiocytosis NOS.
Related terminology
Not
recommended:
Langerhans
cell
granulomatosis; histiocytosis
X;
eosinophilic
granuloma; Hand–Schüller–Christian disease; Letterer–Siwe disease.
Subtype(s)
Single-system Langerhans cell histiocytosis, with one organ/system involved (unifocal or
multifocal); multisystem Langerhans cell histiocytosis, with two or more organs/systems involved
Localization

1008

Single-system LCH of the head and neck affects the cranial and facial bones, skin, gingiva, or
cervical lymph nodes. In multi-system LCH, other organs (liver, spleen, and/or bone-marrow) are
also involved { 25200852 ; 29164473 }.
Clinical features
Unifocal LCH preferentially affects older children and adults, multifocal LCH young children, and
multisystem LCH infants. Oral lesions represent the commonest manifestation, mainly in the form
of an intra-osseous ill-defined unilocular radiolucency with gingival involvement and tooth mobility.
Diabetes insipidus occurs in case of hypothalamic pituitary involvement. Other findings include
facial swelling and cervical lymphadenopathy. In multisystem LCH, cutaneous lesions may precede
visceral compromise { 25200852 ; 29164473 }.
Epidemiology
The annual incidence in children and adults is about 5 and 1-2 cases/1 million population,
respectively { 18260117 }. The M:F ratio is 1.2:1 { 30157397 }. The disease is more common
among European descendants and Hispanics. LCH can be associated with Erdheim–Chester
Disease { 26966089 }.
Etiology
Unknown.
Pathogenesis
The cell of origin is closer to a myeloid dendritic cell than to an epidermal LC { 20220088 }.
MAPK pathway mutations at a pluripotent haematopoietic, tissue-restricted, or local precursor level
might give rise to multisystem, multifocal, or unifocal LCH, respectively { 30157397 }. These
mutations may favour tissue-site accumulation via disrupted cell migration and apoptosis inhibition
{ 29263218 }. Rare familial cases are reported { 11452942 }. About 30% of cases show
clonal IGH, IGK, and/or T-cell receptor gene rearrangements { 20551822 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Not clinically relevant.
Histopathology
Diagnostic LCH cells are oval (10–15 μm diameter) with grooved, folded, indented, or lobed nuclei,
fine chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, and thin nuclear membranes { 12121233 }. Nuclear atypia
is minimal, but mitotic activity is variable (like the Ki-67 index) and can be high without atypical
forms { 12121233 }. The cytoplasm is moderately abundant, slightly eosinophilic, and devoid of
dendritic processes { 12121233 }. The characteristic milieu includes variable numbers of
eosinophils, histiocytes, neutrophils, small lymphocytes, and plasma cells { 12121233 }.
Occasionally, eosinophilic microabscesses with central necrosis are found. LCH cells predominate
in early lesions but decrease in late lesions, with increased foamy macrophages and fibrosis. The
ultrastructural hallmark is the cytoplasmic Birbeck granule (200–400 nm long and 33 nm wide, with
a tennis-racket shape and a zipper-like appearance) { 12121233 }. LCH cells express CD1a,
CD207/langerin, S100, CD68, and HLA-DR { 30157397 }. The BRAF p.Val600Glu mutation can be
identified by a specific antibody { 28219109 ; 30923995 }. Rare cases associated with follicular
lymphoma or B- or T-lymphoblastic leukaemia are considered a transdifferentiation phenomenon
and behave more aggressively { 23759932 ; 15925822 ; 19642834 }.
Cytology
LCH cell clusters are diagnostic, but caution is needed in lymph nodes because
paracortical dendritic/LCs are indistinguishable from sinus-based LCH cells.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
In > 85% of cases, MAPK-pathway gene mutations are recorded, affecting BRAF or
alternatively MAP2K1 or much more rarely ARAF. ERBB3, NRAS, and KRAS mutations can also
occur in adults { 30157397 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Typical LC morphology; CD1a, S100, and CD207 (langerin) positivity
Desirable: Detection of MAPK-pathway mutations.
Staging
Based on the subtypes ± risk-organ involvement.
Prognosis and prediction
Stage is a more important prognosticator than age at presentation, with ≥ 99% survival for
unifocal disease and < 20% mortality in risk-organ multisystem LCH with prolonged therapy
{ 23589673 ; 12376981 ; 11496348 ; 30157397 }. Progression from unifocal to multisystem LCH
can occur, most commonly in infants with hypothalamic-pituitary involvement. Multisystem LCH can

1009

be complicated by macrophage activation syndrome { 11910507 }. BRAF p.Val600Glu mutation
correlates with increased relapse-risk, resistance to first-line therapy and neurodegenerative CNS
LCH { 24638167 ; 27382093 }. In previously diagnosed LCH patients with BRAF p.Val600Glu
mutation, additional lesions with BRAF–positive, CD1a/CD207-negative histiocytes may still
represent active disease { 28194436 }.

L#26311 Langerhans cell histiocytosis. In this ear canal polyp with an ulcerated surface the
submucosa is expanded with lesional cells.
R#26314 Langerhans cell histiocytosis. The cells are bland with ample cytoplasm and
characteristic grooved and folded nuclei with fine chromatin. Occasional mitoses can be found.

L#26318 Langerhans cell histiocytosis. Langerin (CD207) staining is granular and cytoplasmic,
and serves as a surrogate for cytoplasmic Birbeck granule detection.
R#26319 Langerhans cell histiocytosis. The mutant specific BRAF VE1 immunostain corresponds
to the BRAF p.V600E mutation when dark granular staining in seen in the cytoplasm of lesional
cells.

#26320 Langerhans cell
histiocytosis. Lesional
cell clusters are often
admixed with
inflammatory cells.
Ulceration of the surface
may introduces more
neutrophils and
hemorrhage.

#26325 Langerhans cell histiocytosis. pERK expression is nuclear with variable cytoplasmic
staining, corresponding to up-regulation of the ERK pathway. Staining in endothelial nuclei serves
as an internal control.
#26951 Langerhans cell histiocytosis. The Langerhans cells have ample cytoplasm surrounding
coffee-bean shaped or cleaved nuclei. There are numerous eosinophils in the background.

Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma
Definition
Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma (FDCS) is a tumour exhibiting phenotypic features of
mesenchymal FDCs.
ICD-O coding
9758/3 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma
ICD-11 coding
2B31.5 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: Follicular dendritic cell tumour
Subtype(s)
EBV-positive inflammatory follicular dendritic cell (FDC) sarcoma
Localization

1011

The majority of cases in the head and neck are isolated and extranodal, involving the palatine
tonsil, nasopharynx, parapharyngeal space, soft and hard palate, retromolar trigone
{ 25831474 ; 23755890 }.
Less
frequently,
FDCS involves cervical
lymph
nodes
{ 25831474 ; 27318412 }. The EBV-positive inflammatory follicular dendritic cell sarcoma occurs
with near exclusivity in the liver and spleen { 24966952 } [[DIG5]] with rare head and neck examples
{ 33264138 }.
Clinical features
Patients with nodal tumours typically present with slow growing neck masses, asymptomatic or
painful. In the Waldeyer ring, there may be intraoral swelling or dysphagia. Systemic symptoms are
rare but may include fever, weight loss, and fatigue. Most patients (80–90%) have localized disease
at presentation { 23684177 ; 25831474 }.
Epidemiology
FDCS accounts for < 1% of all head and neck tumours, with no gender predilection. While
occurring in all ages (9-90), the median age is typically in the late 4th to 5th decade
{ 23755890 ; 25831474 } with exceptional paediatric cases { 23684177 }.
Etiology
No true risk factors or genetic predisposition exists. Association with hyaline-vascular Castleman
disease is estimated in a minority (7.5-15%) { 11422494 ; 8172326 ; 18596475 ; 27318412 }. A
loose association with autoimmunity has been noted in case reports, including paraneoplastic
pemphigus with or without myasthenia gravis { 23755890 }.
Pathogenesis
Non-neoplastic FDC are derived from common perivascular progenitors (i.e. vascular mural cells)
rather than hematopoietic precursors { 26966089 }. A hyperplasia–dysplasia–neoplasia model of
FDC proliferation has been proposed for the link between hyaline-vascular Castleman disease and
FDCS. The shared clonality and overexpression of EGFR in the dysplastic FDCs and
FDCS provides further evidence that the two conditions are related { 14562277 ; 23755890 }.
Macroscopic appearance
The tumours are solitary, circumscribed masses with a fleshy cut surface. Areas of haemorrhage
and necrosis may be present. Average sizes in the head and neck are 40 - 50 mm { 25917851 }.
Histopathology
In both nodal and extranodal sites, FDCS shows a pushing invasion, composed of spindled to ovoid
cells arranged in fascicular, whorled, or storiform patterns, accompanied by an admixture of small
lymphocytes or aggregates around blood vessels. The background matrix ranges from myxoid to
densely hyaline. The tumour cells have a moderate amount of pale eosinophilic cytoplasm and
indistinct cell borders, imparting a syncytial appearance. The nuclei have vesicular or granular finely
dispersed chromatin, small distinct nucleoli, and a smooth nuclear membrane. Nuclear
pseudoinclusions, binucleated (Reed-Sternberg-like), and multinucleated cells are seen. The
mitotic rate is usually 0–10 mitoses per mm2. High-grade nuclear pleomorphism/anaplasia, atypical
mitoses, and coagulative necrosis are uncommon.
Extranodal tumours may cause diagnostic confusion with other head/neck tumours, histiocytic
tumours, lymphomas, and sarcomas { 32547956 }. Expression of a FDC marker is essential for
diagnosis but can be variably lost or only focally positive; a broad immunohistochemical panel
should include CD21, CD23, CD35, clusterin, CXCL13 (focal cytoplasmic/Golgi dot-like pattern),
while NGFR (p75NTR /CD271), and podoplanin (D2-40) are also expressed. Exceptionally, the cells
can be variably positive for cytokeratin, EMA, TTF1, fascin, S100, CD4, CD68, CD14 and CD30.
Cytokeratin,
ALK,
CD3,
CD20
are
typically
negative.
{ 11796841 ; 18774654 ; 21836499 ; 27318412 ; 32547956 }.
Cytology
The cells borders are indistinct even on smears. Cell block preparation with immunohistochemical
staining is essential.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
A small percentage of cases have demonstrated BRAF p.V600E mutations { 24720374 } but other
MAPK pathway mutations are uncommon { 23888072 ; 28695297 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Spindled to ovoid cells arranged in fascicular, whorled, or storiform patterns with
expression of one or more FDC markers by immunohistochemistry.
Desirable: Comprehensive molecular analysis and EGFR expression.
Staging

1012

Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
FDCS is a low- to intermediate-grade malignancy with a local recurrence rate of 28-40% and a
distant metastasis rate of >25% { 9010103 ; 25917851 ; 23684177 }. The overall survival is (91%,
2-years; 81%, 5-years) and disease-specific survival rates (64%, 2-years; 34%, 5-years). The
documented mortality rate for FDCS was 16.7% based on 42 cases { 9010103 }. Surgery is
potentially curative for early-stage disease, but late recurrence and metastasis can occur many
years after initial presentation { 10931467 }. Ligand-dependent EGFR activation, along
with expression of the inhibitory immune receptor PD-1 and its ligands PD-L1 and PD-L2, could be
potential therapeutic targets along with tyrosine kinase inhibitors in exceptional cases
{ 23888072 ; 26298731 ; 28130401 ; 28695297 }.
The lung, liver, and lymph nodes are common sites of metastasis { 23684177 }. A simple, risk
stratification was developed based on tumour size and histology including: Low risk (16% of local
recurrence, size < 50 mm, low- or high-grade histology), intermediate risk (46% of local recurrence,
size ≥50 mm, low-grade histology), and high risk (73% of recurrence, ≥50 mm, high-grade
histology) { 20503450 }. Other proposed poor prognostic factors include disseminated disease,
extensive necrosis, high mitotic count (>2 mitoses per mm2), and significant nuclear pleomorphism
{ 9010103 ; 20503450 ; 22772431 ; 23755890 ; 25310210 ; 25917851 }.

L#26298 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma. The nodal architecture is replaced by the follicular
dendritic cell sarcoma infiltrate and shows capsular reaction and central focus of necrosis (lower
right).
R#26299 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma. There are plump, spindly cells with indistinct borders,
moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm, and ovoid nuclei with vesicular chromatin. There are a few
intermixed lymphocytes. In this example there are frequent mitotic figures.

L#26300 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma. Positive membranous staining for CD21.
R#26301 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma. Positive membranous staining for CD35.

L#26302 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma. CXCL13, while not specific, shows cytoplasmic staining
often with a Golgi/cytoplasmic dot-like staining pattern.
R#26303 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma. PD-L1. Expression of the inhibitory immune receptor
PD-1 and its ligands PD-L1 and PD-L2, could be potential therapeutic targets.

1014

#27197 Follicular dendritic cell sarcoma of the tonsil. The tumour (central field) forms an
expansile, pushing growth in the tonsil. Ulceration is present at the left.

borders, vesicular nuclei and distinct nucleoli. There are many intermingled small lymphocytes.

1015

12. Melanocytic tumours
Introduction
Mucosal melanoma

Introduction
Mucosal melanoma arising in the sinonasal region, oral cavity and larynx are combined and
presented in this chapter on melanocytic tumours rather than separated based on anatomic site as
described in the previous edition.
Rare compared to cutaneous melanoma, head and neck mucosal melanomas are biologically
distinct from both cutaneous and uveal melanomas. Mucosal melanomas of the head and neck
harbor distinct genetic alterations characterized by numerous chromosomal structural aberrations
and copy number alterations. The common MAPK activating mutations seen in cutaneous
melanoma (BRAF, NRAS, NF1), are much less common, with only 28% of head and neck mucosal
melanomas showing any of these mutations { 28325255 ; 31655118 }. Knowledge of the mutational
landscape of head and neck mucosal melanoma is evolving, and potential therapeutic targets are
being identified.

Mucosal melanoma
Definition
Mucosal melanoma (MM) is a malignant neoplasm of mucosal melanocytes.
ICD-O coding
8720/3
Mucosal
melanoma
8746/3
Mucosal
lentiginous
melanoma
8721/3 Nodular melanoma
8720/3 Malignant melanoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2C11.2 & XH4846 Other specified malignant neoplasms of other or ill-defined digestive organs &
Malignant
melanoma
NOS
2C11.2 & XH5QP3 Other specified malignant neoplasms of other or ill-defined digestive organs &
Mucosal
lentiginous
melanoma
2C11.2 & XH4QG5 Other specified malignant neoplasms of other or ill-defined digestive organs &
Nodular melanoma
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
Desmoplastic mucosal melanoma; mucosal lentiginous melanoma
Localization
About 80% of head and neck MM involve the nasal cavity or septum, and maxillary sinus
{ 31670075 }. In the oral cavity, most cases arise on the maxillary gingiva and palate { 26753505 }.
Tumours arising in other paranasal sinuses, pharynx and larynx are rare { 28325255 ; 25242350 }.
Clinical features
Sinonasal MM present with non-specific symptoms simulating inflammatory conditions which can
delay diagnosis for months and sometimes years. Symptoms include nasal obstruction, sinus
congestion, rhinorrhea including melanorrhea and epistaxis. Endoscopy shows a polypoid fleshy
or pigmented mass. Oral MM is often asymptomatic, with dark brown to black macules, plaques or
nodules { 26753505 }. Some patients have a long history of mucosal melanosis. Ulceration is
common. Laryngeal MM may present with hoarseness or breathing problems. Lymph node and
distant metastases at presentation may be seen in approximately 20% and 10% of cases
respectively { 31670075 }.
Imaging

1016

Radiologic imaging reveals the extent of invasion of the primary tumour, including destruction of
cartilage and bone, orbital and intracranial extension. It also demonstrates lymph node and distant
metastases for staging purposes { 21295201 ; 10720207 }.
A combination of CT and MRI is used to assess the primary tumour- CT demonstrates bone erosion
and is useful for surgical planning. MRI better differentiates tumour from retained secretions and
demonstrates intracranial and intraorbital invasion more effectively.
MRI with contrast is the modality of choice to detect cerebral metastases with higher sensitivity and
specificity than contrasted enhanced CT.
With regards to nodal metastases, these are frequently T1W hyperintense and low signal on T2W,
unlike metastases from other primaries. Melanin content reduces T1 relaxation times, leading to
T1W high signal. Following contrast heterogeneous enhancement is often noted.
CT is the modality of choice for detecting distant metastases. The lungs and pleura are most
frequently affected, followed by the liver. Lesions may be hypervascular on CT in the arterial phase
and hypodense in the portal phase. Rim enhancement and central necrosis is common. High signal
is often noted on non-contrast T1W liver MRI.
When combined with dedicated CT, PET-CT is 98% sensitive and 94% specific for detecting
metastatic melanoma, sites normally being FDG avid { 17641374 }.
Epidemiology
Head and neck MM represent less than 1% of all melanomas and affect the sinonasal and oral
mucosa more frequently that other mucosal sites { 25242350 }. MM constitute about 4% of all
sinonasal tumours and 0.26% of all oral malignancies { 22024859 ; 20198705 ; 18675580 }.
Tumours affect older adults with a peak incidence in the seventh decade and are rare in the first
three decades of life { 31670075 ; 26753505 }. Predilection for sex or race have not been
conclusively demonstrated { 32057276 ; 28092366 ; 31670075 ; 25242350 }.
Etiology
MM are biologically distinct from cutaneous melanomas with no definitive precursor lesions but
may be associated with mucosal melanosis { 26399561 }. There is no known correlation with
exposure to
environmental carcinogens, e.g. tobacco,
chemicals, or
viruses
{ 28092366 ; 28325255 }.
Pathogenesis
The genomic landscape of MM is distinct from cutaneous and uveal melanomas
{ 31655118 ; 31358010 ; 31320640 ; 25147369 }. Compared to cutaneous melanomas, MM lack
the UV-signature, have a significantly lower somatic mutational burden, and show higher rates
of KIT mutations but a lower prevalence of NRAS and BRAF mutations (Table #25622)
{ 31655118 ; 31320640 ; 25147369 ; 23860532 }. Mutational profiles vary even across mucosal
subsites { 31320640 ; 31655118 ; 28296713 }. More recent studies show co-mutations
of NF1 and KIT { 28296713 }, mRNA splice factor SF3B1 alterations { 31655118 }, and cell cycle
aberrations { 31358010 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Sinonasal MM are polypoid, exophytic nodules that may be tan-grey firm to friable or deeply
pigmented. Oral MM tend to be pigmented flat lesions. Surface ulceration is frequent.
Histopathology
Tumour cells are polymorphic, displaying spindled, epithelioid, plasmacytoid or rhabdoid, round,
clear and undifferentiated cells, often within the same tumour. Epithelioid tumour cells show
amphophilic hyaline cytoplasm and atypical nuclei with high N:C ratio and prominent
macronucleoli arranged in solid sheet-like or nested growth patterns. Spindled tumour cells form
intersecting fascicles. Invasive MM is primarily submucosal but can invade the underlying bone and
cartilage. Mitoses are readily apparent and often atypical. Necrosis and discohesion frequently lead
to a pseudopapillary or peritheliomatous pattern. Pseudoalveolar pattern has been described
{ 26753505 }. Lymphovascular and perineural invasion are common. The intact respiratory or
squamous epithelium may show a pagetoid or lentiginous pattern of involvement with contiguous
or separate, single or clusters of intraepithelial malignant melanocytes. A lentiginous growth pattern
is more common in oral MM.
As many as 50% of sinonasal MM are amelanotic resulting in a broad differential diagnosis that
includes small blue cell tumours (olfactory neuroblastoma, Ewing sarcoma and
rhabdomyosarcoma), high-grade carcinomas (sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma, poorly
differentiated squamous cell carcinoma, NUT carcinoma, SMARCB1-deficient carcinoma),
neuroendocrine carcinomas, diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, myeloma and epithelioid sarcoma.

1017

Tumours with predominantly spindle cell morphology must be differentiated from various spindle
cell carcinoma and sarcomas.
Desmoplastic melanoma affects the epidermis adjacent to the oral cavity mucosa, usually lip, and
nasal vestibule where exposure to sun occurs. The tumours usually are comprised of amelanotic
spindle cells in a collagenous matrix which may be paucicellular and slightly atypical and may not
show mitosis, necrosis and lymphovascular invasion or express melanocytic markers but frequently
show perineural invasion { 30312883 }. Presence of junctional activity in the overlying epithelium
may aid in their distinction from other low grade spindle cell tumours { 15054741 }. In the absence
of an epithelial component and melanin pigment in a biopsy an adequate panel of
immunohistochemical markers are necessary { 32068045 ; 30312883 }.
Immunohistochemistry is essential to diagnose amelanotic tumours. S100 protein and melanocytic
markers (HMB45, tyrosinase, melan-A (MART1), MITF, and SOX10) show variable sensitivity
depending on tumour cell morphology with robust expression in epithelioid/undifferentiated cells
and weak and focal expression in spindled cells { 11395556 ; 12717245 }. Individual markers can
be negative and if melanoma is suspected a single marker should not be relied on for screening
purposes.
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration cytology, frequently obtained from metastatic sites, show features similar to
cutaneous melanomas, e.g. large discohesive epithelioid or spindled cells with or without melanin
pigment.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Although low yield, a minor subset will have a targetable molecular alteration by molecular
evaluation.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Highly atypical or undifferentiated malignant cells demonstrating melanocytic
differentiation (melanin-producing or expressing melanocytic markers by immunohistochemistry)
involving the submucosa in the absence of any evidence of metastasis from a cutaneous primary.
Desirable: Pagetoid or lentiginous involvement of epithelium. Evidence of melanin production.
Staging
All head and neck mucosal MM are T3–4 and stage III-IV in both the UICC and AJCC 8TH ed.
Prognosis and prediction
The 5 year overall survival of head and neck MM ranges from 20% to 50%
{ 24548569 ; 31670075 ; 28325255 }. Large tumour size and advanced age and stage including
distant metastasis portend poor prognosis. { 24548569 ; 31077407 }. MM of the oral and nasal
cavity tend to be localized (T3) and resectable and have better outcome than tumours involving
paranasal sinuses { 31670075 ; 31077407 }. Cutaneous melanoma prognostic factors (e.g. Clark
level of invasion and Breslow tumour thickness) do not apply. Surgical resection remains the
mainstay of therapy. Adjuvant radiotherapy improves local control but not survival { 27043023 }. In
a small number of cases targeted and immunotherapy appear to be promising { 31172258 }.

L#24982: Sinonasal mucosal melanoma, Post contrast MRI demonstrates a large SNMM
involving nasal cavity and maxillary, ethmoid, frontal and sphenoid sinuses with orbital and
intracranial (extradural) extension.
R#27396: Sinonasal mucosal melanoma, Tumor presents as a pigmented nodule in the nasal
cavity (same as WSI).
