13. Tumours and tumour-like lesions of the neck and lymph nodes
Introduction
Cysts and cyst-like lesions
Ranula
Lymphoepithelial cyst
Branchial cleft cyst
Thyroglossal duct cyst
Dermoid and teratoid cysts
Other tumours
Branchioma
Heterotopia-associated carcinoma
Squamous carcinoma of unknown primary site

Introduction
The neck is divided into the anterior and posterior triangles by the sternocleidomastoid muscle. The
contents of the neck include lymph nodes, soft tissues, vascular/lymphatic structures, and nerves.
Excluding cutaneous lesions/neoplasms, owing to the various tissue types, the neck is host to a
wide variety of primary nonneoplastic lesions and primary neoplasms. The spectrum of
hematolymphoid and mesenchymal neoplasms are beyond the scope of this section. This section
will focus on select tumours and tumour-like lesions. Noteworthy is the biphenotypic branchioma
previously referred to as ectopic hamartomatous thymoma. The inclusion of lymphoepithelial cyst
as an independent entity separate from branchial cleft cysts or HIV-related or HIV-unrelated cystic
lesions of the parotid gland, is debatable, but has been added to this section. Heterotopiaassociated carcinoma, an entity introduced in the prior edition, primarily relates to papillary thyroid
carcinoma arising in associated with heterotopic thyroid tissue in the neck, the latter associated
with thyroglossal duct cyst. A less well recognized phenomenon is the intranodal origin of salivary
gland lesions / neoplasms (benign and malignant) wholly without involvement of the salivary gland
parenchyma.
The most common malignancies of the neck are metastases from primary head and neck cancers
in particular squamous cell carcinoma and melanoma or, less commonly, from non-head and neck
primary cancers. However, the head and neck may harbour a small clinically occult primary
carcinoma with numerous positive neck nodes. Among such carcinomas of unknown primary
include oropharyngeal HPV-related carcinomas, oropharyngeal neuroendocrine carcinomas,
{ 21997688 ; 22301491 ; 30468701 } and nasopharyngeal EBV-related carcinomas. Pathologists
should have an understanding of the topographic anatomy of the cervical neck lymph node
sites/levels (see figure #28797) as well as an understanding of lymphatic drainage from site
specific mucosal sites of the head and neck as providing a possible clue to identifying the primary
source for the metastasis (Table #26425). It is equally imperative that clinicians provide exact
localization of the involved nodes as opposed to “neck mass” in order to assist the pathologist in
determining the origin for the metastasis.
Cysts and cyst-like lesions

Ranula
Definition
Ranula is a pseudocyst that develops from extravasation of mucin, typically located in the floor of
mouth adjacent to the sublingual gland.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA04.5 Mucocele of salivary gland

1025

Related terminology
Mucocele, retention cyst, mucus extravasation
Subtype(s)
Simple or
oral
ranula
and
plunging
or
cervical
ranula
{ 15356464 ; 22038368 ; 32109322 ; 32809690 }.
Localization
Ranula typically occurs in the lateral side of the floor of mouth in association with an excretory duct
of the sublingual gland. The plunging type of ranula passes around the posterior border of the
mylohyoid
muscle
or
through
a
hiatus
in
the
muscle
{ 15356464 ; 20054853 ; 22038368 ; 32809690 }.
Clinical features
Simple ranula presents as a painless mass in the floor of the mouth whereas a plunging ranula
presents as a neck swelling. Ranulas are mostly unilateral and unifocal but can cross the midline
{ 15356464 ; 22038368 ; 32109322 ; 32809690 }.
Imaging: CT and MRI images of a plunging ranula may demonstrate extension of the lesion into
the submandibular space (tail sign) { 31334858 }.
Epidemiology
Ranulas are rare and have a frequency of 0.2 cases per 1000 persons. The lesions affect patients
of any age but are more common in teenagers and young adults. No gender predilection has been
reported { 12618979 ; 32809690 }.
Etiology
The most common etiology is trauma to the sublingual gland, which is a spontaneous secretor that
produces a continuous flow of mucin { 15356464 ; 20054853 ; 22038368 ; 32809690 }. An
association between ranulas and primary Sjögren syndrome has been reported
{ 28964279 ; 31141241 ; 32803681 }.
Pathogenesis
Not relevant
Macroscopic appearance
The lesion appears as a blue and fluctuant dome-shaped mass that resembles a frog’s underbelly.
It ranges from 1 to 40 mm in size { 3343879 ; 32809690 }.
Histopathology
Ranula is a cyst-like structure consisting of mucin surrounded by fibroconnective or granulation
tissue that frequently contains histiocytes devoid of an epithelial lining. Thrombi and blood-filled
vessels can be seen in the surrounding wall or the adjacent salivary gland.
Differential The entities include mucoepidermoid carcinoma, dermoid cyst, thyroglossal duct cyst,
branchial cleft cyst, and a vascular malformation. The presence of mucin and the absence of an
epithelial lining help to differentiate ranula from other cystic and vascular lesions
{ 15356464 ; 22038368 ; 32809690 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: cyst-like structure containing mucin surrounded by a fibrous or granulation tissue, without
an epithelial lining
Desirable: imaging: Extension of the lesion into the submandibular space (tail sign)
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Both simple and plunging ranulas are treated by complete surgical excision of the gland with the
removal of the adjacent traumatized salivary duct. Recurrence may occur due to inadequate
excision { 15635557 ; 20594633 ; 21757145 ; 32087991 }.

1026

L#24722 Ranula, A cyst-like structure filled with mucin, lined by inflammatory cells and fibrous
tissue with adjacent salivary gland and ducts.
R#25087 Ranula, T2-weighted MRI showing a lobulated T2 hyperintense mass along the floor of
the mouth along the lateral left mylohyoid musculature within the sublingual gland.

#26228 Ranula, Cyst-like structure lined by granulation tissue with adjacent salivary glands and
ducts.

Lymphoepithelial cyst
Definition
Lymphoepithelial cyst is a descriptive term for a benign acquired epithelial-lined cystic lesion
associated with dense lymphoid stroma.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.Y Lymphoepithelial cyst of mouth
Related terminology
Acceptable: benign lymphoepithelial cyst, HIV-related sialadenitis
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Lymphoepithelial cysts affect multiple locations, including the parotid, angle of mandible, lateral
neck, and oral cavity, particularly the floor of the mouth { 13608417 ; 28936296 }.
Clinical features
In immunocompetent individuals, lymphoepithelial cysts present as a unilateral painless mass.
Patients with HIV infection may develop bilateral disease { 3393663 ; 31456841 }.
Imaging: Imaging is useful to confirm their simple cystic nature and delineate their anatomical
location. Lymphoepithelial cysts appear as well-circumscribed T2W hyperintense and T1W low
signal lesions on MRI, which may demonstrate thin peripheral enhancement following contrast. On
1027

ultrasound, lesions are hypo/ an-echoic with well-defined margins. In HIV-related cases, enlarged
adenoidal tissue and cervical lymph nodes are also often noted.
Epidemiology
Lymphoepithelial cyst arise over a broad age range with peak in 4th-5th decade
{ 7535539 ; 9496829 ; 20303054 }. It has classically been described in AIDS and is reported to
occur in up to 6% and 10% of HIV-positive adults and children, respectively { 1407984 ; 3393663 }.
Cystic lesions with similar morphology also occur in middle-aged immunocompetent individuals
and patients with Sjogren syndrome { 10335942 ; 31456841 }.
Etiology
The etiology is unclear. It has been postulated that lymphoepithelial cysts arise from epithelial
inclusions located in intra- and/or periparotid lymph nodes that subsequently undergo cystic
dilatation due to obstruction { 3393663 ; 6809660 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Lesions may be unilocular or multilocular with mean size of 25 mm { 29491615 }.
Histopathology
Lymphoepithelial cyst is a dilated cystic structure lined by squamous epithelium with variable
degrees of maturation and/or columnar epithelium. The epithelium lacks cytomorphological
evidence of malignancy. The cyst wall exhibits a dense lymphoid infiltrate with well-developed
lymphoid follicles, and the epithelium is commonly permeated by lymphocytes. In the setting of
AIDS and Sjogren syndrome, the background parenchyma may show features of lymphoepithelial
sialadenitis (LESA). In HIV-positive patients, immunoreactivity for the HIV-1 major-core protein
(p24) can be seen in follicular dendritic cells and interfollicular multinucleated giant cells within the
lymphoid stroma adjacent to the cyst { 8394048 ; 28963391 }.
Differential Branchial cleft cysts and isolated lymphoepithelial cysts have striking overlapping
morphology. Since both lesions have a benign clinical course, the distinction between them is
merely academic. Cystic salivary gland neoplasms with tumour associated lymphoid proliferation
(TALP) can mimic a lymphoepithelial cyst.
Cytology
Smears show a mixed lymphoid population and rare benign epithelial elements in a background of
proteinaceous ﬂuid { 2294703 ; 10450101 ; 12357495 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: dilated cystic structure; benign epithelial lining; dense lymphoid stroma in cyst wall
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Surgical management is curative for isolated lesions, while cysts in the setting of AIDS resolve with
highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) or radiotherapy { 21237435 ; 23532713 ; 31456841 }.

L#26250 Lymphoepithelial cyst, Multilocular lymphoepithelial cyst in a immunocompetent patient.
R#26251 Lymphoepithelial cyst, Benign epithelium of lymphoepithelial cyst permeated by mature
lymphocytes.

1028

#26905 Lymphoepithelial cyst, This cyst in an immunocompetent patient characterized by a
multilocular cystic structure lined by squamous epithelium and associated lymphoid stroma.

Branchial cleft cyst
Definition
Branchial cleft cyst is a developmental anomaly that arises from the first, second, third or fourth
pharyngeal clefts that clinically manifests as a lateral neck mass.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
A05.Y Other specified cysts of oral or facial-neck region & Branchial cleft cyst
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Location depends on the branchial cleft of origin. The most common location is the second
branchial cleft, along the anterior surface of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, lateral to the carotid
space, and posterior to the submandibular gland { 9925396 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a painless mass in the neck, which may acutely increase in size after an
upper respiratory tract infection { 16616308 ; 22494467 }. Bilateral branchial cleft cysts may be
associated with branchio-oto-renal syndrome { 29500469 }.
Epidemiology
Branchial cleft cysts are equally common in males and females with a bimodal age distribution that
peaks during early childhood and later between the third and fifth decades of life
{ 16616308 ; 21680029 ; 22494467 }. Second branchial cleft cysts are most common (>95%),
while the remainder are rare.
Etiology
The most common theory proposes that branchial cleft cysts derive from incomplete involution of
branchial cleft structures during embryogenesis { 1529135 ; 16616308 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
A cyst with smooth surface lining containing brown mucoid material { 25421295 }.
Histopathology
Branchial cleft cyst is usually unilocular and lined with stratified squamous epithelium lacking
cytomorphological evidence of malignancy. Columnar respiratory epithelial lining may be seen.
Lymphoid tissue is typically present in the cyst wall. In inflamed or ruptured cysts, an admixture of
acute inflammatory cells, fibrosis and granulation tissue may predominate.
Differential Importantly, the differential diagnosis of branchial cleft cyst includes a cystic lymph
node metastasis from an HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), particularly for patients
1029

over the age of 40 { 18383529 ; 26457358 ; 29983308 }. In this setting, p16 should be interpreted
with caution, as 30-40% of branchial cleft cysts overexpress this marker { 19365840 ; 20004950 }.
In such situations, more specific high risk-HPV testing (PCR-based assay or RNA in-situ
hybridization) should be performed, as high risk-HPV is not an established etiology of these cysts
{ 19365840 ; 20004950 ; 29764277 }. Notably, most tumours previously reported as carcinoma
arising in branchial cleft cysts represent cystic metastasis of HPV-related SCC { 23328541 }.
Cytology
Aspirates may show acute inflammatory cells and macrophages admixed with mature squamous
cells { 22507494 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: lateral neck location + bland cytomorphology + negative HPV status
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Surgical excision is curative. Incomplete resection may lead to recurrence and unresected cysts
have a risk for infection { 17346566 }. There is essentially no risk of carcinomatous transformation
{ 23328541 }.

L#26256 Branchial cleft cyst, Lined by stratified squamous epithelium with lymphoid tissue in the
cyst wall.
R#26257 Branchial cleft cyst, Benign stratified squamous epithelium lining a branchial cleft cyst.

L#26259 Branchial cleft cyst aspirate (Diff Quick stain) showing inflammatory cells admixed with
mature squamous cells
R#26909 Branchial cleft cyst, Unilocular branchial cleft cyst showing a stratified squamous
epithelial lining lacking cytomorphological evidence of malignancy and lymphoid tissue in cyst
wall.

1030

Thyroglossal duct cyst
Definition
Persistence and cystic dilatation of the thyroglossal duct in the anterior neck, commonly midline
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.Y Other specified cysts of oral or facial-neck region & Thyroglossal cyst
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
The majority (> 95%) of thyroglossal duct cysts (TGDC) occur in the midline neck at or just below
(75% infrahyoid) the level of hyoid bone, with 2-4% just at the tongue base
{ 22192899 ; 23458940 ; 27161104 }. Rarely, TGDC may be seen within the thyroid gland
(pyramidal lobe; isthmus) { 9731820 }. The lateral (nonmidline) neck cases are usually displaced
by recurrent infections or prior surgery { 27161104 }.
Clinical features
Patients generally present with a mobile, midline, infrahyoid, painless neck mass that moves
upward with swallowing { 19248939 ; 21237423 ; 22338239 ; 27161104 }. An associated skin
fistula (about 10% of patients) is twice as common in paediatric as adult patients
{ 19248939 ; 22338239 ; 27161104 }. A malignancy may be identified in about 3%
{ 1745980 ; 9121176 ; 11510730 ; 23318645 ; 27161104 }.
Imaging: By imaging, a midline round or elongated cystic lesion, sometimes with irregular walls,
septae and solid components, may expand into or destroy the hyoid bone
{ 22338239 ; 23689821 ; 30717638 ; 30725193 }. The amount of thyroid follicular tissue is usually
insufficient to be detected on radioisotope studies. Nodular soft tissue excrescences may suggest
carcinoma.
Epidemiology
About 2.23/100,000 general population have TGDC, representing 60-70% of congenital neck
cysts { 27161104 }. Further, in adult patients undergoing neck ultrasound, 0.9% were noted to
have a TGDC { 30467213 }, while 7% of adult laryngectomy specimens demonstrate TGD
remnants { 850454 }. There is a wide age range (0.8-87.0 years), with a median age of 32 years
{ 27161104 ; 23676810 }, but with bimodal peaks in the first and fifth decades
{ 21237423 ; 23458940 ; 27161104 }. There is an equal sex distribution overall, although slightly
more common in males in paediatric patients and in females in adults
{ 21237423 ; 23676810 ; 24332664 ; 25577275 ; 27161104 }. In patients with carcinoma, females
are more frequently affected (F:M, 2.3:1), with a mean age at presentation of 40 years
{ 11510730 ; 21453651 ; 23318645 ; 24457400 ; 27704385 ; 28322121 }, although paediatric
patients can be affected { 12764621 ; 27704385 ; 28293858 }.
Etiology
The etiology is unknown.
Pathogenesis
The thyroglossal duct (TGD) is an embryonic developmental structure whose remnant remains
anywhere along the thyroid anlage migration pathway from the tongue foramen cecum to a final
pretracheal position. Normal involution between 7-10 weeks gestation may anomalously persist,
with dilatation resulting in a TGDC { 27161104 ; 25577275 ; 23689821 ; 17720342 ; 7169333 }.
Macroscopic appearance
A smooth-walled cystic structure includes clear, mucinous fluid to purulent material when
secondarily infected. The size ranges from 2 - 85 mm (median 25 mm), with cysts smaller in
paediatric than adult patients { 18314017 ; 27161104 }.
Histopathology
There is an epithelial-lined cyst adjacent to the hyoid bone, containing respiratory or squamous
epithelium alone, or a mixture of both (50% of cases show both), with squamous epithelium alone
more common in inflamed cases { 27161104 }. Some cases lack an epithelial lining. Cysts rupture
1031

frequently (65%) inciting fibrosis, with histiocytes, cholesterol clefts, and foreign body giant cell
reaction. Thyroid follicular epithelium, commonly localized in the cyst wall as several mm of tissue,
is identified in about 70% { 27161104 ; 11404612 }. Mucoserous salivary gland tissue (15%) and
cartilage (<1%) may also be identified.
Malignant
neoplasms
are
identified
in
about
3%
of
TGDC
{ 1745980 ; 25577275 ; 27704385 ; 27161104 ; 28293858 ; 29938764 }, with papillary thyroid
carcinoma
(PTC)
accounting
for
>
95%
of
malignancies
{ 1745980 ; 11510730 ; 19565286 ; 21453651 ; 23318645 ; 25577275 ; 27704385 ; 27161104 ;
28322121 ; 28293858 }, with other carcinoma types rarely detected { 9413065 ; 21512669 }. Soft
tissue involvement is common (60%). Some patients may show concurrent malignancy in
thyroidectomies
performed
as
part
of
management
{ 19565286 ; 21453651 ; 24457400 ; 25577275 ; 27704385 ; 27161104 ; 30091370 }.
C-cellderived tumours are not identified due to origin from ultimobranchial apparatus which is not seen
in lingual-based foramen cecum lesions { 27161104 ; 28536894 }.
Differential The differential diagnosis includes epidermal inclusion cyst, dermoid cyst, branchial
cleft cyst, bronchogenic cyst, cervical thymic cyst, and metastatic PTC, which can usually be
separated based on anatomic site of involvement, imaging findings, and pertinent histologic
features.
Cytology
Smears from TGDCs are often nondiagnostic, with a low cellularity with more inflammatory cells
than epithelial cells { 12049106 ; 19248939 ; 22277268 ; 25577275 ; 27161104 }. The epithelial
cells are usually squamous or respiratory, seldom containing thyroid follicular cells. Smears
obtained from firm, fixed lesions may show nuclear features of PTC when malignancy is
concurrently present { 25577275 ; 27704385 ; 27161104 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Perihyoidal location.
Desirable: Midline lesion; respiratory or squamous lined cyst with associated thyroid follicular
epithelium.
Staging
Not applicable.
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence is higher in patients with infected cysts as well as those not managed by a Sistrunk
procedure, with perihyoidal TGD remnants result in persistent clinical disease
{ 30194760 ; 30388395 ; 23331819 ; 17165669 }. Infection, rupture, haemorrhage, and fistula
formation
are
recognized
complications
{ 19248939 ; 22249624 ; 23676810 ; 24332664 ; 29521676 ; 30388395 }. When carcinoma is
identified, outcome is excellent, although staging is not presently required for extrathyroidal sites
{ 19565286 ; 27161104 ; 32042395 }.

contains follicles of papillary thyroid carcinoma (right), showing characteristic nuclear findings.

L#22219 Neck Thyroglossal duct cyst, A whole slide show fragments of hyoid bone with
respiratory epithelial lined cyst and numerous thyroid follicles in the soft tissues.
R#22221 Neck thyroglossal duct cyst, Multiple fragments of hyoid bone with respiratory
epithelium-lined cyst and isolated thyroid follicles in the stroma.

1035

Dermoid and teratoid cysts
Definition
A dermoid cyst is a developmental cystic lesion composed of ectodermal and mesodermal tissues,
typically including epidermis-like squamous epithelium and cutaneous adnexal structures. A
teratoid cyst includes endodermal tissues.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
DA05.Y Other specified cysts of oral or facial-neck region & Dermoid cyst of mouth
Related terminology
Acceptable: dermoid, cystic dermoid, cystic teratoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Approximately 7% of all dermoid cysts found in the head and neck region { 32884763 }. Most
commonly periorbital, also orbital, ear, dorsum of nose, floor of mouth/submental region, mid-neck.
Clinical features
Patients present with a non-specific, firm, palpable mass. They may present with a cutaneous pit
secreting sebaceous material and protruding hair follicles.
Imaging: Imaging studies are recommended to rule out osseous or intracranial extension. In the
floor of mouth / submandibular region imaging may reveal a well-defined lesion classically
containing intracystic corpuscles (the “sack of marbles” sign); hypodense on CT, hyperintense on
T1W and low signal on T2W MRI scans due to lipid content. Occasionally these “marbles” may be
calcified. Such imaging features permit differentiation from simple cystic lesions in these anatomical
sites (e.g. ranula) { 21918039 }.
Epidemiology
Equivocal gender predilection, possible slight female preponderance. Often present since birth,
approximately 70% diagnosed before the age of 5 years { 25439551 ; 30466230 }.
Etiology
Developmental cystic lesion. No reported genetic etiology.
Pathogenesis
Most commonly due to entrapped ectoderm and mesoderm along planes of embryonic fusion.
Macroscopic appearance
Cystic lesion up to 120 mm in dimension, commonly filled with keratinaceous debris { 32884763 }.
Histopathology
Dermoid cyst wall composed of keratinized, epidermis-like squamous epithelium. Abundant
desquamated keratin or hair follicles may be noted within the cystic lumen. Cutaneous adnexal
structures are present in the underlying connective tissues and possibly attached to the cyst wall,
and may include hair follicles, or sebaceous, eccrine or apocrine glandular elements. Teratoid cyst
includes endodermal tissues, to potentially include gastrointestinal or respiratory epithelium, or
glandular tissues to include pancreatic or salivary tissue.
Cytology
May contain desquamated anucleated and nucleated squamous epithelial cells and keratin debris
{ 10352915 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Dermoid cyst: squamous epithelial-lined cystic lesion with associated adnexal structures, to
possibly include hair follicles and glandular elements (sebaceous, eccrine, and/or apocrine)
Teratoid cyst: same as above, but including endodermal tissues such as respiratory or
gastrointestinal mucosa
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Complete surgical excision. Recurrence is unusual.

1036

L#26391 Dermoid cyst, Excised cystic lesion from floor of the mouth.
R#26392 Dermoid cyst, Cyst wall composed of epidermis-like squamous epithelium, with
associated underlying adnexal structures to include sebaceous and focal eccrine glands.

#28640 Dermoid cyst, Axial T2W, T2W and T1W post contrast fat suppressed images of the floor
of mouth demonstrate a left-sided lesion, moderately hyperintense on T1W with no post-contrast
enhancement. On the T2W image multiple rounded low signal foci are noted causing a 'sack of
marbles' appearance, classical of a dermoid cyst.

Other tumours

Branchioma
Definition
Branchioma is a benign neoplasm composed of an admixture of spindled cells, epithelial islands,
and adipocytes, generally affecting the lower anterior neck.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
XH0707 Ectopic hamartomatous thymoma (name to be changed)
Related terminology
Acceptable: biphenotypic branchioma
Not recommended: ectopic hamartomatous thymoma; branchial anlage mixed tumour; thymic
anlage tumour
Subtype(s)
None

1037

Localization
Principally affects the lower anterior neck, proximate to supra-sternoclavicular area
{ 15371953 ; 27428736 ; 28879635 ; 29995801 ; 32026292 },
rarely
presternal
{ 9720942 ; 26763841 }.
Clinical features
Patients present with a slow-growing subfascial supra-sternoclavicular swelling, most commonly,
left sided more often than right sided
{ 6742312 ; 1693594 ; 2050369 ; 15371953 ; 27428736 ; 28879635 ; 29995801 ; 32026292 }.
Epidemiology
This very rare tumour is identified in a wide age range (19 – 89 years, median 40 years), more
common in males than females (3.4:1)
{ 6742312 ; 1693594 ; 2050369 ; 15371953 ; 27194678 ; 27428736 ; 28879635 ; 29995801 ; 320
26292 }.
Etiology
Unknown
Pathogenesis
Few cases have been studied, with NGS analysis revealing a hotspot mutation
in HRAS (pGln61Lys) without other known fusions (specifically PLAG1 rearrangement) in one
case { 23339366 ; 32026292 }. The tumour is considered to arise from misplaced branchial pouch
derivatives, containing tissues from two primordial layers (mesoderm and endoderm: biphasic)
{ 32026292 }. The branchial apparatus embryologically gives rise to a very complex and
coordinated development of the neck structures, including fat, myoepithelial and epithelial cells,
supporting a branchial origin.
Macroscopic appearance
A lobular to multilobular mass, ranging in size from 14 to 190 mm (mean, 50 mm) { 32026292 }. It
shows grey-white to yellow solid cut surfaces.
Histopathology
Typically well circumscribed or encapsulated, there is a haphazard proliferation of spindled cells,
epithelial islands and adipocytes, in variable proportions. The epithelial cells form non-keratinizing
squamoid islands, anastomosing cords, cysts, or glandular structures. The glands are often
surrounded by myoepithelial cells. Skin adnexal differentiation (including sebaceous, eccrine, and
apocrine elements) may occur { 17574947 }. Plump spindled cells show fascicular, storiform or
lattice-like growth, often merging into the epithelial component. They possess bland-looking
elongated nuclei and light-staining cytoplasm. Myoid features may occasionally be prominent
{ 2076891 ; 8496655 }. Fat cells are haphazardly present, sometimes associated with
lymphocytes. Among the epithelial and spindled cell components, there are delicate spindly stromal
cells with small nuclei and scanty cytoplasm. Rarely, carcinoma may develop in the epithelial
component, but is generally contained macroscopically { 8130171 ; 8857650 ; 32026292 }.
Immunohistochemistry: The epithelial component is immunoreactive for pancytokeratin; the
squamoid islands are also positive for CK5/6, p63 and p40. The plump spindly cells are positive for
pancytokeratin, CK5/6, p63, p40 and smooth-muscle actin, indicating myoepithelial differentiation
{ 1693594 ; 12390415 ; 15371953 ; 16640545 ; 17574947 ; 27428736 ; 28879635 ; 32026292 }.
The delicate spindly cells are negative for pancytokeratin and positive for CD34.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant, although reported { 10097715 ; 17718153 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
- Solitary, well circumscribed mass located in suprasternoclavicular area.
- Haphazard proliferation of benign spindled cells, epithelial islands, and adipocytes, in the absence
of primitive elements.
Staging
Not performed
Prognosis and prediction
Recurrence is rare { 6742312 ; 15371953 ; 26617926 ; 28879635 ; 32026292 }. Even with
supervening carcinoma component, death from disease has not been reported.

1038

L#23368 Branchioma, A low power shows a well circumscribed mass containing numerous cysts
and a spindled stroma with adipocytes.
R#23369 Branchioma, There is a cellular spindled cell population immediately adjacent and

throughout.

L#23371 Branchioma, Various components of branchioma are haphazardly distributed, with

tissue.
R#23372 Branchioma, The features of branchioma may be subtle, with adipocytes and fibrosis
partly obscuring the squamous epithelium and associated spindled cells.

L#23373 Branchioma with carcinoma, In some cases, malignant transformation of the epithelial
component may be seen, here showing a complex papillary architecture of cells with moderate
pleomorphism and an apocrine morphology.
R#25243 Branchioma, Fascicles of plump spindly cells with uniform nuclei and lightly eosinophils.
The cells in the right field show myoid features, characterized by densely eosinophilic cytoplasm.

L#25244 Branchioma, An example of "monophasic" branchioma with absent to inconspicuous
epithelial component. The adipose component is present only very focally.
R#25245 Branchioma, Immunostaining for pancytokeratin highlights the epithelial and plump
spindly cell components. Note the frequent presence of anastomosing narrow strands (left upper
field).

L#25246 Branchioma, Immunostaining for smooth-muscle actin highlights the plump spindly cells,
while the epithelial component is negative.
R#23209 Branchioma, There is a blending of cystic epithelium with strands of spindled cells
associated with adipose tissue in a well circumscribed mass.

1040

Heterotopia-associated carcinoma
Definition
Heterotopia-associated carcinoma is a primary malignant epithelial neoplasm arising from
histologically normal, congenitally displaced tissue, most commonly thyroid and salivary tissue.
ICD-O coding
8010/3 Heterotopia-associated carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2D42 & XH26N1 Malignant neoplasms of ill-defined sites & Heterotopia-associated carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: ectopia-associated carcinoma, choristoma-associated carcinoma, accessory
tissue-associated carcinoma, primary intranodal carcinoma
Subtype(s)
Thyroid carcinoma
Salivary carcinoma
Localization
Heterotopia-associated thyroid carcinomas arise in the midline anterior neck, along the path of the
thyroglossal duct from the tongue to the pyramidal thyroid { 21752893 }. Heterotopia-associated
salivary carcinomas most commonly arise in intraparotid, periparotid, and cervical lymph nodes or
at extranodal sites along the sternocleidomastoid muscle { 1710048 ; 15742261 }. However,
thorough radiographic and/or pathologic evaluation is necessary to exclude an unusual
presentation of an orthotopic primary neoplasm or a metastasis { 26691539 }.
Clinical features
Most patients present with an asymptomatic neck mass, with rare patients reporting pain or
dysphagia { 28322121 }.
Epidemiology
Heterotopia-associated carcinomas are rare. Approximately 1-3% of thyroglossal duct cysts
develop carcinoma, occurring more commonly in women (female-to-male ratio: 2-3:1) and
spanning a wide age range (9-83 years), with an average age of 39
{ 9121176 ; 27704385 ; 28322121 }.
Heterotopia-associated
salivary
carcinomas
also
demonstrate a wide age range (7-81 years) with a female predominance seen in acinic cell
carcinoma { 1710048 ; 33177745 }.
Etiology
Heterotopia-associated carcinomas arise from malpositioned embryologic remnants, but the cause
is unknown.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Average tumour size is 10 - 40 mm, although some tumours grow to larger sizes
{ 27704385 ; 28322121 ; 33177745 }.
Histopathology
The most common carcinoma arising from heterotopic thyroid tissue is papillary thyroid carcinoma
(PTC), with rare reports of follicular, Hürthle cell, or squamous cell carcinoma { 28322121 }.
Heterotopia-associated PTC demonstrates identical nuclear features to those seen in primary
thyroid tumours. Architecture may be papillary or follicular, and the tumour may proliferate into the
lumen of a cyst or infiltrate into the surrounding tissue { 27704385 }.
Heterotopia-associated salivary carcinomas display the same morphologic features as their
orthotopic counterparts. To differentiate from orthotopic carcinomas with tumour-associated
lymphoid proliferation (TALP), heterotopia-associated salivary carcinomas arising in lymph nodes
should be separate from the salivary gland and confined to nodal tissue with a subcapsular sinus
or hilum { 33177745 }. Mucoepidermoid and acinic cell carcinoma are the most frequent
heterotopia-associated salivary carcinomas { 1710048 ; 15742261 }. A subset of salivary
intraductal carcinomas arising within intraparotid lymph nodes has also been described
{ 32661669 }.

1041

For both heterotopia-associated thyroid and salivary carcinomas, identification of adjacent nonneoplastic heterotopic tissue supports the diagnosis but is not always present.
Differential
For differential diagnosis of heterotopia-associated papillary thyroid
carcinoma (see Table #26577)
For differential diagnosis of heterotopia-associated salivary carcinoma (see Table #26578)
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration of carcinomas arising in heterotopic tissue shows the same morphologic
features as primary carcinomas. For heterotopia-associated thyroid carcinomas, there is a higher
risk of a false-negative result due to aspiration of paucicellular cyst fluid only
{ 27161104 ; 28322121 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The most common mutation reported in thyroglossal duct cyst PTC is BRAF p.V600E { 24777145 }.
Heterotopic mucoepidermoid carcinomas harbor MAML2 rearrangements, similar to their
orthotopic counterparts { 21590088 }. Salivary intraductal carcinomas arising within intraparotid
lymph nodes harbor RET and ALK rearrangements { 32661669 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: carcinoma arising in a known location for heterotopic tissue + clinical and/or pathologic
exclusion of a prior or concurrent orthotopic carcinoma
Desirable: adjacent benign heterotopic tissue
Staging
No formal staging criteria exist for heterotopia-associated carcinoma.
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis of heterotopia-associated papillary thyroid carcinoma is excellent, with a recurrence
rate of <5% and mortality rate of <1% for surgically treated thyroglossal duct carcinoma
{ 28322121 }. Metastases to lateral cervical lymph nodes are seen in 10-20% of patients
{ 28322121 }. Synchronous primary thyroid carcinomas are identified in 25-35% of patients,
comparable to the incidence reported for occult incidental thyroid carcinomas in autopsy studies
{ 11870661 ; 9121176 }. Age ≥ 45 and soft tissue infiltration are associated with increased risk of
locoregional recurrence { 27704385 }. Squamous cell carcinoma arising in a thyroglossal duct cyst
is rare and associated with a worse prognosis { 29623203 }.
For heterotopia-associated salivary carcinomas, high-grade histopathology is associated with a
more aggressive clinical course { 7260848 ; 15742261 }. Metastases to cervical lymph nodes are
predominantly seen in high-grade carcinomas or low-grade tumours with high-grade transformation
{ 1710048 ; 33177745 }.

#25130 Heterotopia-associated carcinoma. Papillary thyroid carcinoma arising in a thyroglossal
duct cyst, Papillary structures lined by cells with nuclear features of papillary thyroid carcinoma
are present within a cyst located in the upper midline neck (left). Skeletal muscle and benign
heterotopic glandular structures are also identified (right). This carcinoma arose above the thyroid
gland, and there were no lesions identified in the thyroid.

diagnostic for papillary thyroid carcinoma

Squamous carcinoma of unknown primary site
Definition
Squamous carcinoma of unknown primary (CUP) is clinical term that refers to a sqamous
carcinoma metastatic to cervical lymph nodes with no evident primary site.
ICD-O coding
8010/6 Carcinoma, metastatic, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2D4Z Unspecified malignant neoplasms of ill-defined or unspecified sites
Related terminology
Not recommended: branchial cleft cyst carcinoma; branchiogenic carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Metastases to the neck are determined by lymphatic drainage; therefore, the location of a positive
lymph node may indicate the site of origin { 11754505 }. For Carcinoma of unknown primary,
expected pattern of lymph node metastasis, “first echelon”, in the head and neck (see Table
##26364). CUP most commonly affects upper jugular lymph nodes (level II), which suggests an
oropharyngeal primary { 9886695 ; 10799723 }. Most metastases to supraclavicular nodes arise
from non-head and neck sites (e.g. Thorax, abdomen).
Clinical features
The cardinal sign in most patients (>90%) affected with CUP is a neck mass/swelling
{ 10799723 ; 12684829 }.
Epidemiology
Approximately
5–10
%
of
head
and
neck
cancers
present
as
CUP.
{ 10799723 ; 12957453 ; 16698706 ; 30616785 }. With recent advances, after clinical,
radiographic, surgical and pathological evaluation the primary site is found in the head and neck
region in over 90% of these cases, and the oropharynx is the most common location of the primary
tumour { 12684829 ; 19718744 ; 20737490 ; 22855133 ; 23345170 ; 24812081 }. Conversely, a
neck mass is the most common (44%) initial symptom of an oropharyngeal HPV-associated
squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) { 24652023 }. In areas where EBV infection is endemic, the
nasopharynx is a common primary site { 11933178 }. Patients affected with metastatic cutaneous

1045

head and neck SCC have an unknown index lesion in about one quarter of cases
{ 16783833 ; 18849863 }. The percentage of unknown primary tumour ("true" CUP) where the
primary is not identified following extensive endoscopic evaluation, represents less than 3% of
suspected CUP { 10799723 ; 17657782 ; 33244460 }.
Etiology
HR-HPV is a common driver of tumours presenting as CUP, and EBV plays an etiological role in a
subset of cases { 11933178 ; 20737490 ; 22855133 ; 23345170 ; 24812081 }.
Pathogenesis
Human papillomaviruses (HPV) and Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) are provable carcinogenic
factors and represent a significant etiologic component of CUP { 33244460 }. Ultraviolet (UV)signature mutations indicate sun-induced DNA damage, characterized by a preponderance of C→T
substitutions at dipyrimidine sites, including CC→TT { 25354245 }, The identification of UVsignature mutations in CUP indicates a skin primary and has been reported in metastatic cutaneous
SCC and polyomavirus-negative MCC { 30684551 ; 30986802 }. Polymorphous adenocarcinomas
with PRKD1/2/3 gene rearrangements have a higher risk of nodal metastasis { 31492931 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Cystic lymph node metastases frequently correlate with an oropharyngeal primary site
{ 18383529 }.
Histopathology
For Carcinoma of unknown primary. Morphological diagnostic approach to CUP in the head and
neck (see Table #26365).
The non-keratinizing morphology characteristic of HPV-associated SCC is the most common
pattern encountered in CUP { 22211374 }. A subset of tumours can have ciliated or columnar
features{ 26457358 ; 26411881 }. Metastatic extra-oropharyngeal mucosal and cutaneous SCCs
typically show keratinizing morphology. Undifferentiated (lymphoepithelial-like) morphology is
strongly associated with EBV-related nasopharyngeal carcinomas but can be seen in HPVassociated carcinomas { 11933178 ; 20421782 }. Neuroendocrine neoplasms presenting as CUP
are rare. For well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumours with immunoreactivity for calcitonin but
normal serum levels, the supraglottic larynx should be examined for possible occult primary
{ 3056608 }. If high grade neuroendocrine carcinoma is encountered, an oropharyngeal HPVassociated neuroendocrine carcinoma or a Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) should be suspected.
Salivary gland adenocarcinomas rarely present as CUP, with the notable exception of the cribriform
variant of polymorphous adenocarcinoma { 10583573 }. Non-head and neck adenocarcinomas
may have heterogeneous morphology and require immunohistochemical evaluation to identify the
site of origin.
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration biopsy of a neck mass suspected of being a metastasis is the first diagnostic
approach to CUP, and is usually performed under ultrasound guidance or following ultrasound and
other imaging assessment. It is essential for the proceduralist to provide material for cell block
preparation for immunohistochemistry and potentially for molecular studies, and this may take
several passes ideally performed with rapid on site evaluation to facilitate triaging of the specimen
as lymphoid or metastatic carcinoma. The cytomorphology of CUP mirrors that of tumours of known
primary sites, and it is important to correlate the location of the tumour in the head and neck region
(see Table #26365) and the clinical history and findings. Possible primary sites especially for
supraclavicular lymph nodes include lung, breast and ovary and other sites.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
HR-HPV testing should be routinely performed in CUP with SCC, undifferentiated and
neuroendocrine morphology { 29251996 ; 30188786 }. p16 is a very sensitive surrogate marker of
HR-HPV; however, 20% of cutaneous head and neck SCC are p16 positive { 30640756 }.
Additional specific HR-HPV testing (PCR-based assay or RNA in-situ hybridization) should be
performed to confirm HPV status in tumours located outside levels II/III and/or with keratinizing
morphology { 30188786 }. EBV-encoded small RNA (EBER) probe is useful in identifying EBVrelated nasopharyngeal carcinomas in metastatic lymph nodes { 12622545 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential (True CUP): p16 and EBER negativity, and no primary site identified after extensive
diagnostic work-up and clinical history
Desirable (if test is available): lack of UV-signature mutations

1046

Staging
The UICC/TNM classification of Unknown Primary
Prognosis and prediction
HPV positivity is associated with improved overall and disease-specific survival in CUP
{ 20737490 ; 25369118 }.

presented as unknown primary.

L#26911 Carcinoma of unknown primary. Metastatic HPV-associated carcinoma, Core biopsy of
neck mass in a patient with unknown history of cancer.
M#26912 Carcinoma of unknown primary. Cystic metastasis, HPV-associated carcinoma.
R#26342 Carcinoma of unknown primary, Cystic lymph node metastases of HPV-associated
carcinoma.

1048

14. Germ cell tumours
Germ cell tumours of the head and neck

Germ cell tumours of the head and neck
Definition
Teratoma in the head and neck is a germ cell tumour defined by the presence of mature or
immature tissues derived from all three germ cell layers. Extragonadal malignant germ cell tumours
encompass a spectrum of malignant germ cell neoplasms identical to those occurring in the
gonads.
ICD-O coding
9080/0 Mature teratoma
9080/3 Immature teratoma
9084/3 Teratoma with somatic-type malignancy
9064/3 Germinoma
9070/3 Embryonal carcinoma
9071/3 Yolk sac tumour
9100/3 Choriocarcinoma
9085/3 Mixed germ cell tumour
ICD-11 coding
2E90.6 & XH83G5 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx & Teratoma, NOS
2E90.6 & XH1E13 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx & Germinoma
2E90.6 & XH8MB9 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx & Embryonal carcinoma, NOS
2E90.6 & XH09W7 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx & Yolk sac tumour
2E90.6 & XH8PK7 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx & Choriocarcinoma, NOS
2E90.6 & XH2PS1 Benign neoplasm of nasopharynx & Mixed germ cell tumour
Related terminology
Acceptable: Benign teratoma, immature teratoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Head and neck teratomas and malignant germ cell tumours are exceptionally rare, described in the
sinonasal tract (maxillary sinus and nasal cavity) and nasopharynx most frequently
{ 27437234 ; 26881568 ; 23975485 ; 18598836 ; 17902152 ; 9251738 ; 9078942 ; 2982716 }
Rarely reported sites include the oral cavity, oropharynx, ear and temporal bone, neck, and salivary
gland
{ 31620699 ; 33128732 ; 29113273 ; 22560784 ; 18937314 ; 18302061 ; 12953777 ; 9251738 ; 9
078942 ; 1399316 ; 1690172 }.
Clinical features
Both tumours generally present as mass lesions, with additional symptoms referrable to the site
affected, including nasal obstruction and facial deformity { 1690172 ; 2982716 }. Associations
include stillbirth, prematurity, fetal malpresentation and maternal polyhydramnios for teratomas
{ 1690172 ; 2982716 }. Congenital abnormalities may be concurrently present. Aicardi and Dandy
Walker syndromes have been associated with head and neck yolk sac tumours
{ 31640397 ; 9715538 }. Serum alpha-fetoprotein or ß-HCG levels may be elevated, particularly
the former for yolk sac tumours { 9853671 }. Teratomas may undergo malignant transformation
{ 21319013 l 2982716 }.
Imaging demonstrates a heterogeneous cystic and solid mass with variable amounts of fat and
calcifications, which may suggest the diagnosis { 31640397 ; 23975485 ; 23009771 ; 21319013 }.
Epidemiology
Teratomas of the head and neck account for a small proportion of teratomas overall, while
malignant
germ
cell
tumours
are
exceptionally
uncommon
{ 26881568 ; 18937314 ; 1690172 ; 2982716 }. The majority of teratomas occur in neonates and
older infants, while the majority of malignant germ cell tumours develop in adults, although
paediatric
patients
may
be
affected.
There
is
no
sex
bias
{ 35073631 ; 33128732 ; 18937314 ; 9078942 ; 1690172 ; 2982716 }.
Etiology

1049

Unknown
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Tumours generally have a smooth but lobulated exterior and a heterogeneous, multiloculated solid
to cystic cut surface, containing variable amounts of serous, mucinous, glairy or haemorrhagic fluid.
Tumours measure up to 70 mm { 9078942 ; 2982716 }.
Histopathology
Teratomas are defined by the presence of organoid mature or immature tissues from all embryonic
germ cell layers, including endoderm, mesoderm, and ectoderm. Tumours display multiple cystic
spaces lined by a variety of epithelial types, including simple or stratified squamous epithelium,
often associated with skin adnexal structures, pseudostratified and ciliated respiratory epithelium,
mucin-producing intestinal epithelium, and transitional epithelium. Teratomas may also contain a
significant component of neural tissue, encompassing both mature glial elements, retinal anlage,
choroid plexus or immature neuroectodermal tissue composed of small to medium-sized cells with
hyperchromatic nuclei and scant fibrillary cytoplasm arranged in sheets and rosettes. When the
latter is predominant, immature teratoma is diagnosed { 1690172 }. A variety of mesenchymal
elements including skeletal muscle, smooth muscle, cartilage, and bone can be identified. Solid
organ parenchyma including pancreatic, liver, or lung tissue is rare.
Carcinomas may be seen as a somatic malignancy in a teratoma { 31442873 ; 3511885 }, referred
to as teratoma with malignant transformation.
The most common head and neck malignant extragonadal germ cell tumour is yolk sac tumour,
which recapitulates embryonic yolk sac of the gonads with a reticular/microcystic pattern or the
classical round or elongated tumour cell–lined papillae with a large central vessel, protruding into
a cystic space surrounded by tumour cells (Schiller–Duval bodies). Choriocarcinoma, embryonal
carcinoma,
and
seminoma
are
exceptionally
rare
{ 35073631 ; 33128732 ; 1690172 ; 9078942 ; 2982716 }.
Immunohistochemistry
Immunohistochemistry is usually unnecessary for diagnosis, with the various teratoma components
displaying staining characteristics of their constituent tissues. Yolk sac tumours are reactive with
AFP, SALL4 (while negative for OCT3/4), glypican-3, LIN28 and pankeratins, with focal coexpression of CDX2 and GATA3 in many cases { 29753846 ; 26772394 ; 19365862 ; 1708402 },
while choriocarcinoma is positive with ß-HCG { 33128732 }.
Differential
diagnosis
SMARCB1-deficient carcinomas (including adenocarcinoma) in the sinonasal tract may show yolk
sac differentiation, but are considered within the spectrum of SWI-SNF complex-deficient sinonasal
carcinomas { 35257325 ; 33824593 ; 34420180 ; 31468350 }. Teratocarcinosarcoma may show
SALL4 and ß-catenin immunoreactivity, but shows distinct histologic features of malignant
squamous or glandular tissues along with mesenchymal and primitive neural elements, shown to
be SMARCA4-deficient { 34106411 ; 32520761 }. Limited sampling may result in misdiagnosis
depending on which component(s) may be sampled. Metastatic disease to the head and neck from
a distant primary site must be considered and excluded { 21119432 }. Teratocarcinoma is covered
in section 1.2.2.7.
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration is rarely performed, but may contain only cyst contents or be interpreted as
“insufficient” with only normal elements seem (mature teratoma).
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
- Involvement of a head and neck site

1050

- Teratoma: proliferation of tissue from all three germ layers; usually mature tissues, but foci of
immature neuroectodermal tissues may be seen
- Malignant germ cell tumour: yolk sac tumour most common
- Exclusion of SMARCB1-deficient carcinomas in the sinonasal tract and teratocarcinosarcoma
Desirable: Exclusion of primary in another site
Staging
Not available
Prognosis and prediction
Teratomas can cause death due to airway compression and impaired development of other vital
structures. There is a poor outcome of malignant germ cell tumours irrespective of site
{ 35073631 ; 31620699 ; 31442873 ; 18302061 ; 1690172 ; 9078942 ; 2982716 },
although
syndrome association and multimodality therapies may yield a better outcome { 31640397 }.

L#33104 Germ cell tumours: Teratoma, A neonate demonstrated a midline maxilla pedunculated
mass which protruded out of the mouth. Histologically this was a mature teratoma.
R#33105 Germ cell tumours: Teratoma, A sagittal T1 SE image of a large mass filling the
nasopharynx, representing an immature teratoma.

L#33106 Germ cell tumours: Teratoma, An axial T2 fat suppressed image shows the same
immature teratoma, with the characteristic bright signal.
R#33108 Germ cell tumours: malignant teratoma, Ultrasound shows a 36.7 mm midline anterior
neck mass that extended into the floor of the mouth. Histologically, this represented a malignant
teratoma.
1051

L#33109 Germ cell tumours: mature teratoma,Mature cartilage adjacent to more primitive
mesenchyme in a mature teratoma.
R#33110 Germ cell tumours: teratoma, Cartilage juxtaposed to various epithelia, arranged in a
haphazard appearance. Primitive blastema-type tissue is noted (upper left).

L#33111 Germ cell tumours: teratoma, Cysts lined by low cuboidal epithelium are subtended by
primitive appearance muscle tissue.
R#33113 Germ cell tumours: yolk sac tumour, The histologic features of yolk sac tumour are
seen in this sinonasal tract lesion. Note the numerous globules in this field.

L#33114 Germ cell tumours: yolk sac tumour, A Schiller-Duval body is seen, with a projection into
the lumen. The neoplastic cells show cleared cytoplasm and a high nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio.
R#33115 Germ cell tumours: yolk sac tumour, The globules are highlighted by alpha feto protein
or alpha-1-antitrypsin, as shown in this field of yolk sac tumour.

1052

15. Metastasis
Metastases to head and neck region

Metastases to head and neck region
Definition
Metastatic tumours are neoplasms that involve various head and neck sites as a result of lymphatic
or vascular spread from non-contiguous primary malignancies. Haematolymphoid tumours are
excluded by definition.
ICD-O coding
None
ICD-11 coding
2D60.0 Malignant neoplasm metastasis in lymph nodes of head, face or neck
Related terminology
None
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Neck metastases are to cervical lymph nodes; soft tissues metastases rarely occur, particularly for
non-epithelial neoplasms. The most common squamous cell carcinoma metastases to the neck
depend on anatomic site: level II from oropharynx; and level III from larynx. Upper/mid neck node
metastases arise from other head and neck primary sites; lower neck metastases are from
infraclavicular primary tumours. Virchow node (left supraclavicular) receives metastases from
abdominal and pelvic malignancies { 7646330 }. Thyroid carcinomas metastasize to the central
compartment before the lateral cervical nodes { 23148663 }. Local extension by metastatic
malignancy occurs most often with lymph node deposits originating from a variety of cancers where
extranodal extension is a known adverse prognostic factor { 12570052 }.
Oral Cavity metastases are almost equal between oral soft tissues and jaw bones { 31570205 }.
Tooth-bearing gingiva is most commonly involved, although any subsite can be affected
{ 18061527 ; 23646853 ; 27891304 ; 31570205 }.
Major
salivary
glands
(SG) metastases
involve
the
parotid
most
commonly
{ 16444748 ; 17113505 ; 29549903 } with other sites rarely involved { 20596975 }. A high
percentage of metastases are to intra- and periglandular lymph nodes rather than glandular
parenchyma.
In Nasopharynx the posterior nasopharyngeal wall and fossa of Rosenmüller are affected
{ 23924557 ; 26541404 ; 32728442 }.
Pharynx metastases are mostly to base of tongue and palatine tonsils { 668170 ; 20596975 }, and
usually on the left { 668170 } although bilateral cases occur { 1624294 ; 17657505 }.
Sinonasal tract metastases frequently involve maxillary sinus followed by sphenoid, ethmoid and
frontal sinuses, with involvement of multiple paranasal sites synchronously or metachronously
being possible { 11602931 }.
in the Ear, the petrous apex is most commonly affected (1/3 of cases), followed by internal auditory
canal,
mastoid,
middle
ear,
and
external
auditory
canal
{ 1869438 ; 1919860 ; 1991061 ; 2995524 ; 4328134 ; 4938079 ; 7964154 ; 8892576 ; 9509102 ;
10680873 ; 11945183 ; 15768802 ; 18754073 ; 24077267 ; 26545469 ; 26545474 ; 29909612 }.
Mucosal involvement is more common in Larynx metastases; spread to laryngeal cartilages is
usually to ossified areas { 26743607 }.
Metastases to Maxillofacial bones involve the mandible (molar area) three or more times more
often
than
the
maxilla;
both
jaws
are
affected
in
5%
of
cases
{ 7815371 ; 17138711 ; 25409855 ; 23798834 ; 19304027 }.
Clinical features
Clinical presentation is highly variable and dependent on anatomic subsite involved. Most
metastatic deposits present as a mass. Neck nodes/soft tissue: painful and non-painful, single or
multiple, solid and/or cystic masses.
Oral cavity: exophytic, ulcerated and non-ulcerated, asymptomatic or symptomatic mass with
swelling, pain, bleeding, toothache, and altered tooth mobility, numbness with bone involvement

1053

and halitosis { 31146958 }. Metastases are an inflammatory mimic when associated with dental
implants { 22670251 ; 23646853 ; 27720649 }.
SG: pre-auricular, facial, or submandibular mass, facial pain, trismus, tenderness, numbness, and
facial nerve paralysis { 12820326 ; 20091686 ; 26245749 }.
Pharynx: swelling, dysphagia, nasal obstruction, tonsillitis, sinusitis, post-nasal drip, pain, cranial
nerve motor deficits { 32728442 }, and asphyxiation { 21841958 }.
Sinonasal tract: nasal obstruction, headache, epistaxis, facial pain, visual disturbances, facial
swelling, and cranial nerve deficits.
Ear/temporal bone: hearing loss, facial palsy and/or ear pain { 21854691 }, haemorrhage or
compression of adjacent structures { 379358 ; 2019918 ; 9509102 ; 11945183 }, Collet-Sicard
syndrome (paralysis of IX-XII cranial nerves) { 7407020 ; 15885949 ; 16929891 }. Incidental
detection in one-third during primary tumour staging.
Larynx: dysphonia, persistent cough, difficulty swallowing, haemoptysis, and sore throat.
Maxillofacial bones: pain (70%), paresthesia, swelling, exophytic growth and mobile teeth
{ 7815371 ; 17138711 ; 25409855 }.
Epidemiology
Head/neck metastases may occur after a short or prolonged interval (years) after initial discovery
of the primary tumour { 27844410 ; 30273434 ; 27218239 }. ‘‘True’’ carcinoma of unknown primary
(CUP) accounts for 1–2% of head and neck cancers { 25804376 }.
Neck lymph node metastases mostly originate from head and neck sites { 23870760 }; cystic nodal
metastasis predicts tonsil, tongue base, or thyroid primary { 25804376 ; 12478648 }. Metastatic
CUP is more common in older patients (55-65 years) and men { 22034046 }. Breast and lung
carcinoma appear in supraclavicular nodes { 10208667 ; 27844410 }; pelvic/intra-abdominal
metastases favor Virchow’s node { 7646330 }. Less common carcinomas include cervix,
esophagus, ovary, and pancreas { 10208667 }.
Oral cavity metastases (1% of all oral malignancies) { 18061527 } are the sentinel event in about
one third { 23646853 ; 25409855 }. Tumour type is gender dependent: breast, kidney and skin
carcinoma in women, and lung carcinoma in men { 27891304 ; 31570205 } with most patients in
6th - 7th decades { 18061527 }. Tongue metastases are from lung and renal cell tumours
{ 27891304 }.
SG metastases range from 4% to 24% of SG tumours { 28001329 }, are increasing
{ 22281605 ; 28872155 } and affect males predominantly. Parotid metastases arise from
cutaneous head/neck malignancies: squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma, Merkel cell carcinoma,
and basal cell carcinoma { 3562340 ; 28534378 ; 33544379 }; melanoma is second only to
squamous carcinoma { 17141071 ; 28872155 }. Less common metastases include sebaceous
carcinoma { 25521100 }, and carcinomas of the lung, breast, kidney, stomach, nasopharynx, and
prostate { 9578259 ; 19186459 ; 25129420 ; 26416080 ; 29212872 ; 29449729 }.
About 1% of Pharyngeal malignancies are metastases { 668170 ; 20596975 ; 23110013 }, typically
associated with disseminated disease { 17331151 ; 20596975 }, and affect middle aged adults
{ 20596975 }.
Sinonasal tract metastasis is rare { 22705221 ; 27218239 }, higher in elderly patients, and slightly
more common in males. Renal cell carcinoma is common followed by carcinomas of lung, breast,
prostate and thyroid { 11602931 ; 20596975 ; 27649110 ; 30273434 }. Metastatic hepatocellular
carcinoma and gastrointestinal adenocarcinoma are common in certain countries like Taiwan
{ 31010194 }.
Metastases to the Ear/temporal bone are rare (< 2%), increase to 20% in autopsy studies
{ 379358 }, and include breast, uterine carcinoma in females, and prostate adenocarcinoma in
males
{ 1869438 ; 1919860 ; 1991061 ; 2995524 ; 4328134 ; 4938079 ; 7964154 ; 8892576 ; 9509102 ;
10680873 ; 11945183 ; 15768802 ; 18754073 ; 24077267 ; 26545469 ; 26545474 ; 29909612 }.
Metastases are < 0.5% of Laryngeal malignancies, increase with increasing age and are twice as
common in males. Melanoma is followed by carcinomas of kidney, lung, breast, colon, and prostate.
Metastatic
sarcoma
is
exceptionally
rare
{ 3655540 ; 20596975 ; 21500157 ; 26743607 ; 30119910 }. Breast carcinoma is the most
common malignancy to metastasize to maxillofacial bones (<1% of head /neck metastatic bone
disease)
followed
by
metastatic
lung,
colorectal,
and
renal
cancers
{ 7815371 ; 17138711 ; 25409855 }.
Etiology

1054

Human papillomaviruses (HPV) and Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) are provable carcinogenic
factors and represent a significant etiologic component of CUP { 33244460 }. Of these, HPVassociated oropharyngeal cancer is the predominant viral-induced head and neck cancer in North
America and much of Europe { 34066342 ; 31893516 }. EBV infection exists in 100% of
nonkeratinizing nasopharyngeal carcinomas and is widely predominant in southern China
{ 28893937 }. A statistically significant association exists between hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection
and cancers of the oral cavity, and oropharynx, and larynx { 32599500 }.
Pathogenesis
Pathways of metastatic spread include haematogenous dissemination via the pulmonary circulation
or via the paravertebral plexus of Batson, bypassing the lungs { 17857618 }. Lymphatic spread is
either by anterograde, or in the case of the palatine tonsil that lacks afferent vessels, retrograde
flow { 443718 }. Another possible route is direct implantation following local trauma associated with
instrumentation e.g endoscopy { 443718 ; 8730375 ; 17657505 }.
As the most common cause of head and neck metastases, squamous carcinoma is a multi-step
process caused by genomic instability secondary to variety of key genetic pathways leading to
epithelial dysplasia followed by penetration of an epithelial basement membrane, and invasion of
the angiolymphatic system thus empowering malignant cells with the ability of metastatic
dissemination { 33243986 ; 34353444 ; 34518141 }. Tumour dissemination seems to depend on
both pre-existing lymphatics and lymphangiogenesis within the tumour or at the tumour periphery
{ 17160713 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Metastases range from microscopic to large clinically evident masses. They may be single or
multiple, ulcerated, solid or cystic or both, and possess variable necrosis, haemorrhage and/or
calcification { 18383529 ; 31145296 }.
Histopathology
Correlation with clinical, imaging, and serologic findings is generally required along with tumour
histopathology for definitive diagnosis. Histopathology frequently mirrors the primary tumour
phenotype; grade progression may occur. Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma
and melanoma are most frequent. Selective and targeted immunohistochemistry may be required
to support a specific phenotype, or origin from a specific anatomic site (see Table #27219). Highgrade transformation (dedifferentiation) is a component of some neoplasms producing metastases
whose histopathology differs from the primary tumour { 21876843 ; 23821210 ; 26245749 }.
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration (FNA) functions as a highly accurate, efficient, cost efficient triage and
diagnostic tool. This is particularly true for salivary gland, lymph node, and soft tissue masses
{ 7646330 ; 17041956 ; 24935698 ; 25656853 ; 28001329 ; 28032725 ; 28371507 ; 33017519 }.
Because of high sensitivity (83-97%) and specificity (91-100%) for diagnosis of metastatic lesions
{ 18528906 ; 22034046 ; 30488579 }, FNA is often recommended as the initial diagnostic modality
{ 22034046 }. A false negative rate (up to 42%) may be seen with cystic lesions { 22034046 }.
Cytologic features are dependent on tumour type. Ancillary studies (immunohistochemistry, in situ
hybridization, and molecular analysis) can frequently be performed from FNA specimens
{ 24478259 ; 28727266 ; 30600323 ; 32507626 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Selective molecular testing may confirm specific tumour types.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Malignant neoplasm at region that is distant from a primary tumour site.
Desirable: Known primary tumour; clinical, imaging, and serologic correlation (in selected cases).
Staging
Stage according to the UICC TNM classification for the primary site.
Prognosis and prediction
Metastatic
disease,
particularly
with
extra-nodal
extension
{ 2030629 ; 12570052 ; 26514096 ; 31077394 } is indicative of high clinical stage, poor prognosis,
and
generally
reduced
survival
{ 12244407 ; 15805872 ; 20592390 ; 20596975 ; 20596978 ; 23646853 ; 26743607 }.

metastatic to the parotid gland.

parotid gland.

16. Neuroendocrine neoplasms and paraganglioma
Introduction
Neuroendocrine neoplasms
Neuroendocrine tumours
Neuroendocrine tumour
Ectopic or invasive PitNET/adenoma
Neuroendocrine carcinoma
Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
Merkel cell carcinoma
Paraganglion tumours
Head and neck paraganglioma

Introduction
The 5th edition of the WHO classification of the head and neck neuroendocrine neoplasms (NEN)
has been revised to reflect both the WHO/IARC unified terminology framework { 30140036 } as
well as progress made in diagnostic biomarkers. The upper aerodigestive tract and salivary gland
NENs are discussed as a group, but invasive/ectopic pituitary neuroendocrine tumour
(PitNET)/pituitary adenoma, Merkel cell carcinoma, and paraganglioma are each presented in
separate sections given their distinct clinical and pathological characteristics. Previously classified
as middle ear adenoma, middle ear neuroendocrine tumour (MeNET) is formerly recognized as a
NEN { 34041698 }, but discussed in the Ear chapter given its unique anatomic considerations.
In this new classification, the term neuroendocrine carcinoma (NEC) is applied only to poorly
differentiated NENs including small cell and large cell NECs. Neuroendocrine tumours (NETs)
represent well-differentiated epithelial NENs, and are assigned to three proliferative tumour grades
(G1, G2, G3) (see Table #28634). Importantly at present, the G3 NET category remains provisional.
G2 NETs may exhibit necrosis and/or 2-10 mitoses per 2mm2 while G1 NETs lack necrosis and
have <2 mitoses per 2mm2. The Ki67 proliferation index for NETs is generally <20%
{ 22082601 ; 26830400 }, but optimal Ki67 cut-off level for the biologic distinction between G1 and
G2 NETs remains to be determined { 34401980 }.
Immunohistochemical biomarkers that are useful in the diagnostic workup of NENs are discussed
in each section. Given the complexity of NENs, it is often insufficient to classify a tumour with
chromogranin and synaptophysin alone. The importance of keratin immunohistochemistry and
immunohistochemical confirmation of diffuse neuroendocrine differentiation, including the
emerging role of INSM1 transcription factor, is emphasized in the diagnostic workup of NECs.
The current WHO classification of PitNETs/pituitary adenomas requires routine use of transcription
factors (PIT1, SF1, TPIT, GATA3, and ER-alpha) and antibodies against adenohypophysial
hormones as well as other biomarkers including but not limited to keratins. GATA3 expression
alone should not be used to confirm PitNETs/pituitary adenomas since GATA3 is also expressed
in various other neoplasms including paragangliomas, the latter potentially arising from dispersed
paraganglia that are closely aligned with components of the autonomous nervous system. The use
of tyrosine hydroxylase may help distinguish paraganglioma in a keratin-negative NEN
{ 27529763 }.
Paraganglioma is the most heritable human neoplasm, with the reported rate of at least 40%
genetic predisposition and a high probability of synchronous/metachronous multifocal disease
{ 28162975 ; 33433885 }. The value of molecular biomarkers and Ki67 proliferation index in
predicting metastasis is highlighted. The significant proportion of head and neck region germline
disease is linked to pathogenic variants in the SDH genes { 29299512 }. The paraganglioma
section particularly expands on the role of molecular immunohistochemistry, which can be used as
screening tools to predict the pathogenetic basis of paragangliomas including SDHB (SDHx-related
pathogenesis), alpha-inhibin (including SDHx- and VHL-related pseudohypoxia pathway)
{ 33826547 },
and
carbonic
anhydrase
IX (VHL-related
pseudohypoxia
pathway)
{ 23257898 ; 31383958 ; 33826547 }.
Routine HPV testing in NECs is not recommended due to lack of proven prognostic benefit. The
role of p53 and Rb immunohistochemistry is discussed in the distinction between NET and NEC,
especially G3 NET from NEC. Further work is ongoing to determine whether IDH2-mutated tumours

1064

are unique or represent a common pathway in several high grade poorly differentiated malignancies
of the sinonasal tract { 31934917 ; 34265800 }.
Merkel cell carcinoma is a high-grade cutaneous NEC that is distinguished from other NECs. The
role of MCPyV-positivity and/or other immunohistochemical features supportive of Merkel cell
carcinoma is discussed.
