nuclei with prominent nucleoli, peripheral palisading, rosette formation, comedo-pattern necrosis

Neuroendocrine neoplasms
Neuroendocrine tumours

Neuroendocrine tumour
Definition
Neuroendocrine tumours are well differentiated epithelial neuroendocrine neoplasms that arise in
the upper aerodigestive tract and salivary glands.
ICD-O coding
8240/3
Neuroendocrine
tumour,
NOS
8240/3
Neuroendocrine
tumour,
grade
1
8249/3 Neuroendocrine tumour, grade 2
8249/3 Neuroendocrine tumour, grade 3
ICD-11 coding
2B6B.Y & XH9LV8 Other specified malignant neoplasms of nasopharynx & Neuroendocrine
tumour,
grade
1
2B6B.Y & XH51K1 Other specified malignant neoplasms of nasopharynx & Neuroendocrine
tumour, grade 2
2B6B.Y & XH5QW8 Other specified malignant neoplasms of nasopharynx & Neuroendocrine
tumour, grade 3
Related terminology
Acceptable: Well differentiated neuroendocrine tumour

1065

Not recommended: Carcinoid tumour, typical carcinoid tumour, atypical carcinoid tumour, welldifferentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma, moderately differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma,
neuroendocrine carcinoma
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Neuroendocrine tumours (NETs) arise from the cells of the dispersed neuroendocrine system that
are found in respiratory mucosa of the nasal cavity, paranasal sinuses, nasopharynx, oropharynx
and larynx. They also rarely occur in salivary glands { 22614165 ; 23456649 ; 27610258 } and the
oral cavity { 10484121 ; 22359135 ;}. Sinonasal NETs are usually within the ethmoid sinus, nasal
cavity, and maxillary sinus { 25727332 ; 26041714 ; 29427030 ;}. NETs also arise from the middle
ear mucosa (MeNET) { 34041698 }.
Clinical features
Nasopharyngeal NETs present with obstruction and epistaxis. Paranasal sinus tumours cause
headache and obstructive symptoms { 26830400 }. Middle ear tumours give rise to unilateral
conductive hearing loss, pain and/or discharge; facial nerve paralysis suggests aggressive local
invasion. Laryngeal tumours may cause hoarseness, dysphonia, sore throat, and hemoptysis
{ 29557536 }, while some are asymptomatic and detected incidentally during laryngoscopy or
intubation for unrelated reasons. Salivary gland NETs present as a mass
{ 22614165 ; 23456649 ; 27610258 }. Metastases to lymph nodes may present as a neck mass.
Rarely, elevated serum calcitonin levels due to calcitonin secretion from laryngeal NETs can occur
{ 8236510 ; 32765421 } and requires distinction from medullary thyroid carcinoma. In such
situations, functional imaging studies (e.g., Ga68-DOTA PET/CT) are useful in the identification of
laryngeal or thyroid origin { 32765421 }. Exceptionally, presentation may be attributed to hormone
excess syndromes such as carcinoid syndrome { 2692106 }.
Imaging
Structural imaging studies (CT and MRI) are often used to delineate extent and tumour location of
NETs; however, functional imaging studies (e.g., Ga68-DOTA PET/CT) that target somatostatin
receptors located on the tumour cell membrane are particularly useful in localizing, staging and
follow-up of these tumours { 32765421 }.
Epidemiology
Nasal cavity and Paranasal sinuses: NETs account for about 4% of sinonasal NENs { 29103747 },
representing only about 3% of sinonasal tumours. The age at diagnosis ranges from 13 to 83 years.
There is no sex predilection.
Larynx: Neuroendocrine neoplasms are the second most common laryngeal tumour after
squamous cell carcinomas { 29557536 ; 31033507 ; 34401980 }. Grade 2 NET (atypical
carcinoid) is the most frequent followed by NEC and Grade 1 NET (typical carcinoid) is the least
common { 25674282 ; 29557536 ; 31033507 ; 34401980 }. The accuracy of these statistics,
however,
is
questionable
given
the
changing
diagnostic
terminologies
{ 19536850 ; 31033507 ; 34401980 }. Laryngeal NETs are more frequent in males in their 6th and
7th decades with a male-to-female ratio of 2.4:1 { 34401980 }.
Middle ear: NETs are very rare neoplasms in the middle ear. They are discussed in chapter
12.1.1.2.
Salivary gland: NETs are exceptionally rare in the salivary glands; they are reported without a sex
bias
in
the
parotid
and
submandibular
glands
{ 16999063 ; 22614165 ; 23456649 ; 27610258 ; 28635990 }.
Etiology
No etiologic factors have been identified to date. Smoking has been implicated in the etiology of
laryngeal NENs { 31033507 } but this seems to apply mainly to the more aggressive
neuroendocrine carcinomas and grade 2 NETs { 19536850 ; 19883182 ; 25351497 ; 3056608 }.
There
is
no
well-established
association
with
HPV
in
these
tumours
{ 18617341 ; 22699626 ; 26886629 ; 31033507 }.
Pathogenesis
Unknown
Macroscopic appearance
Macroscopically, NETs present as polypoid, nodular, pedunculated, exophytic masses that may
ulcerate

1066

Histopathology
These tumours are well differentiated epithelial neoplasms composed of cords, trabecula or small
nests of neuroendocrine cells with ample granular pale acidophilic cytoplasm and relatively
monotonous nuclei with the typical “salt-and-pepper” appearance that is characteristic of most
NETs. They usually underlie the mucosa; ulceration may develop. The stroma is usually highly
vascular but may be fibrotic or hyalinized. Pleomorphism is not generally seen. Grade 2 NETs
exhibit necrosis (often punctate or coagulative) and/or 2-10 mitoses per 2mm2; in contrast grade 1
NETs lack necrosis and have <2 mitoses per 2mm2. The Ki67 proliferation index is generally <20%
{ 22082601 ; 26830400 ; 34401980 }. The optimal Ki67 level for the distinction between grade 1
and grade 2 NETs remains to be determined, although certainly suggested { 34401980 }. NETs
with Grade 3 proliferative features remain to be defined in the upper aerodigestive tract and salivary
glands.
These tumours show diffuse nuclear INSM1 immunopositivity { 29438167 } and cytoplasmic
synaptophysin and chromogranin-A staining. They are positive for keratins including CK7/8 and
CAM5.2. There is very limited data on their transcription factor and hormone profiles. Particularly
some laryngeal NETs are known to express serotonin, calcitonin and carcinoembryonic antigen
(CEA) that is identified with a monoclonal antibody { 12071530 ; 29557536 ; 32765421 }. TTF1
expression is often negative but may be focal and weak { 15098009 }. MeNETs express SATB2
{ 34041698 } and ISL1 (islet-1) { 24766278 }, and often have predominant L-cell differentiation
(immunoreactive for pancreatic polypeptide, peptide-YY, glucagon or glucagon-like peptides)
{ 34041698 ; 12011260 ; 7599794 }. MeNETs are TTF1-negative but variable CDX2, GATA3 and
PSAP expression can be identified { 34041698 }. Unlike poorly differentiated neuroendocrine
carcinomas,
NETs
do
not
show
aberrant
expression
of
p53
and
Rb
{ 22082601 ; 22699626 ; 27392929 ; 34401980 }. The routine use of Ki67 is encouraged; it is
particularly helpful in the diagnostic workup of all small biopsy specimens with crush artifact to
avoid overdiagnosis of high-grade NECs.
Exceptional cases can be mixed neuroendocrine and nonneuroendocrine tumours (MiNENs) in the
nasal cavity { 32138448 } and larynx { 19930775 }. Occasional tumours may have oncocytic or
mucinous features, and can simulate other neoplasms. The presence of large nests resembling
“zellballen” may prompt consideration of paraganglioma (see section 12.2.0.2). The differential
diagnosis includes medullary thyroid carcinoma in the larynx, PitNET in the nasopharynx and
sinuses, and paraganglioma in any location { 28338500 ; 28009611 }. Low grade olfactory
neuroblastoma { 26830400 } and lymphoma should also be considered { 25992139 }; other lesions
such as primitive neuroectodermal tumours, Ewing sarcoma, desmoplastic round cell tumour, NUT
carcinoma, sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, nonkeratinizing
carcinoma, and basaloid carcinoma are more likely to be confused with high-grade NECs
{ 26830400 ; 31415081 }.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
None
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Well-differentiated neuroendocrine neoplasm with immunohistochemical evidence of
neuroendocrine differentiation Positivity for at least one keratin Arising in the upper aerodigestive
tract and/or salivary gland
Desirable:
Ki67 proliferation index No abnormal p53 staining and no loss of Rb (in selected cases with Ki67
>20%)
Staging
Site specific AJCC/UICC TNM staging 8th edition
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis of these tumours is variable as with other NETs and especially difficult to determine
because of their rarity. Low-grade laryngeal NETs have a reported 5-year survival of approximately

1067

80% after conservative surgical resection { 12071530 }; however, distant metastasis has been
described, frequently involving the liver { 24013480 }. Lymph node metastases are infrequent,
therefore neck dissection is not routinely performed. Laryngeal tumours formerly classified as
atypical carcinoids or moderately differentiated NEC (currently Grade 2 NET) present with
advanced disease in about 20-30% of cases and about 60% of cases recur. Treatment includes
surgical resection with neck dissection followed by adjuvant chemo- and/or radiotherapy when
there are regional lymph node metastases. Chemoradiotherapy alone does not appear to be
effective { 19536850 }. In a meta-analysis of 436 reported cases published in 2015, the 5-year DFS
and OS were 52.8% and 46%, respectively { 24596175 }. Delayed recurrences are documented
{ 28635990 }. Nevertheless, the existing data should be interpreted with caution given the
diagnostic heterogeneity previously applied in these tumours.

#26794: These well-differentiated tumours present as mass lesions and have trabecular or
organoid architecture underlying the squamous mucosa.

L#26795: The tumour cells have intense cytoplasmic positivity for chromogranin A
R#26796: The proliferative rate of these tumours is generally relatively low, but cut-off levels are
not yet established to define grading.

1068

L#26923: A trabecular, organoid and nested architecture is noted, as the tumour surrounds
cartilage islands. There is a very even chromatin distribution in cells with a relatively high nuclear
to cytoplasmic ratio. Mitoses are inconspicuous
R#27107: This supraglottic tumour shows a nested to ribbon like and solid growth, with neoplastic
cells that are small to medium with salt-and-pepper nuclear chromatin distribution. There are
limited mitoses and no necrosis.

Ectopic or invasive PitNET/adenoma
Definition
A well differentiated pituitary neuroendocrine tumour composed of adenohypophysial cells occuring
within the sinonasal tract.
ICD-O coding
8272/3 Pituitary neuroendocrine tumour, NOS / pituitary adenoma, NOS
8272/3 Metastatic PitNET/adenoma, NOS
ICD-11 coding
2B6B.Y & XH5RH2 Other specified malignant neoplasms of nasopharynx & Pituitary adenoma,
ectopic
Related terminology
Acceptable:
Ectopic PitNET; ectopic pituitary adenoma; invasive PitNET; invasive
pituitary adenoma
Subtype(s)
Subtypes of pituitary neuroendocrine tumours (PitNETs) are outlined in Table 1.
Localization
Invasion into the sinonasal tract from the sella is much more common than truly ectopic PitNETs
{ 28009611 ; 29434339 }. Ectopic PitNETs arise in the sphenoid sinus or upper nasopharynx
{ 22430769 ; 28009611 ; 10773251 ; 15274075 ; 3017137 } and less frequently in the ethmoid
sinus and nasal bridge { 22430769 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms may be attributed to endocrinopathies, to mass effect, or both. Cushing syndrome,
acromegaly, or amenorrhea/galactorrhea are the most common endocrine manifestations
{ 8550790 ; 12114673 ; 12170097 ; 12611618 }. Mass effects include obstruction, sinusitis,
rhinorrhea, and headache { 22430769 ; 28009611 }. Laboratory investigation for elevated pituitary
hormones, with and without stimulation or suppression testing informs functional status as part of
classification.
Inherited
or
syndrome
association
may
require
investigation
{ 19153518 ; 19407507 ; 19945022 }.

1069

Imaging
Preoperative imaging studies determine tumour size and extent, and exclude direct extension from
the sella, a common occurrence { 28009611 }. Gallium 68 DOTATATE positron emission
tomography shows increased uptake { 30797906 }.
Epidemiology
Neuroendocrine tumours of the sinonasal tract comprise about 3% of all sinonasal tract tumours,
and PitNETs account for a significant proportion, especially those of the sphenoid sinus
{ 10773251 ; 15274075 ; 22430769 }, recognizing that direct extension is much more common than
ectopic disease. In this group, patients present over a broad age range (2–84 years) with the
majority in the 6th decade; females are affected slightly more often than males (1.3:1)
{ 22430769 ; 28009611 }.
Etiology
The majority of PitNETs occur sporadically { 1977759 }. A minority of tumours is associated with
familial predisposition syndromes, implicating specific germline mutations in the development of
these tumours { 25905184 ; 32201880 ; 31658440 }.
Pathogenesis
Among sporadic tumours, the most common recurrent somatic mutations that drive tumorigenesis
affect the GNAS gene in up to 40% of somatotroph tumours { 17514647 ; 1977758 ; 2549426 },
and the USP8 and USP48 genes in approximately 50% of corticotroph tumours
{ 25485838 ; 25675982 ; 25942478 ; 26578638 ; 28505279 ; 28982703 ; 29957855 ; 31222332 ;
30093687 }. Novel mutations associated with sporadic tumours include CABLES1
{ 26695862 ; 28533356 } in corticotroph tumours, and ATRX or TP53 { 18026859 ; 33106857 } in
metastatic PitNETs. Apart from these rare events, recurrent molecular alterations have not been
identified and instead epigenetic alterations may be relevant to tumourigenesis
{ 20060434 ; 28711607 ; 29396598 ; 31139150 ; 16873443 ; 18544619 }.
Chromosomal
alterations are common, which is unusual for tumours with largely indolent behaviour { 28486603 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Ectopic PitNETs range in size from 8 to 80 mm (mean 35 mm) { 3017137 ; 22430769 ; 28009611 }.
Generally removed as fragments, they have no specific gross features.
Histopathology
PitNETs are generally monomorphic, with cells arranged in a variety of histologic patterns, including
diffuse, glandular, papillary, and trabecular arrangements. Cytologically, tumour cells may be
acidophilic, basophilic, or chromophobic; however, these tinctorial characteristics are nonspecific.
PitNETs reflect their derivation from six adenohypophysial cell types with multiple variants of each.
Some PitNETs are composed of less differentiated cells, such as acidophil stem cell and immature
PIT1 lineage tumours (formerly known as silent subtype 3 adenomas, or poorly differentiated PIT1
lineage PitNETs) { 26743473 ; 28766057 }. Null cell tumours fail to demonstrate evidence of
differentiation into SF1, TPIT or PIT1 lineage { 31401793 ; 29434339 ; 25403448 }. Cells may have
densely or sparsely granulated cytoplasm according to the tumour type (see Table), explaining the
variable histological tinctorial qualities. Nuclei tend to be bland with regularly distributed chromatin.
Mitotic activity is in generally low. The presence of extensive cellular pleomorphism, brisk mitotic
activity, lymphatic or vascular invasion, atypical mitoses, and necrosis is not typical and should
prompt consideration of alternate diagnoses. Calcification or ossification is rare, occurring in
occasional lactotroph and thyrotroph tumours. Infiltration of bone/cartilage is common and minor
mucoserous glands may be surrounded or displaced by tumours.
Hormone immunohistochemistry alone can be misleading due to nonspecific or diffusion-type
staining; therefore, the use of stains for pituitary transcription factors along with hormone profiling
is required to ensure accurate tumour characterization { 32813227 }. These tumours frequently
have trapped non-tumourous elements that can complicate interpretation { 32813227 }, further
emphasizing the importance of thorough assessment. The distinction of multiple synchronous
PitNETs of distinct lineages requires the use of transcription factors { 30215160 }. For these
reasons, the current WHO classification of PitNETs requires routine use of transcription factors
(PIT1, SF1, TPIT, GATA3, and ER-alpha) and antibodies against adenohypophysial hormones
(ACTH, GH, PRL, beta-TSH, beta-LH, beta-FSH and alpha-subunit of glycoprotein hormones) as
well as other biomarkers including but not limited to keratins (e.g., CAM5.2, CK8/18)
{ 29453601 ; 32813227 ; 34017065 ; 29434339 } (see Table) [[ENDO5]]. Similar to other NETs, the
Ki67 proliferation index should be assessed using either manual counting or automated image

1070

analysis nuclear algorithms { 29434339 ; 34017065 }. When metastatic disease occurs, the term
metastatic PitNET is preferred; the use of “adenoma” and “carcinoma” creates a problem since
most patients present with an initial primary tumour and the development of metastases would
require a change in the initial diagnosis { 34017065 } [[ENDO5]]. There is no single morphological
feature
or
biomarker
that
can
accurately
predict
metastatic
spread
{ 34017065 ; 22822048 ; 33433883 }. There is also no widely accepted grading system for
PitNETs.
The distinction of PitNETs from other tumours and detailed histological subtyping of a PitNET based
on transcription factor lineage, cell type, and in some instances, subtype as determined by other
characteristics are clinically critical since accurate classification provides risk stratification and
predicts
differential
response
to
therapies
{ 34017065 ; 32813227 ; 26177314 ; 24144285 ; 29434339 }.
The differential diagnosis includes paraganglioma, olfactory neuroblastoma, hypothalamic
neurocytoma, sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma, teratocarcinosarcoma, primary sinonasal well
differentiated neuroendocrine tumour, melanoma, meningioma, metastatic tumours, and even
rhabdomyosarcoma { 28060373 ; 30217041 ; 30379651 }. Many sinonasal tract tumours may coexpress neuroendocrine markers, including sinonasal adenocarcinomas, sinonasal
undifferentiated carcinoma, and melanoma, among others, but morphologic features with specific
biomarker reactivity is required for this distinction.
Cytology
In cytologic preparations, PitNETs exhibit hypercellularity and a homogeneous appearance. Due
to a relative lack of stroma compared to normal, the cells of tumours do not aggregate in large
numbers and are seen predominantly as individual cells and small clusters. Papillary formations
may be seen. These tumours cannot be distinguished from other NETs without
immunohistochemistry.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Epithelial well differentiated neuroendocrine neoplasm (NET) Immunohistochemical confirmation
of adenohypophysial differentiation using pituitary transcription factors and hormones
Desirable:
CAM5.2 or CK8/18 Ki67 proliferation index
Staging
There is no formal staging system that has been universally applied for pituitary/sellar neoplasms
and any proposals would not apply to ectopic tumours. However, by definition dellar tumours
presented as a sinonasal mass would have a high stage classification using the Knosp
classification { 8232800 }.
There are no pathology-standardized reporting templates yet endorsed by international pathology
organizations. A pathology reporting template for pituitary tumours was introduced in 2011
{ 21526962 } followed by the European Pituitary Pathology Group proposal in 2019 { 31578606 }.
Prognosis and prediction
PitNETs continue to grow without intervention and those with hormone hypersecretion cause
medical complications that can be lethal.Prognosis is dependent on tumour subtype and response
to therapy { 24144285 }. Recurrences may develop and are more likely when incompletely excised
initially { 7857023 ; 19945022 ; 22430769 }. Complete tumour resection is challenging given
tumour
location.
Exceptionally,
metastatic
behaviour
may
be
seen
{ 3095506 ; 7857023 ; 12170097 ; 26891865 }.

Neuroendocrine carcinoma

Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
Definition
Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (SCNEC) is a poorly differentiated (high-grade)
neuroendocrine carcinoma composed of epithelial cells with scant cytoplasm, hyperchromatic
nuclei, finely granular chromatin, inconspicuous nucleoli, high mitotic count, and frequent necrosis.
ICD-O coding
8041/3
Small
cell
carcinoma
8045/3 Combined small cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2C23.1Y & XH0YB0 Other specified malignant neoplasms of larynx, glottis & Small cell carcinoma,
NOS
Related terminology
Acceptable: Neuroendocrine carcinoma, small cell type; poorly differentiated neuroendocrine
carcinoma, small cell type

1076

Not recommended: oat cell carcinoma; small cell undifferentiated carcinoma; grade III
neuroendocrine carcinoma; high grade neuroendocrine neoplasm
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
Approximately 60% of head and neck SCNEC arise in the larynx, the majority in the supraglottis
{ 27859290 }. 35% occur in the sinonasal tract, most frequently affecting the nasal cavity
{ 26880574 ; 27859290 }. Less than 5% involve the oropharynx, oral cavity, nasopharynx, or major
salivary glands { 20659699 ; 26258144 ; 27859290 }.
Clinical features
Symptoms depend on site of origin. In the larynx, patients frequently present with hoarseness or
dysphagia. Sinonasal tumours often show mass effect with nasal obstruction or epistaxis. 50-70%
of all SCNEC have regional or distant metastasis at the time of presentation
{ 1653928 ; 27938993 ; 31437725 }. Paraneoplastic syndromes rarely occur { 17727081 }.
Epidemiology
SCNEC comprises 40-50% of laryngeal neuroendocrine neoplasms { 24596175 ; 26183520 }.
Prevalence in the sinonasal tract is not well-established. Laryngeal SCNEC has a mean age of 5962 years and a male-to-female ratio of 1.6-3.4:1 { 6299507 ; 24596175 ; 27859290 ; 31437725 },
while sinonasal SCNEC has a mean age of 51-58 and a male-to-female ratio of 1-2.2:1
{ 9712424 ; 16526967 ; 26880574 ; 27859290 ; 27938993 }.
Etiology
Risk factors correspond to anatomic site. More than 90% of laryngeal SCNEC are associated with
tobacco use, but this association is not strong in the sinonasal tract { 31437725 ; 25992139 }. The
majority of oropharyngeal SCNEC are positive for high-risk HPV, although patients often have
smoking history as well { 21997688 ; 22301491 }. A subset of sinonasal SCNEC also harbour highrisk HPV { 23095507 ; 26229021 }. There is a disproportionate incidence of HPV18 involvement in
SCNEC, although HPV16 likely remains the prevalent genotype { 21997688 ; 23095507 ;
31550725, 33232848}. With the exception of rare SCNEC that arise ex pleomorphic adenoma
{22736150,33741286}, most salivary gland tumours previously classified as SCNEC carry Merkel
cell polyomavirus or UV-signature mutations and are best classified as metastatic Merkel cell
carcinoma { 22204708 ; 25457888 ; 30986802 }.
Pathogenesis
The molecular basis of laryngeal, oropharyngeal, and oral SCNEC have not been fully evaluated.
Immunohistochemistry demonstrates consistent RB1 loss and p53 overexpression, similar to lung
SCNEC { 27392929 ; 22082601 ; 33433884 }. RNA in-situ hybridization highlights transcriptional
activity in HPV-associated SCNEC, suggesting that viral oncoproteins drive these neoplasms
{ 29556964 }. Emerging molecular data in sinonasal SCNEC suggests that they arise along unique
pathways with recurrent ARID1A mutations { 31186531 }. Although some sinonasal tumours with
immunohistochemical SMARCA4 loss have small cell morphology and express neuroendocrine
markers, such tumours are now regarded as SMARCA4-deficient sinonasal carcinoma rather than
SCNEC { 31934917 }.
Macroscopic appearance
SCNEC are generally large and highly infiltrative and form fleshy submucosal masses with frequent
ulceration and necrosis.
Histopathology
SCNEC grows in sheets and nests, with occasional trabeculae, peripheral palisading, or rosettes.
Tumour cells are usually smaller than the diameter of three lymphocytes with scant cytoplasm and
indistinct cellular borders. They have large hyperchromatic nuclei, finely granular to stippled
chromatin, and absent or inconspicuous nucleoli with frequent nuclear moulding. The mitotic count
is >10 mitoses per 2mm2 but is often much higher with numerous apoptotic bodies and necrosis.
Crush artifact with extravasated DNA coating blood vessels (Azzopardi phenomenon) is also
common. Approximately 5% of laryngeal SCNEC but up to 80% of oropharyngeal SCNEC occur in
combination with another histology, usually squamous cell carcinoma { 24596175 ; 21997688 }
Immunohistochemistry:
SCNEC should be positive for cytokeratin, particularly low-molecular weight cocktails such as
CAM5.2 and frequently in a perinuclear dot-like distribution { 9712424 ; 20090218 }. The
expression of first-generation neuroendocrine differentiation markers (e.g., synaptophysin and

1077

chromogranin-A) may be variable with dot-like staining pattern { 9712424 ; 27529763 }. For this
reason, the use of second-generation neuroendocrine differentiation biomarker, especially the
INSM1 (nuclear transcription factor that regulates neuroendocrine differentiation), has been shown
to be useful in the diagnostic workup of neoplasms with variable synaptophysin and/or focal/absent
chromogranin-A expression { 29438167 ; 32813226 ; 31857137 ; 33786701 }. The use of nonspecific biomarkers alone (e.g., CD56, CD57 and neuron-specific enolase) is discouraged, and one
has to interpret them in conjunction with diffuse INSM1 or diffuse synaptophysin and chromograninA expression. TTF1 is positive in a subset of cases { 10976695 }. The role of Ki67 for grading head
and neck SCNEC is not well-established, but most have a Ki67 index of >20%, and often >70%
{ 22082601 ; 22699626 }. Abnormal p53 expression { 22082601 ; 27392929 } and global Rb loss
{ 27392929 } are common. p16 is often overexpressed in SCNEC regardless of HPV status
{ 27392929 ; 29251996 }.
Differential diagnosis:
In all sites, SCNEC displays histologic overlap with large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (abundant
cytoplasm/prominent nucleoli), well differentiated neuroendocrine tumours with crush artifact (<10
mitoses per 2mm2 and <20% Ki67 with no aberrant p53 and Rb expression), basaloid squamous
cell carcinoma (squamous pearls/surface dysplasia and p63/p40 positive; lacking diffuse INSM1),
and solid adenoid cystic carcinoma (neuroendocrine marker negative). Sinonasal SCNEC should
be distinguished immunohistochemically from many small round blue cell tumours, including
olfactory neuroblastoma (cytokeratin negative), lymphoma (CD45 positive), melanoma
(S100/SOX10 positive), rhabdomyosarcoma (desmin/myogenin positive), Ewing sarcoma
(CD99/NKX2.2 positive), NUT carcinoma (NUT1 positive), SMARCB1-deficient sinonasal
carcinoma (SMARCB1 loss), SMARCA4-deficient sinonasal carcinoma (SMARCA4 loss), and
sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma (neuroendocrine marker negative).
Cytology
Fine-needle aspiration specimens show hypercellular sheets of small round blue cells with nuclear
molding, numerous mitotic figures and apoptotic bodies, and powdery salt and pepper chromatin
with background crush artifact and necrotic debris { 30468701 ; 30475447 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular testing is not needed for diagnosis of SCNEC. Routine high risk HPV RNA in situ
hybridization testing is not recommended { 27392929 ; 29251996 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
High grade carcinoma with minimal cytoplasm, hyperchromatic molded nuclei, finely granular
chromatin, and inconspicuous nucleoli >10 mitoses per 2 mm2 Immunohistochemical evidence of
diffuse neuroendocrine differentiation Immunohistochemical reactivity at least for one cytokeratin
Desirable:
Cell size smaller than diameter of three lymphocytes Prominent apoptotic bodies and necrosis
Paranuclear dot-like cytokeratin reactivity Ki67 proliferation index >20%, and often >70%
Staging
AJCC/UICC TNM staging for the appropriate anatomic site can be applied.
Prognosis and prediction
In the larynx, up to 90% of SCNEC develop distant metastases, and 5-year survival is 5-20%
{ 1653928 ; 22082601 ; 24596175 }. Sinonasal SCNEC have better outcomes, with 5-year
survival of 40-50% { 26880574 ; 27859290 ; 27938993 ;}. Regardless of site, lower stage tumours
are associated with longer survival { 26183520 ; 27859290 }. HPV-related pathogenesis does not
improve prognosis in SCNEC { 23095507 ; 26229021 }.

1078

L#25937: Scant cytoplasm, nuclear molding, and numerous mitotic figures and apoptotic bodies.
R#25943: Sheets and nests of small hyperchromatic cells with occasional peripheral palisading
and rosettes.

L#25944: Positivity for high-risk HPV by RNA in-situ hybridization is seen in a subset of
oropharyngeal and sinonasal cases.
R#28591: Diffuse strong nuclear INSM1 expression is illustrated in a small cell neuroendocrine
carcinoma.

L#28592: Paranuclear dot-like CAM5.2 expression is illustrated in a small cell neuroendocrine
carcinoma.
R#25927: Sheets of cells with scant cytoplasm, hyperchromatic molded nuclei, finely granular
chromatin, numerous mitoses and apoptotic bodies, and extensive crush artifact.

1079

Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
Definition
Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC) is a poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma
composed of cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, vesicular chromatin and prominent
nucleoli.
ICD-O coding
8013/3 Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2C23.1Y & XH0NL5 Other specified malignant neoplasms of larynx, glottis & Large cell
neuroendocrine carcinoma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Poorly differentiated neuroendocrine carcinoma, large cell type; Neuroendocrine
carcinoma, large cell type
Not recommended: grade III neuroendocrine carcinoma; large cell NET; high grade
neuroendocrine neoplasm
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
LCNEC occur with similar frequency in the larynx, oropharynx, and sinonasal tract
{ 22024350 ; 26735857 }. In the larynx, more than 80% of LCNEC arise in the supraglottis
{ 24596175 ; 20589486 }. Additional tumours are rarely reported in the hypopharynx, nasopharynx,
and salivary glands { 10824928 ; 15882834 ; 22718848 }.
Clinical features
Presenting symptoms correspond to anatomic site. In the larynx or oropharynx, patients often
demonstrate hoarseness or dysphagia. Sinonasal tract tumours frequently cause mass effect
including nasal obstruction, epistaxis, or anosmia. Regional or distant metastasis are identified at
presentation in 60-80% of laryngeal LCNEC { 20589486 ; 24596175 ; 31437725 ; 34401980 }.
Epidemiology
LCNEC are uncommon laryngeal neoplasms { 24596175 ; 26183520 ; 34401980 }. The exact
prevalence of LCNEC in the sinonasal tract is difficult to determine because of variable terminology,
but they are relatively rare. Across head and neck sites, LCNEC has a median age of 60-64 years
and a male-to-female ratio of 1.5-4:1 { 20589486 ; 22082601 ; 26735857 ; 31437725 ; 34401980 }.
Etiology
Risk factors for LCNEC are also dependent on site of origin. Laryngeal LCNEC are associated with
tobacco use in more than 90% of cases { 24596175 }. A subset of oropharyngeal and sinonasal
LCNEC harbour high-risk HPV, although most patients also have smoking history { 26735857 }.
Rare cases of nasopharyngeal LCNEC are EBV positive { 26064748 ; 29302900 }.
Pathogenesis
The pathogenesis of LCNEC in the larynx, oral cavity, and hypopharynx is not well-established. By
immunohistochemistry, tumours show frequent loss of RB1 and p53 overexpression, raising
parallels to a subset of lung LCNEC { 22082601 ; 22718848 ; 27392929 ; 33433884 }. In
oropharyngeal and sinonasal LCNEC where HPV is detected, RNA in-situ hybridization confirms
transcriptionally active virus, suggesting a driver role for viral oncoproteins { 26735857 }. In the
sinonasal tract, a significant proportion of LCNEC have recurrent IDH2 mutations similar to those
frequently seen in sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma (SNUC). They also share a global
methylation phenotype and pathway activation profile with SNUC, raising the possibility IDH2mutated LCNEC and SNUC represent a phenotypic spectrum of a single entity
{ 28493366 ; 30332658 ; 31186531 ; 34265800 }. While large cell morphology and positivity for
neuroendocrine markers is also seen in a subset of sinonasal tumours that show
immunohistochemical loss of SMARCA4, these tumours are now included within the spectrum of
SWI/SNF complex-deficient sinonasal carcinoma rather than LCNEC { 31934917 }.
Macroscopic appearance
LCNEC are generally large and highly infiltrative and form fleshy submucosal masses with frequent
ulceration and necrosis.
Histopathology

1080

LCNEC shows nested, organoid, or trabecular growth, with frequent peripheral palisading, rosette
formation, and central, comedo-pattern necrosis. The tumour cells are generally larger than the
diameter of 3 lymphocytes and have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. Nuclei have coarse to
speckled chromatin, often with a single prominent nucleolus. The mitotic rate is >10 mitoses per
2mm2 but is generally much higher.
Immunohistochemistry
LCNEC should be positive for cytokeratin, particularly low-molecular weight cocktails such as
CAM5.2. The expression of first-generation neuroendocrine differentiation markers (e.g.,
synaptophysin and chromogranin-A) may be variable with a dot-like staining pattern
{ 20589486 ; 22718848 ; 27529763 }. For this reason, the use of second-generation
neuroendocrine differentiation biomarkers, especially INSM1, has been shown to be useful in the
diagnostic workup of neoplasms with variable synaptophysin and/or focal/absent chromogranin-A
expression { 29438167 ; 31186531 ; 31857137 ; 32813226 ; 33786701 }. The use of non-specific
biomarkers alone (e.g., CD56, CD57, and neuron-specific enolase) is discouraged. TTF1
expression is present in a minority of cases { 22718848 }, and focal positivity for p63 or p40 can be
seen { 22718848 }. The role of Ki67 for grading is not well-established, but LCNEC have a Ki67
index of >20%, and often >50% { 22082601 ; 34401980 }. Abnormal p53 expression
{ 22082601 ; 27392929 } and global Rb loss { 27392929 ; 34401980 } can occur. p16 may be
overexpressed in LCNEC regardless of HR HPV status { 27392929 }.
Differential diagnosis
In all sites, LCNEC can show morphologic overlap with small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma,
basaloid and non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, and solid adenoid cystic carcinoma. In the
sinonasal tract, it should also be distinguished from olfactory neuroblastoma, NUT carcinoma, and
SWI/SNF complex deficient sinonasal carcinomas. LCNEC can be distinguished from SNUC,
but IDH2-mutant tumours in both categories may represent a phenotypic spectrum, which can be
recognized
by
mutant
IDH2
immunohistochemistry
{ 29683816 ; 30206411 ; 31186531 ; 33017591 }.
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration of LCNEC shows hypercellular smears composed of large cells with ample
cytoplasm, large nuclei, and vesicular chromatin with frequent large nucleoli { 30475447 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Routine high risk HPV RNA in situ hybridization testing is not recommended
{ 27392929 ; 29251996 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
High grade malignancy with nested, organoid, or trabecular growth of neuroendocrine-type cells
with abundant cytoplasm and round nuclei with prominent nucleoli >10 mitoses / 2
mm2 Immunohistochemical evidence of diffuse neuroendocrine differentiation Positive at least for
one cytokeratin
Desirable
Peripheral palisading, rosette formation, or comedo-pattern necrosis Ki67 proliferation index
>20% and often much higher
Staging
AJCC/UICC TNM staging for the appropriate anatomic site can be applied.
Prognosis and prediction
Laryngeal LCNEC has a poor prognosis with 5-year survival of 10-15%
{ 20589486 ; 22082601 ; 24596175 ; 26611246 }. Outcomes in sinonasal LCNEC are unclear
overall, although the subgroup with IDH2 mutations have similar prognosis to IDH2 mutated SNUC
with a 3-year survival of 53% { 31186531 }. HPV positivity is not associated with improved
prognosis in LCNEC { 26735857 }. The prognostic implications of EBV positive LCNEC is not yet
clear.

1081

L#25945: Large nests of tumour cells with peripheral palisading and comedonecrosis.
R#25946: Abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm with prominent nucleoli.

L#25947: Diffuse synaptophysin expression in a large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
R#25948: A subset of oropharyngeal and sinonasal tumours are positive for high-risk HPV by
RNA in-situ hybridization.

L#25950: Positivity for cytokeratin such as Cam 5.2 should be seen.
R#28593: INSM1 expression is illustrated in a large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma.

1082

L#25949: Sheets and nests of large cells with abundant cytoplasm, prominent nucleoli, and
comedonecrosis.
R#25930: Large nests of cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, vesicular chromatin with
prominent nucleoli, and comedonecrosis.

Merkel cell carcinoma
Definition
Merkel cell carcinoma is a high-grade primary cutaneous neuroendocrine carcinoma.
ICD-O coding
8247/3 Merkel cell carcinoma
ICD-11 coding
2C34 & XH81N8 Cutaneous neuroendocrine carcinoma & Merkel cell carcinoma
Related terminology
Not recommended: Primary neuroendocrine carcinoma of skin
Subtype(s)
none
Localization
Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) most commonly arises on sun-exposed sites. A review of 14,414
MCC patients demonstrated that 43% of cases developed on the head/neck { 27198511 }.
Clinical features
MCC presents as a rapidly-growing often violaceous papule or nodule. The broad clinical
differential diagnosis includes basal cell and squamous cell carcinoma, lymphoma, atypical
fibroxanthoma and melanoma. The majority (65%) of patients present with disease localized to the
skin, 26% with regional lymph node metastases, and 8% with distant metastases { 27198511 }.
Epidemiology
MCC most commonly affects older, white, non-Hispanic men. A population-based survey of 11,028
cases from 21 countries showed highest rates in Australia, New Zealand and United States
{ 29533867 }. MCC incidence per million has increased significantly in the United States from 1.5
cases (1986) to 4.4 cases (2001) to 7.9 cases in 2011 { 15611998 ; 26215243 ; 29102486 }.
Etiology
Risk factors for MCC development include male sex, old age, chronic sun exposure, and
immunosuppression. Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) infection and UV-induced mutagenesis
reflect two distinct etiologies of MCC. Asymptomatic MCPyV infection occurs early in life, with
seropositivity rates of 60-70% { 28893943 }.
Pathogenesis
Clonally integrated McPyV expresses the viral oncoproteins large T-antigen (LT) and small Tantigen { 25681708 ; 28802497 ; 29072302 ; 30417062 ;} and is responsible for 60-80% of cases
{ 18202256 ; 27815175 }. Truncated LT binds and inactivates retinoblastoma (Rb) protein allowing
cell cycle progression. Virus-negative MCC possesses a significantly higher mutational burden than
MCPyV-positive
MCC,
predominantly
UV-signature
mutations
affecting TP53, RB1,

1083

and NOTCH family
members
{ 26215243 ; 26238782 ; 26655088 ; 26627015 ; 31399473 }.
MCPyV-positive MCCs predominate in high ambient UV areas, whereas MCPyV-negative MCCs
predominate in low exposure areas { 30417062 }.
Macroscopic appearance
none
Histopathology
Scanning magnification shows a densely cellular dermal malignancy with frequent extension into
the subcutis, infiltrative or pushing borders, and variably dense lymphohistiocytic inflammatory
infiltrate. Intraepidermal MCC is rare { 27198511 ; 28802497 }. Tumour cells with scant cytoplasm,
round to oval nuclei, and finely granular chromatin are arranged as sheets, cords and trabeculae.
Mitotic figures and apoptotic bodies are common. Tumour cells are usually intermediate in size, but
large cell and small cell morphologies may occur { 28802497 ; 29556962 }. MCC may show
divergent differentiation, most commonly glandular { 8751563 ; 23530585 ; 25699980 },
squamous
{ 9810922 ; 19609205 ; 19702685 ; 21453956 ; 23664542 ; 23530585 ; 26541273 }, and less
commonly sarcomatoid or neuroblastic { 28802497 }. Distinct foci of invasive SCC or SCCIS, found
in up to 15%, may represent collision phenomenon rather than transdifferentiation { 30417062 }.
Immunohistochemistry
Immunohistochemical studies are essential to confirm the diagnosis of MCC. The most specific
marker is cytokeratin 20 (CK20) which highlights most MCCs (around 88%)
{ 9042291 ; 10027519 ; 11175640 ; 11127923 ; 16625069 ; 30067951 ; 31201352 }
with
characteristic perinuclear dots. MCCs show neuroendocrine differentiation, including
synaptophysin (92%), chromogranin-A (84%), and CD56 (88%). Neurofilament (NF) is expressed
in ~80% of MCCs. Immunohistochemical detection of MCPyV LT (CM2B4) is sensitive (88%) and
specific (94%) { 27815175 }. A subset of MCPyV-negative MCC demonstrate divergent
immunophenotypic findings (TTF1 positivity or negativity for NF and/or CK20)
{ 30067951 ; 31201352 }.CK20-negative MCCs may be diagnosed by NF-positivity and TTF-1
negativity; MCPyV staining has limited sensitivity for CK20-negative tumours { 25394777 }. A metaanalysis identified frequent PAX5 (around 90%), terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT)
(around 65%), and CD117 (63%) in MCC { 28693804 }. SATB2 is also expressed in MCCs
{ 30349028 ; 31233624 }. Expression of B-cell markers is associated with MCPyV { 32134459 }.
These are all important pitfalls since detection of these biomarkers and MCPyV can assist the
distinction of MCCs from other neuroendocrine carcinomas { 30349028 }.
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration typically reveals a cellular proliferation of malignant cells with crush artifact.
The tumor cells contain scant cytoplasm, variable nuclear size and shape, finely granular
chromatin, indistinct nucleoli, and frequent nuclear moulding { 24678011 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Molecular studies are not typically used in diagnosing MCC, although viral gene expression and
UV-signature mutation detection have enabled distinction from histologic mimickers
{ 30986802 ; 32445471 }. Immunohistochemical detection of MCPyV LT (CM2B4) is sensitive
(88%) and specific (94%) { 27815175 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
- Rapidly growing skin lesion
- Highly cellular dermal malignancy composed of cells with round to oval nuclei, and finely granular
chromatin
- Immunohistochemistry - cytokeratin (e.g. CK20) positive with dot-like perinuclear staining and
markers of neuroendocrine differentiation
Desirable:
Expression of B-cell markers and/or MCPyV may be helpful in the distinction from other NECs.
Staging
The 8th edition of the AJCC TNM system forms the basis for MCC staging { 27198511 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Five-year overall survival (OS) rates are ~51% for those with localized disease, 35% for patients
with regional lymph node metastases, and 14% when distant metastases are present. In nodenegative patients, T-stage robustly predicts 5-year OS: 55.8% (pT1); 41.1% (pT2/pT3) and 31.8%

1084

(pT4) { 27198511 }, though recent data suggests survival rates are substantially higher
{ 32103415 }. Clinically evident lymph node metastases have worse prognosis than clinically occult
lymph node involvement { 27198511 }, and the pattern and extent of SLN involvement are
additional prognostic factors { 27166398 ; 31094923 }. Patients with clinically evident regional
lymph node disease without a known primary MCC are distinguished from patients with a known
primary
MCC
(Stage
IIIA
versus
IIIB)
and
have
a
better
prognosis
{ 22030017 ; 23182060 ; 23835211 }.
Additional prognostic features of the primary tumour include tumour thickness, pattern of growth,
lymphovascular invasion, and tumour infiltrating lymphocytes, all of which are independently
associated with patient survival { 18798233 ; 21422430 ; 22467679 ; 27166398 }. The pattern
of SLN involvement and the extent of disease burden in the SLN are additional prognostic factors
in MCC { 27166398 ; 31094923 }.
MCPyV-positive MCCs have improved survival compared to MCPyV-negative MCCs
{ 19535775 ; 21642382 ; 22261808 ; 22795182 ; 27815175 ; 28763479 }, although controversial
{ 21422430 ; 22882157 }, and MCPyV status does not predict survival independently of stage
{ 27815175 }.
p63
expression
correlates
with
shorter
survival
in MCC
{ 17599745 ; 21765392 ; 22795182 ; 24746200 }, but not independently of stage { 24225752 }.
Ki67 labeling index ≥55% { 32696301 } and TERT methylation (mhTERT) { 33909215 } were
associated with bad prognosis.
Integrity of the host immune system is both a risk factor and prognostic factor in MCC
{ 10609948 ; 11853800 ; 18280333 ; 22120659 }. Immunocompromised patients have a worse
survival compared to immunocompetent patients { 23190897 }. Increased expression of immune
related genes such as CD8A and granzyme genes and higher levels of tumor-infiltrative CD8+ Tcells independently correlate with longer survival { 21422430 ; 22467679 ; 27166398 }. Further,
in MCPyV-positive MCCs, decreased expression of immune markers including granzyme and
IDO1 correlated with shorter survival, whereas in MCPyV-negative MCCs, expression of several
genes including UBE2C correlated with shorter survival { 33547200 }.

L#24034: Histopathology of Merkel cell carcinoma. Tumor cells arranged as syncytia and
trabeculae with large, irregular nuclei and slight cytoplasm. The nuclei have finely granular
chromatin, and mitotic figures are numerous.
R#24035: Divergent differentiation may be seen in Merkel cell carcinoma, and squamous cell
carcinoma is the most common.

R#24036: Merkel cell polyomavirus T-antigen shows nuclear positivity.
L#24037: Cytokeratin 20 stains Merkel cell carcinoma with a characteristic perinuclear dot-like
positivity.
1085

Paraganglion tumours

Head and neck paraganglioma
Definition
Head and neck paragangliomas are well differentiated non-epithelial neoplasms derived from
paraganglion cells of the autonomic nervous system.
ICD-O coding
8692/3 Head and neck paraganglioma
ICD-11 coding
2D12.Y & XH5LK3 Other specified malignant neoplasms of other endocrine glands or related
structures & Parasympathetic paraganglioma
Related terminology
Acceptable: Paraganglioma; SDH-deficient paraganglioma
Not recommended: glomus tumour; carotid body tumour; glomus caroticum; glomus jugulare;
glomus tympanicum; chemodectoma
Subtype(s)
Composite paraganglioma
Localization
Tumours are typically associated with the vagus and glossopharyngeal nerves. Although
“parasympathetic” and “head and neck” are generally considered synonyms, head and neck lesions
may include sympathetic paragangliomas associated with the cervical sympathetic chains
{ 11400237 ; 16782577 ; 20310044 ; 24652566 ; 33100776 } or arise from paraganglia with mixed
sympathetic and parasympathetic innervation { 11889402 }.
Tumours develop around the carotid body (40-60%), middle ear (jugulotympanic; 30%), vagal nerve
trunk in or near the nodose ganglion (10%), sympathetic chain (4%), and rarely in larynx, thyroid
gland, parathyroid gland, mandible, parotid gland, nasopharynx and paranasal sinuses, sella
turcica, orbit, and clivus { 9236830 ; 20237987 ; 28299533 ; 30217041 }. Bilateral/multifocal
paragangliomas account for 10-40% { 28321772 ; 29299512 }.
Clinical features
Carotid body paragangliomas present as asymptomatic mass lesions. Middle ear paragangliomas
present with pulsatile tinnitus, hearing abnormalities, or a sensation of aural fullness. Large or
infiltrative tumours and most vagal paragangliomas are associated with cranial nerve symptoms
{ 28321772 }. Horner’s syndrome and first bite syndrome are associated with sympathetic
paragangliomas. Only about 4% of head and neck paragangliomas are biochemically functional,
producing predominantly dopamine and/or 3-methoxytyramine. Occasionally norepinephrine or
normetanephrine
is
elevated
{ 17400487 ; 19328652 ; 22431860 ; 23526121 ; 24119888 ; 31168271 },
but
elevated
metanephrine/epinephrine
levels
usually
indicate
a
concurrent
sympathetic
paraganglioma/pheochromocytoma { 29299512 }. Increasingly, tumours are discovered by
screening families with known genetic predisposition syndromes (see Chapter 14.0.3.1 Table 1).
These patients require lifelong surveillance and management tailored to specific genotypes.
Paragangliomas are detected with the highest sensitivity by PET/CT { 29204718 }; 68GaDOTATATE PET/CT is most commonly available in North America and other methods utilize 68GaDOTATOC PET/CT or 68Ga-DOTANOC. This technique has the added advantage of predicting
efficacy of somatostatin-based therapies including medical therapy { 25554089 } and Peptide
Receptor Radionuclide Therapy (PRRT). Other techniques (131I-labelled meta-iodobenzylguanidine
[MIBG]; somatostatin receptor scintigraphy with 111In-pentreotide) have significant limitations,
although
useful
if
other
studies
are
unavailable
{ 14602858 ; 18632829 ; 19190077 ; 22399235 }. 18FDG-PET/CT is not very sensitive for sporadic
disease but is valuable in patients with SDHB-related disease { 25873086 }.
Epidemiology
Paragangliomas represent less than 0.5% of head and neck tumours with an estimated annual
incidence of 1 in 30,000–100,000 { 29299512 }. They can occur from childhood to old age, with a
mean age at presentation in the 6th decade { 29299512 }. In contrast to sympathetic counterparts,
there is a definite female predominance (female:male 1.7:1) { 11701678 ; 19223516 }. Paediatric
1086

cases are rare, reported in the teenage years, with 9-23% of paragangliomas affecting the head
and neck in this population { 32432211 ; 32491270 }.
Etiology
Paragangliomas have a hereditary predisposition in at least 40% of patients, approaching 85% of
patients of all ages tested in some series { 29299512 }. Consequently, there is a high probability of
synchronous or metachronous multicentric paragangliomas, which may be confined to the head
and neck or occasionally include sympathetic paragangliomas and phaeochromocytomas
{ 15774781 ; 29299512 }.
Pathogenesis
Genetic factors
The most common germline mutation in patients with head and neck paragangliomas is
in SDHD (47%), followed by SDHB (30%), and SDHC (16%) { 29299512 }. SDHD mutation is
associated predominantly with single or multifocal tumours in the head and neck,
whereas SDHB mutation is more often associated with thoracoabdominal tumours { 22584701 }.
Paragangliomas in the head and neck are rarely associated with mutations of VHL, RET,
or NF1 { 22584701 }. A rare, syndromic but non-hereditary association is Carney triad, where most
cases show down-regulation of SDH through site-specific hyper-methylation of the SDHC gene
{ 24859990 ; 25540324 }.
Risk factors
For carotid body paragangliomas in particular, tumour incidence is increased and the female
predominance in some populations living at high altitudes, probably reflecting effects of hypoxia as
a phenotypic modifier in patients with SDHD { 12811540 ; 29681642 } or SDHB { 20592014 }
mutations. An association with congenital cyanotic heart disease has also been reported
{ 26961564 }. While the SDHD mutation can be transmitted by either a male or female carrier,
tumours usually occur only after paternal transmission, causing skipped generations { 26067997 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Head and neck paragangliomas are typically encapsulated, pink to tan, firm nodules. There may
be areas of haemorrhage, especially after pre-operative embolization, the latter frequently
employed in head and neck sites.
Histopathology
Tumours exhibit a variably present zellballen architecture composed of chief cells with abundant
pale eosinophilic cytoplasm with slightly to moderately atypical nuclei, and peripherally located
sustentacular cells, usually difficult to detect in H&E sections. The stroma is highly vascular. Mitotic
figures are usually rare.
Composite paraganglioma, composed of paraganglioma admixed with ganglioneuroma, is at most
exceptionally rare in the head and neck.
Chief cells in head and neck paragangliomas usually show nuclear staining for the transcription
factors insulinoma associated protein-1 (INSM1) and GATA3{ 29438167 ; 32813226 }, while
cytoplasmic functional markers of neuroendocrine differentiation including synaptophysin, and
chromogranin A are highly variable. The catecholamine-synthesizing enzymes tyrosine
hydroxylase and dopamine β-hydroxylase are expressed in approximately 30% and 10%,
respectively, of head and neck paragangliomas { 25677368 ; 34743284 }. A recent study showed
choline acetyltransferase (an enzyme involved in acetylcholine synthesis) expression virtually in all
head and neck paragangliomas even in the absence of expression for catecholamine-synthesizing
enzymes { 34743284 }. However, the specificity of this biomarker remains to be validated in
additional studies. Chief cells are usually nonreactive for keratins { 33098891 }. Sustentacular cells
express S100 protein, SOX10, and uncommonly GFAP { 32548761 }. Loss of reactivity for SDHB
is associated with mutations of SDHA, SDHB, SDHC, SDHD, and SDHAF2 { 19576851 }. Alphainhibin is expressed in pseudohypoxic tumours (including SDHx-related and VHL-related types)
{ 33826547 }
and
CAIX
is
expressed
strongly
in
VHL-associated
tumours
{ 23257898 ; 31383958 ; 33826547 }. Paragangliomas express somatostatin receptors (SSTR)
{ 14602858 }, and SDH-deficient paragangliomas have been reported to demonstrate stronger
staining for SSTR2A and SSTR3 { 19936639 }.

1087

Differential diagnosis
Numerous primary and metastatic tumours comprise the differential diagnosis in head and neck
regions, including epithelial neuroendocrine neoplasms of the larynx { 28247224 }, parathyroids
{ 27406876 }, pituitary { 30217041 }, middle ear and temporal bone, and thyroid gland (medullary
thyroid carcinoma) { 28493102 }. Olfactory neuroblastoma, Merkel cell carcinoma, and
rhabdomyosarcoma may also be considerations.
Negativity for keratins distinguishes paragangliomas from most but not all epithelial neuroendocrine
neoplasms { 33098891 }. While GATA3 is a useful biomarker of paragangliomas, it is also
expressed
in
normal
and
neoplastic
parathyroid
and
pituitary
{ 24145643 ; 27529763 ; 30217041 ; 32193825 ; 32303954 }. Paragangliomas can also express
peptide hormones including calcitonin { 27529763 ; 28493102 ; 29744727 }. While medullary
thyroid carcinomas can also focally stain for tyrosine hydroxylase, they are typically negative for
GATA3 and positive for monoclonal CEA and TTF1 { 28493102 ; 29744727 }. Sustentacular cells
identified by S100 or SOX10 { 32548761 } are not specific for paraganglioma
{ 27529763 ; 32632839 }.
Cytology
Fine needle aspiration biopsy is generally not advocated due to potential complications, but if
inadvertently sampled, smears usually contain blood and isolated tumour cells or groups forming
rosettes. The cells are often large, polygonal, ovoid or elongated. The abundant granular/dense
cytoplasm appears eosinophilic on H&E and pale on PAP stain. The nuclei are centrally or
eccentrically located and show variation in size and shape. Intra-nuclear cytoplasmic inclusions
may be seen. Pleomorphic nuclei, prominent nucleoli, clumped chromatin, intra-nuclear inclusions,
and granular cytoplasm may cause misdiagnosis. Cell block material can be used for
immunohistochemistry to aid in diagnosis { 10945911 ; 20715670 ; 31168271 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Nested/zellballen pattern; round, hyperchromatic nuclei with stippled chromatin Confirmation of
keratin-negativity and positivity for neuroendocrine differentiation markers
Desirable:
GATA3 expression Positivity for one of the catecholamine-synthesizing enzymes (e.g., tyrosine
hydroxylase, dopamine β-hydroxylase) SDHB immunohistochemistry or relevant genetic
evaluation to exclude hereditary risk (as available)
Staging
No TNM system as published by AJCC or UICC for head and neck paragangliomas, but ICCR and
other expert-initiated reporting guidelines have been introduced { 24476517 ; 32407815 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Head and neck paragangliomas have low metastatic potential, with only a 4-6% risk of metastasis
for those in the carotid body and 2% in the middle ear { 28321772 }. Although few or no metastases
are reported in paediatric patients short term { 32432211 }, they can occur after many years,
especially in patients harbouring germline SDHB mutations { 24169168 ; 31194233 }. The main
putative risk factor for metastasis is hereditary mutation of SDHB { 24169168 ; 31194233 }.
However locally invasive tumours can have a very aggressive course if unresectable { 32548761 }.
Familial cases require lifelong surveillance to evaluate for multicentric disease.

R#27149: This neck mass was discovered on follow up scanning in a patient with a

years earlier. The carotid body lesion was 4 - 8 mm in diameter and protruded from the lumen of
a vein. In contrast to the initial tumour which for the most part highly cellular and consisted of
small cells with infiltrative and solid growth patterns and extremely rare sustentacular cells, this
tumour consists of relatively larger cells with relatively well-formed nests and readily identified
sustentacular cells. The tumour was initially suspected of being a lymph node metastasis. Since
no lymph node was present, it was considered to be a local recurrence, most likely from
peripheral areas of the original neoplasm. Ki67 proliferation index was comparable in solid areas
of the initial tumour.

17. Genetic tumour syndromes
Introduction
Naevoid basal cell carcinoma (Gorlin) syndrome
Neurofibromatosis (NF1)
Familial Adenomatous Polyposis with Gardner syndrome
Brooke-Spiegler and related syndromes
Cowden syndrome
Familial paraganglioma syndromes
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2
Hyperparathyroidism jaw tumour syndrome
Li Fraumeni syndrome
Fanconi anaemia
Dyskeratosis congenita
Ataxia telangiectasia
Bloom syndrome
Von-Hippel Lindau
Tuberous sclerosis syndrome

Introduction
It has been recognized that hereditary factors can contribute to the development of tumours
involving the head and neck for over 100 years. Clinically recognizable tumour-predisposing
syndromes have been more widely recognized during the last decades due to the rapidly increasing
use of sequencing of DNA in both diagnostic pathology and clinical genetics. What appears to be
a sporadic tumour is becoming less often so with more intense investigation and more often new
syndromes and tumours associated with these syndromes are being reported.
The 5th edition of the WHO classification of the Head and Neck Tumours has a newly created
section dedicated to heritable syndromes with tumors in the head and neck region (listed in the
table #28600). This initiative was needed to better understand the tumours, diseases, and
associated syndromes, as well as establish recommendations for monitoring and treating these
patients.
As an example, middle ear endolymphatic sac tumour (ELST) is a well-recognized tumour
associated with von Hippel Lindau syndrome (VHLS) in over a third of the cases. Data from the
“International ELST Registry “ { 25867206 } show a prevalence of ELST of 3.6% in
VHL. VHL germline mutations were present in almost 40% of ELSTs, with an apparent sporadic
presentation. These findings support genetic testing in all patients with ELSTs { 20351605 }.
Gorlin syndrome/Naevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome is a cancer predisposition syndrome
associated with basal cell carcinomas, odontogenic keratocysts and medulloblastomas, related to
a constitutional pathogenic variation (PV) of a gene in the Sonic Hedgehog pathway
(PTCH1 or SUFU) { 8326488 ; 31725470 }. Odontogenic keratocyst screening should begin at age
2 with annual orthopantogram beginning around age 8 for PTCH1 PV carriers only
{ 28620006 ; 30826393 }.
Brooke-Spiegler syndrome or familial cylindromatosis or CYLD cutaneous syndrome is an inherited
autosomal dominant disease characterized by multiple spiradenomas, cylindromas,
spiradenocylindromas and trichoepitheliomas and salivary gland tumours. Salivary gland
involvement by basal cell adenoma, membranous-type { 33526221 } is characteristic of this
syndrome. This tumour involves primarily the parotid gland. These tumours are usually
bilateral/multicentric and well-circumscribed.
Genodermatosis affecting skin and mucosa of the head and neck have been well characterized
{ 26975628 }. PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome caused by mutation of the phosphatase and
tensin homolog (PTEN) gene, includes Cowden syndrome as the principal PTEN-related disorder.
This syndrome is characterized by multiple neoplasms and hamartomas, mucosal papillomatosis,
and skin lesions, as trichilemmomas. Multiple trichilemmomas are clinically significant sign of CS.
The most distinctive and peculiar facial features of CS consist of multiple small and keratotic
papules concentrated around the orifices. Early recognition of these skin lesions may help with
diagnosing an underlying malignancy and early cancer screening { 26975628 }.

1095

Although cancers of the head and neck region are uncommon, the diagnosis of multicentric head
and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC) particularly, occurring in the larynx, the pharynx and
the oral cavity can be seen in Li-Fraumeni syndrome { 27328919 ; 11120478 }. While this
syndrome has many tumour associations, this chapter is focused on head and neck tumours.
The most common solid cancer in Fanconi Anemia (FA) patients is also head and neck squamous
cell carcinoma (HNSCC). Compared to the general population, risk of HNSCC in FA is increased
by 500- to 800-fold and the diagnosis of HNSCC often precedes the diagnosis of FA { 22504776 }.
FA-HNSCC frequently occur as multifocal disease, preceded by oral potentially malignant disorders
presenting as lichenoid keratosis or leukoplakia.
Patients with a familial adenomatous polyposis, which is characterized by over 100 adenomatous
colorectal polyps, may present also multiple extracolonic manifestations, as Gardner syndrome
variant. Desmoid tumors may arise in soft tissue of the head and neck, but may also originate in
the maxillary sinus, nasopharynx, and oral cavity { 27387679 }. Other features involving the head
and neck are osteomas { 19157925 ; 22010067 } and supernumerary teeth.
Manifestations of tuberous sclerosis in the head and neck are predominantly cutaneous and oral.
Angiofibroma has a predilection for the face typically arising in the centrofacial cheek areas,
nasolabial folds and chin { 30246432 }. Oral manifestations are frequent and specifically include
angiofibromas in the anterior gingiva, lips, tongue, and palate and dental enamel pits loss on the
incisal border and tooth wear are also observed { 24310804 ; 30246432 }.
Neurofibromatosis type 1 is recognized in patients with orbitofacial neurofibromas and numerous
neurofibromas at other sites. This syndrome is now described in the head and neck book focusing
on the head and neck pathology findings. Other important syndromes with involvement of the head
and neck, as familial paragangliomas, with jaw tumours (hyperparathyroidism-jaw tumor
syndrome), with oral mucosa leukoplakia (dyskeratosis congenita), and oropharyngeal carcinoma
(Bloom syndrome) are described in detail.
Pathologists have a very important role in recognizing tumours of the head and neck tumours
associated with the heritable syndromes addressed in this section. Pathologists must be aware of
the morphologic features associated with distinct syndromes that should prompt consideration of
germline screening. The routine use of NGS will undoubtedly continue to widen the spectrum of
known predisposing genetic variants among patients with tumours of the head and neck. The
identification of syndromes has made possible identification of at-risk individuals in affected
families, who can then be offered genetic counseling. Information on underlying genetic variants
can also have an important impact on the choice of therapy and surveillance guidelines. Virtually
all of these disorders have now been characterized at the molecular level; patients with these
disorders may also require distinct forms of therapy and personalized follow-up.
