Naevoid basal cell carcinoma (Gorlin) syndrome
Definition
Naevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome (NBCCS) is a complex multi-organ syndrome caused by
germline sequence variants in genes involved in the hedgehog signalling pathway (most
commonly PTCH1). The most common head and neck pathology finding is
odontogenic keratocyst (OKC).
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering:
#109400
ICD-11 coding
LD2D.4 Gorlin syndrome
Related terminology
Acceptable: Gorlin syndrome; Gorlin–Goltz syndrome,
Not recommended: Basal cell naevus syndrome (BCNS); Fifth phacomatosis
Subtype(s)
None.
Localization
Skin - BCCs, sebaceous cysts; Jaw - odontogenic keratocyst (OKC); Bone - congenital anomalies;
Brain - macrocephaly, falx calcification, medulloblastoma; as well as eyelid, cleft lip/palate and
ovarian fibromas.

1099

Clinical features
The most frequent manifestations of NBCCS are multiple basal cell carcinomas, as well
as odontogenic keratocyst (OKC) of the jaw. In one study, basal cell carcinomas and odontogenic
keratocyst were found together in > 90% of affected individuals by 40 years of age { 8326488 }.
Other major diagnostic criteria include calcification of the falx cerebri, palmar and plantar pits, and
bifid or fused ribs { 9096761 ; 8042673 } (Table). Clinical features manifest at different times of
life. Macrocephaly and rib anomalies can be detected at birth, while medulloblastoma typically
develops within the first 3 years of life. OKCs are not usually evident before 8 years of age, while
basal cell carcinomas are usually found 10 years later { 25131638 }. In one series of 50 children
with OKCs, 36% were associated with NBCCS and over half of those had no known family history;
as such genetic testing is often required to establish the diagnosis { 30826393 }. Radiation
exposure induces multiple basal cell carcinomas of the skin as well as various other tumour types
(e.g. meningioma) { 9010431 ; 33125361 }. Rare examples of odontogenic myxoma have also
been reported { 24163866 }.
Epidemiology
The syndromic prevalence has been estimated between 1:31,000 and 1:256,000 { 27054559 }. The
overall
prevalence
ofodontogenic
keratocyst
(OKC) in
NBCCS
is
66-86%
{ 8326488 ; 8042673 ; 9096761 ; 22246785 } but OKCs have an age-dependent onset and by the
age of 40, the prevalence is reported to be 91% { 8326488 }. Cysts commonly arise at a relatively
early age with mean age of onset estimated at 15.5 years { 8042673 } but they may occur much
later in life { 8326488 }.
Some differences have been noted between sporadic and syndromic OKCs, such as a higher
frequency of epithelial rests/islands and microcysts in the cyst wall { 2441019 }. However, these
can also occur in non-syndromic cysts and do not represent diagnostic criteria for syndromic cysts.
Etiology
NBCCS results from inactivating germline sequence variants in PTCH1 on 9q22
{ 8681379 ; 8658145 }, its paralog PTCH2 on 1p34 in rare cases { 18285427 ; 23479190 }, or
the SUFU gene on 10q24 { 25403219 }. However, patients with germline SUFU mutation do not
develop odontogenic keratocyst (OKC), with most OKCs associated with PTCH1 inactivation
instead { 29356994 ; 31725470 }.
Numerous PTCH1 germline
pathogenetic
sequence
variants
have
been
reported
{ 25131638 ; 16419085 }, although variants were historically detected in only 50-60% of cases
{ 16088933 }. That detection rate has increased significantly (up to 93%) due to recent testing
advances { 25131638 }. The mostly truncating mutations are distributed over the
entire PTCH1 coding region, with no mutational hotspots { 8981943 }. Missense mutations cluster
in a highly conserved region (the sterol-sensing domain), and particularly in transmembrane
domain 4. SUFU pathogenetic variants are only detected in 5-6% of tested probands, and are
mostly truncating { 25403219 }. It has been estimated that a high proportion (14–81%) of cases are
the result of new pathogenetic variants { 8326488 ; 3547011 ; 8042673 ; 9415689 }.
Pathogenesis
The PTCH1 gene encodes a 12-transmembrane protein expressed on many progenitor cell types.
It functions as a receptor for members of the secreted hedgehog protein family of signalling
molecules (SHH, IHH, and DHH) { 8906794 ; 8906787 }. PTCH1 controls another transmembrane
protein, SMO { 8906787 ; 9278137 }. In the absence of ligand, PTCH1 inhibits the activity of SMO
{ 8906787 ; 9278137 }. Hedgehog signalling takes place in the primary cilium { 25172843 }.
Binding of hedgehog proteins to PTCH1 can relieve its inhibition of SMO, allowing its translocation
to the tip of the primary cilium, which results in the activation and translocation of GLI transcription
factors into the cell nucleus and transcription of a set of specific target genes controlling the survival,
differentiation, and proliferation of progenitor cells. SUFU has been found to directly interact with
GLI proteins and is a negative regulator of hedgehog signaling { 10564661 }. The inactivation
of PTCH or SUFU genes lead to pathological activation of the SHH signaling pathway.
Macroscopic appearance
Same features as sporadic counterparts.
Histopathology
All subtypes of basal cell carcinoma including the pigmented variant can be seen. NBCCSassociated OKCs resemble sporadic counterparts.
Cytology
Not applicable.

1100

Diagnostic molecular pathology
A comprehensive mutational analysis of the PTCH1 and SUFU genes by DNA sequencing and
search for deletions/duplications by appropriate methods (e.g. MLPA, targeted array-based
analysis) is able to identify most NBCCS-related germline pathogenetic variants { 20301330 }. To
detect more complex alterations, additional analysis at the transcript level is occasionally
necessary. Despite testing advances, no PTCH1 or SUFU pathogenetic variant is identified in
15%-27% of index cases { 28596197 }.
Genetic counselling should be offered to NBCCS patients and their families. The detection of a
germline condition is important in tumour treatment planning, e.g., avoiding radiation therapy
{ 19276247 ; 29186568 ; 33125361 }
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Major and minor diagnostic criteria are depicted in the Table. The diagnosis of NBCCS requires
two major and one minor diagnostic criterion or one major and three minor diagnostic criteria
{ 8326488 ; 20301330 }.
In cases with inconclusive clinical presentation, identification of a heterozygous germline
pathogenic variant of PTCH1 or SUFU is required.
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
The life expectancy of NBCCS patients at 73.4 years is only slightly reduced from unaffected
populations when compared with other genetic tumour syndromes; however, there is considerable
impact on quality of life { 22362873 }. Surveillance guidelines are well-established { 28620006 }.
For SUFU pathogenetic variant carriers, these include a recommendation for repeated brain MRI
screening for medulloblastoma development within the first five years of life { 28620006 }.

#28315: Naevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome, multiple odontogenic keratocysts, Panoramic
radiograph of a 13 year old child showing four large odontogenic keratocysts, one in each jaw
quadrant posteriorly, displacing the developing third molars and upper second molars.

1101

#28606, 28608: Odontogenic keratocyst (OKC)
L: Cyst wall lined by uniform epithelium stratified squamous type without rete ridges but with
hyperchromatic palisaded basal cell nuclei and characteristic corrugated surface parakeratin.
R:The epithelial cyst lining displays surface parakeratosis, cuboidal to columnar basal cells with
hyperchromatic nuclei and focal reverse nuclear polarity.

Neurofibromatosis (NF1)
Definition
Neurofibromatosis type 1 is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a pathogenic germline
variant of the NF1 (neurofibromin) gene.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering 162200 Neurofibromatosis type 1; NF1
ICD-11 coding
LD2D.10 Neurofibromatosis type 1
Related terminology
Not recommended: Von Recklinghausen disease; peripheral neurofibromatosis
Subtype(s)
Mosaic neurofibromatosis type 1, including segmental neurofibromatosis type 1; Spinal
neurofibromatosis; Neurofibromatosis-Noonan syndrome; 17q11.2 microdeletion syndrome
Localization
Neurofibromatosis type 1 affects many different cell types and tissues in the body, predominantly
localizing to central and peripheral nervous system sites.
Clinical features
Multiple café-au-lait macules are usually present at birth, and increase in number during the first
two years of life. Skinfold freckling in the axillary, inguinal, and submammary regions occurs in
>80% of adults with NF1. Lisch nodules and skinfold freckling are usually detectable before puberty.
Cutaneous neurofibromas typically develop during puberty and are identified in >85% of adults
{ 28230061 }, In contrast, plexiform neurofibromas are considered congenital, developing in 30–
50% of children with NF1 { 28230061 }. Individual plexiform neurofibromas have variable growth
rates, which may remain stable over time, but exhibit the highest growth potential during infancy
and childhood. A subset of plexiform neurofibromas transform into atypical neurofibroma/ANNUBP
(atypical neurofibromatous neoplasms of uncertain biological potential), which are associated with
an increased risk to progress to high-grade malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST)
{ 28551330 ; 21987445 }. Specific bone abnormalities include severe scoliosis, sphenoid wing
dysplasia, non-ossifying fibromas, and congenital tibial bowing. Progressive tibial bowing can result
in pathological fracture, with the development of pseudarthrosis { 18248783 }. Other neoplasms
observed at increased frequency are optic pathway glioma/pilocytic astrocytoma (OPG) and other
gliomas { 32300062 }, juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia, rhabdomyosarcoma, glomus tumours of

1102

the digits, gastrointestinal stromal tumours, pheochromocytoma, breast cancer in females, and
duodenal neuroendocrine tumours (somatostatinomas) { 19410195 }.
Orbitofacial neurofibromas
Orbitofacial neurofibromatosis type 1 (OFNF) has been historically studied as a clinical variant of
NF1 { 22193879 }. Orbitofacial NFs is often characterized by progressive, large disfiguring tumours
involving orbit and periorbital tissues and associated with remodeling of adjacent skull bones.
Recurrence after excision is relatively high, but tumour growth may stabilize as the individual ages.
Epidemiology
NF1 has a birth incidence of approximately 1 per 3000 { 25354145 }.
Etiology
NF1 is caused by heterozygous pathogenic variants in the NF1 gene, encoding neurofibromin
{ 2134734 ; 2114220 ; 1694727 }. Half of the individuals with NF1 have unaffected parents and
represent de novo mutations.
Pathogenesis
Neurofibromin primarily functions as a GTPase activating protein (GAP) for the RAS protooncogene { 8499945 }. The EVH1-domain of SPRED1 (the protein implicated in Legius syndrome)
binds to neurofibromin on both sides of the GAP-related domain and recruits neurofibromin to the
membrane where it can accelerate RAS inactivation { 22751498 ; 26635368 ; 27313208 }.
Most NF1 clinical features, including café-au-lait macules, bony abnormalities, and benign tumours
result from complete loss of neurofibromin function (biallelic NF1 inactivation), leading to increased
RAS and RAS effector (MEK/ERK or AKT/mTOR) signaling. Atypical neurofibromas/ANNUBP
harbour additional genetic alterations, including loss of CDKN2A/CDKN2B and SMARCA2 regions
{ 21987445 ; 30722027 ; 18984156 }. MPNSTs frequently show additional aberrations of genes
encoding components of the PRC2 complex, and to a lesser extent, TP53 genes
{ 25119042 ; 25240281 ; 25305755 }. In both neurofibromas and OPGs, murine studies have
revealed that non-neoplastic (stromal) cells with a heterozygous NF1 mutation, including mast
cells, macrophages, neurons, T cells, and microglia, are critical for tumourigenesis and continued
growth.
Novel NF1 mutations associated with orbitofacial neurofibromatosis have been detected in some
Chinese families, as well as more severe ocular phenotypes in successive generations (e.g.
anticipation) { 31533651 }. Differential promoter methylation and expression of the HOX family
genes has also been reported in orbitofacial neurofibroma developing in NF1 patients when
compared to neurofibromas developing at other sites { 32366326 }.
Macroscopic appearance
See individual tumours.
Histopathology
Nerve sheath tumours in NF1 are predominantly neurofibromas, which resemble their sporadic
counterparts. Cells of the hematopoietic lineage, including histiocytes and mast cells are also
encountered, which contribute to the tumour microenvironment { 28001089 }. Recently proposed
criteria for ANNUBP (atypical neurofibromatous neoplasms of undetermined biological potential)
are used to describe premalignant or potentially early malignant changes in neurofibromas, and
include at least two of the following: cytological atypia, hypercellularity, loss of neurofibroma
architecture, and increased mitotic rate (< 1.5 mitoses/mm2, equating to 3 mitoses/10 HPF of 0.51
mm in diameter and 0.20 mm2 in area { 28551330 }). Most MPNSTs in individuals with NF1 are
high-grade malignant spindle cell neoplasms with brisk mitotic activity and necrosis, which either
develop in a pre-existing plexiform neurofibroma or arise de novo.
Cytology
Similar to sporadic counterparts.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Although no mutational hotspots have been identified, a pathogenic NF1 variant is detected in 95%
of people with NF1 { 10862084 }. Nonsense, frameshift, and splice-site mutations, small insertions,
small deletions or small duplications all result in NF1 inactivation.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: A clinical diagnosis of NF1 requires the presence of at least two of the following features:
(1) Six or more CALs (> 5 mm diameter in children, > 15 mm in adults) (2) Two or more
neurofibromas of any type or one plexiform neurofibroma (3) Axillary/inguinal freckling (4) Optic
pathway glioma (OPG) (5) Two or more iris hamartomas (Lisch nodules) (6) Distinctive bony

1103

abnormality (tibial dysplasia, pseudarthrosis, orbital dysplasia) (7) First-degree relative with NF1
(by the above criteria).
Recently these criteria have been revised to include genetic testing and other common but newly
recognized features, particularly choroidal abnormalities { 34012067 }. It must be highlighted that
some of these clinical features may be seen in other genetic syndromes including Legius syndrome
(OMIM #641331), Noonan syndrome and constitutive mismatch repair deficiency (CMMRD)
{ 30415209 }, making molecular analysis of the NF1 gene increasingly more important for
diagnostic purposes { 19920235 ; 25074460 ; 26337637 }.
Staging
Staging for NF1-associated MPNSTs is identical to that utilized for sporadic counterparts.
Prognosis and prediction
Individuals often have a shortened life span, mainly due to malignant disease and stroke
{ 25354145 ; 21694737 ; 11283797 }. The standard mortality ratio for many complications is higher
in women under the age of 50 { 25354145 }. Genetic counselling is recommended and includes the
possibility of prenatal and/or preimplantation genetic testing.

L#26931: Orbitofacial Neurofibromatosis type 1, Orbitofacial neurofibromatosis type 1 is a unique
clinical variant that affects the bone and soft tissues of the orbital region and may results in
profound asymmetry. Cafe au lait spots and conventional cutaneous neurorfibromas are also
present in this patient.
R#26932: Orbital neurofibroma, Orbital neurofibromas frequently have plexiform components and
may intimately involve ocular adnexa, such as the lacrimal gland as in this example.

L#26955: Orbitofacial neurofibroma, Orbitofacial neurofibromas frequently have coexisting
plexiform and diffuse components
R#26956: Cutaneous neurofibroma, Cutaneous neurofibromas often involve the face and scalp in
NF1 patients, and they resemble their sporadic counterparts.

1104

L#26957: Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, These tumours are relatively rare in the
head and neck region. This low grade tumour developed in progression from an orbitofacial
neurofibroma in a NF1 patient.
R#26963: Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, High cellularity and mitotic activity
characterize this tumour developing in the posterior scalp of a NF1 patient within a long standing
plexiform neurofibroma.

L#26959: Orbitofacial NF1, This Axial CT scan using bone windows highlights prominent bone
remodeling, facial neurofibromatosis and proptosis associated with a large orbital neurofibroma in
a NF1 patient.
R#26961: Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, Malignant transformation in the form of a
rapidly enlarging nodule (arrows) within a plexiform neurofibroma of the posterior scalp of a NF1
patient.

Familial Adenomatous Polyposis with Gardner syndrome
Definition
Classic familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is an autosomal dominant syndrome caused by
pathogenic APC mutations, characterized by >100 adenomatous colorectal polyps. More than 70%
develop multiple extracolonic manifestations that define the Gardner syndrome subtype.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering:
# 175100 Familial adenomatous polyposis
ICD-11 coding
2B90.Y Other specific malignant neoplasms of colon

1105

Related terminology
Acceptable: Gardner syndrome.
Not recommended: Turcot syndrome.
Subtype(s)
Attenuated familial adenomatous polyposis.
Localization
The bulk of FAP manifestations are colorectal, with gastric and duodenal polyps also common.
Desmoid tumours mostly occur in the small bowel mesentery, abdominal wall, extremities or less
frequently in the head and neck, mostly arising in soft tissue { 975005 ; 8154779 }, but also the
maxillary sinus, nasopharynx, and oral cavity.
Less frequent extraintestinal malignancies involve the thyroid, biliary tree, pancreas, liver, and
brain. Frequent benign extraintestinal features involve bone (osteomas) and dental structures
(supernumerary teeth and odontomas).
Clinical features
Classic FAP is characterized by numerous (usually >100 sometimes thousands) adenomatous
polyps of the large intestine, usually occurring in the second decade of life. Without colectomy there
is a near 100% risk of colorectal adenocarcinoma by 45 years of age { 18194984 }.
Attenuated FAP is distinguished from classic FAP by fewer (1–100) colorectal adenomas and a
slightly reduced risk (80%) and later onset (56 years) of colorectal cancer { 16461775 ; 20105204 }.
Most FAP patients develop duodenal adenomas, mostly in the periampullary region and distal
duodenum.
The most common head and neck manifestations are desmoid-type fibromatosis, thyroid
carcinoma, osteomas, and dental anomalies.
Desmoid-type fibromatosis, a locally infiltrative, non-metastasizing (myo)fibroblast neoplasm,
occurs in 10-15%, mostly in the small intestinal mesentery, abdominal wall, extremities or less
frequently head and neck soft tissues, where it may cause an enlarging painless neck mass, nasal
obstruction or epistaxis in the sinonasal tract, facial deformity, proptosis, or dysphagia. The risk of
desmoid-type fibromatosis in FAP is increased by prior surgery and certain APC mutations
{ 27387679 }. Although it has no metastatic potential, it can cause severe morbidity
{ 10732754 ; 9014661 }.
Multiple osteomas occur due to germline APC mutations in 50-90% patients { 19157925 }. Such
osteomas increase in size over time. They are benign neoplasms composed of mature cortical or
cancellous lamellar bone, mostly in the craniofacial bones, most commonly the mandible
{ 18602294 }. They often present as a painless swelling on the bone surface, although they may
be asymptomatic { 22010067 }. Radiographically osteomas form a well-demarcated radiopaque
mass on the cortical surface (<20 mm).
The thyroid carcinomas are usually multifocal and bilateral in this syndrome and are specifically of
the cribriform morular thyroid carcinoma type { 29239041 ; 21455198 ; 20878367 }. The cribriform
component is usually admixed with morulae and in some there is a cribriform-predominant
architecture { 29239041 ; 29785019 ; 34019236 }
Multifocal odontomas or supernumerary teeth may occur. Odontomas can occur in any toothbearing area, and are mixed epithelial and mesenchymal tumour-like hamartomatous
malformations, composed of dental hard and soft tissues, that can be either compound odontomas
mainly located in the anterior maxilla, or complex odontomas mostly in the posterior mandible or
anterior maxilla.
FAP is associated with an increase in risk of carcinoma of the thyroid (~15%, cribriform morular
thyroid carcinoma) { 34019236 }, hepatobiliary tree and pancreas, childhood hepatoblastoma (1%),
adrenocortical
adenomas
/adenocarcinomas, medulloblastoma (<1%)
and
rarely,
adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma.
Epidemiology
FAP prevalence is 1 in 8000–10,000. It affects males and females equally and accounts for <1%
of all colorectal cancers { 18194984 }.
Up
to
30%
of
FAP-associated
desmoids
occur
in
children
{ 975005 ; 24206198 ; 23331794 ; 24290952 }; there is no sex predilection and syndromic
desmoids may be multifocal.
Etiology
FAP is a Mendelian autosomal dominant syndrome caused by germline variants in the APC gene
(chromosome 5q22.2) that result in a truncated or absent APC protein. Disease severity varies with

1106

mutational position in the APC gene { 10470088 ; 12057910 ; 20105204 }. Desmoid tumours are
associated with germline mutations in APC involving codons 1310–2011 in the mid- to C-terminal
portion of the encoded protein { 26179480 }.
APC acts as a classic tumour suppressor gene and a tumour phenotype arises when the nonmutant allele is spontaneously lost or mutated as a somatic event.
Pathogenesis
The classic APC protein isoform is 2843 amino acids long (amongst other isoforms), and regulates
WNT signalling. Normal APC restrains colorectal epithelial cell proliferation through its role as a
scaffold protein that binds critical components that tag (by phosphorylation and ubiquitination) the
WNT effector protein β-catenin for proteasomal destruction { 10733430 }. With APC protein
truncation (or absence), the loss of critical APC functions (especially its binding of the AXIN and βcatenin proteins) results in greatly impaired degradation of β-catenin with nuclear translocation and
upregulation of WNT signalling { 23589686 }.
Macroscopic appearance
FAP and Gardner-associated lesions grossly resemble their sporadic counterparts.
Histopathology
The histopathology of FAP and Gardner-associated lesions is indistinguishable from sporadic
counterparts with exceptions of thyroid carcinoma. Thyroid tumours are those of the cribriform
morular
thyroid
carcinoma
type,
which
is
characteristic
for
this
syndrome
{ 18948764 ; 20966648 ; 21455198 ; 29239041 ; 34019236 ; 29785019 }.
The
cribriform
component is positive for TTF1, nuclear beta-catenin strong immunopositivity and are negative
negative for thyroglobulin { 21455198 ; 29239041 }. PAX8 is absent in half of tumours and focal in
the others { 34019236 }. Morulae are positive for keratin 5 and CD5 and negative for p63, p40,
TTF1, and PAX8 { 29239041 ; 34019236 }.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
APC mutation is the tumour-initiating event for most colorectal adenomas { 8861899 }. Similarly,
FAP-associated desmoid tumours are caused by APC rather than CTNNB1 mutation, the latter
being more common in sporadic counterparts; nevertheless, they also show nuclear beta catenin
immunoreactivity secondary to WNT pathway activation { 22419028 }. Germline testing and genetic
counselling for possible FAP with Gardner syndrome should be considered for patients with head
and neck desmoids driven by APC inactivation.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Presence of >100 colorectal adenomas indicates diagnosis of classic FAP. Given the phenotypic
variability of FAP, several other polyposis conditions can have near-identical features. The
essential
molecular
criterion
is
the
presence
of
a
pathogenic
germline
(constitutional) APC mutation.
Staging
FAP and Gardner syndrome tumours are staged in the same way as equivalent sporadic tumours
at each site.
Prognosis and prediction
Management of tumours is guided by their clinical presentation and severity. Colorectal screening
by endoscopy and chemoprevention are in use { 28674119 ; 22122775 ; 26315524 }. Colectomy is
often required to prevent the development of colorectal adenocarcinoma. Upper gastrointestinal
endoscopy is guided by the Spigelman stage of duodenal polyposis { 2571019 ; 15951555 }. In
addition, screening for extraintestinal manifestations is recommended { 28674119 ; 25645574 }.
Desmoid tumours generally have a good prognosis { 25434922 }; however, positive surgical
margins and young age are associated with recurrence { 20085516 ; 23913621 ; 25341748 }.

1107

L#28580FAP-associated cribriform morular thyroid carcinoma, The multifocal thyroid carcinomas
in FAP are those of cribriform modular thyroid carcinoma. The cribriform component is usually
admixed with morulae. Morulae are positive for keratin 5 and CD5 and negative for p63, p40,
TTF1, and PAX8.
R#28581FAP-associated cribriform morular thyroid carcinoma, The morulae cells immunostain
for CD5 in a membranous pattern. The scattered CD5-positive T cells are also staining.

L#28626FAP-associated cribriform morular thyroid carcinoma, This tumour type, associated or
not with FAP shows absent staining for thyroglobulin by the tumour cells.
R#28627FAP-associated cribriform morular thyroid carcinoma, Another characteristic of the
cribriform morular thyroid carcinoma is the strong nuclear and cytoplasmic expression of betacatenin by the tumour cells.

#28628Cribriform morular thyroid carcinoma, Typical cribriform morular carcinoma are easily
recognized in low-power microscopy view. Combination of cribriform, morular, solid, follicular,
papillary, spindled, or trabecular patterns of growth can be identified. Cribriform architecture
formed by anastomosing bars or arches 1-2 cell layers thick and the morular component has a
peculiar nuclear inclusions.

1108

Brooke-Spiegler and related syndromes
Definition
Brooke-Spiegler syndrome is an inherited autosomal dominant disease characterized by multiple
spiradenomas, cylindromas, spiradenocylindromas and trichoepitheliomas (cribriform
trichoblastomas), and with salivary gland involvement by basal cell adenoma, membranous-type.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering:
#605041
ICD-11 coding
2F22 Benign neoplasms of epidermal appendages
Related terminology
Acceptable: Familial
cylindromatosis; CYLD cutaneous
syndrome;
multiple
familial
trichoepithelioma;
Not recommended: turban tumours; skin-salivary gland tumour diathesis
Subtype(s)
Multiple familial trichoepithelioma
Localization
Spiradenoma, cylindroma and spiradenocylindroma mainly involve the skin of the head and neck
area. Trichoepitheliomas predominantly affect the nasolabial folds. Salivary gland tumours mostly
occur in the parotid gland (membranous type of basal cell adenoma), whereas submandibular
glands are rarely affected { 19455704 }. Extremely rare is the occurrence of mammary cylindroma
{ 15252315 }.
A phenotypic variant of BSS is multiple familial trichoepitheliomas (MFT, OMIM 601606)
characterized by the development of trichoepitheliomas without other tumours
{ 19730223 ; 23249834 }.
Clinical features
Spiradenomas, cylindromas and spiradenocylindromas occur as multiple (dozens to hundreds),
often coalescing, variably sized (mostly 5 - 30 mm) skin-colored, reddish or blue nodules.
Trichoepitheliomas are bilateral small (2 - 10 mm), often confluent, skin-colored papules.
Cutaneous tumours demonstrate growth throughout life, and their number increases with age.
Rapid growth, ulceration and bleeding, especially in older patients usually indicate malignant
transformation, especially for cylindroma, spiradenoma and spiradenocylindroma (around 5% 10% of cases). Parotid neoplasms present as a freely movable mass usually measuring between
5 - 50 mm in diameter. Multifocal unilateral involvement of the parotid gland as well as bilateral
metachronous and synchronous parotid neoplasms can be seen. Facial nerve paralyses or visual
alterations are rare { 19194280 ; 19730223 ; 23249834 }.
Epidemiology
BSS and MFT are rare, but the exact incidence is unknown. About 1-2% of patients with BSS
develop salivary gland tumours, whereas patients with MFT appear to have skin lesions only.
Cutaneous tumours usually occur around puberty, whereas salivary gland involvement is usually
seen after the age of 40 years { 23249834 }.
Etiology
Germline heterozygous pathogenic sequence variant in CYLD in a proband with multiple skin
tumours and basal cell adenoma, membranous-type { 31093340 ; 32298062 }.
Pathogenesis
Most patients (80-85% with the classical BSS phenotype and 40-50% the MFT phenotype) harbour
germline sequence variants in the CYLD gene, a tumour suppressor gene, located on chromosome
16q12-q13 { 10835629 ; 26329847 ; 16922728 }. More than one hundred CYLD mutations have
been reported. Most mutations (~50%) are frameshift, (~25%), nonsense (~15%), missense, and
(~10%) putative splice-site and are predicted to result in truncated proteins. Most mutations involve
exons 9-20, whereas exons 4–8 are spared { 20132422 ; 20502185 }. Other rare mechanisms
include large deletions in CYLD and mutations in intronic sequences leading to intronic
exonization { 19668078 ; 25347032 }. Cutaneous neoplasms demonstrate a large spectrum of
somatic mutations representing both loss of heterozygosity (LOH) and sequence alterations
in CYLD { 26861065 }.
Macroscopic appearance

1109

Benign cutaneous and salivary gland tumours are usually circumscribed, non-encapsulated, ovoid
to round nodules with smooth surface. In larger lesions (especially spiradenomas), the cut surface
may show foci of haemorrhage and cystic change { 22978388 }.
Basal cell adenoma, membranous type, present in Brooke Spiegler syndrome are usually
multicentric and bilateral. These tumours have a solid homogeneous tan cut surface { 25141971 }.
Histopathology
Salivary gland involvement in this syndrome is characterized by the presence of multicentric basal
cell adenoma, which appears identical to cutaneous cylindroma. These tumours are usually
bilateral/multicentric, well-circumscribed, and characteristically of the membranous-type growth
pattern. The tumour nests show the characteristic peripheral palisading of dark cells, with paler
cells located more centrally within these nests. The cells have a vesicular nuclei. The cells nests
are separated by dense hyaline stroma { 19455704 ; 28060371 }. By immunohistochemistry, both
epithelial and myoepithelial markers are present { 23209336 ; 24206768 }.
Basal cell adenoma, membranous-type, are distinguished from cellular pleomorphic adenomas by
their consistent basaloid appearance, peripheral palisading, and the presence of thick hyaline
basement membranes. Basal cell adenomas are distinguished from basal cell adenocarcinoma by
the absence of invasion { 25141971 }.
Spiradenoma, cylindroma and spiradenocylindroma are related tumours, usually well-demarcated,
multinodular or uninodular, with regular or somewhat pushing borders. Spiradenoma is composed
of small basaloid cells arranged in a trabecular, reticular or solid fashion intermixed with paler cells
and lymphocytes; an integral component of the tumour. A few tubular structures are often present.
Droplets of PAS-positive hyaline basal membrane material can be seen. Cylindroma in most cases
displays a jigsaw puzzle (mosaic-like) arrangement of islands of basaloid cells surrounded by
variably thickened eosinophilic basement membrane material. The peripheral cells often show
palisading and are usually darker compared to centrally located cells. Intratumoural lymphocytes
are absent or are very few in number. Spiradenocylindroma combines areas of cylindroma and
spiradenoma that either closely intermingled or sharply segregated. The ratio between
spiradenomatous and cylindromatous components varies among cases.
Malignant neoplasms evolving from cutaneous tumours include low-grade and high-grade lesions
with a basaloid morphology resembling salivary gland basal cell adenocarcinomas. Less common
are adenocarcinoma, not otherwise specified, and sarcomatoid (metaplastic) carcinoma
{ 19633533 ; 22588548 }.
Cytology
Basal cell adenoma, membranous type, is composed by monomorphic small basaloid cells with
scant cytoplasm with clean background and with a dense hyaline stroma.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Identification of germline heterozygous pathogenic variant in CYLD. Offspring of an
individual have a 50% chance of inheriting the variant { 32298062 }. No genotype-phenotype
correlations with respect to the severity of the disease, the possibility of malignant transformation,
and development of extracutaneous lesions has been found { 15854031 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
The diagnosis of Brooke-Spiegler syndrome is established in a proband with multiple skin tumours
(multiple
inherited
and
histologically
confirmed cutaneous cylindromas, spiradenomas, spiradenocylindromas and/or trichoepithelioma
s);
AND/OR
Identification of a germline heterozygous pathogenic variant in CYLD by molecular genetic testing.
Desirable:
The syndrome should be suspected with a salivary gland multicentric basal cell adenoma,
membranous-type, in a patient with single cylindroma, trichoepithelioma or spiradenoma. Cases
with few such lesions (up to 4) are usually sporadic and not associated with a
germline CYLD mutation { 22588548 }.
Staging
Not clinically relevant.

1110

Prognosis and prediction
Salivary gland basal cell adenoma, membranous type is benign. Spiradenoma, cylindroma,
spiradenocylindroma are benign lesions. Malignant tumours developing from these neoplasms may
show local aggressive growth or distant metastases. Because of their rarity and microscopic
heterogeneity the exact prognosis is unknown.

L#28478Basal Cell Adenoma, Low power view showing a well-circumscribed parotid basal cell
adenoma, present in a patient with Brooke-Spiegler syndrome/familial cylindromatosis syndrome.
R#28625Basal Cell Adenoma, Membranous Type, Salivary gland involvement in this syndrome is
characterized by the presence of basal cell adenoma, membranous type, which appears identical
to cutaneous cylindroma. The tumour nests show the characteristic peripheral palisading of dark
cells, with paler cells separated by dense hyaline stroma.

#28624Parotid Tumour in Brooke Spiegler syndrome, Salivary gland involvement is characterized
by the presence of multicentric membranous-type basal cell adenoma. These tumors are usually
bilateral/multicentric, well-circumscribed. The tumour nests show the characteristic peripheral
palisading of dark cells, with paler cells located more centrally within these nests. The cells nests
are separated by dense hyaline stroma.

1111

#28272Basal cell adenoma, The basal cell adenomas in Brooke Spiegler are often multifocal.
One of the adenomas shows a trabecular pattern while the second shows a membranous
architecture.

#28274Skin cylindroma-spiradenoma, The skin lesions in Brooke Spiegler syndrome often show
overlap between eccrine spiradenoma and cylindroma, where the tumor has a round/nodular
architecture with hyaline droplets in the nodules while the second component has more polygonal
tumor nodules that fit together like a jigsaw puzzle

Cowden syndrome
Definition
Cowden syndrome (CS) is an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by multiple hamartomas
involving organs derived from any of the three germ layers, with cancer predisposition.
ICD-O coding
158350 Cowden syndrome 1
615107 Cowden syndrome 4
615108 Cowden syndrome 5
615109 Cowden syndrome 6
616858 Cowden syndrome 7
ICD-11 coding
LD2D.Y Cowden syndrome

1112

Related terminology
Acceptable: PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome; Cowden disease; multiple hamartoma
syndrome.
Subtype(s)
PTEN hamartoma tumour syndrome (PHTS) is a heterogeneous group of disorders with autosomal
dominant inheritance, caused by germline mutation of the PTEN gene. PHTS also includes
Bannayan–Riley–Ruvalcaba syndrome and Proteus syndrome. However, most cases of PHTS
correspond to CS, and the terms “PHTS” and “CS” are often used interchangeably { 19668082 }.
Localization
Oral papillomas are a major diagnostic criterion for CS and are present in virtually all CS patients.
Papillomas are usually present on the lips but also common on the tongue, buccal mucosa (Figure
1A) and gingivae. Mucocutaneous neuromas have also been reported in CS patients. Facial
trichilemmomas are a major diagnostic criterion of CS. Trichilemmomas are typically multiple and
found in the central portion of the face, around the eyes, nose, mouth and forehead
{ 26975628 ; 27889943 ; 24560406 }.
Clinical features
CS is characterized by mucocutaneous lesions (multiple facial trichilemmomas, acral keratoses,
papillomatous papules, and mucosal lesions are considered pathognomonic), an increased cancer
risk, benign hamartomatous overgrowth of tissues (including gastrointestinal polyposis), and
macrocephaly (Table 1 #25223) { 24136893 ; 26975628 }. Diffuse oesophageal glycogenic
acanthosis in combination with colonic polyposis may be diagnostic of CS
{ 21437855 ; 23238744 }. Cancer risk is relatively broad, with risk of breast, thyroid, endometrial,
renal cell, and colon cancers; melanoma; and other cancers (Table 2 #25224) { 20301661 }.
Trichilemmomas present in the central portion of the face, including the eyes, nose, mouth, and
forehead and mucocutaneous papillomatous papules are one of the first signs of the
disease. Facial lesions occur in the majority of patients. The most distinctive and peculiar facial
features of CS consist of multiple small and keratotic papules concentrated around the
orifices. A trichilemmoma is rarely a sporadic feature, and the literature regularly reports numerous
lesions at presentation in individuals with a clinical and/or genetic diagnosis { 24136893 }.
Consequently, trichilemmoma is a clinically significant sign of CS when seen in multiplicity (at
least ≥ 3), but at least one lesion should be biopsy-proven given the difficulty with clinical
diagnosis. Early recognition of these skin lesions may help on diagnosing an underlying
malignancy and early cancer screening { 26975628 }.
Epidemiology
The prevalence has been estimated at about 1 case per 200 000–250 000 individuals in a European
population. This may be an underestimate given the difficulty in diagnosing this syndrome
{ 10234502 }.
Etiology
CS is an autosomal dominant disorder with age-related penetrance and variable expression.
Germline mutation in PTEN (10q23.3) is found in about 85% of CS cases, as well as in subsets of
other PHTS disorders (in 65% of Bannayan–Riley–Ruvalcaba syndrome cases, 20% of Proteus
syndrome
cases,
and
50%
of
Proteus-like
syndrome
cases)
{ 19668082 ; 18794875 ; 9140396 ; 8673088 }. Individualized risk calculation may aid assessment
of a patient’s risk of carrying a germline PTEN mutation based on clinical features { 21194675 }.
Germline succinate dehydrogenase mutations have been found in about 5% of PTEN mutation–
negative CS / CS-like individuals and are associated with increased frequencies of breast, thyroid,
and renal cancers beyond those conferred by germline PTEN mutation { 18678321 }.
Pathogenesis
PTEN is a virtually ubiquitously expressed tumour suppressor and a dual-specificity lipid and
protein phosphatase that regulates cell proliferation, cell migration, and apoptosis through inhibition
of AKT via the PI3K/AKT pathway { 23613428 }. Inactivation of the second copy of the gene allows
deregulation of the AKT pathway.
Macroscopic appearance
Mucosal papillomas present as small smooth whitish papules, that often coalesce resulting in
characteristic cobblestone appearance on the lips, gingiva, buccal or tongue mucosa. Presence of
a
fissured
tongue
is
also
considered
a
characteristic
feature
of
CS
{ 26975628 ; 27889943 ; 24560406 }.
Histopathology

1113

Oral papillomas are histologically non-specific and indistinguishable from irritation fibroma. They
are composed of fibrous connective tissue, that may contain some dilated blood vessels and
inflammatory infiltrate, lined by hyperplastic non-keratinizing squamous epithelium. The skin
lesions and other tumours associated with Cowden syndrome lose PTEN staining by
immunohistochemistry { 30730313 }.
Clinically, trichilemmomas are often indistinguishable from other verrucous skin lesions and
therefore histopathological confirmation is necessary. Trichilemmomas are typically associated
pre-existing hair follicles and characterized by thickening of the follicular infundibulum, with
superficial
hypergranulosis
and
hyperkeratosis
and
focal
trichilemmal
differentiation. Tricholemmomas can be difficult to distinguish from basal cell carcinoma with
trichilemmal differentiation and the surface of trichilemmoma may be indistinguishable from verruca
vulgaris.
Cytology
Not clinically relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Not clinically relevant
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
The International Cowden Consortium (ICC) operational diagnostic criteria are listed in Table
1 #25223.
Staging
Not clinically relevant
Prognosis and prediction
Patients with PHTS are at increased risk of several types of cancer, and interval screening is
recommended (Table 2) { 22252256 ; 23613428 ; 23335809 }.

L#24581Oral papilloma in Cowden Syndrome, A lesion is shown on the buccal mucosa.
R#24584Trichilemmoma in CS, This low power view shows a small facial trichilemmoma.

L#24585Trichilemmoma in CS, Shave excision of a verruca vulgaris-like trichilemmoma.
R#24587Oral papilloma in CS, The histology of oral mucosal papillomas in CS is non-specific and
indistinguishable from irritation fibroma.

1114

L#28582PTEN-hamartoma Tumour Syndrome/Cowden Syndrome, Adenomatous thyroid nodules
in patients with this syndrome show PTEN loss within the lesional follicular cells. PTEN
immunostaining loss is identified within numerous skin, thyroid, cerebellar and other lesion
associated with Cowden syndrome.
R#28623Multiple thyroid adenomatous nodules in Cowden Syndrome, One of the characteristic
finding in Cowden syndrome is the presence of multiple thyroid adenomatous nodules and
hyperplastic nodules.

Familial paraganglioma syndromes
Definition
A group of inherited cancer syndromes characterized by the presence of paragangliomas.
ICD-O coding
See table #27513
ICD-11 coding
Not available
Related terminology
Familial
paraganglioma-pheochromocytoma
syndromes;
hereditary
paragangliomapheochromocytoma syndromes; hereditary pheochromocytoma-paraganglioma
Subtype(s)
Familial paraganglioma syndromes are shown in table #27513
Localization
Parasympathetic-derived paragangliomas commonly arise in the head-and-neck region, including
in the carotid body and along cervical branches of the glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves
{ 24642075 }. Familial paragangliomas may be multifocal; they occur anywhere in the body.
Sympathetic-derived paragangliomas are usually intra-adrenal (phaeochromocytoma) or
retroperitoneal, para-aortic, inferior mesenteric artery and above the aortic bifurcation.
Clinical features
Clinical manifestations may be caused by catecholamine excess and/or mass effects. In the head
and neck, mass effects predominate.
Symptoms of adrenaline/noradrenaline excess include sweating, palpitation and anxiety; signs
include hypertension and tachycardia. These are generally associated with sympathetic
paragangliomas. Parasympathetic paragangliomas may secrete dopamine with minimal clinical
manifestations and others, mainly those of the head-and-neck, are non-secretory. There is strong
genotype-phenotype correlation in catecholamine profile. Tumors with pseudohypoxic
pathogenesis are Cluster 1 that tends to be clinically silent and non-secretory or dopaminesecreting; Cluster 2 comprises tumours with kinase signaling and rare pheochromocytomas with
WNT-pathway activation that are usually functional.
Cluster 1 tumours express somatostatin receptors and are well visualized with 68Ga-DOTATATE
PET/CT or the less sensitive indium-labeled somatostatin. Cluster 2 tumours can be imaged
with 18F-DOPA PET/CT or Iobeguane (metaiodobenzylguanidine; 123I-MIBG).
Epidemiology

1115

Overall, ~40% of paragangliomas are hereditary. Younger age at presentation, multiple tumors,
and extra-adrenal tumors are significantly associated with the presence of a germline mutation
{ 12000816 }.
Etiology
The etiology is attributed to inheritance of genetic alterations that predispose to tumour
development { 28162975 ; 29239044 }.
Pathogenesis
See Table
Macroscopic appearance
Familial paragangliomas are often multifocal. Adrenal disease in patients with MEN2 is often
bilateral and grossly multinodular.
Histopathology
The morphology of most familial paragangliomas overlaps that of sporadic paragangliomas. Some
features may be suggestive of genetic predisposition. Some SDHx-associated sympathetic
paragangliomas consist of monotonous cells with vacuolated eosinophilic cytoplasm
{ 31789631 }. Some have a distinct pseudorosette pattern { 24659481 }. SDHx-related
paragangliomas from the head and neck usually have small cells with clear cytoplasm. Unlike
sporadic paragangliomas, SDHx-associated tumours are rarely associated with a spindled
morphology or densely granular cytoplasm { 31789631 }. VHL-associated tumors may have clear
cells with vacuolated cytoplasm and stromal edema { 2114747 }.
Immunohistochemistry localizes neuroendocrine markers nuclear INSM1 and cytoplasmic
synaptophysin and chromogranin in tumour cells. S100 and SOX10 highlight sustentacular cells.
Paragangliomas express nuclear GATA3 and cytoplasmic tyrosine hydroxylase. Most
paragangliomas are immuno-negative for cytokeratins { 31442521 }. Loss of SDHB
immunoreactivity in tumor cells with granular cytoplasmic staining of stromal cells supports the
diagnosis of SDHx disease { 16103922 ; 20236688 ; 19576851 }; in tumors that lack SDHB
immunoreactivity, loss of SDHA staining identifies patients with SDHA mutations
{ 16103922 ; 21752896 }. VHL-associated tumors have membranous positivity for CAIX. Tumors
of the pseudohypoxic types express inhibin. Fumarate hydratase (FH) mutations are characterised
by loss of expression of FH, which is highly specific but imperfectly sensitive, and aberrant
expression of 2-succinocysteine (2SC) which is highly sensitive but imperfectly specific
{ 24334767 }.
Cytology
Cytological diagnosis of paragangliomas is challenging, but use of immunohistochemistry may
assist in the diagnosis and in determination of familial disease { 29575826 }.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Genetic testing is required to confirm the mutation responsible for the disease
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: The presence of more than one paraganglioma in a kindred or individual or confirmed
germline mutation in a susceptibility gene.
Desirable: Loss of SDHB immunoreactivity has a high predictive value for SDHB, SDHC, or SDHD
mutations.
Staging
While the 8th edition of the AJCC TNM staging system is applied to pheochromocytomas and
sympathetic paragangliomas, no TNM system is published for extra-adrenal paragangliomas in the
head and neck. The ICCR and other expert-initiated reporting guidelines have been introduced
{ 24476517 ; 32407815 }.
Prognosis and prediction
Most paragangliomas can be surgically resected; however, large tumours and some head and neck
tumour locations may preclude complete excision. Familial lesions are often multifocal; the
presentations
may
be
asynchronous,
mimicking
metastasis.
Catecholamine
profile, SDHB mutation and somatic ATRX or TERT promoter mutations increase risk of
metastasis {30301828, 31705439}. Five-year overall survival in patients with metastatic
paraganglioma ranges from 50-70% { 28746746 }.

1116

L#25342SDHx-related paraganglioma, These tumours often have abundant granular
eosoinophilic cytoplasm. They express cytoplasmic tyrosine hydroxylase. Lack of cytoplasmic
SDHB with intact stromal positivity indicates SDHx-related disease. Tumours associated with a
pseudohypoxia pathway alteration also express inhibin

L#28660Middle ear paraganglioma, This example shows the typical paraganglioma architecture
with mucosal cells overlying the tumour.

Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2
Definition
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2 (MEN2) is an autosomal dominant tumour syndrome caused
by activating mutations in the RET proto-oncogene.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering
171400 Multiple endocrine neoplasia, type 2A
162300 Multiple endocrine neoplasia, type 2B
ICD-11 coding
2F7A.0 Multiple polyglandular tumours
Related terminology
Acceptable:
MEN2
syndrome
Not recommended: Sipple syndrome, multiple endocrine neoplasia type 3 (MEN3), Gorlin
syndrome, Wagenmann-Froboese syndrome, mucosal neuroma syndrome, Farndon syndrome
Subtype(s)
Multiple endocrine neoplasia, type 2A
Multiple endocrine neoplasia, type 2B (formerly MEN3)
Localization
Medullary thyroid carcinoma, which is the hallmark of this disorder, involves the thyroid gland, and
specifically arises most often at the junction of the upper third and lower two-thirds of the lateral
lobes of the gland. The disease begins as microscopic proliferations (classified as nodular C cell
hyperplasia) in this location, usually bilaterally, and progresses to multifocal invasive carcinoma.
Parathyroid proliferations arise in the parathyroid glands beside and below the thyroid in the neck.
They are bilateral in 70% of patients.
Mucosal neuromas in MEN 2B can involve the oropharynx, lips, and eyelids with the addition of
thickened corneal nerves, and ganglioneuromatosis of the GI tract with neuroma or diffuse
ganglioneuromatosis. Cutaneous lichen amyloidosis involves the skin of chest.
Clinical features
The disorder has several variants. MEN2A is the classical form, characterized by the development
of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) often associated with pheochromocytoma and/or parathyroid
proliferations. MEN2B, originally called MEN3, is a rare subtype characterized by a highly
aggressive early-onset MTC associated with a high risk of pheochromocytoma, oral and intestinal
ganglioneuromas, and a Marfanoid body habitus.

1118

Medullary thyroid carcinoma usually presents as neck mass(es); occasional patients complain of
diarrhea and flushing caused by calcitonin excess. Rarer presentations are due to
pheochromocytoma. Patients with Hisrschsprung’s disease present in childhood with constipation,
diarrhea, vomiting and abdominal distention. Patients with MEN2B have a marfanoid habitus and
ganglioneuromas may be seen on oral examination.
Increasingly MEN2 is being diagnosed early due to genetic screening of known kindreds.
Epidemiology
The prevalence of MEN2 (all subtypes) is about 1/30,000 population. The incidence of MEN2A is
about 1/1,973,500 persons; MEN2B is much lower, at 1/38,750,000 persons. There is no gender
predilection; some studies have shown a slight female predominance.
Etiology
Monoallelic gain-of-function germline mutations of RET. A negative family history does not exclude
germline disease, as the disorder can skip generations or be misdiagnosed.
MEN2B is more often due to a de novo germline mutation.
Pathogenesis
The RET proto-oncogene encodes a tyrosine kinase receptor which is activated by glial cell line
derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF) family ligands bound to GFRas (GDNF family receptor alphas)
{ 32816064 }. Intracellular RET signalling is needed for the development of the enteric nervous
system, kidney and urinary tract { 32816064 }.
MEN2A mutations mainly alter cysteines of codons 609, 611, 618, 620 (exon 10) or 634 (exon 11)
of the extracellular domain { 8918855 ; 21552134 ; 7907913 }. RET p.C634R is the most frequent
mutation and is associated with the classical triad of MTC, PCC and hyperparathyroidism. FMTC
mutations do not affect cysteines in exons 8, 13,14 and 15 or codons 609-620. The p.A883T
mutation only induced MTC in a family in a homozygous setting only. In about 2% of MEN2 patients,
no mutation in the RET proto-oncogene can be detected { 20516206 }.
The vast majority of MEN2B patients carries the RET p.M918T mutation in the kinase domain
leading to a constitutive auto-phosphorylation. RET p.A883P is found in 2-3% of MEN2B patients
{ 9360560 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) in this disorder is typically bilateral and multicentric. The cut
surface of the tumours is white to yellow and homogeneous.
Mucosal neuromas in MEN 2B can involve the oropharynx, lips, and eyelids.
Histopathology
Medullary thyroid carcinoma in MEN2 appears histologically indistinguishable from sporadic MTC,
but familial cases are more often multifocal, bilateral, and associated with C cell hyperplasia.
Parathyroid proliferations occur in 15-30% of individuals with MEN2A, but are not seen in
MEN2B. They have been classified as adenomas and so called “hyperplasia” when there is
multiglandular disease that is more likely multiple neoplasms involving single or multiple glands.
Mucosal neuromas in MEN2B can involve the oropharynx, lips, and eyelids with the addition of
thickened corneal nerves, and ganglioneuromatosis of the GI tract with neuroma or diffuse
ganglioneuromatosis { 4957444 ; 5660196 ; 11356339 ; 8880581 }.
Cytology
Not applicable
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Identification of a pathogenic germline mutation in the RET gene
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: documented pathogenic sequence variant of the RET gene and/or medullary thyroid
carcinoma associated with any of the other features of MEN2.
Desirable: C cell hyperplasia
Staging
There is no uniformly accepted syndrome-specific staging system for MEN2. Malignant neoplasms
associated with this syndrome generally follow cancer staging suggested by UICC/AJCC criteria
[[Amin MB, Edge SB, Greene FL, et al. editors. AJCC Cancer Staging Manual. 8th ed. Switzerland:
Springer, 2017]].
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis in MEN2 is determined by MTC and is superior to that of sporadic MTC with 10-year
survival rates depending on disease stage, currently at 100%, 93%, 71% and 21% for stage I, II,
III, and IV, respectively { 9578814 }. The mutation risk profile has led to recommendations for

1119

prophylactic thyroidectomy in mutation carriers in MEN2 families, with surgery performed before
age 1 for the highest risk group, before age 5 for those in the high risk group of codon 634 mutations
in MEN2A and after 10 years for those in the moderate risk group { 25810047 ; 28609830 }.

R#31975 Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2, 21 year old male with MEN2 with tongue mucosal
