1121

Hyperparathyroidism jaw tumour syndrome
Definition
Hyperparathyroidism jaw tumour (HPTJT) syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by
pathogenic germline sequence variants variants or deletions in CDC73. It is characterised
predominantly by parathyroid adenoma and carcinoma, with a lower incidence of fibro-osseous jaw
lesions.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering
145001 Hyperparathyroidism jaw tumour syndrome; HPTJT
ICD-11 coding
5A51.0 Primary hyperparathyroidism
Related terminology
Acceptable: CDC73-related disorder
Not recommended: Familial cystic parathyroid adenomatosis; Hyperparathyroidism 2 with jaw
tumours (HRPT2)
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
In most kindreds, parathyroid tumours are the only feature { 19529956 ; 18436011 ; 29324469 }.
Jaw tumours are relatively uncommon, being found in as few as 2.5% to 20% of
known CDC73 mutation
carriers
{ 25444225 ; 19529956 ; 18436011 ; 23029104 ; 18755853 ; 29324469 ; 33505612 ; 32590342 }.
Clinically significant renal and uterine tumours occur at increased incidence, but are rare
{ 25444225 ; 30452964 ; 15606373 ; 26450137 ; 32590342 }.
Clinical features
Primary hyperparathyroidism is the usual presentation of HPTJT syndrome. Contrasting with other
forms of hereditary hyperparathyroidism where multi-gland involvement is very common, a single
adenoma or carcinoma is found in 60 to 80% at presentation { 32468209 ; 31929790 }.
Metachronous tumours occur during long term follow up (decades) in the majority of patients in
many series { 18436011 ; 2123361 ; 25444225 ; 19529956 }. In addition to family history, clinical
clues to the diagnosis of HPTJT syndrome include young age of onset (particularly if < 40 years),
very large tumours, cystic change, markedly elevated serum PTH levels and severe hypercalcemia
{ 18755853 ; 19529956 ; 23293331 ; 29324469 ; 28674121 }. Parathyroid carcinoma is extremely
rare outside the setting of germline or somatic CDC73 mutation, whereas the lifetime risk of
parathyroid
carcinoma
in
HPTJT
syndrome
may
be
as
high
as
15%
{ 12434154 ; 25444225 ; 19529956 ; 18436011 ; 23293331 ; 29324469 }. Therefore the possibility
of HPTJT syndrome should be considered in all patients with parathyroid carcinoma even in the
absence of a family history { 16931959 ; 24402736 ; 29324469 }. In fact up to 30% of patients with
apparently sporadic parathyroid carcinomas have pathogenic germline sequence variants variants
or
deletions
in CDC73 indicating
previously
undiagnosed
HPTJT
syndrome
{ 15531515 ; 14585940 ; 29324469 }. The jaw tumours arise more often in the mandible than the
maxilla and about 15% are multiple { 3856818 ; 11294964 ; 24678936 ; 33505612 }. They are
commonly incidental findings on imaging where they present as a well-demarcated, usually
radiolucent and unilocular lesions { 24678936 ; 16458039 }. They may present as an enlarging
mass that interrupts dentition. Although clinically benign, they show a variable rate of growth and
are usually slowly progressive.
The incidence of germline CDC73 mutations in patients with clinically sporadic ossifying fibromas
has not been extensively studied. It is likely to be very low given that even somatic
only CDC73 mutations are only found in up to 5% { 27658992 }. Older reports suggested that a
significant proportion of HPTJT patients develop renal pathology, predominantly simple cysts, but
also adenomas, Wilms tumours, and mixed epithelial and stromal tumours (MESTs)
{ 23293331 ; 25444225 ; 10770180 ; 30452964 }. However simple cysts are not uncommon in the
general population and in practice clinically significant renal pathology is very rare, being absent
from
many
large
series
{ 19529956 ; 18436011 ; 23029104 ; 24716902 ; 21652691 ; 18755853 ; 29324469 }. Similarly,
historically a significant proportion of female patients have been reported to have some form of

1122

uterine pathology { 25444225 ; 30452964 }. These are usually lesions which are also very common
in the general community – particularly benign leiomyomas, but also endometrial hyperplasias,
polyps, adenomyosis or adenofibromas, which may represent incidental findings in patients under
surveillance { 18755853 ; 25444225 ; 30452964 }. However a small number of uterine
adenosarcomas (otherwise a rare pathology) have been reported { 25444225 }, and
adenosarcomas also arise in mouse models of CDC73 mutation suggesting a causal relationship
and supporting an increased risk { 28288139 }.
Epidemiology
HPTJT syndrome is a rare cause of hyperparathyroidism and it has been estimated that only 0.19%
of unselected parathyroid tumours are associated with CDC73 mutation { 29324469 }. In contrast
to sporadic hyperparathyroidism, there is an equal gender distribution for both the parathyroid and
jaw tumours { 29324469 ; 33505612 }. The median age at presentation with either
hyperparathyroidism or jaw tumours is the third to fourth decade (reported range 7 to 66 years for
the
parathyroid
tumours,
and
10
to
66
years
for
the
jaw
tumours)
{ 33505612 ; 23293331 ; 25444225 ; 21652691 ; 25444225 ; 29324469 ; 33505612 }.
The
estimated penetrance of primary hyperparathyroidism in non-index mutation carriers increase from
8% at 25 years, to 53% at 50 years, and 75% at 70 years; whilst the penetrance of any
manifestation is 11% at 25 years, 65% at 50 years, and 83% at 70 years { 29040582 }.
Etiology
HPTJT syndrome is caused by heterozygous pathogenic germline sequence variants variants or
deletions in the CDC73 gene (previously known as HRPT2), a 17 exon gene which maps to 1q25q31
and
encodes
the
531
amino
acid
protein
parafibromin
{ 7717405 ; 24402736 ; 12434154 ; 16931959 }.
Pathogenesis
CDC73 is ubiquitously expressed in most tissues and functions as a bona fide tumour suppressor
gene. A somatic second hit is usually, but not always, able to be demonstrated in neoplasms arising
in the setting of HPTJT syndrome { 12434154 ; 24402736 ; 16989776 }. Parafibromin is a critical
component of the polymerase-associated factor 1 complex (PAF1C) that binds to RNA polymerase
II and controls transcription { 11294964 ; 25388829 ; 29142233 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Compared to sporadic parathyroid tumours, parafibromin deficient parathyroid tumours are usually
larger. In one study the median weight was 1.84 g (range 0.231 g to 14 g) { 29324469 }. Like
sporadic parathyroid carcinomas, carcinomas arising in the setting of HPTJT syndrome may appear
to be stuck down or adherent to other structures { 29324469 }.
Histopathology
Parathyroid tumours associated with biallelic CDC73 mutation/inactivation (with or without a
germline mutation) are termed ‘parafibromin-deficient’ { 29324469 }. There are morphological clues
to a parafibromin-deficient parathyroid tumour including a sheet-like (rather than acinar) growth
pattern, eosinophilic cytoplasm, perinuclear cytoplasmic clearing, nuclear enlargement
occasionally with multinucleation and a distinctive arborizing vasculature often with staghorn
vessels { 32468209 ; 29324469 }. Cystic change was originally described as a common feature
{ 12434154 }, but may be found in less than half of the parathyroid tumours
{ 18436011 ; 29324469 }. Loss of immunohistochemical expression of parafibromin is usually seen
in
parafibromin-deficient
parathyroid
tumours
{ 24402736 ; 29324469 ; 16931959 ; 19017757 ; 30923346 }. It is emphasized that for a tumour to
be considered parafibromin-deficient, there should be completely absent nuclear staining in all
neoplastic cells, in the presence of preserved nuclear expression in non-neoplastic cells which act
as an internal positive control { 16931959 ; 29324469 ; 30923346 }. Interpreted in this way loss of
nuclear expression of parafibromin is highly specific but not completely sensitive for biallelic CDC73
inactivation. The significance of absent nucleolar expression of parafibromin with preserved
expression elsewhere in the nucleus is uncertain, but some authors have suggested this pattern is
also
associated
with CDC73 mutation
{ 30903444 ; 21221636 ; 29626276 ; 29324469 ; 30923346 }.
Because
some CDC73 point
mutations may be associated with preserved parafibromin expression, positive staining for
parafibromin in parathyroid neoplasms does not exclude the diagnosis of HPT-JT
{ 19017757 ; 16931959 ; 21240254 ; 25444225 }. In most cases aberrant diffuse strong expression
of PGP9.5 may serve as an adjunct to increase sensitivity, but on its own PGP9.5 overexpression
lacks specificity for biallelic CDC73 inactivation { 19017757 ; 24402736 ; 24081804 }. Although

1123

biallelic somatic-only mutations do occur; as many as two thirds of all parafibromin-deficient
parathyroid tumours appear to be associated with pathogenic germline variants { 29324469 }.
Therefore the identification of a parafibromin-deficient parathyroid tumour is considered an
indication for genetic testing at any age and irrespective of family history { 29324469 }.
The diagnosis of parathyroid carcinoma in HPTJT syndrome is based on the standard criteria of
unequivocal local invasion, vascular space invasion, and/or metastasis { 32687943 }. Given the
known high risk of malignancy, it has been argued that a lower threshold for invasive growth should
be applied in parafibromin-deficient parathyroid tumours { 24402736 ; 29324469 ; 24081804 }.
Although terminology is not uniform, the jaw tumours are usually classified as ossifying fibromas
{ 24678936 ; 16458039 ; 33505612 }. They are composed of a relatively avascular cellular
fibroblast-rich stroma sometimes with a prominent storiform pattern admixed with bony trabeculae
and/or cementum-like spherules. The histological appearance is quite different from osteitis fibrosa
cystica (so called ‘brown tumours’) associated with sporadic primary hyperparathyroidism with
which they may be confused clinically { 24678936 ; 16458039 }. In contrast to the parathyroid
tumours, parafibromin immunohistochemistry does not appear to be effective in identifying HPTJTrelated ossifying fibromas { 24402736 }. Being rare, the renal and uterine pathologies are less well
described
{ 23293331 ; 25444225 ; 10770180 ; 19529956 ; 18436011 ; 23029104 ; 24716902 ; 21652691 ;
18755853 ; 25444225 }. Interestingly an HPTJT kindred with three family members with mixed
epithelial and stromal tumour (MEST) of the kidney was recently reported { 30452964 } and,
together with uterine adenosarcoma { 25444225 ; 15606373 }, these relatively rare histologies may
be a clue to the diagnosis.
Cytology
As the parathyroid tumours are often very large, they occasionally undergo fine needle aspiration
biopsy if mistaken clinically for thyroid lesions.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
There are no true hotspots in CDC73, although mutations in exons 1, 2 and 7 are overrepresented.{ 20052758 ; 23489357 ; 23293331 ; 29324469 }. The majority of coding alterations
are frameshift, nonsense and splice site mutations which are predicted to encode a truncated
protein { 11294964 ; 24340015 ; 25959515 ; 23293331 }. Up to 35% of germline variants
in CDC73 are large scale deletions and may not be detected unless specifically sought with MLPA
or other dedicated studies { 23293331 ; 29324469 }.
There are no true hotspots in CDC73, although mutations in exons 1, 2 and 7 are over-represented
{ 20052758 ; 23489357 ; 23293331 ; 29324469 }. The majority of coding alterations are frameshift,
nonsense and splice site mutations which are predicted to encode a truncated protein
{ 11294964 ; 24340015 ; 25959515 ; 23293331 }. Up to 35% of germline variants in CDC73 are
large scale deletions and may not be detected unless specifically sought with MLPA or other
dedicated studies { 23293331 ; 29324469 }. Unidentified deletions or mutations in the promoter,
UTRs, or introns may account for the small proportion of otherwise typical HPTJT cases for which
no mutations are identified { 20052758 ; 20026646 }. Recently it has been suggested that high
impact mutations in CDC73 (defined as those that cause conformational disruption or loss of
expression and always impact the C-terminal domain of parafibromin) are associated with a
significantly higher risk of parathyroid carcinoma and jaw tumours, despite a similar risk of
hyperparathyroidism to low impact mutations { 32590342 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: Demonstration of a germline pathologenic variant in CDC73
OR
The presence of multiple histologically confirmed parafibromin deficient neoplasms in an individual
or kindred
Staging
Staging of the individual tumours is based on the UICC/AJCC systems.
Prognosis and prediction
Lifelong surveillance is indicated for all HPTJT patients. This is primarily because of the risk of
parathyroid adenoma and carcinoma { 29324469 } but also because of the risk of jaw lesions and
much lower risk of gynaecological or renal pathology. The jaw lesions are benign, but may be locally
infiltrative and recur { 3856818 ; 11294964 }. The optimal surgical approach to patients with HPTJT
syndrome related hyperparathyroidism is debated and it is unclear whether to offer subtotal

1124

parathyroidectomy { 25444225 ; 18436011 } or selective parathyroidectomy when there is clear
localization to one gland { 19529956 ; 19529956 ; 31929790 }. The majority of non-infiltrative
parafibromin deficient parathyroid tumour/adenomas are cured by surgery. Parafibromin deficient
parathyroid carcinomas may be at increased risk of recurrence and metastasis compared to nonCDC73 related carcinomas { 16931959 ; 24402736 ; 24081804 ; 29324469 }. Whilst it is a very
rare event, it is worth noting that there are isolated case reports of unequivocally histologically
benign
parafibromin
deficient
parathyroid
tumours
which
have
metastasized
{ 18436011 ; 21899683 ; 24402736 ; 24081804 ; 29324469 }. However the incidence of
metastasis by an otherwise benign parafibromin deficient parathyroid tumour is very low –
estimated be significantly less than 4% { 29324469 }, and far exceeded by the risk of a new primary
tumour
in
a
different
gland
during
long
term
follow
up
{ 19529956 ; 25444225 ; 29324469 ; 18436011 ; 19169472 }.

#23907 Ossifying fibroma arising in HPTJT syndrome, High power view of the avascular
hypercellular stroma composed of bland spindled cells surrounding bony trabeculae.

L#23909 Parafibromin deficient parathyroid tumour, Cytological features include nuclear
enlargement often with preserved N/C ratios, multinucleation, perinuclear cytoplasmic clearing
and eosinophilic cell type, sometimes with a glassy quality.
R#24185 Parafibromin deficient parathyroid tumour, It is emphasized that for parafibromin
immunohistochemistry to be considered negative there should be an internal positive control in
non-neoplastic cells including non-neoplastic parathyroid tissue.

1125

Li Fraumeni syndrome
Definition
An autosomal dominant cancer predisposition syndrome caused by germline variants of
the TP53 gene.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering
#151623 Li–Fraumeni syndrome; LFS
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Not recommended: sarcoma family syndrome of Li and Fraumeni
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
LFS is associated with cancers of the breast, soft tissue, bone, brain, adrenal glands, and female
genital tract. Reported cases in the IARC LFS database with respect to the head and neck region,
involved the larynx, the parotid gland, the nasal cavity and the nasopharynx; oral cancer may also
occur.
Clinical features
LFS is characterized by the early onset of a broad spectrum of cancers and a high lifetime cancer
risk. Breast and adrenocortical carcinomas, brain tumours (particularly choroid plexus carcinoma),
leukaemia, and soft tissue and bone sarcomas are considered to be the core tumours, which
constitute about 70% of LFS-related neoplasms { 29076966 }. Cancers of the head and neck region
are very uncommon. The expanded clinical definition allows a broader recognition of individuals
and families with LFS (see Box #16235) { 26014290 ; 32457520 }.
Epidemiology
Head and neck and larynx tumours are very rare, accounting for 0.31% and 0.12% of cases,
respectively (8 and 3 of 2591 included tumours, respectively) in version R20 (July 2019) of the
IARC TP53 Database [[IARC TP53 Database [Internet]. Lyon (France): International Agency for
Research on Cancer; 2019. Version R20, July 2019. Available from: http://p53.iarc.fr/.]]
{ 27328919 }.
Etiology
LFS is caused by germline sequence variants of the TP53 gene { 1978757 ; 2259385 }. A broad
spectrum of mutations involving the coding regions of the gene has been found in families with
LFS, but 20–40% of individuals with LFS and the majority of families with Li–Fraumeni–like
syndrome lack detectable mutations { 14583457 ; 12619118 }. The lack of 100% concordance
between TP53 mutations and the classic LFS phenotype may be explained in several ways,
including posttranslational alterations, complete deletion, the effects of modifier genes, and
alterations of other genes influencing the phenotype generated by the presence of specific germline
alterations { 21779515 }. Mutations may occur at specific hotspot codons that either interfere with
DNA binding or disrupt the structure of the binding surface, thus interfering with its ability to
modulate the transcription of target genes { 20182602 }. Missense mutations lead to a codon
change, posing challenges to the functional interpretation of new variants { 23161690 }. Further
mutations may occur outside the DNA-binding domain and include rearrangements and deletions
{ 27984644 }. Future studies with novel sequencing technologies such as next-generation
sequencing may be able to uncover a higher number of mutations in TP53, as well as in other
genes in LFS and Li–Fraumeni–like syndrome { 28509937 }.
Pathogenesis
The activation of TP53, one of the most prominent tumour suppressors, leads to protective cellular
processes including cell-cycle arrest, apoptosis, and senescence to prevent the propagation of
genetically altered cells { 11099028 }. Under normal conditions, the p53 protein is maintained at
low levels as a result of rapid turnover mediated by MDM2, its main negative regulator
{ 28254861 }. Cells from individuals with LFS exhibit genomic instability, telomere dysfunction, and
spontaneous immortalization { 23587008 }. Mutant p53 isoforms vary in their abilities to inhibit
wildtype p53 in a dominant negative fashion { 12826609 ; 17311302 }. Some even exhibit overt
oncogenic qualities, although this gain-of-function mechanism is poorly understood { 30224644 }.

1126

Macroscopic appearance
Not clinically relevant
Histopathology
Most tumours are squamous cell carcinomas, particularly, occurring in the larynx, the pharynx and
the oral cavity { 27328919 ; 11120478 }. Various sarcomas appear to be the most common head
and neck malignancies in pediatric LFS patients. In particular, rhabdomyosarcoma, mandibular
osteosarcoma, synovial cell sarcoma and pleomorphic myxoid liposarcoma have been reported
{ 33971750 ; 32959210 }.
Cytology
Tumour cytopathology is similar to sporadic counterparts.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Guidelines for the selection of candidates for TP53 germline testing have recently been updated
(see Box #16235).
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: pathogenic germline mutation in TP53; appropriate family history.
Staging
Staging is performed according to the affected organ sites.
Prognosis and prediction
Prognosis and therapy varies with tumour types encountered.

Fanconi anaemia
Definition
Fanconi anaemia (FA) is a heterogeneous disorder resulting from germline sequence variants in
one of the 22 genes involved in DNA repair and maintenance of genomic stability. It is characterized
by cellular hypersensitivity to DNA cross-linking agents, physical abnormalities, bone marrow
failure and an increased risk for haematologic and solid malignancies.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering:
#227650 FANCA
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Not recommended: Fanconi Pancytopenia/FANC
Subtype(s)
FANC Complementation groups A-W/ FA Type A-W
Localization
Malignancies of upper digestive and urogenital tracts; myeloid leukaemia; breast cancer only in
FANCD1/BRCA2, FANCS/BRCA1 and FANCN/PALB2. The most common solid cancer in Fanconi
anaemia (FA) patients is head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC).
Clinical features
FA is the most common hereditary cancer syndrome, described first by the Swish paediatrician
Guido Fanconi in 1927 { 12525534 }. FA is characterized by cellular hypersensitivity to DNA crosslinking agents, physical abnormalities, bone marrow failure and an increased risk for haematologic
and solid malignancies { 20507306 ; 12525534 }.
The clinical manifestations of FA are pleotropic without genotype-phenotype correlation
{ 12525534 }. FA is characterized by premature aging, short stature, microcephaly, hypoplastic
thumb, multiorgan disorders, cutaneous pigmentation, hearing loss and intellectual disability
{ 12525534 }. FA patients present with progressive bone marrow failure and a predisposition to
haematologic and solid malignancies { 12525534 ; 12393516 ; 25307146 }. FA patients with bone
marrow failure are treated with allogenic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (aHSCT)
{ 24144640 }.
Epidemiology
FA is a rare inherited disorder with a prevalence of 3 cases per million with a carrier frequency of
1 in 300 in Europe and the United States { 12525534 }. Some ethnic groups such as Ashkenazi
Jews, Afrikaners, and Tunisians have the highest carrier frequency (1 in 80-90) due to founder
1127

effects and isolation { 12525534 ; 33679882 }. FANC-A is the most common type accounting 65%
of all FA cases, followed by FANC-C (12%), FANC-G (8%) (Table 1 #24688) { 29254745 }. The
most common solid cancer in FA patients is head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC)
{ 12525204 ; 22504776 ; 15331448 ; 18831513 }, the risk of which is increased by 500 - 800 fold
{ 12525204 ; 15331448 ; 18831513 ; 22504776 ; 23558727 ; 26567103 }.
Etiology
FA is a heterogeneous genetic chromosomal instability disorder resulting from
pathogenic inactivating germline sequence variants of one of the 22 genes involved in DNA repair
and maintenance of genomic stability { 29254745 ; 16675878 ; 26593718 }. The majority of FA
subtypes have autosomal-recessive inheritance except for the FANC-B and FANC-R with the Xlinked and autosomal-dominant modes of inheritance, respectively { 29254745 }.
Pathogenesis
Proteins encoded by FA genes orchestrate a common signaling pathway (FA-BRCA) that is critical
for
inter-strand
DNA
crosslink
(ICL)
repair
and
genomic
stabilization
{ 29254745 ; 16675878 ; 26593718 }. Increased risk for HNSCC among FA patients is not linked
to any specific FA genotype { 32480311 }. Defective DNA damage response predisposes FA
patients to HNSCC { 25307146 }. Increased risk for HNSCCs among FA occurs without exposure
to tobacco and alcohol { 15331448 ; 26567103 }. Oral human papilloma virus (HPV) prevalence
was higher in FA patients compared to their unaffected family members and unrelated controls
{ 33803570 }. However, the etiologic role for HPV in FA-HNSCC remains unproven
{ 23558727 ; 15735012 }. FA patients with aHSCT have a higher risk of HNSCC compared to nontransplanted FA patients which is attributed to the conditioning regimen and the occurrence of
chronic oral graft-versus-host disease { 22504776 }. Some FA patients have reversion of genomic
duplication to the wild type allele in the hematopoietic tissue leading to spontaneous correction of
bone marrow failure { 32480311 }. These FA patients are still at risk for solid malignancies by
harbouring the bi-allelic FA gene mutations in non-haematopoietic tissue (mosaicism)
{ 32480311 }. In these patients, the diagnosis of HNSCC often precedes the diagnosis of
FA { 22504776 ; 32480311 }. FA-HNSCC frequently occur as multifocal disease, preceded by oral
potentially malignant disorders (OMPD) presenting as lichenoid keratosis or leukoplakia
{ 25662766 ; 26276748 ; 29481998 } (Figure 1 #24686). FA-OPMDs have a higher risk for
malignant transformation than those of general population { 29481998 }. FA-OPMDs must be
biopsied and kept under close surveillance, even if they have low-risk clinical appearance.
Macroscopic appearance
None
Histopathology
Typical histological features of FA- and sporadic-HNSCC are similar. FA-HNSCC frequently
present as poorly differentiated with epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and exhibit higher
proportion of cancer stem cells (Figure 2 #24687) { 21138479 ; 25340704 } .
Cytology
See HNSCC and other tumour types for specific details.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
The diagnosis of FA is established in a proband by diepoxybutane (DEB) or mitomycin (MMC)
induced formation of tri- and quadri-radial figures in chromosome spreads of FA patients’
lymphocytes. Molecular testing options include single gene testing, a multi-gene panel testing and
a more comprehensive genomic sequencing if serial single gene and multi-gene panel analyses
fail to confirm a diagnosis of a patient with clinical features of FA .
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Please see table #31915
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
FA-HNSCCs have an aggressive biologic behaviour characterized by poor survival and high
relapse rates { 26484938 ; 32480311 } . FA patients exhibit higher rates of toxicity for radiation and
chemotherapy due to DNA repair defects and hence, surgery remains the preferred treatment of
choice for FA-HNSCC { 26484938 ; 32480311 }.

tongue in Fanconi anemia patients. A: Leukoplakia; B: Lichenoid keratosis; C: Squamous cell
carcinoma presenting as a non-healing ulcer.

tongue in Fanconi anemia patients. A: Leukoplakia; B: Lichenoid keratosis; C: Squamous cell
carcinoma presenting as a non-healing ulcer.

Dyskeratosis congenita
Definition
Dyskeratosis congenita is an inherited syndrome due to defective telomere maintenance
characterized by a triad of oral leukoplakia, nail dystrophy, and reticulate hyperpigmentation.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering:
#127550, Dyskeratosis congenita autosomal dominant 1, DKCA1;
#613989, Dyskeratosis congenita autosomal dominant 2; DKCA2
#613990, Dyskeratosis congenita autosomal dominant 3; DKCA3
#224230, Dyskeratosis congenita autosomal recessive 1, DKCB1;
#613987, Dyskeratosis congenita autosomal recessive 2, DKCB2;
#613988, Dyskeratosis congenita autosomal recessive 3; DKCB3
#615190, Dyskeratosis congenita autosomal recessive 5; DKCB5
#616353, Dyskeratosis congenita autosomal recessive 6; DKCB6
#305000, Dyskeratosis congenita X-linked, DKCX;
ICD-11 coding
3A70.0 Congenital aplastic anaemia (includes Dyskeratosis congenita)
Related terminology
Zinsser-Engman-Cole syndrome, Hoyeraal-Hreidarsson syndrome, Revesz syndrome
Subtype(s)
Autosomal dominant dyskeratosis congenita (DKCA)

1132

Autosomal recessive dyskeratosis congenita (DKCB)
X-linked dyskeratosis congenita (DKCX), Zinsser-Engman-Cole syndrome
Hoyeraal-Hreidarsson syndrome
Revesz syndrome
Localization
The disease involves the skin as hyperpigmentation, the nail as dystrophy, and oral mucosa as
leukoplakia.
Clinical features
Clinical features are characterized by a triad of oral leukoplakia (80%), nail dystrophy (90%), and
reticulate hyperpigmentation/poikiloderma (80-90%) (see table #27193). Oral leukoplakia is most
commonly seen on the tongue. Signs of dystrophic nails, typically longitudinal ridging, are seen at
the ages of 5-13 years { 19327580 }. Reticular hyperpigmentation is typically fine, lace-like pattern
frequently on the face, upper trunk, and upper arms. Other mucocutaneous findings include
adermatoglyphia (no finger prints), palmo-plantar hyperkeratosis, early graying, scalp or eyelash
hair loss, and epiphora (watering eyes). Bone marrow failure seen in the second decade in 90% of
cases. The patients have an increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck;
upper aerodigestive tract cancers (esophageal carcinoma, gastric adenocarcinoma); and
hematologic malignancy including myelodysplasia, acute myelogenous leukaemia, and Hodgkin
disease.
Epidemiology
The prevalence is approximately 1 in 1,000,000.
Etiology
Dyskeratosis congenita is caused by germline mutations in genes important in telomere biology.
Inheritance is most commonly X-linked recessive (DKC1). Other modes of inheritance include
autosomal dominant (TERC, TINF2 mutations), autosomal recessive (NOP10, NHP2, WRAP53,
CTC1), autosomal dominant or recessive (TERT). De novo / sporadic can occur with TERF1
interacting nuclear factor 2 gene (TINF2) { 30047419 ; 33482595 }.
Pathogenesis
Telomeres are noncoding sequences at chromosome ends. Telomerase adds repeat nucleotides
to 3’end of DNA after replication, to prevent shortening of telomeres with each cell division. A
number of genes has been implicated in dyskeratosis congenital, most commonly defect
in DKC1 which encodes dyskerin protein necessary for telomere maintenance. Without dyskerin,
telomeres progressively shorten which causes cellular apoptosis or senescence, DNA replication
problems and replicative senescence { 33482595 ; 19327580 ; 17785587 ; 20008900 }. For
telomere elongation DKC1 (dyskeratosis congenita 1, dyskerin), TERC (telomerase RNA
component), TERT (telomerase reverse transcriptase), NOP10 (NOP10 ribonucleoprotein)
and NHP2 (NHP2 ribonucleoprotein) encode components of the telomerase complex
responsible. DKC1 is the most commonly involved gene. For telomere protection, TINF2 (TERF1
interacting nuclear factor 2) and ACD (adrenocortical dysplasia homolog) encode components of
the shelterin complex. For recruitment and docking of telomerase on to the telomere CTC1 (CST
telomere maintenance complex component 1) encodes part of the CST complex. WRAP53 (WD
repeat containing antisense to TP53) involves in telomere trafficking. RTEL1 (regulator of telomere
elongation helicase 1) and STN1 involve in telomere replication. PARN (poly(A)-specific
ribonuclease) involves in the control of mRNA stability.
Macroscopic appearance
Not relevant
Histopathology
Cutaneous findings are nonspecific comprising of epidermal atrophy, dermal telangiectasia and
pigment incontinence { 26089061 }.
Cytology
Not relevant
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Clinical diagnosis may be confirmed by telomere length testing. Testing of peripheral blood
leukocytes shows the telomere lengths to be below the first percentile { 19327580 }. Sequencing
of affected genes may be helpful in confirming the exact diagnosis.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
Demonstration of telomere shortening in peripheral blood; clinical findings in the skin, nail or mouth.

1133

Desirable:
Demonstration of mutations of affected genes such as DKC1, TERC, TERT, and others.
Staging
Not relevant
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis varies depending on both genetic and environmental factors. Patients with mild form
of the disease can present with one or more clinical features; whereas, others can develop bone
marrow failure by 20 years of age. By the age of 40, 40% of the patients experience bone marrow
failure.

Ataxia telangiectasia
Definition
Ataxia-Telangiectasia (AT) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by pathogenic germline
homozygous or compound heterozygous variants of the ATM gene.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering
208900 Ataxia-Telangiectasia; AT
ICD-11 coding
4A01.31 DNA repair defects other than combined T-cell or B-cell immunodeficiencies

1135

Related terminology
Not recommended: Louis-Bar Syndrome
Subtype(s)
Variant Ataxia-Telangiectasia
Localization
Neurologic manifestations and conjunctival telangiectasias are important features of the disorder.
Haematolymphoid and solid malignancies involve a variety of anatomic compartments, including
the head and neck region.
Clinical features
Patients develop progressive cerebellar ataxia associated with cerebellar degeneration in early
childhood, telangiectases in sun exposed areas (bulbar conjunctiva, bridge of nose), other
progressive neurologic symptoms, immunosuppression with deficiency of cellular immunity,
recurrent sinopulmonary infections, hypogonadism, insulin resistance and malignancies. Lymphoid
neoplasms of the B and T-cell lineage predominate in AT patients { 31537806 }, and may present
in the head and neck, including involvement of the Waldeyer ring. Myeloid neoplasms are distinctly
uncommon. Other solid tumors developing in these patients may involve brain, gastrointestinal
tract, endocrine system, parotid gland { 11764097 } and liver.
Variant AT is associated with milder disease and protracted manifestations often identified in
adulthood. Telangiectasias are often absent in these patients { 19535770 ; 22345219, 30549301}.
Epidemiology
AT has a birth incidence of approximately 1 per 1 per 40,000-100,000 { 33194896 }. The disorder
is encountered in all geographic locations with variable prevalence.
Etiology
AT is caused by homozygous pathogenic gene variants involving the ATM gene located in
chromosome region 11q22.3. In a small proportion of cases mutations in MRE11A in chromosome
region 11q21 are associated with variant AT-like disorder.
Pathogenesis
The ATM gene is large, comprising 66 exons and pathogenic mutations are scattered throughout
the gene with no distinct hotspots { 12552559 }. Most germline mutations are truncating and result
in classic phenotypes. Missense and in-frame mutations associated with residual protein levels
may account for milder phenotypes denoted as variant AT. ATM encodes a ~ 300 kDa
serine/threonine protein kinase with sequence homology to PI3K family but DNA repair functions.
It is a nuclear protein that plays an important role in the repair of double stranded chromosome
breaks. Affected patients demonstrate hypersensitivity to ionizing irradiation. Activated ATM protein
monomers are recruited to areas of DNA damage. The MRN (MRE11A/RAD50/NBS1) complex is
required for optimal activation of ATM to areas of double-stranded DNA breaks, while several
protein kinases (e.g., CHK2) and p53 key substrates are phosphorylated by ATM. Other functions
of ATM include regulation of cell cycle, apoptosis, telomere maintenance, response to oxidative
stress, mitochondrial homeostasis, and insulin signaling. Heterozygous ATM pathogenic variant
carriers account for a small fraction of hereditary breast, ovarian and pancreatic cancer
{ 22585167 ; 33471991 }.
Macroscopic appearance
Resembles sporadic tumour types.
Histopathology
Resemble the sporadic counterparts.
Cytology
See sporadic tumour types.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Relevant to head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC), deletions near or involving
the ATM have been reported in subsets of HNSCC { 9829743 }. In one study, hypermethylation of
the ATM promoter was detected in 25% of HNSCC { 14744748 } which was associated with
decreased expression in vitro in cell lines. In a targeted sequencing study, putative somatic
pathogenic variants were detected in 6/10 (60%) high risk cutaneous HNSCC { 28984303 }.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
- Characteristic neurologic (ataxia, cerebellar degeneration) and non-neurologic manifestations
(telangiectasias), which may be absent in young children.
- Elevated alpha-fetoprotein in serum (>95% of patients)

1136

- Decreased immunoglobulins (IgA and IgE, selective IgG subtypes), lymphopenia
- Increased chromosome breakage after exposure of blood cells to x-rays
- Pathogenic ATM germline mutation
Staging
See individual tumour types.
Prognosis and prediction
The lifetime risk of malignancy in AT patients is ~25%. Cancer and respiratory insufficiency are
leading causes of death in these patients.

L#26964: Retropharyngeal lymphadenopathy and Waldeyer's ring fullness in a patient with
Ataxia-Telangiectasia.
R#26965: Strong CD20 expression in a diffuse large B-cell lymphoma involving the neck in a
patient with ataxia telangiectasia.

#26966: Somatic mutations in ATM have been reported with increasing frequency in a variety of
tumor types, including squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck. In this example, next
generation sequencing detected several somatic gene variants involving ATM, including this ATM
p.Q1416* nonsense variant, in a lymph node metastasis from a patient with a history of facial
squamous cell carcinomas.

1137

Bloom syndrome
Definition
Bloom syndrome is an autosomal recessive chromosomal breakage syndrome with distinct
clinical features and the development of tumours including oropharyngeal carcinoma.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering:
#210900 Bloom syndrome, BS.
ICD-11 coding
4A01.31 DNA repair defects other than combined T-cell or B-cell immunodeficiencies
Related terminology
Bloom-Torre-Machacek syndrome; Congenital teleangectatic erythema
Subtype(s)
None
Localization
None
Clinical features
The most prominent and constant physical feature is the small size, (height, weight and head size).
Also common are lack of subcutaneous fat tissue, and a rash most commonly on cheeks and nose,
usually first appearing during infancy after sun exposure { 28846287 }. High pitched voice and
distinctive facial features (long narrow face, prominent nose and ears, small lower jaw) can be
observed. Most affected individuals have normal intellectual ability. Men tend to be
infertile (azoospermia and oligospermia) while women may be fertile but often have early
menopause. BS individuals have mild immune system abnormalities (often low IgM and IgA levels)
leading to recurrent infections of the upper respiratory tract, ears, and lungs during infancy and are
at greater-than-normal risk of chronic obstructive lung disease and diabetes mellitus resembling
the adult-onset type. In addition, they are 150-300 times more likely to develop cancers than normal
individuals (66% solid tumours, 33% leukaemia/lymphoma). Colorectal cancer is the most common
tumour in the BS registry (29 cases), followed by skin (24 cases), breast and oropharyngeal cancer
(24 cases each) { 17407155 }.
Epidemiology
BS, first described in New York City in 1954 by dermatologist David Bloom, is extremely rare, with
about 283 cases reported to the Bloom Syndrome Registry { 17407155 }. While the overall
prevalence is unknown, in the Ashkenazi Jewish population it is estimated at approximately
1/48,000 births. A founder mutation, known as BLMash is present in approximately 1 in 100
persons of Ashkenazi Jewish background. There are also founder mutations in the Slavic and
Hispanic populations.
Etiology
Caused by mutations in the BLM gene on15q26.1. which encodes for a member of the RecQ family
of DNA helicases.
Pathogenesis
Loss of BLM function results in accumulation of errors during DNA replication and increased
chromosomal rearrangements and breakages, leading to genetic instability and predisposing to
cancer { 33736941 }.
Macroscopic appearance
None specific.
Histopathology
See tumour types involved.
Cytology
See tumour types involved.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
When BS is clinically suspected, detection of biallelic pathogenic variants in the BLM gene and/or
identification of increased frequency of sister-chromatid exchanges on specialized cytogenetic
studies may help establishing the diagnosis.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential:
- Clinical features including small size, and distinctive facial features.

1138

- BLM mutation
Staging
None
Prognosis and prediction
Cancer and related complications are the leading cause of death, on average occurring in the third
decade of life { 20301572 }. Sun exposure to the face and other exposed areas, particularly in
infancy and early childhood, should be avoided and exposure to ionizing radiation should be
minimized. Cancer screening and surveillance are recommended { 18359209 }.

Von-Hippel Lindau
Definition
An autosomal dominant tumour syndrome caused by inactivating mutations in the VHL gene.
ICD-O coding
MIM numbering
193300 Von Hippel–Lindau syndrome; VHLS
ICD-11 coding
None
Related terminology
Von Hippel Lindau (VHL) disease.
Subtype(s)
VHL types 1, 2A, 2B, and 2C (Table 1)
Localization
Ears. Data from the International ELST Registry { 5867206 "> 2 5867206 } show a 3.6% prevelance
of ELSTs in VHLS. VHL germline mutations were identified in 39% of apparently sporadic ELSTs,
showing ELSTs are the initial presentation of VHLS in 32% of patients. These findings support
genetic
testing
in
all
patients
with
ELSTs
{ 20351605 ; 20495761 ; 21451430 ; 25650230 ; 5867206 "> 2 5867206 } and all VHLS patients
should
be
radiographically
screened
for
ELSTs
{ 15035284 ; 15190140 ; 23070752 }. Endolymphatic sac tumours are bilateral in 30% of the VHL
cases { 9145719 ; 5867206 }.
Central nervous system (CNS) haemangioblastomas (80% of patients) occur in cerebellum, spinal
cord and brainstem, but also rarely supratentorial brain { 23400300 ; 12546356 }.
Ocular manifestations are expected in roughly half of VHL patients. Retinal haemangioblastomas
(85%) Retinal capillary haemangioblastomas are the most commonly observed tumours in VHL
and are often the initial manifestation of the disease { 17057815 ; 28972023 ; 31588386 }, are often
multiple and are bilateral in about 50% { 33720516 }.
Kidney cysts (>70%) and renal cell carcinomas (40%) are usually multiple and bilateral
{ 8929948 ; 2274658 }.
Adrenal/paraganglia. Adrenal pheochromocytomas (30%) and/or extraadrenal paragangliomas,
rare { 10458336 }.
Pancreatic
neuroendocrine
tumours,
cysts
and
serous
cystadenomas
(>90%)
{ 11040195 ; 19238077 ; 22659535 ; 9665483 ; 19238077 }.
Duodenum, ampulla and gall bladder/cystic duct NETs develop in a minority
{ 23913169 ; 11688471 ; 2379881 }.
Reproductive organs. Epididymal papillary cystadenomas (60% of males) are often bilateral
{ 20367315 }; they occur rarely in the broad ligament and mesosalpinx in women
{ 22317868 ; 20157715 }
Other. Occasionally, haemangioblastomas develop in peripheral nerves { 15350042 ; 22133049 }
or extraneural tissues { 22544391 ; 24145646 ; 17895756 ; 1898683 }.
Clinical features
Clinical presentations include neurologic, obstructive, and hormone excess conditions. Hearing
loss results from endolymphatic sac tumours. Neurologic manifestations often begin with
decreased visual acuity { 31095066 }. Cerebellar ataxia and spinal cord compression result from
haemangioblastomas. Obstructive features include outflow interruptions along the pancreaticobiliary or more commonly the genitourinary tracts from expanding cysts or renal cell
1139

carcinomas. Hormone excess symptoms are caused by pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma or
pancreatic neuroendocrine tumours { 32606780 ; 31368132 ; 17122523 }.
Epidemiology
VHL syndrome has an incidence of 1 in 36,000 births. Data from the International ELST Registry
{ 25867206 } show a 3.6% prevelance of ELSTs in VHLS. VHL germline mutations were identified
in 39% of apparently sporadic ELSTs, showing ELSTs are the initial presentation of VHLS in 32%
of patients. These findings support genetic testing in all patients with ELSTs
{ 20351605 ; 20495761 ; 21451430 ; 25650230 ; 25867206 } and all VHLS patients should be
radiographically screened for ELSTs { 15035284 ; 15190140 ; 23070752 }. There is a wide age
range at presentation (10 to 88 years), but the majority are between 30 and 40 years, although
younger in VHLS patients { 30291511 }. There is a female to male ratio of 1.6:1
{ 2804921 ; 15035284 ; 15190140 ; 23070752 ; 30291511 }.
Etiology
Germline mutations in the VHL gene that inactivate the VHL protein. Germline mutations of the
VHL tumour suppressor gene are usually detected in the patients with endolymphatic sac tumour.
Somatic and germline mutation analysis of ELSTs has been performed, with many VHL mutations
and
allelic
deletions
identified
{ 9214679 ; 10932304 ; 11085513 ; 15796386 ; 16322231 ; 20351605 ; 20495761 ; 20850701 }.
Pathogenesis
Type 1 VHL is associated with large deletion or truncation mutations of the VHL gene that generally
yield a protein with very little or no biological activity. Type 2 is usually due to missense mutations
that result in a protein with limited activity. Somatic second hits of the wildtype allele inactivate the
VHL protein in various tissues.
Macroscopic appearance
The unique features of all VHL-associated lesions are their multiplicity, bilaterality and striking bright
yellow appearance attributable to lipid accumulation { 17877533 ; 27247714 ; 3592062 }.
Histopathology
Ear Endolymphatic Sac Tumours: The tumours are papillary and/or cystic, commonly with bone
remodeling. A single layer of low cuboidal to columnar cells are arranged in simple, coarse, broad
interdigitating papillary projections with fibrovascular cores, showing limited branching, found within
cystic spaces. Cystic spaces contain serum, secretions, and/or erythrocytes. Fibrosis may be
present. The acinar spaces filled with inspissated material with similarity to thyroid colloid. The
cytoplasm is ample, clear, vacuolated with indistinct cell membranes. The nuclei are uniformly
small, round, and hyperchromatic with coarse nuclear chromatin and small nucleoli
{ 2804921 ; 7936748 ; 9145719 ; 16778477 ; 18423895 ; 20614260 ; 21167761 ; 30291511 }.
Pleomorphism, increased mitotic figures, and necrosis are inconspicuous.
Immunohistochemistry: The neoplastic cells are positive for pancytokeratin, CK7, HIF-1α, EMA,
GLUT1, CAIX (membranous), and PAX-8 (nuclear), with limited S100 protein, GFAP, and vimentin,
and negative for TTF-1, thyroglobulin, PSA, CD10, P504S, p63, synaptophysin, GATA3, and RCC
{ 7630290 ; 7936748 ; 9023246 ; 11455007 ; 15035285 ; 16778477 ; 18423895 ; 30291511 }.
Haemangioblastomas are circumscribed, often associated with a pseudocyst, and composed of
variable proliferations of abundant mature vasculature thought to be reactive and the neoplastic
“stromal” cell component of haemangioblast progenitor cells { 23400300 ; 9158701 }, There are
two morphologic variants. Cellular (epithelioid) haemangioblastomas have prominent, larger,
epithelioid “stromal” cells with clear cytoplasm that may be clustered in groups. The more common,
and usually smaller, vascular-rich reticular (mesenchymal) haemangioblastomas have abundant
vascularity and few scattered “stromal” cells {16281910, 16949923}.
Paragangliomas in patients with VHL may have a thick fibrous or vascular capsule and myxoid,
edematous, or hyalinized stroma. The tumour cells have clear vacuolated cytoplasm
{ 3592062 ; 12114747 }, and lack hyaline globules { 12114747 }. CAIX is usually positive with a
membranous staining pattern; they also can express alpha-inhibin { 23257898 ; 31383958 }.
Renal pathology ranges from benign cysts to multifocal, bilateral clear cell RCCs { 31332543 };
clear cell papillary-like RCC has also been reported { 31332543 ; 26559379 }.
The pancreas in VHL exhibits multiple pancreatic cysts, pancreatic cystadenomas, neuroendocrine
tumours and islet abnormalities { 15299200 ; 28697137 }. Distinctive features of NETs include
nuclear atypia, clear cell change, oncocytic cytology and hypervascularity { 15299200 } and they
may be cystic{ 28697137 }. Precursor lesions resembling MEN1 include islet dysplasia,
microtumours and peliosis { 15299200 } Pancreatic NETs also express inhibin { 23913169 }.

1140

Neuroendocrine tumours of the duodenum, ampulla, cystic duct, and gallbladder resemble sporadic
tumours but also express inhibin { 23913169 ; 11688471 }.
Papillary Cystadenoma of the Epididymis are associated with multifocal “epithelial tumourlets”
{ 16841375 }. The cyst lining has intracystic papillary projections with fibrovascular cores and bland
epithelial
cells
with
glycogen-rich
clear
cytoplasm
showing
reverse
polarity
{ 33673939 ; 20367315 ; 24441657 }.
The
tumour
cells
are
positive
for
CAIX
{ 33673939 ; 24441657 }
Papillary Cystadenoma of the Mesosalpinx and Broad Ligament in females resembles papillary
cystadenoma of the epididymis in males. They are more benign than sporadic cases
{ 22296276 ; 22296276 }.
Cytology
Not applicable
Diagnostic molecular pathology
Pathogenic germline mutation in the VHL gene
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Essential: documented pathogenetic variant of the VHL gene and manifestation of any associated
neoplasm(s).
Desirable: haemangioblastomas of retina and/or CNS and any other associated neoplasm in the
absence of a pathogenic mutation in the VHL gene
Staging
There is no uniformly accepted syndrome-specific staging system for VHL. Malignant neoplasms
associated with this syndrome generally follow cancer staging suggested by UICC/AJCC criteria
{Amin MB, Edge SB, Greene FL, et al. editors. AJCC Cancer Staging Manual. 8th ed. Switzerland:
Springer, 2017}. It is recognized, however, that neoplastic components of this syndrome may not
be sufficiently separated from a prognostic standpoint. Such limitations have prompted some
groups to offer modifications that may address this inherent weakness { 30306741 }.
Prognosis and prediction
The prognosis for patients with VHL depends on the lesion(s) that develop in each individual patient
and kindred. Outcome is improved by screening that allows earlier detection of
malignancies. Guidelines for clinical surveillance have been published { 24355456 ; 28620007 }.

cerebellopontine angle and was therefore resected by a neurosurgeon.
R#26819: A CT scan of this same tumour shows destruction of the temporal bone, essentially
excluding the diagnostic consideration of choroid plexus papilloma.

L#26817: The histopathology is reminiscent of choroid plexus papilloma, but the bone invasion
essentially excludes that diagnosis.

essentially excludes that diagnosis.

projections and dilated cystic spaces.

Tuberous sclerosis syndrome
Definition
Tuberous sclerosis is an autosomal dominant genetic disorder resulting from a pathogenic variant
of theTSC1 (Ch 9q34) or TSC2 (Ch 16p13) genes. The disorder is characterized by a variety of
hamartomas and benign neoplasms affecting many organ systems, including skin, soft tissues,
CNS, heart, kidneys and lungs.
ICD-O coding
MIM number: 191100 Tuberous sclerosis 1; TSC1 613254 Tuberous sclerosis 2; TSC2
ICD-11 coding
LD2D.2 Tuberous sclerosis
Related terminology
None.
Subtype(s)
Tuberous sclerosis 1; TSC1 Tuberous sclerosis 2; TSC2
Localization

1142

Major manifestations include cutaneous angiofibromas, shagreen patches, subungual fibromas,
cardiac rhabdomyomas, pulmonary lymphangioleiomyomatosis, and renal angiomyolipomas.
Central nervous system involvement is characterized by cortical tubers, white matter glioneuronal
hamartomas, subependymal nodules, and subependymal giant cell astrocytomas (SEGAs).
Clinical features
Cardiac rhabdomyomas are often a presenting feature of tuberous sclerosis in newborns and
infants aged < 2 years, and more than half of cardiac rhabdomyomas are associated with tuberous
sclerosis. Cutaneous manifestations include hypomelanotic nodules, evident from birth, facial
angiofibromas, shagreen patches, ungual/subungual fibromas, renal angiomyolipomas, renal
cysts, and lymphangioleiomyomatosis. It is important to highlight that the individual phenotypic
features of tuberous sclerosis can also occur sporadically.
Head and Neck Manifestations Manifestations of tuberous sclerosis in the head and neck are
predominantly cutaneous and oral. Angiofibroma has a predilection for the face and are benign
neoplasms typically arising in the centrofacial cheek areas, nasolabial folds and chin { 30246432 }.
Other non-neoplastic cutaneous manifestations include shagreen patches and poliosis, i.e. patches
of white hair involving the scalp or eyelids. Fibrous cephalic plaques appear as rubbery or firm redbrown plaques typically involving the forehead or scalp. Oral manifestations are frequent and
specifically include angiofibromas and dental enamel pits. Angiofibromas are more common in the
anterior gingiva, lips, tongue, and palate. Dental enamel loss on the incisal border and tooth wear
are also observed {24310804, 32644893, 21055980}.
Epidemiology
The disorder affects 25,000–40,000 individuals in the USA and about 1–2 million individuals
worldwide, with an estimated prevalence of 1 case per 6000–10,000 live births and a population
prevalence of around 1 in 20,000 { 24053982 }.
Etiology
Most tuberous sclerosis cases (~60%) result from sporadic (de novo) germline mutations
in TSC1 or TSC2 { 2918523 }. In affected kindreds, the disease follows an autosomal dominant
pattern of inheritance, with high penetrance but considerable phenotypic variability { 7670658 }.
Pathogenesis
TSC1 gene The TSC1 gene maps to chromosome region 9q34 { 2889832 } and contains 23 exons
{ 9242607 }. The TSC1 encoded protein is hamartin (MW 130 kDa) which is strongly expressed in
brain, kidney, and heart { 10349994 }. The most common TSC1 mutations are small deletions and
nonsense mutations (each accounting for ~30% of all mutations in the gene)
{ 11030407 ; 10227394 }. Virtually all mutations result in a truncated gene product. Large deletions
of the TSC1 gene are rare.
TSC2 gene The TSC2 gene maps to chromosome region 16p13.3 { 1303246 } and contains 42
exons. The TSC2 transcript shows widespread expression in many tissues, including the brain and
other organs affected in tuberous sclerosis. Tuberin is the protein product (MW 180 kDA) and bears
significant homology with the catalytic domain of RAP1GAP, a member of the RAS family. The
mutational spectrum of TSC2 is wider than that of TSC1, including large deletions, missense and
splice junction mutations { 9863590 ; 11112665 ; 10205261 }. Large deletions in the TSC2 gene
may extend into the adjacent PKD1 gene, with a resulting phenotype of tuberous sclerosis and
polycystic kidney disease { 17185137 ; 10227394 }.
Tuberin and hamartin form a heterodimeric complex that integrates growth factor and stress signals
from the PI3K/AKT pathway and transmits signals downstream to coordinate multiple cellular
processes, including cell proliferation and cell size { 26893383 ; 2889832 ; 1303246 ; 9809973 }.
The
heterodimer
complex
negatively
regulates
the
mTOR
pathway
{ 16288294 ; 12172555 ; 12271141 }. The understanding of the basic mechanism of mTOR
pathway activation in tuberous sclerosis lesions has led to the use of mTOR inhibitors in the
treatment of manifestations of tuberous sclerosis.
Macroscopic appearance
Not relevant.
Histopathology
Angiofibromas form well circumscribed nodules composed of bland oval cells, with delicate
collagen deposition and evenly distributed small to medium sized vessels { 27140177 }. Fibrous
cephalic plaques are characterized by thickened collagen bundles with a decrease or absence of
elastic fibers. They may have dilated vessels resembling angiofibromas { 29258863 }

1143

Cytology
Not relevant.
Diagnostic molecular pathology
In the revised diagnostic criteria, a pathogenic germline variant of TSC1 or TSC2 is an independent
diagnostic criterion and is sufficient for a definitive diagnosis of Tuberous Sclerosis
{ 24053983 ; 24053983 }. Genetic testing is recommended for family members of an affected
patient, especially in infants, and may also be offered as a preimplantation or prenatal test.
Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria
Diagnostic clinical and genetic criteria are summarized in Table 1 #32378.
Staging
Not relevant.
Prognosis and prediction
Tuberous sclerosis tends to shorten lifespan slightly when compared with non-syndromic
Caucasian controls { 1861550 }. The most common causes of death in the second decade of life
are brain tumours and status epilepticus, followed by renal abnormalities { 24053982 ; 27935023 }.
In patients aged > 40 years of age, mortality is most commonly associated with renal complications
or lymphangioleiomyomatosis.
